- [x] **Pneumatic/Hydraulic Force:** $F = (P_s - P_b) \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ — always subtract back pressure.
- [x] **Retraction Force < Extension Force** due to rod area loss.
- [x] **Pneumatic vs Hydraulic:** Air is compressible (less precise, fast), Oil is incompressible (precise, slow).
- [x] **SMA Phases:** Martensite (soft, low-T) ↔ Austenite (rigid, high-T).
- [x] **Electric Actuators** = Industry 4.0 friendly (Profinet, IO-Link, HART).
- [x] **Selection Criteria:** Force, stroke, precision, environment, cleanliness, cost.
- [x] **Valve control in process industries** predominantly uses **pneumatic diaphragm actuators**.
- [x] **Aerospace & Biomedical** rely heavily on **piezoelectric and SMA** micro actuators.
- [x] **Mention real-world applications** in every answer — KTU examiners reward application-oriented thinking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ACTUATOR (KTU 2024 Definition):** An **actuator** is a mechanical or electro-mechanical device that converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal energy) from a controller into a corresponding physical action — typically **linear displacement, rotary motion, force, or torque** — to perform work on an industrial process or load.

In the context of **Industrial Networks (PEICT747)**, an actuator is the **final control element** in any automation loop. It sits at the field level of the **ISA-95 / Purdue Model**, receiving commands from controllers (PLCs, DCS, SCADA) over industrial communication protocols such as **PROFIBUS, PROFINET, Modbus, EtherNet/IP, or Foundation Fieldbus**.

The generalized input-output relationship of an actuator can be expressed as:

$$
y_{actuator}(t) = f\!\left(u_{control}(t),\; E_{source}\right)
$$

where $u_{control}(t)$ is the command signal and $E_{source}$ represents the energy domain (electrical, fluid, mechanical).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Real-World Analogy — The Human Body Analogy**
>
> Think of an industrial automation system as a **human body**:
> - **Sensors** = the five senses (eyes, ears, skin) — they *sense* the environment.
> - **Controller (PLC/DCS)** = the brain — it *decides* what to do.
> - **Actuator** = the **muscles and limbs** — they *act* and produce physical movement.
>
> Just as your brain sends electrical signals through nerves to your bicep to lift a cup, a PLC sends a **4–20 mA current signal** or a **digital fieldbus telegram** to a valve actuator to open a pipeline. **No matter how intelligent the brain is, without muscles (actuators), nothing physical happens.**

## 1.3 The Role of Actuators in the Industrial Control Loop

A typical closed-loop industrial process follows this chain:

$$
\text{Reference} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{Actuator} \rightarrow \text{Process} \rightarrow \text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Feedback}
$$

The actuator is the **only element that produces physical energy transfer** to manipulate the process variable (flow, pressure, temperature, level).

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics for Actuators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Performance Parameters to remember:**
> - **Stroke** — total linear displacement (mm)
> - **Torque / Thrust** — rotational (Nm) or linear (N) force output
> - **Response Time** — typically **< 1 s** for fast actuators
> - **Accuracy** — often **±0.1 % to ±1 %** of full scale
> - **Operating Pressure** — for fluid-powered actuators (**1–700 bar** for hydraulics, **1–10 bar** for pneumatics)
> - **Ingress Protection (IP Rating)** — **IP65 / IP67** standard for industrial environments

## 1.5 Why Actuator Classification Matters in Industrial Networks

Different actuator classes support **different industrial protocols and addressing schemes**. For example:
- **Smart Electrical Actuators** support HART, PROFIBUS-PA.
- **Pneumatic actuators** are typically paired with **positioners** (smart I/P converters) for digital feedback.
- **Hydraulic actuators** dominate in high-force, low-speed applications (presses, forging).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Classification — By Energy Source

The **ISA (International Society of Automation)** and most KTU-recommended textbooks classify actuators into **four primary energy domains**:

### 2.1.1 ⚡ Electrical Actuators
Convert **electrical energy → mechanical motion**.

**Sub-classes:**
| Sub-Type | Operating Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **DC Motor** | Commutator-based rotation | Small valves, robotics |
| **AC Induction Motor** | Electromagnetic induction | Conveyors, pumps |
| **Servo Motor / Stepper** | Closed-loop precise positioning | CNC, pick-and-place |
| **Solenoid** | Magnetic field pulls a plunger | On/Off valves, latches |
| **Piezoelectric** | Crystal deformation under voltage | Micro-positioning, valves |

### 2.1.2 💧 Hydraulic Actuators
Convert **hydraulic (oil) pressure energy → mechanical motion**.

**Sub-classes:**
- **Single-acting cylinder** — pressure on one side, spring return.
- **Double-acting cylinder** — pressure alternates on both sides.
- **Hydraulic motor** — rotary output.
- **Vane / Gear / Piston pump** (acting as actuator in some loops).

### 2.1.3 🌬️ Pneumatic Actuators
Convert **compressed air energy → mechanical motion**.

**Sub-classes:**
- **Spring-return diaphragm** — common in process control valves.
- **Piston-type (Scotch yoke / Rack-pinion)** — high-torque rotary.
- **Vane-type rotary** — quarter-turn valves.

### 2.1.4 ⚙️ Mechanical Actuators
Convert **stored mechanical energy → motion**.

**Examples:** springs, cams, ratchets, escapements, thermal bimetallic strips.

---

## 2.2 Secondary Classification — By Motion Type

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** This classification is **frequently asked as a 3-mark question** in Part A.

| Motion Type | Symbol | Linear/Angular | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $L$ | $s$ (mm) | Hydraulic cylinder, solenoid |
| **Rotary** | $R$ | $\theta$ (rad) | Servo motor, pneumatic vane |

The relationship between linear and rotary motion via a lead screw of pitch $p$:

$$
s = \frac{p \cdot \theta}{2\pi}
$$

where:
- $s$ = linear displacement (m)
- $p$ = screw pitch (m/rev)
- $\theta$ = angular displacement (rad)

---

## 2.3 Tertiary Classification — By Control Action

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-State (On/Off) vs. Continuous (Modulating) Actuators**
> - **On/Off Actuators:** operate at **0 % or 100 %** — no intermediate position. Example: solenoid valve.
> - **Modulating Actuators:** operate at **any position between 0 % and 100 %** — proportional to control signal. Example: control valve with 4–20 mA positioner.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is your **exam-day revision master** for the topic "Classification of Actuators". Note the use of `\mid` instead of `\|` to keep the markdown table safe.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Force from Pressure** | $F = P \cdot A$ | $F$ in N, $P$ in Pa, $A$ in m² | Hydraulic / Pneumatic cylinder |
| 2 | **Cylinder Bore Diameter** | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4F}{\pi \cdot P}}$ | m | Sizing a cylinder |
| 3 | **Piston Velocity** | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m/s | Fluid-powered actuators |
| 4 | **Power — Hydraulic** | $P_h = P \cdot Q$ | W | Pump sizing |
| 5 | **Power — Electrical Motor** | $P_e = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | W | AC motor selection |
| 6 | **Torque** | $\tau = F \cdot r$ | Nm | Rotary actuator |
| 7 | **Angular Velocity** | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N}{60}$ | rad/s | Motor speed |
| 8 | **Mechanical Power (rotary)** | $P_m = \tau \cdot \omega$ | W | Motor / gearbox |
| 9 | **Screw-driven Linear Motion** | $s = \dfrac{p \cdot \theta}{2\pi}$ | m | Stepper-driven slide |
| 10 | **Stiffness (Spring Actuator)** | $k = \dfrac{F}{\Delta x}$ | N/m | Mechanical actuator |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Dominant Actuator Type | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Oil & Gas / Petrochemical** | Pneumatic diaphragm + Hydraulic | Intrinsic safety, high reliability |
| **Automotive Assembly** | Electrical (Servo) + Pneumatic | Precision + speed |
| **Steel / Forging** | Hydraulic | Extreme force (thousands of tonnes) |
| **Food & Pharma** | Pneumatic / Electric | Clean, no oil contamination |
| **Aerospace** | Electro-hydraulic / Piezo | High power density, precision |
| **Robotics** | Servo / Stepper | Closed-loop accuracy |

## 2.6 Decision Logic — Choosing the Right Actuator

A KTU-style "why this and not that" reasoning flow:

- **Need high force (>\mathbf{10^5 \, N})?** → **Hydraulic**
- **Need clean, fast, mid-range force?** → **Pneumatic**
- **Need precise positioning + networked control?** → **Electrical (Servo)**
- **Need on/off, low cost, intrinsically safe?** → **Solenoid / Pneumatic**
- **Need micro-motion (µm level)?** → **Piezoelectric**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Force of a Pneumatic / Hydraulic Cylinder

> **Problem Statement:** A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has a bore diameter of $D = 0.10$ m and a rod diameter of $d = 0.025$ m. The supply pressure is $P_s = 6 \times 10^5$ Pa (i.e., 6 bar). Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 1.01 \times 10^5$ Pa. Calculate the **extension force** and the **retraction force**, assuming the gauge pressure is the working pressure.

### Step 1 — Identify the working area

The piston face area (annular geometry during retraction):

$$
A_{extend} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_{retract} = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the values

$$
A_{extend} = \frac{\pi (0.10)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.01}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{retract} = \frac{\pi ((0.10)^2 - (0.025)^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.01 - 0.000625)}{4} = 7.365 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Newton's second law for force

$$
F_{extend} = P_s \cdot A_{extend}
$$

$$
F_{extend} = (6 \times 10^5) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) = 4712.4 \text{ N} \approx 4.71 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
F_{retract} = P_s \cdot A_{retract}
$$

$$
F_{retract} = (6 \times 10^5) \times (7.365 \times 10^{-3}) = 4419.0 \text{ N} \approx 4.42 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the result

> **Result:** $F_{extend} \approx 4.71 \text{ kN}$ and $F_{retract} \approx 4.42 \text{ kN}$. The retraction force is **lower** because the rod side has a smaller effective area. This is a **standard KTU textbook derivation** showing the asymmetric nature of double-acting cylinders.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Transmission in an Electric Actuator

> **Problem Statement:** A 3-phase AC induction motor drives a valve through a gear train of ratio $G = 20 : 1$ (speed reduction). Motor speed $N_m = 1450$ RPM, motor torque $\tau_m = 12$ Nm. Calculate the **output shaft speed**, **output torque** (assuming 90 % efficiency), and **mechanical power**.

### Step 1 — Convert motor speed to rad/s

$$
\omega_m = \frac{2\pi N_m}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1450}{60} = 151.84 \text{ rad/s}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute output speed (reduced by gear ratio)

$$
\omega_{out} = \frac{\omega_m}{G} = \frac{151.84}{20} = 7.592 \text{ rad/s}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute input mechanical power

$$
P_{in} = \tau_m \cdot \omega_m = 12 \times 151.84 = 1822.1 \text{ W}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply gear efficiency

$$
P_{out} = \eta \cdot P_{in} = 0.90 \times 1822.1 = 1639.9 \text{ W} \approx 1.64 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute output torque

$$
\tau_{out} = \frac{P_{out}}{\omega_{out}} = \frac{1639.9}{7.592} = 216.0 \text{ Nm}
$$

### Step 6 — Cross-check using gear ratio directly

$$
\tau_{out,\,ideal} = G \cdot \tau_m = 20 \times 12 = 240 \text{ Nm}
$$

After efficiency loss:

$$
\tau_{out} = \eta \cdot \tau_{out,\,ideal} = 0.90 \times 240 = 216.0 \text{ Nm} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Result:** The gear train **increases torque by factor G** while **reducing speed by 1/G**, conserving power (minus losses). This is the **fundamental principle of every motorised valve actuator in process industries**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Actuator Classes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU students must memorize this comparative table** — it is a **favourite 14-mark question** in ESE.

| Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Electrical (Servo) | Mechanical |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Oil under pressure | Compressed air | Electrical | Stored strain / mass |
| **Force / Torque Range** | Very high (>\mathbf{10^6 \, N}) | Medium (\mathbf{10^3 - 10^4 \, N}) | Low–Medium | Low |
| **Speed** | Slow (0.1 – 1 m/s) | Fast (1 – 5 m/s) | Very fast / precise | Variable |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate | Moderate | **Very high (µm)** | Low |
| **Position Control** | Good (with servovalve) | Fair (with positioner) | Excellent (closed-loop) | Limited |
| **Stiffness** | **High** (incompressible fluid) | Low (compressible air) | High | Variable |
| **Cleanliness** | Poor (oil leak risk) | **Excellent** | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Intrinsic Safety** | Poor | **Excellent** | Poor (needs barriers) | Excellent |
| **Cost** | High | Low | Medium–High | Low |
| **Maintenance** | High (seals, filters) | Medium (air dryer) | Low (brushless) | Very low |
| **Network Compatibility** | Limited (smart valves) | Good (with I/P + HART) | **Excellent (native fieldbus)** | None |
| **Typical Industrial Use** | Presses, Injection moulding | Process valves, clamping | Robotics, CNC | Safety relief, thermostats |

## 3.4 Comparative Trade-Off Equation

A simplified **figure-of-merit (FoM)** that KTU examiners often quote:

$$
FOM = \frac{F_{output} \cdot v_{max}}{\eta_{system} \cdot Cost}
$$

- A **hydraulic actuator** wins on $F_{output}$ but loses on $\eta_{system}$ (pump losses ~ 30–50 %).
- A **servo-electric actuator** wins on $\eta$ (>\mathbf{90 \%}) and clean operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Master Classification Tree of Actuators

The diagram below shows the **complete hierarchical classification** of industrial actuators as per KTU 2024 syllabus. Note: every node ID is alphanumeric to avoid Mermaid reserved-keyword conflicts.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[ACTUATORS] --> B1[By Energy Source]
    A1 --> B2[By Motion Type]
    A1 --> B3[By Control Action]

    B1 --> C1[Electrical]
    B1 --> C2[Hydraulic]
    B1 --> C3[Pneumatic]
    B1 --> C4[Mechanical]

    C1 --> D1a[DC Motor]
    C1 --> D1b[AC Induction Motor]
    C1 --> D1c[Servo / Stepper]
    C1 --> D1d[Solenoid]
    C1 --> D1e[Piezoelectric]

    C2 --> D2a[Single Acting Cylinder]
    C2 --> D2b[Double Acting Cylinder]
    C2 --> D2c[Hydraulic Motor]
    C2 --> D2d[Vane / Gear / Piston]

    C3 --> D3a[Diaphragm Type]
    C3 --> D3b[Piston Type]
    C3 --> D3c[Rotary Vane]
    C3 --> D3d[Spring Return]

    C4 --> D4a[Spring]
    C4 --> D4b[Cam Follower]
    C4 --> D4c[Ratchet]
    C4 --> D4d[Thermal Bimetal]

    B2 --> E1[Linear]
    B2 --> E2[Rotary]

    B3 --> F1[On Off Two State]
    B3 --> F2[Modulating Continuous]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Actuator Inside an Industrial Network Loop

This block diagram shows **how the actuator integrates into a networked control system** (PROFIBUS / PROFINET / HART layer).

```mermaid
graph LR
    HMI[Operator HMI SCADA] --> CTRL[Controller PLC DCS]
    CTRL -->|Digital Fieldbus PROFIBUS PROFINET| GW[Network Gateway]
    GW -->|Analog 4 to 20 mA OR Digital Fieldbus| DRIVER[Actuator Driver Power Stage]
    DRIVER --> ACT[ACTUATOR Field Device]
    ACT -->|Mechanical Output Force Torque Stroke| PROC[Industrial Process]
    PROC -->|Process Variable| SENS[Sensor Transmitter]
    SENS -->|Feedback 4 to 20 mA OR HART| CTRL
```

**Key takeaways for the student:**
- The **actuator driver** (e.g., VFD for motors, I/P converter for pneumatic valves) is what bridges the **network layer (digital)** and the **physical layer (energy conversion)**.
- **Smart actuators** integrate the driver + feedback + diagnostics into a single fieldbus node, enabling **predictive maintenance** and **condition monitoring** over the industrial network.

## 4.3 Schematic — Force / Torque Comparison Across Actuator Types

The diagram below illustrates the **typical operating envelope** of each actuator class in terms of force output and stroke speed.

```mermaid
graph TD
    X[Operating Envelope of Actuator Classes] --> Y1[Hydraulic: Very High Force 10 to the 6 N, Slow Speed]
    X --> Y2[Pneumatic: Medium Force 10 to the 4 N, High Speed]
    X --> Y3[Electric Servo: Low to Medium Force 10 to the 3 N, Very High Precision]
    X --> Y4[Mechanical Spring: Low Force, One Shot]
    X --> Y5[Piezoelectric: Micro Force, Micro Stroke nanometer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to *select* an actuator in a 14-mark question, the **correct KTU answer format** is:
> 1. State the requirement (force, speed, accuracy, environment).
> 2. Reference the comparison matrix above.
> 3. Justify the choice with at least **two quantitative parameters**.
> 4. Mention **network compatibility** if the question involves an industrial network context.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an actuator. List the four primary classifications based on energy source.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **actuator** is a device that converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal) into a corresponding physical output such as **linear displacement, rotary motion, force, or torque**, thereby performing work on an industrial process.

The four primary classifications based on energy source are:

1. **Electrical Actuators** — convert electrical energy to mechanical motion (e.g., DC motor, servo motor, solenoid, stepper).
2. **Hydraulic Actuators** — convert fluid (oil) pressure energy to mechanical motion (e.g., double-acting cylinder, hydraulic motor).
3. **Pneumatic Actuators** — convert compressed air energy to mechanical motion (e.g., diaphragm actuator, piston-type, rotary vane).
4. **Mechanical Actuators** — convert stored mechanical energy to motion (e.g., spring, cam, ratchet, bimetallic strip).

> **Marking note:** Definition = 1 mark; Four classifications with examples = 2 marks.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between two-state (on/off) and modulating (continuous) actuators with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Two-State (On/Off) | Modulating (Continuous) |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Positions** | Only **0 % or 100 %** (fully open / fully closed) | **Any position between 0 % and 100 %** |
| **Control Signal** | Discrete (binary) | Proportional (4–20 mA, 0–10 V, fieldbus) |
| **Accuracy** | Low — no intermediate positions | High — proportional positioning |
| **Example** | **Solenoid valve** in a coolant line | **Motorised control valve** with positioner in a flow loop |
| **Application** | Safety shut-off, simple sequencing | Closed-loop PID control of flow, pressure, temperature |

> **Marking note:** Tabular comparison = 2 marks; one example each = 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, every Module question in Part B carries **14 marks** with an **internal choice**. You must attempt **ONE** of the two alternatives. The mark split below is **7 + 7** for the two sub-parts, which is the standard KTU distribution.

---

### ✍️ Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Analytical Question

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`**

> **(a)** Explain, with neat block diagrams, the **classification of industrial actuators** based on (i) energy source, (ii) motion type, and (iii) control action. Give **two examples** under each sub-category. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **double-acting hydraulic cylinder** has a bore diameter $D = 0.15$ m, rod diameter $d = 0.04$ m, and operates at a gauge pressure of $P = 120 \times 10^5$ Pa (120 bar). Determine:
>   (i) the **extension force**, (ii) the **retraction force**, and (iii) the **fluid flow rate** required to achieve a piston speed of $v = 0.5$ m/s during extension. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**(i) Energy Source Classification (3 marks):**
- **Electrical Actuators** — DC motor, AC induction motor, servo motor, stepper motor, solenoid, piezoelectric. *Example:* Servo motor used in CNC machine-tool feed drives.
- **Hydraulic Actuators** — single-acting cylinder, double-acting cylinder, hydraulic motor, vane-type, gear-type. *Example:* Hydraulic cylinder in a forging press.
- **Pneumatic Actuators** — diaphragm, piston (rack-pinion), spring-return, rotary vane. *Example:* Diaphragm actuator in a process control valve.
- **Mechanical Actuators** — spring, cam, ratchet, bimetallic strip. *Example:* Bimetallic strip in a thermostat.

**(ii) Motion Type Classification (2 marks):**
- **Linear actuators** — produce straight-line displacement $s$ (mm). *Example:* Hydraulic cylinder, solenoid plunger.
- **Rotary actuators** — produce angular displacement $\theta$ (rad). *Example:* Servo motor, pneumatic vane actuator.

**(iii) Control Action Classification (2 marks):**
- **Two-state (On/Off)** — operate at either 0 % or 100 %. *Example:* Solenoid valve.
- **Modulating (Continuous)** — operate at any intermediate position. *Example:* Motorised valve with 4–20 mA positioner.

*Diagrams:* refer to the **Master Classification Tree (Section 4.1)** and the **Network Loop Block Diagram (Section 4.2)**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $D = 0.15$ m, $d = 0.04$ m, $P = 120 \times 10^5$ Pa, $v = 0.5$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Compute the piston face area (extension):**

$$
A_{extend} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.15)^2}{4} = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the annular area (retraction):**

$$
A_{retract} = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi ((0.15)^2 - (0.04)^2)}{4}
$$

$$
A_{retract} = \frac{\pi (0.0225 - 0.0016)}{4} = 1.640 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Extension force:**

$$
F_{extend} = P \cdot A_{extend} = (120 \times 10^5) \times (1.767 \times 10^{-2})
$$

$$
F_{extend} = 2.121 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{2121 \; kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Retraction force:**

$$
F_{retract} = P \cdot A_{retract} = (120 \times 10^5) \times (1.640 \times 10^{-2})
$$

$$
F_{retract} = 1.968 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{1968 \; kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Flow rate during extension:**

$$
Q = A_{extend} \cdot v = (1.767 \times 10^{-2}) \times 0.5 = 8.84 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
Q \approx \mathbf{8.84 \times 10^{-3} \; m^3/s \;\; (\approx \; 530 \; L/min)}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $F_{extend} \approx 2121$ kN, $F_{retract} \approx 1968$ kN, $Q \approx 8.84 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s.

---

### ✍️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Design-Oriented)

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`**

> **(a)** With a clear **block diagram**, describe the role of an **actuator** in an **industrial networked control system**. Explain how a **smart electrical actuator** differs from a conventional actuator in terms of **fieldbus connectivity, diagnostics, and predictive maintenance**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **3-phase servo motor** rated at $V = 400$ V, $I = 8$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.85$, drives a ball-screw linear actuator with lead $p = 5$ mm/rev and gear ratio $G = 5 : 1$ (speed-up, i.e., motor turns 5× for 1 screw turn). Motor speed $N_m = 3000$ RPM. Calculate the **(i) input electrical power**, **(ii) mechanical power output** (assume $\eta_m = 90$ %), **(iii) output shaft speed**, and **(iv) linear thrust speed** of the actuator. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**Role of Actuator in Industrial Networked System (3 marks):**
- The actuator is the **final control element** in the loop, receiving commands from the **PLC / DCS / SCADA** over industrial protocols (PROFIBUS, PROFINET, Modbus, EtherNet/IP, HART).
- The block flow is: **HMI → Controller → Network Gateway → Driver (Power Stage) → Actuator → Process → Sensor → Feedback to Controller.**
- The actuator driver translates the **low-energy control signal** into **high-energy physical action**.

**Smart vs. Conventional Actuator (4 marks):**

| Feature | Conventional Actuator | Smart Electrical Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Signal** | 4–20 mA analog (point-to-point) | **Digital fieldbus** (multi-drop, bidirectional) |
| **Feedback** | Position transmitter (separate) | **Integrated** position, torque, temperature sensors |
| **Diagnostics** | Manual inspection | **Self-diagnostics** — valve seat wear, friction, cycle count |
| **Maintenance** | Reactive / scheduled | **Predictive** — alerts based on real-time data trends |
| **Configuration** | Mechanical / DIP switch | **Software-configurable** via EDD / FDT / GSD files |
| **Asset Management** | None | **NAMUR NE107** status (failure, function check, out-of-spec, maintenance) |

*Diagram:* refer to the **Functional Block Diagram (Section 4.2)**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $V = 400$ V, $I = 8$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.85$, $p = 5$ mm/rev, $G = 5 : 1$ (speed-up), $N_m = 3000$ RPM, $\eta_m = 0.90$.

**Step 1 — Input Electrical Power:**

$$
P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi
$$

(3-phase formula — KTU examiners explicitly test this)

$$
P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 8 \times 0.85 = 4712.9 \text{ W} \approx \mathbf{4.71 \; kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical Power Output (motor shaft):**

$$
P_m = \eta_m \cdot P_{in} = 0.90 \times 4712.9 = 4241.6 \text{ W} \approx \mathbf{4.24 \; kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Output Shaft Speed (after speed-up gear):**

$$
N_{out} = G \cdot N_m = 5 \times 3000 = \mathbf{15{,}000 \; RPM}
$$

In rad/s:

$$
\omega_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 15000}{60} = 1570.8 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Linear Thrust Speed (ball-screw):**

$$
v_{linear} = N_{out} \times p = \left(\frac{15000}{60}\right) \text{ rev/s} \times 5 \text{ mm/rev}
$$

$$
v_{linear} = 250 \times 5 = \mathbf{1250 \; mm/s \; = \; 1.25 \; m/s}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $P_{in} \approx 4.71$ kW, $P_m \approx 4.24$ kW, $N_{out} = 15{,}000$ RPM, $v_{linear} = 1.25$ m/s.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Where KTU students LOSE MARKS in this topic — read carefully:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 3-phase factor $\sqrt{3}$ in motor power calculations.** Using $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ instead of $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Ignoring the rod-side area in retraction force.** Many students use $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ for **both** extension and retraction, which is **wrong**. Always use the **annular area** $A = \pi (D^2 - d^2) / 4$ for retraction. This costs **2 marks** if missed.
> 3. **Confusing "control action" with "motion type".** They are **different classification axes**. Control action = on/off vs. modulating; motion type = linear vs. rotary. Mixing them up loses **1–3 marks** depending on depth of the question.
> 4. **Not justifying actuator choice.** In a 14-mark design question, the answer must include **at least one quantitative reason** (force, speed, accuracy, cost) — not just "we choose pneumatic because it's cheaper". Examiners explicitly mark for **justification**.
> 5. **Unit errors.** Pa vs. bar, mm vs. m, RPM vs. rad/s. Always state units. KTU penalises missing units by **0.5–1 mark per step**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **📋 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Classification of Actuators (Module 1)**
>
> ✅ An **actuator** converts a control signal into physical motion/force — it is the **"muscle"** of any industrial system.
>
> ✅ **Four primary classes (by energy):** Electrical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Mechanical — memorize two examples under each.
>
> ✅ **By motion:** Linear (stroke in mm) vs. Rotary (angle in rad). Conversion via lead screw: $s = p\theta / 2\pi$.
>
> ✅ **By control action:** On/Off (binary) vs. Modulating (proportional, 0–100 %).
>
> ✅ **Hydraulic = high force, slow, oily, intrinsically unsafe.** **Pneumatic = clean, fast, intrinsically safe, moderate force.** **Electrical (servo) = precise, networked, clean, lower force.** **Mechanical = simple, low force, one-shot.**
>
> ✅ **Cylinder force formula:** $F = P \cdot A$. For extension use $A = \pi D^2 / 4$. For retraction use $A = \pi (D^2 - d^2) / 4$ (annular).
>
> ✅ **3-phase motor power:** $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$. Do not forget the $\sqrt{3}$ factor.
>
> ✅ **Flow rate in cylinder:** $Q = A \cdot v$. (Very common in 7-mark sub-parts.)
>
> ✅ **Smart actuators** = integrated sensor + driver + fieldbus — supports **PROFIBUS-PA, HART, Foundation Fieldbus** — enables **predictive maintenance** and **NAMUR NE107** diagnostics.
>
> ✅ **Selection rule of thumb:** Force \mathbf{>\; 10^5 \, N} → Hydraulic; mid-range + clean → Pneumatic; precision + networked → Electric Servo; µ-scale motion → Piezo.
>
> ✅ **Always** include **units, justification, and a diagram** in long-answer questions. These three items alone can add **3–4 marks** in a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Linear Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electric Linear Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electric Linear Actuator (ELA)** is a precision electromechanical positioning device that converts electrical energy into controlled linear (push–pull) displacement along a single axis. It accomplishes this by integrating an electric prime mover (DC, AC, stepper, servo, or BLDC motor) with a mechanical motion-conversion train (typically a lead screw, ball screw, trapezoidal screw, or rack-and-pinion assembly), and a feedback transducer (potentiometer, incremental/absolute encoder, or Hall-effect sensor). In the context of KTU's **Industrial Networks** syllabus, the ELA is classified as a **Field-Level Final Control Element (FCE)** — it physically executes the positioning command issued by a PLC, DCS, or remote I/O station over industrial buses such as **PROFIBUS-DP, PROFINET, Modbus RTU/TCP, EtherCAT, or DeviceNet**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (Module 1 – Industrial Actuators):** The actuator is the *physical interface* between the cyber control layer (network, PLC, SCADA) and the mechanical process variable (valve stem, damper blade, conveyor gate, solar tracker).

### 1.2 Subsystem Inventory (Every ELA has 5 mandatory subsystems)

| # | Subsystem | Function | Typical Component |
|---|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Drive Motor** | Converts electrical → rotary mechanical | DC brush, BLDC, Stepper, Servo |
| 2 | **Gear Train** | Speed reduction, torque multiplication | Spur, planetary, harmonic |
| 3 | **Drive Screw** | Converts rotation → translation | Lead screw, ball screw, ACME |
| 4 | **Position Feedback** | Reports actual stroke position | Potentiometer, encoder, Hall IC |
| 5 | **Drive Electronics** | Power switching & commutation | H-bridge, VFD, chopper driver |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition Builder)

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of an electric linear actuator as the *muscle* of an industrial robot. Just as your bicep receives an electrical signal from the brain, contracts, and pulls your forearm — the ELA receives a 4–20 mA or digital command from a PLC, energizes its motor, and pushes/pulls a rod. **The motor is the "muscle fiber," the lead screw is the "tendon," and the feedback sensor is the "stretch receptor" that tells the brain (controller) where the arm currently is.**

A real-world example: When a bottling plant's SCADA commands a filling-valve to open to 60% stroke, a PROFINET-connected ELA physically pushes the valve stem to that exact position, while a built-in potentiometer reports the actual stem position back to the controller for closed-loop trimming.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold the constants)

- **Stroke length** (mm): Maximum linear travel of the output rod.
- **Rated Force / Thrust** (N): Maximum axial load the actuator can push or pull.
- **Linear Speed** (mm/s): Speed of rod extension/retraction at rated load.
- **Duty Cycle** (%): $\text{DC\%} = (t_{\text{on}} / t_{\text{total}}) \times 100$.
- **Protection Class**: **IP65 / IP67 / IP69K** for industrial environments.
- **Operating Temperature**: typically **−20 °C to +65 °C**.
- **Input Signal Standards**: 4–20 mA, 0–10 V, PWM, or fieldbus (PROFIBUS, PROFINET).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Structured Logic Walkthrough

1. **Command Reception:** The host controller (PLC / DCS) issues a setpoint over the fieldbus. For analog systems, a 4–20 mA current loop encodes the target position.
2. **Drive Electronics Activation:** A power driver (H-bridge for DC, VFD for AC, chopper for stepper) energizes the motor windings with the correct polarity & phase sequence.
3. **Rotary Torque Generation:** The motor develops electromagnetic torque $T_m$ at rotational speed $n_m$.
4. **Gear Reduction:** A gear train of ratio $N$ (always $\ge 1$) multiplies torque and reduces speed:
   $$T_{\text{screw}} = T_m \cdot N \cdot \eta_g$$
   $$n_{\text{screw}} = n_m / N$$
5. **Rotary-to-Linear Conversion:** The lead screw (pitch $L$) drives a nut/push-rod. Each screw revolution advances the rod by exactly $L$ millimeters.
6. **Force Delivery:** The thrust force $F$ acts axially on the load.
7. **Feedback:** A position transducer (potentiometer/encoder) returns the actual rod position to the controller.
8. **Closed-Loop Trim:** The controller computes error = (setpoint − actual) and adjusts drive voltage/PWM duty cycle to minimize it.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot T_m \cdot N \cdot \eta_{\text{total}}}{L}$ | Output linear thrust force | N |
| 2 | $v = \dfrac{n_m \cdot L}{60 \cdot N}$ | Linear speed of push rod | mm/s |
| 3 | $P_{\text{out}} = F \cdot v$ | Mechanical output power | W |
| 4 | $P_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot T_m \cdot n_m}{60}$ | Electrical→mechanical input power | W |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \eta_g \cdot \eta_s$ | Combined gearbox & screw efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L}{\pi d_m}\right)$ | Lead angle of screw thread | rad or deg |
| 7 | $\phi = \arctan(\mu)$ | Friction angle of nut–screw pair | rad or deg |
| 8 | **Self-locking** iff $\alpha < \phi$ | Backdriving prevention condition | — |
| 9 | $\text{DC\%} = \dfrac{t_{\text{on}}}{t_{\text{on}}+t_{\text{off}}} \times 100$ | Duty cycle rating | % |
| 10 | $\theta_{\text{resolution}} = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{\text{steps/rev} \cdot N}$ | Stepper angular resolution at load | degrees |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Cheat Code:** Equations (1) and (2) are the *MUST-MEMORIZE* formulas for any numerical problem on ELAs. Equation (8) is the examiner's favourite for the "self-locking" concept question.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern Industry 4.0 plants, ELAs are networked via **IO-Link**, **PROFINET**, or **EtherCAT** to enable:
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Vibration & current sensors on the actuator feed ML algorithms to forecast failure.
- **Energy Optimization:** Variable-speed drives reduce consumption by 30–60% versus pneumatic/hydraulic equivalents.
- **Cleanroom Compatibility:** Electric actuators eliminate hydraulic oil contamination — vital for pharma & semiconductor fabs.
- **Remote Diagnostics:** Status bits (over-temp, over-current, end-of-travel) are broadcast to SCADA for condition monitoring.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Linear Thrust Force from Motor Torque

**Starting Premise:** Power balance between motor input shaft and screw output.

Step 1 — Motor input mechanical power (at motor shaft):
$$P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m = T_m \cdot \frac{2\pi n_m}{60}$$

Step 2 — Power delivered to the screw shaft (after gear train losses):
$$P_s = P_m \cdot \eta_g = T_m \cdot \frac{2\pi n_m}{60} \cdot \eta_g$$

Step 3 — The screw shaft torque is amplified by the gear ratio:
$$T_s = T_m \cdot N \cdot \eta_g$$

Step 4 — Power at the screw is also expressed as $P_s = T_s \cdot \omega_s$:
$$P_s = (T_m \cdot N \cdot \eta_g) \cdot \frac{2\pi n_m}{60 \cdot N}$$
$$P_s = \frac{2\pi T_m n_m \eta_g}{60} \quad \text{(N cancels — proves consistency)}$$

Step 5 — The screw nut converts rotary motion to linear thrust. With screw pitch $L$ (linear advance per revolution) and screw efficiency $\eta_s$:
$$F \cdot v = T_s \cdot \omega_s \cdot \eta_s$$

Step 6 — Substitute $v = (n_m / N) \cdot L$ and $T_s$ from Step 3:
$$F \cdot \frac{n_m L}{N} = (T_m N \eta_g) \cdot \frac{2\pi n_m}{60 N} \cdot \eta_s$$

Step 7 — Cancel $n_m$ and $N$ on both sides:
$$F \cdot L = \frac{2\pi T_m \eta_g \eta_s}{60} \cdot 60$$

Step 8 — Define $\eta_{\text{total}} = \eta_g \cdot \eta_s$ and isolate $F$:
$$\boxed{F = \frac{2\pi \cdot T_m \cdot N \cdot \eta_{\text{total}}}{L}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation II — Self-Locking Condition

Consider a screw with mean thread diameter $d_m$ and axial pitch $L$. When unloaded, the helix makes an angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{L}{\pi d_m}$$

The friction angle of the contacting surfaces is:
$$\tan \phi = \mu$$

For **backdriving prevention** (the load cannot spin the screw back), the helix angle must be *less* than the friction angle:
$$\alpha < \phi \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \tan \alpha < \tan \phi \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \boxed{\frac{L}{\pi d_m} < \mu}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Trapezoidal (ACME) screws with low pitch are *self-locking*. Ball screws with high efficiency ($\eta \approx 90\%$) are **NOT** self-locking — they require a brake. This is critical when the actuator holds a vertical load (e.g., a damper held open against gravity).

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Given:**
- Motor torque $T_m = 0.5~\text{N·m}$
- Motor speed $n_m = 3000~\text{rpm}$
- Gear ratio $N = 20$
- Lead screw pitch $L = 4~\text{mm/rev}$
- Total efficiency $\eta_{\text{total}} = 0.60$

**Find:** (i) Thrust force $F$, (ii) Linear speed $v$, (iii) Output power $P_{\text{out}}$.

**Solution (i) — Force:**
$$F = \frac{2\pi \cdot 0.5 \cdot 20 \cdot 0.60}{4 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$F = \frac{37.699}{0.004} = 9424.78~\text{N} \approx 9.42~\text{kN}$$

**Solution (ii) — Linear Speed:**
$$v = \frac{3000 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-3}}{60 \cdot 20} = \frac{12}{1200} = 0.01~\text{m/s} = 10~\text{mm/s}$$

**Solution (iii) — Output Power:**
$$P_{\text{out}} = F \cdot v = 9424.78 \cdot 0.01 = 94.25~\text{W}$$

**Verification via input power:**
$$P_{\text{in}} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 0.5 \cdot 3000}{60} = 157.08~\text{W}$$
$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{94.25}{157.08} = 0.60 \checkmark$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Selection Guide

| Parameter | DC Brush Motor | BLDC | Stepper | AC Induction | Servo (PMSM) |
|-----------|---------------|------|---------|--------------|--------------|
| **Position Holding** | With brake | Sensorless ok | Open-loop ok | Brake needed | Encoder required |
| **Efficiency** | 60–70% | 85–95% | 50–70% | 75–90% | 90–95% |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Low | High | High |
| **Network Suitability** | PROFINET via I/O | PROFINET, EtherCAT | Modbus, IO-Link | PROFINET, PROFIBUS | EtherCAT, SERCOS |
| **KTU Best Application** | Small dampers | Solar trackers | Valves, gates | Heavy industry valves | High-precision CNC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Industrial Electric Linear Actuator (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PLC / DCS Controller] -->|Fieldbus PROFINET Modbus| B[Drive Electronics H-bridge VFD Chopper]
    B -->|PWM Voltage Phase sequence| C[Electric Motor DC BLDC Stepper Servo]
    C -->|Rotary Torque Tm| D[Gear Train Ratio N]
    D -->|Screw Torque Ts| E[Lead Screw Pitch L]
    E -->|Linear Thrust F| F[Push Rod Output Stroke]
    F -->|Mechanical load| G[Valve Damper Gate Conveyor]
    F -->|Coupled shaft| H[Position Sensor Potentiometer Encoder Hall]
    H -->|4-20mA SSI IO-Link| A
    I[24V DC 230V AC 400V 3phase Power Supply] --> B
    I --> A
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Conceptual Schematic (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph input["Input Subsystem"]
        S1[Power Supply 24V DC / 230V AC]
        S2[Fieldbus Receiver PROFINET Modbus]
    end
    subgraph control["Control Subsystem"]
        S3[Microcontroller / DSP]
        S4[Current Sensing Shunt]
    end
    subgraph power["Power Subsystem"]
        S5[H-bridge Driver]
        S6[MOSFET / IGBT Bank]
    end
    subgraph mech["Mechanical Subsystem"]
        S7[BLDC / Stepper Motor]
        S8[Planetary Gearbox N:1]
        S9[Lead Screw ACME Ball]
        S10[Push Rod Linear Output]
    end
    subgraph sense["Feedback Subsystem"]
        S11[Absolute Encoder]
        S12[Temperature Sensor NTC]
        S13[Limit Switches End of Travel]
    end
    S1 --> S5
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11
    S11 --> S3
    S7 --> S12
    S12 --> S3
    S10 --> S13
    S13 --> S3
    S4 --> S3
```

### 4.3 Signal-Flow & Control Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[Reference Setpoint from SCADA] -->|Subtract| SUM[Error Summation Node]
    FB[Position Feedback Encoder] -->|Subtract| SUM
    SUM --> CTRL[PID Controller in PLC]
    CTRL --> DRV[Motor Drive PWM Signal]
    DRV --> MOT[Electric Motor]
    MOT --> ACT[Linear Actuator Mechanism]
    ACT --> LOAD[Mechanical Load Valve / Damper]
    LOAD --> FB
```

### 4.4 Network Integration Topology (Multi-Actuator Fieldbus Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SCADA[SCADA HMI Server] -->|Ethernet TCP IP| PLC[Master PLC S7-1500]
    PLC -->|PROFINET Real Time Class 1| SW1[PROFINET Managed Switch]
    SW1 --> A1[ELA 1 Valve Actuator]
    SW1 --> A2[ELA 2 Damper Actuator]
    SW1 --> A3[ELA 3 Gate Actuator]
    SW1 --> A4[ELA 4 Conveyor Positioner]
    A1 --> DIAG[Diagnostic Cloud OPC UA MQTT]
    A2 --> DIAG
    A3 --> DIAG
    A4 --> DIAG
    DIAG --> MAINT[Predictive Maintenance AI Module]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define an Electric Linear Actuator. List its main components with one-line functions.

**Model Answer:**
An Electric Linear Actuator is an electromechanical device that converts electrical energy into controlled linear motion, used as a final control element in industrial automation networks.
*Main components:*
1. **Electric Motor** – Generates rotary torque from electrical input.
2. **Gear Train** – Reduces speed and multiplies torque.
3. **Lead/Ball Screw** – Converts rotary motion into linear thrust.
4. **Position Sensor** – Provides feedback of actual rod position.
5. **Drive Electronics** – Controls motor commutation and power switching.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Explain the term *self-locking* with reference to a lead-screw-driven linear actuator. State the condition for self-locking.

**Model Answer:**
*Self-locking* is the property of a lead-screw mechanism where the screw cannot be backdriven by the axial load — i.e., the load cannot spin the screw and cause uncontrolled rod motion. This is critical for vertical applications (e.g., dampers held against gravity).
**Condition:** Self-locking occurs when the helix (lead) angle $\alpha$ is less than the friction angle $\phi$ of the nut-screw interface:
$$\alpha < \phi \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{L}{\pi d_m} < \mu$$
ACME and trapezoidal screws with low pitch are typically self-locking; ball screws are generally *not* self-locking.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks in the long-answer questions by: **(a)** forgetting the *gear-ratio factor* $N$ in the force equation, **(b)** mixing up *mm* with *m* in numerical problems, **(c)** not drawing the closed-loop block diagram with feedback arrow, and **(d)** omitting the efficiency term in derivations. Always write the **FULL equation with all symbols defined**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an industrial electric linear actuator integrated with a PROFINET network. Describe the role of each subsystem in achieving precise position control.

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:** An industrial ELA receives a positioning command from a PLC over the PROFINET fieldbus. The drive electronics decode the command and energize the motor. The motor's rotary torque is multiplied by a gear train and converted to linear thrust by a lead screw, pushing the rod to the commanded position. An encoder or potentiometer provides real-time position feedback to the controller, forming a closed PID loop.

**Block Diagram Explanation:**

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **PLC Controller** | Issues PROFINET telegrams (setpoint). |
| **Drive Electronics** | H-bridge chops DC/AC into PWM pulses for the motor. |
| **Electric Motor** | DC/BLDC/Stepper — develops torque $T_m$. |
| **Gearbox** | Multiplies torque by factor $N$ and reduces speed. |
| **Lead Screw** | Converts rotation to linear motion at pitch $L$. |
| **Push Rod** | Delivers thrust force $F$ to the mechanical load. |
| **Position Sensor** | Reports actual stroke to controller for closed-loop. |
| **Power Supply** | 24 V DC / 230 V AC feeds driver and logic. |

The PROFINET integration enables real-time cyclic data exchange (typically 1 ms update) and acyclic diagnostics for predictive maintenance.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 48 V DC linear actuator has the following specifications:
Lead screw pitch $L = 5~\text{mm}$, Gear ratio $N = 30$, Motor torque $T_m = 0.8~\text{N·m}$, Motor speed $n_m = 2400~\text{rpm}$, Total efficiency $\eta = 0.65$, Mean screw diameter $d_m = 20~\text{mm}$, Friction coefficient $\mu = 0.12$.

Calculate: **(i)** Thrust force, **(ii)** Linear velocity, **(iii)** Output power, **(iv)** Verify whether the screw is self-locking.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Thrust Force:**
$$F = \frac{2\pi \cdot T_m \cdot N \cdot \eta}{L} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 0.8 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.65}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$F = \frac{97.99}{0.005} = 19597.5~\text{N} \approx 19.6~\text{kN}$$

**(ii) Linear Velocity:**
$$v = \frac{n_m \cdot L}{60 \cdot N} = \frac{2400 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3}}{60 \cdot 30} = \frac{12}{1800} = 6.67 \times 10^{-3}~\text{m/s} = 6.67~\text{mm/s}$$

**(iii) Output Power:**
$$P_{\text{out}} = F \cdot v = 19597.5 \cdot 6.67 \times 10^{-3} = 130.66~\text{W}$$

**(iv) Self-Locking Check:**
$$\tan \alpha = \frac{L}{\pi d_m} = \frac{5}{\pi \cdot 20} = 0.0796$$
$$\alpha = 4.55^{\circ}$$
$$\tan \phi = \mu = 0.12 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = 6.84^{\circ}$$
Since $\alpha = 4.55^{\circ} < \phi = 6.84^{\circ}$, **the screw is SELF-LOCKING**. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the different types of electric motors used as prime movers in industrial linear actuators. Discuss at least four motor types in terms of efficiency, control complexity, position-holding capability, and typical industrial application.

**Model Solution:**

| Motor Type | Efficiency | Control Complexity | Position Holding | Typical Application |
|------------|------------|-------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **DC Brushed** | 60–70% | Simple (H-bridge + PWM) | Requires brake | Small dampers, vents |
| **BLDC** | 85–95% | Moderate (electronic commutation) | Sensorless / encoder | Solar trackers, valves |
| **Stepper (PM)** | 50–70% | Low (open-loop chopper) | Open-loop holding torque | Conveyor gates, low-cost valves |
| **AC Induction** | 75–90% | High (VFD required) | Brake mandatory | Heavy industrial valves |
| **Servo (PMSM)** | 90–95% | High (PID + encoder) | Encoder-locked | CNC, precision robotics |

**Discussion:**
- **DC Brushed Motors** are the simplest and cheapest; suited to low-power, low-precision damper control. They suffer brush wear, limiting life to ~2000 hours.
- **BLDC Motors** offer the best efficiency–cost trade-off. Electronic commutation eliminates brushes; sensorless variants use back-EMF for position. Preferred in PROFINET-connected solar trackers and HVAC dampers.
- **Stepper Motors** provide *open-loop* position holding via detent torque — ideal where feedback wiring is impractical. Drawback: resonance at low speeds and torque drop at high speeds.
- **AC Induction Motors** handle the highest power levels (multi-kW gate valves in power plants) but require VFDs and a holding brake, increasing system complexity.
- **Servo (PMSM) Motors** are the premium choice for high-precision applications, offering sub-millimeter accuracy and dynamic response below 10 ms.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the linear thrust force developed by an electric linear actuator in terms of motor torque, gear ratio, screw pitch, and overall efficiency. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Gear train and screw-nut pair are the only sources of mechanical loss.
2. Motor delivers constant torque $T_m$ at constant speed $n_m$.
3. Lead screw pitch $L$ is uniform along the thread length.
4. No external load friction (only screw friction is considered via $\eta$).

**Derivation:**

Power at the motor shaft:
$$P_m = \frac{2\pi T_m n_m}{60}$$

Power reaching the screw (after gearbox with efficiency $\eta_g$):
$$P_s = P_m \cdot \eta_g = \frac{2\pi T_m n_m \eta_g}{60}$$

Screw shaft torque is amplified by gear ratio $N$:
$$T_s = T_m \cdot N \cdot \eta_g$$

Linear speed of the push rod (one revolution advances the rod by $L$):
$$v = \frac{n_s \cdot L}{60} = \frac{n_m \cdot L}{60 N}$$

Output mechanical power = $F \cdot v = T_s \cdot \omega_s \cdot \eta_s$ (where $\eta_s$ is screw efficiency):
$$F \cdot \frac{n_m L}{60 N} = T_m \cdot N \cdot \eta_g \cdot \frac{2\pi n_m}{60 N} \cdot \eta_s$$

Cancelling $n_m$ and $N$ and rearranging:
$$\boxed{F = \frac{2\pi T_m N \eta_g \eta_s}{L} = \frac{2\pi T_m N \eta_{\text{total}}}{L}}$$

This shows that thrust force is **directly proportional** to motor torque, gear ratio, and total efficiency, and **inversely proportional** to screw pitch.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision – Bullet-Point Summary:**

- Electric Linear Actuator (ELA) = **Motor + Gearbox + Lead Screw + Position Feedback + Driver**.
- Primary role in industrial networks: **Final Control Element (FCE)** executing PLC commands over PROFINET, PROFIBUS, Modbus, EtherCAT, or IO-Link.
- **Force Formula (Must Memorize):** $F = (2\pi T_m N \eta_{\text{total}}) / L$.
- **Velocity Formula (Must Memorize):** $v = (n_m L) / (60 N)$.
- **Power Formula:** $P_{\text{out}} = F \cdot v$ and $P_{\text{in}} = (2\pi T_m n_m)/60$.
- **Self-Locking Condition:** $L / (\pi d_m) < \mu$ — satisfied by low-pitch ACME screws; **NOT** satisfied by high-efficiency ball screws.
- **Higher gear ratio $N$** → More force, but slower linear speed (force–speed trade-off).
- **Higher screw pitch $L$** → Faster speed, but lower thrust force.
- **DC motors** = simple & cheap; **BLDC** = best efficiency; **Stepper** = open-loop holding; **Servo PMSM** = highest precision.
- **Feedback Sensors:** Potentiometer (analog), Incremental/Absolute Encoder (digital SSI/IO-Link), Hall-effect (commutation).
- **Standard industrial ratings:** IP65/IP67 protection, −20 °C to +65 °C, duty cycle 25% / 50% / 75% / 100%.
- **Common Examiner Traps:** Unit mismatch (mm vs m), missing gear-ratio factor, omitting efficiency, forgetting backdriving in vertical loads.
- **Industry 4.0 Trend:** ELAs increasingly ship with IO-Link, OPC UA, and MQTT interfaces for cloud-based predictive maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Rotary Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electric Rotary Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** An **Electric Rotary Actuator** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that receives a controlled electrical input signal (voltage or current) from a controller or network node and converts it into a precisely regulated **angular displacement (rotation)** of its output shaft, while simultaneously delivering a controlled **torque** to drive an industrial load such as a control valve, damper, or robotic joint.

In the context of **Industrial Networks (PEICT747)**, the electric rotary actuator is treated as the **final control element** that bridges the cyber layer (PLC/DCS/SCADA commands transmitted over fieldbus networks such as **PROFIBUS, FOUNDATION Fieldbus, HART, or Industrial Ethernet**) with the physical process layer (valve position, flow rate, pressure).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** Electric rotary actuators are categorized by their operating principle into four major families:
> 1. **DC Servo Motors** (brushed and brushless)
> 2. **AC Servo / Induction Motors**
> 3. **Stepper Motors** (open-loop positioning)
> 4. **Direct-Drive Torque Motors**

The output of an electric rotary actuator is characterized by three primary variables:

$$
\theta \;\; (\text{angular position, in radians}), \quad \omega \;\; (\text{angular velocity, in rad/s}), \quad T \;\; (\text{torque, in N·m})
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Steering Wheel"

Imagine you are driving a car. The **steering wheel** is connected via a shaft to the front wheels. When you rotate the steering wheel by a small amount, the wheels turn by a proportional (and amplified) angle, and the car changes direction.

An **electric rotary actuator** operates on the exact same principle, except:
- **Your hands on the steering wheel** = the **electrical input signal** (e.g., 4–20 mA current loop, or a digital setpoint from PROFIBUS).
- **The steering column and power steering pump** = the **motor and gearbox** that amplify effort.
- **The front wheels** = the **final load** (e.g., a butterfly valve disc that must rotate 90°).

Just as power steering makes your effort **stronger and more precise**, the electric actuator's internal servo loop makes its rotation **stronger, faster, and far more precise** than manually turning the load.

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Network Perspective:** In a typical DCS loop, the controller sends a **setpoint (SP)** of, say, 12 mA (representing 50% valve opening). The actuator's internal positioner compares this to the **actual position (PV)** measured by a feedback potentiometer or encoder, and drives the motor until the error is zero. This is a classic **closed-loop position control** system embedded inside the actuator.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard SI units and physical constants are fundamental to characterizing any electric rotary actuator:

- **Torque constant:** $K_t$ in **N·m/A**
- **Back-EMF constant:** $K_e$ in **V·s/rad** (numerically equal to $K_t$ in SI units)
- **Moment of inertia of rotor:** $J$ in **kg·m²**
- **Viscous damping coefficient:** $B$ in **N·m·s/rad**
- **Electrical armature resistance:** $R_a$ in **ohms (Ω)**
- **Armature inductance:** $L_a$ in **henries (H)**
- **Supply voltage:** $V$ in **volts (V)**
- **Speed:** $N$ in **rpm** or $\omega$ in **rad/s**, where $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Five Logical Stages

The behaviour of every electric rotary actuator can be decomposed into five logical stages. Understanding these stages is critical for both KTU theory questions and industrial network troubleshooting.

### Stage 1 — Command Reception
The actuator receives a control command from the network layer. The signal type may be:
- **Analog:** 4–20 mA current loop or 0–10 V voltage.
- **Digital:** PROFIBUS-DP, Modbus RTU, FOUNDATION Fieldbus, HART, or EtherNet/IP.

### Stage 2 — Signal Conditioning
The raw command is filtered, scaled, and converted into an internal reference (e.g., a PWM duty cycle).

### Stage 3 — Power Conversion
A power electronics stage (e.g., an H-bridge with IGBTs/MOSFETs, or a VFD for AC motors) modulates the supply power delivered to the motor windings.

### Stage 4 — Electromechanical Conversion
Current flowing through stator/rotor windings interacting with magnetic fields produces torque $T$. For a DC motor:

$$
T = K_t \cdot I_a
$$

### Stage 5 — Mechanical Output with Feedback
The torque accelerates the rotor inertia against load friction and external load, producing rotation. An **encoder or resolver** measures the actual shaft position and closes the loop back to Stage 2.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Stage

| Stage | Why It Exists | How It Is Implemented |
|---|---|---|
| Command Reception | Network protocols use standardized signals to ensure interoperability across vendors. | Communication transceivers, optocouplers, protocol stacks. |
| Signal Conditioning | Raw signals contain noise and may need level shifting. | RC filters, op-amp scaling, ADC. |
| Power Conversion | Motors need higher current/voltage than control electronics can supply directly. | H-bridge, chopper, VFD inverter (PWM at 4–20 kHz typical). |
| Electromechanical Conversion | Lorentz force on current-carrying conductors in magnetic field produces torque. | Permanent magnets + wound armature, or induction cage. |
| Feedback | Open-loop control cannot reject disturbances (load changes, friction). | Incremental encoder (e.g., 2048 PPR), absolute encoder, resolver, Hall sensors. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The table below consolidates every formula you must memorize for Module 1 electric rotary actuators. Internalize units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary / Limiting Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Motor Torque (DC) | $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ | $K_t$: N·m/A, $I_a$: A | Torque is linear in current; saturated at max current $I_{max}$. |
| 2 | Back-EMF | $e_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | $K_e$: V·s/rad, $\omega$: rad/s | $K_e = K_t$ in SI units. |
| 3 | Armature KVL | $V = I_a R_a + L_a \dfrac{dI_a}{dt} + e_b$ | $V$: V, $R_a$: Ω, $L_a$: H | At steady state, $\dfrac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$. |
| 4 | Mechanical Dynamics | $T - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B \omega$ | $J$: kg·m², $B$: N·m·s/rad | $T_L$: load torque. |
| 5 | Steady-State Speed | $\omega = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e}$ | All in SI | At no-load, $I_a \approx 0$, so $\omega_0 = V / K_e$. |
| 6 | Mechanical Power | $P_m = T \cdot \omega$ | $P_m$: W | This is the output power at the shaft. |
| 7 | Electrical Power | $P_e = V \cdot I_a$ | $P_e$: W | Efficiency $\eta = P_m / P_e$. |
| 8 | Stepper Step Angle | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N \cdot m}$ | $N$: number of phases, $m$: teeth | For a 1.8° motor, $\theta_s = 1.8°$ (200 steps/rev). |
| 9 | Stepper Holding Torque | $T_h = K_h \cdot I$ | $K_h$: N·m/A | Torque drops sharply as speed rises above pull-out. |
| 10 | Servo Transfer Function (Position) | $\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{V(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{s \cdot \left[(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_e\right]}$ | Laplace domain | Two real poles + one integrator; order-3 system. |

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

Electric rotary actuators dominate modern process industries because of:
- **Precision:** Sub-arc-minute positioning accuracy (essential in semiconductor and pharmaceutical industries).
- **Networkability:** Direct integration with fieldbus networks reduces cabling cost by up to **40%** compared to traditional 4–20 mA point-to-point wiring.
- **Diagnostics:** Smart actuators provide predictive maintenance data (vibration, temperature, torque trending) over networks.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Variable-speed drives on AC actuators can save **20–50%** energy in pump and fan applications.
- **Safety:** SIL-rated actuators with built-in partial stroke testing for emergency shutdown (ESD) valves.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Industrial Network Context:** When integrated over **PROFIBUS-PA** or **FOUNDATION Fieldbus**, the actuator becomes a true "field device" with its own device description (DD) or GSD file, enabling remote configuration, trending, and alarm management from the central DCS.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Speed Equation of a DC Rotary Actuator

We begin from the armature circuit and mechanical dynamics equations simultaneously.

### Step 1 — Write the Armature Voltage Equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law)

Applying KVL around the armature loop:

$$
V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + e_b
$$

### Step 2 — Express the Back-EMF

The back-EMF is generated because the rotating armature cuts the stator magnetic flux. It is proportional to angular velocity:

$$
e_b = K_e \cdot \omega
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Steady-State Condition

At steady state, the current is no longer changing, so $\dfrac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$. Substituting:

$$
V_a = I_a R_a + K_e \omega
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Armature Current

$$
I_a = \frac{V_a - K_e \omega}{R_a}
$$

### Step 5 — Write the Mechanical Equation at Steady State

At steady state, $\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = 0$, so:

$$
T_m = T_L + B \omega
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute the Motor Torque Equation

Since $T_m = K_t I_a$:

$$
K_t I_a = T_L + B \omega
$$

### Step 7 — Substitute $I_a$ from Step 4

$$
K_t \cdot \frac{V_a - K_e \omega}{R_a} = T_L + B \omega
$$

### Step 8 — Rearrange to Isolate $\omega$

$$
K_t V_a - K_t K_e \omega = T_L R_a + B \omega R_a
$$

$$
K_t V_a - T_L R_a = K_t K_e \omega + B \omega R_a
$$

$$
\omega \left( K_t K_e + B R_a \right) = K_t V_a - T_L R_a
$$

### Step 9 — Final Steady-State Speed Equation

$$
\boxed{\;\omega = \frac{K_t V_a - T_L R_a}{K_t K_e + B R_a}\;}
$$

### Step 10 — No-Load Speed Special Case

When $T_L = 0$:

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{K_t V_a}{K_t K_e + B R_a} \approx \frac{V_a}{K_e} \;\; (\text{if } B R_a \ll K_t K_e)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The no-load speed depends only on supply voltage and back-EMF constant. The slope of the speed-torque curve is the **mechanical regulation**, which is negative — as load increases, speed drops linearly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Transfer Function of a DC Servo Actuator (Position Control)

This derivation is critical for KTU problems involving **servo amplifiers and closed-loop positioners** integrated with industrial networks.

### Step 1 — Take the Laplace Transform of the Armature Equation

$$
V(s) = (L_a s + R_a) I_a(s) + K_e \Omega(s)
$$

### Step 2 — Take the Laplace Transform of the Mechanical Equation

$$
T(s) = (J s + B) \Omega(s) + T_L(s)
$$

### Step 3 — Express Torque as a Function of Current

$$
T(s) = K_t I_a(s)
$$

### Step 4 — Eliminate $I_a(s)$ Using Step 3 in Step 1

$$
V(s) = \frac{(L_a s + R_a)}{K_t} T(s) + K_e \Omega(s)
$$

### Step 5 — Eliminate $T(s)$ Using Step 2

$$
V(s) = \frac{(L_a s + R_a)}{K_t} \left[ (J s + B) \Omega(s) + T_L(s) \right] + K_e \Omega(s)
$$

### Step 6 — Group the $\Omega(s)$ Terms

$$
V(s) = \frac{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B)}{K_t} \Omega(s) + K_e \Omega(s) + \frac{(L_a s + R_a)}{K_t} T_L(s)
$$

$$
V(s) - \frac{(L_a s + R_a)}{K_t} T_L(s) = \Omega(s) \left[ \frac{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B)}{K_t} + K_e \right]
$$

### Step 7 — Simplify the Bracket

$$
\Omega(s) \left[ \frac{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_e K_t}{K_t} \right] = V(s) - \frac{(L_a s + R_a)}{K_t} T_L(s)
$$

### Step 8 — Solve for $\Omega(s)/V(s)$ with $T_L = 0$

$$
\frac{\Omega(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_e K_t}
$$

### Step 9 — Integrate to Get Position

Since $\Omega(s) = s \cdot \Theta(s)$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\Theta(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_e K_t\right]}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance for KTU:** This third-order transfer function has one integrator (the "$s$" in the denominator) and two electrical-mechanical poles. It is a **Type 1 system**, meaning it can track a step input with zero steady-state position error — the very property that makes servo actuators ideal for networked position control.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for KTU Design Problems

| Application Requirement | Recommended Actuator Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Precise angular positioning (e.g., valve 0–90°) | DC or AC Servo Motor with encoder | Closed-loop, high stiffness, sub-degree accuracy. |
| Open-loop positioning (no feedback possible) | Stepper Motor | Holds position without feedback, but loses steps under overload. |
| High torque at low speed (e.g., large damper) | Direct-Drive Torque Motor or Geared Servo | Eliminates gearbox backlash; high torque density. |
| Hazardous area (oil & gas) | Pneumatic or Hydraulic (comparison point) | Electric may require Ex-proof enclosures. |
| High-speed rotation (e.g., centrifuge) | AC Induction Motor with VFD | Robust, low maintenance, high power density. |
| Networked valve actuation | Smart Electric Actuator with HART/FF | Remote calibration, diagnostics, partial stroke test. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Networked Electric Rotary Actuator (Full Closed-Loop Architecture)

The Mermaid diagram below depicts the complete signal and power flow of an electric rotary actuator integrated into an industrial network. It captures the interaction between the DCS controller, the fieldbus, the actuator internals, and the mechanical load.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCS["DCS / SCADA Controller"]
    NET["Industrial Fieldbus PROFIBUS HART FF"]
    POSC["Positioner Electronics"]
    DRV["Power Driver H-Bridge VFD"]
    MTR["Electric Motor DC Servo AC Induction Stepper"]
    GEAR["Gearbox Optional"]
    LOAD["Mechanical Load Valve Damper Robotic Joint"]
    FBK["Feedback Sensor Encoder Resolver Potentiometer"]
    PRO["Protection Overcurrent Overtemperature"]

    DCS -- Setpoint SP 4-20mA or Digital --> NET
    NET -- Fieldbus Signal --> POSC
    POSC -- Reference PWM or Analog --> DRV
    DRV -- Power V I --> MTR
    MTR -- Torque T --> GEAR
    GEAR -- Reduced Speed Higher Torque --> LOAD
    LOAD -- Angular Position Theta --> FBK
    FBK -- Feedback Signal PV --> POSC
    POSC -- Fault Trip --> PRO
    DRV -- Status Word --> NET
    NET -- HMI Diagnostics --> DCS
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrow from **POSC** to **DRV** carries the command (forward path), while the arrow from **FBK** back to **POSC** is the feedback path. Together they form the inner **servo loop**. The outer loop runs from **DCS** through **NET** to **POSC**, representing the **process control loop**.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Functional Architecture of a Smart Electric Actuator

This second diagram zooms into the actuator itself, showing its internal modules.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN["Input Module Analog 4-20mA Digital Fieldbus"]
    MCU["Microcontroller DSP"]
    PWR["Power Supply SMPS Isolated"]
    INVR["Inverter / Chopper IGBT MOSFET"]
    SENS["Current Sensor Shunt Hall Effect"]
    ENC["Shaft Encoder"]
    MOT["Brushless DC Motor Core"]
    CLUTCH["Electromagnetic Clutch Optional"]
    BRK["Fail-Safe Brake Spring Applied"]
    OUT["Output Shaft"]

    IN --> MCU
    PWR --> INVR
    MCU --> INVR
    INVR --> MOT
    MOT --> CLUTCH
    CLUTCH --> BRK
    BRK --> OUT
    OUT --> ENC
    ENC --> MCU
    SENS --> INVR
    INVR -. Fault .-> MCU
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Network Significance:** The **MCU/DSP** node is where the actuator's profile (e.g., characterization curve, travel limits, ESD partial stroke test interval) is stored. When the fieldbus signal carries a new setpoint, the MCU executes a PID algorithm locally and modulates the inverter — this is the principle of **field-mounted intelligent devices** in modern FOUNDATION Fieldbus architecture.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node identifiers above use alphanumeric prefixed names (`DCS`, `NET`, `POSC`, `DRV`, `MTR`, etc.). No reserved keywords are used as node names. All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text, free of markdown formatting tags, ensuring clean rendering.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Q:** Define an electric rotary actuator. List any four types commonly used in industrial automation.

**Model Answer:**
An electric rotary actuator is an electromechanical device that converts an electrical input signal into a precisely controlled rotational motion of its output shaft, delivering torque to drive an industrial load such as a control valve or damper.

Four common types are:
1. DC Servo Motor (brushed or brushless)
2. AC Servo / Induction Motor
3. Stepper Motor
4. Direct-Drive Torque Motor

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing the four types)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Q:** State the function of an encoder in a rotary actuator system.

**Model Answer:**
An encoder is a feedback sensor mounted on the actuator output shaft that converts the mechanical angular position (or velocity) into an electrical signal — typically a series of digital pulses (incremental encoder) or a unique binary code per position (absolute encoder). This signal is fed back to the positioner/controller, closing the servo loop. The encoder enables accurate position tracking, rejects load disturbances, and provides diagnostic data (e.g., stall detection) to the network.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for transducer function, 1 mark for closed-loop feedback role, 1 mark for industrial benefits)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by:
> - Forgetting to mention **units** alongside numerical values.
> - Skipping the derivation of intermediate formulas (e.g., jumping directly to the speed equation).
> - Not drawing the **block diagram** when the question says "with a neat sketch."
> - Confusing **back-EMF constant $K_e$** with **torque constant $K_t$** (numerically equal in SI, but conceptually distinct).
> - Omitting the **feedback path** in block diagrams — a frequent 2-mark deduction.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a networked electric rotary actuator integrated over an industrial fieldbus. Describe the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The architecture consists of **six major blocks** (as drawn in Section 4.1 above):

1. **Network / Command Input Block:** Receives the setpoint from the DCS over PROFIBUS/HART/FOUNDATION Fieldbus. Handles protocol stack, address resolution, and cyclic data exchange. *(1 mark)*

2. **Positioner Electronics Block:** A microcontroller or DSP that runs a local PID algorithm. It compares the setpoint (SP) with the actual position (PV) from the encoder and generates a control signal (typically PWM). *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Power Driver Block:** An H-bridge (DC) or VFD inverter (AC) that amplifies the low-power control signal into a high-current variable-voltage/variable-frequency power signal applied to the motor. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Motor and Gearbox Block:** The motor converts electrical power into torque. The gearbox (if present) trades speed for torque to match the load requirements. *(1 mark)*

5. **Feedback Sensor Block:** Encoder/resolver/potentiometer providing the actual shaft position to close the inner loop. *(1 mark)*

6. **Protection and Diagnostics Block:** Monitors overcurrent, overtemperature, and provides status/alarm data back to the network. *(1 mark)*

**(b)** Derive the steady-state speed-torque relation of a separately excited DC rotary actuator. A 24 V DC servo motor has armature resistance $R_a = 1.2$ Ω, torque constant $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A, and back-EMF constant $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad. If the load torque is 0.4 N·m and viscous damping $B = 0.001$ N·m·s/rad, calculate the steady-state speed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation** (recap from Section 3.1):

At steady state, the equations reduce to:

$$
V_a = I_a R_a + K_e \omega
$$

$$
K_t I_a = T_L + B \omega
$$

Eliminating $I_a$:

$$
\omega = \frac{K_t V_a - T_L R_a}{K_t K_e + B R_a}
$$

*(3 marks for the derivation)*

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $V_a = 24$ V
- $R_a = 1.2$ Ω
- $K_t = K_e = 0.05$
- $T_L = 0.4$ N·m
- $B = 0.001$ N·m·s/rad

Substitute:

$$
\omega = \frac{(0.05)(24) - (0.4)(1.2)}{(0.05)(0.05) + (0.001)(1.2)}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{1.20 - 0.48}{0.0025 + 0.0012}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{0.72}{0.0037}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\omega \approx 194.6 \;\; \text{rad/s}\;}
$$

Convert to rpm:

$$
N = \omega \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = 194.6 \cdot 9.549 \approx 1858 \;\; \text{rpm}
$$

*(3 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final numerical answer with units)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**(a)** Compare DC servo motors, AC servo motors, and stepper motors as electric rotary actuators. Your comparison must cover: principle of operation, feedback requirement, torque-speed behaviour, and typical industrial application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | DC Servo Motor | AC Servo Motor | Stepper Motor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Principle | Armature current in magnetic field produces torque $T = K_t I_a$ | Rotating magnetic field from 3-phase supply induces rotor currents | Discrete step rotation from sequential phase excitation |
| Feedback | Always requires encoder/ resolver for closed-loop | Always requires encoder/ resolver | Open-loop (no feedback needed) |
| Torque-Speed | Constant torque region at low speed, constant power at high speed | Similar, but with field-weakening range | High holding torque at standstill, drops sharply with speed |
| Efficiency | High (85–95%) | High (90–96%) | Lower (50–70%) due to continuous excitation |
| Cost | Moderate | Higher (due to VFD) | Lowest |
| Application | Precision valve, robotic joint | CNC spindle, large damper | 3D printer, low-cost indexing table |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per row plus 1 mark for overall conclusion)*

**(b)** Derive the open-loop transfer function $\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{V(s)}$ of a DC servo position control system. Explain why the system is classified as Type 1 and state its significance for tracking a step input. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Refer to derivation in Section 3.2.

Final result:

$$
\frac{\Theta(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_e K_t\right]}
$$

*(4 marks for derivation)*

**Type 1 classification and significance:**

The system has **one pole at the origin** (the factor $s$ in the denominator). A Type 1 system has **zero steady-state error for a step input** and **finite steady-state error for a ramp input**.

Significance:
- When the network sends a step setpoint (e.g., "move valve to 50%"), the actuator will **eventually settle at the new position with no residual error**. *(1.5 marks)*
- This is critical for process control loops where bias in the actuator would cause permanent offset in the controlled variable. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Bulletins — Electric Rotary Actuators:**

- An electric rotary actuator converts an electrical signal into **angular motion** with controlled **torque**, and is the **final control element** in networked process loops.

- The **fundamental torque equation** is $T = K_t I_a$ for DC motors and the **back-EMF equation** is $e_b = K_e \omega$. In SI units, $K_t = K_e$.

- **No-load speed** is $\omega_0 \approx V / K_e$. This is the upper bound of operating speed.

- The **steady-state speed-torque relation** is **linear with negative slope** — the line drops from $\omega_0$ at $T_L = 0$ to $\omega = 0$ at the **stall torque** $T_s = K_t V / R_a$.

- The servo position transfer function contains **one integrator**, making the closed-loop system **Type 1** — zero steady-state error to a step setpoint.

- **Steppers** are the only family that operates **open-loop**; they can lose steps under overload and are therefore unsuitable for safety-critical valve applications.

- **Smart actuators** integrate a microcontroller, encoder, and fieldbus communication — they support **remote calibration, partial stroke testing, and predictive diagnostics** over HART, PROFIBUS-PA, or FOUNDATION Fieldbus.

- **Power electronics** (H-bridge for DC, VFD inverter for AC) modulates supply using **PWM at 4–20 kHz** typical switching frequency.

- The **mechanical time constant** is $\tau_m = J/B$ and the **electrical time constant** is $\tau_e = L_a/R_a$. For good servo performance, $\tau_e \ll \tau_m$.

- The **gear ratio** trades speed for torque: $T_{out} = N_g \cdot T_{motor}$ and $\omega_{out} = \omega_{motor} / N_g$, where $N_g$ is the gear ratio.

- **Fieldbus diagnostics** carried by smart actuators typically include: position, torque, temperature, number of strokes, motor runtime, and ESD event log.

- Always specify **units** in numerical answers — rad/s vs rpm, N·m vs N·cm, V vs mV. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.

- A **resolver** is preferred over an encoder in **high-temperature or harsh environments** (e.g., oil & gas downhole) because it has no electronic components and operates on electromagnetic induction.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Power Linear Actuators
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Power Linear Actuators

# Fluid Power Linear Actuators

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
