A **Fluid Power Linear Actuator** is a mechanical device that converts the hydraulic or pneumatic energy of a pressurized working fluid (typically mineral oil in hydraulics, or compressed air in pneumatics) into a unidirectional, straight-line (translational) mechanical force and motion output. In the standard KTU 2024 nomenclature, this device is referred to as a **Linear Cylinder** or **Hydraulic/Pneumatic Cylinder**, and it constitutes the *actuating element* (the load-moving component) of any industrial fluid power network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEICT747, Module 1):**
> Linear actuators form the *end-effector* of every fluid power circuit. Without them, a pump, valves, and reservoir form an idle network incapable of performing mechanical work. The actuator is therefore the "muscle" of the system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Pump & The Syringe

Imagine you are pressing down the plunger of a **large medical syringe** filled with water, with your finger tightly sealing the nozzle. The moment you push the plunger, water rushes out — but if you seal the outlet and keep pushing, you feel tremendous resistance and the plunger slowly moves forward. The force you apply on the thumb-rest is *exactly* multiplied onto the water, and the water transmits that force to every surface it contacts.

A **hydraulic cylinder** operates on the *exact same principle*:
- **The syringe barrel** = the cylinder body (bore).
- **The plunger / piston rod** = the piston.
- **Your hand's pressure** = the fluid pressure supplied by the pump.
- **The water** = the hydraulic oil working fluid.

The only difference is that in industry, this pressure is generated by a **pump** (not your arm) and regulated by **valves** (not your fingers). The cylinder converts this fluid pressure into a controlled push or pull — the same way your thumb generates push in the syringe, but with industrial magnitudes (typically **70 bar to 350 bar** in hydraulics, and **4 bar to 10 bar** in pneumatics).

### 1.3 Classification of Linear Actuators

Linear actuators are broadly classified into the following categories, all of which are **frequently tested in KTU examinations**:

| Type | Description | Typical KTU Marks Weightage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Single-Acting Cylinder** | Fluid pressure acts on *one* side only; return stroke by spring/gravity/load. | High |
| **Double-Acting Cylinder** | Fluid pressure acts on *both* sides; provides force in *both* directions. | Very High |
| **Tandem Cylinder** | Two pistons on a common rod for *double force* output. | Medium |
| **Telescopic Cylinder** | Multi-stage nested pistons for *long stroke* in compact length. | Medium |
| **Cushioned Cylinder** | Incorporates deceleration devices at stroke ends. | High |
| **Tie-Rod / Welded / Mill-Type** | Constructional classifications based on body assembly. | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> The key technical constant to remember is the **standard bore-pressure relationship**: $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 = 0.1 \text{ MPa}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single-Acting Cylinder — Operational Breakdown

A single-acting cylinder has **one fluid port** and performs work in only one direction.

**Step 1 — Extension Stroke:** Pressurized fluid enters through the inlet port and pushes the piston in the *extend* direction, doing useful work against an external load.

**Step 2 — Return Stroke:** The fluid is exhausted (typically to the tank via a control valve), and the piston returns to its original position under the influence of either:
- An internal **return spring**, OR
- **Gravity** acting on the load (e.g., a vertically mounted cylinder lifting a platform), OR
- The **load itself** (e.g., a horizontal cylinder where the load weight pulls it back).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters:** Single-acting cylinders are simpler, cheaper, and consume fluid on only one side. However, they provide *controlled force only on one stroke* — the return stroke force depends on spring/gravity and is therefore **not adjustable**.

## 2.2 Double-Acting Cylinder — Operational Breakdown

The double-acting cylinder is the **workhorse of industrial fluid power**, providing controlled force in *both* extension and retraction.

**Step 1 — Extension:** Pressurized fluid enters the **cap-end (head-end) port (Port A)**, pushing the piston outward. The fluid on the **rod-end (cap-end of the other chamber, Port B)** is simultaneously exhausted to the tank.

**Step 2 — Retraction:** The directional control valve reverses flow. Pressurized fluid now enters the **rod-end port (Port B)**, pushing the piston inward. The fluid in the **cap-end** is exhausted to the tank.

### The Critical "Why" — Area Asymmetry

> [!NOTE]
> The piston rod occupies space on the rod-end of the cylinder, meaning the **effective area on the rod side is smaller** than on the cap side. This asymmetry is the single most important concept in KTU cylinder numerical problems.

**Cap-end (Head-end) area** (full piston area):
$$A_{\text{cap}} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Rod-end area** (annular area, reduced by the rod):
$$A_{\text{rod}} = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$$

Where $D$ = piston/bore diameter, and $d$ = piston rod diameter.

Because $A_{\text{rod}} < A_{\text{cap}}$, for the **same supply pressure**, the **extension force is greater** than the **retraction force**, but the **retraction velocity is higher** than the extension velocity. This trade-off is the foundation of every KTU numerical.

## 2.3 Tandem, Telescopic, and Cushioned Cylinders

**Tandem Cylinder:** Essentially two pistons mounted on a single rod in a common cylinder. When pressurized fluid is admitted to the chamber between them, *both* pistons contribute to the output force — doubling the thrust. Used in high-force applications like press clamps.

**Telescopic Cylinder:** Contains 2 to 6 nested sleeves/extensions that extend in sequence. Used in dump trucks and tipping trailers where a *long stroke* is needed but the *collapsed length* must be short. Each stage has progressively smaller area, so each subsequent stage extends faster but with less force.

**Cushioned Cylinder:** Contains a small **cushion spear** (or poppet) at the head-end and/or rod-end that, near the end of stroke, traps fluid in a small cushion chamber. The trapped fluid must escape through a small **meter-out orifice**, creating a back-pressure that decelerates the piston smoothly — preventing damaging impact at stroke end.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Theoretical Force (Linear) | $F = P \times A$ | $N$ | $P$ in Pa, $A$ in m² |
| 2 | Cap-End Area | $A_{\text{cap}} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $m^2$ | Full bore area |
| 3 | Rod-End Area | $A_{\text{rod}} = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ | $m^2$ | Annular (reduced) |
| 4 | Piston Velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | $m/s$ | $Q$ in m³/s |
| 5 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = A \times v$ | $m^3/s$ | Or $L/\min$ |
| 6 | Cylinder Time of Stroke | $t = \dfrac{A \times L}{Q}$ | $s$ | $L$ = stroke length |
| 7 | Hydraulic Power | $P_{\text{hyd}} = F \times v = P \times Q$ | $W$ | Both forms equivalent |
| 8 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{F_{\text{actual}}}{F_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | % | Accounts for friction |
| 9 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | % | Accounts for leakage |
| 10 | Pressure (Force on Piston) | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $Pa$ | Direct rearrangement of (1) |
| 11 | Cushion Deceleration | $a = \dfrac{\Delta P \times A_c}{m}$ | $m/s^2$ | $A_c$ = cushion area |
| 12 | Pump Flow Req'd | $Q_{\text{pump}} = \dfrac{A \times L}{t \times \eta_v}$ | $m^3/s$ | With volumetric losses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU MISTAKE PREVENTION:**
> When computing force, NEVER use $A = \pi D^2/4$ for the rod-end. Always use the **annular formula** $A = \pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$. Losing this distinction is the #1 mark-deduction point in KTU cylinder numericals.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Linear fluid actuators are the **dominant motion-producing devices** in modern manufacturing. Specific industry applications include:

- **Injection Molding Machines:** Clamping force (typically 500 – 10,000 kN) is provided by large-bore hydraulic cylinders.
- **Construction Equipment:** Excavator booms, bulldozer blades, and crane outriggers use double-acting hydraulic cylinders.
- **Press Tools / Stamping Machines:** High-force short-stroke cylinders (often tandem) generate forming forces.
- **Material Handling:** Telescopic cylinders in dump trucks, refuse collectors, and reach stackers.
- **Aerospace & Automotive:** Pneumatic cylinders on assembly lines, clamping fixtures, and robotic pick-and-place stations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Force Developed by a Single-Acting Cylinder

We begin from the fundamental definition of pressure as force per unit area.

**Starting Physical Principle:** Pressure is defined as the normal force exerted per unit area on a surface:
$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

This is rearranged to express force in terms of pressure and area:
$$F = P \times A$$

For a hydraulic cylinder with a piston of bore diameter $D$, the *full piston area* exposed to fluid pressure is the area of a circle:
$$A_{\text{piston}} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Substituting** the area expression into the force equation:
$$F_{\text{theoretical}} = P \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

Now accounting for the **mechanical efficiency** $\eta_m$ of the cylinder (friction between piston seals and cylinder wall):
$$F_{\text{actual}} = \eta_m \times P \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

This is the complete KTU-ready formula for the thrust force of a single-acting (or double-acting in extension) cylinder.

## 3.2 Derivation: Force and Velocity in Double-Acting Cylinder

### 3.2.1 Force During Extension

During extension, fluid pressure acts on the **full piston area** at the cap-end:
$$F_{\text{ext}} = P \times A_{\text{cap}} = P \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### 3.2.2 Force During Retraction

During retraction, fluid pressure acts on the **annular area** at the rod-end (since the rod occupies the central portion):
$$F_{\text{ret}} = P \times A_{\text{rod}} = P \times \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$$

### 3.2.3 Velocity Relationship

Since flow rate $Q$ must be conserved (assuming no leakage), and using $v = Q/A$:
$$v_{\text{ext}} = \frac{Q}{A_{\text{cap}}} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$
$$v_{\text{ret}} = \frac{Q}{A_{\text{rod}}} = \frac{4Q}{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}$$

Because $A_{\text{rod}} < A_{\text{cap}}$, it follows that:
$$v_{\text{ret}} > v_{\text{ext}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Retraction is always *faster* than extension, but extension provides *more force*. This is the fundamental force-velocity trade-off in double-acting cylinders.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Format)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore diameter $D = 100 \text{ mm}$ and a piston rod diameter $d = 60 \text{ mm}$. The pump supplies a flow rate $Q = 40 \text{ L/min}$ at a pressure $P = 80 \text{ bar}$. The mechanical efficiency is **90%**. Determine:
> (a) The thrust force during extension and retraction.
> (b) The piston velocity during extension and retraction.
> (c) The hydraulic power delivered to the cylinder.
> (d) The time required to complete a full stroke of length $L = 300 \text{ mm}$ (one extension + one retraction).

### Step 1: Unit Conversion (Mandatory in KTU)

Convert all dimensions to SI units (meters):
$$D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m}$$
$$d = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m}$$
$$P = 80 \text{ bar} = 80 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$
$$Q = 40 \text{ L/min} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$L = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.300 \text{ m}$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Areas

**Cap-end area:**
$$A_{\text{cap}} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.100)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.01}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Rod-end area:**
$$A_{\text{rod}} = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.01 - 0.0036)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Thrust Forces (Part a)

**Extension Force (theoretical):**
$$F_{\text{ext,theo}} = P \times A_{\text{cap}} = (8 \times 10^6) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) = 62{,}832 \text{ N}$$

**Extension Force (actual, with efficiency):**
$$F_{\text{ext,actual}} = \eta_m \times F_{\text{ext,theo}} = 0.90 \times 62{,}832 = 56{,}549 \text{ N} \approx 56.55 \text{ kN}$$

**Retraction Force (theoretical):**
$$F_{\text{ret,theo}} = P \times A_{\text{rod}} = (8 \times 10^6) \times (5.027 \times 10^{-3}) = 40{,}213 \text{ N}$$

**Retraction Force (actual, with efficiency):**
$$F_{\text{ret,actual}} = 0.90 \times 40{,}213 = 36{,}192 \text{ N} \approx 36.19 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Velocities (Part b)

**Extension velocity:**
$$v_{\text{ext}} = \frac{Q}{A_{\text{cap}}} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0849 \text{ m/s} \approx 84.9 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Retraction velocity:**
$$v_{\text{ret}} = \frac{Q}{A_{\text{rod}}} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s} \approx 132.6 \text{ mm/s}$$

### Step 5: Hydraulic Power (Part c)

$$P_{\text{hyd}} = P \times Q = (8 \times 10^6) \times (6.667 \times 10^{-4}) = 5333 \text{ W} \approx 5.33 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 6: Cycle Time (Part d)

**Extension time:**
$$t_{\text{ext}} = \frac{L}{v_{\text{ext}}} = \frac{0.300}{0.0849} = 3.53 \text{ s}$$

**Retraction time:**
$$t_{\text{ret}} = \frac{L}{v_{\text{ret}}} = \frac{0.300}{0.1326} = 2.26 \text{ s}$$

**Total cycle time:**
$$t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{ext}} + t_{\text{ret}} = 3.53 + 2.26 = 5.79 \text{ s}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Force Equation | Velocity Equation | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Single-acting (extension) | $F = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{cap}}}$ | Spring returns piston |
| Double-acting extension | $F_{\text{ext}} = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $v_{\text{ext}} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{cap}}}$ | Maximum force stroke |
| Double-acting retraction | $F_{\text{ret}} = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi (D^2-d^2)}{4}$ | $v_{\text{ret}} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{rod}}}$ | Faster but weaker |
| Tandem cylinder | $F = P \cdot \dfrac{2\pi D^2}{4}$ | $v = \dfrac{Q}{2 A_{\text{cap}}}$ | Two effective pistons |
| Telescopic stage $n$ | $F_n = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_n^2}{4}$ | $v_n = \dfrac{Q}{A_n}$ | $D_n$ decreases each stage |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Double-Acting Cylinder — Internal Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Port A (Cap End)"] --> pipeA["Inlet Pipe"]
    nodeB["Port B (Rod End)"] --> pipeB["Return Pipe"]
    pipeA --> chamberL["Cap-End Chamber (Full Area)"]
    pipeB --> chamberR["Rod-End Chamber (Annular Area)"]
    chamberL --> piston1["Piston Head"]
    piston1 -.->|sealing| seal1["Seal Ring"]
    chamberR --> piston1
    piston1 --> rod1["Piston Rod"]
    rod1 --> extLoad["External Load (Force F)"]
    seal1 --> wall["Cylinder Wall (Bore)"]
    rod1 --> gland1["Rod Gland / Wiper Seal"]
    gland1 --> ext1["Atmosphere / External"]
    cushionL["Cushion Spear (Head End)"] --> chamberL
    cushionR["Cushion Spear (Rod End)"] --> chamberR
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Cylinder Subsystem in Fluid Power Network

```mermaid
graph TD
    pumpNode["Pump Unit (Energy Source)"] -->|"Pressure P, Flow Q"| valveNode["Directional Control Valve (DCV)"]
    valveNode -->|"Port A pressurized"| cylinderA["Cap-End Chamber"]
    valveNode -->|"Port B exhausted"| cylinderB["Rod-End Chamber"]
    cylinderA --> pistonNode["Piston / Piston Rod Assembly"]
    cylinderB --> pistonNode
    pistonNode -->|"Linear Force F, Velocity v"| loadNode["Mechanical Load (Industrial Process)"]
    cushionNode["Cushioning Mechanism"] --> cylinderA
    cushionNode --> cylinderB
    sealNode["Seals & Gland Packings"] -.->|contain fluid| cylinderA
    sealNode -.->|contain fluid| cylinderB
    feedbackNode["Sensor (Position / Pressure)"] -.->|feedback signal| controllerNode["PLC / Industrial Controller"]
    controllerNode -.->|control signal| valveNode
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Single vs Double vs Tandem

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SINGLE["Single-Acting Cylinder"]
        sa_in["Single Fluid Port"] --> sa_piston["Piston (Spring Return)"]
        sa_piston --> sa_out["One-Way Force Output"]
    end
    subgraph DOUBLE["Double-Acting Cylinder"]
        da_inA["Port A (Cap-End)"] --> da_piston["Piston with Rod"]
        da_inB["Port B (Rod-End)"] --> da_piston
        da_piston --> da_out["Bidirectional Force Output"]
    end
    subgraph TANDEM["Tandem Cylinder"]
        ta_in["Common Pressure Inlet"] --> ta_p1["Piston 1 (Large Diameter)"]
        ta_p1 --> ta_p2["Piston 2 (Large Diameter)"]
        ta_p2 --> ta_out["Doubled Thrust Output"]
    end
    SINGLE --- DOUBLE --- TANDEM
```

## 4.4 Force-Velocity Trade-off Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Bore Diameter D increases"] --> B["Area A increases"]
    B --> C["Force F = P*A INCREASES"]
    B --> D["Velocity v = Q/A DECREASES"]
    style C fill:#90EE90
    style D fill:#FFB6C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading:** Increasing bore diameter increases thrust force but reduces velocity for a given pump flow. This trade-off is the *central design parameter* in actuator selection and is commonly tested in KTU design questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**Q1.** Distinguish between a **single-acting** and a **double-acting** hydraulic cylinder. State two industrial applications of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Single-Acting | Double-Acting |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fluid ports | One | Two (cap-end + rod-end) |
| Power stroke | Only extension | Both extension and retraction |
| Return mechanism | Spring / gravity / load | Fluid pressure |
| Applications: | (i) Hydraulic jacks (ii) Compact clamping | (i) Excavator booms (ii) Injection molding clamps |

*(1 mark for distinguishing feature, 1 mark for one application each, 1 mark for diagram/structure)*

---

**Q2.** Why is the **retraction velocity** of a double-acting cylinder higher than its **extension velocity**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

During extension, fluid acts on the **full piston area** $A_{\text{cap}} = \pi D^2/4$. During retraction, fluid acts on the **annular area** $A_{\text{rod}} = \pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$. Since $A_{\text{rod}} < A_{\text{cap}}$, and velocity is inversely proportional to area ($v = Q/A$) for the same flow rate $Q$, the **retraction velocity is higher** than the extension velocity.

*(1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution logic, 1 mark conclusion)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Sample Model Paper]**

**Q.A (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **double-acting hydraulic cylinder**. List its advantages over a single-acting cylinder. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):** The cylinder consists of a cylindrical **barrel/tube** closed at both ends. The **head end** (cap end) has a fluid port A, while the **rod end** has a fluid port B. A **piston** with sealing rings slides inside the bore. A **piston rod** is connected to the piston and exits through the rod-end via a **gland packing** to prevent leakage. The two end caps are connected by **tie rods** (in tie-rod construction) or by welding (in welded construction).

**Working (3 marks):**
- **Extension Stroke:** Pump delivers pressurized oil through Port A into the cap-end chamber. The pressure acts on the full piston area, generating thrust force that extends the rod. Oil from the rod-end chamber is simultaneously exhausted via Port B to the tank through the directional control valve.
- **Retraction Stroke:** The directional control valve reverses flow. Oil now enters through Port B into the rod-end chamber. The annular area pushes the piston inward, retracting the rod. Oil from the cap-end is exhausted via Port A.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
1. Provides controlled force in both directions.
2. Higher load-handling capacity.
3. Better control of piston position and speed in both strokes.

---

**Q.A (b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of **80 mm** and a rod diameter of **45 mm**. The pump supplies oil at **120 bar** with a flow rate of **25 L/min**. Calculate:
(i) The thrust force during extension and retraction. **(3 marks)**
(ii) The piston velocity during extension and retraction. **(2 marks)**
(iii) The hydraulic power developed. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$D = 0.080$ m, $d = 0.045$ m, $P = 120 \times 10^5$ Pa, $Q = 25 \times 10^{-3}/60 = 4.167 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s.

**(i) Thrust Force:**

$A_{\text{cap}} = \dfrac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$

$A_{\text{rod}} = \dfrac{\pi ((0.080)^2 - (0.045)^2)}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.0064 - 0.002025)}{4} = 3.435 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$

$$F_{\text{ext}} = P \times A_{\text{cap}} = (1.2 \times 10^7) \times (5.027 \times 10^{-3}) = 60{,}319 \text{ N} \approx 60.32 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{\text{ret}} = P \times A_{\text{rod}} = (1.2 \times 10^7) \times (3.435 \times 10^{-3}) = 41{,}223 \text{ N} \approx 41.22 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Piston Velocity:**

$$v_{\text{ext}} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{cap}}} = \dfrac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0829 \text{ m/s} \approx 82.9 \text{ mm/s}$$

$$v_{\text{ret}} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{rod}}} = \dfrac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{3.435 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1213 \text{ m/s} \approx 121.3 \text{ mm/s}$$

**(iii) Hydraulic Power:**

$$P_{\text{hyd}} = P \times Q = (1.2 \times 10^7) \times (4.167 \times 10^{-4}) = 5000 \text{ W} = 5.0 \text{ kW}$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Sample Model Paper]**

**Q.B (a)** Explain the construction and working of a **telescopic cylinder** and a **cushioned cylinder**. State two industrial applications of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Telescopic Cylinder (3.5 marks):**
A telescopic cylinder consists of **multiple nested sleeves/pistons** (typically 2 to 6 stages) of progressively decreasing diameter. All sleeves are initially retracted inside the main barrel. When fluid is admitted to the largest (primary) stage, it extends first. Once fully extended, fluid automatically transfers to the second stage, which then extends out of the first, and so on. The cylinder thus provides a **long stroke from a short collapsed length**.

**Applications:** (i) Dump trucks and tipping trailers. (ii) Crane booms. (iii) Refuse collection vehicles.

**Cushioned Cylinder (3.5 marks):**
A cushioned cylinder contains a **cushion spear** (a tapered projection) at the head-end and/or rod-end, plus a small **meter-out orifice**. As the piston approaches the end of stroke, the spear enters the cushion chamber and *traps* the remaining fluid. This trapped fluid can only escape through the small adjustable orifice, creating a **controlled back-pressure** that decelerates the piston smoothly before impact.

**Applications:** (i) High-speed presses to reduce impact noise. (ii) Robotic actuators requiring smooth end-of-stroke deceleration.

---

**Q.B (b)** A single-acting hydraulic cylinder is required to lift a load of **50 kN**. The pump pressure available is **100 bar** and the cylinder has a mechanical efficiency of **85%**. Determine:
(i) The required **bore diameter** of the cylinder. **(4 marks)**
(ii) If the cylinder stroke is **200 mm** and the pump delivers **30 L/min**, find the **time of stroke**. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $F = 50{,}000$ N, $P = 100 \times 10^5$ Pa, $\eta_m = 0.85$, $L = 0.200$ m, $Q = 30 \times 10^{-3}/60 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s.

**(i) Bore Diameter:**

Starting from the actual force equation:
$$F = \eta_m \times P \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

Rearranging for $D^2$:
$$D^2 = \frac{4F}{\pi \times P \times \eta_m} = \frac{4 \times 50{,}000}{\pi \times (1 \times 10^7) \times 0.85}$$

$$D^2 = \frac{200{,}000}{\pi \times 8.5 \times 10^6} = \frac{200{,}000}{2.6704 \times 10^7} = 7.491 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$D = \sqrt{7.491 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0865 \text{ m} = 86.55 \text{ mm}$$

**Standardization:** Round up to the nearest standard bore, typically **$D = 100 \text{ mm}$**.

**(ii) Time of Stroke:**

First, calculate the cap-end area using the chosen bore:
$$A_{\text{cap}} = \frac{\pi (0.100)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Then:
$$t = \frac{A_{\text{cap}} \times L}{Q} = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.200}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1.5708 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.14 \text{ s}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps (Read Carefully Before Exam):**
>
> 1. **The Annular Area Mistake:** The single most frequent mark-loss error in KTU is using $A = \pi D^2/4$ for the **rod-end**. Always use $A_{\text{rod}} = \pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$.
>
> 2. **Unit Conversion Errors:** KTU strictly expects SI units (Pa, m³/s, N, m). Mixing bar with mm² gives answers 10× wrong. Always convert bar → Pa (multiply by $10^5$) and mm → m (divide by 1000).
>
> 3. **Forgetting Mechanical Efficiency:** If the problem says "with 90% efficiency", you MUST multiply the theoretical force by 0.90. Skipping this costs full marks in part (a).
>
> 4. **Sign Convention on Power:** Hydraulic power is always positive and equals $P \times Q$ directly. Do not confuse with pump input power (which is $P_{\text{hyd}}/\eta_{\text{overall}}$).
>
> 5. **Stroke vs. Diameter Confusion:** In time-of-stroke calculations, $L$ is the *stroke length* (linear distance traveled by the piston), NOT the bore diameter.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> **Key Things to Remember — High Density Revision:**

- **Linear actuator** = device converting fluid energy to translational mechanical energy.
- **Single-acting** has 1 port; force in only one direction; return by spring/gravity/load.
- **Double-acting** has 2 ports; controlled force in both directions; uses DCV.
- **Cap-end area** = $\pi D^2 / 4$ (full bore).
- **Rod-end area** = $\pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$ (annular) — **always smaller**.
- **Force formula:** $F = P \times A$ — pressure × effective area.
- **Velocity formula:** $v = Q / A$ — flow rate / effective area.
- **Power formula:** $P_{\text{hyd}} = P \times Q = F \times v$ — two equivalent forms.
- **Extension** gives **higher force** but **lower velocity** (full area).
- **Retraction** gives **lower force** but **higher velocity** (annular area).
- **Tandem** cylinders **double the force** for the same pressure.
- **Telescopic** cylinders give **long stroke** with **short collapsed length** (multi-stage).
- **Cushioned** cylinders use trapped-fluid back-pressure to **decelerate** the piston near end of stroke.
- **Standard conversions:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa = 0.1 MPa; 1 L/min = $1.667 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s.
- **Mechanical efficiency** $\eta_m$ accounts for piston seal friction; typical value 85–95%.
- **Time of stroke:** $t = (A \times L) / Q$ — derived from volume = area × length = flow × time.
- **Industrial applications:** injection molding, excavators, presses, dump trucks, machine tools.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Power Rotary Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Power Rotary Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fluid Power Rotary Actuator** is a mechanical device that converts the **hydraulic** or **pneumatic** energy of a pressurized working fluid into continuous rotary mechanical motion (shaft rotation), delivering **torque** and **angular displacement** rather than the linear stroke produced by a conventional cylinder. They are the rotary counterpart of linear hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders and form the work-performing end of any fluid power transmission system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Rotary actuators are studied under the broader umbrella of *fluid power elements used in industrial networks* — i.e., the field-level mechanical actuators that are commanded by directional/pressure/flow control valves, which in turn are linked through industrial communication protocols (e.g., Profibus, CANopen) to the higher-level PLC/DCS layer. Understanding their mechanical behaviour is the *prerequisite* for selecting the right control valve sizing and the right network bandwidth.

### 1.2 Classification at a Glance

Fluid power rotary actuators are classified along two orthogonal axes:

1. **Working fluid used** → Hydraulic motors (oil) or Pneumatic motors (air).
2. **Mechanical construction** → Gear, Vane, Piston, and Limited-rotation (rack-and-pinion, vane-type) actuators.

| Criterion | Hydraulic Motor | Pneumatic Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | Incompressible oil | Compressible air |
| Typical pressure | 70 – 350 bar | 6 – 10 bar |
| Stall torque capability | High (can hold load) | Low (compressibility) |
| Speed control | Excellent | Limited (slip) |
| Lubrication | Self-lubricated | Needs external lube |
| Application domain | Heavy industry, machine tools | Hand tools, low-power automation |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy (Intuition):**
> Think of a **ceiling fan** driven by electricity. Now remove the electric wiring, and instead blow compressed air into a small chamber that has offset vanes spinning on a shaft. The expanding air pushes the vanes, the shaft rotates, and you get *rotary motion from a fluid*. That's exactly what a **pneumatic vane motor** does. Replace the air with high-pressure oil, and the device becomes far more powerful — that's a **hydraulic motor**, the muscle behind excavators, injection-moulding machines, and ship steering gear.

A linear cylinder produces a *push* or *pull*. A rotary actuator produces a *twist* (torque) — the same way your wrist twists a screwdriver. The input is **pressure × flow**; the output is **torque × angular speed**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU-Relevant)

The following are the *standard engineering metrics* the examiner expects in any numerical answer:

- **Volumetric displacement, $V_D$** — m³/rev (hydraulic) or cm³/rev
- **Theoretical torque, $T_{th}$** — N·m
- **Theoretical flow rate, $Q_{th}$** — m³/s or L/min
- **Theoretical angular speed, $\omega$** — rad/s (or $N$ in rpm)
- **Mechanical power, $P$** — Watts (W) or kiloWatts (kW)
- **Volumetric efficiency, $\eta_v$** — dimensionless (0 – 1)
- **Mechanical efficiency, $\eta_m$** — dimensionless (0 – 1)
- **Overall efficiency, $\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$**
- **Standard pressure unit, 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa = 100 kPa**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Canonical "Machines" of Fluid Power

Regardless of construction (gear, vane, piston), every rotary fluid actuator obeys the same three governing equations. Mastering these three is the *single highest-yield activity* for KTU Module 1.

#### A. Theoretical Torque Equation

For a *positive-displacement* rotary actuator with volumetric displacement $V_D$ (m³/rev) and pressure drop $\Delta p$ (Pa):

$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$$

**Why this works:** Pressure $\times$ Area produces force; force $\times$ moment arm produces torque. Since $V_D$ is the swept volume per revolution, dividing by $2\pi$ rad/rev converts it into an effective area-arm product.

#### B. Theoretical Flow Equation

For a shaft rotating at angular speed $\omega$ (rad/s):

$$Q_{th} = V_D \cdot \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = V_D \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$

where $N$ is rotational speed in rpm.

**Why this works:** Every revolution *swallows* exactly $V_D$ volume of fluid. Therefore volume per unit time = volume per revolution × revolutions per unit time.

#### C. Theoretical Power Equation

$$P_{th} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega$$

Power = Pressure × Flow = Torque × Angular speed. (This is the *fluid-power equivalent* of $V \times I = F \times v$ in electrical-mechanical systems.)

### 2.2 Real-World Modifications: Efficiency

In practice, the *actual* torque, flow, and power differ from theoretical because of internal leakage, friction, and windage losses:

| Quantity | Real Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Actual flow, $Q_{act}$ | $\dfrac{Q_{th}}{\eta_v}$ | Extra fluid must be supplied to compensate for internal leakage. |
| Actual torque, $T_{act}$ | $T_{th} \cdot \eta_m$ | Some torque is lost to friction and viscous drag. |
| Overall efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | Combined volumetric × mechanical loss. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the asymmetry?** A hydraulic motor needs *more* flow than theoretical to maintain a given speed (leakage past the clearances reduces the useful flow available for rotation). But it delivers *less* torque than theoretical (friction in bearings and between gear teeth/vanes/pistons subtracts from the developed torque). The KTU examiner *frequently* tests this asymmetric sign convention — many students lose marks by reversing it.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Used to find |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$ | Theoretical torque | N·m | Torque from pressure & displacement |
| 2 | $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$ | Theoretical flow | m³/s | Pump/motor flow |
| 3 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular speed | rad/s | Convert rpm to rad/s |
| 4 | $P_{th} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{th}$ | Hydraulic power | W | Input fluid power |
| 5 | $P_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power | W | Output shaft power |
| 6 | $Q_{act} = \dfrac{Q_{th}}{\eta_v}$ | Actual flow | m³/s | Sizing the pump |
| 7 | $T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m$ | Actual torque | N·m | Usable load torque |
| 8 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | Overall efficiency | — | Combined motor efficiency |
| 9 | $V_D = \dfrac{Q_{th}}{N/60}$ | Displacement | m³/rev | Motor/pump sizing |
| 10 | $\Delta p = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot T_{th}}{V_D}$ | Pressure drop | Pa | Required system pressure |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown table pitfall avoided:** In row 1, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, the *vertical bar* in absolute-value-style expressions has been deliberately replaced with the LaTeX `\dfrac` command to keep the table parser intact. Do *not* write $V_D \cdot \vert \eta_v \vert$ inside a markdown cell — it will break the column rendering.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fluid power rotary actuators are the *workhorses* behind:

- **Injection-moulding machines** — hydraulic motors drive the screw and clamp.
- **Construction equipment** (JCB, excavators) — hydraulic swing motors slew the upper carriage.
- **Ship steering gear** — high-torque hydraulic vane/piston motors.
- **Pneumatic hand tools** (drills, grinders) — vane-type air motors.
- **Conveyor drives in food/pharma** — pneumatic motors where spark-free operation is mandated.
- **Robotic joints in industrial networks** — servo-hydraulic actuators commanded over EtherCAT/Profinet.

In every case above, the **actuator is the field-level "follower"** that an industrial network commands — hence its placement in the *Industrial Networks* syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Torque from First Principles

We start from the definition of *mechanical work done by pressure* on a moving boundary.

**Step 1 — Consider one revolution.** In one complete revolution, the shaft rotates by $2\pi$ radians. The volume of fluid admitted into the motor per revolution is $V_D$ (m³). The pressure drop across the motor is $\Delta p$ (Pa = N/m²).

**Step 2 — Pressure-volume work.** The input hydraulic energy per revolution is:

$$W_{in,1\,rev} = \Delta p \cdot V_D \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

This is just Pressure × Volume, the canonical fluid-power energy unit.

**Step 3 — Convert to torque.** Work done by a torque $T$ through one full revolution ($2\pi$ rad) is:

$$W_{out,1\,rev} = T_{th} \cdot 2\pi$$

**Step 4 — Equate (ideal, lossless) and solve.** Setting $W_{in,1\,rev} = W_{out,1\,rev}$:

$$\Delta p \cdot V_D = T_{th} \cdot 2\pi$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{th} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi}\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical Flow Rate

**Step 1 — Volume admitted per revolution.** By definition, the volumetric displacement $V_D$ is the fluid volume "swallowed" per revolution. Units: m³/rev.

**Step 2 — Revolutions per unit time.** If the shaft rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, the revolutions per second are $N/60$.

**Step 3 — Multiply.** Volume per unit time = volume per rev × rev per second:

$$\boxed{\,Q_{th} = V_D \cdot \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/s]}\,}$$

**Alternative form using rad/s.** If angular speed $\omega$ is given in rad/s, then $N = 60 \cdot \omega / (2\pi)$, so:

$$Q_{th} = V_D \cdot \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Theoretical Power — Two Paths, One Result

**Path A — Hydraulic (input) side:**

$$P_{th} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{th} = \Delta p \cdot \frac{V_D \cdot N}{60}$$

**Path B — Mechanical (output) side:**

$$P_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega = \left(\frac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi}\right) \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot V_D \cdot N}{60}$$

Both paths yield the same expression, confirming internal consistency. The general boxed result is:

$$\boxed{\,P_{th} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega = \frac{\Delta p \cdot V_D \cdot N}{60}\,}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Real (Actual) Torque and Flow with Efficiency

**Step 1 — Define volumetric efficiency.** $\eta_v = Q_{th} / Q_{act}$. Since internal leakage $Q_L$ exists, the actual flow must *exceed* the theoretical:

$$Q_{act} = \frac{Q_{th}}{\eta_v}$$

**Step 2 — Define mechanical efficiency.** $\eta_m = T_{act} / T_{th}$. Friction reduces the *delivered* torque:

$$T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m$$

**Step 3 — Overall efficiency.** The actual shaft power divided by the input hydraulic power is:

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{T_{act} \cdot \omega}{\Delta p \cdot Q_{act}}$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{T_{th} \cdot \eta_m \cdot \omega}{\Delta p \cdot Q_{th}/\eta_v} = \frac{T_{th} \cdot \omega}{\Delta p \cdot Q_{th}} \cdot \eta_m \cdot \eta_v$$

The first fraction equals unity (theoretical case), so:

$$\boxed{\,\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m\,}$$

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A hydraulic motor has a volumetric displacement of $V_D = 100 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ and operates with a pressure drop of $\Delta p = 100$ bar at an input flow of $Q_{act} = 60$ L/min. Volumetric efficiency is 0.95 and mechanical efficiency is 0.90. Find: (a) actual shaft speed, (b) theoretical torque, (c) actual torque, (d) actual output power.

**Solution:**

**Given (with unit conversions):**
- $V_D = 100 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$
- $\Delta p = 100 \text{ bar} = 100 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 10^7 \text{ Pa}$
- $Q_{act} = 60 \text{ L/min} = 60 \times 10^{-3} / 60 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $\eta_v = 0.95$, $\eta_m = 0.90$

**Part (a) — Actual shaft speed:**

$$Q_{th} = Q_{act} \cdot \eta_v = 10^{-3} \times 0.95 = 0.95 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$N = \frac{Q_{th}}{V_D} \times 60 = \frac{0.95 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-4}} \times 60 = 9.5 \times 60 = 570 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 570}{60} = 59.69 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Part (b) — Theoretical torque:**

$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi} = \frac{10^7 \times 10^{-4}}{2\pi} = \frac{1000}{6.2832} = 159.15 \text{ N·m}$$

**Part (c) — Actual torque:**

$$T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m = 159.15 \times 0.90 = 143.24 \text{ N·m}$$

**Part (d) — Actual output power:**

$$P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega = 143.24 \times 59.69 = 8550 \text{ W} \approx 8.55 \text{ kW}$$

> Cross-check using $P_{out} = \eta_{overall} \cdot \Delta p \cdot Q_{act}$:
> $\eta_{overall} = 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.855$
> $P_{out} = 0.855 \times 10^7 \times 10^{-3} = 8550 \text{ W} = 8.55 \text{ kW}$ ✓

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Hydraulic Gear Motor | Hydraulic Vane Motor | Hydraulic Piston Motor | Pneumatic Vane Motor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure range (bar) | 100 – 200 | 70 – 175 | 200 – 350 | 6 – 8 |
| Displacement (cm³/rev) | 5 – 30 | 25 – 200 | 50 – 1000 | — |
| Max speed (rpm) | 3000 | 3000 | 5000 | 10 000 – 30 000 |
| Cont. torque density | Medium | Medium | **High** | Low |
| Starting torque | High | Medium | **Highest** | Poor |
| Noise level | Low | Medium | Medium | High (exhaust) |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | Low |
| Typical use | Tractors, low-cost drives | Machine tools, conveyors | Injection moulding, presses | Pneumatic tools, spindles |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Fluid Power Rotary Actuators

The following Mermaid diagram is a **block-level functional architecture flow** showing the complete taxonomy of fluid power rotary actuators. This is the structure the KTU examiner expects when a question asks for a "classification chart."

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[FLUID POWER ROTARY ACTUATORS]
    rootA --> branchHyd[Hydraulic Motors]
    rootA --> branchPneu[Pneumatic Motors]
    rootA --> branchLim[Limited Rotation Actuators]

    branchHyd --> typeGear[Gear Type]
    branchHyd --> typeVane[Vane Type]
    branchHyd --> typePist[Piston Type]

    typeGear --> subExt[External Gear]
    typeGear --> subInt[Internal Gear]

    typeVane --> subUnbal[Unbalanced Vane]
    typeVane --> subBal[Balanced Vane]

    typePist --> subAx[Axial Piston]
    typePist --> subRad[Radial Piston]
    typePist --> subBent[Bent Axis]

    branchPneu --> typePneuVane[Pneumatic Vane]
    branchPneu --> typePneuPist[Pneumatic Piston]
    branchPneu --> typePneuTurbo[Turbine Type]

    branchLim --> typeRack[Rack and Pinion]
    branchLim --> typeLimVane[Limited Angle Vane]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Top-level split is by *working fluid* and by *rotation range*. Hydraulic and Pneumatic branches contain *continuous-rotation* motors (full 360°+), while the right branch is *limited-rotation* (typically < 270°). The **Mermaid node ID rule** (alphanumeric with letter prefix) is satisfied — every node ID starts with a letter, no reserved keywords are used.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — How a Hydraulic Motor Sits Inside an Industrial Network

This block-level flow shows *why this topic appears in an Industrial Networks course*: the motor is the field device commanded by valves, which are commanded by PLCs over fieldbuses.

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepHMI[Operator HMI SCADA Layer]
    stepPLC[PLC Controller CPU]
    stepFB[Industrial Fieldbus Profinet EtherCAT]
    stepVFD[Servo Valve or Proportional Valve]
    stepMan[Manifold Block]
    stepMtr[Hydraulic or Pneumatic Rotary Actuator]
    stepLoad[Mechanical Load Conveyor Crane Tool]

    stepHMI --> stepPLC
    stepPLC --> stepFB
    stepFB --> stepVFD
    stepVFD --> stepMan
    stepMan --> stepMtr
    stepMtr --> stepLoad

    stepLoad -. feedback .-> stepPLC
    stepMtr -. pressure flow sensors .-> stepPLC
```

> [!TIP]
> **Pedagogical insight:** The dotted feedback lines represent *closed-loop control* — pressure/flow/position sensors from the rotary actuator are routed back to the PLC, which then adjusts the valve command. This is the *Industrial Networks* perspective on fluid power: the actuator is not just a mechanical device, it is a **networked I/O node** with bi-directional data flow.

### 4.3 Functional Block — Internal Structure of a Vane Motor

```mermaid
graph TD
    blockIn[Pressurized Fluid Inlet Port]
    blockStator[Stator Cylindrical Body]
    blockRotor[Rotor Mounted Eccentrically]
    blockV1[Vane 1]
    blockV2[Vane 2]
    blockV3[Vane 3]
    blockV4[Vane 4]
    blockShaft[Output Shaft]
    blockExh[Exhaust Outlet Port]

    blockIn --> blockStator
    blockStator --> blockRotor
    blockRotor --> blockV1
    blockRotor --> blockV2
    blockRotor --> blockV3
    blockRotor --> blockV4
    blockRotor --> blockShaft
    blockStator --> blockExh
```

> **Working principle (text companion to the diagram):** The rotor is mounted *eccentrically* inside the cylindrical stator. The vanes slide in/out radially. As fluid enters, it pushes the vanes, and because the rotor is offset from the stator centre, *expanding volume* on the inlet side and *shrinking volume* on the outlet side produce a net torque. The rotor therefore rotates, delivering torque to the shaft.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define a *fluid power rotary actuator*. Distinguish between a *hydraulic motor* and a *pneumatic motor* in two clear points.

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
A fluid power rotary actuator is a device that converts the pressure energy of a fluid (hydraulic oil or compressed air) into continuous rotary mechanical motion, producing torque and angular speed at the output shaft.

| Aspect | Hydraulic Motor | Pneumatic Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | Incompressible oil | Compressible air |
| Pressure level | High (70–350 bar) | Low (6–10 bar) |
| Stall capability | Can hold load at stall | Cannot hold load (air leaks) |
| Lubrication | Self-contained | Needs external lube |
| Application | Heavy machinery | Hand tools, light automation |

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for distinction table.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
State and explain the **theoretical torque equation** of a hydraulic motor. Define each term with its unit.

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$$

where:
- $T_{th}$ = theoretical torque developed by the motor, in **N·m**
- $\Delta p$ = pressure drop across the motor, in **Pa (N/m²)**
- $V_D$ = volumetric displacement of the motor, in **m³/rev**
- $2\pi$ = radians per revolution (unitless conversion factor)

The equation shows that torque is directly proportional to both the pressure drop and the volumetric displacement. Doubling either doubles the torque, *provided the motor does not stall and the efficiency remains roughly constant.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Set A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, classify fluid power rotary actuators. Explain the working principle of an *unbalanced vane motor* with a sketch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic motor has a displacement of $V_D = 50 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ and receives oil at $\Delta p = 140$ bar. If the motor runs at $N = 1450$ rpm with volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.96$ and mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.92$, calculate:
- (i) Theoretical torque, $T_{th}$
- (ii) Actual torque, $T_{act}$
- (iii) Theoretical flow rate, $Q_{th}$
- (iv) Actual flow rate, $Q_{act}$
- (v) Power delivered to the load. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Classification (3 marks):** Use the Mermaid taxonomy given in Section 4.1. Briefly list:
- **Hydraulic motors:** gear, vane, piston
- **Pneumatic motors:** vane, piston, turbine
- **Limited-rotation:** rack-and-pinion, limited-angle vane

**Unbalanced vane motor (4 marks):**
- A cylindrical **rotor** is mounted *eccentrically* inside a circular **stator** (offset by eccentricity $e$).
- **Sliding vanes** fit in slots cut in the rotor; springs (or hydraulic pressure) keep the vane tips sealed against the stator bore.
- **Two ports** — inlet (high pressure) and outlet (low pressure) — are positioned such that one half of the stator always communicates with the inlet and the other half with the outlet.
- On the inlet side, the trapped volume *expands* as the rotor turns (stator bore is farther from rotor centre), so the fluid does *positive* work on the vane, producing torque.
- On the outlet side, the trapped volume *shrinks*, expelling the spent fluid to the exhaust.
- The net result is a continuous unidirectional torque, and the rotor therefore turns at an angular speed determined by the input flow.

##### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Unit conversions:**
- $V_D = 50 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$
- $\Delta p = 140 \text{ bar} = 140 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.4 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$
- $N = 1450 \text{ rpm}$
- $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1450}{60} = 151.84 \text{ rad/s}$

**(i) Theoretical torque:**

$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi} = \frac{1.4 \times 10^7 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{2\pi} = \frac{700}{6.2832} = 111.41 \text{ N·m}$$

**(ii) Actual torque:**

$$T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m = 111.41 \times 0.92 = 102.50 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Theoretical flow rate:**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{V_D \cdot N}{60} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-5} \times 1450}{60} = 1.2083 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

In L/min: $Q_{th} = 1.2083 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times 1000 = 72.5 \text{ L/min}$

**(iv) Actual flow rate:**

$$Q_{act} = \frac{Q_{th}}{\eta_v} = \frac{1.2083 \times 10^{-3}}{0.96} = 1.2587 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

In L/min: $Q_{act} \approx 75.5 \text{ L/min}$

**(v) Power delivered to load:**

$$P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega = 102.50 \times 151.84 = 15\,563.6 \text{ W} \approx 15.56 \text{ kW}$$

**Cross-check:** $P_{out} = \eta_{overall} \cdot \Delta p \cdot Q_{act}$
$\eta_{overall} = 0.96 \times 0.92 = 0.8832$
$P_{out} = 0.8832 \times 1.4 \times 10^7 \times 1.2587 \times 10^{-3} = 15\,562.4 \text{ W}$ ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Set B]**

**(a)** Derive the *theoretical torque equation* and the *theoretical flow equation* of a hydraulic motor from first principles. State clearly the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic piston motor delivers an actual torque of $T_{act} = 250$ N·m at a shaft speed of $N = 950$ rpm when supplied with oil at $\Delta p = 175$ bar. The mechanical efficiency is $\eta_m = 0.90$ and the volumetric efficiency is $\eta_v = 0.94$. Determine:
- (i) Theoretical torque $T_{th}$
- (ii) Volumetric displacement $V_D$ in cm³/rev
- (iii) Theoretical flow $Q_{th}$ in L/min
- (iv) Actual flow $Q_{act}$ in L/min
- (v) Overall efficiency and input hydraulic power. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The motor is *positive displacement* (zero slip in the ideal case).
2. Working fluid is *incompressible* (valid for hydraulics, not pneumatics).
3. *No leakage* across clearances (volumetric efficiency = 1 in the ideal case).
4. *No friction* in bearings, seals, or vane/piston interfaces (mechanical efficiency = 1).
5. Steady-state, constant pressure drop.

**Derivation of torque equation (3 marks):**

Consider one revolution of the shaft. The volume of oil admitted is $V_D$. The hydraulic energy input per revolution is $W_{in} = \Delta p \cdot V_D$. This is converted (ideally) to mechanical work output $W_{out} = T_{th} \cdot 2\pi$. Equating and solving:

$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$$

**Derivation of flow equation (3 marks):**

In one revolution, $V_D$ m³ of fluid enters. If the shaft turns at $N$ rpm = $N/60$ rev/s, then volume per second = $V_D \times N/60$:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{V_D \cdot N}{60}$$

Equivalently, using $\omega$ in rad/s: $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot \omega / (2\pi)$.

##### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given:**
- $T_{act} = 250$ N·m, $N = 950$ rpm, $\Delta p = 175 \text{ bar} = 1.75 \times 10^7$ Pa
- $\eta_m = 0.90$, $\eta_v = 0.94$
- $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 950}{60} = 99.48$ rad/s

**(i) Theoretical torque:**

$$T_{th} = \frac{T_{act}}{\eta_m} = \frac{250}{0.90} = 277.78 \text{ N·m}$$

**(ii) Volumetric displacement:**

$$V_D = \frac{2\pi \cdot T_{th}}{\Delta p} = \frac{2\pi \times 277.78}{1.75 \times 10^7} = 9.97 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$$

$$V_D = 9.97 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^6 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 99.7 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$$

**(iii) Theoretical flow:**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{V_D \cdot N}{60} = \frac{9.97 \times 10^{-5} \times 950}{60} = 1.579 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 94.74 \text{ L/min}$$

**(iv) Actual flow:**

$$Q_{act} = \frac{Q_{th}}{\eta_v} = \frac{94.74}{0.94} = 100.79 \text{ L/min}$$

**(v) Overall efficiency and input hydraulic power:**

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.94 \times 0.90 = 0.846$$

$$P_{in} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{act} = 1.75 \times 10^7 \times \frac{100.79 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 29\,398 \text{ W} \approx 29.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Cross-check:** $P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega = 250 \times 99.48 = 24\,870$ W
$\eta_{overall} = P_{out}/P_{in} = 24\,870 / 29\,398 = 0.846$ ✓

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Inverting efficiency formulas** — Writing $Q_{th} = Q_{act} \cdot \eta_v$ instead of $Q_{th} = Q_{act} / \eta_v$ (i.e., confusing "loss" with "gain"). Always remember: *leakage means the pump must supply MORE than theoretical.*
> 2. **Unit errors on displacement** — Using cm³/rev in the torque equation without converting to m³/rev. One unit slip = 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Forgetting the $2\pi$ factor** — In the torque equation, the $2\pi$ is the *radians per revolution* conversion. Skipping it gives an answer 6.28× too large.
> 4. **Conflating actual vs theoretical angular speed** — In a real motor, the *shaft* speed equals the *theoretical* speed (set by flow), not the actual flow divided by displacement. The actual flow is what the *pump must deliver*; the actual shaft speed is determined by the *useful* flow.
> 5. **No sketch in part (a) 7-mark questions** — A vane motor without a labelled eccentricity/stator/rotor/vane sketch will lose 2–3 marks even if the description is perfect. *Always draw.*
> 6. **Pressure unit confusion** — 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa, NOT $10^6$ Pa. This is the most common numerical error in KTU scripts.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — must-know bullet points:**

- A **fluid power rotary actuator** converts pressure energy of a fluid into *rotary* mechanical motion (torque + angular speed).
- **Three master equations** (memorize them in sleep):
  - $T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta p \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$
  - $Q_{th} = \dfrac{V_D \cdot N}{60}$
  - $P_{th} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{th} = T_{th} \cdot \omega$
- **Efficiency is asymmetric**: $Q_{act} = Q_{th} / \eta_v$ (more flow needed) and $T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m$ (less torque delivered).
- **$\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$** is the *only* place where the two efficiencies multiply.
- **Classification:** Hydraulic (gear, vane, piston) | Pneumatic (vane, piston, turbine) | Limited-rotation (rack-pinion, limited-angle vane).
- **Vane motor eccentricity** $e$ is the *physical reason* a vane motor produces torque: expanding volume on inlet side, contracting on outlet side.
- **Piston motors** come in *axial*, *radial*, and *bent-axis* variants — axial is the most common in industry.
- **Industrial network context**: the rotary actuator is the *field-level mechanical follower*, commanded by a servo/proportional valve, which is in turn commanded by a PLC over a fieldbus (Profinet/EtherCAT/CANopen). Sensor feedback closes the loop.
- **Numerical problem-solving flow**: (1) Convert all units to SI (Pa, m³/s, m³/rev, rad/s). (2) Compute $T_{th}$ and $Q_{th}$ from the *master* equations. (3) Apply efficiencies to get actual values. (4) Compute power using either side (hydraulic or mechanical) and cross-check.
- **Power conversion shortcuts** to remember: $1 \text{ kW} = 10^3 \text{ W}$, $1 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.

> **End of Module 1 — Fluid Power Rotary Actuators Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Chain Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear Chain Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Industrial Networks**, a **Linear Chain Actuator** is defined as an electromechanical, pneumatic, or hydraulic device that converts a control signal received over an industrial communication network (e.g., Fieldbus, Profibus, DeviceNet, Industrial Ethernet) into a **linear (straight-line) mechanical motion**, producing a pushing, pulling, lifting, or positioning force along a single axis.

The term **"Chain"** refers to the sequential signal flow in an industrial control chain:

$$
\text{Sensor} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Controller (PLC / DCS)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Network (Bus)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Linear Actuator} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Process Variable}
$$

A Linear Chain Actuator is, therefore, the **terminal "actuating" node** of a closed-loop or open-loop industrial network chain. It receives digital/analog commands via networked protocols and translates them into precise **positional displacement** along a linear axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Linear Chain Actuators are studied under *Module 1 – Fundamentals of Industrial Automation Hardware* because they form the **physical output interface** of every networked automation pyramid — from Field Level (sensors/actuators) to Control Level (PLCs/SCADA).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Robotic Arm of a Factory"

Imagine a **surgical robotic arm** in a hospital. The surgeon (analogous to the **Controller/PLC**) decides how the arm should move. The instruction travels through a **cable** (analogous to the **Industrial Network / Fieldbus**) and reaches the **end-effector motor** (analogous to the **Linear Actuator**), which pushes or pulls a needle in a perfectly straight line.

- The **surgeon's hand** = PLC control logic.
- The **cable carrying the signal** = industrial bus (Profibus, Modbus, EtherCAT).
- The **needle pusher** = the **Linear Chain Actuator**.
- The **patient's tissue** = the **process load**.

A **Linear Chain Actuator** does exactly this — but in factories, oil rigs, packaging lines, and CNC machines — translating networked electrical signals into **clean, repeatable, straight-line mechanical motion**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | Unit |
|:------|:------:|:------|:------|
| Stroke Length | $L_s$ | **0.05 – 2.0** | m |
| Thrust / Push Force | $F$ | **100 – 100,000** | N |
| Linear Velocity | $v$ | **0.001 – 1.5** | m/s |
| Positioning Resolution | $r$ | **0.01 – 0.1** | mm |
| Operating Pressure (Hydraulic) | $P$ | **70 – 350** (typically **1.4 × 10⁷** Pa for heavy industry) | Pa |
| Air Pressure (Pneumatic) | $P_{air}$ | **4 – 8** (typically **6 × 10⁵** Pa) | bar |
| Network Cycle Time | $T_c$ | **0.1 – 10** | ms |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 examinations, remember that **stroke length $L_s$** is the maximum achievable linear displacement, and **thrust force $F$** is the maximum load the actuator can move against without stalling.

---

## 1.4 Classification Overview (Foundation for KTU Questions)

Linear Chain Actuators are classified along **three axes**:

1. **Energy Source Axis**
   - **Pneumatic** (compressed air driven)
   - **Hydraulic** (oil/fluid driven)
   - **Electric** (motor + lead screw / ball screw)
   - **Piezoelectric** (nano-positioning, advanced applications)

2. **Motion Conversion Axis**
   - **Direct Linear** (e.g., solenoid, voice coil)
   - **Rotary-to-Linear** (motor + screw / rack-pinion)

3. **Network Integration Axis**
   - **Hard-wired analog** (4–20 mA, 0–10 V)
   - **Digital Fieldbus** (Profibus, CANopen, DeviceNet)
   - **Industrial Ethernet** (EtherCAT, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Linear Chain Actuator

A Linear Chain Actuator operates through a **signal-to-force chain** that mirrors the classic industrial control hierarchy. The breakdown proceeds in **four logical stages**:

### Stage 1 — Command Signal Generation
The supervisory controller (PLC, DCS, or Industrial PC) generates a **setpoint command** $x_{sp}(t)$, representing the **desired linear position** of the actuator. In networked systems, this setpoint is encoded into a **digital telegram** and broadcast via the industrial bus.

### Stage 2 — Network Transmission
The telegram travels over a deterministic fieldbus (e.g., PROFINET IRT with cycle time **≤ 1 ms**). The actuator's network interface decodes the telegram into a **drive command**, typically:
- An analog reference voltage ($V_{ref} = 0\text{–}10$ V), or
- A digital position setpoint in engineering units (mm or µm).

### Stage 3 — Energy-to-Mechanical Conversion
The drive command energizes the actuator's prime mover:
- In a **pneumatic actuator**: solenoid valve admits compressed air → pressure differential pushes piston.
- In a **hydraulic actuator**: proportional valve meters oil flow → pressure drives cylinder.
- In an **electric actuator**: servo-drive modulates current to BLDC/stepper motor → rotary motion is converted to linear motion via a lead/ball screw.

### Stage 4 — Mechanical Output (Linear Displacement)
The mechanical element (piston rod, screw nut, or thrust rod) translates along the linear axis, producing measurable displacement $x(t)$ and force $F(t)$. A position sensor (LVDT, encoder, or magnetostrictive sensor) closes the **feedback loop**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **"Chain"** in *Linear Chain Actuator* emphasizes that the actuator is the **final link** in a deterministic signal chain: Sensor → Network → Controller → Actuator → Process. Loss or delay at any link degrades the entire chain.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Model of Linear Motion

For a **mass $m$** driven by a Linear Chain Actuator with applied force $F(t)$, viscous damping coefficient $b$, and spring stiffness $k$, Newton's Second Law yields:

$$
m \ddot{x}(t) + b \dot{x}(t) + k x(t) = F(t)
$$

Where:
- $x(t)$ = linear displacement (m)
- $\dot{x}(t)$ = linear velocity (m/s)
- $\ddot{x}(t)$ = linear acceleration (m/s²)
- $F(t)$ = applied thrust force (N)
- $b$ = viscous damping coefficient (N·s/m)
- $k$ = load stiffness (N/m)

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table — these formulas appear repeatedly in **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** KTU questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Description | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:---------------------|:------|
| 1 | Newton's Law (Linear Motion) | $m \ddot{x} + b \dot{x} + k x = F(t)$ | Mass–spring–damper model | SI (m, kg, N, s) |
| 2 | Pneumatic Cylinder Force | $F_{pneu} = P_{air} \cdot A_{piston} \cdot \eta_{mech}$ | $P_{air}$ = air pressure, $A$ = piston area, $\eta_{mech}$ = mech. efficiency | Pa, m², N |
| 3 | Hydraulic Cylinder Force | $F_{hyd} = P_{oil} \cdot A_{piston} - F_{friction}$ | $P_{oil}$ = oil pressure | Pa, N |
| 4 | Electric Screw Actuator Force | $F_{elec} = \dfrac{T_{motor} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \eta_{screw}}{L_{lead}}$ | $T_{motor}$ = torque, $L_{lead}$ = screw lead, $\eta_{screw}$ = screw efficiency | N·m, m, N |
| 5 | Linear Velocity from Screw | $v = \dfrac{\omega \cdot L_{lead}}{2\pi}$ | $\omega$ = motor angular speed | rad/s, m/s |
| 6 | Motor Torque to Thrust | $F = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot \eta \cdot T}{L_{lead}}$ | Combined conversion | N |
| 7 | Transfer Function (Position) | $\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{m s^2 + b s + k}$ | Laplace-domain | m/N |
| 8 | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Undamped natural freq | rad/s |
| 9 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2 \sqrt{k m}}$ | Dimensionless | — |
| 10 | Hydraulic Power | $P_{hyd} = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{\eta_{overall}}$ | Power required | W |
| 11 | Network Cycle Time | $T_c = \dfrac{N_{nodes} \cdot t_{bit}}{B}$ | $N_{nodes}$ = no. of actuators, $B$ = bus bandwidth | s |
| 12 | Pneumatic Air Consumption | $Q_{air} = A \cdot v \cdot \dfrac{P_{supply}}{P_{atm}}$ | Free air delivery | m³/s |
| 13 | Solenoid Force (Magnetic) | $F_{mag} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2 g^2}$ | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m | N |
| 14 | Closed-Loop Position Error | $e(t) = x_{sp}(t) - x(t)$ | Tracking error | m |
| 15 | PI Controller Output | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\, dt$ | Standard PI law | V |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Linear Chain Actuators form the **muscular backbone** of every modern automated industry:

- **Automotive Assembly Lines:** Robotic welding arms use linear actuators for door-panel positioning with sub-millimeter accuracy.
- **Oil & Gas:** Hydraulic linear actuators control downhole safety valves at depths exceeding **3,000 m**, communicating over Modbus/Profibus.
- **Pharmaceutical Packaging:** Electric linear actuators drive piston-filling nozzles with **±0.1 mL** precision, networked via EtherCAT.
- **CNC Machine Tools:** Ball-screw-driven linear actuators position tool heads with resolutions down to **1 µm**, controlled via PROFINET IRT.
- **Aerospace:** Piezoelectric linear actuators adjust turbine blade tips with **nanometer** precision for active vibration damping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 examinations, **application-based questions** often test whether you can map a real industrial scenario to the correct **actuator type + network protocol** combination.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transfer Function of a Linear Chain Actuator (Mass-Spring-Damper System)

We derive the Laplace-domain transfer function that maps **applied thrust force $F(t)$** to **output displacement $x(t)$** for a Linear Chain Actuator driving a generic mechanical load.

### Step 1 — Start from Newton's Second Law

For a rigid body of mass $m$ moving linearly under applied force $F(t)$, opposed by viscous friction $b\dot{x}$ and spring restoring force $kx$:

$$
m \ddot{x}(t) = F(t) - b \dot{x}(t) - k x(t)
$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange to Standard Form

Bring all output-side terms to the LHS:

$$
m \ddot{x}(t) + b \dot{x}(t) + k x(t) = F(t)
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Laplace Transform (Zero Initial Conditions)

Using the standard Laplace identities:
- $\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}(t)\} = s X(s)$
- $\mathcal{L}\{\ddot{x}(t)\} = s^2 X(s)$

Substitute:

$$
m s^2 X(s) + b s X(s) + k X(s) = F(s)
$$

### Step 4 — Factor Out $X(s)$

$$
X(s) \cdot \left[ m s^2 + b s + k \right] = F(s)
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Transfer Function $X(s)/F(s)$

$$
\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{m s^2 + b s + k}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Standard Second-Order Form

Divide numerator and denominator by $m$:

$$
\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{m}}{s^2 + \dfrac{b}{m} s + \dfrac{k}{m}}
$$

Define the **natural frequency** $\omega_n$ and **damping ratio** $\zeta$:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}
\qquad
\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{k\,m}}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{k}}{\left( \dfrac{s}{\omega_n} \right)^2 + \dfrac{2\zeta}{\omega_n} s + 1}
$$

This is the **canonical second-order transfer function** of a linear chain actuator's mechanical load, used universally in KTU control-system analysis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thrust Force of an Electric Screw-Driven Linear Actuator

An electric linear actuator converts motor torque $T_{motor}$ into linear thrust via a lead screw with lead $L_{lead}$ (mm/rev) and efficiency $\eta_{screw}$.

### Step 1 — Mechanical Work Equivalence

In one revolution, the screw advances the nut by $L_{lead}$ metres. The work done by thrust force $F$ equals motor torque work:

$$
F \cdot L_{lead} = T_{motor} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \eta_{screw}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Thrust Force $F$

$$
F = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot \eta_{screw} \cdot T_{motor}}{L_{lead}}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Linear Velocity

If motor angular speed is $\omega$ (rad/s), one revolution takes $\dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$ seconds, advancing $L_{lead}$ per revolution:

$$
v = \dfrac{\omega \cdot L_{lead}}{2\pi}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:** $T_{motor} = 5$ N·m, $L_{lead} = 5$ mm $= 0.005$ m, $\eta_{screw} = 0.9$, $\omega = 300$ rad/s.

**Step 4a — Compute Thrust:**

$$
F = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 0.9 \cdot 5}{0.005} = \dfrac{28.274}{0.005} = 5654.87 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F \approx 5.65 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 4b — Compute Linear Velocity:**

$$
v = \dfrac{300 \cdot 0.005}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1.5}{6.2832} \approx 0.2387 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{v \approx 238.7 \text{ mm/s}}
$$

**Step 4c — Compute Output Power:**

$$
P_{out} = F \cdot v = 5654.87 \cdot 0.2387 \approx 1349.8 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{out} \approx 1.35 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pneumatic Cylinder Force

A pneumatic linear actuator generates thrust from compressed air acting on a piston of effective area $A_{piston}$.

### Step 1 — Pressure-Area Force

Theoretical force generated by gauge pressure $P_{air}$:

$$
F_{theoretical} = P_{air} \cdot A_{piston}
$$

### Step 2 — Account for Mechanical Efficiency

Real cylinders lose force to friction, seal drag, and rod-side pressure reduction:

$$
F_{actual} = P_{air} \cdot A_{piston} \cdot \eta_{mech}
$$

Typical $\eta_{mech} \in [0.7, 0.95]$.

### Step 3 — Numerical Example

**Given:** $P_{air} = 6 \times 10^{5}$ Pa (6 bar), bore diameter $D = 80$ mm, rod diameter $d = 30$ mm, $\eta_{mech} = 0.85$.

**Step 3a — Effective Areas:**

$$
A_{piston} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{rod} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.030)^2 = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{effective,push} = A_{piston} - A_{rod} = 4.320 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3b — Push Force:**

$$
F_{push} = 6 \times 10^{5} \cdot 4.320 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.85
$$

$$
F_{push} = 600000 \cdot 0.004320 \cdot 0.85 \approx 2203.2 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{push} \approx 2.2 \text{ kN}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Industrial Network Protocols for Linear Actuators

| Protocol | Cycle Time | Typical Application | KTU Exam Relevance |
|:---------|:----------:|:--------------------|:-------------------|
| **Profibus DP** | 1–10 ms | Discrete I/O + actuator positioning | High |
| **PROFINET IRT** | ≤ 1 ms | High-speed servo linear actuators | Very High |
| **EtherCAT** | ≤ 0.1 ms | Ultra-fast multi-axis positioning | Very High |
| **DeviceNet** | 2–5 ms | Mid-tier pneumatic actuator banks | Medium |
| **CANopen** | 1–5 ms | Embedded motion control | High |
| **Modbus RTU** | 10–50 ms | Slow-positioning hydraulic valves | Medium |
| **Industrial Ethernet (TCP/IP)** | 10–100 ms | SCADA-level supervisory control | Low–Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to select a network protocol for a linear chain actuator application, always justify based on **cycle time**, **determinism**, and **number of actuators per segment**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Linear Chain Actuator in a Networked Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Setpoint Generator PLC"] --> B["Industrial Network Bus Profibus PROFINET EtherCAT"]
    B --> C["Network Interface Decoder Actuator Slave Node"]
    C --> D["Drive Amplifier Servo Drive or Proportional Valve"]
    D --> E["Prime Mover BLDC Motor or Pneumatic Piston"]
    E --> F["Mechanical Conversion Lead Screw or Rack Pinion"]
    F --> G["Linear Output Thrust Rod or Piston Rod"]
    G --> H["Process Load Workpiece or Valve Stem"]
    H --> I["Position Feedback Sensor LVDT or Encoder"]
    I --> J["Closed Loop Comparator Error Computation"]
    J --> A
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**

- The PLC generates a **setpoint** (e.g., position = 250 mm).
- The setpoint travels over the **industrial network bus** (Profibus / PROFINET / EtherCAT).
- The actuator's slave-node interface **decodes** the telegram into an analog/digital drive command.
- The **drive amplifier** modulates energy to the prime mover.
- The **mechanical converter** (lead screw) translates rotary motion to **linear motion**.
- The **position sensor** closes the feedback loop back to the PLC.

---

## 4.2 Architecture Diagram — Hierarchical Integration of Linear Chain Actuators

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["Level 4 Enterprise ERP and Manufacturing Execution"]
    L3["Level 3 Supervisory SCADA and HMI Stations"]
    L2["Level 2 Control PLC DCS and Industrial PC"]
    L1["Level 1 Field Devices Sensors and Linear Chain Actuators"]
    L0["Level 0 Physical Process Mechanical Load"]

    L4 -- Ethernet TCP IP --> L3
    L3 -- Industrial Ethernet PROFINET --> L2
    L2 -- Fieldbus Profibus CANopen --> L1
    L1 -- Direct Mechanical Coupling --> L0

    subgraph fieldLayer [Field Level Network Segment]
        direction LR
        F1["Sensor 1 Temperature"] --> busA["Fieldbus Segment"]
        F2["Sensor 2 Pressure"] --> busA
        F3["Linear Actuator 1"] --> busA
        F4["Linear Actuator 2"] --> busA
        busA --> L2
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Classification Tree — Types of Linear Chain Actuators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["Linear Chain Actuators"]
    root --> A1["Pneumatic Type"]
    root --> A2["Hydraulic Type"]
    root --> A3["Electric Type"]
    root --> A4["Piezoelectric Type"]

    A1 --> A1a["Single Acting Cylinder"]
    A1 --> A1b["Double Acting Cylinder"]
    A1 --> A1c["Rodless Cylinder"]

    A2 --> A2a["Single Stage Telescopic"]
    A2 --> A2b["Double Acting Hydraulic Cylinder"]
    A2 --> A2c["Servo Proportional Hydraulic"]

    A3 --> A3a["Lead Screw Driven"]
    A3 --> A3b["Ball Screw Driven"]
    A3 --> A3c["Linear Motor Direct Drive"]
    A3 --> A3d["Rack and Pinion Driven"]

    A4 --> A4a["Stack Type Piezo"]
    A4 --> A4b["Tube Type Piezo"]
    A4 --> A4c["Walk Type Piezo Actuator"]
```

---

## 4.4 Signal Flow Sequence — Position Command from PLC to Actuator

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PLC as PLC Controller
    participant BUS as Industrial Fieldbus
    participant DRV as Actuator Drive
    participant ACT as Linear Actuator
    participant SEN as Position Sensor
    participant PROC as Process Load

    PLC->>BUS: Send Position Setpoint xsp equals 250 mm
    BUS->>DRV: Decode Telegram into Drive Command
    DRV->>ACT: Energize Prime Mover Motor or Valve
    ACT->>PROC: Apply Linear Thrust and Move Load
    PROC->>SEN: Displacement Reflected to Sensor
    SEN->>BUS: Encode Actual Position x equals 248 mm
    BUS->>PLC: Return Feedback Telegram
    PLC->>PLC: Compute Error e equals 2 mm
    PLC->>BUS: Issue Corrected Command
```

> [!NOTE]
> This sequence diagram captures the **closed-loop deterministic cycle** typical of PROFINET IRT and EtherCAT-based linear actuator networks, where cycle times as low as **0.1 ms** are achievable.
