# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** Define a Linear Chain Actuator and explain its role in an industrial network. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Linear Chain Actuator** is a field-level device that converts a control signal received over an industrial communication network into a **linear mechanical motion** along a single axis, producing thrust or positioning force.

Its role in an industrial network is as the **final actuating node** in the control chain:

$$
\text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Network} \rightarrow \text{Controller (PLC)} \rightarrow \text{Network} \rightarrow \text{Linear Chain Actuator} \rightarrow \text{Process}
$$

It serves as the **physical interface between the cyber layer (network) and the physical process**, executing commands such as valve opening, rod extension, or load positioning. **(3 marks — Definition 1, Chain Diagram 1, Industrial Role 1)**

---

### **Question 2:** List any four types of linear actuators based on energy source. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The four types of linear actuators based on energy source are:

1. **Pneumatic Linear Actuator** — uses compressed air (typically 4–8 bar) acting on a piston to generate linear motion.
2. **Hydraulic Linear Actuator** — uses pressurized oil (typically 70–350 bar) for high-thrust applications.
3. **Electric Linear Actuator** — uses an electric motor (BLDC, stepper, servo) coupled to a lead/ball screw for precise positioning.
4. **Piezoelectric Linear Actuator** — uses inverse piezoelectric effect of ceramic stacks for nano-scale positioning.

---

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** *[Answer Question A OR Question B]* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a Linear Chain Actuator integrated into an industrial network. Describe the function of **each block** in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has bore diameter $D = 100$ mm, rod diameter $d = 40$ mm, supply pressure $P = 6 \times 10^5$ Pa, and mechanical efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. Calculate the **extension (push) force**, **retraction (pull) force**, and **air consumption** at a piston velocity $v = 0.2$ m/s. Assume atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

Refer to the functional block diagram in Section 4.1 above. The block sequence is:

$$
\text{PLC Setpoint} \rightarrow \text{Network Bus} \rightarrow \text{Network Decoder} \rightarrow \text{Drive Amplifier} \rightarrow \text{Prime Mover} \rightarrow \text{Mechanical Converter} \rightarrow \text{Linear Output Rod} \rightarrow \text{Process Load}
$$

**Explanation of Each Block (5 marks — 1 mark per major block):**

1. **PLC Setpoint Generator:** Produces the desired position setpoint $x_{sp}(t)$ in engineering units.
2. **Industrial Network Bus:** Transmits the setpoint as a deterministic telegram (Profibus, PROFINET, EtherCAT) within a guaranteed cycle time.
3. **Network Interface Decoder:** Converts the digital telegram into an analog reference signal ($V_{ref}$ or current $I_{ref}$) accepted by the drive.
4. **Drive Amplifier:** Modulates energy (current, voltage, hydraulic flow, or pneumatic flow) to the prime mover based on the reference signal.
5. **Prime Mover:** Converts electrical/hydraulic/pneumatic energy into mechanical energy (motor torque or piston force).
6. **Mechanical Converter:** Translates rotary motion to linear motion via lead screw, ball screw, or rack-pinion.
7. **Linear Output Rod + Position Sensor:** Delivers thrust to the load; sensor (LVDT/encoder) feeds back actual position to the controller.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m
- $d = 40$ mm $= 0.040$ m
- $P_{supply} = 6 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $\eta_{mech} = 0.85$
- $v = 0.2$ m/s
- $P_{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa

**Step 1 — Compute Piston Areas (1 mark):**

$$
A_{piston} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{rod} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.040)^2 = 1.257 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Extension (Push) Force (2 marks):**

Gauge pressure on full piston face:

$$
F_{push} = \eta_{mech} \cdot (P_{supply} - P_{atm}) \cdot A_{piston}
$$

$$
F_{push} = 0.85 \cdot (6 \times 10^5 - 1.013 \times 10^5) \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
F_{push} = 0.85 \cdot 4.987 \times 10^5 \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
F_{push} = 0.85 \cdot 3916.4 \approx 3328.9 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{push} \approx 3.33 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 3 — Retraction (Pull) Force (2 marks):**

Effective area on rod side:

$$
A_{eff,retract} = A_{piston} - A_{rod} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} - 1.257 \times 10^{-3} = 6.597 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
F_{pull} = 0.85 \cdot (6 \times 10^5 - 1.013 \times 10^5) \cdot 6.597 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
F_{pull} = 0.85 \cdot 4.987 \times 10^5 \cdot 6.597 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
F_{pull} = 0.85 \cdot 3289.8 \approx 2796.3 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{pull} \approx 2.80 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 4 — Free Air Consumption (2 marks):**

Free air delivery (FAD) is computed by converting compressed-air volume to atmospheric-equivalent volume:

$$
Q_{FAD} = A_{piston} \cdot v \cdot \dfrac{P_{supply}}{P_{atm}}
$$

$$
Q_{FAD} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{6 \times 10^5}{1.013 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
Q_{FAD} = 1.571 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 5.923 \approx 9.305 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{FAD} \approx 9.30 \text{ L/s}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** *[Alternative choice to Question A]* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the **transfer function** of a Linear Chain Actuator driving a mass-spring-damper load. Express the result in standard second-order form and define **natural frequency** and **damping ratio**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An electric linear actuator uses a **BLDC motor** with rated torque $T = 8$ N·m and a **ball screw** with lead $L = 4$ mm and efficiency $\eta = 0.92$. The motor runs at $\omega = 250$ rad/s. Calculate the **maximum thrust force**, **linear velocity**, and **output mechanical power**. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Starting from Newton's Second Law:

$$
m \ddot{x}(t) + b \dot{x}(t) + k x(t) = F(t)
$$

Apply Laplace transform with zero initial conditions:

$$
(m s^2 + b s + k) X(s) = F(s)
$$

Transfer function:

$$
\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{m s^2 + b s + k}
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $m$:

$$
\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{m}}{s^2 + \dfrac{b}{m} s + \dfrac{k}{m}}
$$

Define:
- Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ rad/s
- Damping ratio: $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{km}}$

Final standard form:

$$
\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{k}}{\left( \dfrac{s}{\omega_n} \right)^2 + \dfrac{2\zeta s}{\omega_n} + 1}
$$

**Interpretation (2 marks):**
- $\omega_n$ is the **undamped oscillation frequency** of the actuator-load system.
- $\zeta < 1$: underdamped; $\zeta = 1$: critically damped; $\zeta > 1$: overdamped.
- For KTU industrial positioning applications, **$\zeta \approx 0.7$** is preferred for fast settling without overshoot.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $T = 8$ N·m
- $L = 4$ mm $= 0.004$ m
- $\eta = 0.92$
- $\omega = 250$ rad/s

**Step 1 — Maximum Thrust Force (3 marks):**

$$
F = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot \eta \cdot T}{L} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 0.92 \cdot 8}{0.004}
$$

$$
F = \dfrac{46.246}{0.004} = 11561.5 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F \approx 11.56 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear Velocity (2 marks):**

$$
v = \dfrac{\omega \cdot L}{2\pi} = \dfrac{250 \cdot 0.004}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1.0}{6.2832} \approx 0.1592 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{v \approx 159.2 \text{ mm/s}}
$$

**Step 3 — Output Power (2 marks):**

$$
P_{out} = F \cdot v = 11561.5 \cdot 0.1592 \approx 1840.6 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{out} \approx 1.84 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $P_{atm}$** in pneumatic force calculations — KTU specifically tests gauge vs absolute pressure. Always use **gauge pressure** for actuator thrust.
> 2. **Mixing up effective areas** — Extension uses full $A_{piston}$; retraction uses $A_{piston} - A_{rod}$.
> 3. **Neglecting mechanical efficiency $\eta_{mech}$** — Real actuators are not 100% efficient; omitting $\eta$ costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Wrong unit conversion** — Screw lead must be in **metres** before computing thrust in Newtons. Common mistake: using mm directly.
> 5. **Confusing transfer function derivation** — Students often forget to divide by $m$ to obtain the standard form, missing the **1 mark** for natural frequency and damping ratio definitions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A Linear Chain Actuator is the **terminal actuating node** in a networked industrial control chain that converts network commands into linear mechanical displacement.
- **Three Classification Axes:** Energy source (Pneumatic / Hydraulic / Electric / Piezo), motion conversion (direct vs rotary-to-linear), and network integration (analog / fieldbus / Industrial Ethernet).
- **Core Formula:** Newton's Second Law for linear motion — $m \ddot{x} + b \dot{x} + kx = F(t)$.
- **Transfer Function:** $\dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{m s^2 + b s + k}$ — **memorize** in standard second-order form.
- **Natural Frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$; **Damping Ratio:** $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{km}}$.
- **Electric Screw Force:** $F = \dfrac{2\pi \eta T}{L_{lead}}$ — convert $L_{lead}$ to metres.
- **Pneumatic Push Force:** $F_{push} = \eta \cdot (P_{supply} - P_{atm}) \cdot A_{piston}$ — always use **gauge pressure**.
- **Pneumatic Pull Force:** $F_{pull} = \eta \cdot (P_{supply} - P_{atm}) \cdot (A_{piston} - A_{rod})$.
- **Free Air Consumption:** $Q_{FAD} = A \cdot v \cdot \dfrac{P_{supply}}{P_{atm}}$.
- **Network Protocol Selection:** EtherCAT / PROFINET IRT for high-speed servo positioning; Profibus / CANopen for mid-tier discrete I/O; Modbus for slow supervisory control.
- **Standard Cycle Times:** EtherCAT ≤ 0.1 ms; PROFINET IRT ≤ 1 ms; Profibus DP 1–10 ms; Modbus 10–50 ms.
- **Typical Stroke Lengths:** $L_s \in [0.05, 2.0]$ m.
- **Typical Thrust Range:** $F \in [100, 100{,}000]$ N.
- **KTU Frequently Tested Topics:** Transfer function derivation (Part B), pneumatic force with rod-side area subtraction (Part A & B), protocol selection justification (Part A).
- **Always include:** Units, mechanical efficiency $\eta$, and gauge pressure in numerical answers.
- **Industrial Mapping:** Pneumatic → food/packaging; Hydraulic → heavy industry/oil-gas; Electric → precision/CNC; Piezo → nano-positioning/semiconductor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Manual Linear Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Manual Linear Actuator** is a purely mechanical, hand-operated power transmission device that converts the **rotational input motion** applied by an operator on a handwheel (or crank) into a precisely controlled **linear (translational) output motion**, which is then delivered through a thrust rod or stem to actuate a final control element such as a valve, damper, or gate.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** industrial networks syllabus, the manual linear actuator represents the foundational, non-powered layer of the actuator hierarchy — preceding pneumatic, hydraulic, and electro-mechanical actuators. It forms the baseline reference against which all powered actuators in process industries are compared during performance benchmarking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Manual actuators are mandatory study items because they define the *zero-power fail-safe* category, are used as emergency backups for powered actuators, and remain the primary control mechanism in remote, hazardous, or explosion-proof zones (Zone 0/1) where electrical actuators are prohibited.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are turning the steering wheel of a heavy truck. The rotational motion of your hands is far away from the actual wheels that must turn left or right. A long shaft with gears (the **steering column**) carries this rotation deep into the vehicle's chassis. Similarly, in a factory, an operator stands on a floor **50 meters** away from a giant valve controlling steam flow. He turns a **handwheel** on his control panel. A **gear train** + **lead screw** combination translates that gentle turn into a powerful push/pull that drives the valve plug up or down.

The "actuator" is simply the *translator* between human muscle power and the heavy industrial load.

### 1.3 Core Performance Parameters

The performance of a manual linear actuator is defined by four **bold** standard metrics:

- **Thrust Force (F):** The linear output force, typically in **kN**, that the actuator can deliver against the process load.
- **Stroke Length (L):** The maximum linear travel, in **mm**, that the output rod can move.
- **Mechanical Advantage (MA):** The ratio of output thrust to input hand effort — dimensionless.
- **Handwheel Ratio (N):** The number of handwheel turns required to achieve full stroke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Standard:** Most industrial manual actuators are designed and tested per **ISO 5211** (mounting interface) and **EN 15714** (actuator terminology) standards. Torque handwheels are typically **250 N·m** to **1000 N·m** per operator.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

A manual linear actuator is built from five functional sub-systems. Understanding each block is mandatory for KTU 14-mark derivations.

1. **Input Stage — Handwheel (HW)**
   - The operator applies a tangential force $F_h$ on the rim of radius $r_h$.
   - This produces a **handwheel torque** $T_h = F_h \times r_h$.

2. **Speed Reduction Stage — Gear Train**
   - A bevel gear, spur gear, or **worm-and-wheel** arrangement multiplies torque and reduces rotational speed.
   - The **gear ratio** $G = \dfrac{N_{out}}{N_{in}}$ where $N$ is the number of teeth.

3. **Motion Conversion Stage — Lead Screw (LS)**
   - A lead screw is a helical ridge (thread) on a cylindrical shaft.
   - Each full rotation advances the nut (or shaft) by exactly one **lead distance** $L_{lead}$.
   - Linear velocity $v_{rod} = L_{lead} \times \omega_{screw}$ where $\omega_{screw}$ is in rev/sec.

4. **Output Stage — Thrust Rod / Stem**
   - The thrust rod is coupled to the process load (valve stem, damper blade).
   - The linear force delivered is the **thrust** $F_{th}$.

5. **Indication Stage — Position Indicator**
   - A mechanical dial or digital counter shows the percentage of stroke already traversed.
   - Critical for open-loop manual control of continuous processes.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Gear Train

Without a gear train, an operator's hand effort of ~**250 N** at a handwheel radius of **0.3 m** would yield a torque of only **75 N·m** — entirely insufficient to move a gate valve requiring **5000 N·m** of stem torque. The gear train provides the **mechanical leverage** to bridge this gap.

### 2.3 The "How" of Self-Locking (Critical Concept)

A unique property of worm-geared manual actuators is **self-locking**. A worm gear with a lead angle less than the angle of friction **cannot be back-driven** by the load. This means:

> The valve **stays exactly where it was left**, even under full line pressure — no drift, no creeping.

This is why worm-gear manual actuators dominate high-pressure process industries.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Handwheel Torque | $T_h = F_h \cdot r_h$ | $F_h$ = hand force (N), $r_h$ = wheel radius (m), $T_h$ (N·m) | Operator input sizing |
| Gear Train Output Torque | $T_{out} = T_h \cdot \eta_g \cdot G$ | $\eta_g$ = gear efficiency (0.85–0.95), $G$ = ratio | Torque amplification |
| Linear Velocity of Stem | $v = L_{lead} \cdot \omega$ | $L_{lead}$ (m/rev), $\omega$ (rev/s) | Stroke time calculation |
| Stroke Time | $t_{stroke} = \dfrac{L_{total}}{L_{lead} \cdot \omega_{in} \cdot G}$ | All terms as above | Manual operation timing |
| Mechanical Advantage | $MA = \dfrac{F_{th}}{F_h} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot r_h \cdot G \cdot \eta}{L_{lead}}$ | Dimensionless | Force amplification |
| Handwheel Turns for Full Stroke | $N = \dfrac{L_{total}}{L_{lead} \cdot G}$ | Unitless | Operator effort count |
| Efficiency of Lead Screw | $\eta_{ls} = \dfrac{\tan(\lambda)}{\tan(\lambda + \phi)}$ | $\lambda$ = lead angle, $\phi$ = friction angle | Power loss estimation |
| Self-Lock Condition | $\lambda < \phi$ | Both in radians or degrees | Determines if back-driving possible |
| Lead Angle | $\lambda = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L_{lead}}{\pi \cdot d_m}\right)$ | $d_m$ = mean screw diameter (m) | Thread geometry |
| Axial Thrust on Lead Screw | $F_{th} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot T_{screw} \cdot \eta_{ls}}{L_{lead}}$ | $T_{screw}$ (N·m), $L_{lead}$ (m) | Output force delivered to load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Note:** In the formula table above, all absolute-value bars have been rendered using the LaTeX `\vert` symbol to prevent any markdown table parsing failure. Use `$\vert x \vert$` form, never `|x|`, inside KTU answer sheets when typing tables.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Manual linear actuators are the unsung heroes of:

- **Oil & Gas:** Wellhead Christmas tree valves on offshore platforms.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler feedwater isolation valves in non-energized states.
- **Shipbuilding:** Ballast tank valve control (fail-safe mandatory by IMO).
- **Chemical Plants:** Emergency shut-off (ESD) valves in explosion-proof zones.
- **Water Treatment:** Large sluice gates and penstocks on dams.

Their enduring relevance comes from one unbeatable advantage: **they need no electricity, no compressed air, and no hydraulic fluid — they cannot fail due to power loss.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mechanical Advantage of a Manual Linear Actuator

**Problem Statement:** Derive an expression for the mechanical advantage (MA) of a manual linear actuator consisting of a handwheel, gear train of ratio $G$ and efficiency $\eta_g$, and a lead screw of lead $L_{lead}$ and efficiency $\eta_{ls}$.

**Step 1 — Input Work Done by Operator per Revolution**

The operator applies a tangential force $F_h$ at radius $r_h$ and rotates the handwheel through one full revolution (angle $2\pi$).

$$
W_{in} = F_h \cdot (2\pi \cdot r_h) = 2\pi \cdot F_h \cdot r_h
$$

The work input is the product of force and the circular path length traced by the hand.

**Step 2 — Output Work Delivered to the Load**

During this same one revolution, the output rod advances linearly by a distance $d$ given by:

$$
d = \frac{L_{lead}}{G}
$$

Here, the lead screw produces $L_{lead}$ of linear motion per revolution, but the gear train reduces the input rotation by factor $G$. The useful work output is:

$$
W_{out} = F_{th} \cdot d = F_{th} \cdot \frac{L_{lead}}{G}
$$

The thrust force $F_{th}$ is the load force that the actuator overcomes.

**Step 3 — Equate with Overall Efficiency**

The overall efficiency $\eta$ of the actuator (gear train + lead screw combined) is:

$$
\eta = \eta_g \cdot \eta_{ls} = \frac{W_{out}}{W_{in}}
$$

Substituting the expressions for $W_{in}$ and $W_{out}$:

$$
\eta = \frac{F_{th} \cdot \dfrac{L_{lead}}{G}}{2\pi \cdot F_h \cdot r_h}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange for Mechanical Advantage**

$$
MA = \frac{F_{th}}{F_h} = \frac{2\pi \cdot r_h \cdot G \cdot \eta}{L_{lead}}
$$

This is the canonical KTU formula for manual actuator force amplification.

**Step 5 — Numerical Verification (Sanity Check)**

Take: $r_h = 0.3$ m, $G = 40$, $\eta = 0.7$, $L_{lead} = 0.01$ m (10 mm).

$$
MA = \frac{2\pi \cdot 0.3 \cdot 40 \cdot 0.7}{0.01} = \frac{52.78}{0.01} = 5278
$$

So an operator pulling with **100 N** can deliver **527.8 kN** of thrust. This is consistent with real industrial ratings of **500 kN–1000 kN** for large gate valves.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Self-Locking Condition of a Worm-Driven Lead Screw

**Problem Statement:** Show that a lead screw is self-locking if and only if its lead angle $\lambda$ is less than the friction angle $\phi$.

**Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram of the Screw Block**

Consider a square-threaded screw with mean diameter $d_m$, lead $L_{lead}$, and a normal load $W$ acting on the thread helix. The thread helix unwraps into an inclined plane of angle $\lambda$ (the lead angle) and length $\pi d_m$.

**Step 2 — Forces on the Inclined Plane**

The horizontal force $P$ required to push the block *up* the incline (raising the load) is given by the classic inclined-plane equilibrium:

$$
P_{raise} = W \cdot \tan(\lambda + \phi)
$$

The horizontal force $P$ required to *lower* the block (allowing the load to descend) is:

$$
P_{lower} = W \cdot \tan(\lambda - \phi)
$$

**Step 3 — Self-Locking Test**

For self-locking, the screw must **not back-drive** even when the horizontal force $P = 0$ (i.e., the load alone is trying to push the screw back). Self-locking requires:

$$
P_{lower} \leq 0
$$

$$
W \cdot \tan(\lambda - \phi) \leq 0
$$

For a positive downward load $W > 0$, this simplifies to:

$$
\tan(\lambda - \phi) \leq 0
$$

The tangent is negative only when the angle is negative, i.e., when:

$$
\lambda - \phi < 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda < \phi}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation**

When the lead angle is shallower than the friction angle, the microscopic teeth engagement of the thread creates more friction than the gravitational/load-driven tangential component along the helix. The load simply cannot spin the screw backwards — it locks in place.

**Step 5 — Design Implication**

For manual gate valve actuators, designers typically choose $\lambda$ between **2° and 4°**, while the friction angle $\phi$ for steel-on-steel lubricated threads is around **5° to 6°**. The conservative margin ensures reliable self-locking even after years of wear.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Spur-Gear Manual Actuator | Bevel-Gear Manual Actuator | Worm-Gear Manual Actuator |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Efficiency | High (**90–95%**) | Medium (**85–90%**) | Low (**50–70%**) |
| Self-Locking | **No** | **No** | **Yes** (intrinsic) |
| Typical Gear Ratio | 4:1 to 10:1 | 5:1 to 20:1 | 30:1 to 100:1 |
| Back-Drivable | Yes (load can drop) | Yes | **No** |
| Compactness | Moderate | High (right-angle drive) | High |
| Best Use | Small quarter-turn valves | Quarter-turn with offset mounting | **Multi-turn gate/globe valves** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Operational Architecture of a Manual Linear Actuator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Hand Force Fh] --> B[Handwheel Radius rh]
    B --> C[Handwheel Torque Th = Fh x rh]
    C --> D[Gear Train Reduction G]
    D --> E[Screw Torque Ts = Th x G x eta_g]
    E --> F[Lead Screw Helix]
    F --> G[Linear Thrust Force Fth]
    G --> H[Thrust Rod and Stem]
    H --> I[Process Load - Valve or Damper]
    I --> J[Position Indicator Dial]
    J --> K[Visual Feedback to Operator]
    K --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_STAGE
        A1[Handwheel Assembly]
        A2[Bearing and Shaft Support]
    end
    subgraph TRANSMISSION_STAGE
        B1[Worm or Bevel Gear Pair]
        B2[Spur Gear Reducer]
        B3[Lead Screw and Nut]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT_STAGE
        C1[Thrust Bearing]
        C2[Linear Thrust Rod]
        C3[Coupling Flange ISO 5211]
    end
    subgraph INDICATION_STAGE
        D1[Mechanical Counter]
        D2[Position Scale]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Operator Decision Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Turning: Rotate Handwheel CW or CCW
    Turning --> Reading: Observe Position Indicator
    Reading --> Adjust: Compare with Setpoint
    Adjust --> Turning: Continue or Reverse
    Adjust --> Locked: Target Position Reached
    Locked --> Idle: Release Handwheel
    Locked --> [*]: End of Stroke
    Turning --> Emergency_Stop: Load Exceeds Capacity
    Emergency_Stop --> Locked: Back off and Inspect
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Manual Actuator in an Industrial Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Control_Room
        CR1[Operator Console]
        CR2[Setpoint Input]
    end
    subgraph Field_Level
        F1[Manual Linear Actuator]

        F3[Pipe with Process Fluid]
    end

        M1[Handwheel Input]
        M2[Gear Train]
        M3[Lead Screw and Nut Assembly]
        M4[Thrust Rod]
    end
```
