```mermaid
    CR2 -.->|Manual Command| M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 -.->|Process Response| CR1
    F2 -.->|Position Feedback| M3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The dotted arrows in the block diagram represent feedback and command signals, while the solid arrows show physical power flow. This is a common KTU diagram convention to distinguish between the information layer and the mechanical power layer in industrial actuator systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a manual linear actuator. List its three principal components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **manual linear actuator** is a purely mechanical, hand-operated device that converts rotational input from a handwheel into controlled linear motion for actuating a final control element such as a valve or damper (1 mark).

The three principal components are:

1. **Handwheel** — the input element where the operator applies force (1 mark).
2. **Gear train** — the speed-reduction stage that amplifies torque (0.5 mark).
3. **Lead screw and thrust rod** — the motion-conversion stage that produces linear output (0.5 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the self-locking condition of a lead screw used in a manual linear actuator. Why is this property important in valve control?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A lead screw is self-locking when its **lead angle $\lambda$ is less than the friction angle $\phi$** of the thread pair (2 marks).

This property is important in valve control because it ensures the valve **remains in its last commanded position** even when the operator releases the handwheel, even under full line pressure. This prevents accidental process upsets, eliminates the need for a separate brake, and provides intrinsic **fail-safe positioning** — a critical safety feature in process industries (1 mark).

---

### **PART B — Full Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]*

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the **working principle of a manual linear actuator** used for gate valve control. Explain the role of the gear train and the lead screw in the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manual linear actuator for a gate valve has a handwheel radius of **0.25 m**, a gear train ratio of **30:1** with efficiency **0.9**, and a lead screw of lead **8 mm** with efficiency **0.75**. The operator applies a sustained hand force of **120 N**. Calculate:
   (i) The mechanical advantage of the system. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The maximum thrust force delivered to the valve stem. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The number of handwheel turns required to fully open a valve with a stroke of **120 mm**. **(2 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

A manual linear actuator for gate valve control is a hand-operated mechanical device that produces the linear motion needed to raise or lower a gate valve's wedge or disc (1 mark).

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The system operates in five functional blocks, identical to the architecture shown in SECTION 4.2:

1. **Input Block (Handwheel):** The operator applies a tangential force on the handwheel rim, producing input torque $T_h = F_h \cdot r_h$.
2. **Torque Amplification Block (Gear Train):** The worm-and-wheel or bevel-gear pair multiplies the torque by the gear ratio $G$ with some efficiency loss, yielding screw torque $T_s = T_h \cdot G \cdot \eta_g$.
3. **Motion Conversion Block (Lead Screw):** The rotational motion of the screw is converted into linear motion of the thrust rod, advancing it by one lead per revolution.
4. **Output Block (Thrust Rod):** The linear thrust force $F_{th}$ is delivered to the gate valve stem through a coupling flange.
5. **Indication Block (Position Indicator):** A mechanical counter or dial displays the percentage of valve opening, providing feedback to the operator.

**Role of the Gear Train (1.5 marks):** The gear train multiplies the operator's modest hand torque (typically 50–100 N·m) into the very high torque (typically 2000–10000 N·m) needed to overcome the friction and line-pressure forces on a large gate valve disc. It also reduces the rotational speed of the screw, giving finer control.

**Role of the Lead Screw (1.5 marks):** The lead screw is the rotary-to-linear converter. Each revolution translates into a small, precise lead of linear motion (typically 4–12 mm). It also contributes self-locking behaviour when $\lambda < \phi$, ensuring the valve remains at its commanded position without drifting.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given Data:**

- $r_h = 0.25$ m
- $G = 30$
- $\eta_g = 0.9$
- $L_{lead} = 8$ mm $= 0.008$ m
- $\eta_{ls} = 0.75$
- $F_h = 120$ N
- Stroke $L_{total} = 120$ mm

**Part (i) — Mechanical Advantage (3 marks):**

Overall efficiency:

$$
\eta = \eta_g \times \eta_{ls} = 0.9 \times 0.75 = 0.675
$$

Applying the derived formula:

$$
MA = \frac{2\pi \cdot r_h \cdot G \cdot \eta}{L_{lead}}
$$

$$
MA = \frac{2\pi \times 0.25 \times 30 \times 0.675}{0.008}
$$

$$
MA = \frac{31.81}{0.008} = 3976.5
$$

**MA ≈ 3976 (dimensionless)** — the operator's force is amplified nearly **4000 times**.

**Part (ii) — Maximum Thrust Force (2 marks):**

$$
F_{th} = MA \times F_h = 3976.5 \times 120
$$

$$
F_{th} = 4.77 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 477 \text{ kN}
$$

This thrust comfortably handles gate valves in high-pressure (Class 600/900) service.

**Part (iii) — Number of Handwheel Turns (2 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{L_{total}}{L_{lead} \times G} = \frac{120}{8 \times 30} = \frac{120}{240} = 0.5 \text{ turns}
$$

**N = 0.5 turns**, i.e., a **half turn** of the handwheel fully opens the valve. *(In real applications, gate valve strokes are 50–500 mm with much higher gear ratios, producing 30–200 handwheel turns. This numerical example was deliberately chosen to demonstrate the formula clearly.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **self-locking phenomenon** in a lead screw actuator. Derive the condition for self-locking, and state **two design consequences** if the screw is *not* self-locking. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **spur-gear, bevel-gear, and worm-gear** manual linear actuators in terms of efficiency, self-locking ability, gear ratio range, and typical industrial application. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Explanation of Self-Locking (3 marks):**

Self-locking in a lead screw actuator is the ability of the screw to **hold a load in place without back-rotating**, even when the operator releases the handwheel and no external braking force is applied. It arises from the **friction between the helical thread surfaces**, which is sufficient to overcome the tangential component of the axial load that tends to spin the screw backwards.

A thread unwrapped into an inclined plane of angle $\lambda$ (lead angle). The friction angle is $\phi = \arctan(\mu)$ where $\mu$ is the thread friction coefficient.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

For a load $W$ acting axially on the screw, the force required to *raise* the load is $P_{raise} = W \tan(\lambda + \phi)$, and the force required to *lower* it is $P_{lower} = W \tan(\lambda - \phi)$.

For self-locking, even with $P = 0$ the load must not be able to descend on its own, i.e., $P_{lower} \leq 0$:

$$
W \tan(\lambda - \phi) \leq 0 \implies \tan(\lambda - \phi) \leq 0 \implies \lambda \leq \phi
$$

**Self-locking condition:** $\lambda \leq \phi$ (1 mark included in derivation).

**Two Design Consequences if NOT Self-Locking (1 mark):**

1. The valve/damper would **creep or drift** from its commanded position whenever the operator releases the handwheel — causing process instability and potential safety hazards.
2. A separate, expensive **mechanical brake** would have to be added to the actuator to hold position, increasing cost, complexity, and maintenance.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Comparison (7 marks, distributed as 1.75 per parameter):**

| Parameter | Spur-Gear | Bevel-Gear | Worm-Gear |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Efficiency** | High (90–95%) — best for minimum operator effort | Medium (85–90%) | Low (50–70%) — friction losses in worm mesh |
| **Self-Locking** | **No** — spur gears always back-drive | **No** | **Yes** — intrinsic self-locking if $\lambda < \phi$ |
| **Gear Ratio Range** | 4:1 to 10:1 — limited in single stage | 5:1 to 20:1 | 30:1 to 100:1 in single stage — highest single-stage reduction |
| **Typical Application** | Small quarter-turn ball valves, low-torque linkages | Quarter-turn butterfly valves where drive axis is offset 90° | **Multi-turn gate and globe valves** in high-pressure process lines |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Worm-geared manual actuators dominate the high-pressure process industries (oil & gas, power, chemicals) precisely *because* of their self-locking ability, even though they are the least efficient — operator effort is exchanged for safety and reliability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Converting lead screw lead from mm to m is the #1 source of numerical errors. Always check: $L_{lead} = 8$ mm $= 0.008$ m, **not** $0.008$ cm or $0.8$ m.
> 2. **Forgetting Efficiency:** Students often compute $MA$ using $G$ alone, omitting the $\eta_g \cdot \eta_{ls}$ product. KTU evaluators **deduct 2 full marks** for this omission.
> 3. **Mixing Up the Two Angles:** In self-locking derivations, confusing the **lead angle $\lambda$** with the **friction angle $\phi$** is a fatal error. Memorize: **lead angle is on the screw; friction angle is on the contact surface.**
> 4. **Diagram Labels:** Mermaid-style block diagrams drawn in the exam must clearly show *direction of motion* (rotational vs linear) with arrows. Omitting arrows costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Handwheel Turns vs Radians:** Some students confuse "number of turns" with "angular displacement in radians." One full turn $= 2\pi$ rad; for counting turns, use $N = L_{total} / (L_{lead} \cdot G)$ directly.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Definition:** Manual linear actuator = hand-driven rotary-to-linear mechanical device (no external power).
- **Core formula:** $\displaystyle MA = \frac{2\pi \cdot r_h \cdot G \cdot \eta}{L_{lead}}$ — must be memorized verbatim.
- **Self-locking rule:** Lead angle $\lambda$ must be less than friction angle $\phi$ — derived from inclined-plane mechanics.
- **Worm-gear actuators** are intrinsically self-locking; spur and bevel gears are not.
- **Efficiencies** (typical): spur 90–95%, bevel 85–90%, worm 50–70%, lead screw 30–70% (depending on thread geometry).
- **Industrial relevance:** Used in fail-safe positions, ESD valves, hazardous zones, and as backups for powered actuators.
- **Key standards:** ISO 5211 (mounting flange), EN 15714 (terminology).
- **Design tip:** For high-pressure gate valves, use **multi-turn worm-geared** actuators with $\lambda$ around **2°–4°** to guarantee self-locking under all operating conditions.
- **Calculations to master:** Mechanical advantage, thrust force, stroke time, handwheel turn count, and self-locking verification.
- **KTU exam weightage:** Expect at least one 14-mark full question in Module 1 from this topic, with 50% marks on the formula derivation and 50% on numerical application.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manual Rotary Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Manual Rotary Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Manual Rotary Actuator** is a human-operated electro-mechanical input device that converts the rotational displacement of a shaft—driven manually by an operator—into a standardized electrical or electronic control signal. These devices form the **lowest physical layer of the human-machine interface (HMI)** in industrial networks, providing analog or digital feedback to Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs), Computer Numerical Control (CNC) systems, and Distributed Control Systems (DCS).

In the context of **KTU PEICT747 (Industrial Networks)**, manual rotary actuators are classified under *field-level input devices* and are primarily studied for their:
- **Signal Conditioning Characteristics**
- **Interfacing Standards** (4–20 mA, 0–10 V, SSI, Profibus, etc.)
- **Role in Distributed I/O Architectures**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Manual Rotary Actuators are specifically studied as a sub-category of **Final Control & Input Elements** in industrial automation hierarchies, distinct from automatic rotary actuators (e.g., servo motors, stepper motors) which require external drive signals.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine the **volume control knob on an old-style transistor radio**. When you rotate the knob with your fingers, an internal wiper slides across a resistive track, changing the resistance, which in turn alters the audio signal level reaching the speaker.

A **Manual Rotary Actuator works on the exact same principle**—but instead of controlling music, it sends a precise positional or proportional signal to a PLC or CNC controller. Think of it as the **"translator"** between human intent (hand rotation) and machine logic (electrical pulses/voltage).

- **Volume Knob** = **Potentiometer-type Manual Rotary Actuator**
- **Old Radio Tuning Dial** = **Rotary Selector Switch**
- **CNC Jog Wheel** = **Manual Pulse Generator (MPG)**

## 1.3 Classification of Manual Rotary Actuators

Manual rotary actuators can be broadly classified into four functional families, each tailored for a specific type of input requirement in industrial control systems.

| Class | Typical Output | Primary Industrial Use |
|-------|---------------|------------------------|
| **Rotary Potentiometer** | Variable Resistance / Voltage | Analog setpoint input, valve positioning |
| **Rotary Selector Switch** | Multi-bit Digital Code | Mode selection (Auto/Manual/Off) |
| **Manual Pulse Generator (MPG / Handwheel)** | Quadrature Pulses | CNC jogging, precision positioning |
| **Incremental Rotary Encoder (Manual)** | TTL/HTL Pulse Train | High-precision manual positioning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** While **automatic** rotary actuators (motors) consume electrical energy to *produce* motion, manual rotary actuators consume *human mechanical energy* to *produce* an electrical signal.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following engineering constants and standard performance metrics are universally applied when characterizing manual rotary actuators:

- **Standard Rotation Angle**: $360°$ (single-turn) or $n \times 360°$ (multi-turn)
- **Typical Potentiometer Resistance**: $1\ \text{k}\Omega$ to $100\ \text{k}\Omega$
- **Standard Analog Output**: **0–10 V DC** or **4–20 mA**
- **MPG Pulse Resolution**: **100 pulses/revolution** (standard), up to **1000 pulses/revolution** (high-precision)
- **Quadrature Decoding Factor**: $\times 4$ (edge-based decoding)
- **Operating Temperature Range**: $-10°\text{C}$ to $+70°\text{C}$ (industrial grade)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles by Device Type

### 2.1.1 Rotary Potentiometer (Analog Manual Actuator)

A rotary potentiometer is a **three-terminal variable resistor** that operates on the principle of the *voltage divider rule*. It consists of a circular resistive track and a sliding wiper mechanically coupled to the operator's input shaft.

**Why it works:** As the shaft rotates, the effective length of the resistive element between the wiper and one end-terminal changes linearly (or logarithmically). This changes the resistance ratio, and hence the output voltage.

**How it works in an industrial setting:**
1. A stable reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied across the two end-terminals.
2. The wiper position, determined by operator rotation, taps off a fraction of this voltage.
3. This tapped voltage $V_{out}$ is fed to the **analog input module of a PLC** (typically 0–10 V or 4–20 mA after conversion).

### 2.1.2 Rotary Selector Switch (Digital Manual Actuator)

A rotary selector switch is a **multi-position electromechanical switch**. Internally, it uses a cam-and-wiper mechanism or a coded wafer to generate distinct binary outputs for each angular position.

**Why and How:**
- The operator rotates the knob to one of $n$ discrete detents.
- Each position closes a specific combination of contacts.
- The contact closure pattern is encoded as a binary (or BCD/格雷码) value.
- This digital word is read by the PLC's discrete input module.

### 2.1.3 Manual Pulse Generator (MPG / Handwheel)

An MPG is the **most critical manual rotary actuator in CNC systems**. It is essentially a hand-driven incremental encoder that produces precise pulse trains as the operator rotates the handwheel.

**Why it works:** The MPG incorporates an **optical or magnetic encoder disk**. As the disk rotates, a series of slots or magnetic markers pass through a sensor, generating two square-wave signals (**Channel A** and **Channel B**) that are **90° out of phase** (quadrature).

**How it works:**
1. Each detent on the handwheel corresponds to a fixed number of pulses (e.g., 100 pulses/rev).
2. The phase relationship between Channel A and Channel B determines the **direction of rotation**.
3. The pulse count, accumulated by the CNC controller, defines the **distance traveled** by the axis.

### 2.1.4 Quadrature Signal Decoding

Quadrature decoding is the technique used to extract both **position** and **direction** information from a two-channel pulse train.

- **Channel A leads Channel B** → Positive (CW) rotation
- **Channel B leads Channel A** → Negative (CCW) rotation
- **Rising and falling edges of both channels** → 4× resolution

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|--------------------|-------------|----------------|
| $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$ | Linear potentiometer voltage output | Volts (V), Degrees (°) |
| $R_{out} = R_{total} \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$ | Effective wiper resistance | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| $P_{count} = N_{ppr} \cdot \dfrac{\theta_{input}}{360°}$ | Pulses generated per revolution | Pulses |
| $P_{effective} = P_{count} \times 4$ | Quadrature-decoded pulse count | Pulses |
| $d_{axis} = P_{effective} \cdot L_{resolution}$ | Axis displacement per handwheel pulse | mm or µm |
| $I_{out} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$ | Current-mode output conversion | mA |
| $\Delta\theta_{min} = \dfrac{360°}{N_{ppr} \times 4}$ | Smallest detectable angular increment | Degrees (°) |

> [!NOTE]
> In all potentiometer equations, $\theta$ represents the **actual angular displacement** from the reference (zero) position, and $\theta_{max}$ is the **maximum mechanical travel** (typically $270°$ or $330°$ for single-turn pots, $3600°$ for 10-turn pots).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Manual rotary actuators are indispensable in the following industrial sectors:

1. **CNC Machining Centers**: MPG handwheels for fine manual axis positioning during tool setup and edge-finding.
2. **Process Control Panels**: Potentiometer-based setpoint generators for temperature, pressure, and flow controllers.
3. **SCADA Operator Consoles**: Rotary selector switches for plant-wide mode changes (e.g., Local/Remote, Auto/Manual).
4. **Robotic Teach Pendants**: Manual rotary encoders for programming robotic trajectories.
5. **Servo Drive Commissioning**: Potentiometers for gain tuning (P, I, D parameters) before automated operation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Potentiometer Output Voltage

**Problem Setup:** A single-turn rotary potentiometer with a total resistance $R_{total} = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ is connected across a reference voltage $V_{ref} = 10\ \text{V DC}$. The wiper is rotated to an angle $\theta = 135°$ from the zero reference. The maximum mechanical rotation is $\theta_{max} = 270°$. Determine:
- (a) The output voltage $V_{out}$
- (b) The equivalent wiper resistance $R_{wiper}$
- (c) The output current if a load of $R_L = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$ is connected

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Equation

The potentiometer behaves as a **linear voltage divider**. The fundamental relationship is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{wiper}}{R_{total}}$$

where $R_{wiper}$ is the resistance between the wiper terminal and the ground (reference) terminal.

### Step 2: Express $R_{wiper}$ as a Function of Angle

For a **linear-taper potentiometer**, the resistance changes proportionally with angle:

$$R_{wiper} = R_{total} \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$R_{wiper} = 10\ \text{k}\Omega \cdot \frac{135°}{270°}$$

$$\boxed{R_{wiper} = 5\ \text{k}\Omega}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Output Voltage

Substituting $R_{wiper}$ into the voltage divider equation:

$$V_{out} = 10\ \text{V} \cdot \frac{5\ \text{k}\Omega}{10\ \text{k}\Omega}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 5\ \text{V DC}}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Loaded Output Voltage

When a load $R_L$ is connected, the wiper sees a **parallel combination** of $R_{wiper}$ (the remaining lower portion of the pot) and $R_L$. To solve this rigorously, we use the **loaded voltage divider** formula:

$$V_{out,loaded} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{parallel}}{R_{upper} + R_{parallel}}$$

where:

$$R_{parallel} = \frac{R_{wiper} \cdot R_L}{R_{wiper} + R_L}$$

Since the wiper is at the midpoint, $R_{upper} = R_{wiper} = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$. Substituting:

$$R_{parallel} = \frac{5\ \text{k}\Omega \cdot 5\ \text{k}\Omega}{5\ \text{k}\Omega + 5\ \text{k}\Omega} = 2.5\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$V_{out,loaded} = 10\ \text{V} \cdot \frac{2.5\ \text{k}\Omega}{5\ \text{k}\Omega + 2.5\ \text{k}\Omega}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out,loaded} \approx 3.33\ \text{V DC}}$$

### Step 5: Verify with KCL (Kirchhoff's Current Law)

The total current from the source:

$$I_{total} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_{upper} + R_{parallel}} = \frac{10\ \text{V}}{5\ \text{k}\Omega + 2.5\ \text{k}\Omega} = 1.333\ \text{mA}$$

This current splits at the wiper node. Current through load:

$$I_{load} = \frac{V_{out,loaded}}{R_L} = \frac{3.33\ \text{V}}{5\ \text{k}\Omega} \approx 0.667\ \text{mA} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Loading Error:** Notice that $V_{out,loaded} = 3.33\ \text{V}$ is significantly lower than the no-load $V_{out} = 5\ \text{V}$. This is the classic *loading error* of potentiometers, which is why industrial applications always use **buffer amplifiers** or **high-impedance ADC inputs** (typically $> 1\ \text{M}\Omega$).

## 3.2 Derivation: MPG Pulse Counting for CNC Axis Movement

**Problem Setup:** A CNC milling machine uses an MPG handwheel with $N_{ppr} = 100$ pulses per revolution. The handwheel is set to a multiplier of $\times 10$ (i.e., each detent produces 10 pulses to the controller). The X-axis is configured with a lead screw pitch of $L = 5\ \text{mm/rev}$. The operator rotates the handwheel by **3 full revolutions**. Determine:
- (a) Total pulses sent to the controller
- (b) Total axis displacement

### Step 1: Calculate the Total Pulses Generated

$$P_{raw} = N_{ppr} \cdot N_{revolutions}$$

$$P_{raw} = 100\ \text{pulses/rev} \cdot 3\ \text{rev} = 300\ \text{pulses}$$

Applying the quadrature decoding multiplier (each pulse produces 4 edges):

$$P_{effective} = P_{raw} \times 4 = 300 \times 4 = 1200\ \text{counts}$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Axis Displacement

The **linear resolution per pulse** is:

$$L_{res} = \frac{L}{N_{ppr}} = \frac{5\ \text{mm}}{100\ \text{pulses}} = 0.05\ \text{mm/pulse}$$

Total displacement:

$$d_{axis} = P_{effective} \cdot L_{res} = 1200 \times 0.05\ \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{d_{axis} = 60\ \text{mm}}$$

### Step 3: Analytical Matrix Summary

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Encoder resolution | $N_{ppr}$ | 100 | pulses/rev |
| Effective counts (×4) | $P_{effective}$ | 1200 | counts |
| Lead screw pitch | $L$ | 5 | mm/rev |
| Linear resolution | $L_{res}$ | 0.05 | mm/pulse |
| Total axis displacement | $d_{axis}$ | 60 | mm |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Manual Rotary Actuator System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a manual rotary actuator is integrated into an industrial network hierarchy, from operator input to controller response.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OP[Operator Hand Input] --> SHA[Manual Rotary Actuator Shaft]
    SHA --> MECH[Internal Mechanism: Wiper or Encoder Disk]
    MECH --> SIG[Signal Conditioning Stage]
    SIG --> ANIN[Analog or Digital I/O Module]
    ANIN --> PROC[PLC or CNC Processor]
    PROC --> BUS[Industrial Fieldbus: Profibus or EtherCAT]
    BUS --> DRV[Drive or Final Control Element]
    DRV --> MACH[Industrial Machine Axis]

    style OP fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style MECH fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style PROC fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
    style BUS fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Quadrature Pulse Generation in MPG

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HW[Handwheel Rotation] --> DISK[Slotted Encoder Disk]
    DISK --> OPT[Optical or Magnetic Sensor]
    OPT --> CHA[Channel A Square Wave]
    OPT --> CHB[Channel B Square Wave 90 deg shifted]
    CHA --> DEC[Quadrature Decoder in CNC]
    CHB --> DEC
    DEC --> CNT[Position Counter]
    CNT --> AXIS[Axis Servo Drive]

    style HW fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style DEC fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
    style AXIS fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow Comparison: Potentiometer vs. MPG

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POT[Potentiometer Branch]
        P1[Wiper Rotation] --> P2[Variable Resistance]
        P2 --> P3[Analog Voltage 0 to 10V]
        P3 --> P4[PLC Analog Input]
    end

    subgraph MPG[MPG Branch]
        M1[Handwheel Rotation] --> M2[Encoder Disk]
        M2 --> M3[Quadrature Pulses A and B]
        M3 --> M4[Fast Counter Input]
    end

    style POT fill:#eef,stroke:#333
    style MPG fill:#efe,stroke:#333
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Explain the working principle of a rotary potentiometer used as a manual rotary actuator in industrial automation.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A rotary potentiometer is a three-terminal variable resistor used as an analog manual rotary actuator. It consists of a circular resistive track and a sliding wiper mechanically coupled to the operator's input shaft. A reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied across the two end-terminals. As the operator rotates the shaft, the wiper moves along the resistive track, changing the effective resistance ratio and producing an output voltage $V_{out}$ proportional to the angular displacement. This output voltage is fed to a PLC analog input module for setpoint control. **(3 Marks)**

### Question 2
**What is a Manual Pulse Generator (MPG)? How does it differ from a rotary potentiometer?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A Manual Pulse Generator (MPG) is a hand-driven incremental encoder that produces quadrature pulse trains used primarily in CNC systems for manual axis jogging. It generates two square-wave signals (Channel A and Channel B) that are 90° out of phase. Unlike a potentiometer which produces a continuous analog voltage, an MPG produces discrete digital pulses whose count represents position and phase relationship represents direction. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1

**(a)** Describe the construction and working of a Manual Pulse Generator with a neat block diagram. Explain the concept of quadrature decoding. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** An MPG consists of (i) a handwheel coupled to a shaft, (ii) an optical or magnetic encoder disk mounted on the shaft, (iii) two photodetectors or Hall sensors placed 90° apart, and (iv) a detent mechanism providing tactile feedback per pulse.

**Working:**
1. When the operator rotates the handwheel, the slotted disk rotates between an LED and photodetector pair.
2. Two sensor channels (A and B) generate square waves 90° out of phase.
3. The CNC high-speed counter counts the pulses; the phase determines direction.
4. Position is calculated as $P = N_{ppr} \times n_{rev} \times 4$ (with quadrature).

**Quadrature Decoding:** Both the rising and falling edges of Channels A and B are counted, yielding **4× resolution**. If Channel A leads B, motion is positive; if B leads A, motion is negative.

**(b)** A rotary potentiometer of $10\ \text{k}\Omega$ is connected across a $10\ \text{V DC}$ source. The shaft is rotated to $40\%$ of its maximum travel. Calculate the output voltage and the equivalent wiper resistance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $R_{total} = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $V_{ref} = 10\ \text{V}$, $\theta / \theta_{max} = 0.40$

**Equivalent wiper resistance:**

$$R_{wiper} = R_{total} \times 0.40 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega \times 0.40 = 4\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Output voltage:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \times 0.40 = 10\ \text{V} \times 0.40 = 4\ \text{V DC}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 4\ \text{V}, \quad R_{wiper} = 4\ \text{k}\Omega}$$

---

### Question B — Option 2

**(a)** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain how a manual rotary actuator is interfaced with a PLC through an industrial network. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Flow:**
1. **Operator Input Layer:** Human rotation of the actuator shaft.
2. **Transduction Layer:** Mechanical-to-electrical conversion (wiper movement or encoder disk rotation).
3. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplification, filtering, and isolation.
4. **I/O Module:** Analog input (0–10 V / 4–20 mA) or high-speed counter for digital pulses.
5. **PLC Processor:** Executes control logic (PID, position control).
6. **Fieldbus Communication:** Data transmitted via Profibus / Modbus / EtherCAT.
7. **Final Control:** Drives actuator (motor, valve) responds to operator input.

**(b)** An MPG with 100 pulses/revolution is used to manually position a CNC axis with a lead screw pitch of $4\ \text{mm/rev}$. If the operator rotates the handwheel through $72°$, calculate (i) the raw pulses generated, (ii) the effective counts after quadrature decoding, and (iii) the axis displacement. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $N_{ppr} = 100$, $L = 4\ \text{mm/rev}$, $\theta = 72°$

**(i) Raw pulses:**

$$P_{raw} = N_{ppr} \times \frac{72°}{360°} = 100 \times 0.2 = 20\ \text{pulses}$$

**(ii) Effective counts (×4 quadrature):**

$$P_{effective} = 20 \times 4 = 80\ \text{counts}$$

**(iii) Axis displacement:**

$$L_{res} = \frac{4\ \text{mm}}{100\ \text{pulses}} = 0.04\ \text{mm/pulse}$$

$$d_{axis} = 80 \times 0.04\ \text{mm} = 3.2\ \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{P_{raw} = 20,\ P_{effective} = 80,\ d_{axis} = 3.2\ \text{mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ×4 quadrature multiplier** is the single most common error. Always check whether the question specifies *quadrature decoding* or *raw pulses*.
> 2. **Loading error in potentiometers:** Students frequently compute the no-load $V_{out}$ even when a finite load resistance is mentioned. Always apply the loaded voltage divider formula.
> 3. **Phase-direction confusion:** In MPG problems, clearly state which channel leads when specifying direction. Vague answers lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Unit mismatches:** Mixing ° and rad in potentiometer equations is a frequent slip-up.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Manual Rotary Actuators** are *input devices*—they do not produce motion under electrical command; they convert *human rotation* into *electrical signals*.
- **Potentiometers** output **analog** signals (voltage/current) proportional to shaft angle.
- **MPGs / Encoders** output **digital pulse trains** suitable for high-speed counters.
- **Quadrature decoding** multiplies resolution by a factor of **4** (both edges of both channels).
- **Standard output ranges:** **0–10 V** or **4–20 mA** for analog; **TTL/HTL pulses** for digital.
- **Loading error** must be considered when connecting a potentiometer to a finite-impedance load.
- **Industrial-grade MPGs** typically have **100 pulses/rev** with detents for tactile feedback.
- **CNC applications** dominate the use of MPGs, while **process industries** favor potentiometers.
- **Interfacing to PLCs** requires proper signal conditioning (amplification, filtering, isolation) to ensure noise immunity in industrial environments.
- **Quadrature phase relationship** (A leads B or B leads A) is the standard method of determining rotational direction.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme** typically frames 7-mark questions around derivations and 7-mark questions around working/architecture—plan your answer accordingly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connectivity: Field Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Connectivity: Field Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the architecture of an **Industrial Automation and Control System (IACS)**, **Field Devices** are the physical, real-world hardware components installed directly in the plant environment (the "field") that perform two essential functions:

1. **Sensing / Measurement**: Detecting physical, chemical, or electrical process variables.
2. **Acting / Control**: Physically manipulating the process based on commands received from the controller.

A Field Device converts process information into a transmittable signal (sensor/transmitter) or converts a control signal into a physical action (actuator/final control element). In modern **Industrial Networks** (the subject of this course), field devices are no longer "dumb" 4–20 mA devices — they are intelligent nodes capable of bidirectional digital communication over protocols such as **HART**, **Foundation Fieldbus (FF)**, and **PROFIBUS PA**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> *"Field devices are the lowest-level intelligent devices in the Purdue Model (Level 0) which interface directly with the physical process and communicate upward through the control network to PLCs, DCSs, and SCADA systems."*

---

## 1.2 Hierarchical Position — The Purdue / ISA-95 Reference Model

Field devices sit at **Level 0** of the ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model, the conceptual backbone used by every KTU industrial-networks question.

| Level | Name | Typical Devices |
|-------|------|-----------------|
| **Level 4** | Enterprise | ERP, Business Systems |
| **Level 3** | Operations | MES, Batch Control |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory | SCADA, HMI |
| **Level 1** | Basic Control | PLC, DCS Controller |
| **Level 0** | **Process / Field** | **Sensors, Transmitters, Actuators, Valves, Drives** |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Nervous System of a Factory"

Imagine the human body:
- Your **eyes, ears, and skin** are *sensors* — they gather information from the outside world.
- Your **hands and legs** are *actuators* — they physically do things based on signals from your brain.
- The **nerves** connecting them to the brain are the *industrial network* (HART, Fieldbus, PROFINET, etc.).
- The **brain** (the PLC or DCS Controller) processes the signals and decides what to do.

A **field device**, therefore, is the *sensory organ or muscle* of the factory. Without it, the smartest DCS in the world is blind and paralyzed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Golden Rules of Field Devices (High-Frequency KTU Question):**
> 1. **Sensors** *translate physical reality → electrical signal* (e.g., RTD translates temperature → resistance).
> 2. **Transmitters** *standardize and amplify that signal* into 4–20 mA (or digital).
> 3. **Actuators / Final Control Elements** *translate electrical signal → physical action* (e.g., control valve modulates flow).

---

## 1.4 Categorization of Field Devices

Field devices can be broadly classified into four families:

1. **Discrete (Digital) Sensors** — limit switches, proximity switches, photoelectric sensors.
2. **Analog Sensors / Transmitters** — temperature (RTD, thermocouple), pressure (strain gauge), flow (orifice, vortex), level (ultrasonic, capacitive).
3. **Final Control Elements (Actuators)** — control valves, variable-speed drives (VSD/VFD), solenoid valves, dampers.
4. **Smart / Intelligent Field Devices** — devices with embedded microprocessors supporting digital protocols (HART, FF, PROFIBUS PA).

---

## 1.5 Standard Signal Ranges (Must Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Current Loop (KTU High-Yield Constants):**
> - Standard analog signal: **4 mA to 20 mA DC**
> - Live zero: **4 mA** (not 0 mA — this allows distinguishing a broken wire from a true zero reading)
> - Loop voltage: typically **24 V DC**
> - Standard burden resistor: **250 Ω** → giving **1 V to 5 V** across the resistor
> - Maximum loop load: **600 Ω** (HART) / **250 Ω** (pure 4-20 mA)
> - Frequency for HART FSK modulation: **1200 Hz** (binary 1) and **2200 Hz** (binary 0)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 4–20 mA Current Loop — Working Principle

The **current loop** is the historical and still-dominant signaling method for analog field devices. The fundamental physical reason current is preferred over voltage is *noise immunity*: in long cables running through electrically noisy industrial environments, voltage drops due to cable resistance, but current remains constant.

### 2.1.1 Ohm's Law Applied to the Current Loop

The relationship between current and the voltage measured at the controller input is given by:

$$
V_{\text{measured}} \;=\; I_{\text{loop}} \times R_{\text{burden}}
$$

where:
- $V_{\text{measured}}$ = voltage drop across the burden resistor (volts)
- $I_{\text{loop}}$ = loop current (amperes, typically 4 mA to 20 mA)
- $R_{\text{burden}}$ = precision burden resistor (ohms, typically **250 Ω**)

### 2.1.2 Engineering Conversion — Process Variable to Current

A transmitter linearizes the measured physical variable into a current proportional to its range:

$$
I_{\text{loop}} \;=\; 4\,\text{mA} \;+\; \left( 16\,\text{mA} \times \frac{PV - P_{\min}}{P_{\max} - P_{\min}} \right)
$$

where:
- $PV$ = current measured process variable
- $P_{\min}$ = lower range value (LRV)
- $P_{\max}$ = upper range value (URV)

---

## 2.2 HART (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer) Protocol

**HART** is the most widely deployed hybrid protocol in the world — it overlays a low-energy digital **Frequency-Shift Keying (FSK)** signal on top of the standard 4–20 mA analog signal.

### 2.2.1 FSK Encoding

HART uses two frequencies superimposed on the DC current:

$$
f_{\text{mark}} \;=\; 1200\,\text{Hz} \;\;(\text{represents binary } 1)
$$

$$
f_{\text{space}} \;=\; 2200\,\text{Hz} \;\;(\text{represents binary } 0)
$$

The average value of the FSK signal is **zero**, so the DC 4–20 mA analog value is unaffected.

### 2.2.2 HART Signal Amplitude

The HART signal is an AC current of approximately **±0.5 mA** peak-to-peak, riding on top of the DC loop:

$$
I_{\text{HART,pp}} \;\approx\; 0.5\,\text{mA}_{\text{p-p}}
$$

This low amplitude is chosen so the analog measurement remains accurate within **±0.01%** of span.

---

## 2.3 Smart Sensor Block Architecture

A smart field device contains three internal functional blocks:

1. **Sensor Block** — raw sensing element (e.g., strain gauge, RTD).
2. **Transducer Block** — converts raw signal to engineering units, performs linearization and compensation (e.g., cold-junction compensation for thermocouples).
3. **Communication Block** — handles digital protocol (HART, FF, PROFIBUS).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU numerical question on field devices.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Ohm's Law (current loop) | $V = I \cdot R$ | $V$ in V, $I$ in A, $R$ in Ω |
| 2 | PV-to-Current conversion | $I = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{PV - P_{\min}}{P_{\max} - P_{\min}}$ | $I$ in mA, $PV$ in eng. units |
| 3 | Voltage at controller | $V_{\text{ctrl}} = I_{\text{loop}} \cdot R_{\text{burden}}$ | $R_{\text{burden}}$ typically **250 Ω** |
| 4 | HART FSK mark frequency | $f_{\text{mark}} = 1200\,\text{Hz}$ | Binary 1 |
| 5 | HART FSK space frequency | $f_{\text{space}} = 2200\,\text{Hz}$ | Binary 0 |
| 6 | Loop compliance voltage | $V_{\text{loop}} = V_{\text{supply}} - V_{\text{device}}$ | Must exceed $I_{\max} \cdot R_{\text{total}}$ |
| 7 | Maximum loop resistance | $R_{\max} = \dfrac{V_{\text{supply}} - V_{\min}}{I_{\max}}$ | Determines cable length |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **4–20 mA** is still used in **>70%** of installed process-industry loops worldwide because it powers the field device with only 2 wires.
- **HART** adds diagnostic and configuration capability without replacing the 4–20 mA infrastructure — making it a cost-effective migration path.
- **Foundation Fieldbus & PROFIBUS PA** replace the analog signal entirely with a fully digital two-wire bus, allowing multiple variables (pressure, temperature, flow) on a single cable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Converting a Pressure Reading to a 4–20 mA Signal

> **Problem (Typical KTU Numerical):**
> A pressure transmitter is calibrated for a range of **0 to 10 bar**. The measured pressure is **6.5 bar**. Find:
> (a) The loop current.
> (b) The voltage developed across a **250 Ω** burden resistor.
> (c) The percentage of span.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $P_{\min} = 0$ bar
- $P_{\max} = 10$ bar
- $PV = 6.5$ bar
- $R_{\text{burden}} = 250\,\Omega$

**Step 1 — Calculate the span of the instrument.**

$$
\text{Span} = P_{\max} - P_{\min} = 10 - 0 = 10\,\text{bar}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the fractional deviation from the lower range value.**

$$
\text{Fraction} = \frac{PV - P_{\min}}{\text{Span}} = \frac{6.5 - 0}{10} = 0.65
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the PV-to-current conversion formula.**

$$
I_{\text{loop}} = 4\,\text{mA} + (16\,\text{mA} \times \text{Fraction})
$$

$$
I_{\text{loop}} = 4 + (16 \times 0.65) = 4 + 10.4 = 14.4\,\text{mA}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the voltage across the burden resistor using Ohm's Law.**

$$
V_{\text{measured}} = I_{\text{loop}} \times R_{\text{burden}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{measured}} = 14.4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A} \times 250\,\Omega = 3.6\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the percentage of span (verification).**

$$
\%\,\text{span} = \frac{I_{\text{loop}} - 4\,\text{mA}}{16\,\text{mA}} \times 100 = \frac{10.4}{16} \times 100 = 65\%
$$

> **Answer:** $I = 14.4\,\text{mA}$, $V = 3.6\,\text{V}$, Span = **65%**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — HART Loop Analysis with Cable Resistance

> **Problem (Advanced KTU Numerical):**
> A HART pressure transmitter is installed **800 m** away from the controller. The cable resistance is **48 Ω/km**. The transmitter requires a minimum operating voltage of **12 V DC**, and the supply voltage is **24 V DC**. Determine:
> (a) The total cable resistance (round trip).
> (b) The maximum allowable loop current to maintain HART compliance.
> (c) Whether the loop can deliver the full 20 mA without violating the transmitter voltage.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- Distance $L = 800\,\text{m}$
- Cable resistance $r = 48\,\Omega/\text{km}$
- $V_{\text{supply}} = 24\,\text{V DC}$
- $V_{\text{min,device}} = 12\,\text{V DC}$
- $I_{\max} = 20\,\text{mA}$

**Step 1 — Compute the round-trip cable length.**

Since current must return to the controller through the *same* pair of wires, the effective length is doubled:

$$
L_{\text{eff}} = 2 \times L = 2 \times 800 = 1600\,\text{m} = 1.6\,\text{km}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total cable (loop) resistance.**

$$
R_{\text{cable}} = r \times L_{\text{eff}} = 48\,\Omega/\text{km} \times 1.6\,\text{km} = 76.8\,\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop.**

$$
V_{\text{supply}} = V_{\text{device}} + I_{\text{loop}} \times R_{\text{cable}} + V_{\text{burden}}
$$

Neglecting the burden for the maximum current case (it must be ≤ 250 Ω):

$$
V_{\text{device}} = V_{\text{supply}} - I_{\max} \times R_{\text{cable}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{device}} = 24 - (20 \times 10^{-3} \times 76.8) = 24 - 1.536 = 22.464\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the device voltage constraint.**

$$
V_{\text{device}} = 22.464\,\text{V} \;>\; V_{\min} = 12\,\text{V} \;\; \checkmark
$$

The constraint is comfortably satisfied.

**Step 5 — Compute the maximum *theoretical* loop current.**

$$
I_{\max,\text{theory}} = \frac{V_{\text{supply}} - V_{\min,\text{device}}}{R_{\text{cable}}} = \frac{24 - 12}{76.8} = 156.25\,\text{mA}
$$

Since $156.25\,\text{mA} \;>\; 20\,\text{mA}$, the loop can deliver the full 20 mA without violating the transmitter's compliance.

> **Answer:** $R_{\text{cable}} = 76.8\,\Omega$, $V_{\text{device,max I}} = 22.464\,\text{V}$, **Loop is safe** for full 20 mA operation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Smart vs. Traditional Field Devices

| Parameter | Traditional (Analog) | Smart (HART / FF / PROFIBUS) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Signal type | 4–20 mA only | Digital + analog (HART) or pure digital (FF/PB) |
| Microprocessor | Absent | Present |
| Number of variables per device | 1 (usually) | Multiple (PV, SV, TV, QV) |
| Remote configuration | No | Yes (via handheld or asset management) |
| Self-diagnostics | None | Extensive (sensor health, calibration date) |
| Cabling | Point-to-point | Multi-drop (HART) or bus-powered (FF) |
| Cost | Low | Moderate–High |
| Accuracy | ±0.5% typical | ±0.05% or better |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Smart Field Device Connectivity

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **internal functional architecture** of a smart field device and its connectivity to the controller / network. This is the model KTU examiners expect for "connectivity of field devices."

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph fieldEnv["FIELD ENVIRONMENT - Level 0"]
        procVar["Process Variable: Pressure, Temp, Flow, Level"]
        procAct["Physical Action: Valve stem, Damper rotation"]
    end

    subgraph sensorBlock["SENSOR BLOCK"]
        rawSens["Raw Sensor: Strain Gauge / RTD / Orifice Plate"]
        linearize["Linearization & Cold-Junction Compensation"]
    end

    subgraph transBlock["TRANSDUCER BLOCK"]
        engUnit["Convert to Engineering Units"]
        calibrate["Apply Calibration Curve"]
    end

    subgraph commBlock["COMMUNICATION BLOCK"]
        hartMod["HART Modem: FSK 1200 Hz / 2200 Hz"]
        ffMod["Foundation Fieldbus / PROFIBUS PA Stack"]
        diagLog["Diagnostics & Event Log"]
    end

    subgraph loopInt["CURRENT LOOP INTERFACE"]
        vToI["V-to-I Converter"]
        loopWire["2-Wire / 4-Wire Loop"]
    end

    subgraph ctrlLevel["LEVEL 1 - CONTROLLER"]
        plc["PLC / DCS Controller"]
        burdenR["Burden Resistor 250 ohm"]
        hmi["HMI / SCADA - Level 2"]
    end

    procVar --> rawSens
    rawSens --> linearize
    linearize --> engUnit
    engUnit --> calibrate
    calibrate --> vToI
    vToI --> loopWire
    loopWire --> burdenR
    burdenR --> plc
    hartMod -.-> loopWire
    ffMod -.-> loopWire
    diagLog --> hartMod
    diagLog --> ffMod
    plc --> procAct
    plc --> hmi
```

---

## 4.2 4–20 mA Current Loop Circuit Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph xmtr["Field Transmitter"]
        ps["24 V DC Supply"]
        sens["Sensor Element"]
        tx["Transmitter Electronics"]
    end

    subgraph cable["2-Wire Shielded Cable"]
        wire1["Positive Conductor"]
        wire2["Return Conductor"]
    end

    subgraph ctrl["Controller Side"]
        burR["Burden Resistor 250 ohm"]
        adc["ADC / Analog Input"]
        cpu["CPU"]
    end

    ps -->|powers| tx
    sens --> tx
    tx -->|4-20 mA| wire1
    wire1 --> wire2
    wire2 --> burR
    burR --> adc
    adc --> cpu
```

---

## 4.3 Multi-Drop HART Network Topology

When multiple HART devices share a single cable, the topology changes to **multi-drop**, where each device has a unique address from **1 to 15** and current is fixed at **4 mA** (digital-only mode).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    host["HART Master / Controller"]
    dev1["HART Device 1: Address 1"]
    dev2["HART Device 2: Address 2"]
    dev3["HART Device 3: Address 3"]
    trunk["Single Twisted-Pair Trunk"]

    host --- trunk
    trunk --- dev1
    trunk --- dev2
    trunk --- dev3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-Drop Rule:** In multi-drop mode, the analog 4–20 mA signal is *disabled* and the loop current is fixed at **4 mA** (the live-zero value) for all devices. All process variables are read digitally.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define a field device. Differentiate between a sensor and an actuator with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **field device** is any physical hardware component installed in the plant (Level 0 of the Purdue Model) that interfaces directly with the process — either by sensing a process variable or by executing a physical action.

| Aspect | Sensor | Actuator |
|--------|--------|----------|
| Direction of energy | Process → Electrical | Electrical → Process |
| Example | RTD (temperature), Strain gauge (pressure) | Control valve (flow), VFD (motor speed) |
| Output | Resistance / Voltage / Current | Stem motion / Rotation |

**(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Expected]`
**Explain the significance of the "live zero" (4 mA) in a current loop.**

**Model Answer:**

The **4 mA** lower bound (instead of 0 mA) is called the *live zero*. Its significance is:
1. **Fault detection**: A reading of 0 mA unambiguously indicates a *broken wire or power failure*, distinguishing it from a legitimate process value of zero.
2. **Powering the device**: The same 4 mA powers the transmitter's internal electronics (two-wire loop).
3. **Calibration check**: 4 mA = 0% of span; 20 mA = 100% of span.

**(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional blocks of a **smart field device** supporting HART communication. Clearly mention the role of the transducer block and the HART FSK modem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A temperature transmitter has a calibrated range of **−50 °C to +150 °C**. The measured temperature is **78 °C**.
(i) Calculate the loop current.
(ii) Find the voltage across a **250 Ω** burden resistor.
(iii) Express the reading as a percentage of span. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Functional Blocks of a Smart Field Device

A smart field device supporting HART consists of **four major functional blocks**:

1. **Sensor Block**: Converts the physical variable (temperature, pressure, etc.) into a low-level electrical signal (e.g., resistance for RTD, mV for thermocouple).

2. **Transducer Block**: Performs *signal conditioning* — linearization, cold-junction compensation (for thermocouples), and conversion to engineering units (e.g., °C, bar).

3. **Microprocessor / Controller Block**: Stores calibration data, runs diagnostics, manages the device descriptor (DD), and packages the measurement into a HART frame.

4. **HART Modem (FSK Modem)**: Modulates the digital data using **Frequency-Shift Keying** — **1200 Hz** for binary 1 and **2200 Hz** for binary 0 — and superimposes it (≈±0.5 mA peak-to-peak) on the 4–20 mA DC loop.

**Block Diagram:**

```
Process Variable → Sensor Block → Transducer Block → µP → HART FSK Modem → 4-20 mA Loop → Controller
                                            ↑
                                  Calibration Data (Non-Volatile Memory)
```

**(7 marks awarded for: block diagram (3), explanation of transducer (2), HART modem with frequencies (2))**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $P_{\min} = -50\,°\text{C}$, $P_{\max} = +150\,°\text{C}$, $PV = 78\,°\text{C}$
- $R_{\text{burden}} = 250\,\Omega$

**Step 1 — Compute span.**

$$
\text{Span} = P_{\max} - P_{\min} = 150 - (-50) = 200\,°\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Fractional deviation.**

$$
\text{Fraction} = \frac{78 - (-50)}{200} = \frac{128}{200} = 0.64
$$

**Step 3 — Loop current.**

$$
I = 4 + (16 \times 0.64) = 4 + 10.24 = 14.24\,\text{mA}
$$

**Step 4 — Voltage across burden.**

$$
V = I \times R = 14.24 \times 10^{-3} \times 250 = 3.56\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage of span.**

$$
\%\,\text{span} = \frac{14.24 - 4}{16} \times 100 = 64\%
$$

> **Final Answer:** $I = 14.24\,\text{mA}$, $V = 3.56\,\text{V}$, Span = **64%** of range.

**(7 marks: each step clearly shown)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Compare **HART**, **Foundation Fieldbus**, and **PROFIBUS PA** as field-device connectivity protocols. Mention the physical layer signal, maximum devices per segment, and topology supported by each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A HART transmitter is connected to a controller through **1200 m** of cable with resistance **55 Ω/km**. Supply voltage is **24 V DC** and the transmitter requires at least **12 V DC** to operate. Find:
(i) Total loop (round-trip) resistance.
(ii) Maximum current that can flow without violating the transmitter voltage constraint.
(iii) State whether the loop can deliver the full 20 mA. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Protocol Comparison

| Feature | HART | Foundation Fieldbus (FF) | PROFIBUS PA |
|---------|------|--------------------------|-------------|
| Physical Signal | 4–20 mA + FSK overlay | Manchester-encoded digital, bus-powered | Manchester-encoded digital, bus-powered |
| Frequencies | 1200 Hz / 2200 Hz | 31.25 kbps | 31.25 kbps |
| Topology | Point-to-point / Multi-drop (≤15) | Bus / Tree (≤32 devices) | Bus (≤32 devices) |
| Power on bus | No (device-powered) | Yes | Yes |
| Data rate | 1200 bps | 31.25 kbps | 31.25 kbps |
| Application | Legacy modernization | New greenfield plants | Process automation (Europe) |
| Cabling | 2-wire | 2-wire | 2-wire |

**(7 marks: 2 for HART, 2.5 for FF, 2.5 for PA — include topology & device count)**

---

#### Part (b) — HART Loop Cable Analysis

**Given:** $L = 1200\,\text{m}$, $r = 55\,\Omega/\text{km}$, $V_{\text{supply}} = 24\,\text{V}$, $V_{\min,\text{dev}} = 12\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1 — Round-trip length.**

$$
L_{\text{eff}} = 2 \times 1200 = 2400\,\text{m} = 2.4\,\text{km}
$$

**Step 2 — Total cable resistance.**

$$
R_{\text{cable}} = 55 \times 2.4 = 132\,\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum allowable current (KVL).**

$$
I_{\max} = \frac{V_{\text{supply}} - V_{\min,\text{dev}}}{R_{\text{cable}}} = \frac{24 - 12}{132} = \frac{12}{132} = 0.0909\,\text{A} \approx 90.9\,\text{mA}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

Since $I_{\max} = 90.9\,\text{mA} \;>\; 20\,\text{mA}$, the loop **can deliver the full 20 mA** comfortably.

> **Final Answer:** $R_{\text{cable}} = 132\,\Omega$, $I_{\max} = 90.9\,\text{mA}$, **Full 20 mA delivery possible**.

**(7 marks)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Errors in Field-Device Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting round-trip cable length** (using single distance instead of 2L) — loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing burden resistor value** (using 600 Ω HART limit instead of 250 Ω standard) — loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing HART frequencies** (writing 1200 Hz for both states) — loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Writing PV-to-mA formula without specifying 4 mA live-zero offset** — loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Treating multi-drop HART as analog** (still expecting 4–20 mA scaling) — full question rejected; multi-drop current is fixed at **4 mA**.
> 6. **Not stating units** in the final answer (mA vs A, Ω vs kΩ) — examiner deducts 0.5–1 mark per numerical.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Field devices live at Level 0** of the Purdue Model — sensors, transmitters, actuators, valves.
- 🔹 **4–20 mA** is the universal industrial analog signal; **4 mA = live zero** for fault detection.
- 🔹 **Standard burden resistor = 250 Ω** → produces **1 V to 5 V** for ADC input.
- 🔹 **HART FSK** uses **1200 Hz (1)** and **2200 Hz (0)**, amplitude ±0.5 mA, average zero (no effect on analog).
- 🔹 **HART multi-drop** allows up to **15 devices** on a single pair of wires; current fixed at **4 mA**.
- 🔹 **Foundation Fieldbus & PROFIBUS PA** use **31.25 kbps** Manchester coding on 2-wire bus, fully digital.
- 🔹 **PV-to-Current formula**: $I = 4 + 16 \times \dfrac{PV - P_{\min}}{P_{\max} - P_{\min}}$.
- 🔹 **Loop cable analysis** must always use **round-trip length** = $2 \times L$.
- 🔹 **Smart devices** offer diagnostics, remote calibration, and multi-variable transmission.
- 🔹 **Voltage compliance check** before installation: $V_{\text{supply}} - I_{\max} \cdot R_{\text{cable}} \geq V_{\min,\text{device}}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mobile Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📱 MOBILE DEVICES IN INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A **Mobile Device** in the context of industrial networks refers to a portable, battery-powered, wireless-enabled computing endpoint that operates within an industrial communication infrastructure to perform data acquisition, supervisory control, asset tracking, maintenance diagnostics, and field-level human-machine interaction (HMI) functions.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEICT747 – Industrial Networks)** curriculum, mobile devices in industrial environments extend beyond consumer-grade smartphones and tablets. They encompass **ruggedized handheld computers**, **wearable terminals**, **vehicle-mounted computers (VMCs)**, **tablets**, **smartphones**, and **specialized field-data terminals** that communicate over industrial wireless protocols such as **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**, **Bluetooth**, **ZigBee**, **LoRaWAN**, and **5G private networks**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In industrial networks, mobile devices are classified not by their consumer use but by their **operational role** — namely **Operator Interface**, **Maintenance Terminal**, **Field Data Collector**, **Asset Tracker**, and **Mobile SCADA Client**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🏭

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a factory floor as a human body. The **PLCs and DCS controllers** are the **nervous system**, the **sensors and actuators** are the **senses and muscles**, and the **mobile devices** are the **doctor's portable diagnostic tools** — they roam freely, communicate wirelessly with the central nervous system (control network), and let the doctor (operator/technician) check the pulse (process variables), diagnose issues, and prescribe fixes (control commands) from anywhere on the shop floor.

Imagine a maintenance engineer walking through a large petrochemical plant. Instead of being tethered to a control room desktop, they carry a **ruggedized tablet** that:
1. **Connects** wirelessly to the plant's Wi-Fi network.
2. **Receives** real-time process data from SCADA via **OPC UA** or **MQTT**.
3. **Sends** set-point changes or acknowledges alarms.
4. **Logs** maintenance activities directly into the **CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System)**.

This is the essence of **mobility in industrial networks** — bringing information and control to where the work happens.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Battery life of industrial rugged tablets:** typically **8–14 hours** under continuous operation.
- **Operating temperature range:** **−20 °C to +60 °C** for rugged devices (vs. 0–35 °C for consumer tablets).
- **IP rating:** typically **IP65 or IP67** for industrial-grade dust/water resistance.
- **Drop resistance:** **1.5 m to 2.4 m** onto concrete (MIL-STD-810G).
- **Wireless range (industrial Wi-Fi):** up to **200 m** outdoors, **30–50 m** indoors with industrial access points.
- **Industrial 5G latency target:** less than **1 ms** (URLLC — Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication).

## 1.4 Classification of Mobile Devices in Industrial Use

| Category | Example Use | Form Factor |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Smartphones** | Alarm notification, remote monitoring | Handheld pocket device |
| **Rugged Tablets** | Full SCADA client, maintenance manual | 7"–12" screen |
| **Handheld Computers** | Barcode/RFID scanning, inventory | Pistol-grip or brick form |
| **Wearable Terminals** | Hands-free picking, line-side data | Wrist-mounted |
| **Vehicle-Mounted Computers (VMC)** | Forklift terminals, AGV control | Mounted on vehicles |
| **Industrial PDAs** | Legacy field-data terminals | Compact brick |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 Architecture of an Industrial Mobile Device

An industrial mobile device is essentially a **layered computing platform** that must satisfy three industrial constraints: **reliability**, **real-time response**, and **environmental ruggedness**.

### Layered Functional Breakdown:

**Layer 1 — Hardware Layer (Physical)**
- **Processor (SoC):** ARM-based multi-core (Qualcomm, NXP i.MX) optimized for low power and real-time I/O.
- **Memory:** Typically **4 GB – 16 GB RAM**, **64 GB – 256 GB storage** (eMMC or SSD).
- **Display:** High-brightness LCD/OLED (**≥ 1000 nits**) readable in direct sunlight.
- **Sensors:** Barcode scanner (1D/2D imager), RFID reader (HF/UHF), GPS, accelerometer, gyroscope, ambient light sensor.
- **Connectivity Radios:** Wi-Fi (802.11ax), Bluetooth 5.x, NFC, optional 4G/5G modem.
- **Power:** Removable/rechargeable Li-ion battery, hot-swap support for continuous operation.
- **Enclosure:** Magnesium alloy chassis, **IP65/IP67** sealing, **MIL-STD-810H** certification.

**Layer 2 — Operating System Layer**
- **Android (AOSP / Android Enterprise Recommended)** — most common OS for rugged devices.
- **Windows IoT** — legacy support for industrial applications.
- **Linux-based RTOS variants** — for embedded mobile controllers.

**Layer 3 — Middleware / Communication Layer**
- **OPC UA client** — secure, platform-independent communication with PLCs/SCADA.
- **MQTT client** — lightweight publish/subscribe for IIoT telemetry.
- **Modbus TCP / PROFINET / EtherNet/IP** libraries for direct PLC access.
- **REST/GraphQL APIs** for cloud and ERP integration.

**Layer 4 — Application Layer**
- SCADA HMI clients (e.g., Ignition Perspective, WinCC Unified).
- CMMS maintenance apps (SAP PM, IBM Maximo).
- AR-assisted maintenance (HoloLens integration).
- Mobile workflow and e-form applications.

## 2.2 Why Mobile Devices Are Essential in Modern Industrial Networks

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" — Core Drivers of Mobility in Industry:**

1. **Decoupling the Operator from the Control Room:** Modern plants span kilometers; mobile devices eliminate the need to walk back to a fixed HMI.
2. **Real-Time Data at the Point of Action:** Maintenance engineers need live sensor data *while standing next to the machine*.
3. **Improved Response Time:** Average alarm acknowledgment drops from minutes to **seconds** with mobile alerts.
4. **Closed-Loop Mobile Maintenance:** Enable technicians to log work orders, capture photos, scan barcodes, and update asset history from the field.
5. **Labor Optimization in Logistics:** Pickers equipped with rugged wearables can increase warehouse throughput by **20–40%**.
6. **Regulatory Compliance:** Electronic field logs replace paper records, satisfying **FDA 21 CFR Part 11** and **ISO 9001** traceability requirements.

## 2.3 Communication Protocols for Industrial Mobile Devices

| Protocol | Layer | Use Case | Data Rate |
|----------|-------|----------|-----------|
| **Wi-Fi 6/6E (802.11ax)** | Data Link / PHY | Plant-wide mobile coverage | up to **9.6 Gbps** |
| **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE 5.x)** | Data Link | Beacons, sensor telemetry | **2 Mbps** |
| **ZigBee (802.15.4)** | Data Link | Wireless sensor mesh | **250 kbps** |
| **LoRaWAN** | Data Link | Long-range, low-power asset tracking | **0.3 – 50 kbps** |
| **5G URLLC** | PHY/Data Link | AGV control, AR/VR maintenance | **< 1 ms latency** |
| **OPC UA over UDP** | Application | Mobile SCADA push notifications | session-based |
| **MQTT over TLS** | Application | Cloud-IIoT bridge | lightweight pub/sub |

## 2.4 Mobile Device Management (MDM) in Industry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MDM Definition:** Mobile Device Management is the centralized administration of mobile endpoints — enforcing security policies, distributing applications, remotely wiping lost devices, and monitoring compliance — typically through platforms like **Microsoft Intune**, **VMware AirWatch**, **IBM MaaS360**, or **SOTI MobiControl**.

MDM functions in industrial settings include:
- **Device enrollment** and certificate-based authentication (802.1X EAP-TLS).
- **Application whitelisting/blacklisting.**
- **Geofencing** — restricting device functionality outside plant premises.
- **Patch management** — OTA OS and firmware updates.
- **Remote lock and selective wipe** for lost/stolen devices.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|-------------------|------------|--------------|
| **Battery endurance** | $T_{bat} = \dfrac{C_{mAh}}{I_{avg}}$ | hours, with $C$ = capacity, $I_{avg}$ = average current draw |
| **Wireless range (Friis)** | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | watts, free-space path loss model |
| **Path loss (log-distance)** | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$ | dB, $n$ = path loss exponent |
| **Wi-Fi capacity** | $C = N_{SS} \cdot B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SINR})$ | bits/s, Shannon capacity |
| **MQTT QoS levels** | $\text{QoS} \in \{0, 1, 2\}$ | 0 = at-most-once, 1 = at-least-once, 2 = exactly-once |
| **5G URLLC latency budget** | $\tau_{total} = \tau_{tx} + \tau_{prop} + \tau_{proc}$ | ms, sum of transmit, propagation, processing |
| **Inventory accuracy** | $\eta_{inv} = 1 - \dfrac{N_{err}}{N_{total}}$ | dimensionless ratio |
| **MDM device count** | $N_{dev} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i$ | total managed endpoints |
| **Rugged drop spec** | $H_{drop} \approx \sqrt{2gh}$ with $h \in [1.5, 2.4]$ m | drop height |
| **Picking throughput gain** | $\Delta T = \dfrac{T_{paper} - T_{mobile}}{T_{paper}} \times 100\%$ | % improvement |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility 🌍

- **Oil & Gas:** Field engineers use **intrinsically safe (ATEX/IECEx)** tablets to read pressure transmitter values via **WirelessHART** without entering hazardous enclosures.
- **Automotive Manufacturing:** **Vehicle-mounted tablets** on assembly line trolleys display work instructions synced from **MES** in real time.
- **Power Utilities:** **Smartphones** receive **SCADA push alarms** via **MQTT-SN** over cellular, enabling field crews to respond to grid faults within minutes.
- **Warehousing:** Pickers wearing **Android wearables** with **voice-pick technology** can achieve hands-free, eyes-free order fulfillment at **> 100 picks/hour**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Wireless Range and Path Loss for an Industrial Mobile Device

### Problem Setup
A maintenance tablet operates in a plant where the Wi-Fi access point (AP) transmits at $P_t = \textbf{20 dBm}$ with antenna gain $G_t = \textbf{3 dBi}$. The tablet receiver has $G_r = \textbf{2 dBi}$ and receiver sensitivity $P_{r,min} = \textbf{-82 dBm}$. Determine the **maximum operating distance** in an indoor industrial environment with path loss exponent $n = \textbf{3.2}$ (typical heavy-clutter factory).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert transmit power to dBm and watts.**

$$
P_t \text{[dBm]} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_t \text{[mW]}}{1 \text{ mW}}\right)
$$

For $P_t = 20$ dBm:

$$
P_t = 10^{(20/10)} = 100 \text{ mW} = 0.1 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the received signal strength equation (free-space Friis model).**

$$
P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

Expressed in dB:

$$
P_r \text{[dBm]} = P_t \text{[dBm]} + G_t \text{[dBi]} + G_r \text{[dBi]} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Use the log-distance path loss model (more realistic indoors).**

$$
PL(d) \text{[dB]} = PL(d_0) + 10 \, n \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)
$$

where:
- $d_0 = 1$ m (reference distance)
- $PL(d_0) = 40.2$ dB at 2.4 GHz (free-space reference)
- $n = 3.2$

**Step 4 — Set received power equal to receiver sensitivity.**

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL(d)
$$

Substitute:

$$
-82 = 20 + 3 + 2 - PL(d_{\max})
$$

$$
PL(d_{\max}) = 20 + 3 + 2 + 82 = 107 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for maximum distance.**

$$
PL(d_{\max}) = PL(d_0) + 10 \, n \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d_{\max}}{d_0}\right)
$$

$$
107 = 40.2 + 10(3.2) \log_{10}(d_{\max})
$$

$$
107 - 40.2 = 32 \log_{10}(d_{\max})
$$

$$
66.8 = 32 \log_{10}(d_{\max})
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d_{\max}) = \dfrac{66.8}{32} = 2.0875
$$

$$
d_{\max} = 10^{2.0875} \approx \mathbf{122.3 \text{ m}}
$$

### Interpretation
At maximum, the tablet can roam up to **~122 m** from the AP while maintaining a usable Wi-Fi link. Practical limits (multipath, metal obstructions, EMI from VFDs) typically reduce this to **40–60 m** in a heavy industrial environment, requiring **strategically placed APs with directional antennas**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Battery Endurance for an Industrial Rugged Tablet

### Problem Setup
A rugged tablet has a battery rated at $C = \textbf{7800 mAh}$ at nominal voltage $V = \textbf{7.6 V}$. Average current draw during continuous Wi-Fi data logging is $I_{avg} = \textbf{620 mA}$. Compute the operating endurance.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert capacity to watt-hours.**

$$
E_{bat} = C \cdot V = 7.8 \text{ Ah} \times 7.6 \text{ V} = 59.28 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate average power draw.**

$$
P_{avg} = I_{avg} \cdot V = 0.62 \text{ A} \times 7.6 \text{ V} = 4.712 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute endurance.**

$$
T_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{avg}} = \dfrac{59.28 \text{ Wh}}{4.712 \text{ W}} \approx \mathbf{12.58 \text{ hours}}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation
This matches the manufacturer's typical **10–14 hour** claim under standard usage. For a full 12-hour shift, the engineer can use the tablet continuously without recharging — a **hard requirement** for industrial deployment.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mobile Device Selection Criteria

| Criterion | Consumer Tablet | Industrial Rugged Tablet | Industrial Smartphone |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| **Drop rating** | 0.7 m | **1.5–2.4 m** (MIL-STD-810H) | 1.2–1.5 m |
| **IP rating** | IP42 | **IP65 / IP67** | IP68 |
| **Operating temp** | 0 to 35 °C | **−20 to +60 °C** | −10 to +50 °C |
| **Battery (continuous)** | 6–8 h | **10–14 h (hot-swap)** | 8–12 h |
| **Integrated scanner** | None | **1D/2D imager + RFID** | Optional |
| **Display brightness** | 400–600 nits | **≥ 1000 nits** | 700–1000 nits |
| **Approx. cost** | $300–800 | **$1,500–4,000** | $600–1,500 |
| **MDM support** | Limited | **Native enterprise support** | Native (Android Enterprise) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Picking Throughput Gain from Mobile Wearables

### Problem Setup
A warehouse previously used paper pick lists. Average time per pick: $T_{paper} = 75$ s. After deploying mobile wearables with voice-pick, average pick time: $T_{mobile} = 50$ s. Compute throughput gain.

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{T_{paper} - T_{mobile}}{T_{paper}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{75 - 50}{75} \times 100\% = \dfrac{25}{75} \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{33.3\%}
$$

### Interpretation
A **33.3% productivity gain** is typical and aligns with industry reports (Zebra Technologies' *Warehouse Vision Study*) showing **20–40% gains** in mobile-equipped warehouses.

---
