# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a "ruggedized mobile device" used in industrial networks. List any four key environmental specifications it must meet.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **ruggedized mobile device** is a portable computing endpoint specifically engineered to operate reliably in harsh industrial environments such as factories, oil rigs, and outdoor field sites.

Four key specifications:
1. **Drop resistance** — must survive **1.5–2.4 m** drops onto concrete (MIL-STD-810H).
2. **Ingress Protection** — at least **IP65** rating against dust and water jets.
3. **Operating temperature** — wide range typically **−20 °C to +60 °C**.
4. **Vibration and shock resistance** — certified to withstand industrial machinery-induced vibrations.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for specifications with 0.5 each)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is Mobile Device Management (MDM)? Mention any three functions performed by an MDM system in an industrial plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **MDM** is a centralized administrative framework used to manage, secure, and monitor mobile endpoints deployed across an organization.

Three MDM functions:
1. **Remote lock and selective wipe** of lost or stolen devices to prevent data leakage.
2. **Application whitelisting and patch management** to ensure only approved SCADA/CMMS apps run and OS is up to date.
3. **Certificate-based device authentication** (e.g., 802.1X EAP-TLS) so only enrolled devices join the plant Wi-Fi.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for functions with ~0.66 each)*

---

## 📕 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **layered architecture of an industrial mobile device** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each layer in enabling field-level industrial communication.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Compare **Wi-Fi 6**, **Bluetooth Low Energy**, and **5G URLLC** as wireless technologies for mobile devices in industrial automation. State one specific use case for each.

---

### **Model Solution A(a) — Layered Architecture (7 marks)**

The industrial mobile device is best understood as a **four-layer stack**:

**1. Hardware Layer (2 marks):**
- **SoC / Processor** — ARM-based multi-core for low-power real-time computation.
- **Sensors** — barcode imager, RFID, GPS, accelerometer, ambient light.
- **Radios** — Wi-Fi 6, BLE 5.x, optional 4G/5G.
- **Rugged enclosure** — IP65+, MIL-STD-810H, hot-swap Li-ion battery.
- **High-brightness display (≥ 1000 nits)** for sunlight readability.

**2. Operating System Layer (1.5 marks):**
- Android (AOSP/Android Enterprise), Windows IoT, or RTOS-Linux.
- Provides process scheduling, memory protection (SELinux), and device drivers for industrial peripherals.

**3. Middleware / Communication Layer (1.5 marks):**
- **OPC UA client** — primary industrial protocol for PLC/SCADA communication.
- **MQTT client** — lightweight publish/subscribe for IIoT telemetry to the cloud.
- **Modbus TCP / PROFINET / EtherNet/IP libraries** — direct PLC interaction.
- **REST/GraphQL APIs** — ERP and CMMS integration.

**4. Application Layer (2 marks):**
- SCADA HMI clients (Ignition Perspective), CMMS apps, AR maintenance overlays, WMS picking apps, mobile e-forms for compliance and quality audits.

**Role in field communication:** The hardware layer captures physical signals (barcode scans, sensor readings). The OS layer orchestrates them. The middleware layer translates them into **standardized industrial protocols** that any plant asset can understand. The application layer presents information to the field engineer in human-readable form, enabling **decisions and actions at the point of work**.

---

### **Model Solution A(b) — Wireless Technology Comparison (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) | Bluetooth LE 5.x | 5G URLLC |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------|----------|
| **Frequency** | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz | 2.4 GHz | Sub-6 GHz / mmWave |
| **Max data rate** | **9.6 Gbps** | 2 Mbps | **20 Gbps** (theoretical) |
| **Latency** | ~10 ms | ~6 ms | **< 1 ms** |
| **Range** | 30–200 m | 10–100 m | Up to several km |
| **Power** | High | **Very low** | Medium–High |
| **Mobility support** | Excellent | Excellent (beacon) | Excellent (seamless handover) |
| **Industrial use case** | Plant-wide **mobile SCADA tablets** | **Wireless beacons** for indoor asset tracking | **AGV / robotic control** requiring deterministic latency |

**Specific Use Cases:**
1. **Wi-Fi 6 →** Maintenance engineer using a rugged tablet for **full SCADA HMI access** across the factory floor.
2. **BLE 5.x →** Indoor **beacon-based positioning** of tools and small assets with year-long coin-cell batteries.
3. **5G URLLC →** Real-time control of **autonomous mobile robots (AMRs)** where sub-millisecond response is non-negotiable.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **role of mobile devices in industrial automation** with at least **five real-world industrial use cases**. For each, identify the wireless technology and industrial protocol used.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With the help of a diagram, explain how **MQTT-based push notification architecture** delivers SCADA alarms from a plant to an engineer's mobile device. List three security considerations.

---

### **Model Solution B(a) — Industrial Use Cases (7 marks)**

| # | Use Case | Wireless Technology | Industrial Protocol |
|---|----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Remote SCADA monitoring** on plant floor | Wi-Fi 6 | OPC UA |
| 2 | **Warehouse picking** with wearable scanners | Wi-Fi 6 + BLE | WMS REST API |
| 3 | **Predictive maintenance** data logging on machine | BLE 5.x | MQTT to cloud |
| 4 | **Oil rig field data acquisition** | Cellular 4G/5G | Modbus TCP over TLS |
| 5 | **Forklift/AGV guidance** | 5G URLLC | PROFINET over 5G |
| 6 | **Smart meter reading** at substations | LoRaWAN | DLMS/COSEM |
| 7 | **Pipeline leak detection** | Satellite / cellular | MQTT-SN |

For each, **1 mark** for clearly stating use case, wireless tech, and protocol in context. *(≈ 0.5 × 6 = 3 marks distributed)*; remaining **4 marks** for the explanation narrative covering why mobility adds value (operator proximity, faster response, electronic records, etc.).

---

### **Model Solution B(b) — MQTT Alarm Push Architecture (7 marks)**

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
┌─────────────┐    Modbus/OPC UA    ┌──────────────┐
│  PLC / RTU  │ ──────────────────► │ SCADA Server │
└─────────────┘                     └──────┬───────┘
                                            │ Publish
                                            ▼
                                    ┌──────────────┐
                                    │ MQTT Broker  │
                                    │  (Mosquitto) │
                                    └──────┬───────┘
                                           │ Subscribe (TLS 1.3)
                              ┌────────────┼────────────┐
                              ▼            ▼            ▼
                        [Smartphone]  [Rugged Tab]  [Watch]
```

**Explanation (2 marks):**
The PLC detects an alarm condition (e.g., high pressure). The SCADA server identifies this as an alarm, formats it as a JSON payload, and **publishes** it to a specific topic (e.g., `plant/area3/press101/alarm`) on the local MQTT broker. The mobile devices have a persistent **MQTT subscription** to relevant alarm topics via TLS-secured WebSockets. On receiving the message, the mobile app triggers a push notification, audible alarm, and vibrates — all within **< 2 seconds**.

**Three Security Considerations (2 marks):**
1. **Mutual TLS authentication** between device and broker to prevent rogue subscribers.
2. **X.509 certificate-based device enrollment** through MDM.
3. **Topic-level access control lists (ACLs)** so engineers only see alarms for their authorized plant area.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing consumer-grade specs with industrial specs** — students often write "IP rating = IP54" which is **insufficient**; industrial demands **IP65+**.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention MDM** — in any question about mobile devices, the examiner expects at least a mention of **device management, security policies, and certificate-based authentication**.
> 3. **Missing the protocol layer** — students describe hardware and OS but forget that the *industrial* value of a mobile device lies in its **middleware (OPC UA/MQTT) integration**.
> 4. **Skipping diagrams** — KTU valuation gives **1–2 marks** specifically for neat labelled diagrams; a missing block diagram can cost a full sub-question's weight.
> 5. **Ignoring latency numbers** — for real-time industrial use, vague "fast" is unacceptable; always cite **< 1 ms (5G URLLC)** vs **~10 ms (Wi-Fi)** vs **6 ms (BLE)**.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** A mobile device in industrial networks is a portable, wireless-enabled, often ruggedized computing endpoint used for field-level data acquisition, monitoring, control, and maintenance.
- [x] **Five use-case categories:** Operator Interface, Maintenance Terminal, Field Data Collector, Asset Tracker, Mobile SCADA Client.
- [x] **Rugged specs:** IP65+, MIL-STD-810H, −20 to +60 °C, 1.5–2.4 m drop, ≥ 10 hr battery.
- [x] **OS layer:** Android Enterprise dominates; Windows IoT for legacy; RTOS-Linux for embedded.
- [x] **Middleware layer:** OPC UA (machine-to-machine), MQTT (cloud telemetry), Modbus TCP / PROFINET / EtherNet/IP (field protocols).
- [x] **Wireless options:** Wi-Fi 6 (broadband plant coverage), BLE (low-power sensors), 5G URLLC (real-time motion control), LoRaWAN (long-range low-power).
- [x] **MDM is mandatory** — certificate-based auth, app whitelisting, remote wipe, geofencing, OTA patching.
- [x] **Formula to remember:** $T_{bat} = C/I_{avg}$, $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n\log_{10}(d/d_0)$, Picking gain $\Delta T = (T_{paper}-T_{mobile})/T_{paper}$.
- [x] **ISA-95 mapping:** Mobile devices span **L0–L4** depending on use — from field asset trackers to executive KPI dashboards.
- [x] **Security triad:** Mutual TLS + X.509 certs + topic-level ACLs (MQTT) is the gold standard.
- [x] **Industrial 5G latency:** **< 1 ms** for URLLC — remember this number for real-time control questions.
- [x] **Throughput gain:** Mobile picking typically delivers **20–40%** productivity improvement over paper.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operating Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Devices** are the field-level hardware components deployed in an industrial automation system that interact directly with the physical process. They form the sensory (input) and effector (output) layers of the automation pyramid, enabling the conversion of physical phenomena into electrical signals (via **sensors/transducers**) and electrical signals into mechanical/physical actions (via **actuators**).

In the context of **KTU PEICT747 (Industrial Networks, 2024 Scheme)**, operating devices are classified under the **Field Level** of the ISA-95/Purdue automation hierarchy, interfacing directly with **PLCs, DCS, SCADA, and Industrial IoT (IIoT)** gateways through standardized industrial communication protocols.

## 1.2 Classification of Operating Devices

Operating devices are broadly grouped into the following categories:

| Class | Direction | Function | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensors** | Input (Process → Controller) | Convert physical variables to electrical signals | RTD, Thermocouple, LVDT, Photoelectric sensor |
| **Actuators** | Output (Controller → Process) | Convert electrical signals to physical action | Solenoid valve, Motor, Pneumatic cylinder |
| **Transducers** | Bidirectional | Convert one form of energy to another | Piezoelectric crystals, Ultrasonic sensors |
| **Indicators / HMI Devices** | Output (Display) | Display process status to operator | Pilot lamps, Buzzers, HMI Panels |
| **Manual Input Devices** | Input (Human → System) | Allow operator commands | Push buttons, Selector switches, Keypads |
| **Protection Devices** | Passive/Safety | Protect circuit and personnel | Fuses, MCBs, Safety relays, E-Stops |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Human Body

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of an industrial plant as a human body:**
> - **Sensors = Nerves & Senses** (touch, temperature, vision) — they perceive the world and send signals to the brain.
> - **Controller (PLC/DCS) = Brain** — receives signals, decides what to do.
> - **Actuators = Muscles & Limbs** — execute the brain's commands (move, push, open, close).
> - **HMI/Indicators = Voice & Expressions** — communicate the internal state outward.
> - **Manual Input Devices = Hands of the operator** — direct intervention.

Just as the body cannot function without nerves and muscles, an automated plant cannot operate without sensors (input) and actuators (output) — collectively called **Operating Devices**.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Field Definitions)

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard metrics are universally used by industry to grade the quality of any operating device — important for KTU short-answer questions:

- **Range** — The span between the lowest and highest measurable value.
- **Accuracy** — Closeness of the reading to the true value.
- **Precision / Repeatability** — Ability to produce the same reading under identical conditions.
- **Resolution** — Smallest detectable change in input.
- **Sensitivity** — Ratio of output signal change to input variable change.
- **Linearity** — How closely the output follows a straight-line relationship with input.
- **Hysteresis** — Maximum difference in output for the same input approached from opposite directions.
- **Dead Band / Dead Zone** — Range of input where no output change occurs.
- **Drift** — Slow change of output with time at constant input.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification with Operating Principles

### A. Sensors (Input Devices)

**Sensors** convert a physical stimulus (temperature, pressure, flow, level, position, light, etc.) into an electrically measurable quantity.

#### 1. Temperature Sensors
- **RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector):** Resistance changes with temperature. Platinum (Pt100/Pt1000) is common.
- **Thermocouple (TC):** Two dissimilar metals generate a Seebeck EMF at the junction.
- **Thermistor:** Semiconductor-based; high sensitivity, non-linear response.
- **IC Sensor (e.g., LM35):** Analog voltage output proportional to temperature.

#### 2. Pressure Sensors
- **Strain Gauge Type:** A diaphragm deflects under pressure, altering the resistance of bonded strain gauges (Wheatstone bridge).
- **Capacitive Type:** Deflection changes the gap of a capacitor.
- **Piezoelectric Type:** Generates charge proportional to applied pressure (dynamic only).

#### 3. Flow Sensors
- **Orifice Plate + Differential Pressure (DP) transmitter.**
- **Magnetic Flowmeter (Faraday's Law):** $E = B \cdot L \cdot v$
- **Vortex Shedder (Strouhal Number based).**
- **Coriolis Mass Flowmeter.**

#### 4. Level Sensors
- **Ultrasonic:** Time-of-flight measurement.
- **Capacitive:** Change in capacitance with level.
- **Float / Conductivity / Radar / Laser-based.**

#### 5. Position / Displacement Sensors
- **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer):** Excellent linearity.
- **Encoder (Incremental / Absolute):** Digital pulse output.
- **Proximity Sensor (Inductive, Capacitive, Magnetic).**
- **Photoelectric Sensor (Through-beam, Retro-reflective, Diffuse).**

### B. Actuators (Output Devices)

**Actuators** take electrical commands from the controller and produce physical action.

#### 1. Electrical Actuators
- **Solenoid** — Converts electrical energy to linear motion via magnetic field.
- **DC Motor / Servo Motor / Stepper Motor** — Rotational motion.
- **AC Induction Motor / BLDC Motor.**
- **Relay / Contactor** — Electrically operated switch.

#### 2. Hydraulic Actuators
- Use incompressible fluid; capable of very high force output.
- Components: Pump, Valve, Cylinder/Motor.

#### 3. Pneumatic Actuators
- Use compressed air; clean, fast, ideal for food/pharma.
- Components: Compressor, FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator), Cylinder, Solenoid Valve.

### C. Indicators & Manual Input Devices

- **Pilot Lamps / Tower Lights / Buzzers** — Visual and audible indication.
- **Push Buttons (NO/NC), Selector Switches, Key Switches, Mushroom-head E-Stop.**
- **Selector & Potentiometer** — Continuous input adjustment.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Operating Devices in Industrial Networks

- **Why?** Without operating devices, controllers receive no data and cannot influence the process. They are the *physical interface* between the cyber (network/controller) world and the physical process.
- **How?** Modern operating devices often integrate **microprocessors**, becoming **"smart sensors"** or **"intelligent actuators"** that perform self-calibration, linearization, diagnostics, and communicate via **digital bus protocols** (HART, IO-Link, Foundation Fieldbus, PROFIBUS-PA).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Operating Device Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the high-yield formula table for KTU board exam numericals/derivations. Memorize these:

| # | Metric | Formula | Symbol Notes | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sensitivity** | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | $y$ = output, $x$ = input | (output unit)/(input unit) |
| 2 | **Resolution** | $R = \dfrac{\text{FS range}}{2^N}$ | $N$ = ADC bits | Engineering unit |
| 3 | **Linearity (Non-linearity %)** | $NL = \dfrac{\text{Max deviation from ideal}}{\text{FS output}} \times 100$ | — | % |
| 4 | **Hysteresis** | $H = \dfrac{y_{\text{inc}} - y_{\text{dec}}}{\text{FS}} \times 100$ | inc = increasing, dec = decreasing input | % |
| 5 | **Repeatability** | $R_p = \dfrac{\text{Max spread}}{\text{FS}} \times 100$ | Spread of repeated readings | % |
| 6 | **Accuracy** | $A = \pm \dfrac{e_{\max}}{\text{FS}} \times 100$ | $e_{\max}$ = max error | % |
| 7 | **Thermocouple EMF** | $E = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (S_B - S_A) \, dT$ | $S_A, S_B$ = Seebeck coefficients | Volts |
| 8 | **RTD Resistance** | $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$ | $\alpha$ = temp. coeff. | $\Omega$ |
| 9 | **Magnetic Flow EMF** | $E = B \cdot L \cdot v$ | Faraday's Law | Volts |
| 10 | **Piezo Charge** | $Q = d \cdot F$ | $d$ = piezoelectric constant | Coulombs |
| 11 | **Ultrasonic Distance** | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | $v$ = speed of sound, $t$ = round trip time | m |
| 12 | **LVDT Output** | $V_o = V_s \cdot \dfrac{N_p}{N_s} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | Mutual inductance-based | Volts |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Relevance of Operating Devices:**
> - In **smart factories (Industry 4.0)**, operating devices with **IO-Link** or **HART-IP** provide rich diagnostic data, enabling **predictive maintenance**.
> - In **process plants**, smart transmitters (Rosemount, Yokogawa) replace analog 4–20 mA devices, reducing wiring costs by up to **60%**.
> - In **automotive assembly lines**, smart actuators with embedded CAN bus nodes provide closed-loop position control.
> - In **hazardous areas (Zone 1/2)**, intrinsically safe (IS) operating devices limit energy so no spark can ignite.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are exhaustive derivations that have been asked multiple times in KTU university examinations. Do not skip steps; this is where full-mark answers are built.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sensitivity, Range, and Resolution of an Operating Device

Consider a pressure sensor with:
- Input range: **0 to 10 bar**
- Output: **4 to 20 mA**
- ADC bits: **12-bit**

### Step 1: Define Full-Scale (FS) Range

$$\text{FS}_{\text{input}} = 10 - 0 = 10 \text{ bar}$$

### Step 2: Define Full-Scale Output Span

$$\text{FS}_{\text{output}} = 20 - 4 = 16 \text{ mA}$$

### Step 3: Sensitivity (S)

Sensitivity is the slope — output change per unit input change.

$$S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{\text{FS}_{\text{output}}}{\text{FS}_{\text{input}}}$$

$$S = \frac{16 \text{ mA}}{10 \text{ bar}} = 1.6 \text{ mA/bar}$$

**Meaning:** Every 1 bar increase in pressure produces a **1.6 mA** increase in output current.

### Step 4: Resolution (R)

Resolution is the smallest input change detectable, determined by the ADC bits.

$$\text{Number of discrete levels} = 2^N = 2^{12} = 4096$$

$$R = \frac{\text{FS}_{\text{input}}}{2^N - 1} = \frac{10}{4095}$$

$$R \approx 0.00244 \text{ bar}$$

**Meaning:** The sensor can resolve pressure changes as small as **~2.44 mbar**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hysteresis and Repeatability Error

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-style derivation**, often appearing in Part B (14-mark) questions.

A temperature sensor is calibrated by sweeping the input upward (increasing) and then downward (decreasing). The data recorded is shown below:

| Input $T$ (°C) | Output $V$ (Up) (V) | Output $V$ (Down) (V) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.05 |
| 25 | 1.20 | 1.25 |
| 50 | 2.45 | 2.50 |
| 75 | 3.65 | 3.72 |
| 100 | 5.00 | 5.05 |

### Step 1: Compute Output Difference at Each Point

Define hysteresis deviation as $\Delta V = V_{\text{down}} - V_{\text{up}}$ at each input level.

$$\Delta V_0 = 0.05 - 0.00 = 0.05 \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta V_{25} = 1.25 - 1.20 = 0.05 \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta V_{50} = 2.50 - 2.45 = 0.05 \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta V_{75} = 3.72 - 3.65 = 0.07 \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta V_{100} = 5.05 - 5.00 = 0.05 \text{ V}$$

### Step 2: Identify Maximum Deviation

$$\Delta V_{\max} = 0.07 \text{ V (at } T = 75^\circ\text{C)}$$

### Step 3: Compute Hysteresis as Percentage of Full-Scale Output

$$H\% = \frac{\Delta V_{\max}}{\text{FS output}} \times 100 = \frac{0.07}{5.00} \times 100$$

$$H\% = 1.4\%$$

### Step 4: Repeatability (Three Consecutive Readings at $T = 50°C$)

Let readings be: $2.45, 2.47, 2.44$ V.

$$\text{Mean} = \frac{2.45 + 2.47 + 2.44}{3} = 2.453 \text{ V}$$

$$\text{Max deviation from mean} = |2.47 - 2.453| = 0.017 \text{ V}$$

$$R_p\% = \frac{0.017}{5.00} \times 100 = 0.34\%$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RTD (Pt100) Resistance Calculation (Callendar-Van Dusen Linear Form)

> [!NOTE]
> **Frequently asked KTU derivation** — shows understanding of sensor physics.

For Platinum RTD (Pt100), the linear approximation is:

$$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$$

Given:
- $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$ at $0^\circ\text{C}$
- $\alpha = 0.00385 \, /^\circ\text{C}$

### Step 1: Find Resistance at $T = 100^\circ\text{C}$

$$R_{100} = 100 \times (1 + 0.00385 \times 100)$$

$$R_{100} = 100 \times (1 + 0.385)$$

$$R_{100} = 138.5 \, \Omega$$

### Step 2: Find Temperature if Resistance is $R_T = 120 \, \Omega$

$$120 = 100 (1 + 0.00385 T)$$

$$1.2 = 1 + 0.00385 T$$

$$0.2 = 0.00385 T$$

$$T = \frac{0.2}{0.00385} \approx 51.95^\circ\text{C}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Ultrasonic Distance Sensor (Time-of-Flight)

An ultrasonic level sensor sends a 40 kHz pulse downward into a tank. The echo is received after $t = 12 \, \text{ms}$. Speed of sound in air at 20°C is **$v = 343 \, \text{m/s}$**.

### Step 1: Round-Trip Distance

$$d_{\text{round trip}} = v \cdot t = 343 \times 12 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$d_{\text{round trip}} = 4.116 \text{ m}$$

### Step 2: One-Way Distance (Level Height)

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2} = \frac{4.116}{2}$$

$$d = 2.058 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3: Correct for Temperature

If temperature is $T = 40^\circ\text{C}$, $v = 331 + 0.6T = 331 + 0.6 \times 40 = 355 \, \text{m/s}$

$$d = \frac{355 \times 12 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 2.13 \text{ m}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Operating Device by Application

| Application | Best-Suited Operating Device | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Furnace temperature 1200°C | **Type-K Thermocouple** | Wide range, rugged, fast response |
| Precision lab temperature | **Pt100 RTD (Class A)** | High accuracy ($\pm 0.15°C$ at $0°C$) |
| Liquid level in chemical tank | **Capacitive / Guided Wave Radar** | Non-contact, works with corrosive liquids |
| Bottle counting on conveyor | **Photoelectric (Through-beam)** | Long range, reliable detection |
| Valve position feedback | **LVDT** | Linear, frictionless, high accuracy |
| Conveyor belt motor control | **VFD + 3-phase Induction Motor** | Energy efficient variable speed |
| Clean room pneumatic gripper | **Pneumatic cylinder + Solenoid valve** | Clean, no sparks |
| Hydraulic press actuator | **Hydraulic cylinder + servo valve** | High force, precise position control |
| Vibration monitoring | **Piezoelectric accelerometer** | Wide freq range, high sensitivity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are the **block-level functional architecture diagrams** mapping the interactions of operating devices in a typical industrial network. These are designed for KTU board answer sheets where a clear block diagram fetches 2–3 extra marks.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Operating Devices in Industrial Network Architecture

<!-- Mermaid block-level functional architecture flow mapping operating devices in an industrial network -->

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    processA[Physical Process Variable]
    sensorNode1[Sensor RTD Pt100]
    sensorNode2[Pressure Transmitter]
    sensorNode3[Level Sensor Ultrasonic]
    condNode1[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]
    adcNode1[ADC Converter]
    ioNode1[PLC or DCS Controller]
    netNode1[Industrial Network PROFINET MODBUS]
    scadaNode1[SCADA HMI Server]
    actuatorNode1[Pneumatic Control Valve]
    actuatorNode2[Variable Frequency Drive VFD]
    actuatorNode3[Solenoid Operated Valve]
    hmiNode1[Operator HMI Panel]
    cloudNode1[IIoT Cloud Historian]

    processA --> sensorNode1
    processA --> sensorNode2
    processA --> sensorNode3
    sensorNode1 --> condNode1
    sensorNode2 --> condNode1
    sensorNode3 --> condNode1
    condNode1 --> adcNode1
    adcNode1 --> ioNode1
    ioNode1 --> netNode1
    netNode1 --> scadaNode1
    scadaNode1 --> hmiNode1
    scadaNode1 --> cloudNode1
    ioNode1 --> actuatorNode1
    ioNode1 --> actuatorNode2
    ioNode1 --> actuatorNode3
    actuatorNode1 --> processA
    actuatorNode2 --> processA
    actuatorNode3 --> processA
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Left column** → sensors (input side)
- **Center column** → controller + network
- **Right column** → actuators (output side, feeding back to process)
- **Top-right** → operator interface and cloud analytics (Industry 4.0 layer)

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification Tree of Operating Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootNode1[OPERATING DEVICES]
    branchInput[Input Devices]
    branchOutput[Output Devices]
    branchDisplay[Display and Manual Devices]

    rootNode1 --> branchInput
    rootNode1 --> branchOutput
    rootNode1 --> branchDisplay

    branchInput --> subTemp[Temperature RTD Thermocouple Thermistor]
    branchInput --> subPress[Pressure Strain Gauge Capacitive Piezo]
    branchInput --> subFlow[Flow Magnetic Vortex Coriolis]
    branchInput --> subLevel[Level Ultrasonic Capacitive Radar]
    branchInput --> subPos[Position LVDT Encoder Proximity]

    branchOutput --> subElec[Electrical Solenoid Motor Relay]
    branchOutput --> subHyd[Hydraulic Cylinder Pump]
    branchOutput --> subPneu[Pneumatic Cylinder Valve]

    branchDisplay --> subHMI[HMI Touch Panel]
    branchDisplay --> subInd[Indicator Lamp Buzzer]
    branchDisplay --> subMan[Push Button Selector Switch]
```

## 4.3 Smart Sensor Internal Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    physNode1[Physical Stimulus]
    sensElem[Primary Sensing Element]
    condBlock[Signal Conditioning]
    adcBlock[ADC]
    microBlock[Microcontroller DSP]
    memBlock[Non-Volatile Memory Calibration Data]
    protoBlock[Communication Interface HART IO Link PROFIBUS]
    busNode1[Industrial Fieldbus]

    physNode1 --> sensElem
    sensElem --> condBlock
    condBlock --> adcBlock
    adcBlock --> microBlock
    memBlock --> microBlock
    microBlock --> protoBlock
    protoBlock --> busNode1
```

**Key Insight for Exam:** A *smart sensor* differs from a *basic sensor* by the addition of the **microcontroller + memory + digital bus interface** — enabling linearization, self-test, and remote configuration.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on **KTU University Exam (Dec 2023 / June 2024)** pattern. Mark distribution strictly follows the 2024 Scheme.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] — 3 Marks**
**Define an Operating Device. List any four categories of operating devices used in industrial automation.**

**Model Answer:**
An operating device is a field-level hardware component used in an industrial automation system to interface between the controller and the physical process.

Four categories:
1. **Sensors** (e.g., RTD, LVDT) — sense process variables.
2. **Actuators** (e.g., solenoid valve, motor) — perform control action.
3. **Indicators / HMI devices** (e.g., pilot lamps, touch panels) — display status.
4. **Manual input devices** (e.g., push buttons, selector switches) — operator commands.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 3 Marks**
**Distinguish between a Sensor and a Transducer with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sensor | Transducer |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Device that detects a physical quantity and gives an output signal | Device that converts energy from one form to another |
| Scope | Subset of transducer | Broader category |
| Output | Usually electrical | Any form of energy |
| Example | Thermistor, LVDT | Piezoelectric crystal, Speaker |

**Note:** All sensors are transducers, but not all transducers are sensors.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choice between A and B)

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model] — 14 Marks**

**Q.A (a)** Explain with neat diagrams the construction and working of an **LVDT** as a position-sensing operating device. Mention its advantages. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer — Step-by-Step:**

**Construction:**
- A Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT) consists of:
  - One primary winding ($P$) wound at the center of a hollow cylindrical former.
  - Two identical secondary windings ($S_1$ and $S_2$) wound on either side of the primary, connected in series-opposition.
  - A movable soft-iron core slides inside the former.

**Working Principle:**
- Primary is excited by an AC source (typically 1–10 V at 1–10 kHz).
- Voltages $E_{S1}$ and $E_{S2}$ are induced in the secondaries based on mutual inductance with the core.

**Case 1 — Core at Null (Center) Position:**
- $E_{S1} = E_{S2}$, hence net output $E_o = E_{S1} - E_{S2} = 0$.

**Case 2 — Core Displaced Toward $S_1$:**
- $E_{S1} > E_{S2}$, net output is positive and proportional to displacement.

**Case 3 — Core Displaced Toward $S_2$:**
- $E_{S2} > E_{S1}$, net output is **negative**, indicating direction.

**Output Equation (Linear Range):**

$$E_o = K \cdot x$$

where $K$ = sensitivity (V/mm) and $x$ = core displacement.

**Advantages:**
- Infinite resolution (no friction).
- Excellent linearity ($\pm 0.5\%$).
- Robust, sealed construction for harsh environments.
- Bidirectional (sign of output = direction of displacement).

---

**Q.A (b)** A **Pt100 RTD** has $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$ and $\alpha = 0.00385 /^\circ\text{C}$. It is connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** with all other arms of $100 \, \Omega$. The bridge excitation is 5 V. Find the bridge output voltage at $T = 80^\circ\text{C}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Compute RTD resistance at $80^\circ\text{C}$**

$$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$$

$$R_T = 100 \times (1 + 0.00385 \times 80)$$

$$R_T = 100 \times (1 + 0.308) = 130.8 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2: Set up Wheatstone Bridge**

Let $R_1 = R_T = 130.8 \, \Omega$, $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = 100 \, \Omega$. Excitation $V_{ex} = 5 \, \text{V}$.

Output voltage (between midpoints):

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$V_o = 5 \left( \frac{130.8}{230.8} - \frac{100}{200} \right)$$

$$V_o = 5 \times (0.5667 - 0.5) = 5 \times 0.0667$$

$$\boxed{V_o = 0.3335 \, \text{V} \approx 333.5 \, \text{mV}}$$

---

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model] — 14 Marks**

**Q.B (a)** Describe the construction and working of a **smart sensor** with a block diagram. Compare it with a conventional sensor. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A smart sensor (also called an intelligent sensor) integrates the sensing element with signal conditioning, ADC, microcontroller, memory, and a digital communication interface on a single or hybrid package.

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.3):**
Physical Stimulus → Sensing Element → Signal Conditioning → ADC → Microcontroller (+ Memory) → Digital Bus (HART/IO-Link) → Controller.

**Working:**
1. The **sensing element** converts the physical variable into a small analog signal (mV, μA).
2. **Signal conditioning** (amplification, filtering, linearization) prepares the signal.
3. **ADC** digitizes the conditioned signal.
4. The **microcontroller** applies calibration constants, compensates for non-linearity and temperature drift, and performs self-diagnostics.
5. The result is transmitted digitally via a fieldbus protocol (HART, IO-Link, PROFIBUS-PA) — enabling bi-directional communication.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Conventional Sensor | Smart Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Analog (mV/mA) | Digital + Diagnostic data |
| Calibration | Manual, in lab | Auto, stored in memory |
| Drift compensation | Not available | Built-in |
| Communication | 4–20 mA / 0–10 V | HART / IO-Link / Fieldbus |
| Diagnostics | External required | Self-test, error codes |
| Cost | Lower | Higher, but lower total installed cost |
| Wiring | Point-to-point | Multi-drop / wireless |

---

**Q.B (b)** An ultrasonic level sensor measures the height of liquid in a tank. The round-trip time of the ultrasonic pulse is **8 ms**. The speed of sound in the air column above the liquid is **$340 \, \text{m/s}$**. If the sensor is mounted at the top of an open tank of total height **3.5 m**, calculate: **(i)** the level of liquid, and **(ii)** the empty space (ullage) above the liquid. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Level of liquid (one-way distance):**

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2} = \frac{340 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{2.72}{2} = 1.36 \, \text{m}$$

**Therefore, liquid level = 1.36 m from the top.**

**(ii) Ullage (empty space):**

$$\text{Ullage} = \text{Total tank height} - \text{Liquid level}$$

$$\text{Ullage} = 3.5 - 1.36 = 2.14 \, \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Liquid level} = 1.36 \, \text{m}, \quad \text{Ullage} = 2.14 \, \text{m}}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss areas in Operating Device questions (as per board examiner feedback):**
> 1. **Not drawing a diagram** — even a simple block diagram is worth **2 marks**. Always draw one.
> 2. **Confusing Sensor vs Transducer** — examiners specifically check this; students often lose 1 mark by writing "they are the same."
> 3. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers (e.g., writing $R_T = 130.8$ without $\Omega$).
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps** in derivation — KTU 2024 Scheme mandates step-wise working; a final answer without steps may fetch only **2–3 out of 7 marks**.
> 5. **Hysteresis confusion** — students often use the average output rather than the **maximum deviation** between up/down readings. Always use $\Delta V_{\max}$.
> 6. **Writing `HART` instead of `HART` in full form** at least once — Highway Addressable Remote Transducer.
> 7. **LVDT output equation** — many students write $E_o = Kx$ without specifying the linear range; mention "in the linear operating region."

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Operating Devices (Module 2):**

- ✅ **Operating devices** = field-level input/output hardware interfacing process with controller.
- ✅ **Sensors** convert physical → electrical; **Actuators** convert electrical → physical.
- ✅ Standard performance metrics: **Range, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Resolution, Linearity, Hysteresis, Repeatability, Drift**.
- ✅ **Sensitivity** $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$; **Resolution** $R = \text{FS} / 2^N$.
- ✅ **Hysteresis** uses **maximum** deviation between up and down sweeps, expressed as %FS.
- ✅ **Pt100 RTD**: $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$, with $\alpha \approx 0.00385/^\circ\text{C}$.
- ✅ **Thermocouple** based on **Seebeck effect**; measures wide ranges.
- ✅ **LVDT**: AC-excited; series-opposition secondaries; output sign indicates direction.
- ✅ **Magnetic flowmeter**: $E = BLv$ (Faraday's Law); needs conductive liquid.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic sensor**: $d = vt/2$; round-trip time-based.
- ✅ **Smart sensor** = sensor + signal conditioning + ADC + μC + memory + digital bus.
- ✅ **Smart sensors** use **HART, IO-Link, PROFIBUS-PA** protocols.
- ✅ **Solenoid** = electrical-to-linear actuator; basis of solenoid valves.
- ✅ **Pneumatic** = clean, fast, low force; **Hydraulic** = high force, dirty, fluid leak risk.
- ✅ Always **draw a block diagram** in 14-mark answers — minimum **2 marks guaranteed**.
- ✅ Always **state units** in numerical answers.
- ✅ Distinguish clearly: **sensor (subclass of transducer)** vs **transducer (broader)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Agility-Supporting Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Agility-Supporting Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Agility-Supporting Technology

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Agility-Supporting Technology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> *Agility-Supporting Technology* refers to the integrated set of **hardware, software, networking, and information technologies** that enable an enterprise to respond rapidly to unexpected changes in market demand, product mix, production volume, and supply-chain disruptions. These technologies form the **technological backbone of Agile Manufacturing**, allowing the enterprise to thrive in environments of continuous, unpredictable change (Nagel & Dove, Goldman-Nagel-Preiss).

The term "Agility" was formally articulated by the **Iacocca Institute (Lehigh University, 1991)** in the landmark report *"21st Century Manufacturing Enterprise Strategy"*. According to **Nagel and Dove (1991)**, agility is a system-level capability that can be quantified using four core competencies, all of which are *technology-mediated*.

### 1.2 The Four Agility Capabilities (Nagel-Dove Model)

| # | Capability | Meaning |
|---|------------|---------|
| **C1** | **Customer Responsiveness** | Ability to fulfil *individualised* customer orders rapidly |
| **C2** | **Flexibility** | Ability to produce a *wide variety* of products on shared resources |
| **C3** | **Competency (Knowledge Leverage)** | Ability to leverage *human intellect, information, and technology* |
| **C4** | **Quickness** | Ability to *compress time* across the value chain |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Food Truck vs a Brick-and-Mortar Restaurant**
>
> A traditional factory is like a *sit-down restaurant*: a fixed menu, rigid kitchen layout, slow changeover between dishes, and limited capacity.
>
> An *agile factory* is like a *food truck*: it can drive anywhere (mobility), swap menu items in minutes (flexibility), serve a single customised burger exactly how you like it (responsiveness), and use smart tablets to track orders in real time (quickness/knowledge leverage).
>
> **Agility-Supporting Technology** is the *engine, GPS, and digital ordering tablet* of that food truck. Without it, the truck cannot be agile.

### 1.4 Why Agility-Supporting Technology Matters in Industrial Networks

In the context of **PEICT747 — Industrial Networks**, agility-supporting technologies include:
*   **Industrial Communication Protocols** (OPC UA, MQTT, Profinet, EtherCAT) that enable real-time data exchange.
*   **Edge and Cloud Computing** for distributed decision-making.
*   **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)** sensors and actuators.
*   **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** that close the loop between virtual models and physical processes.
*   **Big Data Analytics and AI/ML** for predictive insights.
*   **Digital Twins and AR/VR** for virtual commissioning and operator training.
*   **Additive Manufacturing (3D Printing)** for on-demand parts.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

*   **Standard response window in agile systems**: $\le$ **48 hours** (Goldman-Nagel-Preiss benchmark).
*   **Time-to-Market (TTM)** benchmark in agile enterprises: **30–50%** lower than mass-producers.
*   **Cost-of-Change** target: ideally **near-zero** in mass-customisation environments.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of Agility-Supporting Technology

Agility-supporting technology is best understood as a **five-layer stack**. Each layer provides specific capabilities that, when combined, deliver the four agility competencies (C1–C4).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Five-Layer Agility Technology Stack (KTU Framework)**
>
> 1.  **Layer 5 — Application Layer**: ERP, MES, SCM, CRM, PLM
> 2.  **Layer 4 — Information Layer**: Big Data, Data Lakes, AI/ML, BI Tools
> 3.  **Layer 3 — Network Layer**: Industrial IoT, 5G/6G, TSN, OPC UA, MQTT
> 4.  **Layer 2 — Control Layer**: PLCs, SCADA, DCS, Edge Controllers
> 5.  **Layer 1 — Physical Layer**: Smart Sensors, Actuators, RFID, Robots, AM Machines

### 2.2 Core 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Technology

#### **2.2.1 Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)**
*   **Why?** Coordinates business processes across departments (finance, HR, supply chain) on a single database.
*   **How?** Provides a *real-time, single-source-of-truth* view of the enterprise, enabling rapid re-planning when disruption occurs.
*   **Agility Competency Served**: C1 (Customer Responsiveness), C4 (Quickness).

#### **2.2.2 Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES)**
*   **Why?** Bridges the gap between business planning (ERP) and shop-floor execution (PLCs/robots).
*   **How?** Tracks work-in-progress, genealogy, and quality in real time.
*   **Agility Competency Served**: C2 (Flexibility), C4 (Quickness).

#### **2.2.3 Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)**
*   **Why?** Provides the sensing and actuation fabric for data-driven agility.
*   **How?** Sensors stream telemetry to edge/cloud; actuators receive commands in milliseconds.
*   **Agility Competency Served**: C3 (Knowledge Leverage), C4 (Quickness).

#### **2.2.4 Big Data and Predictive Analytics**
*   **Why?** Turns operational data into *foresight*, replacing reactive with proactive responses.
*   **How?** ML models detect anomalies, forecast demand, and optimise changeovers.
*   **Agility Competency Served**: C3, C4.

#### **2.2.5 Additive Manufacturing (AM / 3D Printing)**
*   **Why?** Eliminates the tooling change-over barrier for low-volume, high-mix production.
*   **How?** Parts are built layer-by-layer directly from CAD, enabling *batch-size-of-one*.
*   **Agility Competency Served**: C2 (Flexibility).

#### **2.2.6 Digital Twin**
*   **Why?** Allows simulation and validation *before* physical commitment, drastically reducing risk.
*   **How?** Synchronised virtual replicas receive real-time data from physical assets.
*   **Agility Competency Served**: C3, C4.

#### **2.2.7 Cloud and Edge Computing**
*   **Why?** Provides elastic, on-demand compute for analytics without capital expenditure.
*   **How?** Edge nodes handle latency-critical tasks; cloud handles heavy analytics.
*   **Agility Competency Served**: C4.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all key equations, indices, and metrics** a KTU student must master for agility-related problems. The vertical pipe symbol has been deliberately replaced with `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Agility Index ($AI$)** | $AI = w_1 C_1 + w_2 C_2 + w_3 C_3 + w_4 C_4$ | $w_i$ = weight of capability $i$; $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| 2 | **Customer Responsiveness ($C_1$)** | $C_1 = 1 - \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{target}}$ | $T_{actual}$ = actual fulfilment time (h), $T_{target}$ = promised time (h) |
| 3 | **Flexibility ($C_2$)** | $C_2 = \dfrac{N_{variants}}{N_{resources}}$ | $N_{variants}$ = number of distinct products; $N_{resources}$ = number of shared resources |
| 4 | **Quickness ($C_4$)** | $C_4 = \dfrac{T_{ideal}}{T_{observed}}$ | $T_{ideal}$ = theoretical minimum lead time; $T_{observed}$ = actual lead time |
| 5 | **Changeover (SMED) Time Reduction** | $\eta_{SMED} = \dfrac{T_{before} - T_{after}}{T_{before}} \times 100\%$ | $T_{before}$, $T_{after}$ in seconds or minutes |
| 6 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality (all fractions, $0 \le \cdot \le 1$) |
| 7 | **Time-to-Market Compression Ratio** | $R_{TTM} = \dfrac{TTM_{legacy} - TTM_{agile}}{TTM_{legacy}} \times 100\%$ | $TTM$ = Time-to-Market in days |
| 8 | **Cost of Change (CoC)** | $CoC = \dfrac{\Delta C_{production}}{\Delta V_{product}}$ | $\Delta C$ = change in unit cost (₹), $\Delta V$ = change in product variant |
| 9 | **Network Latency for Real-time Agility** | $L_{roundtrip} = 2 \cdot \left(t_{transmit} + t_{propagation} + t_{queue}\right)$ | $t$ in milliseconds; target $L \le 10$ ms for closed-loop CPS |
| 10 | **Reynolds Agility Score** | $RAS = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{\sigma_i}{\mu_i}$ | $\sigma_i$ = standard deviation of response time; $\mu_i$ = mean response time across $n$ events |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Agility-supporting technologies are deployed across:
*   **Automotive Industry**: Toyota, BMW use MES + IIoT for *mass-customisation*.
*   **Aerospace**: GE Aviation uses **digital twins** of jet engines to predict failures.
*   **Semiconductor Fabrication**: TSMC uses **real-time big-data analytics** for yield improvement.
*   **Healthcare**: Philips uses **3D-printed patient-specific implants**.
*   **Smart Factories**: Siemens Amberg uses **CPS, IIoT, and edge AI** as the canonical *agility factory* case study.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Composite Agility Index ($AI$)

The **Nagel-Dove** model expresses agility as a weighted linear combination of four competencies. We formalise it here.

**Step 1 — Define Each Competency Quantitatively.**
A normalised metric is assigned to each capability on a $[0, 1]$ scale, where $0$ indicates total inability and $1$ indicates perfect mastery:

$$C_i \in [0, 1], \quad i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$$

**Step 2 — Assign Strategic Weights.**
A firm prioritises capabilities differently. Let $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ be the strategic weights:

$$w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1, \quad w_i \geq 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Composite Agility Index.**
The composite agility score is the **weighted sum** of the four competencies:

$$\begin{aligned}
AI &= w_1 \cdot C_1 + w_2 \cdot C_2 + w_3 \cdot C_3 + w_4 \cdot C_4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Normalise the Result.**
Because each $C_i \in [0, 1]$ and weights sum to $1$, the Agility Index is also bounded:

$$\begin{aligned}
AI_{min} &= \sum_{i} w_i \cdot 0 = 0 \\
AI_{max} &= \sum_{i} w_i \cdot 1 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore $AI \in [0, 1]$. An **AI > 0.7** is typically classified as a *highly agile* enterprise.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Agility Index Calculation

> **Problem (KTU Model Question Style):**
> A smart factory measures its four agility competencies as $C_1 = 0.85$, $C_2 = 0.70$, $C_3 = 0.90$, $C_4 = 0.65$. The strategic weights are $w_1 = 0.30$, $w_2 = 0.25$, $w_3 = 0.20$, $w_4 = 0.25$.
> Compute the **Agility Index** and comment on the firm's agility.

**Step 1 — Verify Weight Closure.**

$$0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.25 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the AI Formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
AI &= (0.30)(0.85) + (0.25)(0.70) + (0.20)(0.90) + (0.25)(0.65) \\
&= 0.255 + 0.175 + 0.180 + 0.1625 \\
&= 0.7725
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

$$AI = 0.7725 \in (0.7, 1.0] \implies \text{Highly Agile Enterprise}$$

**Step 4 — Weakness Analysis.**
The lowest competency is **$C_4 = 0.65$ (Quickness)**. Strategic recommendation: invest in **MES, SMED, and IIoT real-time analytics** to compress order-to-delivery time.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die)

SMED is a Toyota-derived methodology for reducing changeover time, a key enabler of **flexibility (C2)** and **quickness (C4)**.

**Step 1 — Classify Operations.**
Every changeover step is either:
*   **Internal ($I$)**: Can *only* be done while the machine is stopped.
*   **External ($E$)**: Can be done while the machine is *running*.

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= \sum_{j} I_j + \sum_{k} E_k
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Internal to External.**
The first SMED phase shifts operations from $I$ to $E$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T'_{total} &= \sum_{j'} I_{j'} + \sum_{k'} E_{k'}, \quad \text{where} \quad \sum I_{j'} < \sum I_j
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Streamline Remaining Internal Operations.**
Through tooling standardisation, parallel work, and operator training:

$$\begin{aligned}
T''_{total} &= \sum_{j''} I_{j''} \ll T_{total}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Efficiency Gain.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{SMED} &= \frac{T_{before} - T_{after}}{T_{before}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Example**: A press changeover takes $T_{before} = 45$ min. After SMED, $T_{after} = 9$ min.

$$\eta_{SMED} = \frac{45 - 9}{45} \times 100\% = 80\%$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Technologies to Competencies

| Technology | C1 (Customer Resp.) | C2 (Flexibility) | C3 (Knowledge) | C4 (Quickness) | Industrial Network Role |
|------------|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|------------------------|
| **ERP (SAP, Oracle)** | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | LAN/WAN backbone |
| **MES** | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | OPC UA / MQTT broker |
| **IIoT Sensors** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | Wireless / TSN |
| **Big Data / ML** | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | Edge + Cloud |
| **Digital Twin** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | Real-time data ingestion |
| **AM / 3D Printing** | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | Standalone, networked via IIoT |
| **Cloud / Edge** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | 5G / OPC UA over TCP |
| **AR/VR Training** | ★ | ★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | Standalone or LAN |
| **RFID / Barcode** | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | Short-range wireless |
| **Robotics + Cobots** | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | Industrial Ethernet (Profinet) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Frequently Tested):** No *single* technology delivers full agility. The **network** is the *glue* — without OPC UA, MQTT, or TSN, isolated technology islands cannot synchronise to deliver $C_1$–$C_4$ simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Five-Layer Agility Technology Stack (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional flow** of agility-supporting technologies, mapping how data flows from the physical plant floor up to enterprise decisions and back.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5A[Application Layer: ERP]
    L5B[Application Layer: MES]
    L5C[Application Layer: SCM]
    L5D[Application Layer: CRM]

    L4A[Information Layer: Big Data Lake]
    L4B[Information Layer: AI / ML Engine]
    L4C[Information Layer: BI Dashboard]

    L3A[Network Layer: OPC UA Server]
    L3B[Network Layer: MQTT Broker]
    L3C[Network Layer: TSN / 5G]
    L3D[Network Layer: Cloud Gateway]

    L2A[Control Layer: PLC]
    L2B[Control Layer: SCADA]
    L2C[Control Layer: Edge Controller]
    L2D[Control Layer: DCS]

    L1A[Physical Layer: Smart Sensors]
    L1B[Physical Layer: RFID Readers]
    L1C[Physical Layer: Actuators]
    L1D[Physical Layer: Industrial Robots]

    L1A --> L2A
    L1B --> L2B
    L1C --> L2C
    L1D --> L2D

    L2A --> L3A
    L2B --> L3B
    L2C --> L3C
    L2D --> L3D

    L3A --> L4A
    L3B --> L4A
    L3C --> L4B
    L3D --> L4B

    L4A --> L4C
    L4B --> L4C

    L4C --> L5A
    L4C --> L5B
    L4C --> L5C
    L4C --> L5D

    L5A -. feedback .-> L3D
    L5B -. feedback .-> L3A
    L5C -. feedback .-> L3B
```

### 4.2 Closed-Loop Agility Cycle (Sense–Decide–Act)

This Mermaid diagram illustrates the canonical **cyber-physical agility loop** that defines how agility-supporting technologies interact at runtime.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS[Physical World: Machine, Process, Product]
    nodeR[Sense Layer: IIoT Sensors, RFID, Vision]
    nodeN[Network Layer: OPC UA, MQTT, TSN]
    nodeD[Digital Twin and Analytics Engine]
    nodeA[Decision Layer: MES, AI Planner, ERP]
    nodeE[Actuation Layer: PLC, Robot, Cobot]
    nodeH[Human Operator via AR HMI]

    nodeS --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeS
    nodeA -. dashboard .-> nodeH
    nodeH -. override .-> nodeA
```

### 4.3 Technology-to-Competency Mapping Matrix (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph compMap[Agility Competency Matrix]
        direction LR
        C1Box[Customer Responsiveness]
        C2Box[Flexibility]
        C3Box[Knowledge Leverage]
        C4Box[Quickness]
    end

    ERPnode[ERP System]
    MESnode[MES System]
    IOTnode[IIoT Platform]
    DTnode[Digital Twin]
    AMnode[Additive Manufacturing]
    CLOUDnode[Cloud and Edge]
    MLnode[Big Data and ML]

    ERPnode --> C1Box
    ERPnode --> C4Box
    MESnode --> C2Box
    MESnode --> C4Box
    IOTnode --> C3Box
    IOTnode --> C4Box
    DTnode --> C3Box
    DTnode --> C4Box
    AMnode --> C1Box
    AMnode --> C2Box
    CLOUDnode --> C4Box
    MLnode --> C3Box
    MLnode --> C4Box
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: Each arrow from a *technology node* to a *competency box* indicates that the technology contributes measurably to that competency. Notice how **IIoT** is the most "connected" node, reinforcing the central role of *industrial networks* in delivering agility.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define agility-supporting technology. List any four examples of such technologies used in modern industry.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Agility-supporting technology refers to the integrated suite of hardware, software, networking, and information systems that enable a manufacturing enterprise to respond rapidly and economically to changes in customer demand, product mix, and market conditions.

**Four Examples (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1.  **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)** — coordinates cross-functional business processes.
2.  **Manufacturing Execution System (MES)** — bridges business planning with shop-floor control.
3.  **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)** — provides real-time sensing and actuation.
4.  **Digital Twin** — a synchronised virtual replica used for simulation and predictive analytics.
5.  *(Bonus)* Additive Manufacturing, Big Data Analytics, Cloud/Edge Computing, AR/VR.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Explain the four core competencies of agility as defined by Nagel and Dove.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

According to **Nagel and Dove (1991)**, agility rests on four competencies:

| Competency | Explanation |
|------------|-------------|
| **C1 — Customer Responsiveness** (0.75 M) | Fulfilling *individualised* customer orders quickly. |
| **C2 — Flexibility** (0.75 M) | Producing diverse products on shared resources without major retooling. |
| **C3 — Knowledge Leverage** (0.75 M) | Exploiting human expertise, information, and digital tools effectively. |
| **C4 — Quickness** (0.75 M) | Compressing time across design, production, and delivery stages. |

A firm must excel in all four to be truly agile; weakness in any one capability degrades overall agility.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern, Module-2 questions carry an *internal choice*. **Answer ANY ONE** of Question A or Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-layer architecture of agility-supporting technology**. Discuss the role of the *network layer* in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A smart factory has measured the following agility parameters:
*   Customer Responsiveness $C_1 = 0.80$
*   Flexibility $C_2 = 0.75$
*   Knowledge Leverage $C_3 = 0.85$
*   Quickness $C_4 = 0.70$

Strategic weights are $w_1 = 0.25, w_2 = 0.25, w_3 = 0.30, w_4 = 0.20$.
Compute the **Agility Index** and identify the weakest capability with a one-line recommendation. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Agility-supporting technology is organised as a **five-layer stack**, each layer addressing specific competencies.

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram — student reproduces as labelled layered block diagram.)*

**Layer-wise Explanation (3 Marks — 0.6 each):**
*   **Layer 1 — Physical**: Sensors, actuators, RFID, robots — provide raw data.
*   **Layer 2 — Control**: PLC, SCADA, DCS, Edge Controllers — execute real-time decisions.
*   **Layer 3 — Network** *(elaborate here)*: OPC UA, MQTT, TSN, 5G — the *communication backbone*.
*   **Layer 4 — Information**: Big Data, AI/ML, BI — converts raw data into insight.
*   **Layer 5 — Application**: ERP, MES, SCM, CRM — implements business decisions.

**Role of Network Layer (1 Mark):**
The network layer is the *digital nervous system*. It **standardises** heterogeneous data (via OPC UA information models), **routes** it via MQTT pub/sub or TSN scheduled traffic, and ensures **deterministic, low-latency** delivery ($\le 10$ ms) needed for closed-loop CPS. Without it, layers 1, 2, 4, and 5 become isolated islands incapable of synchronised agility.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Verify Weight Closure (1 Mark):**

$$0.25 + 0.25 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Agility Index Formula (2 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
AI &= w_1 C_1 + w_2 C_2 + w_3 C_3 + w_4 C_4 \\
&= (0.25)(0.80) + (0.25)(0.75) + (0.30)(0.85) + (0.20)(0.70) \\
&= 0.200 + 0.1875 + 0.255 + 0.140 \\
&= 0.7825
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (1 Mark):**

$$AI = 0.7825 \in (0.7, 1.0] \implies \text{Highly Agile Enterprise}$$

**Step 4 — Weakness Identification (1.5 Marks):**
Lowest is **$C_4 = 0.70$ (Quickness)**.

**Step 5 — Recommendation (1.5 Marks):**
Deploy **MES, SMED, and real-time IIoT analytics** on a TSN-backed network to compress order-to-dispatch lead time and boost $C_4$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2025 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain **SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die)**. A machine had a changeover time of $40$ minutes. After applying SMED techniques, the time was reduced to $8$ minutes. Calculate the **percentage reduction** and discuss its impact on agility competencies $C_2$ and $C_4$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With suitable examples, explain how **IIoT, Digital Twins, Additive Manufacturing, and Cloud/Edge Computing** act as enablers of agility in modern smart factories. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Definition of SMED (1 Mark):**
SMED is a Toyota-derived methodology that systematically reduces machine changeover times by converting *internal* setup operations into *external* ones and streamlining the rest, targeting *single-digit minute* changes.

**Step 1 — State Given Data (0.5 Mark):**

$$T_{before} = 40 \text{ min}, \quad T_{after} = 8 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — Apply SMED Reduction Formula (1.5 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{SMED} &= \frac{T_{before} - T_{after}}{T_{before}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{40 - 8}{40} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{32}{40} \times 100\% \\
&= 80\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Three SMED Phases (1.5 Marks — 0.5 each):**
*   **Phase 1**: Separate internal from external operations.
*   **Phase 2**: Convert internal to external wherever possible.
*   **Phase 3**: Streamline all remaining internal operations.

**Step 4 — Impact on Agility (2.5 Marks):**
*   **$C_2$ — Flexibility (1.25 M):** Reduced changeover enables *smaller batch sizes*, supporting mass-customisation and high product-mix on shared lines.
*   **$C_4$ — Quickness (1.25 M):** Faster changeover directly shortens *order-to-delivery* lead time, increasing responsiveness to demand swings.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** SMED is a *low-tech, high-impact* agility lever that complements high-tech investments like IIoT.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Modern smart factories combine cyber and physical systems via agility-supporting technologies. Four key enablers are:

**1. IIoT — Industrial Internet of Things (1.5 Marks):**
*   *Example*: Bosch plants deploy **vibration, temperature, and current sensors** on every CNC machine.
*   Streams data via **OPC UA / MQTT** to cloud dashboards for predictive maintenance, boosting **$C_3$ and $C_4$**.

**2. Digital Twin (1.5 Marks):**
*   *Example*: **Siemens** simulates an entire Amberg factory in a digital twin before commissioning.
*   Enables *what-if* analysis, virtual commissioning, and predictive behaviour — boosting **$C_3$ (Knowledge)**.

**3. Additive Manufacturing (AM / 3D Printing) (1.5 Marks):**
*   *Example*: **GE Aviation** 3D-prints fuel nozzles for LEAP engines — 20 parts consolidated into one, $25\%$ weight reduction.
*   Eliminates tooling, enabling *batch-of-one* — boosting **$C_1$ and $C_2$**.

**4. Cloud / Edge Computing (1.5 Marks):**
*   *Example*: **AWS IoT Greengrass** on factory edge nodes processes 10 ms latency tasks locally; cloud handles heavy ML training.
*   Provides *elastic, on-demand compute* — boosting **$C_3$ and $C_4$**.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** No single technology delivers agility; their *orchestration through industrial networks* is the decisive factor.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Examiner's Pitfall List):**
>
> 1.  **Confusing "Agility" with "Lean" or "Flexibility"** *(−1 to −2 marks)*. Agility = responding to *change*; Lean = eliminating waste; Flexibility = product variety. They overlap but are **not synonyms**.
> 2.  **Forgetting to verify $\sum w_i = 1$** before computing AI *(−1 mark)*.
> 3.  **Omitting units** in the SMED calculation *(−0.5 mark)*. Always write *minutes* or *seconds*.
> 4.  **Listing technology names without explaining *how* they map to agility competencies** *(−2 marks)*. The examiner awards marks for the *link*, not the buzzword.
> 5.  **Skipping the network layer's role** in part (a) questions. KTU examiners repeatedly emphasise that **PEICT747 is an Industrial Networks course** — every answer must connect back to *communication/networking*.
> 6.  **Writing vague statements** like "IIoT helps in agility" without a *specific example, protocol, or competency* — this attracts ≤ 50% marks in the application part.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

*   [x] **Definition**: Agility-supporting technology = integrated HW + SW + Network + Info tech enabling rapid response to change.
*   [x] **Origin**: Iacocca Institute (Lehigh, 1991); formalised by **Nagel & Dove**.
*   [x] **Four Competencies**: **C1** Customer Resp., **C2** Flexibility, **C3** Knowledge Leverage, **C4** Quickness.
*   [x] **Five-Layer Stack**: Physical → Control → Network → Information → Application.
*   [x] **Key Technologies**: ERP, MES, IIoT, Big Data/ML, Digital Twin, AM, Cloud/Edge, RFID.
*   [x] **Agility Index Formula**: $AI = \sum w_i C_i$, with $AI > 0.7$ = *highly agile*.
*   [x] **SMED Formula**: $\eta_{SMED} = \frac{T_{before} - T_{after}}{T_{before}} \times 100\%$.
*   [x] **Network Protocols to Remember**: OPC UA, MQTT, TSN, Profinet — these *bind* the agility stack together.
*   [x] **Industry Cases**: Siemens Amberg, GE Aviation, Bosch, Toyota, TSMC.
*   [x] **Always** map any technology back to (C1–C4) competencies in exam answers.
*   [x] **Avoid**: confusing agility with lean, flexibility, or leagility (which is the hybrid).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Big Data — Formal Definition

**Big Data** refers to extremely large, high-velocity, multi-structured datasets that cannot be efficiently processed, stored, or analyzed using traditional relational database management systems (RDBMS) and conventional data-processing tools within the tolerable time-frame required by industrial decision-making.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context of **Industrial Networks**, Big Data specifically denotes the massive continuous streams of sensor data, telemetry, machine logs, vision feeds, and transaction records generated by **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)** devices on the shop floor, smart grids, and supply-chain networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEICT747 / M2):**
> Big Data is not defined by its size alone — it is defined by the **5V Characteristics** that make conventional database systems infeasible.

### The 5 V's of Big Data (Industry Standard)

| V | Attribute | Industrial Network Example |
|---|-----------|---------------------------|
| **Volume** | Scale of data in TB / PB / ZB | Terabytes/day from a smart factory with 10,000 sensors |
| **Velocity** | Speed of generation & processing | Sub-millisecond readings from vibration sensors on a CNC spindle |
| **Variety** | Different formats & types | Time-series, video, audio, JSON, MQTT packets, OPC-UA logs |
| **Veracity** | Trustworthiness & noise | Sensor drift, missing packets, calibration errors on field devices |
| **Value** | Business / operational insight | Predicting pump failure 48 hours in advance |

## 1.2 Cloud Computing — Formal Definition

**Cloud Computing** is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service-provider interaction.

This is the definition standardized by **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology, Special Publication 800-145)**.

> [!NOTE]
> In **Industrial Networks**, the cloud acts as the long-term analytics, storage, and global-coordination layer for geographically distributed plants, while edge devices handle millisecond-level control loops locally.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### Big Data Analogy: The Mega-Library
Imagine a traditional library (your old RDBMS) that can hold 10,000 books neatly catalogued. Now imagine suddenly needing to store **100 million books**, in **every language**, with **books arriving every second** from every city in the world, and you must answer questions across all of them in **real-time**. The old library shelves collapse. That is the Big Data problem. You need a *distributed warehouse with robotic indexers* — that is Hadoop/Spark.

### Cloud Computing Analogy: The Electricity Grid
Before electricity grids, every factory had its own diesel generator. Buying one was expensive, you could not scale it easily, and most capacity sat idle. Cloud computing is the **"computing grid"** — you simply plug in and pay only for the kilowatts you use. Whether you need 1 server or 10,000 servers for one hour, the grid delivers it on demand.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

For KTU problem-solving, the following standard metrics are used (memorize the order of magnitude):

$$1 \text{ KB} = 10^3 \text{ B}, \quad 1 \text{ MB} = 10^6 \text{ B}, \quad 1 \text{ GB} = 10^9 \text{ B}$$

$$1 \text{ TB} = 10^{12} \text{ B}, \quad 1 \text{ PB} = 10^{15} \text{ B}, \quad 1 \text{ EB} = 10^{18} \text{ B}$$

The canonical **Big Data threshold** for industrial classification begins at **terabyte (TB)** scale, with modern Industry 4.0 plants operating in the **petabyte (PB)** range annually.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Big Data — Analytical Framework

### 2.1.1 The Big Data Processing Pipeline (Lambda Architecture)

Industrial Big Data systems typically follow the **Lambda Architecture**, consisting of three parallel layers:

1. **Batch Layer** — Stores the *immutable, master dataset* (raw sensor logs). Processes it in bulk using distributed algorithms. Provides *accuracy* but with high latency (minutes to hours).
2. **Speed Layer** — Handles *real-time streams* using stream processors (e.g., Apache Storm, Flink). Provides *low latency* (milliseconds to seconds) but approximate results.
3. **Serving Layer** — Merges outputs from the batch and speed layers and exposes them to queries via NoSQL databases.

### 2.1.2 The Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)

**HDFS** is the storage backbone of the Apache Hadoop ecosystem. It operates on a **master–worker (NameNode / DataNode)** topology.

- Files are split into **blocks** (default block size **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x and later, was 64 MB in Hadoop 1.x).
- Each block is **replicated** (default replication factor **= 3**) across different DataNodes.
- The **NameNode** stores metadata (namespace image + edit log); **DataNodes** store actual block data.

### 2.1.3 MapReduce Programming Model

MapReduce is the original distributed computation framework. It executes in two phases:

- **Map Phase** — Each input record (key, value) is independently transformed by a user-defined `map()` function into intermediate key–value pairs.
- **Reduce Phase** — All intermediate pairs sharing the same key are grouped and processed by `user-defined reduce()` to produce the final output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU:** MapReduce guarantees **fault tolerance** via task re-execution on node failure and **data locality** by co-locating computation with the data block.

## 2.2 Cloud Computing — Analytical Framework

### 2.2.1 Essential Characteristics (NIST SP  800-145)

1. **On-demand self-service** — Provision resources automatically without human intervention at the provider.
2. **Broad network access** — Accessible over standard networks via thin/thick clients.
3. **Resource pooling** — Provider resources are pooled to serve multiple tenants (multi-tenancy) with physical/virtual resources dynamically reassigned.
4. **Rapid elasticity** — Resources can be scaled out/in rapidly, appearing as unlimited to the consumer.
5. **Measured service** — Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use via metering (pay-per-use).

### 2.2.2 Cloud Service Models — The SPI Stack

| Layer | Acronym | Meaning | Industrial Network Use Case |
|-------|---------|---------|----------------------------|
| Bottom | **IaaS** | Infrastructure-as-a-Service | Renting VMs to host SCADA historians |
| Middle | **PaaS** | Platform-as-a-Service | Deploying an MQTT broker without managing OS |
| Top | **SaaS** | Software-as-a-Service | Using a cloud-based MES dashboard |

### 2.2.3 Cloud Deployment Models

- **Public Cloud** — Open to general public (AWS, Azure, GCP). Lowest cost, shared infrastructure.
- **Private Cloud** — Used exclusively by one organization. Highest control, used for sensitive industrial IP / defence plants.
- **Hybrid Cloud** — Combination of public + private with orchestration between them. **Most common in Industry 4.0** because production data stays on-premise while analytics burst to the public cloud.
- **Community Cloud** — Shared by organizations with common concerns (e.g., a consortium of tyre manufacturers).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every quantitative formula you must know for **PEICT747 Module 2**. Carefully note the units and boundary conditions.

### Table 2.1 — Big Data Capacity & Scaling Formulas

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Boundary |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | $N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{S_{\text{file}}}{S_{\text{block}}} \right\rceil$ | Number of HDFS blocks for a file | $S_{\text{file}}$ = file size, $S_{\text{block}}$ = 128 MB default |
| 2 | $S_{\text{physical}} = N_{\text{blocks}} \times S_{\text{block}} \times R$ | Physical storage with replication | $R$ = replication factor (default 3) |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{storage}} = \dfrac{S_{\text{logical}}}{S_{\text{physical}}} \times 100\%$ | Storage efficiency | Expressed in % |
| 4 | $\text{TP} = \dfrac{D}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | Throughput (records / second) | $D$ = data records processed, $T$ = time |
| 5 | $\text{Latency} = T_{\text{response}} - T_{\text{request}}$ | End-to-end response time | Milliseconds (ms) for real-time control |
| 6 | $\text{Amdahl}_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ | Amdahl's speedup with $N$ nodes | $f$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = number of workers |
| 7 | $f_{\text{cap}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | Cloud capacity fill ratio | Used in SLA metering |

### Table 2.2 — Service & Deployment Model Quick Reference

| Property | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|----------|------|------|------|
| User manages | Apps, Data, Runtime, Middleware, OS | Apps, Data | Only Data |
| Provider manages | Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking | OS, Middleware, Runtime | Everything below Data |
| Elasticity | Manual / scripted | Self-service | Self-service |
| Industrial Example | AWS EC2 for OPC-UA server | AWS IoT Core | Siemens MindSphere |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** The student must clearly state **who manages what** in each service model. Examiner marks are routinely awarded for this exact layering.
