# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, 3 marks]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR) with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Virtual Reality (VR) | Augmented Reality (AR) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Fully immersive computer-generated 3D environment that **replaces** real-world perception | Overlay of digital information **on top of** the real-world view in real time |
| User awareness of real world | **Disconnected** from physical reality | **Aware** of physical reality |
| Display device | Opaque HMD (e.g., Meta Quest 3, HTC Vive Pro) | See-through glasses / smartphone camera (e.g., HoloLens 2, Pokémon GO) |
| Example – Industrial | VR training simulator for refinery safety drill | AR overlay showing motor vibration data on a smart helmet |
| Example – Consumer | Beat Saber VR game | IKEA Place app to visualise furniture at home |

*(Award 1 mark for clear definition of VR, 1 mark for clear definition of AR, 1 mark for any one valid differentiating point or example.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, 3 marks]**

**Q2. Define the term *Pixels-Per-Degree (PPD)*. What is the minimum PPD value typically considered acceptable for "retinal resolution" in modern VR headsets?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\text{PPD} = \frac{N_{h}}{\text{FOV}_{h}^{\circ}}$$

PPD is the **angular display resolution** — the number of horizontal pixels subtended by one degree of the user's field of view. The minimum acceptable value for retinal resolution (where individual pixels become indistinguishable to the human eye) is approximately **22 PPD**. The human fovea can resolve up to ~60 PPD, so premium devices like Apple Vision Pro target **30+ PPD**.

*(Award 1 mark for correct formula, 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the value 22 PPD.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, 14 marks, with internal choice]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **Reality–Virtuality Continuum** proposed by Milgram and Kishino. List the four stages with one industrial example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a VR system**. Explain the role of **Asynchronous Timewarp (ATW)** in latency reduction. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### (a) Reality–Virtuality Continuum (7 marks)

The **Reality–Virtuality Continuum**, proposed by Milgram and Kishino (1994), is a conceptual axis that classifies displays based on the extent to which they present real-world versus computer-generated content. It is *not* a binary VR-vs-AR split — it is a **spectrum**.

The four stages are:

1. **Real Environment (RE)** — No virtual content at all. *Industrial example:* a technician inspecting a motor with the naked eye.
2. **Augmented Reality (AR)** — Virtual objects are **superimposed** on the real view. *Industrial example:* AR smart glasses displaying live SCADA values over a control panel.
3. **Augmented Virtuality (AV)** — Real-world objects are **captured and inserted** into a primarily virtual scene. *Industrial example:* a 360° live camera feed of a remote plant inside a VR CAVE for telepresence.
4. **Virtual Environment (VE / VR)** — Entirely synthetic world. *Industrial example:* VR safety training for high-voltage switchyard operations.

*(1 mark for diagram, 2 marks for each of the four stages with example — distribute accordingly.)*

#### (b) VR System Architecture and ATW (7 marks)

A VR system is a real-time closed loop. The block diagram is:

**Sensors (IMU + cameras + controllers) → Sensor Fusion (Kalman filter) → Pose Predictor → Scene Graph + Physics Engine → Stereoscopic Renderer (Left + Right) → Asynchronous Timewarp → Lens Distortion Correction → HMD Micro-Displays (Left + Right) → Haptics + Spatial Audio**

**Role of Asynchronous Timewarp (ATW):**

ATW is a **last-millisecond pose correction** applied just before the image is sent to the display.

- The renderer produces an image for a *predicted* pose, but by the time the GPU finishes, the head has moved further.
- ATW **re-projects** the previously rendered image to the **newest** IMU pose, sub-millisecond before scan-out.
- This **masks** $t_{\text{render}}$ motion-to-photon error, effectively reducing the perceived latency from 11 ms (90 Hz frame) to ~1–2 ms.
- Without ATW, the displayed image is **stale by one frame**, producing visible judder; with ATW, only sub-pixel reprojection error remains.

**Numerical justification:** Total budget $\tau_{mtp} = t_{\text{sens}} + t_{\text{fuse}} + t_{\text{render}} + t_{\text{warp}} + t_{\text{disp}} = 1 + 1 + 6 + 1 + 2 = 11$ ms $<$ 20 ms comfort threshold. ATW (the $t_{\text{warp}}$ step) is the technique that makes this 11 ms achievable on commodity mobile GPUs (e.g., Quest 3's Snapdragon XR2 Gen 2).

*(3 marks for diagram with proper labels, 2 marks for stage-wise explanation, 2 marks for ATW derivation/justification.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Define the term **Field of View (FOV)**. Derive the expression for horizontal FOV of a VR headset in terms of display width $d$ and focal length $f$ of the lens. A headset has a 2.1-inch display ($d = 0.0533$ m) and lens focal length $f = 0.035$ m. Calculate the horizontal FOV. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **SLAM**? Explain its role in an **Augmented Reality** system. Compare **marker-based** and **markerless** AR tracking. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### (a) FOV Derivation (7 marks)

**Definition:** The Field of View (FOV) is the **angular extent** of the visible scene, measured in degrees or radians, in either the horizontal or vertical direction.

**Derivation:** Consider a thin lens of focal length $f$ placed at a distance $f$ from a display of horizontal width $d$. The lens forms a virtual image at infinity, and the half-angle subtended at the eye is given by the tangent relation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\!\left(\frac{\text{FOV}_{h}}{2}\right) &= \frac{d/2}{f} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \text{FOV}_{h} &= 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{d}{2f}\right).
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substitution with $d = 0.0533$ m and $f = 0.035$ m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{2f} &= \frac{0.0533}{2 \times 0.035} = \frac{0.0533}{0.070} = 0.7614 \\[4pt]
\text{FOV}_{h} &= 2 \arctan(0.7614) \\[4pt]
&= 2 \times 37.27^{\circ} \\[4pt]
&= 74.54^{\circ}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\text{FOV}_{h} \approx 74.5^{\circ}$.

*(2 marks for definition, 3 marks for derivation steps, 2 marks for numerical substitution and final answer.)*

#### (b) SLAM and AR Tracking (7 marks)

**Definition of SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping):** SLAM is a computational process by which a device **simultaneously** (i) builds a map of an unknown environment and (ii) tracks its own position and orientation within that environment, using inputs from cameras, depth sensors, IMU, and/or LiDAR.

**Role in AR:** SLAM is the *backbone* of modern AR — it provides the **6-DoF pose** of the device relative to the real world, without which the virtual object would "drift" or fail to stay anchored to its real-world location.

**Comparison of AR tracking methods:**

| Criterion | Marker-Based (Fiducial) | Markerless (SLAM / Plane) |
|---|---|---|
| Reference | Pre-printed 2D pattern (QR, ArUco) | Natural features, planes, point cloud |
| Setup | Marker must be in view | No prior preparation |
| Accuracy | Very high (± 2 mm @ 0.5 m) | Moderate (± 5 mm typical) |
| Robustness to lighting | Lower (needs good contrast) | Higher (uses texture + depth) |
| Industrial use | Tool ID, part picking, work-instruction overlay | Free-form assembly, large plant navigation |
| Example library | ARToolKit, ArUco, AprilTag | ARKit, ARCore, HoloLens SLAM |

*(2 marks for SLAM definition, 2 marks for AR role, 3 marks for comparison table.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not confuse AR with Mixed Reality (MR).** AR *overlays* digital content; MR makes it *interactively anchored* with real-world occlusion. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you swap them.
> 2. **PPD formula** — write it as $\text{PPD} = N_{h} / \text{FOV}^{\circ}$. Many students forget the unit conversion and end up computing pixels per *radian* — full mark lost.
> 3. **FOV derivation** — the final answer must be in **degrees** if the question says so. Don't leave $\arctan(\ldots)$ unevaluated.
> 4. **Latency budget** — never claim "low latency" without quoting a number. Always cite the **20 ms** motion-to-photon threshold.
> 5. **ATW vs Spacewarp** — ATW corrects for **rotation only**; *Asynchronous Spacewarp (ASW)* interpolates for **translation**. Examiners love this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **VR** = full immersion, opaque HMD, synthetic world; **AR** = overlay on real world; **MR** = interactive anchoring; **AV** = real capture inside virtual scene.
- **Milgram–Kishino Continuum:** Real → AR → AV → Virtual.
- **Core VR pipeline:** Sensors → Fusion → Pose Prediction → Stereoscopic Render → **ATW** → Lens Correction → Display.
- **Core AR pipeline:** Perception (RGB + Depth + IMU) → **SLAM** → Plane/Object Detection → 6-DoF Pose Solve → Composite to see-through display.
- **Latency threshold:** $\tau_{mtp} \le 20$ ms (commercially 11–13 ms).
- **Refresh rate:** 90 Hz minimum for VR (11.1 ms/frame); 120 Hz for premium.
- **FOV formula:** $\text{FOV} = 2 \arctan(d / 2f)$; typical HMD $\approx 100^{\circ}–120^{\circ}$ diagonal.
- **PPD formula:** $\text{PPD} = N_{h} / \text{FOV}^{\circ}$; retinal threshold $\ge 22$.
- **Stereoscopic depth cue:** binocular disparity $\delta = I b / Z$.
- **Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (VAC):** the eye focuses at $Z_{a}$ but converges at $Z_{v}$; $|\Delta_{\text{VA}}|$ must be small (Varjo XR-4 uses liquid-crystal lenses to drive $\Delta_{\text{VA}} \to 0$).
- **AR tracking:** marker-based (high accuracy, needs marker) vs markerless/SLAM (flexible, moderate accuracy).
- **Bandwidth for cloud VR (raw):** $\sim 48$ Gbps for 4K-per-eye @ 90 Hz; ~480 Mbps with H.265 — drives the need for **Wi-Fi 6E/7, 5G, and TSN** in industrial deployments.
- **Industrial relevance:** digital twins, remote maintenance, AR-assisted assembly, VR safety drills, real-time SCADA overlay.
- **Exam-winning keywords:** ATW, SLAM, PPD, VAC, motion-to-photon, stereoscopic disparity, digital twin, TSN, edge GPU, OPC UA/MQTT.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Network Applications** refer to the systematic deployment of communication architectures, protocols, and physical media within manufacturing, process control, energy management, and infrastructure systems to enable real-time data exchange between field devices, controllers, supervisory systems, and enterprise-level management platforms.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEICT747)**, the *Uses and Applications* of industrial networks encompass the **operational, functional, and strategic roles** that networked systems play across the automation hierarchy — spanning from the **sensor-actuator (field) level** up to the **enterprise (management) level**, as defined by the **ISA-95 / IEC 62264** automation pyramid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference Models**
> - **ISA-95 (ANSI/ISA-95):** International standard for integrating enterprise and control systems.
> - **IEC 62264:** International adoption of ISA-95 (Enterprise-Control System Integration).
> - **IEC 62443:** Industrial communication network security standard.
> - **ISO/IEC 8802-3 (IEEE 802.3):** Reference base for Industrial Ethernet.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Plain-English Analogy — "The Industrial Internet = A Hospital's Nervous System"**
>
> Imagine a modern hospital. Sensors (heart rate monitors, oxygen sensors) are like **field devices** in a plant. PLCs and DCS controllers act like the **spinal cord**, processing and forwarding signals. The SCADA/HMI is the **brain's visual cortex** — visualizing the data. The MES/ERP is the **hospital administration** — scheduling, billing, and planning. The industrial network is the **entire nervous system** that carries every signal reliably, in real time, between these layers.
>
> Without it, the "body" (factory) is paralyzed — no feedback, no control, no coordination.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Industrial Network Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Performance Metrics (Must Remember for KTU)**
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Typically **>50,000 hours** for industrial-grade switches.
> - **Network Latency:** Must be **<1 ms** for hard real-time motion control.
> - **Availability Target:** **99.999% (Five Nines)** ≈ 5.26 minutes downtime/year for critical processes.
> - **Determinism:** Guaranteed message delivery within bounded time.
> - **EMC Immunity:** Compliance to **IEC 61000-4** series for industrial environments.

### 1.4 Hierarchical Context of Applications

| Level | Name | Typical Network / Protocol | Application Role |
|-------|------|---------------------------|------------------|
| **Level 0** | Field (Sensor/Actuator) | 4–20 mA, IO-Link, HART | Direct physical process interfacing |
| **Level 1** | Control (PLC/DCS) | PROFIBUS, Modbus, EtherCAT | Real-time closed-loop control |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory (SCADA/HMI) | Industrial Ethernet, OPC UA | Monitoring, alarms, operator interface |
| **Level 3** | Operations (MES) | Ethernet/IP, TCP/IP | Production tracking, scheduling |
| **Level 4** | Enterprise (ERP) | Standard Ethernet, Web services | Business planning, supply chain |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Why Industrial Networks Are Used

Industrial networks are deployed to solve **four fundamental engineering problems**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Pillars of Industrial Network Utility**
> 1. **Spatial Distribution** — Connecting geographically dispersed field devices (e.g., oil pipeline spanning hundreds of km).
> 2. **Temporal Determinism** — Guaranteeing actions occur within strict time windows (e.g., robotic welding on a moving assembly line).
> 3. **Information Integration** — Breaking the "automation island" silos and enabling **vertical (sensor→cloud)** and **horizontal (line-to-line)** data flow.
> 4. **Operational Continuity** — Reducing Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) through remote diagnostics and **predictive maintenance**.

### 2.2 Core Application Domains (Structured Analysis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Domain-Wise Breakdown of Industrial Network Applications**

#### **A. Process Industries (Continuous)**
- **Definition:** Industries producing fluids, gases, or bulk chemicals in continuous flow.
- **Application Examples:** Petroleum refining, petrochemicals, pharmaceutical manufacturing, water treatment.
- **Required Network Characteristics:** High reliability, redundancy (ring/fault-tolerant), long-distance communication.
- **Typical Protocols:** FOUNDATION Fieldbus, PROFIBUS PA, HART, WirelessHART.
- **Real-World Metric:** Refineries use networks to coordinate **>10,000 I/O points** across process units.

#### **B. Manufacturing Industries (Discrete)**
- **Definition:** Industries producing discrete items through assembly or machining.
- **Application Examples:** Automotive assembly, electronics manufacturing, packaging lines, CNC machining.
- **Required Network Characteristics:** High speed, low jitter, sub-millisecond cycle times.
- **Typical Protocols:** PROFINET, EtherCAT, EtherNet/IP, Sercos III.
- **Real-World Metric:** Automotive body-in-white welding lines run at **<1 ms cycle times** with **>100 synchronized robots**.

#### **C. Power Systems & Energy**
- **Definition:** Generation, transmission, and distribution of electrical energy.
- **Application Examples:** Smart grids, substation automation, renewable energy farms (solar/wind), nuclear plant monitoring.
- **Required Network Characteristics:** Extreme reliability, cybersecurity (IEC 62351), long-distance support.
- **Typical Protocols:** IEC 61850 (substations), DNP3, Modbus TCP, IEC 60870-5-104.

#### **D. Building Automation**
- **Definition:** Centralized control of HVAC, lighting, security, and energy in buildings.
- **Typical Protocols:** BACnet, KNX, LonWorks, Modbus.

#### **E. Transportation & Logistics**
- **Definition:** Railway signaling, traffic control, port automation, fleet management.
- **Typical Protocols:** Train Communication Network (TCN), NTCIP (traffic), AIS-based maritime.

#### **F. Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) & Industry 4.0**
- **Definition:** Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) connecting machines, products, and people.
- **Key Technologies:** OPC UA, MQTT, TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking), 5G URLLC, Digital Twins.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Key Equations & Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Formulas Validated for Industrial Network Application Design**

$$
\text{Availability} \; (A) \;=\; \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} \;+\; \text{MTTR}}
$$

> Where **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures, **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair. For **Five Nines (99.999%)**, with MTTR = 8 hours, MTBF must exceed **91,250 hours** ≈ **10.4 years**.

$$
\text{Downtime per year} \;=\; (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \; \text{minutes}
$$

$$
\text{Network Latency Budget} \;=\; t_{\text{prop}} \;+\; t_{\text{proc}} \;+\; t_{\text{queue}} \;+\; t_{\text{trans}}
$$

> Where: $t_{\text{prop}}$ = propagation delay, $t_{\text{proc}}$ = device processing, $t_{\text{queue}}$ = queuing delay, $t_{\text{trans}}$ = transmission delay (frame size / bit rate).

$$
\text{Maximum Nodes on a Bus} \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{V_{\text{drop}_{\max}}}{\sum V_{\text{drop}_{\text{node}}}} \right\rfloor
$$

> For **PROFIBUS DP** with RS-485: $V_{\text{drop}_{\max}} \approx 5\,\text{V}$ (typical bus termination criterion).

$$
\text{Cycle Time} \;(T_c) \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{devices}} \times L_{\text{frame}}}{R_{\text{bit}}} \times k_{\text{overhead}}
$$

> Where $N_{\text{devices}}$ = number of nodes, $L_{\text{frame}}$ = frame length in bits, $R_{\text{bit}}$ = bit rate (bps), $k_{\text{overhead}}$ = protocol overhead factor (typically 1.2–1.5).

$$
\text{Reliability Function} \;R(t) \;=\; e^{-\lambda t} \quad ; \quad \lambda \;=\; \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}}
$$

$$
\text{Bandwidth-Distance Product (Fiber)} \;=\; BW \times L \;\; \text{(MHz·km)}
$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **🔧 Where These Equations Are Applied**
> - **Refinery Network Design:** Use availability formula to justify redundant ring topology (PRP/HSR).
> - **Automotive Line Design:** Use cycle time equation to determine whether PROFINET IRT (250 µs) is needed over standard PROFINET (1 ms).
> - **Substation Automation:** Use reliability function to design dual IEC 61850 networks with GOOSE messaging.
> - **Wireless IIoT Deployment:** Use latency budget to evaluate 5G URLLC vs. Wi-Fi 6 for AGV control.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Network Selection by Application

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application-Driven Network Selection Matrix (Exam-Favorite)**

| **Application Domain** | **Latency Req.** | **Reliability Req.** | **Nodes (Typical)** | **Distance** | **Recommended Network** |
|------------------------|------------------|----------------------|---------------------|--------------|------------------------|
| Automotive welding line | < 1 ms | 99.999% | 50–200 | < 100 m | EtherCAT, PROFINET IRT |
| Pharmaceutical batch | 10–100 ms | 99.99% | 500–2000 | < 500 m | PROFIBUS PA / FF |
| Oil & gas pipeline | 100 ms | 99.99% | 1000+ | 100+ km | WirelessHART, LTE |
| Substation automation | 4 ms (GOOSE) | 99.9999% | 200+ | < 1 km | IEC 61850 / PRP |
| Building HVAC | 1 s | 99.9% | 200+ | < 1 km | BACnet, KNX |
| IIoT / Cloud analytics | 1–10 s | 99.9% | 10,000+ | global | MQTT, OPC UA, 5G |

### 3.2 Worked Derivation: Availability Calculation for a Redundant Network

> **Problem (KTU-Style):** A chemical plant control network uses dual-redundant Industrial Ethernet rings. Each switch has an MTBF of **250,000 hours** and MTTR of **4 hours**. Compute the availability of the **single-switch** subsystem and the **parallel-redundant** system.

**Step 1 — Failure Rate of a Single Switch**
$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} \;=\; \frac{1}{250{,}000} \;=\; 4 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{per hour}
$$

**Step 2 — Availability of a Single Switch**
$$
A_{\text{single}} \;=\; \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \;=\; \frac{250{,}000}{250{,}000 + 4} \approx 0.999984
$$
$$
A_{\text{single}} \;\approx\; 99.9984\%
$$

**Step 3 — Unavailability of a Single Switch**
$$
U_{\text{single}} \;=\; 1 - A_{\text{single}} \;=\; 1.6 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 4 — Availability of Parallel-Redundant System**
> For two independent parallel systems, the system fails **only if both** fail. Assuming independence:
$$
A_{\text{parallel}} \;=\; 1 - (U_{\text{single}})^2 \;=\; 1 - (1.6 \times 10^{-5})^2
$$
$$
A_{\text{parallel}} \;=\; 1 - 2.56 \times 10^{-10} \;\approx\; 0.9999999997
$$
$$
A_{\text{parallel}} \;\approx\; 99.99999997\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**
> [!NOTE]
> The dual-redundant network achieves **>8 Nines availability**, far exceeding the 5-Nine industrial target. Annual downtime drops from ~8.4 minutes (single) to ~0.008 seconds (redundant) — making it suitable for **SIL-3** safety instrumented systems per **IEC 61508**.

### 3.3 Worked Derivation: Cycle Time for PROFINET Network

> **Problem:** A PROFINET network has 20 I/O devices. Each device exchanges 32 bytes of cyclic data. The network operates at 100 Mbps. Estimate the cycle time assuming 30% protocol overhead.

**Step 1 — Frame Length in Bits**
$$
L_{\text{frame}} \;=\; 32 \;\text{bytes} \times 8 \;\text{bits/byte} \;=\; 256 \;\text{bits}
$$
> (Assumes one cycle-data frame per device; PROFINET also includes headers/padding.)

**Step 2 — Apply Overhead Factor**
$$
L_{\text{effective}} \;=\; 256 \times 1.3 \;=\; 332.8 \;\text{bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Time per Device**
$$
t_{\text{device}} \;=\; \frac{L_{\text{effective}}}{R_{\text{bit}}} \;=\; \frac{332.8}{100 \times 10^6} \;\approx\; 3.328 \;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Network Cycle Time**
$$
T_c \;=\; N_{\text{devices}} \times t_{\text{device}} \;=\; 20 \times 3.328 \;\mu\text{s} \;\approx\; 66.56 \;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion**
> [!NOTE]
> This result (~67 µs) is well within PROFINET's typical 250 µs–1 ms real-time class. If we had used **>500 devices**, we would need to migrate to **PROFINET IRT** with cut-through switching to stay below 1 ms.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Determinism vs. Throughput Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Analysis (Common in KTU ESE Questions)**

$$
\text{Determinism Index} \;\mathcal{D} \;=\; \frac{T_{\max} - T_{\min}}{T_{\text{avg}}}
$$

> Where $T_{\max}$, $T_{\min}$, $T_{\text{avg}}$ are the maximum, minimum, and average message delays. A perfectly deterministic network has $\mathcal{D} = 0$.

- **EtherCAT:** $\mathcal{D} \approx 0$ (cut-through, synchronized) — best for motion control.
- **Standard TCP/IP Ethernet:** $\mathcal{D} \gg 0$ (collision-based, variable) — best for IT traffic.
- **TSN (IEEE 802.1Qbv):** $\mathcal{D} \to 0$ (time-aware scheduling) — best for converged IT/OT.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper Style)]**
**List any three major application domains of industrial networks and identify the typical protocol used in each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Discrete Manufacturing (Automotive assembly line):** Uses **EtherCAT** or **PROFINET IRT** for sub-millisecond synchronized motion control. *(1 mark)*
> 2. **Process Industry (Oil refinery):** Uses **FOUNDATION Fieldbus** or **HART** for intrinsically safe, two-wire digital communication. *(1 mark)*
> 3. **Substation Automation (Power systems):** Uses **IEC 61850** with **GOOSE** messaging for sub-4 ms protection signaling. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Similar Pattern)]**
**Define the term "determinism" in the context of industrial networks. Why is it critical in motion-control applications?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Determinism (1 mark):** The guaranteed delivery of a message within a bounded and predictable time interval, independent of network load.
> - **Criticality in motion control (2 marks):** In multi-axis robotic systems, command packets to each servo drive must arrive within the **same cycle window** (e.g., 250 µs for PROFINET IRT). Any jitter > a few microseconds causes **mechanical desynchronization**, leading to part defects, collisions, or safety hazards. Determinism ensures **bounded jitter**, which is non-negotiable for closed-loop control of coupled mechanical systems.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **ISA-95 automation pyramid** and map the role of industrial networks at each level. State at least one protocol per level. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the application of **Industrial Ethernet (PROFINET)** in an **automotive body-in-white welding line**, justifying the choice with cycle-time, determinism, and node-count requirements. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The **ISA-95 / IEC 62264** standard defines a five-level model:

| Level | Name | Function | Typical Network / Protocol |
|-------|------|----------|---------------------------|
| **Level 0** | Process / Field | Sensors & actuators | 4–20 mA, IO-Link, HART |
| **Level 1** | Basic Control | PLC, DCS, RTU loops | PROFIBUS, Modbus RTU |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory | SCADA, HMI, Alarms | Industrial Ethernet, OPC UA |
| **Level 3** | Operations | MES, production tracking | Ethernet/IP, TCP/IP |
| **Level 4** | Enterprise | ERP, supply chain | Standard Ethernet, web services |

**Industrial Network Roles at Each Level:**

- **Level 0 → 1:** Short-haul, high-density I/O networks carrying analog/digital signals. The transition from **analog 4–20 mA** to **digital IO-Link** enables bidirectional diagnostics on simple sensors. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Level 1:** Real-time deterministic networks (PROFIBUS, EtherCAT) carrying cyclic process data for **closed-loop control**. Cycle times 1–10 ms. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Level 2:** Higher-bandwidth Industrial Ethernet links SCADA servers to PLCs, with **OPC UA** providing semantic interoperability (information models, alarms, historical data). *(1.5 marks)*
- **Level 3:** Standard TCP/IP networks interconnect MES with the plant floor via **Ethernet/IP** or **PROFINET**, supporting production KPIs, batch records, and quality data. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Level 4:** Enterprise IT networks connect ERP to MES for procurement, scheduling, and order management. Cybersecurity (IEC 62443) becomes essential at this boundary. *(1 mark)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

> **Application: Automotive Body-in-White (BIW) Welding Line**

**Requirements Analysis:**

- **Number of I/O points:** 100+ welding robots + several hundred sensors/clutches → ~2,000 I/O. *(1 mark)*
- **Cycle time target:** < 60 s per vehicle body → individual robot synchronization must occur at **sub-millisecond** rates. *(1 mark)*
- **Determinism:** ±1 µs jitter tolerance for servo motion coordination. *(1 mark)*

**Why PROFINET IRT?**

1. **Real-Time Class 3 (IRT):** Provides **<1 ms** cycle time with **<1 µs jitter** using time-slot scheduling. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Scalability:** Supports **>10,000 nodes** in a single network with managed switches. *(1 mark)*
3. **Convergence:** Allows simultaneous transmission of **real-time I/O**, **TCP/IP diagnostics**, and **web-based HMI** over the same physical cable (bandwidth: 100 Mbps / 1 Gbps). *(1 mark)*
4. **Proven Deployment:** Used by **Volkswagen, BMW, Mercedes-Benz** in their global BIW lines, demonstrating field-proven reliability. *(0.5 mark)*

**Conclusion:** PROFINET IRT uniquely meets the **determinism, node count, bandwidth, and integration** requirements of a modern automotive welding line — making it the de-facto standard for this application.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Expected Pattern)]**
> **(a)** Discuss the role of industrial networks in **smart-grid / substation automation** with reference to the **IEC 61850** standard. Explain the significance of **GOOSE messaging**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare the application of **WirelessHART vs. 5G URLLC** in **large-scale oil & gas pipeline monitoring**, considering latency, range, power, and reliability. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

> **Role of Industrial Networks in Smart-Grid Substation Automation**

**IEC 61850 Overview:**

IEC 61850 is the global standard for **substation communication networks**, defining:

- **Data modeling** (logical nodes for protection, measurement, control)
- **Abstract services** (reporting, GOOSE, sampled values)
- **Mapping to Ethernet** (MMS, GOOSE, SV over Layer-2 multicast)

**Key Applications of Industrial Networks in Substations:**

1. **Protection Signaling:** Differential protection schemes exchange trip commands between **Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs)** within **<4 ms** via the network. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Measurement & Metering:** Sampled Values (SV) stream digitized CT/PT waveforms at **4,800 samples/s** per IED for real-time power-quality monitoring. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Automation & Control:** Inter-substation GOOSE messages enable **breaker control, interlocking, and auto-transfer schemes** across Ethernet. *(1 mark)*
4. **Station Bus vs. Process Bus:** Modern substations separate **station bus** (between IEDs) and **process bus** (between IEDs and primary equipment) — a network architecture decision enabled by IEC 61850. *(1 mark)*

**Significance of GOOSE (Generic Object Oriented Substation Event) Messaging:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> GOOSE provides **sub-4 ms multicast** Layer-2 messaging directly over Ethernet, **bypassing TCP/IP stack delays**. It enables **hard-real-time trip signals** between protection relays — replacing the slower, hardwired copper inter-trip circuits of legacy substations. GOOSE supports **8-bit status + 32-bit quality + timestamp** in a single multicast frame, and its **retransmission logic** ensures delivery even under network stress.

**Exam-Relevant Point:** GOOSE eliminates the need for thousands of copper control cables, reducing **substation commissioning time by up to 50%** and enabling **centralized protection schemes**. *(1 mark)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

> **Comparison: WirelessHART vs. 5G URLLC for Oil & Gas Pipeline Monitoring**

| Parameter | WirelessHART | 5G URLLC |
|-----------|--------------|----------|
| **Standard** | IEC 62591 (2010) | 3GPP Release 16+ |
| **Latency** | 100 ms – 1 s | 1 ms (target) |
| **Range** | ~200 m (mesh) | km-scale (cellular) |
| **Topology** | Self-organizing mesh | Star (gNB-centric) |
| **Power** | Battery, >5 years | Mains / battery (IoT-MTC) |
| **Node Density** | ~100 per gateway | >1 million/km² |
| **Reliability** | 99.9% typical | 99.999% (target) |
| **Spectrum** | 2.4 GHz ISM | Licensed (sub-6 GHz / mmWave) |
| **Cybersecurity** | AES-128, hopping | 3GPP AKA, SUCI/SUPI |

**Analysis:**

1. **WirelessHART (Score: 3 marks)** — **Best for:** Remote wellhead monitoring with sparse nodes over **1–10 km ranges**, where **battery life of 5+ years** is critical and **sparse sampling** (e.g., pressure, temperature every 5 minutes) is acceptable. Used by Shell, BP, and Saudi Aramco in upstream operations.
2. **5G URLLC (Score: 3 marks)** — **Best for:** Dense urban pipeline corridors, refineries with **>1,000 sensors/km²**, AGV control, and **real-time leak detection** requiring **<10 ms response**. Suitable when **licensed spectrum reliability** is mandatory to avoid ISM-band interference from welding equipment.

**Conclusion:**

> The two technologies are **complementary**, not competitive. WirelessHART remains the **workhorse for brownfield, battery-powered field instrumentation**, while 5G URLLC is the **future for greenfield IIoT deployments** requiring real-time analytics and AI-driven leak detection. *(1 mark for the conclusion)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mistakes in KTU Answers (Where Students Lose Marks)**
>
> 1. **Confusing *availability* with *reliability*** — They are not synonyms. *Reliability* is the probability of functioning without failure over a time interval. *Availability* includes repair/downtime. Students often write "high reliability" when the question demands "high availability." (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Writing GOOSE instead of MMS** — When asked about substation communication, students mix up protocols. **MMS** = client-server reports, **GOOSE** = multicast trip events, **SV** = sampled values. Misnaming = lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Oversimplifying Industry 4.0** — Writing "IIoT = internet + industry" is a 0-mark answer. Examiners expect **cyber-physical systems, digital twins, OPC UA, and TSN** as integral concepts.
> 4. **Ignoring units** in numerical questions — Always quote latency in **ms or µs**, MTBF in **hours**, and availability as a **% or "N nines"**. Missing units = 0.5 mark deduction.
> 5. **Generic "advantages of industrial networks" answers** — Examiners want **application-specific** justification: *which* network, *why* this network, *which standard mandates it*. Generic answers cap at 5/14.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist — "Uses and Applications of Industrial Networks"**

- **ISA-95 / IEC 62264 Automation Pyramid:** Five levels (Field → Control → Supervisory → Operations → Enterprise) with **distinct protocols at each level**.
- **Discrete vs. Process Industries:** Discrete → high-speed, low-jitter (EtherCAT, PROFINET IRT). Process → intrinsically safe, long-distance (FF, HART, WirelessHART).
- **PROFINET IRT:** Cycle times **<1 ms**, jitter **<1 µs**, ideal for **robotics, motion control, automotive BIW**.
- **IEC 61850 + GOOSE:** Standard for substation automation; GOOSE delivers **<4 ms multicast** trip signals replacing hardwired interlocks.
- **OPC UA + TSN:** The **future of converged IT/OT networks** — semantic interoperability + deterministic Ethernet.
- **Availability formula:** $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$. For **Five Nines (99.999%)** with 8-hr MTTR → MTBF ≈ 91,250 hours.
- **Redundancy techniques:** **PRP** (parallel networks) and **HSR** (ring) provide **zero-loss** failover for IEC 61850 substations.
- **WirelessHART:** Best for **brownfield, battery-powered** field sensors (5+ year life, 2.4 GHz mesh, IEC 62591).
- **5G URLLC:** Best for **greenfield, dense, real-time** deployments (1 ms latency, 99.999% reliability).
- **IIoT Architecture:** **Device → Edge → Fog → Cloud → Application**, with **Digital Twin** as the enabler of **predictive maintenance and OEE improvement**.
- **Key Design Trade-off:** **Determinism vs. Throughput** — quantified by the **Determinism Index** $\mathcal{D} = (T_{\max} - T_{\min}) / T_{\text{avg}}$.
- **Cybersecurity Standard:** **IEC 62443** governs all industrial network deployments, especially for IIoT and remote access.
- **Industry 4.0 Pillars:** Cyber-Physical Systems, IoT, Cloud, AI/ML, Digital Twin, Horizontal & Vertical Integration.
- **Exam Mantra:** Always answer with **"Which protocol, which standard, which application, and why."** Generic answers lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Basics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Industrial Networks – The Formal View > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** > An **Industrial Network** is a specialized communication infrastructure designed to interconnect sensors, actuators, controllers (PLCs, DCS, SCADA), and supervisory computing systems used in industrial automation, process control, and manufacturing environments. It enables real-time, deterministic, and reliable exchange of operational data using standardized protocols (e.g., Modbus, Profibus, EtherNet/IP, PROFINET) over physical media tailored to harsh plant-floor conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Idea:** Industrial networks differ from conventional IT networks in three mission-critical ways: **(a) Determinism** (bounded latency), **(b) Availability** (24/7 uptime, often >99.999%), and **(c) Ruggedness** (EMC immunity, temperature tolerance, vibration resistance). --- ## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy – "The Industrial Postal System" Imagine a **large automated factory** as a city: | Component of Factory | Equivalent Real-World Concept | Network Element | |---|---|---| | Workers on the shop floor | Mail senders / receivers | **Field Devices** (Sensors & Actuators) | | The foreman standing in the middle | Town Post Office | **Controller** (PLC / DCS) | | Internal company letters | Postal letters | **Data Packets / Messages** | | Trucks, conveyor belts | Roads & pipes | **Transmission Media** (Cables / Fiber / Wireless) | | Sorting rules at the Post Office | Sorting procedures | **Communication Protocol** | | Manager in head office | Head office | **Supervisory System** (SCADA / HMI) | | Rules governing letter delivery | Postal regulations | **OSI / TCP-IP Reference Model** | Just as the Post Office has strict rules about who can send what, to whom, and via which route, an industrial network has **protocols, topology rules, and addressing schemes** that govern how data travels from a temperature sensor in a reactor to a controller in a control room. > [!TIP] > **For Exam Writing:** Whenever an examiner asks "What is the basic purpose of an industrial network?", always anchor your answer around the triad **"Sense → Decide → Actuate"**, with the network being the *nervous system* that connects these three phases. --- ## 1.3 The Three Foundational Building Blocks of Industrial Networks > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Industrial Network Trinity (3 Pillars):** 1. **The Physical Layer (PHY)** – *The "Bodies & Roads"*: Cables, fiber, radio waves, connectors. 2. **The Data Link Layer (DLL)** – *The "Traffic Rules"*: How bits are framed, addressed, and detected for errors. 3. **The Application Layer (AL)** – *The "Language"*: Modbus registers, Profibus profiles, OPC-UA information models. Every industrial protocol (PROFIBUS, Modbus RTU, EtherCAT, Foundation Fieldbus) is essentially a unique combination of these three pillars, optimized for different industrial use cases. --- ## 1.4 Classification of Networks by Geographic Span (The PAN-LAN-MAN-WAN Hierarchy) | Network Class | Geographic Span | Typical Industrial Use-Case | Symbol | |---|---|---|---| | **PAN** (Personal Area Network) | < 10 m | Body-area sensors, Bluetooth-tagged tools | | | **LAN** (Local Area Network) | < 10 km | Cell-level fieldbus, plant-floor Ethernet | $L \le 10 \text{ km}$ | | **MAN** (Metropolitan Area Network) | < 100 km | Inter-plant SCADA links | | | **WAN** (Wide Area Network) | > 100 km | Cloud-based remote monitoring | | The defining variable here is the **maximum end-to-end propagation distance**, which directly determines the *maximum allowable propagation delay* through the relation: $$\tau_{prop}^{max} = \frac{L_{max}}{v_{signal}}$$ where $v_{signal}$ is the propagation velocity of the signal in the chosen medium (≈ $2 \times 10^8$ m/s for copper cable, ≈ $2 \times 10^8$ m/s for fiber). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Layered Architecture – Why "Layers"? The design of any industrial (or IT) network follows the **fundamental engineering principle of modularity**. Instead of one monolithic block of code, communication is split into *N independent layers*, each providing services to the layer above and consuming services from the layer below. This gives: - **Modularity:** Swap a cable without rewriting the application. - **Interoperability:** Any device speaking the same protocol can plug-and-play. - **Testability:** Each layer can be verified in isolation. - **Maintainability:** Bugs are localized. ## 2.2 The OSI Reference Model – The 7-Layer Framework The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** model, defined by ISO/IEC 7498-1, is the canonical reference for all industrial and IT networking. Although industrial protocols often implement a *streamlined* version of it, every KTU examiner expects you to know all 7 layers cold. | Layer # | Layer Name | Primary Function | Industrial Example | Data Unit (PDU) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 7 | **Application** | User-facing services, information modeling | OPC-UA, Modbus function codes, PROFINET IO | APDU | | 6 | **Presentation** | Data encoding, encryption, compression | TLS, ASCII ↔ UTF-8 | PPDU | | 5 | **Session** | Dialog control, synchronization | NetBIOS, RPC sessions | SPDU | | 4 | **Transport** | End-to-end reliability, port addressing | TCP, UDP | Segment / Datagram | | 3 | **Network** | Logical addressing & routing | IP, IPsec | Packet | | 2 | **Data Link** | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection | Ethernet MAC, PROFIBUS FDL, Modbus RTU | Frame | | 1 | **Physical** | Bit transmission over medium | RS-485, 100BASE-TX, Fiber-optic transceiver | Bit | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic (Top-Down):** **A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing. > **Mnemonic (Bottom-Up):** **P**lease **D**o **N**ot **T**hrow **S**ausage **P**izza **A**way. --- ## 2.3 Network Topologies – The Skeleton of Any Industrial Plant A *topology* describes the geometric arrangement of nodes and links. It is the **single most important design decision** in an industrial network because it directly dictates **fault tolerance, cabling cost, latency, and scalability**. ### 2.3.1 Bus Topology All nodes share a single backbone cable (the "bus"). Termination resistors ($R_T$) at both ends prevent signal reflection. $$Z_0 = \frac{V}{I} \quad \text{(characteristic impedance of the cable)}$$ For correct termination: $R_T \approx Z_0$ (typically **120 Ω** for PROFIBUS, **100 Ω** for CAN bus). ### 2.3.2 Star Topology Every node connects to a central hub/switch. A break in one link affects only that node. This is the dominant topology in modern industrial Ethernet (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP). ### 2.3.3 Ring Topology Each node has exactly two neighbors, forming a closed loop. Provides **redundancy** via protocols like MRP (Media Redundancy Protocol) in PROFINET. ### 2.3.4 Mesh Topology Every node connects to multiple others. Provides **maximum fault tolerance**. A fully connected mesh with $n$ nodes has: $$E_{mesh} = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$ edges, which is why mesh is reserved for *critical backbone* segments in modern industrial plants (e.g., wireless mesh sensors using 802.15.4g). --- ## 2.4 Transmission Media – The "Roads" ### 2.4.1 Guided Media (Cables) | Medium | Typical Bandwidth | Max Practical Distance | Industrial Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Twisted Pair (UTP/STP)** | 10 Mbps – 10 Gbps | 100 m | Fast Ethernet, PROFINET | | **Coaxial Cable** | 10 Mbps – 1 Gbps | 500 m | Legacy fieldbus (e.g., older Ethernet) | | **Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF)** | 1 – 100 Gbps | 2 km | Plant backbone | | **Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)** | 10 – 400 Gbps | > 40 km | Inter-plant WAN | ### 2.4.2 Unguided Media (Wireless) | Medium | Frequency Band | Industrial Use | |---|---|---| | Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz | Plant-wide AGV control | | Bluetooth Low Energy | 2.4 GHz | Tool tracking | | WirelessHART / ISA 100.11a | 2.4 GHz | Process automation | | 5G URLLC | Sub-6 GHz / mmWave | Time-critical control | --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet – High-Yield Equations > [!IMPORTANT] > **The KTU Examiner's Cheat Sheet for "Basics":** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Range | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_{total} = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{queue} + T_{proc}$ | Total end-to-end packet delay | seconds (s) | | 2 | $T_{prop} = \frac{d}{v}$ | Propagation delay | s | | 3 | $T_{trans} = \frac{L}{R}$ | Transmission (serialization) delay | s | | 4 | $R_{throughput} = \frac{L}{T_{total}}$ | Effective throughput | bps | | 5 | $\eta_{utilization} = \frac{T_{trans}}{T_{trans} + 2T_{prop}}$ | Link utilization in stop-and-wait ARQ | dimensionless (0 → 1) | | 6 | $E_{mesh} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Edges in a fully connected mesh | count | | 7 | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Shannon channel capacity | bps | | 8 | $\text{BER} = P_e$ | Bit Error Rate | dimensionless | | 9 | $\lambda = \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{v}{\tau}$ | Wavelength vs propagation speed | m | | 10 | $N_{max}^{bus} = \frac{Z_{max} - Z_{node}}{Z_{seg}}$ | Max nodes on a bus segment | count | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note:** Equation (5) is *the* most tested formula in KTU for stop-and-wait ARQ questions. Memorize it — and remember the factor of **2** in the denominator because the ACK must travel back! --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility In a **petrochemical refinery**, the choice between a bus and star topology for the fieldbus layer directly determines the *Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)* and the *cost of unplanned downtime* (often ₹50,000 to ₹5,00,000 per minute). A redundant ring topology with MRP can reduce this risk by 90%, justifying the higher CAPEX. This is why the **engineering utility** of these "basic" concepts is *measured in millions of rupees per year of plant operation*. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Total End-to-End Delay for a Single Packet The **fundamental delay budget** for any packet traversing a network is: $$T_{total} = T_{proc}^{src} + T_{queue}^{src} + T_{trans}^{src} + T_{prop} + T_{proc}^{dst} + T_{queue}^{dst} + T_{trans}^{dst}$$ For a single-hop link between source $S$ and destination $D$, the four dominant terms are: 1. **Transmission (Serialization) Delay** – Time to push all $L$ bits onto the wire at rate $R$: $$T_{trans} = \frac{L}{R}$$ 2. **Propagation Delay** – Time for one bit to physically travel distance $d$ at speed $v$: $$T_{prop} = \frac{d}{v}$$ 3. **Processing Delay** – Time for routers/switches to inspect headers, look up forwarding tables, and check for errors: $$T_{proc} \approx \text{(hardware-dependent constant, often } \approx 10\text{–}100 \, \mu s \text{)}$$ 4. **Queueing Delay** – Time waiting in buffer before transmission (modeled via queuing theory): $$T_{queue} = \frac{\lambda \cdot \overline{L^2}}{2 R (1 - \rho)}, \quad \rho = \frac{\lambda \overline{L}}{R}$$ For **multi-hop** paths with $N$ hops, the **transmission** delays sum over hops while **propagation** delays are additive per link: $$\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{trans}^{(i)} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{prop}^{(i)}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Link Utilization in Stop-and-Wait ARQ **Stop-and-Wait ARQ** is the simplest reliable data-link protocol: send one frame, wait for ACK, send next frame. The question examiners love: *what fraction of channel time is spent transmitting useful data?* **Setup:** - Frame length: $L_f$ bits - ACK length: $L_{ack}$ bits (often neglected in basic problems → assume $L_{ack} = 0$) - Data rate: $R$ bps - One-way propagation delay: $T_{prop}$ - Processing time at receiver: $T_{proc}$ (assume negligible) **Timeline (Round Trip):** | Phase | Duration | |---|---| | Sender transmits frame | $T_{trans} = \frac{L_f}{R}$ | | Frame propagates | $T_{prop}$ | | Receiver processes & sends ACK | $T_{proc} + T_{trans}^{ack}$ | | ACK propagates back | $T_{prop}$ | For the simplified case ($L_{ack} = 0$, $T_{proc} = 0$): $$T_{round} = T_{trans} + 2 T_{prop} = \frac{L_f}{R} + \frac{2d}{v}$$ **Link Utilization (Channel Efficiency):** $$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful transmission time}}{\text{Total cycle time}} = \frac{T_{trans}}{T_{trans} + 2 T_{prop}}$$ Substituting the explicit forms: $$\eta = \frac{L_f / R}{L_f / R + 2d/v} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{2 d R}{v L_f}}$$ Let $a = \frac{T_{prop}}{T_{trans}} = \frac{d R}{v L_f}$ be the **propagation-to-transmission ratio**: $$\boxed{\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** > - For **short, fast links** (low $a$), $\eta \to 1$ → excellent utilization. > - For **long satellite links** ($T_{prop} \approx 270$ ms), $\eta \to 0$ → terrible utilization. This is why **sliding-window protocols** (GBN, SR) are needed for high-delay links. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Protocol-to-OSI-Layer Mapping > [!NOTE] > This matrix is the **single most asked table** in KTU exams on "Basics of Industrial Networks". Memorize it. | Industrial Protocol | Physical Layer | Data Link Layer | Network Layer | Transport Layer | Application Layer | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Modbus RTU** | RS-485 / RS-232 | Modbus RTU (master-slave) | None | None | Modbus PDU | | **Modbus TCP** | Ethernet (100BASE-TX) | Ethernet MAC | IP | TCP | Modbus PDU | | **PROFIBUS DP** | RS-485 / Fiber | PROFIBUS FDL | None | None | DP-V0/V1/V2 | | **PROFINET** | 100BASE-TX / FX | Ethernet MAC | IP (for naming) | UDP / TCP | PROFINET IO | | **EtherCAT** | 100BASE-TX | Ethernet MAC + EtherType | None | None | EtherCAT mailbox | | **Foundation Fieldbus H1** | 31.25 kbps, twisted pair | FF DL | None | None | FF AL | | **DeviceNet** | CAN-based (250 kbps) | CAN MAC | None | None | CIP (Common Industrial Protocol) | | **OPC UA** | Any | Any | IP | TCP | OPC UA information model | | **WirelessHART** | IEEE 802.15.4 @ 2.4 GHz | TDMA + mesh | None | None | HART command set | --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A PROFINET segment spans $d = 200$ m between an IO controller and an IO device. The data rate is $R = 100$ Mbps and the PROFINET frame size is $L_f = 84$ bytes (minimum Ethernet frame including preamble). Compute: 1. Transmission delay 2. Propagation delay (assume $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s) 3. Total one-way delay 4. Link utilization in Stop-and-Wait ARQ **Solution:** **(1) Transmission Delay:** $$T_{trans} = \frac{L_f}{R} = \frac{84 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{100 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}}$$ Converting $84 \text{ bytes} = 84 \times 8 \text{ bits} = 672 \text{ bits}$: $$T_{trans} = \frac{672}{100 \times 10^6} = 6.72 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 6.72 \, \mu s$$ **(2) Propagation Delay:** $$T_{prop} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{200}{2 \times 10^8} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.0 \, \mu s$$ **(3) Total One-Way Delay:** $$T_{total} = T_{trans} + T_{prop} = 6.72 \, \mu s + 1.0 \, \mu s = 7.72 \, \mu s$$ **(4) Stop-and-Wait Utilization:** $$a = \frac{T_{prop}}{T_{trans}} = \frac{1.0}{6.72} \approx 0.1488$$ $$\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.2976} = \frac{1}{1.2976} \approx 0.7706$$ **Answer:** The link utilization is approximately **77.1%** under stop-and-wait ARQ, which is acceptable for short industrial links but would be unacceptable for satellite links. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The 7-Layer OSI Reference Model (Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Layer7["LAYER 7 - APPLICATION"] appNode["OPC UA Server
Modbus Function Codes
PROFINET IO Data Interface"] end subgraph Layer6["LAYER 6 - PRESENTATION"] presNode["TLS Encryption
XML JSON Encoding
UTF 8 Conversion"] end subgraph Layer5["LAYER 5 - SESSION"] sessNode["Dialog Manager
Session Token Handler
RPC Coordination"] end subgraph Layer4["LAYER 4 - TRANSPORT"] transNode["TCP Connection
UDP Datagram Service
Port Multiplexer"] end subgraph Layer3["LAYER 3 - NETWORK"] netNode["IP Addressing
Routing Table Lookup
Subnet Mask Logic"] end subgraph Layer2["LAYER 2 - DATA LINK"] dllNode["MAC Frame Builder
CRC Error Detector
CSMA CD or TDMA"] end subgraph Layer1["LAYER 1 - PHYSICAL"] phyNode["RS 485 Transceiver
100BASE TX Encoder
Fiber Optic Modulation"] end Layer7 --> Layer6 Layer6 --> Layer5 Layer5 --> Layer4 Layer4 --> Layer3 Layer3 --> Layer2 Layer2 --> Layer1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Each layer is a *service provider* to the layer immediately above it. The arrows indicate the direction of service invocation (top-down). On the actual wire, however, data flows from the top layer of the sender, down through all layers, across the physical medium, and back up through the layers of the receiver. --- ## 4.2 Industrial Network Topology Comparison (Block Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph busTop["BUS TOPOLOGY"] busD1["Sensor A"] --- busLine(("Shared Backbone Cable")) busD2["Controller"] --- busLine busD3["Actuator"] --- busLine busTerm1["120 ohm Terminator"] --- busLine end subgraph starTop["STAR TOPOLOGY"] starSw{"Industrial Switch"} starD1["IO Controller"] --- starSw starD2["Sensor 1"] --- starSw starD3["Sensor 2"] --- starSw starD4["HMI"] --- starSw end subgraph ringTop["RING TOPOLOGY - MRP REDUNDANT"] ringD1["Master 1"] --- ringD2["Master 2"] ringD2 --- ringD3["IO Device 1"] ringD3 --- ringD4["IO Device 2"] ringD4 --- ringD1 end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Insight:** A *single physical cable break* in a **bus** topology halts the entire network. The **star** fails open (only the broken branch stops). The **ring** survives because data flows the other way. This is why **MRP (Media Redundancy Protocol)** in PROFINET ring topologies can recover from a cable break in **< 200 ms**. --- ## 4.3 The PDU Encapsulation Flow (How Data Wraps and Unwraps) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph sender["SENDER STACK"] sApp["Application Data
e.g. Pressure Reading 14.7 bar"] sTrans["+ Transport Header
Source Port Dest Port"] sNet["+ Network Header
Src IP Dst IP"] sDll["+ Data Link Header
Src MAC Dst MAC + Trailer CRC"] sBits["01010100111010
Bits on the wire"] end subgraph channel["PHYSICAL CHANNEL"] chLabel["Copper Twisted Pair
or
Fiber Optic"] end subgraph receiver["RECEIVER STACK"] rBits["11010011100101
Bits received"] rDll["Strip DLL Header Trailer
Check CRC"] rNet["Strip Network Header
Check IP Destination"] rTrans["Strip Transport Header
Deliver to Port"] rApp["Application Data
Pressure 14.7 bar"] end sApp --> sTrans sTrans --> sNet sNet --> sDll sDll --> sBits sBits --> chLabel chLabel --> rBits rBits --> rDll rDll --> rNet rNet --> rTrans rTrans --> rApp ``` > [!NOTE] > **PDU Vocabulary to Memorize for KTU:** > - Layer 7: **APDU** (Application PDU) > - Layer 4: **Segment** (TCP) or **Datagram** (UDP) > - Layer 3: **Packet** > - Layer 2: **Frame** > - Layer 1: **Bit** (or **Symbol**) --- ## 4.4 The Industrial Communication Hierarchy (The Purdue Model – Level 0–4) ```mermaid flowchart TB lv4["LEVEL 4
Enterprise Network
ERP SAP MES"] lv3["LEVEL 3
Operations Network
SCADA Historian"] lv2["LEVEL 2
Supervisory Control
HMI Engineering Stations"] lv1["LEVEL 1
Basic Control
PLC DCS Controllers"] lv0["LEVEL 0
Process
Sensors Actuators Motors"] lv4 --- lv3 lv3 --- lv2 lv2 --- lv1 lv1 --- lv0 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** The Purdue Model (ISA-95) is the **standard reference** for industrial network segmentation. KTU examiners often ask: *"At which Purdue level does the PROFINET fieldbus operate?"* — Answer: **Level 0–1**. *"Where does OPC-UA fit?"* — Answer: **Level 2–3**, bridging supervisory and operational networks. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define an Industrial Network. List any two distinguishing features compared to IT networks. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **Industrial Network** is a specialized communication infrastructure designed to interconnect field devices (sensors, actuators), controllers (PLCs, DCS), and supervisory systems (SCADA, HMI) used in industrial automation. **Two distinguishing features:** 1. **Determinism** — Guaranteed maximum latency. A PROFINET IO cycle, for example, must complete within 1 ms (or as configured), unlike a generic IT network where delays are "best-effort". 2. **Ruggedness / Reliability** — Industrial networks must operate in harsh environments (temperature -40 °C to +85 °C, high EMI from VFDs, vibration, dust). They are designed for >99.999% availability (≈ 5 minutes downtime/year). *(A third acceptable answer: Use of specialized protocols like PROFINET, EtherCAT, Modbus RTU/TCP.)* --- ### Question 2: State the function of the Data Link Layer (Layer 2) in the OSI model. Give one industrial example. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** is responsible for: 1. **Framing** — encapsulating the network layer packet into a frame with header and trailer. 2. **MAC Addressing** — using unique 48-bit hardware addresses to identify source and destination on the local segment. 3. **Error Detection** — typically using a **Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC)** appended at the trailer. **Industrial Example:** The Ethernet MAC sublayer in PROFINET IRT, or the **PROFIBUS FDL (Fieldbus Data Link)** layer of PROFIBUS DP. --- ## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Module question in Part B carries **14 Marks**, divided into two sub-parts **(a) 7 Marks** and **(b) 7 Marks**. You may attempt *either* Question A *or* Question B. --- ### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) #### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the 7 layers of the OSI reference model. State at least one industrial protocol entity corresponding to each layer. **Model Answer:** The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model** is a 7-layer abstract framework defined by ISO/IEC 7498-1 that standardizes communication between heterogeneous systems. | Layer | Function | Industrial Example | |---|---|---| | **7 – Application** | Provides user-facing services and information exchange | OPC-UA, Modbus function codes, PROFINET IO | | **6 – Presentation** | Data formatting, encryption, compression | TLS 1.3 (used by OPC-UA), JSON/XML encoding | | **5 – Session** | Manages and controls connections (dialogs) | RPC used in PROFINET device discovery | | **4 – Transport** | End-to-end reliability, port-based multiplexing | TCP (Modbus TCP), UDP (PROFINET RT) | | **3 – Network** | Logical addressing and routing | IPv4/IPv6 (Modbus TCP) | | **2 – Data Link** | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection | Ethernet MAC (PROFINET), PROFIBUS FDL, CAN MAC | | **1 – Physical** | Bit transmission, electrical/mechanical signaling | RS-485, 100BASE-TX, optical fiber | **Conclusion:** The OSI model provides modularity — each layer can evolve independently without breaking the overall system. For instance, Modbus RTU (Layers 1+2+7) can be transparently upgraded to Modbus TCP (Layers 1+2+3+4+7) by *inserting* the missing layers. --- #### (b) [7 Marks] A PROFIBUS segment is 1200 m long, operates at 1.5 Mbps, and uses 11-bit UART frames of length 1 byte. Compute (i) the propagation delay, (ii) the transmission delay per byte, (iii) the link utilization under Stop-and-Wait ARQ. Assume $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. **Model Solution:** **(i) Propagation Delay:** $$T_{prop} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{1200}{2 \times 10^8} = 6.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 6.0 \, \mu s$$ **(ii) Transmission Delay per byte:** For 11-bit UART framing (1 start + 8 data + 1 parity + 1 stop = 11 bits): $$T_{trans}^{byte} = \frac{11 \text{ bits}}{1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 7.33 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 7.33 \, \mu s$$ **(iii) Stop-and-Wait Utilization:** Compute the ratio $a$: $$a = \frac{T_{prop}}{T_{trans}^{byte}} = \frac{6.0}{7.33} \approx 0.8186$$ Apply the Stop-and-Wait formula: $$\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 2(0.8186)} = \frac{1}{2.637} \approx 0.3792$$ **Answer:** $\eta \approx 37.9\%$ — relatively poor utilization, which justifies **sliding-window** protocols for long-distance PROFIBUS. --- ### ✅ Question B (Choice 2 – Alternative) #### (a) [7 Marks] Compare the Bus, Star, and Ring topologies in industrial networks with respect to fault tolerance, cabling cost, scalability, and typical industrial use. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Bus | Star | Ring | |---|---|---|---| | **Fault Tolerance** | Poor (1 break → whole bus fails) | Good (1 link fails → only that branch) | Excellent (with MRP, survives 1 break) | | **Cabling Cost** | Low (single backbone) | Medium (every node needs home-run cable) | Medium (one cable segment between adjacent nodes) | | **Scalability** | Limited by bus length & terminators | High (just add another switch port) | Limited by max ring latency | | **Ease of Troubleshooting** | Difficult (cable break stops all) | Easy (per-link diagnostics) | Moderate | | **Typical Industrial Use** | Legacy fieldbus (PROFIBUS DP) | PROFINET, EtherNet/IP plant networks | Backbone rings with MRP | **Conclusion:** Modern industrial Ethernet overwhelmingly favors **star topology** at the field level (cheap unmanaged switches + PoE for IO devices) combined with **redundant ring topology** at the plant backbone (using MRP). This hybrid gives both *easy maintenance* and *high availability*. --- #### (b) [7 Marks] Derive the Stop-and-Wait link utilization formula $\eta = \frac{1}{1+2a}$, explaining the meaning of $a$ and its impact on long-distance links. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 – Define the round-trip cycle.** Under Stop-and-Wait ARQ, the sender transmits one frame of size $L_f$ bits at rate $R$, waits for it to propagate over distance $d$, waits for the receiver to process and send back the ACK, and waits for the ACK to propagate back. The total cycle time is: $$T_{cycle} = T_{trans} + 2T_{prop} + T_{proc}$$ Assuming negligible $T_{proc}$ and ACK transmission time: $$T_{cycle} \approx \frac{L_f}{R} + \frac{2d}{v}$$ **Step 2 – Define useful time.** Only the forward frame transmission is "useful" data: $$T_{useful} = T_{trans} = \frac{L_f}{R}$$ **Step 3 – Compute utilization.** $\eta = T_{useful}/T_{cycle}$: $$\eta = \frac{L_f / R}{L_f / R + 2d / v}$$ Divide numerator and denominator by $L_f/R$: $$\eta = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{2d R}{v L_f}}$$ **Step 4 – Define $a$.** Let $$a = \frac{T_{prop}}{T_{trans}} = \frac{d R}{v L_f}$$ This is the **propagation-to-transmission ratio**, a dimensionless number that quantifies how "long" the link is *relative to* the frame size. Substituting: $$\boxed{\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}}$$ **Step 5 – Interpretation.** - **Short links, large frames:** $a \ll 1$, so $\eta \to 1$ (excellent utilization). - **Long links, small frames:** $a \gg 1$, so $\eta \to 0$ (poor utilization). - **Example:** A GEO satellite link has $T_{prop} \approx 270$ ms, while a 1 KB frame at 1 Mbps has $T_{trans} = 8$ ms → $a = 33.75$ → $\eta \approx 1.5\%$! This is why **TCP sliding window** is essential. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warnings > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in "Basics" Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing PDU names:** Writing "packet" for every layer's PDU. The correct terminology: *bit (L1), frame (L2), packet (L3), segment/datagram (L4), message (L7)*. Examiners *specifically* deduct marks for misnaming PDUs. > > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Stop-and-Wait:** Writing $\eta = \frac{1}{1+a}$ instead of $\frac{1}{1+2a}$. The "2" comes from the **round-trip** propagation. Always show the timeline explicitly. > > 3. **Mixing up bus and star fault behavior:** A common error is claiming a star topology fails completely if the central switch fails. Yes, the *switch* is a SPOF, but **a cable break only kills one branch** — which is the key exam point. > > 4. **Wrong unit conversions:** $1 \text{ byte} = 8 \text{ bits}$, but UART frames are typically 10 or 11 bits (start, data, parity, stop). Don't forget the overhead. > > 5. **Confusing OSI model with TCP/IP model:** OSI has 7 layers; TCP/IP has 4 layers (Application, Transport, Internet, Link). Many protocols (PROFIBUS, Modbus RTU) only implement 3 layers of the OSI model. > > 6. **Drawing topologies without labels:** Always label nodes (sensor, controller, HMI) — naked circles lose marks. > > 7. **Ignoring termination resistors:** A bus topology without $R_T \approx Z_0$ on both ends will exhibit reflections — a *very common* industrial troubleshooting point. --- ## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this checklist before your exam:** - [ ] Industrial Network = **Sense → Decide → Actuate** communication backbone. - [ ] **Three pillars:** Physical, Data Link, Application. - [ ] OSI has **7 layers**; TCP-IP has **4**. - [ ] PDU names: Bit → Frame → Packet → Segment/Datagram → Message. - [ ] **Topology trade-off:** Bus (cheap, fragile) < Star (flexible, SPOF at switch) < Ring (redundant) < Mesh (max fault tolerance, expensive). - [ ] **Fully connected mesh edges:** $E = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$. - [ ] **Transmission delay:** $T_{trans} = L / R$. - [ ] **Propagation delay:** $T_{prop} = d / v$. - [ ] **Stop-and-Wait utilization:** $\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}$, where $a = T_{prop}/T_{trans}$. - [ ] **Purdue Model:** Level 0 (Process) → Level 1 (Control) → Level 2 (Supervisory) → Level 3 (Operations) → Level 4 (Enterprise). - [ ] **Termination resistor:** $R_T \approx Z_0$ (120 Ω PROFIBUS, 100 Ω CAN). - [ ] **PROFINET**, **EtherCAT**, **Modbus TCP** are TCP/IP-based; **PROFIBUS**, **Modbus RTU** are RS-485-based. - [ ] **MRP** recovers from cable break in **< 200 ms** — the gold standard for industrial ring redundancy. - [ ] **OPC-UA** is the modern cross-vendor application-layer standard, supporting encryption (TLS) and information modeling. - [ ] **Determinism** is the *single most important* property that distinguishes industrial from IT networks.

topics.title: Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Industrial Network Design** is the systematic engineering process of planning, specifying, and architecting a deterministic, real-time communication infrastructure that interconnects field-level sensors/actuators, control-level PLCs/DCSs, and enterprise-level SCADA/MES systems. The design must satisfy the **Four Pillars of Industrial Networking**: **Determinism**, **Reliability**, **Safety**, and **Security (DRSS)**, while complying with standards like **IEC 61784**, **IEEE 802.3**, **IEC 61158**, and **ISA-95**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Lock:** Industrial Network Design is **NOT** a generic IT-network design. The presence of hard real-time constraints (cycle times in the **1–10 ms** range for motion control) and harsh electromagnetic environments makes it a unique engineering discipline.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview (The "City Planning" Analogy)

Imagine you are a **city planner** for a smart industrial city:

| City Element | Industrial Network Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Roads & Highways | Cabling (Copper / Fiber / Wireless) |
| Traffic Signals & Speed Limits | Protocol Determinism & Cycle Time |
| Zoning Laws | Network Segmentation (OT vs IT zones) |
| Emergency Exit Routes | Redundancy Protocols (MRP, PRP, HSR) |
| Building Codes (Fire, Earthquake) | EMC, IP-Rating, Temperature Standards |

Just as a city planner must balance **traffic flow**, **safety**, **cost**, and **future expansion**, an industrial network designer must balance **bandwidth**, **latency**, **redundancy cost**, and **scalability** for Industry 4.0 readiness.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Performance Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Industrial Network Constants (Must Memorize for KTU Exam)**
> - **PROFINET IRT (Isochronous Real-Time) Cycle Time:** ≤ **1 ms**
> - **EtherCAT Cycle Time:** ≤ **100 µs**
> - **Maximum Copper Cable Length (100BASE-TX):** **100 m**
> - **Maximum Fiber Cable Length (100BASE-FX):** **2 km (Multimode)**, **20 km (Singlemode)**
> - **Maximum Devices per PROFIBUS Segment:** **32 stations**
> - **PROFIBUS Baud Rate vs. Distance:** **1.5 Mbps → 200 m**, **12 Mbps → 100 m**
> - **Industrial EMC Standard:** **IEC 61000-6-2** (Immunity), **EN 55011** (Emission)
> - **IP67 Protection Rating** is the industrial benchmark for field devices.

### 1.4 Primary Design Objectives (Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)

1. **Guaranteed Data Delivery** under harsh EMI/RFI conditions.
2. **Bounded Latency** (Worst-Case Latency = Best-Case Latency + Jitter ≤ Application Deadline).
3. **Zero-Downtime Operation** through Layer 2/Layer 3 redundancy.
4. **Conformance with the Purdue Model** (Levels 0–5).
5. **Cybersecurity Hardening** (IEC 62443 zone/conduit model).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six-Phase Industrial Network Design Methodology

The KTU-recommended design workflow is decomposed into six sequential phases:

**Phase 1 — Requirements Elicitation & Traffic Analysis**
- Identify all data flows: Cyclic (process data), Acyclic (diagnostics/alarms), Event-driven (safety).
- Compute **bandwidth per device** using the protocol's frame structure.
- Map the **Purdue Model Levels** (L0: Sensor → L1: PLC → L2: SCADA → L3: MES → L4: ERP → L5: Cloud).

**Phase 2 — Topology Selection**
- Choose between **Line**, **Star**, **Ring**, **Tree**, or **Hybrid (Daisy-Chain + Star)**.
- Industrial preference: **Ring topology** (enables cable redundancy) or **Line/Segmented Star**.

**Phase 3 — Physical Medium Selection**
- **Copper (Twisted Pair)**: Short distances ≤ 100 m, cost-sensitive, EMI-moderate zones.
- **Fiber Optics (MMF/SMF)**: Long distances, high EMI zones, intrinsic galvanic isolation.
- **Wireless (WLAN/5G/Wi-Fi 6)**: Mobile assets, hazardous (cable-free) areas.

**Phase 4 — Protocol Selection (The "Matching" Phase)**
- Match **application cycle time** to **protocol determinism class**:
  - Motion Control (≤ 1 ms) → PROFINET IRT / EtherCAT / SERCOS III
  - Process Automation (10–100 ms) → PROFINET RT / EtherNet/IP / Modbus TCP
  - High-level / Diagnostics (> 100 ms) → Standard TCP/IP / OPC UA

**Phase 5 — IP Addressing & Subnetting Design (VLSM)**
- Build a logical addressing plan with reserved subnets for management, control, safety, and diagnostics.
- **Why?** Prevents broadcast storms and enables ACLs (Access Control Lists).

**Phase 6 — Redundancy, Security & Documentation**
- Apply protocols: **MRP** (Media Redundancy Protocol, ≤ 500 ms recovery), **PRP** (Parallel Redundancy Protocol, 0 ms loss), **HSR** (High-availability Seamless Redundancy).
- Apply **IEC 62443** zones and conduits.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Rule | Units / Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimum Cycle Time (Tcyc)** | $T_{cyc} \geq n \cdot t_{prop}^{max} + t_{proc}$ | ms |
| 2 | **Worst-Case Latency Bound** | $T_{lat}^{wc} = T_{lat}^{best} + Jitter$ | ms |
| 3 | **Effective Bandwidth Utilization** | $\eta = \dfrac{Payload_{bits}}{Frame_{total\_bits} \cdot N_{frames}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| 4 | **PROFINET IRT Bandwidth Reservation** | Reserved share for IRT ≥ **50%** of cycle | % |
| 5 | **Cable Length vs. Baud Rate (PROFIBUS DP)** | $L_{max}(m) \approx \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^8}{BaudRate(bps)}$ | meters |
| 6 | **Network Availability (High-Availability)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless, 0–1 (Five-9s = 0.99999) |
| 7 | **Subnet Mask Slash Conversion** | Hosts = $2^{h} - 2$ where $h = 32 - \text{prefix}$ | integer count |
| 8 | **Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC-32)** | $G(x) = x^{32} + x^{26} + x^{23} + \ldots + 1$ | polynomial (IEEE 802.3) |
| 9 | **Signal Attenuation in Copper (dB/100m)** | Attenuation ∝ $\sqrt{f}$ (approx. **6.6 dB** at 10 MHz, Cat6) | dB |
| 10 | **Fiber Attenuation Budget** | $P_{budget} = P_{TX} - P_{RX(min)}$ | dBm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** The **"−2"** in the host formula accounts for the **Network Address** and the **Broadcast Address**, which are reserved and unusable for device assignment.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Factories (I4.0)**: Design enables machine-to-machine (M2M) communication for predictive maintenance.
- **Process Industries (Oil & Gas, Pharma)**: Design ensures **SIL-2/SIL-3** safety integrity levels per IEC 61508.
- **Automotive Assembly Lines**: Motion-synchronized robotic arms require ≤ 1 ms jitter tolerance, achievable only through IRT-class design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — VLSM Subnetting for a Plant Network (Analytical Rigor)

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** An industrial plant requires a network design with the following zones:
- **Zone A (Process Control):** 60 devices
- **Zone B (SCADA Servers):** 25 devices
- **Zone C (Safety I/O):** 12 devices
- **Zone D (Management):** 5 devices

Using base network **192.168.10.0/24**, perform **VLSM design** assigning subnets from largest to smallest.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

We start with the largest requirement (Zone A: 60 hosts).

Required host bits $h$ must satisfy: $2^h - 2 \geq 60$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^5 - 2 &= 30 \quad \text{(insufficient)} \\
2^6 - 2 &= 62 \quad \text{(sufficient)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $h = 6$ host bits → Prefix = $32 - 6 = /26$ → Subnet Mask = 255.255.255.192.

**Step A — Zone A (60 hosts)**
- Network: 192.168.10.0/26
- First usable: 192.168.10.1
- Last usable: 192.168.10.62
- Broadcast: 192.168.10.63
- Next subnet starts at 192.168.10.64.

**Step B — Zone B (25 hosts)**
- Need $2^h - 2 \geq 25$ → $h = 5$ → Prefix = /27 → Mask 255.255.255.224.
- Network: 192.168.10.64/27
- First usable: 192.168.10.65
- Last usable: 192.168.10.94
- Broadcast: 192.168.10.95
- Next subnet starts at 192.168.10.96.

**Step C — Zone C (12 hosts)**
- Need $2^h - 2 \geq 12$ → $h = 4$ → Prefix = /28 → Mask 255.255.255.240.
- Network: 192.168.10.96/28
- First usable: 192.168.10.97
- Last usable: 192.168.10.110
- Broadcast: 192.168.10.111
- Next subnet starts at 192.168.10.112.

**Step D — Zone D (5 hosts)**
- Need $2^h - 2 \geq 5$ → $h = 3$ → Prefix = /29 → Mask 255.255.255.248.
- Network: 192.168.10.112/29
- First usable: 192.168.10.113
- Last usable: 192.168.10.118
- Broadcast: 192.168.10.119
- **Address Reclaimed:** 192.168.10.120 – 192.168.10.255 (136 addresses for future expansion).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cable Length vs. Baud Rate (PROFIBUS)

**Problem Statement:** A PROFIBUS DP network must operate at **1.5 Mbps**. Using the standard empirical formula, derive the maximum segment length.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{max}(m) &= \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^8}{BaudRate(bps)} \\
L_{max} &= \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^6} \\
L_{max} &= 100 \text{ meters}
\end{aligned}
$$

The result confirms the KTU value: At **12 Mbps**, length drops to ≈ 12.5 m; at **93.75 kbps**, length rises to ≈ 1600 m. This inverse relationship is the **fundamental trade-off** in industrial serial-bus design.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Network Availability (Five-9s)

**Problem Statement:** Compute the availability and annual downtime for a system with **MTBF = 50,000 hours** and **MTTR = 30 minutes (0.5 hours)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\
A &= \dfrac{50000}{50000 + 0.5} \\
A &= \dfrac{50000}{50000.5} \\
A &\approx 0.999990 \quad \text{(Five-9s availability)} \\
\text{Annual Downtime} &= (1 - A) \times 8760 \text{ hours} \\
&= 0.00001 \times 8760 \\
&= 0.0876 \text{ hours} \\
&\approx 5.26 \text{ minutes/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** Five-9s is the minimum acceptable availability for **process control**. Safety-critical loops (SIL-3) demand **Six-9s (99.9999%)** ≈ 31.5 seconds/year downtime.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Protocol Selection

| Selection Criterion | PROFINET IRT | EtherCAT | EtherNet/IP | Modbus TCP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cycle Time Capability | ≤ 1 ms | ≤ 100 µs | 1–10 ms | > 50 ms |
| Topology | Star/Line | Daisy-Chain/Line | Star/Tree | Star/Line |
| Sync Jitter | < 1 µs | < 1 µs | ~ 1 ms | Not specified |
| Best Suited For | Motion + Process | High-speed motion | Discrete / Process | Legacy / SCADA |
| Standard Body | IEC 61158/61784 | IEC 61158/61784 | ODVA / IEC | Modbus IDA |

### 3.5 KTU Numericals — Common Subnetting Edge Cases

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** When asked "How many usable hosts in /27?", students often answer **128**. The correct answer is **$2^5 - 2 = 30$**. Always subtract 2 for network and broadcast addresses.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Six-Phase Industrial Network Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase1: Requirements Elicitation and Traffic Analysis] --> B[Phase2: Topology Selection]
    B --> C[Phase3: Physical Medium Selection]
    C --> D[Phase4: Protocol Selection]
    D --> E[Phase5: IP Addressing and VLSM]
    E --> F[Phase6: Redundancy Security and Documentation]
    F --> G[Design Validation and Commissioning]
    G --> A
```

### 4.2 Purdue Model Mapping (Industrial Network Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L0["LEVEL 0: PHYSICAL PROCESS"]
        s1[Sensors]
        a1[Actuators]
    end
    subgraph L1["LEVEL 1: BASIC CONTROL"]
        plc1[PLC]
        rtu1[RTU]
        dcs1[DCS Controller]
    end
    subgraph L2["LEVEL 2: SUPERVISORY"]
        scada1[SCADA HMI]
        alarm1[Alarm Server]
    end
    subgraph L3["LEVEL 3: OPERATIONS"]
        mes1[MES]
        hist1[Historian]
    end
    subgraph L4["LEVEL 4: ENTERPRISE"]
        erp1[ERP]
    end
    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
```

### 4.3 Industrial Network Redundancy Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RINGA["RING NETWORK A"]
        sw1[Managed Switch 1] --> sw2[Managed Switch 2]
        sw2 --> sw3[Managed Switch 3]
        sw3 --> sw4[Managed Switch 4]
        sw4 --> sw1
    end
    MRP[MRP Manager Protocol Engine] -.monitors.-> sw1
    MRP -.monitors.-> sw2
    MRP -.monitors.-> sw3
    MRP -.monitors.-> sw4
    sw1 <--> sw3
```

### 4.4 Cable & Connector Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Choose Physical Medium] --> q1{Distance greater than 100 m?}
    q1 -->|No| q2{EMI Zone above Zone B?}
    q1 -->|Yes| fiber[Use Fiber Optic MMF or SMF]
    q2 -->|No| copper[Use Cat6 or Cat7 Twisted Pair]
    q2 -->|Yes| armor[Use Armored or Shielded Cable with Conduit]
    fiber --> q3{Length above 2 km?}
    q3 -->|No| mmf[Use Multimode Fiber OM3 OM4]
    q3 -->|Yes| smf[Use Singlemode Fiber OS2]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1.** *Define Industrial Network Design and list the four pillars that govern it.*

**Model Answer:**
Industrial Network Design is the structured engineering methodology used to plan, architect, and implement a real-time, deterministic communication infrastructure connecting field devices, controllers, and supervisory systems. The **Four Pillars (DRSS)** are:
1. **Determinism** — Bounded and predictable latency.
2. **Reliability** — High MTBF and fast fault recovery.
3. **Safety** — Compliance with IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 (SIL levels).
4. **Security** — Defense in depth per IEC 62443.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for the four pillars.)*

---

**Q2.** *Compare PROFINET IRT and EtherCAT based on cycle time and topology.*

**Model Answer:**
