| Parameter | PROFINET IRT | EtherCAT |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum Cycle Time | ≤ 1 ms | ≤ 100 µs |
| Topology | Star / Line | Daisy-Chain / Line |
| Sync Jitter | < 1 µs | < 1 µs |
| Standard | IEC 61158 / 61784 | IEC 61158 / 61784 |

*(3 marks: 1 mark cycle time + 1 mark topology + 1 mark standard reference.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, ESE Module 3]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Purdue Reference Model for Industrial Network Design**. Identify the protocols suitable for communication at each level. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An industrial plant is divided into four zones requiring **50, 20, 10, and 4** devices. Using base network **172.16.0.0/24**, perform **VLSM subnetting** and tabulate the results. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Purdue Model (7 marks):**
- **Level 0 – Physical Process:** Sensors, actuators, motors. Protocols: 4–20 mA HART, IO-Link, ASI.
- **Level 1 – Basic Control:** PLCs, DCS, RTUs. Protocols: PROFINET, EtherCAT, PROFIBUS, Foundation Fieldbus.
- **Level 2 – Supervisory:** SCADA, HMI. Protocols: OPC UA, Modbus TCP, Ethernet/IP.
- **Level 3 – Operations:** MES, Historian, Asset Management. Protocols: SQL/ODBC, OPC UA, MQTT.
- **Level 4 – Enterprise:** ERP (SAP, Oracle). Protocols: Web Services, REST APIs.
- **Level 5 – Internet/Cloud:** Protocols: HTTPS, MQTT, AMQP.

The student must draw the **5-level block diagram** with bidirectional data flow and **DMZ** placement between L3 and L4.

**(b) VLSM Subnetting (7 marks):**

Largest to smallest: 50 → 20 → 10 → 4.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Zone 1: } 2^h - 2 \geq 50 \Rightarrow h = 6 \Rightarrow \text{prefix} = /26 \\
\text{Zone 2: } 2^h - 2 \geq 20 \Rightarrow h = 5 \Rightarrow \text{prefix} = /27 \\
\text{Zone 3: } 2^h - 2 \geq 10 \Rightarrow h = 4 \Rightarrow \text{prefix} = /28 \\
\text{Zone 4: } 2^h - 2 \geq 4  \Rightarrow h = 3 \Rightarrow \text{prefix} = /29
\end{aligned}
$$

| Zone | Network | Subnet Mask | First Host | Last Host | Broadcast |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zone 1 (50) | 172.16.0.0/26 | 255.255.255.192 | 172.16.0.1 | 172.16.0.62 | 172.16.0.63 |
| Zone 2 (20) | 172.16.0.64/27 | 255.255.255.224 | 172.16.0.65 | 172.16.0.94 | 172.16.0.95 |
| Zone 3 (10) | 172.16.0.96/28 | 255.255.255.240 | 172.16.0.97 | 172.16.0.110 | 172.16.0.111 |
| Zone 4 (4) | 172.16.0.112/29 | 255.255.255.248 | 172.16.0.113 | 172.16.0.118 | 172.16.0.119 |

*(1 mark for derivation + 1 mark for VLSM table + 1 mark for addressing sequence.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the **redundancy protocols MRP, PRP, and HSR** in industrial network design. Compare their recovery time and use cases. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A PROFIBUS network must be designed for **500 kbps** over a maximum segment length. Using the empirical relation, derive the maximum cable length and recommend the cable type. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Redundancy Protocols (7 marks):**

| Protocol | Full Name | Recovery Time | Topology | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MRP** | Media Redundancy Protocol | ≤ 500 ms | Ring | Standard process automation |
| **PRP** | Parallel Redundancy Protocol | **0 ms (Zero-Loss)** | Dual-star (two parallel LANs) | Substations / IEC 61850 |
| **HSR** | High-availability Seamless Redundancy | **0 ms (Zero-Loss)** | Ring (DANP nodes) | Traction, Smart Grids |

- **MRP:** Uses one **MRM (Manager)** and multiple **MRC (Clients)**. On link failure, the manager sends a **topology change frame** and the ring reconfigures within ≤ 500 ms.
- **PRP:** Sends **duplicate frames** on two disjoint networks. The receiver discards the duplicate. Zero-loss is achieved by physical path diversity, not protocol convergence.
- **HSR:** Similar to PRP but uses a **ring topology** with **DANP (Doubly-Attached Node with PRP support)**. Each frame is forwarded by intermediate nodes; the source removes its own frame.

**(b) PROFIBUS Cable Length (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &= \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^8}{BaudRate} \\
L_{max} &= \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^5} \\
L_{max} &= 300 \text{ meters}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommended Cable Type:** **PROFIBUS DP Cable** (e.g., Siemens **6XV1830-0EH10**) — a **shielded twisted pair** with characteristic impedance of **150 Ω**, capacitance ≤ 30 pF/m, and 24 AWG conductor. For longer distances or high-EMI environments, use **fiber-optic PROFIBUS** with **OLM (Optical Link Module)** repeaters.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Forgetting the "−2" in host count** (1–2 marks lost in every VLSM question).
> - **Confusing PRP and HSR** (both are zero-loss, but PRP uses dual-star while HSR uses ring).
> - **Drawing PROFINET topology as a "ring" without mentioning MRP** — without MRP, a ring becomes a **broadcast storm** because of Ethernet loops. Always pair the topology with the redundancy protocol.
> - **Mixing up MRP recovery time**: It is ≤ 500 ms, **not** 50 ms. Students frequently miswrite this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Design**

- [ ] Industrial Network Design is governed by the **DRSS Pillars**: Determinism, Reliability, Safety, Security.
- [ ] The **Purdue Model** has **6 levels (L0–L5)**; the **DMZ** sits between L3 and L4.
- [ ] **PROFINET IRT** → ≤ 1 ms cycle; **EtherCAT** → ≤ 100 µs cycle.
- [ ] **100BASE-TX Copper** limit = **100 m**; **100BASE-FX MMF** = **2 km**; **SMF** = **20 km**.
- [ ] **PROFIBUS DP** segment: 1.5 Mbps ↔ 100 m; 12 Mbps ↔ 12.5 m (inverse relation).
- [ ] **32 stations max per PROFIBUS segment**; segments extend using **repeaters** (4 max → 126 stations).
- [ ] **MRP** = Ring, ≤ 500 ms recovery; **PRP** = Dual-star, 0 ms loss; **HSR** = Ring, 0 ms loss.
- [ ] **VLSM formula:** Hosts = $2^{h} - 2$ where $h = 32 - \text{prefix length}$.
- [ ] **Five-9s Availability (99.999%)** ≈ 5.26 min/year downtime.
- [ ] **EMC Standards:** IEC 61000-6-2 (Immunity), EN 55011 (Emission).
- [ ] **IP67** is the standard ingress protection for industrial field devices.
- [ ] **Cable Shielding types:** UTP → STP → Armored → Fiber (EMI resistance increases left to right).
- [ ] Always specify **Managed Switches** (not unmanaged) for industrial networks to enable **VLAN, IGMP Snooping, QoS, MRP, and Port Mirroring**.
- [ ] **Cybersecurity:** Use **IEC 62443** zones and conduits, with a **firewall/DMZ** between IT and OT networks.
- [ ] For **SIL-3** safety loops, use **PROFIsafe** over PROFINET or **CIP Safety** over EtherNet/IP.
- [ ] **Documentation deliverables:** Logical diagram, Physical cable schedule, IP plan, Device list, Bill of Materials (BoM), FAT/SAT procedures.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Installation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Installation # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Installation** in the context of Industrial Networks refers to the disciplined, standards-driven physical deployment of fieldbus and industrial Ethernet infrastructure — encompassing **cable selection, routing, connector termination, shielding, equipotential bonding (grounding), surge protection, segment design, and commissioning** — such that the digital communication signals (PROFIBUS DP/PA, FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1/HSE, DeviceNet, ControlNet, Industrial Ethernet / PROFINET) are transported reliably across electrically noisy industrial shop-floors while meeting deterministic timing and EMC (Electromagnetic Compatibility) requirements mandated by IEC 61158 / IEC 61784 and the physical-layer profile of the chosen protocol. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Installation is **NOT** merely "laying cables". It is a structured engineering discipline covering the **Physical Layer (Layer 1 of the OSI model)** combined with the **environmental engineering** required to keep Layer 1 deterministic in harsh industrial environments (temperature, EMI, vibration, chemical exposure, ingress). > [!NOTE] > **Why Installation is a separate KTU Module topic:** Because roughly **70% of all industrial network field failures** reported in plant reliability surveys are attributable to improper installation practices — bad crimps, mixed cable types, daisy-chain vs trunk-line violations, missing or broken shields, and improper grounding. The protocol logic may be perfect, but the bits still cannot arrive intact. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Think of installing an industrial network like **installing the plumbing for a hospital operating theatre**: | Theatre Plumbing (Analogy) | Industrial Network Installation (Reality) | |---|---| | Copper water pipes | Fieldbus cables (copper twisted pair / fiber) | | Pipe diameter & material | Cable type, impedance, gauge (e.g., 18 AWG, 100 Ω) | | Pipe joints and welds | Connectors, splices, terminators (resistive 100/120 Ω) | | Water pressure regulator | Repeaters, segment design, voltage-drop budget | | Earthing strip on metal pipes | Cable shield grounding at one point (avoid ground loops) | | Filters at the inlet | Surge arrestors, EMC glands, ferrite cores | If a pipe is too narrow, the flow drops. If a joint leaks, contamination enters. If the earthing strip forms a loop, electrolytic corrosion happens — equivalent to a **ground loop carrying noise current** that corrupts the digital signal. **Installation is the art of making sure the "plumbing" of bits is leak-free, well-earthed, and sized correctly.** ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold) - **Characteristic impedance of PROFIBUS cable: $Z_0 = 150\ \Omega \pm 10\%$** (IEC 61158-2) - **FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1 cable impedance: $Z_0 = 100\ \Omega \pm 10\%$** - **Industrial Ethernet (PROFINET) cable impedance: $Z_0 = 100\ \Omega \pm 15\%$** (Cat 5e / Cat 6) - **PROFIBUS DP standard baud rate ladder: 9.6 kbaud → 19.2 kbaud → 45.45 kbaud → 93.75 kbaud → 187.5 kbaud → 500 kbaud → 1.5 Mbaud → 3 Mbaud → 6 Mbaud → 12 Mbaud** - **Standard cable capacitance: ≤ 60 pF/m** for PROFIBUS Type A - **Standard cable loop resistance: ≤ 110 Ω/km** for PROFIBUS Type A - **Maximum recommended segment length (PROFIBUS DP at 1.5 Mbaud): 200 m** without repeaters - **Bus termination resistor value: $220\ \Omega$** (PROFIBUS) — two resistors in series across the differential pair, with $390\ \Omega$ pull-up/pull-down to the 5 V idle reference ## 1.4 What Belongs to "Installation" (Scope Checklist) 1. Site survey and cable routing plan 2. Cable selection (type, gauge, jacket material, fire rating) 3. Cable tray / conduit / cable ladder design 4. Connector selection and termination (M12, D-Sub 9-pin, RJ45 industrial, terminal blocks) 5. Shielding and grounding methodology 6. Segment design (bus length, stubs, device count) 7. Surge and lightning protection 8. Ingress protection (IP rating selection) 9. Labeling, documentation, as-built drawings 10. Commissioning tests: cable continuity, insulation resistance, attenuation, signal quality # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Installation Stack (5-Layer Logical Model) Industrial network installation can be broken into five engineered sub-systems. KTU examiners love asking students to **list or diagram** these. ### Layer A — Cable Selection - **Type A** (PROFIBUS DP reference cable): 2-core twisted, shielded, **150 Ω**, ≤ 60 pF/m, ≤ 110 Ω/km loop. - **Type B**: Lower-grade alternative, useful only for short low-speed segments. - **Type C**: Untwisted — generally **not recommended** for new installations (often an exam trick). - **Fiber options**: Multimode 62.5/125 µm (HCS) for H1, single-mode for long-haul. ### Layer B — Connectors & Termination - **D-Sub 9-pin** for PROFIBUS DP (pin 3 = data B, pin 8 = data A, pin 6 = +5 V VP, pin 5 = GND). - **M12 D-coded / X-coded** for PROFINET. - **Terminal-block screw terminators** for FOUNDATION Fieldbus spurs. - **Bus terminators**: Two $220\ \Omega$ resistors in series across the pair, with $390\ \Omega$ bias resistors to +5 V and GND forming a voltage divider (idle ≈ 1 V differential). ### Layer C — Shielding & Grounding - **Shield purpose**: Confine radiated RF emissions and drain induced common-mode currents to ground. - **Single-point grounding** at one end of segment — to **avoid ground loops** carrying 50 Hz mains equalizing current which would otherwise destroy the small differential signal. - For very long segments, **multi-point grounding** through capacitive coupling (e.g., 1 nF + low impedance at the cabinet entry). - The shield must be **360° bonded** (not a "pigtail") to the connector backshell. ### Layer D — Segment Topology - **Daisy chain** (recommended for bus protocols — PROFIBUS, FF H1). - **Tree** (allowed for FF H1 with junction boxes). - **Star** (mandatory for Industrial Ethernet / PROFINET — CSMA/CD principles). - **Stubs (drop cables)**: Have **maximum allowable length** dependent on baud rate. ### Layer E — EMC & Surge Protection - Surge arrestors (gas discharge tube + TVS diode) at building entry points. - Ferrite clamps on long cable runs near VFDs. - Maintain **separation distance ≥ 200 mm** between signal cables and power cables at crossings (per IEC 50174). ## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (the "How" and "Why") | Step | Why It Matters | |---|---| | Match cable impedance to termination | Prevents **signal reflections** at cable joints → eliminated echo that smears bits | | Twisted pair geometry | Cancels **common-mode noise** picked up from VFD / motor cables | | 360° shield bond | Pigtail inductances (~1 µH per cm) become **antennas at 30–100 MHz** — exactly where fast edges of digital signals live | | Single-point shield ground | Prevents 50 Hz mains leakage current from **flowing through the shield** and converting common-mode into differential noise | | Stub length limit | Stubs act as unterminated branches — **T-junctions create reflections** | | Repeater spacing | Each segment has a finite reach due to **attenuation + signal-timing budget** | | Equipotential bonding | Equalizes ground potentials across buildings → prevents flashover during fault or lightning | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formula Sheet — KTU 2024 Module 3 (Installation)** | # | Quantity / Rule | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | PROFIBUS DP max segment length (Type A) | $L_{max} = \dfrac{1000}{v} \times (t_{prop})$ — practically tabulated | see Baud-rate table below | | 2 | Baud-rate vs Segment Length (PROFIBUS Type A) | Empirical table (see §2.4) | Length in **meters** | | 3 | Voltage drop on bus segment | $V_{drop} = I_{total} \times R_{loop} \times L$ | Volts; $I_{total}$ in A, $R_{loop}$ in Ω/km, $L$ in km | | 4 | Characteristic impedance | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L'}{C'}}$ | L' in H/m, C' in F/m | | 5 | Attenuation budget | $A_{budget} = \alpha \cdot L + A_{conn}$ | dB; $\alpha$ in dB/km | | 6 | Stub length limit (PROFIBUS) | $L_{stub} \le \dfrac{v \cdot t_{bit}}{10}$ (rule-of-thumb) | Baud-rate dependent | | 7 | Bus terminator divider (PROFIBUS) | $V_{idle} = 5\text{ V} \cdot \dfrac{390}{220 + 390 + 390}$ ≈ 1.0 V | Idle voltage A–B | | 8 | Insulation resistance test | $R_{ins} \ge 100\ \text{M}\Omega$ at 500 V DC | Megger test | | 9 | Max devices per PROFIBUS DP segment | **32 stations** per segment (addresses 0–127 overall) | Repeaters regenerate the bus | | 10 | Max devices per FF H1 segment | **32 devices** (16 in hazardous area) | Powered by bus | | 11 | Cable bend radius | $\ge 10 \times D$ (static), $20 \times D$ (dynamic) | D = cable outer diameter | | 12 | Separation from power cables | $\ge 200$ mm (parallel), $90°$ at crossings | IEC 50174 | | 13 | Surge arrester clamping voltage | $V_{clamp} \le V_{max,bus}$ | e.g., 8 V for 5 V bus | ## 2.4 Segment Length vs Baud Rate (KTU Hot Table) > [!NOTE] > **PROFIBUS DP Type A cable segment length (without repeaters):** | Baud Rate | Max Segment Length | |---|---| | 9.6 kbps | 1200 m | | 19.2 kbps | 1200 m | | 45.45 kbps | 1200 m | | 93.75 kbps | 1200 m | | 187.5 kbps | 1000 m | | 500 kbps | 400 m | | 1.5 Mbps | 200 m | | 3 Mbps | 100 m | | 6 Mbps | 100 m | | 12 Mbps | 100 m | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Refineries and petrochemical plants**: Installation defines the IECEx/ATEX compliance boundary. A single broken shield bond can void Ex protection. - **Automotive manufacturing (PROFINET)**: Robot cells demand **Cat 6A / Profinet Type C** with M12 X-coded connectors rated for 10 million mating cycles. - **Power plants (FOUNDATION Fieldbus)**: H1 segments powered from the wire itself; **voltage drop budget** governs whether 16 devices fit in one hazardous-area segment. - **Substations (IEC 61850 / process bus)**: Fiber installation must follow **IEEE 1613** for EMI hardening. # SECTION_3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PROFIBUS DP Cable Attenuation Limit (worked example) **Problem (typical 7-mark KTU problem):** > A PROFIBUS DP segment uses Type A cable at **1.5 Mbaud**. Each device draws 5 mA quiescent current. There are 24 devices evenly distributed over a 180 m segment. The bus terminators are at the two ends. Compute the voltage seen by the **worst-case mid-bus device** and decide whether the design is within spec (bus idle voltage at midspan should remain ≥ 0.9 V, minimum supply at any device ≥ 4.0 V for a 24 V supply distribution if it were powering devices — for pure DP, check 5 V VP). ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify cable parameters (Type A, from KTU data table).** $$R_{loop} = 110\ \Omega/\text{km} \quad (\text{loop} = 2 \times 55\ \Omega/\text{km for one core})$$ **Step 2 — Compute the total loop resistance from midspan to either end.** $$L_{half} = \dfrac{180}{2} = 90\ \text{m} = 0.09\ \text{km}$$ $$R_{half} = R_{loop} \times L_{half} = 110 \times 0.09 = 9.9\ \Omega$$ **Step 3 — Compute current contribution from half-bus devices.** Devices on one half = 12. Plus the mid-span device itself draws from both halves symmetrically, so we treat 12 devices on the half-side drawing 5 mA each: $$I_{half} = 12 \times 5\ \text{mA} = 60\ \text{mA} = 0.060\ \text{A}$$ **Step 4 — Compute voltage drop at midspan relative to the terminator (idle bus case).** $$V_{drop,half} = I_{half} \times R_{half}$$ $$\begin{aligned} V_{drop,half} &= 0.060 \times 9.9 \\ &= 0.594\ \text{V} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the idle bus voltage seen by the mid-device.** The terminator Thevenin equivalent voltage at the idle (5 V bias) is: $$V_{idle,ideal} = V_{bias} \cdot \dfrac{R_{term}}{R_{term}+R_{bias,total}}$$ For PROFIBUS terminator: $R_{term} = 2 \times 220 = 440\ \Omega$ (the two $220\ \Omega$ resistors in series across the pair), bias network $390+390 = 780\ \Omega$ in series with the 5 V. The differential idle is approximately: $$V_{idle} \approx 5\ \text{V} \cdot \dfrac{440}{440 + 780} \approx 5 \cdot \dfrac{440}{1220} \approx 1.80\ \text{V}$$ But our midspan sees this voltage **reduced by the voltage drop** along the loop: $$\begin{aligned} V_{idle,mid} &= V_{idle,ideal} - V_{drop,half} \\ &= 1.80 - 0.594 \\ &= 1.21\ \text{V} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Compare to spec.** $$V_{idle,mid} = 1.21\ \text{V} \ge 0.9\ \text{V} \quad \checkmark \text{ PASS}$$ So the design is acceptable. If the bus length had been 600 m at the same loading, the drop would have been: $$V_{drop,half} = 0.060 \times (110 \times 0.300) = 0.060 \times 33 = 1.98\ \text{V}$$ giving $V_{idle,mid} = 1.80 - 1.98 = -0.18\ \text{V}$ → **FAIL** → design requires a **repeater** mid-segment. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Stub Length on PROFIBUS DP **KTU Principle:** A stub (T-junction drop cable) acts as an unterminated transmission line. Reflections at the open end of the stub return to the trunk and corrupt the bit. **Rule:** To keep reflections from corrupting the active bit cell, the stub round-trip time must be **less than approximately 1/10 of the bit period**. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Bit period at chosen baud rate.** $$t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{\text{baud rate}}$$ For 1.5 Mbaud: $$t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 666.7\ \text{ns}$$ **Step 2 — Propagation velocity in PROFIBUS Type A cable.** Typical velocity factor $v_f \approx 0.78$ (i.e., $v = 0.78 c$): $$v = 0.78 \times 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s} = 2.34 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum stub length.** $$L_{stub} = \dfrac{v \cdot t_{bit}}{2 \times 10} = \dfrac{v \cdot t_{bit}}{20}$$ (The factor 2 = round trip; factor 10 = safety divisor on bit cell) $$\begin{aligned} L_{stub,1.5Mbps} &= \dfrac{2.34 \times 10^8 \times 666.7 \times 10^{-9}}{20} \\ &= \dfrac{156.0}{20} \\ &= 7.8\ \text{m} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Practical PROFIBUS stub length table.** | Baud Rate | $t_{bit}$ | Max Stub Length | |---|---|---| | 1.5 Mbps | 667 ns | ≈ 6.6 m (some refs say 6.7 m) | | 500 kbps | 2000 ns | ≈ 20 m | | 187.5 kbps | 5333 ns | ≈ 53 m | | 93.75 kbps | 10667 ns | ≈ 106 m | (The KTU/PI table caps 1.5 Mbps stub length at **6.6 m** in practice.) > [!NOTE] > **Exam tip:** If asked "Why does the stub limit shrink at higher baud rates?" — answer: *"Because the bit cell shrinks in time, and the round-trip delay of the stub represents a larger fraction of that cell, hence more inter-symbol interference."* ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fiber-Optic Link Attenuation Budget **Problem:** A PROFIBUS segment uses multimode fiber with 850 nm VCSEL-based transceivers. The link is 2.4 km long. Compute whether the link works if each connector gives 0.5 dB loss and the fiber itself has attenuation 3 dB/km. **Step 1 — Identify losses.** - Fiber attenuation: $3\ \text{dB/km} \times 2.4\ \text{km} = 7.2\ \text{dB}$ - Connectors (one transmitter-side + one receiver-side): $2 \times 0.5 = 1.0\ \text{dB}$ - Splice margin / aging / temperature: reserve **3 dB** **Step 2 — Total loss.** $$\begin{aligned} A_{total} &= A_{fiber} + A_{conn} + A_{margin} \\ &= 7.2 + 1.0 + 3.0 \\ &= 11.2\ \text{dB} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Compare with transmitter power budget.** Typical 850 nm VCSEL OLM budget: $-3\ \text{dBm}$ transmit into $-23\ \text{dBm}$ required receive sensitivity = **20 dB budget**. $$A_{total} = 11.2\ \text{dB} \le 20\ \text{dB} \quad \checkmark \text{ PASS (with margin)}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Installation Decision Table (KTU Hot Content) | Parameter | PROFIBUS DP | FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1 | PROFINET (Industrial Ethernet) | |---|---|---|---| | Topology | Daisy chain (linear) | Trunk + spurs (tree) | Star (switch-centric) | | Cable impedance | 150 Ω | 100 Ω | 100 Ω (Cat 5e/6) | | Connector | D-Sub 9 / M12 | Terminal block / 7/8" | M12 D/X-coded, RJ45-IP67 | | Terminator | $2 \times 220\ \Omega + 2 \times 390\ \Omega$ | $100\ \Omega \pm 2\%$ at each end | N/A (switch port terminated) | | Bus power | No (external) | Yes (9–32 V on wire) | No (PoE optional) | | Max devices per segment | 32 | 32 (16 hazardous) | Per switch port, no real limit | | Speed ladder | 9.6 k → 12 M | 31.25 k fixed | 100 M / 1 G / 10 G | | Determinism | Token ring | Scheduled LAS | RT / IRT (PROFINET) | # SECTION_4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Industrial Network Installation — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CTRL["CONTROL LAYER - Cabinets & Engineering Room"] PLC["PLC / DCS Controller
PROFIBUS Master CP card"] ENGWS["Engineering Workstation
Configuration & Diagnostics"] end subgraph SEG1["SEGMENT 1 - PROFIBUS DP, 1.5 Mbaud, 180 m"] TERM1["Terminator
220 ohm + 390 ohm"] DEV1["Frequency Inverter
Slave Address 3"] DEV2["Motor Protection Relay
Slave Address 4"] DEV3["Valve Actuator
Slave Address 5"] TERM2["Terminator
220 ohm + 390 ohm"] end subgraph SEG2["SEGMENT 2 - Repeater, 500 kbaud, 350 m"] DEV4["Flow Meter Slave"] DEV5["Pressure Transmitter Slave"] DEV6["Temperature Transmitter Slave"] end subgraph SEG3["SEGMENT 3 - FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1 Trunk + Spurs"] JBOX["Junction Box
Short-circuit protection"] SPUR1["Pressure Transmitter on 30 m spur"] SPUR2["Flow Meter on 20 m spur"] end subgraph PROT["PROTECTION LAYER"] SURGE["Surge Arrester
at cabinet entry"] FERRITE["Ferrite Clamps
on long runs"] end PLC --> SURGE --> TERM1 TERM1 --> DEV1 --> DEV2 --> DEV3 --> TERM2 TERM2 -.RS485 repeater link.-> DEV4 DEV4 --> DEV5 --> DEV6 SURGE --> JBOX --> SPUR1 JBOX --> SPUR2 ENGWS --- PLC ``` ## 4.2 Cable Cross-Section Anatomy (PROFIBUS DP Type A) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph XSEC["PROFIBUS DP TYPE A CABLE CROSS SECTION"] OUTER["Outer Jacket
PVC or PUR, color violet"] SHLD["Overall Shield
Aluminized Foil + Tinned Copper Braid"] DRAIN["Drain Wire
for shield termination"] FILL1["Inner Filler / Filler Cord"] CORE_RED["Core 1 - RED
Conductor A, 0.64 mm^2, 22 AWG"] CORE_GREEN["Core 2 - GREEN
Conductor B, 0.64 mm^2, 22 AWG"] FOAM["Foam PE Insulation
gives 150 ohm impedance
and 60 pF/m capacitance"] end OUTER --> SHLD --> DRAIN SHLD --> FOAM FOAM --> CORE_RED FOAM --> CORE_GREEN FOAM --> FILL1 ``` ## 4.3 Shield Grounding — Single-Point vs Multi-Point Topology ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SINGLE["SINGLE-POINT GROUND - PREFERRED FOR LOW FREQUENCY"] A1["Cabinet A
PE bar"] B1["Cabinet B
PE bar"] C1["Cabinet C
PE bar"] GND1["Central Earth
ground rod"] A1 --> B1 --> C1 --> GND1 end subgraph MULTI["MULTI-POINT GROUND - via Y-capacitor at high frequency"] A2["Cabinet A"] B2["Cabinet B"] C2["Cabinet C"] YC["Y-Capacitor 1 nF
low Z at RF"] PE2["PE bus"] A2 -.Y-cap.- B2 B2 -.Y-cap.- C2 A2 --- PE2 B2 --- PE2 C2 --- PE2 end ``` ## 4.4 Connector Termination Schematic (D-Sub 9-Pin PROFIBUS) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DSUBS["D-SUB 9-PIN MALE / FEMALE PINOUT"] P1["Pin 1 - SHIELD"] P2["Pin 2 - n.c."] P3["Pin 3 - DATA B (RxD / TxD-P)"] P4["Pin 4 - RTS"] P5["Pin 5 - DGND (data ground)"] P6["Pin 6 - VP +5V (termination power)"] P7["Pin 7 - n.c."] P8["Pin 8 - DATA A (RxD / TxD-N)"] P9["Pin 9 - n.c."] end P3 --- P8 P6 --- P5 P1 --- P1 ``` ## 4.5 Segment Design Flow (Stepwise Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB S1["Start: Define Baud Rate
1.5 Mbps chosen"] S2{"Distance less than
200 m?"} S3["Single segment design
32 stations max"] S4["Insert Repeater
each new segment 200 m"] S5["Verify stub lengths
6.6 m max"] S6["Compute voltage drop
at worst-case device"] S7["Voltage drop OK?"] S8["Add mid-segment
power booster or shorten"] S9["Document and commission
Megger test + signal quality"] S1 --> S2 S2 -- Yes --> S3 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 S2 -- No --> S4 --> S5 S7 -- No --> S8 --> S6 S7 -- Yes --> S9 ``` ## 4.6 Surge Protection Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph OUTDOOR["OUTDOOR - FIELD DEVICE"] FD["Field Transmitter
on cable run from cabinet"] end subgraph ENTRY["BUILDING ENTRY POINT"] GDT["Gas Discharge Tube
90V breakdown"] TVS["TVS Diode
6V clamp"] FERR["Ferrite Bead
common-mode choke"] end subgraph INDOOR["INDOOR - CABINET"] PLC2["PLC PROFIBUS Port"] end FD --> GDT --> TVS --> FERR --> PLC2 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2023] **Q: List any three installation rules for PROFIBUS DP cable. Why is the shield bonded 360° at the connector?** **Model Answer (10 lines):** 1. Use **PROFIBUS Type A cable** (150 Ω ± 10 %, ≤ 60 pF/m, ≥ 0.64 mm² conductor). 2. Maintain **maximum segment length 200 m at 1.5 Mbaud**; for longer distances use a repeater. 3. Keep **stub length ≤ 6.6 m at 1.5 Mbaud**; every stub acts as an unterminated branch and causes reflections. 4. Maintain **200 mm separation** from power cables in cable trays; cross at 90°. 5. **360° shield bond** is required because a "pigtail" shield termination has ~1 µH inductance per cm which behaves as an antenna in the 30–100 MHz band — exactly where the fast edges of digital bus signals reside. A 360° bond has near-zero pigtail inductance and drains common-mode current directly to ground without converting it to differential noise. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: Differentiate between single-point and multi-point shield grounding. When is multi-point grounding preferred in industrial installations?** **Model Answer (8 lines):** - **Single-point grounding:** Shield bonded to PE at only **one end** of the cable run. Used for low-frequency signals (PROFIBUS, FF H1). Prevents 50 Hz mains equalizing currents from circulating in the shield and coupling into the data pair as differential noise. - **Multi-point grounding:** Shield bonded to PE at **multiple points** along the run, typically via low-impedance capacitive coupling (Y-capacitors, ~1 nF). Used at **high frequencies / long runs** where the shield would otherwise behave as a long ungrounded antenna radiating at RF. - In modern installations a **hybrid** approach is common: **direct bond at one end, capacitive bond at the other** to ground the RF currents while blocking the 50 Hz loop current. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks] **Q: (a)** Explain the role of bus terminators in PROFIBUS DP. With a neat schematic, describe the PROFIBUS standard terminator circuit and derive the idle voltage seen on the bus. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A PROFIBUS DP Type A segment runs at **500 kbps**, is **380 m long**, has **24 devices** drawing 4 mA each at idle, plus a PLC master drawing 80 mA. Compute the worst-case voltage drop from a midspan device to the terminator and state whether the design passes the spec (bus voltage at any node ≥ 0.9 V). Show all steps. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Role of Bus Terminator + Idle Voltage Derivation [7 Marks] **Role of terminator (3 marks):** - Eliminates signal reflections at the ends of the bus by matching the cable's characteristic impedance. - Provides a DC bias voltage differential (idle ≈ 1 V) so that all slave receivers see a defined logic "1" state during quiet periods between frames. - The terminator is **only at the two physical ends** of the segment — never in the middle. **Standard PROFIBUS Terminator Circuit (2 marks):** The terminator is a resistive network placed at each end of the segment: $$R_1 = R_2 = 220\ \Omega \quad \text{(in series across data A and data B)}$$ $$R_3 = R_4 = 390\ \Omega \quad \text{(pull-up to +5 V on A, pull-down to GND on B)}$$ These four resistors form a Wheatstone bridge. With the cable impedance of 150 Ω terminated by 220 Ω, the small mismatch (150 vs 220) is an engineering compromise: 220 Ω gives a **steeper edge** but ~10 % reflection, which is acceptable at the chosen bit rates. **Derivation of Idle Voltage (2 marks):** The 5 V source drives the divider formed by $R_3 + R_{cable} + R_4$ with the parallel of the two 220 Ω resistors forming the differential load. Treating the divider (ignoring cable for DC): $$V_{idle} = V_{CC} \times \dfrac{R_{diff}}{R_{diff} + R_{bias,total}}$$ where $R_{diff} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = 220 \parallel 220 = 110\ \Omega$ across A and B, and $R_{bias} = R_3 + R_4 = 390 + 390 = 780\ \Omega$ in series. Wait — the actual circuit has the 220 Ω in series across the pair, not in parallel. Re-correcting: $R_1$ (220 Ω) is between +5 V node and line A; $R_2$ (220 Ω) between line A and line B; $R_3$ (220 Ω) between line B and GND; bias resistors $R_4 = R_5 = 390\ \Omega$ provide the +5 V and GND connections in parallel. In steady state (DC, idle), the network is: $$V_A = 5 \cdot \dfrac{110}{110 + 780} + \text{(cable drop)} \approx 0.62\ \text{V + drop}$$ The **simplified, exam-grade answer** the KTU examiner expects: $$V_{idle,A-B} \approx \dfrac{5\ \text{V} \times 220}{220 + 2 \times 390 + 220} \approx \dfrac{1100}{1220} \approx 1.0\ \text{V}$$ So the idle differential is ≈ **1.0 V**, above the receiver threshold of typically 0.6 V. #### Part (b) — Voltage Drop Calculation [7 Marks] **Given:** - Baud rate = 500 kbps → max segment length from table = **400 m** → 380 m is **OK** for segment length rule. - $L = 380$ m = 0.38 km - 24 devices × 4 mA = 96 mA = 0.096 A - Master draws 80 mA = 0.080 A - Total bus current = $0.096 + 0.080 = 0.176$ A **Loop resistance of cable = 110 Ω/km.** **Worst case: midspan device, current flowing from both terminators (current is supplied from both ends — the slave receivers draw from the pair).** Take the midspan device at 190 m. Current on one side of midspan = half of total current (by symmetry): $$I_{half} = \dfrac{0.176}{2} = 0.088\ \text{A}$$ Loop resistance from midspan to one end: $$R_{half} = 110 \times 0.19 = 20.9\ \Omega$$ Voltage drop at midspan (from terminator to midspan along the loop): $$V_{drop} = I_{half} \times R_{half} = 0.088 \times 20.9 = 1.84\ \text{V}$$ Idle voltage at terminator ≈ 1.0 V → idle voltage at midspan: $$V_{idle,mid} = 1.0 - 1.84 = -0.84\ \text{V}$$ **This is NEGATIVE → FAIL** ❌ **Conclusion & Recommendation:** The design violates the spec. Solutions: 1. **Reduce device count** on this segment to ≤ 10 devices, OR 2. **Insert a mid-segment repeater** that also acts as a mid-bus power-feeder, OR 3. **Shorten the segment** to ≤ 150 m and recheck. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks] **Q: (a)** Describe the FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1 installation rules including segment topology, terminators, spurs and maximum device count. Compare with PROFIBUS DP. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compute the maximum allowable stub length for a PROFIBUS DP segment at **12 Mbps** and at **187.5 kbps**. The velocity factor of the cable is 0.78. Why does the stub limit change with baud rate? **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1 Installation vs PROFIBUS DP [7 Marks] **FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1 (3 marks):** - **Topology**: Trunk-and-spur (tree), ideal for instrumentation in process plants. - **Cable**: 100 Ω characteristic impedance, shielded twisted pair (often Type A "bus cable"). - **Terminator**: One 100 Ω ± 2 % resistor at each end of the **trunk** — simpler than PROFIBUS. - **Spurs (drop cables)**: Can be up to 120 m total spur length per segment, with each individual spur ≤ 30 m; total spurs combined ≤ 120 m. - **Bus-powered**: Devices draw power (≥ 9 V) from the wire itself; bus supply voltage typically 24 V DC; current-limited to ≤ 60 mA per spur (Ex) or higher (non-Ex). - **Max devices**: 32 total per segment; in hazardous (Ex ia) areas, **16 devices** due to safety current limits. - **Polarity-sensitive**: Some H1 wiring requires A / B polarity to be observed (whereas PROFIBUS DP is differential and polarity-tolerant except for the +5 V bias). **Comparison with PROFIBUS DP (4 marks):** | Parameter | PROFIBUS DP | FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1 | |---|---|---| | Cable impedance | 150 Ω | 100 Ω | | Topology | Daisy chain only | Trunk + spurs | | Speed | 9.6 k – 12 M baud | 31.25 k baud fixed | | Power on bus | No | Yes | | Terminator | $2 \times 220\ \Omega + 2 \times 390\ \Omega$ | $1 \times 100\ \Omega$ at each trunk end | | Polarity | Differential, tolerant | Polarity may matter | | Ex device count | Per Ex approval | 16 per segment (ia) | | Application | Discrete + process | Pure process | #### Part (b) — Stub Length at 12 Mbps and 187.5 kbps [7 Marks] **Formula (re-derived from §3.2):** $$L_{stub,max} = \dfrac{v_f \cdot c \cdot t_{bit}}{20}$$ where $v_f = 0.78$, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, and $t_{bit} = 1/\text{baud}$. **Step 1 — Propagation velocity.** $$v = 0.78 \times 3 \times 10^8 = 2.34 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Stub length at 12 Mbps.** $$t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{12 \times 10^6} = 83.3\ \text{ns}$$ $$L_{stub} = \dfrac{2.34 \times 10^8 \times 83.3 \times 10^{-9}}{20} = \dfrac{19.5}{20} = 0.97\ \text{m} \approx 1\ \text{m}$$ (PI official table caps this at **1 m** at 12 Mbps.) **Step 3 — Stub length at 187.5 kbps.** $$t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{187.5 \times 10^3} = 5333\ \text{ns}$$ $$L_{stub} = \dfrac{2.34 \times 10^8 \times 5333 \times 10^{-9}}{20} = \dfrac{1248}{20} = 62.4\ \text{m}$$ (PI table caps at **53 m**.) **Step 4 — Why the limit changes (1 mark):** The maximum stub length is bounded by the rule that the **round-trip reflection time of the stub must be much less than one bit cell**, otherwise the reflection from the open stub end returns inside the bit cell and causes inter-symbol interference (ISI). At higher baud rates, $t_{bit}$ is smaller, so the maximum stub length $L_{stub}$ shrinks in direct proportion. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Installation Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to include the loop resistance (×2 for both cores).** Loop resistance of 55 Ω/km per core means **110 Ω/km for the loop**. Many students write 55 Ω/km and end up with half the correct voltage drop. > 2. **Placing the terminator in the middle of the bus.** Terminator = end of segment ONLY. A mid-bus terminator destroys the AC signal. > 3. **Confusing PROFIBUS DP terminator (220+390 Ω) with FOUNDATION Fieldbus terminator (single 100 Ω).** These are different protocols — different networks — different terminators. > 4. **Using "pigtail" shield terminations.** A 2 cm pigtail = ~20 nH of inductance, which resonates at ~50 MHz and pumps noise directly into the data pair. > 5. **Ignoring baud-rate when computing stub length.** At 12 Mbps, stubs must be ≤ 1 m, not 6 m. Students who memorize one figure lose the working-out marks. > 6. **Crossing power and signal cables in the same tray.** KTU has zero tolerance for this in part (a) of installation questions. > 7. **Failing to draw the schematic** when asked "with a neat schematic". A verbal description without circuit diagram loses 1–2 marks. > 8. **Mixing cable types in one segment** (Type A + Type B). Different impedances → reflections at every junction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 3 — Installation — Quick-Recall Bullets** - ⭐ **Installation = Physical Layer engineering**, not just "laying cable". Includes cable, connectors, shielding, grounding, topology, EMC, commissioning. - ⭐ **PROFIBUS DP cable: Type A, 150 Ω, 22 AWG, violet jacket, ≤ 60 pF/m, ≤ 110 Ω/km loop.** - ⭐ **PROFIBUS DP terminator = 2× 220 Ω in series across A/B + 2× 390 Ω bias to +5 V/GND.** - ⭐ **PROFIBUS DP at 1.5 Mbaud: 200 m segment, 6.6 m stub, 32 stations per segment.** - ⭐ **FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1: 100 Ω cable, single 100 Ω terminator, trunk + spurs, 32 devices (16 in Ex ia).** - ⭐ **Industrial Ethernet (PROFINET): 100 Ω Cat 5e/6, star topology with switches, M12 D/X-coded, no terminator (switch port handles it).** - ⭐ **Idle bus voltage on PROFIBUS = ≈ 1 V differential (5 V × 220 / 1220).** - ⭐ **Stub length formula:** $L_{stub} = \dfrac{v \cdot t_{bit}}{20}$; inversely proportional to baud rate. - ⭐ **Shield must be bonded 360°** — no pigtails. - ⭐ **Single-point shield ground for low frequencies; multi-point (via Y-cap) at high frequencies / long runs.** - ⭐ **Cable-to-power separation: ≥ 200 mm parallel, 90° crossings.** - ⭐ **Bend radius: 10× diameter static, 20× diameter dynamic.** - ⭐ **Megger test: ≥ 100 MΩ at 500 V DC.** - ⭐ **Voltage drop formula:** $V_{drop} = I_{total} \times R_{loop} \times L$; must keep bus voltage ≥ 0.9 V at worst-case node. - ⭐ **Repeater** = segment regenerator; doubles segment reach and station count. - ⭐ **Surge arrester + TVS + ferrite** at building entry for outdoor runs. - ⭐ **Grounding rule:** Use **equipotential bonding** to avoid earth-loop currents through the shield. - ⭐ **Top three installation failure causes:** bad crimps, broken shield, wrong cable type.

topics.title: Commissioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Commissioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Commissioning** in the context of industrial networks is the **systematic, structured, and documented process** of verifying, testing, configuring, and validating that a newly installed (or modified) industrial communication network — including its physical media, active devices, controllers, and software — conforms to the design specification, engineering standards, and functional requirements before being handed over for live production operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEICT747, Module 3):**
> *Commissioning is the transitional phase between installation and operation. It ensures the network is electrically safe, logically configured, topologically correct, and functionally interoperable across all connected field devices, controllers, and supervisory systems (HMI/SCADA).*

The commissioning process is governed by international standards such as:
- **IEC 62381** — Factory Acceptance Test (FAT), Site Acceptance Test (SAT), and Site Integration Test (SIT) for automation systems.
- **IEC 61918** — Installation of communication networks in industrial premises.
- **IEEE 802.3** — Ethernet physical layer conformance.
- **ISA-95 / IEC 62264** — Enterprise-control system integration.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The House-Warming Before Moving In"**
> Imagine you have just constructed a new house. Before moving your family in, you do not simply unlock the door and start living there. You first check the plumbing for leaks, test every electrical socket, verify the gas line, calibrate the smoke detectors, and ensure the Wi-Fi reaches every room. You document each test result. If something fails, you fix it **before** it can cause harm. Only after the house passes every "warm-up" check is the family allowed to move in.
> **Industrial network commissioning is exactly this — but for a factory network.** Instead of family members, the "occupants" are PLCs, sensors, drives, and SCADA systems. Instead of a smoke detector, we test cable shielding, grounding, signal integrity, and protocol interoperability.

## 1.3 Why Commissioning Matters in Industrial Networks

Unlike office IT networks, an industrial network failure can cause:
- **Production downtime** (costing $10,000–$250,000 per hour in process industries).
- **Safety hazards** (explosions, chemical leaks, mechanical injury).
- **Data corruption** in real-time control loops.
- **Cascading failures** across tightly coupled PLCs and drives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** A well-commissioned industrial network reduces mean-time-between-failures (MTBF), shortens mean-time-to-repair (MTTR), and ensures deterministic communication for time-critical control loops.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Expansion | Definition |
|------|-----------|------------|
| **FAT** | Factory Acceptance Test | Testing performed at the vendor's facility before shipment. |
| **SAT** | Site Acceptance Test | On-site testing after delivery, verifying installation correctness. |
| **SIT** | Site Integration Test | Verification of interoperability among all subsystems on-site. |
| **Punch List** | Deficiency List | A document listing all defects/non-conformances found during commissioning. |
| **Loop Check** | End-to-End Signal Test | Verifying that a signal path from sensor → controller → actuator is continuous and correct. |
| **EPC** | Engineering, Procurement, Construction | The contractor responsible for project delivery, including commissioning. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Commissioning Lifecycle (V-Model Mapping)

The commissioning lifecycle follows the **V-Model** of project execution. Each design phase has a corresponding verification phase on the descending arm of the V.

**Left arm (design):** User Requirements → Functional Spec → Detailed Design → Implementation
**Right arm (verification):** Unit Test → Integration Test → SAT → FAT/Commissioning → Operation

## 2.2 Phases of Industrial Network Commissioning

### Phase 1: Pre-Commissioning (Documentation & Visual Inspection)
- Verify that the **Bill of Materials (BoM)** matches the installed equipment.
- Confirm that **cable drum test certificates** (from manufacturer) are present.
- Inspect **cable routing, segregation** (e.g., power cables separated from signal cables per **IEC 61537**).
- Verify **bend radius** compliance: minimum **8 × cable diameter** for Cat6/Cat7; **10 × D** for fiber optics.
- Check **IP rating** of enclosures at field junction boxes.

### Phase 2: Electrical & Physical Layer Testing
- **Insulation Resistance (IR) Test:** Apply a 500 V DC megger between each conductor and ground.
  - Acceptance threshold: $\text{IR} \ge 100\,\text{M}\Omega$ for new copper cables.
- **Continuity Test:** Verify end-to-end conductor continuity with a low-voltage ohmmeter.
- **Shield Continuity Test:** Confirm the **drain wire / shield** is continuous from field device to cabinet.
- **Grounding / Earthing Test:** Measure **earth electrode resistance** $\le 5\,\Omega$ for industrial installations (per **IS 3043**).
- **Polarity & Wire-Check Test** for DC circuits.

### Phase 3: Powered Commissioning (Hot Tests)
- Apply power in a controlled ramp-up sequence:
  1. Power up **network switches** first, verify LED status, console login.
  2. Power up **PLCs / controllers**, verify scan cycle, firmware version.
  3. Power up **field devices** (sensors, actuators), verify handshake on bus.
- Use a **protocol analyzer** (e.g., Wireshark with Profinet/Modbus plugins) to capture handshake frames.
- Verify **device description files (GSD / EDS / GSDML)** are loaded correctly in the master configuration tool (e.g., TIA Portal, Step 7, Studio 5000).

### Phase 4: Functional & Performance Verification
- **Loop Checks:** For every control loop (e.g., a temperature transmitter → PID block → control valve), simulate a known input and verify the expected output.
- **Network Performance Metrics:**
  - **Latency** (end-to-end delay) $\le 5\,\text{ms}$ for Profinet IRT, $\le 100\,\text{ms}$ for Modbus TCP.
  - **Jitter** $\le 1\,\text{ms}$ for real-time motion-control networks.
  - **Packet Loss** $\le 0.01\%$ for control traffic; $\le 1\%$ for monitoring traffic.
  - **Bandwidth Utilization** $\le 70\%$ sustained (30% headroom reserved for alarms and diagnostics).

### Phase 5: Documentation & Handover
- **As-built drawings** (CAD/EPLAN) reflecting actual cable runs.
- **Network topology diagram** with IP/MAC address allocation.
- **Commissioning sign-off sheets** signed by client, EPC, and vendor.
- **Punch list closure** — every open item resolved or formally accepted with risk mitigation.

## 2.3 Tools & Instruments Used in Commissioning

| Tool | Function | Typical Use |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **Digital Multimeter (DMM)** | Voltage, current, resistance | Continuity, polarity, voltage checks. |
| **Insulation Tester (Megger)** | High-voltage insulation resistance | IR testing of cable insulation. |
| **Cable Certifier (Fluke DTX/DSX)** | Cat6/7 conformance to TIA-568 | Pass/fail certification with detailed report. |
| **OTDR** | Optical Time-Domain Reflectometer | Fiber-optic link loss, splice quality, fault location. |
| **Oscilloscope** | Waveform visualization | Signal integrity, EMI diagnostics. |
| **Protocol Analyzer** | Frame capture & decode | Profinet, EtherNet/IP, Modbus TCP decoding. |
| **Earth Tester** | Earth electrode resistance | Grounding system verification. |
| **Thermal Camera** | Hot-spot detection | Loose connections, overloaded circuits. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL:** The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used below (not the unescaped `|`) to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $\text{VSWR} = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ | Voltage Standing Wave Ratio — measures impedance matching. | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{Attenuation (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$ | Power loss along a cable or fiber link. | dB |
| 3 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio — link quality indicator. | dB |
| 4 | $R = \rho \dfrac{L}{A}$ | DC resistance of a copper conductor ($\rho$ = resistivity, $L$ = length, $A$ = cross-section). | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | $\Delta V = I \cdot R_{\text{loop}}$ | Voltage drop in a 2-wire loop (critical for 4–20 mA loops). | V |
| 6 | $\text{BER} = \dfrac{\text{Number of bit errors}}{\text{Total bits transmitted}}$ | Bit Error Rate — digital link integrity. | dimensionless |
| 7 | $t_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{n \cdot L}{c}$ | Signal propagation time in fiber ($n$ = refractive index $\approx 1.48$ for silica). | s |
| 8 | $t_{\text{cable,copper}} \approx 5 \text{ ns/m}$ | Rule-of-thumb propagation delay in copper twisted pair. | s/m |
| 9 | $L_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{slew}}}{2 \cdot \pi \cdot f_{\text{max}} \cdot C_{\text{load}}}$ | Maximum cable length vs. signal frequency (RS-485 guideline). | m |
| 10 | $\text{Link Loss Budget} = P_{\text{TX,min}} - P_{\text{RX,min}} - \text{Margin}$ | Fiber-optic loss budget calculation. | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** These formulas are essential in PEICT747 design and troubleshooting viva questions. KTU examiners frequently test the **attenuation formula** (Formula 2) and the **loop voltage drop** (Formula 5) for 4–20 mA current loops, which is the bedrock signal standard in process automation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Loop Voltage Drop in a 4–20 mA Current Loop

A 4–20 mA current loop is the **dominant field signaling standard** in industrial process automation. A transmitter sends a current between **4 mA (zero-scale)** and **20 mA (full-scale)** over a 2-wire (or 4-wire) copper pair. The receiver (e.g., a PLC analog input card) measures the current by sensing the voltage across a precision shunt resistor.

**Problem Statement:**
A pressure transmitter is located **L = 800 m** away from the PLC cabinet. The cable is 2-core copper with a cross-sectional area of **A = 0.5 mm²** and resistivity **$\rho$ = 1.72 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m**. The PLC input card has a **shunt resistor $R_{\text{shunt}} = 250\,\Omega$** and the transmitter requires a **minimum operating voltage $V_{\min} = 12\,\text{V}$**. The loop is powered from a **24 V DC supply**. Determine whether the loop will operate correctly at full scale ($I = 20\,\text{mA}$).

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compute the resistance of one conductor.}
\quad
R_{\text{conductor}} &= \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A} \\[6pt]
&= (1.72 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot \frac{800}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}} \\[6pt]
&= (1.72 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot (1.6 \times 10^{9}) \\[6pt]
&= 27.52\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Compute the loop (round-trip) resistance.}
\quad
R_{\text{loop}} &= 2 \cdot R_{\text{conductor}} \\[6pt]
&= 2 \cdot 27.52 \\[6pt]
&= 55.04\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The current must travel *out* on one core and *return* on the other, so the total conductor length is **2L**. We multiply the single-conductor resistance by 2.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Compute the voltage drop across the cable at full scale.}
\quad
\Delta V_{\text{cable}} &= I_{\max} \cdot R_{\text{loop}} \\[6pt]
&= 0.020 \cdot 55.04 \\[6pt]
&= 1.1008\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 4: Compute the voltage drop across the PLC shunt resistor.}
\quad
\Delta V_{\text{shunt}} &= I_{\max} \cdot R_{\text{shunt}} \\[6pt]
&= 0.020 \cdot 250 \\[6pt]
&= 5.0\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 5: Compute the total voltage consumed in the loop.}
\quad
V_{\text{consumed}} &= \Delta V_{\text{cable}} + \Delta V_{\text{shunt}} + V_{\min,\text{transmitter}} \\[6pt]
&= 1.1008 + 5.0 + 12.0 \\[6pt]
&= 18.1008\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 6: Compare with the 24 V supply.}
\quad
V_{\text{available}} - V_{\text{consumed}} &= 24 - 18.1008 \\[6pt]
&= 5.8992\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Since the residual **5.9 V > 0 V**, the loop will operate correctly. A good engineering practice is to maintain a **safety margin of at least 3 V** — which is satisfied here.

**Conclusion:** $\Delta V_{\text{cable}} = 1.10\ \text{V}$ is well within the budget. The 0.5 mm² cable is **adequate** for this 800 m run.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fiber-Optic Link Loss Budget

A fiber-optic link connects a **Profinet switch** to a **distributed I/O station** across a plant. The link must be commissioned with an OTDR to confirm the loss budget.

**Given Data:**
- Transmitter launch power: $P_{\text{TX}} = -7\ \text{dBm}$
- Receiver sensitivity: $P_{\text{RX,min}} = -22\ \text{dBm}$
- Fiber length: $L = 1500\ \text{m}$
- Fiber attenuation: $\alpha = 0.35\ \text{dB/km}$ (at 1310 nm)
- Connectors: **2 ST connectors** each with loss $0.5\ \text{dB}$
- Splice loss: **1 fusion splice** with loss $0.1\ \text{dB}$
- Design margin: **3 dB** (per TIA-568.3-E for aging and repairs)

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compute fiber attenuation loss.}
\quad
L_{\text{fiber}} &= \alpha \cdot L = 0.35 \cdot 1.5 = 0.525\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Compute connector loss.}
\quad
L_{\text{connector}} &= 2 \cdot 0.5 = 1.0\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Compute splice loss.}
\quad
L_{\text{splice}} = 1 \cdot 0.1 = 0.1\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 4: Compute total link loss.}
\quad
L_{\text{total}} &= L_{\text{fiber}} + L_{\text{connector}} + L_{\text{splice}} + M \\[6pt]
&= 0.525 + 1.0 + 0.1 + 3.0 \\[6pt]
&= 4.625\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 5: Compute received power at the distant end.}
\quad
P_{\text{RX,actual}} &= P_{\text{TX}} - L_{\text{total}} \\[6pt]
&= -7 - 4.625 \\[6pt]
&= -11.625\ \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 6: Compute the power margin at the receiver.}
\quad
\text{Margin} &= P_{\text{RX,actual}} - P_{\text{RX,min}} \\[6pt]
&= -11.625 - (-22) \\[6pt]
&= 10.375\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The received power is **10.375 dB above the receiver sensitivity floor**. The link is **healthy and well within specifications**. A typical industrial fiber link should maintain at least **3 dB of margin** to account for aging, connector contamination, and temperature variation — comfortably met here.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Commissioning Decision Logic

| Test Result | Possible Root Cause | Corrective Action | Re-test Required? |
|-------------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Insulation resistance $<$ 100 MΩ | Moisture ingress, damaged insulation, contaminated junction box | Dry/replace cable, re-terminate, seal junction | Yes |
| Continuity test — OPEN | Broken conductor, cold solder joint, mis-crimped connector | Re-crimp, re-solder, replace connector | Yes |
| Earth resistance $>$ 5 Ω | Loose ground rod, corroded clamp, insufficient rod depth | Drive deeper rods, clean clamp, add soil treatment | Yes |
| Profinet device — red LED | Duplicate IP, missing GSDML, cable polarity swapped | Re-assign IP, install GSDML, swap wires | Yes |
| Latency spike during scan | Broadcast storm, improper QoS, duplex mismatch | Disable unnecessary broadcast, enable QoS, set full-duplex | Yes |
| Fiber loss $>$ budget | Dirty connector, macrobend, bad fusion splice | Clean connectors, re-route cable avoiding bend $<$ 30 mm radius, re-fuse | Yes |
| 4–20 mA reads 3.8 mA at zero | Loop wiring reversed, missing 24 V at transmitter | Re-check polarity at transmitter terminals | Yes |
| Modbus slave unresponsive | Address conflict, baud-rate mismatch, parity error | Match baud rate/parity, set unique slave address | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Industrial Network Commissioning Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **end-to-end commissioning workflow** for an industrial network, from the moment a cable drum arrives on site until the network is signed off for production.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Project Handover from Engineering] --> A2[Receive Approved Design Documents]
    A2 --> A3[Draft Commissioning Plan and Procedures]
    A3 --> A4[Verify Cable Drum Test Certificates]
    A4 --> A5[Visual Inspection of Cable Routing and Segregation]
    A5 --> B1[Pre-Power Visual Checks]
    B1 --> B2[Insulation Resistance Test with Megger]
    B2 --> B3[Continuity and Polarity Test with DMM]
    B3 --> B4[Earthing and Bonding Verification]
    B4 --> B5[Shield Continuity Check]
    B5 --> C1[Power Up Network Switches and Verify LEDs]
    C1 --> C2[Power Up PLCs and Verify Scan Cycle]
    C2 --> C3[Power Up Field Devices and Verify Handshake]
    C3 --> C4[Load GSDML or EDS Files into Master]
    C4 --> C5[Assign IP and Device Names]
    C5 --> D1[Loop Checks Sensor to Controller to Actuator]
    D1 --> D2[Capture and Decode Protocol Traffic with Wireshark]
    D2 --> D3[Measure Latency Jitter and Packet Loss]
    D3 --> D4[Verify Network Redundancy and Failover]
    D4 --> E1[Compile As-Built Drawings and Topology]
    E1 --> E2[Issue Punch List for Open Items]
    E2 --> E3[Close Punch List and Obtain Sign-Off]
    E3 --> E4[Hand Over to Operations Team]
    E4 --> F1[Live Production Operation]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Commissioned Industrial Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FieldLevel["Field Level Devices"]
        S1[Temperature Sensor 4-20 mA]
        S2[Pressure Transmitter HART]
        A1[Control Valve with Positioner]
        VFD1[Variable Frequency Drive EtherNet IP]
    end
    subgraph ControlLevel["Control Level"]
        PLC1[PLC 1 Master Controller]
        SW1[Managed Industrial Switch Profinet]
        PLC2[PLC 2 Redundant Controller]
    end
    subgraph SupervisoryLevel["Supervisory Level"]
        SCADA1[SCADA Server]
        HMI1[Operator HMI Panel]
        HIS1[Historian Database]
    end
    S1 --> SW1
    S2 --> SW1
    A1 --> SW1
    VFD1 --> SW1
    SW1 --> PLC1
    SW1 --> PLC2
    PLC1 --> SCADA1
    PLC1 --> HMI1
    SCADA1 --> HIS1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Field-level instruments (sensors and actuators) communicate with managed switches via industrial protocols. The switches aggregate traffic to redundant PLCs. The SCADA server, HMI, and historian sit on the supervisory level. **Every connection in this diagram is a candidate for a commissioning test point** — physically (cable test) and logically (protocol test).

## 4.3 Commissioning Test Point Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TP1[TP1 Insulation Resistance Test] --> TP2[TP2 Continuity Test]
    TP2 --> TP3[TP3 Earth Resistance Test]
    TP3 --> TP4[TP4 Cable Certification Cat 6]
    TP4 --> TP5[TP5 OTDR Test for Fiber]
    TP5 --> TP6[TP6 Device Handshake Verification]
    TP6 --> TP7[TP7 Loop Check End to End]
    TP7 --> TP8[TP8 Latency and Jitter Measurement]
    TP8 --> TP9[TP9 Redundancy Failover Test]
    TP9 --> TP10[TP10 Final Documentation Sign Off]
```

> **Note for the student:** Mermaid diagrams above use clean, unformatted node labels (no bold, no italics, no HTML tables) to comply with the V10 rendering safety rules. All node IDs are alphanumeric to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define commissioning in the context of industrial networks. Mention any two international standards relevant to commissioning.
`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Commissioning in industrial networks is the systematic process of inspecting, testing, configuring, and validating that a newly installed network — including its cables, switches, controllers, and field devices — meets the design specification and is ready for reliable operation.

**Standards (1 mark):** Two relevant international standards are:
1. **IEC 62381** — Automation systems in the process industry — Factory acceptance test (FAT), Site acceptance test (SAT), and Site integration test (SIT).
2. **IEC 61918** — Industrial communication networks — Installation of communication networks in industrial premises.

---

### Question 2: List any four tools used during the commissioning of an industrial network and state one purpose of each.
`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer (3 marks, 0.75 each):**

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| **Digital Multimeter (DMM)** | Measures voltage, current, and resistance for continuity and polarity checks. |
| **Insulation Tester (Megger)** | Applies high DC voltage (e.g., 500 V) to verify cable insulation resistance. |
| **Cable Certifier (Fluke DSX)** | Conformance-tests Cat 6/7 cabling to TIA-568 and produces a pass/fail certificate. |
| **OTDR (Optical Time-Domain Reflectometer)** | Locates faults, splices, and measures attenuation in fiber-optic links. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Convention:** KTU Part B questions in the 2024 scheme offer an **internal choice** between two questions. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two fully independent alternatives; the student answers either one. Each is structured into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the different phases of commissioning an industrial network. Discuss the significance of the Factory Acceptance Test (FAT) and Site Acceptance Test (SAT) in the project lifecycle. `(7 marks)`
`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer:**

The commissioning of an industrial network is typically executed in the following sequential phases:

**Phase 1 — Pre-Commissioning (2 marks):**
This phase occurs *before* any power is applied. Activities include:
- Verifying that the installed equipment matches the Bill of Materials (BoM).
- Checking the **cable drum test certificates** issued by the manufacturer.
- Visual inspection of cable routing, segregation from power cables, and bend-radius compliance.
- Insulation resistance testing using a 500 V DC megger (acceptance ≥ 100 MΩ).
- Continuity, polarity, shield-continuity, and earth-resistance tests.

**Phase 2 — Powered Commissioning (2 marks):**
Power is applied in a controlled ramp-up sequence:
1. Network switches are powered first, verified via LEDs and console login.
2. PLCs/controllers are powered next; firmware version, scan cycle, and IP address are confirmed.
3. Field devices are powered last; device handshakes are verified using a protocol analyzer.

**Phase 3 — Functional Verification (2 marks):**
- Loop checks (sensor → controller → actuator) are performed for every control loop.
- Network metrics — latency, jitter, packet loss, bandwidth utilization — are measured against design criteria.
- Redundancy protocols (e.g., Profinet MRP, EtherNet/IP DLR, HSR) are triggered and verified.

**Phase 4 — Documentation and Handover (1 mark):**
- As-built drawings, network topology, IP allocation, and commissioning sign-off sheets are compiled.
- The punch list is closed and the system is formally handed over to operations.

**Significance of FAT and SAT (1 mark):**
- **FAT (Factory Acceptance Test):** Conducted at the vendor's factory *before* shipment. It validates that the system meets the contractual specification in a controlled environment, reducing the risk of defects reaching site. FAT is the last opportunity to make changes with minimal cost.
- **SAT (Site Acceptance Test):** Conducted at the actual installation site *after* delivery and installation. It confirms that the system operates correctly under real environmental conditions (temperature, humidity, EMI, grounding) and that all site-specific requirements are met. SAT is the contractual handover milestone.

Together, FAT and SAT form the **V-Model verification backbone** of any industrial automation project.

#### (b) A 4–20 mA current loop runs from a flow transmitter located **1.2 km** away to a PLC analog input card in the control room. The cable is 2-core copper, **0.75 mm²** cross-section, with resistivity **$\rho$ = 1.72 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m**. The PLC input card has a **shunt resistor of 250 Ω** and the transmitter needs a **minimum of 11 V** to operate. The supply is **24 V DC**. Determine:
- (i) The total loop resistance
- (ii) The voltage drop in the cable at full scale (20 mA)
- (iii) Whether the loop will operate correctly
`(7 marks)`
`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Total loop resistance (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{conductor}} &= \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A} \\[6pt]
&= (1.72 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot \frac{1200}{0.75 \times 10^{-6}} \\[6pt]
&= (1.72 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot (1.6 \times 10^{9}) \\[6pt]
&= 27.52\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{loop}} &= 2 \cdot R_{\text{conductor}} = 2 \cdot 27.52 = 55.04\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Voltage drop in the cable at 20 mA (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V_{\text{cable}} &= I_{\max} \cdot R_{\text{loop}} \\[6pt]
&= 0.020 \cdot 55.04 = 1.1008\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Will the loop operate correctly? (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{consumed}} &= \Delta V_{\text{cable}} + (I_{\max} \cdot R_{\text{shunt}}) + V_{\min} \\[6pt]
&= 1.1008 + (0.020 \cdot 250) + 11 \\[6pt]
&= 1.1008 + 5.0 + 11.0 = 17.1008\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{residual}} = 24 - 17.1008 = 6.8992\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $V_{\text{residual}} = 6.90\ \text{V} > 0\ \text{V}$ and exceeds the 3 V safety margin, the loop **will operate correctly** at full scale. The 0.75 mm² cable is **adequate** for this 1.2 km run.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the typical architecture of an industrial network and label the field, control, and supervisory levels. Explain the role of the managed industrial switch at the control level. `(7 marks)`
`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer:**

A typical industrial network follows the **ISA-95 / IEC 62264** hierarchical model, which partitions the plant into four distinct levels. A block diagram is shown below.

```
              +--------------------------+
              |  Enterprise / ERP Level  |   (Level 4)
              +--------------------------+
                              |
              +--------------------------+
              |   Supervisory Level      |   (Level 3)
              |   SCADA / HMI / Historian|
              +--------------------------+
                              |
              +--------------------------+
              |   Control Level          |   (Level 2)
              |   PLCs + Managed Switch  |
              +--------------------------+
                              |
              +--------------------------+
              |   Field Level            |   (Level 1 / 0)
              |   Sensors, Actuators,    |
              |   VFDs, Transmitters     |
              +--------------------------+
```

**Field Level (1.5 marks):** This consists of physical devices — temperature sensors, pressure transmitters, control valves, variable frequency drives (VFDs) — that interface directly with the process. They communicate using fieldbus protocols (HART, Profibus PA, Foundation Fieldbus) or industrial Ethernet (Profinet, EtherNet/IP).

**Control Level (2 marks):** This is the heart of the system. **PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers)** execute the control logic, while **managed industrial switches** interconnect them with the field devices and with the supervisory level. The managed switch supports features critical to industrial operation:
- **VLANs** for traffic segregation (control vs. monitoring vs. voice).
- **QoS / Priority Queues** to give time-critical control traffic precedence.
- **Redundancy protocols** (e.g., Profinet MRP, EtherNet/IP DLR).
- **IGMP Snooping** to manage multicast traffic efficiently.
- **Port-level diagnostics** (link loss, packet counts, error counters) for proactive maintenance.

**Supervisory Level (1.5 marks):** This is where **SCADA servers, operator HMI panels, and historian databases** reside. Operators monitor the process, set setpoints, acknowledge alarms, and view trends. The historian logs all process data for later analysis and regulatory compliance.

**Role of the Managed Industrial Switch (2 marks):**
- Acts as the **central aggregation point** for traffic between field devices and PLCs.
- Provides **deterministic, prioritized delivery** of control messages.
- Supports **network diagnostics and remote management** (SNMP, web interface).
- Enables **redundant ring topologies** for fault-tolerant operation.
- Filters broadcast and multicast traffic to prevent storm conditions that could disrupt real-time control.

#### (b) Explain the procedure for fiber-optic link commissioning using an OTDR. A fiber link is **2.5 km** long with attenuation coefficient **0.4 dB/km** at 1310 nm. It has **3 ST connectors** (each 0.5 dB) and **2 fusion splices** (each 0.08 dB). The transmitter launch power is **–6 dBm**, the receiver sensitivity is **–23 dBm**, and a **3 dB safety margin** is required. Determine if the link passes the loss-budget test. `(7 marks)`
`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer:**

**OTDR Commissioning Procedure (3 marks):**

1. **Preparation:** Clean all connector end-faces with lint-free wipes and isopropyl alcohol. Inspect with a fiber inspection scope (200× magnification).
2. **Launch and Receive Cables:** Connect a "launch reference cable" (typically 100–500 m) between the OTDR and the link under test, and a "receive cable" at the far end. These eliminate the OTDR's "dead zone" near the connectors.
3. **Set OTDR Parameters:** Select the appropriate wavelength (1310 nm or 1550 nm), pulse width, and range. For this link, 1310 nm is used.
4. **Acquire Trace:** Run the OTDR and capture the backscatter trace. The trace shows:
   - Connector events (step-up/step-down in dB).
   - Splice events (small step losses).
   - The slope of the backscatter (which gives attenuation per km).
   - The reflective end (end of fiber).
5. **Analyze Events:** Verify that each event's loss is within the specified budget. Look for unexpected bends or reflective events.
6. **Generate Report:** Save the trace and print a pass/fail commissioning certificate.
7. **Compare with Power Meter Test:** As a cross-check, use an optical power meter + light source to measure end-to-end insertion loss.

**Loss-Budget Calculation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Fiber attenuation loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{fiber}} &= 0.4 \cdot 2.5 = 1.0\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Connector loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{connector}} &= 3 \cdot 0.5 = 1.5\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Splice loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{splice}} &= 2 \cdot 0.08 = 0.16\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total loss budget (including margin):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}} &= 1.0 + 1.5 + 0.16 + 3.0 = 5.66\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Received power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{RX,actual}} &= P_{\text{TX}} - L_{\text{total}} = -6 - 5.66 = -11.66\ \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Power margin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Margin} &= P_{\text{RX,actual}} - P_{\text{RX,min}} \\[6pt]
&= -11.66 - (-23) = 11.34\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since the available margin (**11.34 dB**) is well above the 3 dB safety margin, **the link passes the loss-budget test** and is cleared for service.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PEICT747 Commissioning Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in loop-resistance calculations — examiners frequently deduct **2 full marks** when students compute the resistance of *one* conductor but apply it as the *loop* resistance.
> 2. **Mixing up the dB formula** — using $20 \log_{10}$ for power ratio (it should be $10 \log_{10}$) costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Ignoring the design margin** in fiber loss budgets — students often compute only fiber + connector + splice loss, forgetting to add the 3 dB aging/repair margin. This is a guaranteed **1.5 mark deduction**.
> 4. **Conflating FAT with SAT** — many students write "FAT is done on site" — it is not. FAT is at the vendor factory; SAT is on site. Examiners treat this as a fundamental conceptual error (**1–2 marks lost**).
> 5. **Not stating units** — "$R = 55$" is incomplete; "R = 55.04 Ω" is correct. Always carry units in every calculation step.
> 6. **In 4–20 mA problems, forgetting the shunt-resistor voltage drop** — a classic 1.5 mark error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Commissioning**

- **Commissioning** = systematic verification that the installed industrial network meets design specs before live operation.
- Governed by **IEC 62381 (FAT/SAT/SIT)** and **IEC 61918** (installation).
- **Five phases:** Pre-commissioning → Electrical/physical tests → Powered commissioning → Functional verification → Documentation & handover.
- **Insulation resistance** must be **≥ 100 MΩ** at 500 V DC for new copper cables.
- **Earth resistance** must be **≤ 5 Ω** for industrial installations.
- **Cable bend radius:** **8 × D** for copper, **10 × D** for fiber (minimum 30 mm for fiber to avoid macrobend).
- **Voltage drop formula** in a 4–20 mA loop: $V_{\text{drop}} = I \cdot 2\rho L/A$. **Always use 2L for the round-trip.**
- **Attenuation in dB:** $L = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}} / P_{\text{out}})$.
- **Fiber loss budget** must include fiber attenuation + connector loss + splice loss + 3 dB design margin.
- **Acceptance criteria for control networks:** Latency ≤ 5 ms (Profinet IRT), Jitter ≤ 1 ms, Packet loss ≤ 0.01 %.
- **Tools to remember:** DMM, Megger, Cable certifier, OTDR, Protocol analyzer (Wireshark), Earth tester, Thermal camera.
- **OTDR requires launch and receive cables** to eliminate dead-zone near connectors.
- **FAT is at the factory, SAT is at the site** — do not confuse them.
- **Punch list** = list of open items; must be fully closed before final sign-off.
- **Managed switch** features: VLAN, QoS, redundancy (MRP/DLR/HSR), IGMP snooping, port diagnostics.
- **Loop check** = verifying end-to-end signal path from sensor → controller → actuator.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Documentation and Global System Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Documentation and Global System Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Documentation** in the context of Industrial Networks refers to the systematic, structured collection, organization, and maintenance of all textual, graphical, electrical, logical, and procedural information describing the planning, design, installation, commissioning, operation, and maintenance of an industrial automation and control system (IACS) network. According to the **IEC 62443** and **IEC 61784** series standards adopted by KTU's PEICT747 syllabus, documentation is a *living deliverable* that must be updated every time a network component, address, topology, firmware, or cybersecurity posture changes.

**Global System Architecture (GSA)** refers to the holistic, multi-layered blueprint that integrates field instrumentation, control devices, supervisory systems, and enterprise-level IT infrastructure into a single coherent communication framework. It is rooted in the **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model** which formalizes the five-level hierarchy from physical sensors (Level 0) to enterprise resource planning (Level 4).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 specifically expects students to differentiate between *logical* and *physical* documentation, and to draw/interpret the **Purdue Reference Model** showing how industrial fieldbuses (PROFIBUS, PROFINET, Foundation Fieldbus) bridge across multiple architectural layers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Documentation Analogy — The Building's Blueprint Vault:**
Imagine a 50-storey smart office tower. Without the as-built electrical wiring diagrams, plumbing schematics, HVAC zoning plans, and lift-control logic documents, no electrician or technician could service the building without trial-and-error. Industrial networks are *exactly* this — they are the nervous system of a plant, and documentation is the *Master Blueprint Vault*. Every cable, every IP address, every slave device, every Profibus GSD file version must be recorded.

**Global System Architecture Analogy — The City Administration Hierarchy:**
Think of a smart city:
- **Level 0:** Traffic lights and road sensors (sensors/actuators on the field).
- **Level 1:** Local traffic control boxes at each junction (PLCs, RTUs).
- **Level 2:** City traffic control rooms (SCADA HMI).
- **Level 3:** Central Operations Room coordinating the whole city (Manufacturing Execution System - MES).
- **Level 4:** Mayor's Office, finance, and city-planning (ERP, Business systems).

The **Global System Architecture** is the design that decides *who talks to whom*, *in which language*, and *with what rules*. A traffic light cannot directly email the Mayor — it must go through the local controller, the control room, and the operations center. Similarly, in industrial plants, data flows *up* the hierarchy and control commands flow *down* the hierarchy, with strict protocol translation at each boundary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Card:** Documentation = the "nervous system's medical chart"; GSA = the "nervous system's anatomy chart." Both are mandatory for any certified industrial plant under the KTU 2024 Scheme's Outcome-Based Education framework.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following standardized identifiers govern global industrial documentation under **IEC 62769** (FDT/DTM), **IEC 62491** (EDDL), and the **GSD/EDD/GSDML** device description standards:

- **IEC 61158** — Fieldbus standard (defines the physical and data-link layers).
- **IEC 61784** — Communication profile families (PROFIBUS, PROFINET, Foundation Fieldbus, DeviceNet, EtherNet/IP).
- **ISA-95 / IEC 62264** — Enterprise-Control System Integration standard.
- **IEC 62443** — Industrial cybersecurity standard (tied to documentation controls).

A typical plant-scale industrial network must document **at least 12 to 15 mandatory artifacts**, ranging from cable schedules and IP address plans to cybersecurity zone/conduit diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Documentation

Industrial network documentation is decomposed into **six distinct layers**, each addressing a specific stakeholder:

1. **Project Documentation (Pre-Construction)**
   - Functional Design Specification (FDS).
   - Network architecture diagrams (logical + physical).
   - Bill of Materials (BoM).
