  - **CRC-8:** $x^8 + x^2 + x + 1$
  - **CRC-16 (CCITT):** $x^{16} + x^{12} + x^5 + 1$
  - **CRC-32 (Ethernet):** $x^{32} + x^{26} + x^{23} + \ldots + x + 1$

### Step 3 — **Error Correction**
- **Hamming Code:** Single-bit error correction code using parity bits at positions $2^i$.
- **Burst Error Correction:** Implemented through interleaving.

### Step 4 — **Flow Control**
Two major categories:
- **Stop-and-Wait:** Sender transmits one frame, waits for ACK before sending next.
- **Sliding Window:** Sender can transmit `W` frames before needing ACK.
  - **Go-Back-N ARQ:** Receiver discards erroneous frames and all subsequent ones.
  - **Selective Repeat ARQ:** Receiver buffers correct frames; retransmits only erroneous ones.

### Step 5 — **Medium Access Control (MAC)**
- **Static Channelization:** TDMA, FDMA, CDMA (deterministic, used in industrial).
- **Dynamic Multiple Access:**
  - **ALOHA / Slotted ALOHA**
  - **CSMA / CSMA-CD (Ethernet)**
  - **CSMA-CA (Wireless, CAN, Industrial Wi-Fi)**
  - **Token Passing (Token Ring, Token Bus)**
  - **Polling (Master-Slave) — typical in Profibus, Modbus**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Data Link Layer — Save This Table**

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Notation | Boundary / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Parity Bit Count** | $n = 2^r - 1$ total bits | $r$ = parity bits | Single-bit errors detectable |
| **Hamming Code Distance** | $d_{\min} \ge 2t + 1$ for correction | $t$ = correctable errors | $d_{\min} \ge s + 1$ for $s$-error detection |
| **CRC Generator Polynomial** | $G(x)$ of degree $r$ | $r$ = CRC bits appended | Hamming distance $\ge 4$ |
| **Stop-and-Wait Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{T_f}{T_f + 2T_p}$ | $T_f$ = frame time, $T_p$ = prop. delay | $U \rightarrow 1$ when $T_f \gg T_p$ |
| **Sliding Window Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{W \cdot T_f}{T_f + 2T_p}$ if $W \ge 2a+1$ | $a = T_p / T_f$ | Else $U = W \cdot T_f / (T_f + 2T_p)$ |
| **Pure ALOHA Throughput** | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | Max $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ | at $G = 0.5$ |
| **Slotted ALOHA Throughput** | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | Max $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ | at $G = 1$ |
| **CSMA-CD Throughput** | $S = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a}$ for 1-persistent | $a = T_p / T_f$ | Ethernet 10BASE5 |
| **Token Holding Time (Profibus)** | $T_{TH} \le T_{TR}$ | $T_{TR}$ = Token Rotation Time | Deterministic for real-time |
| **Bit Stuffing Overhead** | Overhead $\approx \dfrac{N_b}{N_b + N_{stuffed}}$ | $N_{stuffed} = \lfloor N_b / 5 \rfloor$ | HDLC worst case $\approx 20\%$ |
| **Ethernet Min Frame Size** | $64$ bytes $= 512$ bits | Collision detection constraint | 10 Mbps: $51.2 \ \mu s$ slot time |
| **Bit Rate** | $R_b = N_b / T$ | $N_b$ = bits transmitted | bits per second (bps) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** When working KTU problems on sliding window protocols, ALWAYS first compute $a = T_p / T_f$, then determine $W_{opt}$ using the formula and compare with given $W$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (CAN Bus):** DLL provides deterministic, priority-based arbitration using 11-bit or 29-bit identifiers; uses **CRC-15** for error detection.
- **Factory Automation (Profibus DP):** Token-passing MAC with token rotation time bounded for deterministic real-time response.
- **Process Control (Foundation Fieldbus):** Uses DLL scheduling called **LAS (Link Active Scheduler)** for deterministic bus access.
- **Power Systems (IEC 61850 / GOOSE):** Direct DLL layer multicast with time-critical delivery, no Network Layer routing.

## 2.4 HDLC Frame Structure (Foundational Industrial DLL Protocol)

```
| Flag (8) | Address (8/16) | Control (8) | Information (n) | FCS (16/32) | Flag (8) |
   01111110    Extended/       N(S),N(R),     Variable        CRC            01111110
              Normal           Poll/Final       Length
```

- **Flag:** Unique bit pattern `01111110` with bit stuffing for transparency.
- **Address:** 8 bits (extended: 16 bits) for point-to-multipoint.
- **Control:** Frame type (I-frame, S-frame, U-frame).
- **FCS:** Frame Check Sequence using CRC-16 or CRC-32.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: CRC Computation

**Problem Statement:** Given a message $M(x) = x^7 + x^5 + x^2 + 1$ (i.e., bits `10100101`) and generator polynomial $G(x) = x^4 + x^3 + 1$ (i.e., `11001`), compute the transmitted codeword $T(x)$.

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Encode Message into Polynomial Form**

The message bits `10100101` correspond to:

$$M(x) = x^7 + x^5 + x^2 + x^0$$

$$M(x) = 1\cdot x^7 + 0\cdot x^6 + 1\cdot x^5 + 0\cdot x^4 + 0\cdot x^3 + 1\cdot x^2 + 0\cdot x^1 + 1\cdot x^0$$

The generator polynomial $G(x) = x^4 + x^3 + 1$ has degree $r = 4$.

**Step 2 — Append r Zero Bits**

The degree of $G(x)$ is $r = 4$, so we append $4$ zeros to $M(x)$:

$$M'(x) = x^4 \cdot M(x) = x^{11} + x^9 + x^6 + x^4$$

In binary: `10100101` followed by `0000` $\rightarrow$ `101001010000`

**Step 3 — Perform Modulo-2 Division (XOR-based)**

We compute $M'(x) \div G(x)$ using XOR (no carry) arithmetic:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dividend} &: 101001010000 \\
\text{Divisor}  &: 11001 \quad (\text{degree} = 4) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Iteration 1 (leading 1 in dividend matches leading 1 in divisor):

$$
\begin{aligned}
101001010000 \;\; \text{XOR} \;\; 110010000000 &= 011011010000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Continue iterating through the remaining quotient bits. After the complete division, the **remainder** of degree at most $r - 1 = 3$ is obtained.

For this specific case, after exhaustive XOR division, we obtain the **4-bit remainder**:

$$R(x) = 1000 \quad (\text{i.e., bits } x^3)$$

**Step 4 — Form the Transmitted Codeword**

$$T(x) = M'(x) + R(x) = x^{11} + x^9 + x^6 + x^4 + x^3$$

The transmitted bits are `101001011000`.

**Step 5 — Receiver Verification**

The receiver divides $T(x)$ by $G(x)$. If the remainder is **zero**, the frame is assumed error-free; otherwise, an error is detected.

$$
\frac{T(x)}{G(x)} = \frac{x^{11} + x^9 + x^6 + x^4 + x^3}{x^4 + x^3 + 1} = Q(x) + 0
$$

**General CRC Equation:**

$$T(x) = x^r \cdot M(x) + R(x), \quad \text{where} \quad R(x) = [x^r \cdot M(x)] \bmod G(x)$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Hamming Code Generation

**Problem Statement:** Encode the dataword `1010` (4 bits) into a Hamming Code with the minimum number of parity bits.

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Determine Number of Parity Bits**

Using the Hamming inequality:

$$2^r \ge m + r + 1$$

Substituting $m = 4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^r &\ge 4 + r + 1 = 5 + r \\
\text{Try } r = 3 &: 2^3 = 8 \ge 5 + 3 = 8 \quad \checkmark \\
\end{aligned}
$$

So we need $r = 3$ parity bits. Total transmitted codeword length: $n = 4 + 3 = 7$ bits.

**Step 2 — Place Parity Bits at Positions $2^i$**

Positions are numbered from **1 to 7**. Parity bits go to positions $1, 2, 4$ (i.e., $2^0, 2^1, 2^2$). Data bits go to remaining positions $3, 5, 6, 7$.

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | P1 | P2 | D1 | P4 | D2 | D3 | D4 |
| **Value** | ? | ? | 1 | ? | 0 | 1 | 0 |

**Step 3 — Compute Parity Bits Using Even Parity**

- **P1** checks positions with bit 0 set in position index: $\{1, 3, 5, 7\}$. Currently: $\{1, 1, 0, 0\}$ $\rightarrow$ sum $= 2$ (even) $\rightarrow P1 = 0$.
- **P2** checks positions with bit 1 set: $\{2, 3, 6, 7\}$. Currently: $\{?, 1, 1, 0\}$ $\rightarrow$ need even total $\rightarrow P2 = 0$.
- **P4** checks positions with bit 2 set: $\{4, 5, 6, 7\}$. Currently: $\{?, 0, 1, 0\}$ $\rightarrow$ need even total $\rightarrow P4 = 1$.

**Step 4 — Final Codeword**

| Position | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Value** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

$$\text{Transmitted Codeword} = 0011010$$

**Step 5 — Error Detection/Correction Example**

Suppose position 6 flips due to noise: received `0011110`. Recompute parities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= P_1 \oplus D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus D_4 = 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
S_2 &= P_2 \oplus D_1 \oplus D_3 \oplus D_4 = 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 = 0 \\
S_4 &= P_4 \oplus D_2 \oplus D_3 \oplus D_4 = 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 = 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Syndrome word $(S_4 S_2 S_1) = (001)_2 = 1 \rightarrow$ **Error in position 6**. Flip bit 6 to correct.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Stop-and-Wait Protocol Efficiency

**Problem Statement:** A 10 Mbps industrial channel has a one-way propagation delay of $T_p = 25 \ \mu s$. Frame length $= 1500$ bytes. Determine channel utilization.

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Compute Frame Transmission Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 1500 \text{ bytes} = 1500 \times 8 = 12000 \text{ bits} \\
T_f &= \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12000 \text{ bits}}{10 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\
T_f &= 1200 \ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Parameter $a$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{T_p}{T_f} = \frac{25 \ \mu s}{1200 \ \mu s} = 0.02083 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Utilization**

For Stop-and-Wait:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{T_f}{T_f + 2T_p} = \frac{1200}{1200 + 2(25)} = \frac{1200}{1250} \\
U &= 0.96 = 96\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** With low propagation delay relative to frame size, Stop-and-Wait achieves **96% utilization**, acceptable for slow industrial links but inadequate for satellite/very long links where $a \gg 1$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of DLL Protocols

| Feature | Stop-and-Wait | Go-Back-N | Selective Repeat | Token Ring | CSMA-CD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Efficiency** | Low ($1/(1+2a)$) | Medium | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Determinism** | N/A | N/A | N/A | **High** (Industrial) | Low |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | High | Medium | Low |
| **Industrial Use** | Simple sensor links | Legacy X.25 | Modern industrial | Token Bus (802.4) | Ethernet/IP |
| **Error Recovery** | Retransmit frame | Retransmit all from $N(R)$ | Retransmit only bad | Master reissues | CSMA-CD retry |
| **Buffer at Rx** | 1 frame | 1 frame | $W$ frames | 1 frame | 1 frame |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Data Link Layer Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Layer Packet] --> B[Logical Link Control Sub-Layer LLC]
    B --> C[Framing Module]
    C --> D[Error Detection CRC Encoder]
    D --> E[MAC Sub-Layer]
    E --> F[Addressing Module]
    F --> G[MAC Access Control Token CSMA CD]
    G --> H[Physical Layer TX]
    H --> I[Transmission Medium]
    I --> J[Physical Layer RX]
    J --> K[MAC Sub-Layer RX]
    K --> L[Frame Validation]
    L --> M[CRC Decoder]
    M --> N{Error Detected?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Discard Frame or Request Retransmission]
    N -- No --> P[Deframing Module]
    P --> Q[Flow Control Verification]
    Q --> R[Deliver to Network Layer]
```

## 4.2 Sliding Window Protocol Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sender Window Size W] --> B[Send Frame 1]
    B --> C[Send Frame 2]
    C --> D[Send Frame 3]
    D --> E{Ack Received?}
    E -- Yes, ACK 1 --> F[Slide Window Forward]
    E -- No --> G[Timer Expires]
    G --> H[Retransmit from Last Unacknowledged]
    F --> I[Send Next Frame]
    H --> B
    I --> J[Receiver Buffers Out of Order Frames]
    J --> K{All Frames Sequential?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Deliver to Network Layer]
    K -- No --> M[Wait for Missing Frame]
    M --> J
```

## 4.3 HDLC Frame Transmission with Bit Stuffing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Stream] --> B{Scan 5 Consecutive 1s?}
    B -- No --> C[Output Bit Unchanged]
    B -- Yes --> D[Insert Stuffed 0 Bit]
    C --> E[Continue Scanning]
    D --> E
    E --> F{End of Frame?}
    F -- No --> B
    F -- Yes --> G[Append Flag 01111110]
    G --> H[Transmit Frame]
    H --> I[Receiver Removes Stuffed 0s]
    I --> J[Original Data Restored]
```

## 4.4 Industrial Network DLL Protocol Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROFINET[PROFINET Industrial Ethernet]
        PE1[Application Layer]
        PE2[TCP UDP IP Layer]
        PE3[Data Link Layer Ethernet II IEEE 802.3]
        PE4[Physical Layer 100 Mbps Copper Fiber]
    end

    subgraph PROFIBUS[PROFIBUS DP Fieldbus]
        PB1[Application Layer DPVO DPV1]
        PB2[Data Link Layer FDL IEC 61158]
        PB3[Physical Layer RS485]
    end

    subgraph CANBUS[CAN Bus Automotive Industrial]
        CB1[Application Layer CANopen DeviceNet]
        CB2[Data Link Layer CAN ISO 11898]
        CB3[Physical Layer Twisted Pair Differential]
    end

    PROFINET --> EthernetProtocol[Common DLL Convergence]
    PROFIBUS --> EthernetProtocol
    CANBUS --> EthernetProtocol
    EthernetProtocol --> IndustrialMaster[Single Industrial Network Backbone]
```

## 4.5 Error Detection and Recovery Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Frame Received]) --> Validate[Compute CRC on Received Frame]
    Validate --> Compare{CRC Matches Expected FCS?}
    Compare -- Yes, Match --> Accept[Accept Frame Deliver Up]
    Compare -- No, Mismatch --> Reject[Discard Frame]
    Reject --> Notify[Send NAK or No ACK]
    Notify --> Wait{Timeout in Sender?}
    Wait -- Yes --> Retransmit[Retransmit Frame]
    Wait -- No --> Notify
    Retransmit --> Wait
    Accept --> End([Processing Complete])
    Retransmit --> End
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Data Link Layer Functions
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

*Briefly explain the main functions of the Data Link Layer in an industrial communication network. Mention any **three** key responsibilities.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Data Link Layer (Layer 2 of OSI) performs the following key functions in industrial networks:

1. **Framing (1 mark):** It segments the bit stream from the Physical Layer into discrete **frames**, each consisting of a header (with addresses and control info), payload (network layer data), and trailer (containing FCS).

2. **Error Detection and Correction (1 mark):** It implements techniques such as **CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check)**, parity checks, and Hamming codes to detect bit errors caused by electromagnetic interference (EMI) common in factory floors. Industrial protocols like CAN use **CRC-15**; Ethernet uses **CRC-32**.

3. **Medium Access Control (MAC) (1 mark):** Governs how multiple industrial devices (PLCs, sensors, actuators) share a common bus. Techniques include **Token Passing** (deterministic, used in Profibus) and **CSMA-CD** (used in industrial Ethernet).

### Question 2: HDLC Frame Components
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

*List the fields of an **HDLC frame** and explain the purpose of the **Flag field**.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An HDLC frame consists of the following fields:

| Flag (8 bits) | Address (8 bits) | Control (8 bits) | Information (n bits) | FCS (16/32 bits) | Flag (8 bits) |

1. **Purpose of Flag Field (2 marks):** The flag is a unique bit pattern **`01111110`** used to mark the beginning and end of every frame. To ensure the data does not accidentally contain the flag pattern, **bit stuffing** is applied: after every **five consecutive `1`s** in the data stream, a `0` bit is automatically inserted. The receiver removes these stuffed zeros to recover the original data, ensuring frame transparency.
2. **Frame Role (1 mark):** Both opening and closing flags enable the receiver to detect frame boundaries on the bit stream without ambiguity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **sliding window protocol** with respect to flow control in industrial data link layers. Compare **Go-Back-N ARQ** and **Selective Repeat ARQ** in detail with diagrams. Discuss their relative advantages in industrial automation networks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1 Mbps industrial satellite link has a one-way propagation delay of $T_p = 270$ ms. Compute the **channel utilization** of the system if:
- Frame size = **256 bits**
- Stop-and-Wait protocol is used
- Sliding Window with $W = 8$ is used.

Also, recommend which protocol is more suitable for this satellite link and justify. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) Solution — Sliding Window Protocol

**Sliding Window Protocol (4 marks):**

The sliding window protocol is a **flow control** mechanism used at the Data Link Layer where the sender can transmit **multiple frames (window size W)** before receiving an acknowledgment. The receiver maintains a window of acceptable sequence numbers.

- **Send Window:** Defines the range of sequence numbers the sender may transmit.
- **Receive Window:** Defines the range of acceptable sequence numbers the receiver will accept.
- Each acknowledged frame allows the window to **slide forward**, enabling continuous transmission.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Go-Back-N ARQ | Selective Repeat ARQ |
|---|---|---|
| **Buffering at Receiver** | Only 1 frame | W frames (out-of-order accepted) |
| **Action on Error** | Discard erroneous frame AND all subsequent frames | Discard only erroneous frame; buffer correct subsequent frames |
| **Retransmission** | All frames from the errored one onward | Only the specific errored frame(s) |
| **Complexity** | Lower (Sender side) | Higher (Both sender and receiver) |
| **Bandwidth Efficiency** | Lower for high-error links | Higher for high-error links |
| **Industrial Use Case** | Older X.25 industrial links, simple PLC networks | Modern industrial Ethernet with TCP/IP, Profinet |

**Industrial Recommendation:**
- **Go-Back-N** is suitable for **low-noise, short-distance** industrial links where error rates are minimal.
- **Selective Repeat** is preferred for **noisy factory environments** with EMI and longer cable runs, where bandwidth efficiency is critical.

---

#### Part (b) Solution — Satellite Link Analysis

**Given:**
- Bit rate $R = 1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps
- Propagation delay $T_p = 270$ ms $= 0.27$ s
- Frame size $L = 256$ bits

**Step 1 — Compute Frame Transmission Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= \frac{L}{R} = \frac{256 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bps}} = 256 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
T_f &= 256 \ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Parameter a:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{T_p}{T_f} = \frac{270 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}{256 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} = 1054.69 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Stop-and-Wait Utilization:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{S\&W} &= \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 2(1054.69)} = \frac{1}{2109.38} \\
U_{S\&W} &\approx 4.74 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0474\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sliding Window Utilization:**

Since $W = 8$ and $W < 2a + 1 = 2109.38 + 1$, the window is smaller than the bandwidth-delay product. The utilization formula for this case:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{SW} &= \frac{W \cdot T_f}{T_f + 2T_p} = \frac{8 \times 256 \times 10^{-6}}{256 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times 270 \times 10^{-3}} \\
U_{SW} &= \frac{2048 \times 10^{-6}}{540.256 \times 10^{-3}} \\
U_{SW} &= \frac{2.048 \times 10^{-3}}{540.256 \times 10^{-3}} \\
U_{SW} &\approx 0.00379 = 0.379\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**

| Protocol | Channel Utilization |
|---|---|
| Stop-and-Wait | $\approx 0.0474\%$ |
| Sliding Window (W=8) | $\approx 0.379\%$ |

Both protocols give **very low utilization** due to the extremely large propagation delay. To utilize the link effectively, we need $W \ge 2a + 1 \approx 2110$. The given window of $W = 8$ is grossly insufficient for this satellite link.

**Recommendation:** For this satellite scenario, use a **Sliding Window with $W \ge 2110$** to fully utilize the link bandwidth. Stop-and-Wait is **completely unsuitable** here.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative 2

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper / Supplementary Exam Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC)** algorithm with a suitable example. Compute the transmitted codeword for a message `1101011011` using generator polynomial $G(x) = x^4 + x + 1$. Show all intermediate division steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **CSMA-CD protocol** as used in industrial Ethernet. Compute the **maximum throughput** for a 1-persistent CSMA-CD network with $a = 0.1$. Discuss how this protocol differs from the **Token Passing** approach used in Profibus in terms of determinism. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) Solution — CRC Computation

**Step 1 — Represent Message and Generator Polynomial:**

Message $M = 1101011011$ (10 bits), Generator $G(x) = x^4 + x + 1$, which corresponds to bit pattern `10011` (degree $r = 4$).

**Step 2 — Append r Zero Bits:**

$$
M' = 11010110110000 \quad (\text{shifted left by 4 bits})
$$

**Step 3 — Modulo-2 Division:**

Divide `11010110110000` by `10011` using XOR:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 1: } & 11010110110000 \oplus 10011000000000 = 01001110110000 \\
\text{Iteration 2: } & 1001110110000 \oplus 1001100000000 = 0000010110000 \\
\text{Iteration 3: } & 10110000 \oplus 10011000 = 00101000 \\
\text{Iteration 4: } & 101000 \oplus 10011 = 001111 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

After all iterations, the **remainder** is `0111` (4 bits).

**Step 4 — Transmitted Codeword:**

$$
T = 11010110110111
$$

**Verification at Receiver:**
Divide `11010110110111` by `10011` $\rightarrow$ remainder = `0000` (zero), confirming correct transmission.

#### Part (b) Solution — CSMA-CD and Token Passing

**CSMA-CD Operation (3 marks):**

**Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection** is used in industrial Ethernet (IEEE 802.3):

1. A station wanting to transmit **first listens** to the medium (Carrier Sense).
2. If the medium is idle, the station begins transmission immediately (1-persistent).
3. While transmitting, the station **monitors for collisions** (Collision Detection).
4. If a collision is detected, all transmitting stations **stop, send a jam signal**, wait a random backoff time (Binary Exponential Backoff), and retry.

**Throughput Calculation (2 marks):**

For 1-persistent CSMA-CD, the throughput is approximated by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a} \\
\text{Given } a &= 0.1: \\
S &= \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.1} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.644} = \frac{1}{1.644} \\
S &\approx 0.608 = 60.8\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with Token Passing (2 marks):**

| Property | CSMA-CD | Token Passing (Profibus) |
|---|---|---|
| **Determinism** | Non-deterministic; worst-case delay unbounded | Deterministic; bounded by Token Rotation Time $T_{TR}$ |
| **Worst-Case Delay** | Unpredictable (collision backoff) | $T_{TR} \le$ specified maximum |
| **Real-Time Suitability** | Limited (suitable for non-critical monitoring) | Excellent (suitable for closed-loop control) |
| **Collision Risk** | Yes (mitigated by CSMA-CD) | None (token ensures exclusive access) |
| **Industrial Application** | EtherNet/IP (best-effort class) | Profibus DP, Foundation Fieldbus |

**Conclusion:** **Token Passing (Profibus)** is preferred for **hard real-time industrial control** because it guarantees bounded latency, while CSMA-CD is acceptable for **soft real-time monitoring** and supervisory traffic.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **In CRC Problems:** Students often forget to **append zeros before division** — this is worth 1 mark. Also, do NOT use binary subtraction; CRC uses **XOR (modulo-2)** exclusively.
> 2. **In Hamming Code Problems:** Always show the **syndrome computation** step explicitly. Writing only "error corrected" without showing $(S_4, S_2, S_1)$ loses 2 marks.
> 3. **In Sliding Window Utilization:** First compare $W$ with $2a + 1$ before applying the formula — incorrect formula selection is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **In Throughput Problems:** For Pure ALOHA, the formula is $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$, NOT $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$. Confusing Pure vs Slotted ALOHA loses 2 marks.
> 5. **In HDLC Framing:** Forgetting to mention **bit stuffing** when describing flag transparency costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Data Link Layer (PEICT747 Module 3)**

- **Position in OSI:** Layer 2, divided into **LLC (802.2)** and **MAC** sub-layers.
- **Three core tasks:** **Framing, Error Control, Flow Control** + **MAC** + **Addressing (48-bit MAC)**.
- **Framing methods:** Character count, byte stuffing, **bit stuffing (HDLC)**, coding violations.
- **CRC polynomial degree = number of FCS bits.** Receiver verifies by computing remainder; non-zero = error.
- **Hamming Code:** Use $2^r \ge m + r + 1$ to find parity bits. Parity bits at positions $2^i$.
- **Stop-and-Wait:** $U = 1/(1+2a)$; simple but inefficient for high-$a$ links (satellites).
- **Sliding Window:** $U = \min(1, W/(1+2a))$; throughput scales with $W$ until $W \ge 2a + 1$.
- **Go-Back-N:** Receiver buffer = 1 frame; retransmits **all** from errored frame onward.
- **Selective Repeat:** Receiver buffers $W$ frames; retransmits only the **errored** ones.
- **Pure ALOHA:** $S_{max} = 1/(2e) \approx 18.4\%$; **Slotted ALOHA:** $S_{max} = 1/e \approx 36.8\%$.
- **CSMA-CD:** Used in IEEE 802.3 industrial Ethernet; throughput drops as $a$ increases.
- **Token Ring (802.5) / Token Bus (802.4):** Deterministic MAC; basis for **Profibus DP** and **ARCNET**.
- **Industrial DLL Standards:** HDLC (ISO 3309), CAN (ISO 11898), Profibus FDL (IEC 61158), Modbus RTU.
- **HDLC Frame:** Flag + Address + Control + Information + FCS + Flag; bit stuffing ensures transparency.
- **Ethernet Min Frame:** 64 bytes; ensures collision detection within slot time.
- **Real-time industrial:** Prefer **deterministic** MAC (Token, TDMA) over **stochastic** (CSMA).
- **Examination Mantra:** Always state assumptions, draw diagrams for sliding window/HDLC, and **show numerical substitution** in every formula step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Layer
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME
- **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
- **Module:** Module 3
- **Topic:** Application Layer

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application Layer (Layer 7)** in the context of **Industrial Networks** is the topmost layer of both the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model** and the **TCP/IP Protocol Suite**. It serves as the **direct interface between the end-user industrial application software** (SCADA, HMI, MES, ERP systems) and the underlying communication stack. In industrial automation, this layer hosts **Industrial Communication Protocols** such as **Modbus TCP, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA, DNP3, and IEC 60870-5-104**, which define the syntax, semantics, and synchronization of industrial messages exchanged between controllers, sensors, actuators, and supervisory systems.

The Application Layer abstracts the complexity of the lower six layers (Physical → Data Link → Network → Transport → Session → Presentation) and exposes standardized **services (Service Access Points – SAPs)** to the application processes. It is governed by standards such as **ISO/IEC 7498-1, IEC 61784, and IEC 61158**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Postal Letter Analogy":** Think of industrial communication like a registered postal mail system. The **Physical Layer** is the truck that physically carries the letter. The **Data Link Layer** is the local post office that ensures the letter isn't torn or duplicated. The **Network Layer** is the addressing system (PIN code) that routes it across cities. The **Transport Layer** is the tracking number that confirms delivery. The **Application Layer**, however, is the **actual readable content inside the envelope** — the industrial command ("Open Valve 3 by 45%") or the sensor reading ("Temperature = 87.4 °C"). Without the Application Layer, the letter arrives but nobody understands what to do with it!

In a **factory floor**, an HMI (Human-Machine Interface) sending "START MOTOR M1" to a PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) is invoking an **Application Layer Service**. The industrial protocol determines *what bytes mean "START"*, *how addresses are encoded*, and *how errors are reported*.

### 1.3 Core Industrial Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Industrial Protocol Port Numbers (IANA Registered):**
  - **Modbus TCP:** Port **502**
  - **EtherNet/IP (CIP):** Port **44818** (TCP) and **2222** (UDP)
  - **PROFINET (DCP/RPC):** Port **34964** (UDP), **34962/34963** (TCP)
  - **OPC UA:** Port **4840**
  - **DNP3:** Port **20000**
  - **IEC 60870-5-104:** Port **2404**
- **OSI Model Layers:** **7** (Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical)
- **TCP/IP Model Layers:** **4** (Application, Transport, Internet, Link) — note TCP/IP *folds* Presentation & Session into the Application Layer.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Operational Breakdown

The Application Layer in industrial networks performs **four core operational functions**:

1. **Service Definition (What can be asked?)**
   - Defines a finite set of services such as **Read, Write, Read/Write Multiple, Report-by-Exception, Event Notification, File Transfer, Time Synchronization**.
   - Example: Modbus defines Function Codes `01` (Read Coils), `03` (Read Holding Registers), `06` (Write Single Register).

2. **Message Syntax (How is it encoded?)**
   - Defines the **byte-level encoding** of requests and responses. Includes fields like Transaction Identifier, Protocol Identifier, Length, Unit ID, Function Code, Data, and CRC/Error Check.
   - Example: Modbus TCP uses an **MBAP (Modbus Application Protocol) Header** of **7 bytes**.

3. **Semantics (What does it mean?)**
   - Specifies the meaning of each field and the rules of state transitions (e.g., what happens when a slave receives an illegal function code? It returns Exception Code `01` — Illegal Function).

4. **Synchronization & State Management (When and how often?)**
   - Handles polling intervals, session establishment, and connection lifecycle in client-server or producer-consumer models.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Layer Function

- **Why an Application Layer?** Lower layers provide *reliable byte delivery*, but industrial automation needs *meaningful domain objects* (tags, alarms, recipes, setpoints). The Application Layer maps raw bytes to these domain objects.
- **How does it work?** Industrial protocols typically follow either:
  - **Client-Server model** (Modbus TCP, OPC UA) — a master/client polls a server/slave.
  - **Producer-Consumer model** (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP) — devices publish data periodically; consumers subscribe.
  - **Publisher-Subscriber model** (OPC UA Pub/Sub, MQTT-SN) — used in IIoT and cloud-edge integration.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations & Metrics for the Application Layer**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| Modbus TCP MBAP Header Size | $H_{MBAP} = 7$ | Bytes |
| Modbus TCP PDU Size | $L_{PDU} = 253$ | Bytes (max data payload) |
| Modbus TCP ADU Size | $L_{ADU} = H_{MBAP} + L_{PDU} = 260$ | Bytes (max) |
| Modbus RTU Frame Size (excluding CRC) | $L_{RTU} = \text{Address} + \text{Function Code} + \text{Data}$ | Bytes |
| CRC-16 Calculation (Modbus RTU) | $\text{Init} = \texttt{0xFFFF}$; Polynomial $= \texttt{0xA001}$ | 16-bit |
| Transaction Throughput | $T = \dfrac{N_{req}}{T_{cycle}}$ | Requests/sec |
| Round Trip Time (RTT) | $RTT = t_{prop} + t_{proc} + t_{queue}$ | seconds (s) or ms |
| Effective Bandwidth Utilization | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload} + L_{overhead}} \times 100\%$ | Percent (%) |
| OPC UA Endpoint URL | $URL = \texttt{opc.tcp://host:port/server}$ | String |
| PROFINET Real-Time Cycle | $t_{RT} \in [1, 2, 4, 8, 16, \dots, 512] \text{ ms}$ | ms |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Automation:** Refineries use **OPC UA** for secure, vendor-independent data exchange between DCS (Distributed Control Systems) and MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems).
- **Discrete Manufacturing:** Automotive assembly lines use **PROFINET IRT** (Isochronous Real-Time) for synchronized robot motion control down to **microsecond** jitter ($\mu s$).
- **Power Systems:** **IEC 61850** (which sits atop the Application Layer) enables substation automation and peer-to-peer GOOSE messaging within **4 ms** critical trip times.
- **IIoT Integration:** **MQTT** (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) running over TCP port **1883** (or **8883** for TLS) bridges PLCs to cloud platforms like AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, and Google Cloud IoT.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Modbus TCP Application Data Unit (ADU) Construction

**Problem:** A client sends a Modbus TCP request to read Holding Registers `40001` to `40010` (10 registers) from a server with Unit ID `1`. Construct the complete ADU.

**Step 1 — Identify the Modbus TCP ADU structure.**
A Modbus TCP ADU consists of:
$$
ADU = \underbrace{\text{MBAP Header}}_{7 \text{ bytes}} + \underbrace{\text{Function Code}}_{1 \text{ byte}} + \underbrace{\text{Data}}{n \text{ bytes}}
$$

**Step 2 — Construct the MBAP Header (7 bytes).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Transaction ID} &= \texttt{0x0001} \quad (2 \text{ bytes, identifies the transaction uniquely}) \\
\text{Protocol ID} &= \texttt{0x0000} \quad (2 \text{ bytes, } 0 = \text{Modbus protocol}) \\
\text{Length} &= \texttt{0x0006} \quad (2 \text{ bytes, count of remaining bytes: Unit ID + FC + Data}) \\
\text{Unit ID} &= \texttt{0x01} \quad (1 \text{ byte, identifies the remote slave})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine Length field value (exhaustive byte counting).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Length} &= \underbrace{1}_{\text{Unit ID}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{Func Code}} + \underbrace{4}_{\text{Data bytes}} \\
&= 6 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Select the Function Code.**
For reading Holding Registers, use Function Code:

$$
FC = \texttt{0x03} \quad \text{(Read Holding Registers)}
$$

**Step 5 — Construct the Data field (4 bytes).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Starting Address} &= 40001 - 40001 = 0 = \texttt{0x0000} \quad (2 \text{ bytes, 0-based offset}) \\
\text{Quantity of Registers} &= 10 = \texttt{0x000A} \quad (2 \text{ bytes})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the final ADU (hexadecimal byte stream).**

$$
ADU = \underbrace{\texttt{00 01}}_{\text{TID}} \; \underbrace{\texttt{00 00}}_{\text{PID}} \; \underbrace{\texttt{00 06}}_{\text{Len}} \; \underbrace{\texttt{01}}_{\text{UID}} \; \underbrace{\texttt{03}}_{\text{FC}} \; \underbrace{\texttt{00 00 \; 00 0A}}_{\text{Data}}
$$

**Total ADU length:** $7 + 1 + 4 = 12$ bytes.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Theoretical Transaction Rate for Modbus TCP

**Problem:** A SCADA master polls 100 RTUs (Remote Terminal Units), each holding 20 registers. The network has a Round Trip Time of $RTT = 10 \text{ ms}$. Each request/response uses 2 TCP frames. Calculate the maximum sustainable transaction rate.

**Step 1 — Model the timing.**
Each poll requires:
- 1 TCP request frame (downlink)
- 1 TCP response frame (uplink)
- Total time per transaction $\approx 1 \times RTT$

$$
t_{txn} = 1 \times RTT = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute transactions per second per RTU.**

$$
T_{RTU} = \dfrac{1}{t_{txn}} = \dfrac{1}{10 \times 10^{-3}} = 100 \text{ transactions/sec per RTU}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute aggregate for 100 RTUs.**

$$
T_{agg} = N_{RTU} \times T_{RTU} = 100 \times 100 = 10{,}000 \text{ transactions/sec}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective application-layer throughput (in registers/sec).**

$$
T_{regs} = T_{agg} \times N_{regs/txn} = 10{,}000 \times 20 = 200{,}000 \text{ registers/sec}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Application Layer Bandwidth Efficiency

**Problem:** A Modbus TCP read of 125 registers has a payload of $L_{payload} = 250$ bytes. Ethernet headers + IP + TCP + MBAP add an overhead of $L_{overhead} = 78$ bytes. Calculate efficiency $\eta$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= L_{payload} + L_{overhead} = 250 + 78 = 328 \text{ bytes} \\
\eta &= \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{total}} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{250}{328} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 76.22\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** For small Modbus packets, efficiency drops sharply. A single-register read ($L_{payload} = 2$ bytes) yields $\eta \approx 2.5\%$ — this is why **batched multi-register reads** are always preferred in industrial applications.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Industrial Application Layer Protocols

| Protocol | Transport | Port | Model | Typical Cycle | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Modbus TCP** | TCP | 502 | Client-Server | 100–1000 ms | Generic, Legacy |
| **PROFINET RT** | UDP/RTC | 34964 | Producer-Consumer | 1–512 ms | Discrete Mfg. |
| **PROFINET IRT** | Ethernet HW | — | Scheduled | 250 µs – 4 ms | Motion Control |
| **EtherNet/IP** | TCP/UDP | 44818 / 2222 | Producer-Consumer | 1–100 ms | Discrete Mfg. |
| **OPC UA** | TCP | 4840 | Client-Server / Pub-Sub | 10 ms – 1 s | IIoT, MES/ERP |
| **DNP3** | TCP | 20000 | Master-Slave | 100 ms – 1 s | Power, Water |
| **IEC 61850** | TCP/UDP | 102 | Client-Server / GOOSE | 3–4 ms (GOOSE) | Substations |
| **MQTT** | TCP | 1883/8883 | Pub-Sub | Variable | IIoT, Cloud |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 OSI Model Highlighting the Application Layer

```mermaid
graph TD
    L7["Layer 7 Application - Industrial Protocols Modbus PROFINET OPC UA"] --> L6["Layer 6 Presentation - Encoding Encryption Compression"]
    L6 --> L5["Layer 5 Session - Dialog Control Synchronization"]
    L5 --> L4["Layer 4 Transport - TCP UDP Port Numbers Reliability"]
    L4 --> L3["Layer 3 Network - IP Routing Subnetting"]
    L3 --> L2["Layer 2 Data Link - MAC Ethernet Switches VLAN"]
    L2 --> L1["Layer 1 Physical - Cables Fiber Connectors Voltages"]
```

### 4.2 Modbus TCP Request-Response Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    Master["SCADA Master HMI"] -->|TCP Connect Port 502| Slave1["PLC Slave Unit ID 1"]
    Master -->|TCP Connect Port 502| Slave2["PLC Slave Unit ID 2"]
    Master -->|MBAP Header FC 03 Read Registers| Slave1
    Slave1 -->|MBAP Header FC 03 Response Data| Master
    Slave2 -->|Exception Code 02 Illegal Data Address| Master
```

### 4.3 Industrial Protocol Stack vs TCP/IP Stack Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Industrial["Industrial Protocol Stack"]
        I7["Application - Modbus PROFINET EtherNetIP OPC UA"]
        I6["Presentation - CIP MMS Encoding"]
        I5["Session - COTP RFC 1006"]
        I4["Transport - TCP UDP"]
        I3["Network - IP"]
        I2["Data Link - Ethernet"]
        I1["Physical - 100BASE-TX Fiber"]
    end
    subgraph TCPIP["TCP IP Reference Model"]
        T4["Application - Combines OSI 5 6 7"]
        T3["Transport - TCP UDP"]
        T2["Internet - IP"]
        T1["Link - Ethernet Physical"]
    end
    I7 -.maps to.-> T4
    I4 -.maps to.-> T3
    I3 -.maps to.-> T2
    I1 -.maps to.-> T1
```

### 4.4 OPC UA Information Model — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Client["OPC UA Client HMI SCADA"] -->|Read Write Subscribe| Server["OPC UA Server"]
    Server --> AddressSpace["Address Space - Set of Nodes"]
    AddressSpace --> Objects["Objects Folder - Devices"]
    AddressSpace --> Variables["Variables Folder - Tags Temperature Pressure"]
    AddressSpace --> Methods["Methods Folder - StartMotor OpenValve"]
    AddressSpace --> Types["Types Folder - Base Data Types"]
    Server --> Subscriptions["Subscription Service"]
    Subscriptions --> MonitoredItems["Monitored Items - DataChangeFilter DeadbandValue"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three industrial communication protocols that operate at the Application Layer and state one typical industrial use case for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Modbus TCP (Port 502):** Used in legacy factory automation for reading sensor values (e.g., temperature, pressure) from PLCs — *simple, vendor-neutral, widely deployed.*
2. **OPC UA (Port 4840):** Used for secure, platform-independent data exchange between PLCs and MES/ERP systems in Industry 4.0 — *supports encryption, authentication, and information modeling.*
3. **PROFINET:** Used in automotive assembly lines for high-speed synchronized motion control with cycle times down to **250 µs** (IRT).

*(1 mark each for protocol + use case, 3 marks total)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*What is the function of the MBAP Header in a Modbus TCP frame? List its four fields.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **MBAP (Modbus Application Protocol) Header** is a **7-byte header** prepended to every Modbus TCP message. Its functions are transaction identification, protocol identification, length declaration, and unit addressing.

The four fields are:
1. **Transaction Identifier (2 bytes)** — unique ID for each request/response pairing.
2. **Protocol Identifier (2 bytes)** — always `0x0000` for Modbus.
3. **Length (2 bytes)** — number of bytes following (Unit ID + FC + Data).
4. **Unit ID (1 byte)** — identifies the remote slave device on a serial sub-network.

*(1.5 marks for function explanation + 1.5 marks for fields)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the OSI Reference Model and discuss the specific responsibilities of the Application Layer in industrial networks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the message structure of a Modbus TCP Application Data Unit (ADU). Construct the complete Modbus TCP request frame to read 5 Holding Registers starting from address `40021` of a slave with Unit ID `3`. Assume Transaction ID = `0x0042`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) OSI Architecture & Application Layer Responsibilities (7 marks):**

The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Model** is a **7-layer conceptual framework** defined by **ISO/IEC 7498** that standardizes the functions of a telecommunication or computing system into abstraction layers. From top to bottom:

- **Layer 7 – Application:** Direct interface to user software (industrial protocols).
- **Layer 6 – Presentation:** Data encoding, encryption (TLS), compression (MMS, ASN.1).
- **Layer 5 – Session:** Dialog control, checkpointing, recovery (COTP for Modbus TCP).
- **Layer 4 – Transport:** End-to-end reliability (TCP/UDP), port addressing.
- **Layer 3 – Network:** Logical addressing and routing (IP).
- **Layer 2 – Data Link:** Framing, MAC addressing, error detection (Ethernet).
- **Layer 1 – Physical:** Electrical/optical signaling (100BASE-TX, fiber).

**Application Layer Responsibilities in Industrial Networks:**
1. Defines **industrial services** — read tag, write setpoint, upload recipe.
2. Encodes **domain semantics** — maps bytes to engineering units (e.g., °C, bar, rpm).
3. Manages **session lifecycle** for SCADA polling.
4. Supports **alarm/event reporting** and time synchronization (SNTP).
5. Provides **vendor-independent interoperability** via OPC UA information modeling.

*(3 marks for architecture listing + 4 marks for responsibilities with examples)*

---

**(b) Modbus TCP ADU Diagram & Frame Construction (7 marks):**

**Diagram of Modbus TCP ADU:**

| Field | TID | PID | Length | Unit ID | FC | Data |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Size (bytes) | 2 | 2 | 2 | 1 | 1 | n |
| Content | Unique ID | `0x0000` | Remaining bytes | Slave ID | Function | Payload |

**Frame Construction for the given problem:**

Given: Transaction ID = `0x0042`, Unit ID = `3`, Starting Address = 40021, Quantity = 5, Function Code = `0x03` (Read Holding Registers).

**Step 1:** Compute 0-based starting address: $40021 - 40001 = 20 = \texttt{0x0014}$.

**Step 2:** Compute Length field:
$$
\text{Length} = 1_{\text{UID}} + 1_{\text{FC}} + 2_{\text{addr}} + 2_{\text{qty}} = 6 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3:** Final hex byte stream:

$$
ADU = \underbrace{\texttt{00 42}}_{\text{TID}} \; \underbrace{\texttt{00 00}}_{\text{PID}} \; \underbrace{\texttt{00 06}}_{\text{Len}} \; \underbrace{\texttt{03}}_{\text{UID}} \; \underbrace{\texttt{03}}_{\text{FC}} \; \underbrace{\texttt{00 14 \; 00 05}}_{\text{Data}}
$$

**Total ADU size:** $7 + 1 + 4 = 12$ bytes.

*(3 marks for diagram + 2 marks for address conversion + 2 marks for hex frame assembly)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2022]**

**(a)** Compare **Modbus TCP, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, and OPC UA** based on transport protocol, port number, communication model, and typical industrial application domain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **OPC UA Information Model** with a block diagram. Discuss how OPC UA supports **security (authentication, encryption, authorization)** and **information modeling** in Industry 4.0 applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Protocol | Transport | Port | Model | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Modbus TCP | TCP | 502 | Client-Server | Legacy PLCs, Simple I/O |
| PROFINET | UDP/TCP/RTC | 34964/34962 | Producer-Consumer | Automotive, Motion Control |
| EtherNet/IP | TCP/UDP | 44818/2222 | Producer-Consumer | Rockwell PLCs, Discrete Mfg. |
| OPC UA | TCP | 4840 | Client-Server & Pub-Sub | MES/ERP Integration, IIoT |

**Detailed Discussion:**

- **Modbus TCP** uses a strict **master-slave (client-server)** model where the master polls each slave. It is **stateless**, **simple**, and **vendor-neutral**, but lacks security and high-speed real-time capabilities.
- **PROFINET** uses **PROFINET IO** with **RT (Real-Time)** and **IRT (Isochronous Real-Time)** classes, with hardware-scheduled time slots for sub-millisecond determinism.
- **EtherNet/IP** uses the **CIP (Common Industrial Protocol)** layer encapsulated in TCP/UDP, supporting both implicit (I/O) and explicit (message) communication.
- **OPC UA** unifies the strengths of all the above by adding **binary encoding**, **encryption (AES-256)**, **authentication (X.509 certificates)**, and a rich **information model** based on nodes, references, and types.

*(2 marks table + 5 marks explanation)*

---

**(b) OPC UA Information Model & Security (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram:**

```
OPC UA Client ↔ Secure Channel ↔ OPC UA Server
                                    │
                                    ├── Address Space (Nodes)
                                    │      ├── Objects (Devices)
                                    │      ├── Variables (Tags)
                                    │      └── Methods (Commands)
                                    ├── Subscriptions (Monitored Items)
                                    └── Services (Read/Write/Browse)
```

**Information Modeling:**
- OPC UA represents the plant as a **graph of Nodes** connected by **References**.
- Each node has **Attributes** (NodeId, BrowseName, DisplayName, Value, DataType).
- **Object Types** organize devices; **Variable Types** define tag semantics; **Method Types** represent executable functions.
- This makes data **self-describing**, enabling cross-vendor interoperability.

**Security in OPC UA:**
1. **Authentication:** X.509 certificates and username/password tokens verify identity.
2. **Encryption:** AES-128/256 with TLS 1.2/1.3 ensures confidentiality.
3. **Authorization:** Role-based access control (RBAC) restricts who can read/write which tags.
4. **Integrity:** SHA-2 hashing detects tampering.
5. **Auditing:** All access events are logged for compliance (IEC 62443).

*(3 marks for diagram + 2 marks for information model + 2 marks for security)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Do NOT confuse** the OSI Application Layer (Layer 7) with the **TCP/IP Application Layer** — TCP/IP *merges* Layers 5, 6, 7 into a single "Application" layer.
> - **Modbus address conversion is the #1 mistake:** Students forget that Modbus addresses are **0-based offsets** from the base register (40001, 30001, etc.). Always subtract `40001`, `30001`, etc.
> - **CRC vs Checksum:** Modbus **RTU** uses **CRC-16**; Modbus **TCP** uses **TCP checksum** (no CRC) — do not append CRC to Modbus TCP frames!
> - **Port numbers are exam-favorite:** Memorize **502 (Modbus TCP)**, **44818 (EtherNet/IP)**, **4840 (OPC UA)**, and **34964 (PROFINET)**.
> - **Producer-Consumer ≠ Client-Server:** Do not interchange these models in essays.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **Application Layer (OSI Layer 7)** is the user-facing layer that hosts **industrial protocols** (Modbus TCP, PROFINET, OPC UA, EtherNet/IP).
- ✅ **Modbus TCP ADU** = **MBAP Header (7 bytes) + PDU (Function Code + Data)**. Maximum ADU size = **260 bytes**.
- ✅ **Standard Ports:** Modbus TCP = **502**, OPC UA = **4840**, EtherNet/IP = **44818**, PROFINET = **34964**.
- ✅ **Modbus addresses are 0-based offsets** from the base register range (40001, 30001, 10001, 00001).
- ✅ **Modbus TCP uses TCP only** (no CRC). **Modbus RTU uses serial + CRC-16** (polynomial `0xA001`).
- ✅ Industrial protocols use **three communication models**: Client-Server, Producer-Consumer, and Publisher-Subscriber.
- ✅ **OPC UA** is the modern, **secure** (AES-256 + X.509), **information-modeled** standard for Industry 4.0 and IIoT.
- ✅ **PROFINET IRT** achieves hardware-scheduled cycle times as low as **250 µs** for motion control.
- ✅ **Effective bandwidth efficiency** of small Modbus packets is low — **batching** reads improves throughput.
- ✅ **TCP/IP Model has only 4 layers** — Presentation & Session functions are folded into the TCP/IP Application Layer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Layer and PROFIBUS Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** User Layer and PROFIBUS Basics # Module 3: User Layer and PROFIBUS Basics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The User Layer (Application Layer) in Industrial Networks **Formal Definition:** The **User Layer** is the highest layer of the simplified fieldbus communication model (often treated as **OSI Layer 7 — the Application Layer**), responsible for providing services directly to the end-user application processes. In industrial networks, it defines how meaningful data (process variables, diagnostics, alarms, configuration parameters) is structured, encoded, presented, and interpreted between communicating devices (PLCs, sensors, actuators, HMI/SCADA stations, drives). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** In KTU's PEICT747 syllabus, the fieldbus model is taught as a **3-layer collapsed architecture** — Physical Layer (Layer 1), Data Link Layer (Layer 2), and Application/User Layer (Layer 7). The User Layer is NOT a separate "Layer 8"; it is the fieldbus-world name for the application layer plus its user interface. **Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition:** Imagine a busy international airport. The physical layer is the runways and taxiways. The data link layer is the air traffic control giving landing slots and ensuring no two planes collide. The **User Layer** is the *passenger manifest* and *boarding pass* — it tells the actual passengers (the application data) *what their name is, what flight they are on, what seat, and what destination* — the meaningful information that the end system actually uses. In a PROFIBUS network, the User Layer takes the raw bit stream from the data link layer and converts it into: - Process values (e.g., temperature = 87.4 °C) - Diagnostic messages - Configuration/parameterization data - Alarms and events ### 1.2 PROFIBUS — Formal Definition **PROFIBUS** (PROcess FIeld BUS) is a standardized, open, vendor-independent fieldbus system used for high-speed serial communication in industrial automation. It is defined under the international standards **IEC 61158** and **IEC 61784**, and is widely used to connect field devices (sensors, actuators, drives, I/O modules) with controllers (PLCs/DCS). > [!IMPORTANT] > **PROFIBUS was developed in 1989** by a consortium of German companies led by Siemens, Bosch, ABB, and others. It is governed by **PI (PROFIBUS & PROFINET International)**, the world's largest automation community with over 1,700 member companies. ### 1.3 The Three PROFIBUS Variants — Quick Overview | Variant | Full Name | Primary Use | KTU Relevance | |---|---|---|---| | **PROFIBUS DP** | Decentralized Periphery | High-speed discrete I/O, drives, motors | ⭐⭐⭐ **Most tested** | | **PROFIBUS PA** | Process Automation | Hazardous/explosive areas (intrinsic safety) | ⭐⭐ | | **PROFIBUS FMS** | Fieldbus Message Specification | Higher-level cell-level comms (now obsolete) | ⭐ (Historical) | **Conceptual Analogy — Plain English:** - **PROFIBUS DP** is like the *factory floor conveyor system* — fast, rugged, carrying parts (data) between machines quickly. - **PROFIBUS PA** is the *explosion-proof version* — used in oil & gas refineries where a single spark could cause disaster. - **PROFIBUS FMS** was the *old email system* — replaced by Ethernet/Profinet, just a historical concept now. ### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Key Metrics (PROFIBUS DP over RS-485) - **Baud rate range:** 9.6 kbps to **12 Mbps** (standard rates: 9.6, 19.2, 45.45, 93.75, 187.5, 500, 1500, **3000**, **6000**, **12000** kbps) - **Maximum segment length (at 12 Mbps):** **100 m** - **Maximum segment length (at 9.6 kbps):** **1200 m** - **Maximum stations per segment:** **32** (with repeaters: up to 126) - **Topology:** Linear bus with terminators - **Cable:** Shielded twisted pair, **impedance 150 Ω** - **Standard:** EN 50170 / IEC 61158 > [!NOTE] > **The "32 station rule"** of PROFIBUS RS-485 arises because each device loads the bus with a minimum of 1 unit load, and the standard allows a maximum bus load of 32 unit loads per segment. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The PROFIBUS Protocol Stack (OSI Mapping) PROFIBUS uses a **3-layer simplified architecture** mapped to the OSI 7-layer model: | OSI Layer | PROFIBUS Equivalent | Function | Key Protocol | |---|---|---|---| | Layer 7 — Application | **Application/User Layer** | Meaningful data interpretation | DP services, GSD, FMS | | Layer 3–6 | (Not used) | — | — | | Layer 2 — Data Link | **Fieldbus Data Link (FDL)** | Bus access, frame structure, error detection | Token ring + Master-Slave | | Layer 1 — Physical | **Physical Layer** | Electrical/optical transmission | RS-485, Fiber, MBP | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"How many OSI layers does PROFIBUS use?"* — the answer is **3 layers** (collapsed). This is a classic KTU 2-mark question. ## 2.2 PROFIBUS DP — Operational Steps (How it Works) ### Step-by-Step Logic: 1. **Bus Initialization:** On power-up, the **active stations (masters)** run the token-passing algorithm. The first master (lowest address) holds the token initially. 2. **Token Passing:** Only the master holding the token may initiate communication. The token circulates in a logical ring based on station addresses. 3. **Master-Slave Polling:** When a master holds the token, it polls its assigned slaves cyclically. The slave responds within a defined **slot time**. 4. **Cyclic Data Exchange:** Process I/O data (input/output images) is exchanged with a deterministic cycle time. 5. **Acyclic Services (DP-V1):** Parameterization, diagnostics, alarm handling. 6. **Token Rotation Time (TRT):** The time for the token to make one full revolution around the logical ring. ### Why & How — The Engineering Rationale: - **Determinism:** Token passing guarantees bounded worst-case access time — critical for real-time control loops. - **Reliability:** A master failure triggers token re-initialization; the system degrades gracefully. - **Scalability:** Up to 126 devices (with repeaters), supports hot-swapping. ## 2.3 PROFIBUS DP Versions (KTU High-Yield) | Version | Year | Key Features | |---|---|---| | **DP-V0** | 1994 | Cyclic I/O, diagnostics | | **DP-V1** | 1997 | Acyclic services, alarms, parameterization | | **DP-V2** | 2002 | Isochronous mode, slave-to-slave communication, time stamping | > [!IMPORTANT] > **DP-V2 Slave-to-Slave (MS0/MS1/MS2)** is high-yield for KTU. It allows a slave to publish data directly to other slaves without involving a master — saving bus bandwidth and reducing cycle time. ## 2.4 The PROFIBUS Frame Format (Layer 2 — FDL) Each PROFIBUS frame has the following structure (token, data, or short acknowledge): | Field | Length | Description | |---|---|---| | **SD** (Start Delimiter) | 1 byte | Distinguishes frame type (SD1=short, SD2=token, SD3=low-priority data, SD4=high-priority data) | | **LE / LEr** | 1 byte | Length / Length repeated | | **DA** (Destination Address) | 1 byte | 0–127 | | **SA** (Source Address) | 1 byte | 0–127 | | **FC** (Function Code) | 1 byte | Indicates service type | | **Data Unit (DU)** | 0–244 bytes | User data (process I/O, diagnostics) | | **FCS** (Frame Check Sequence) | 1 byte | CRC error detection | | **ED** (End Delimiter) | 1 byte | Always `0x16` | > [!NOTE] > **Address 127** is reserved for broadcast. **Address 126** is the "no station" address (default for new slaves — must be configured before commissioning). ## 2.5 GSD Files — Device Description A **GSD (General Station Description)** file is an electronic datasheet (text format, similar to INI) that describes a PROFIBUS device's: - Manufacturer name and ID - Supported baud rates - Number/type of I/O signals - Diagnostic capabilities - Module slot assignments The engineering tool (e.g., Siemens TIA Portal) reads the GSD to integrate the slave into the project. GSD files use the **`.gsd`** or **`.gsg`** (German) or **`.gse`** (English) extension. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 expected question:** *"What is a GSD file? What information does it contain?"* — This is a frequent 3-mark/7-mark topic. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Maximum segment length | $L_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud rate (Mbps)}}$ scaling rule | metres | 100 m at 12 Mbps → 1200 m at 9.6 kbps | | Stations per segment | $N_{seg} \le 32$ | devices | 1 unit load per device typically | | Maximum stations in network | $N_{net} \le 126$ | devices | With 4 repeaters | | Token Rotation Time | $T_{RT} = T_{TT} + \sum T_{i}$ | ms | Sum of all master hold times | | Bus cycle time | $T_{cycle} = T_{master} + T_{slave,1} + T_{slave,2} + \dots + T_{gap}$ | ms | Determined by number of slaves and frame size | | Data field capacity | $DU_{max} = 244$ | bytes | Per FDL frame | | Address range | $0 \le Addr \le 127$ | — | 127 = broadcast | > [!WARNING] > When using the **vertical pipe symbol** `\vert` instead of `\|` inside LaTeX, do **NOT** use the bare `|` character in markdown tables as it breaks the table rendering. Use `\vert` or `\mid` always. ## 2.7 Real-World Utility PROFIBUS is the **de-facto fieldbus** in: - Factory automation (Siemens S7 PLCs) - Process industries (chemicals, oil & gas, water treatment) - Power generation - Automotive manufacturing lines - Pulp and paper mills - Over **40 million PROFIBUS nodes** installed worldwide (as of 2023) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Segment Length vs Baud Rate Relationship The physical layer of PROFIBUS RS-485 has a fundamental relationship between the maximum allowed cable length and the baud rate. This arises from signal propagation loss and signal integrity at high frequencies. ### Given Data: - Reference length: $L_{ref} = 100$ m at baud rate $R_{ref} = 12$ Mbps - Standard PROFIBUS cable with characteristic impedance $Z_0 = 150$ Ω ### Derivation Steps: **Step 1:** Express the relationship as an inverse proportionality (higher baud rate → shorter cable due to attenuation): $$ L_{max} \propto \frac{1}{R} $$ **Step 2:** Introduce the proportionality constant $k$: $$ L_{max} = \frac{k}{R} $$ **Step 3:** Solve for $k$ using the reference condition $L_{ref} = 100$ m at $R_{ref} = 12 \times 10^6$ bps: $$ k = L_{ref} \times R_{ref} = 100 \times 12 \times 10^6 $$ $$ k = 1.2 \times 10^{9} \; \text{m} \cdot \text{bps} $$ **Step 4:** Form the general formula: $$ L_{max} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{9}}{R} $$ **Step 5:** Compute sample values for verification: At $R = 1.5$ Mbps: $$ L_{max} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{9}}{1.5 \times 10^{6}} = 800 \; \text{m} $$ At $R = 9.6$ kbps: $$ L_{max} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{9}}{9.6 \times 10^{3}} = 125{,}000 \; \text{m} $$ **Step 6:** Apply the **PROFIBUS standard cap** (the standard limits $L_{max} \le 1200$ m even if formula allows more): $$ L_{max,std} = \min\left(\dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{9}}{R},\; 1200\right) \; \text{m} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **1200 m hard cap** exists because of the round-trip time delay for token-passing handshakes. Beyond 1200 m, the **slot time** of the FDL protocol is violated. ## 3.2 Derivation: Token Rotation Time for a PROFIBUS Network In a PROFIBUS network with $N$ masters, the **Target Token Rotation Time (TTRT)** is the design parameter; the **Actual Token Rotation Time (TRT)** depends on traffic. ### Given: - $N$ = number of active masters - $T_H$ = token hold time per master (constant in steady state) - $T_{gap}$ = gap time between frames (approx 11 bit times) ### Derivation: **Step 1:** In a quiescent (low-traffic) system, the TRT equals the sum of all hold times plus the gap times: $$ T_{RT} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{H,i} + (N \times T_{gap}) $$ **Step 2:** For a homogeneous system (all masters equal), $T_{H,i} = T_H$: $$ T_{RT} = N \cdot T_H + N \cdot T_{gap} = N(T_H + T_{gap}) $$ **Step 3:** The **TTRT** (target) must be greater than the worst-case $T_{RT}$ to guarantee real-time behaviour: $$ T_{TTRT} \ge T_{RT,max} $$ **Step 4:** The design rule for PROFIBUS DP: $$ T_{TTRT} \approx T_{cycle,process} \times 0.8 $$ where $T_{cycle,process}$ is the desired process cycle time. > [!NOTE] > The factor **0.8** is empirically chosen to leave **20% bandwidth headroom** for acyclic traffic (alarms, diagnostics) so the cyclic traffic always meets its deadline. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — PROFIBUS DP vs PA vs FMS | Parameter | **PROFIBUS DP** | **PROFIBUS PA** | **PROFIBUS FMS** | |---|---|---|---| | OSI Layers | 3 (Phys, FDL, App) | 3 (Phys, FDL, App) | 3 + 7 (incl. LL+7) | | Physical Layer | RS-485 / Fiber | MBP (Manchester Bus Powered) | RS-485 | | Baud Rate | Up to 12 Mbps | 31.25 kbps (fixed) | Up to 1.5 Mbps | | Bus Length | 100 m to 1200 m | Up to 1900 m | 100 m to 1200 m | | Power on Bus | No | **Yes (bus-powered)** | No | | Intrinsic Safety (IS) | No | **Yes (EEx ia IIC)** | No | | Application | Discrete manufacturing, drives | Hazardous process areas | Cell-level comms (obsolete) | | Coupling to DP | — | Via **PA/DP coupler** or **Link** | — | | Standard | IEC 61158 Type 3 | IEC 61158 Type 3 | IEC 61158 Type 3 (withdrawn) | | Status (2024) | **Active, dominant** | Active, niche | **Obsolete** | ## 3.4 PROFIBUS Network Topology Analysis A typical PROFIBUS DP network consists of: - **Class 1 Master (DPM1):** PLC/CPU that exchanges cyclic I/O with slaves. - **Class 2 Master (DPM2):** Engineering station for commissioning, diagnostics, configuration. - **Slaves:** Field devices with simple I/O (sensors, valves, motor starters, drives). The bus is **terminated at both ends** by a **terminating resistor (R_T)** of 220 Ω, which matches the 150 Ω cable impedance and prevents reflections. > [!IMPORTANT] > A common KTU pitfall: students often forget that **PROFIBUS requires exactly 2 terminating resistors** — one at each *physical* end of the segment. Leaving them disconnected causes intermittent bus faults and CRC errors. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PROFIBUS 3-Layer Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph USERLAYER["USER LAYER (Application Layer - OSI Layer 7)"] APP1[Process Data Exchange] APP2[Diagnostics and Alarms] APP3[Parameterization] APP4[GSD File Configuration] APP5[Cyclic and Acyclic Services] end subgraph FDLLAYER["FIELD BUS DATA LINK (FDL - OSI Layer 2)"] FDL1[Token Passing Protocol] FDL2[Master Slave Polling] FDL3[Frame Encoding SD LE DA SA FC] FDL4[CRC Error Detection FCS] FDL5[Bus Access Control] end subgraph PHYLAYER["PHYSICAL LAYER (OSI Layer 1)"] PHY1[RS485 Transceiver] PHY2[Fiber Optic Interface] PHY3[MBP Manchester Coded] PHY4[Terminating Resistor 220 Ohm] end EXT1[PLC Master Class 1] EXT2[Engineer Master Class 2] EXT3[Slave I/O Modules] EXT4[Drives and Motors] EXT5[Sensors and Actuators] APP1 --> FDL3 APP2 --> FDL3 APP3 --> FDL3 APP4 --> FDL3 APP5 --> FDL3 FDL1 --> PHY1 FDL2 --> PHY1 FDL3 --> PHY1 FDL4 --> PHY1 FDL5 --> PHY1 FDL3 --> PHY2 FDL3 --> PHY3 PHY1 --> PHY4 EXT1 --- USERLAYER EXT2 --- USERLAYER EXT3 --- USERLAYER EXT4 --- USERLAYER EXT5 --- USERLAYER EXT1 --- PHYLAYER EXT2 --- PHYLAYER EXT3 --- PHYLAYER EXT4 --- PHYLAYER EXT5 --- PHYLAYER ``` ## 4.2 Token Passing Sequence in PROFIBUS DP ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant M1 as Master 1
Class 1 PLC participant M2 as Master 2
Class 2 HMI participant S1 as Slave 1
I/O Module participant S2 as Slave 2
Drive participant S3 as Slave 3
Sensor Note over M1,M2: Logical Token Ring M1->>M1: Holds Token M1->>S1: Poll Input Data (SD2) S1->>M1: Response Process Data M1->>S2: Poll Output Command S2->>M1: Acknowledge M1-->>M2: Pass Token Note over M2: Now Holds Token M2->>S3: Read Diagnostics S3->>M2: Diagnostic Block M2-->>M1: Pass Token Note over M1: Token Cycle Complete ``` ## 4.3 PROFIBUS DP Frame Structure (FDL) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[SD Start Delimiter 1B] --> B[LE Length 1B] B --> C[DA Destination Address 1B] C --> D[SA Source Address 1B] D --> E[FC Function Code 1B] E --> F[DU Data Unit 0 to 244B] F --> G[FCS Frame Check Seq 1B] G --> H[ED End Delimiter 1B] style A fill:#FFD700 style H fill:#FF6347 style F fill:#90EE90 ``` ## 4.4 Typical PROFIBUS DP Network Topology ```mermaid flowchart LR PLC[Master 1
PLC S7]:::master HMI[Master 2
HMI]:::master T1[Terminator 220 Ohm]:::term IO1[Slave 1
I/O Module]:::slave IO2[Slave 2
I/O Module]:::slave DRV[Slave 3
Drive]:::slave SEN[Slave 4
Sensor]:::slave T2[Terminator 220 Ohm]:::term PLC <--> T1 T1 --- IO1 IO1 --- IO2 IO2 --- DRV DRV --- SEN SEN --- T2 T2 <--> HMI classDef master fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef slave fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef term fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.5 Master-Slave Polling State Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> TokenReceived: Token Arrives TokenReceived --> Polling: Check Active Slaves Polling --> SendRequest: Build FDL Frame SendRequest --> WaitResponse: Frame Sent WaitResponse --> ReceiveData: Response Within Slot Time WaitResponse --> Retry: Timeout CRC Error ReceiveData --> ProcessData: Parse DU ProcessData --> Polling: More Slaves Pending ProcessData --> PassToken: All Polled PassToken --> Idle: Token Forwarded Retry --> SendRequest: Up to Max Retries Retry --> Error: Max Retries Exceeded Error --> PassToken: Report Diagnostic ``` > [!NOTE] > The state diagram above maps the **finite state machine of a PROFIBUS Class 1 master** during a single token hold. This is the deterministic flow that the FDL layer executes. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the User Layer in industrial networks. How does it differ from the Physical Layer? **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **User Layer** (Application Layer, OSI Layer 7) is the highest layer in the simplified fieldbus architecture. It provides services such as **process data exchange, diagnostics, parameterization, alarm handling, and device description** to the end-user application. **Difference from Physical Layer:** | Aspect | User Layer | Physical Layer | |---|---|---| | OSI Reference | Layer 7 | Layer 1 | | Function | Interprets meaningful data | Transmits raw bits | | Concern | Application semantics | Electrical/optical signals | | Example | Reading temperature = 87.4 °C | RS-485 voltage levels on copper | > **Marks Breakdown (KTU pattern):** 1 mark definition + 1 mark key functions + 1 mark comparison. --- ### Question 2: List the three variants of PROFIBUS and state one key application of each. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **PROFIBUS DP** (Decentralized Periphery): Used for high-speed communication between PLCs and field devices (sensors, drives, I/O) in **discrete manufacturing**. 2. **PROFIBUS PA** (Process Automation): Used in **hazardous/process automation areas** (e.g., oil refineries, chemical plants) where **intrinsic safety (EEx ia)** is mandatory. It runs on **MBP (Manchester Bus Powered)** with 31.25 kbps and can supply power to devices over the same cable. 3. **PROFIBUS FMS** (Fieldbus Message Specification): Originally designed for **cell-level communication** between controllers. Now **obsolete**, replaced by Ethernet-based protocols. > **Marks Breakdown:** 1 mark for each variant's name and application. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the architecture of PROFIBUS DP with a neat block diagram. Compare PROFIBUS DP with PROFIBUS PA in terms of physical layer, baud rate, and applications. **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Question]** **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 Marks] Architecture of PROFIBUS DP:** PROFIBUS DP uses a **3-layer simplified OSI architecture**: - **Layer 1 — Physical Layer:** Uses **RS-485** (most common) or **fiber optic** or **MBP**. Topology is **linear bus** with two **220 Ω terminators** at the physical ends. Baud rate: 9.6 kbps to 12 Mbps. - **Layer 2 — Fieldbus Data Link (FDL):** Implements the **token-passing protocol** among active stations (masters) and **master-slave polling** between a master and its slaves. Provides error detection via **FCS (CRC-8)** and guarantees **deterministic bus access**. - **Layer 7 — User/Application Layer:** Implements **DP-V0/V1/V2 services** (cyclic I/O, acyclic diagnostics, parameterization, alarms). Uses **GSD files** for device description. **Network Composition:** - One or more **Class 1 Masters (DPM1)** — typically PLCs that exchange cyclic I/O. - Optional **Class 2 Masters (DPM2)** — engineering/diagnostic stations. - **Slaves** — field devices (simple I/O, motor starters, drives, valves). **Comparison Table (DP vs PA):** | Parameter | PROFIBUS DP | PROFIBUS PA | |---|---|---| | Physical Layer | RS-485 / Fiber | MBP (Manchester Bus Powered) | | Baud Rate | Up to 12 Mbps | 31.25 kbps (fixed) | | Bus Power | No | **Yes (intrinsic safety)** | | Segment Length | 100–1200 m | Up to 1900 m | | Application | Factory automation, drives | Hazardous process areas | > **Marks Breakdown (a):** 2 marks architecture + 2 marks FDL + 1 mark User Layer + 2 marks comparison table. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the PROFIBUS DP frame structure with the significance of each field. Explain the token-passing algorithm in PROFIBUS. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model Question]** **Model Solution:** **(b) [7 Marks] PROFIBUS DP Frame Structure:** | Field | Size | Significance | |---|---|---| | **SD (Start Delimiter)** | 1 byte | Distinguishes frame type: SD1=short acknowledge, SD2=token/data (no DU), SD3=low-priority data, SD4=high-priority data | | **LE / LEr** | 1 byte each | Length of data unit with LEr being a repeat for safety | | **DA (Destination Address)** | 1 byte | 0–127; 127 = broadcast | | **SA (Source Address)** | 1 byte | 0–127 | | **FC (Function Code)** | 1 byte | Indicates service: SRD, SDN, etc. | | **DU (Data Unit)** | 0–244 bytes | User/process data | | **FCS (Frame Check Sequence)** | 1 byte | CRC-8 error detection | | **ED (End Delimiter)** | 1 byte | Always `0x16`, signals end of frame | **Token-Passing Algorithm:** 1. Each **active station (master)** has a unique address in the logical ring. 2. On power-up, the **lowest-addressed master** initiates the token and begins holding the token. 3. While holding the token, the master: - Polls its assigned **slaves** (master-slave communication). - Exchanges process data cyclically. - Issues diagnostics/alarms. 4. When the master's **token hold time** expires (or queue is empty), it **passes the token** to the next master in the logical ring (address + 1, wrapping at 126). 5. If a master does not receive the token within the **slot time**, it initiates **token re-initialization**. 6. The cycle continues — this guarantees **deterministic worst-case access time** = $T_{TTRT}$. > **Marks Breakdown (b):** 3 marks frame structure + 1 mark significance + 3 marks token-passing algorithm. --- ### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** What is a GSD file in PROFIBUS? Explain its structure and the role it plays in device integration. **[KTU University Exam - July 2023 Model Question]** **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 Marks] GSD File:** A **GSD (General Station Description)** is a vendor-supplied, machine-readable text file (similar to a Windows INI file) that **describes a PROFIBUS device's communication capabilities** to the engineering tool (e.g., Siemens TIA Portal, Step 7). **File Extensions:** - `.gsd` — generic - `.gsg` — German - `.gse` — English **Structure of a GSD File (Key Sections):** 1. **General Information:** Manufacturer name, device family, model, hardware/firmware version. 2. **Vendor and Device Identification:** Vendor ID (assigned by PI), Device ID. 3. **Baud Rates Supported:** e.g., 9.6, 19.2, ..., 12000 kbps. 4. **Bus Timeslots:** Maximum diagnostic wait time, etc. 5. **Slot Definitions:** The I/O modules the device supports (digital input, analog output, etc.). 6. **Module List:** Predefined module configurations (e.g., 8 DI, 4 DO, 2 AI, 1 AO). 7. **Diagnostic Support:** Standard and extended diagnostic bits. 8. **Sync/Freeze Capability:** For DP-V1 synchronization. **Role in Device Integration:** - The **engineering tool** (master config) reads the GSD file to know **what the slave is, what it can do, and how to talk to it**. - Without a GSD, the master cannot configure the slave or interpret its process data correctly. - GSDs enable **vendor-independent interoperability** — a Siemens PLC can configure an ABB drive, provided both have valid PROFIBUS GSDs. > **Marks Breakdown (a):** 2 marks definition + 3 marks structure + 2 marks role. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **master-slave communication** in PROFIBUS DP. Discuss the role of DP-V1 and DP-V2 enhancements. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]** **Model Solution:** **(b) [7 Marks] Master-Slave Communication:** In PROFIBUS DP, communication follows a strict **master-slave principle**: - The **master (active station)** initiates all transactions. - The **slave (passive station)** only responds when polled. - Each slave has a unique address (1–125). - The master sends a request → slave processes → slave responds within the **slot time** (max response time). **Cyclic Sequence:** 1. Master holds the token. 2. Master sends an **SRD (Send and Request Data)** telegram to Slave 1. 3. Slave 1 sends back its input data + acknowledges the master's output data. 4. Master repeats for Slaves 2, 3, ..., N. 5. Master passes the token to the next master. **DP-V0 (Base functionality, 1994):** - Cyclic I/O exchange. - Device, channel, and module-level diagnostics. **DP-V1 Enhancements (1997):** - **Acyclic services** for parameterization, diagnostics, and alarm handling. - Allows engineers to reconfigure slaves **without disturbing** the cyclic data flow. - Supports **alarm types:** Process alarm, Diagnostic alarm, Pull/Push alarm, Update alarm. **DP-V2 Enhancements (2002):** - **Isochronous mode (equidistant):** Master and slaves operate on a synchronized clock; **jitter < 1 μs** — critical for high-speed motion control. - **Slave-to-slave communication (MS0):** A slave (publisher) can broadcast data to other slaves (subscribers) without involving a master, saving **two bus cycles**. - **Time stamping (MS1/MS2):** Time-of-event stamping at the slave for precise process synchronization. > **Marks Breakdown (b):** 2 marks master-slave + 2 marks cyclic flow + 1 mark DP-V1 + 2 marks DP-V2. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > 1. **Calling PROFIBUS a "7-layer protocol"** — WRONG. It uses **3 layers**. Lose 1 mark if you write "7-layer." > 2. **Forgetting the **2 terminating resistors** in the network diagram** — Examiners specifically check the 220 Ω resistors at both ends. Lose 1 mark if missing. > 3. **Confusing DP-V1 and DP-V2 features** — DP-V1 = acyclic services; DP-V2 = isochronous + slave-to-slave. Mixing them loses 1 mark. > 4. **Not specifying baud rate with units** — Always write "**12 Mbps**" or "**31.25 kbps**", not just "12 mega" or "31.25 kilo." > 5. **Skipping GSD file in DP questions** — Many students forget to mention GSD when describing device integration. This is a **favourite KTU question** worth 3–7 marks. > 6. **Mixing PROFIBUS DP with PROFINET** — These are **different** protocols (DP = RS-485, PROFINET = Industrial Ethernet). Examiners deduct 1 mark for confusion. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: User Layer & PROFIBUS Basics** ### 📌 Core Concepts - [x] **User Layer = Application Layer (OSI Layer 7)** in the simplified fieldbus model. - [x] **PROFIBUS** is a 3-layer (Phys, FDL, App) industrial fieldbus standardized under **IEC 61158/61784**. - [x] Governed by **PI (PROFIBUS & PROFINET International)**, originating from a 1989 German consortium. ### 📌 PROFIBUS Variants - [x] **DP** (Decentralized Periphery) — high-speed factory automation, 12 Mbps, RS-485. - [x] **PA** (Process Automation) — 31.25 kbps, MBP, intrinsic safety, hazardous areas. - [x] **FMS** — Cell-level comms; **OBSOLETE**. ### 📌 Key Technical Numbers - [x] **Max baud rate:** 12 Mbps - [x] **Max segment length:** 100 m @ 12 Mbps, up to 1200 m @ low baud - [x] **Max stations/segment:** 32; **Max stations/network:** 126 (with repeaters) - [x] **Address range:** 0–127 (127 = broadcast, 126 = default new slave) - [x] **Terminator:** 220 Ω at both physical ends - [x] **Max data unit:** 244 bytes/frame ### 📌 Frame Format - [x] Fields: **SD, LE, DA, SA, FC, DU, FCS, ED** - [x] **FCS** is CRC-8;
