**SD** distinguishes frame type. ### 📌 Protocol Behavior - [x] **Token passing** among masters (logical ring). - [x] **Master-slave polling** for cyclic I/O. - [x] **DP-V0:** Basic cyclic I/O. - [x] **DP-V1:** Acyclic services, alarms, parameterization. - [x] **DP-V2:** Isochronous mode, slave-to-slave (MS0), time stamping (MS1/MS2). ### 📌 Device Description - [x] **GSD file** — vendor-supplied, describes device to engineering tool. - [x] Extensions: `.gsd`, `.gsg`, `.gse`. ### 📌 Standardization & Topology - [x] **Standards:** IEC 61158, IEC 61784, EN 50170. - [x] **Topology:** Linear bus with terminators. - [x] **Cable:** Shielded twisted pair, $Z_0 = 150$ Ω. - [x] **Media:** RS-485, Fiber Optic, MBP. ### 📌 Real-World Use - [x] Dominant in factory automation, especially with **Siemens S7 PLCs**. - [x] **40+ million nodes** installed worldwide (2023). - [x] **Cumulative market leader** for the past two decades in process and factory automation. --- > **📚 End of Module 3 Notes — User Layer and PROFIBUS Basics** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme PEICT747 — Industrial Networks. Outcome-Based Education (OBE) and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L1–L6) cognitive levels covered.*

topics.title: Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Network Architecture** refers to the structured, layered framework that defines how various industrial communication components — sensors, controllers, actuators, supervisory systems, and enterprise applications — are interconnected, how data flows between them, and how communication protocols are organized across hierarchical levels of an industrial automation plant.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (PEICT747, Module 3), **Architecture** encompasses:
- The **OSI Reference Model** applied to industrial communication
- The **Purdue Reference Model (ISA-95)** for plant-wide hierarchy
- **Industrial Network Topologies** (Bus, Star, Ring, Mesh, Hybrid)
- **3-Tier / 5-Tier Network Hierarchy** (Device, Control, Operations/Supervisory, Enterprise)
- **Communication Models** (Client-Server, Producer-Consumer, Publisher-Subscriber)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — The "Smart Hospital"**
>
> Imagine a large modern hospital as an **industrial plant**:
> - **Patients** = Field devices (sensors, valves, motors)
> - **Ward Nurses** = PLCs / Controllers
> - **Department Doctors** = SCADA / HMIs (Supervisory)
> - **Hospital Administration & Insurance** = ERP / MES (Enterprise)
> - **Pneumatic Tube / Intercom System / Computer Network** = The Industrial Network
>
> Just like a hospital cannot send insurance billing data over the same channel as a heart-rate sensor (different speed, criticality, security), an industrial plant uses a **layered architecture** so that critical real-time device data and slow enterprise data do not collide. **Architecture** is the "master blueprint" that decides which "doctor" can talk to which "nurse" using what kind of "intercom" (protocol).

---

### 1.3 Key Physical / Architectural Metrics

- **Number of OSI Layers Used in Industrial Networks:** Typically **3 to 4 layers** (Physical, Data Link, Application, sometimes Network & Transport — simplified compared to full 7-layer OSI).
- **Hierarchy Levels in Purdue Model:** **5 Levels** (Level 0 to Level 4).
- **Typical Network Levels in Industrial Practice:** **3 Functional Tiers** (Device, Control, Information).
- **Latency Target at Device Level:** **< 1 ms** (for high-speed motion control).
- **Latency at Enterprise Level:** **100 ms – seconds** (acceptable for reporting).
- **Standard Reference Model:** **ISA-95 / Purdue Model**.

---

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU PEICT747 Module-3, students must be able to:
> - **CO3:** *Illustrate the architecture of industrial networks including reference models, topologies, and hierarchical levels.*
> - Draw and explain the **Purdue Reference Model**.
> - Compare **OSI 7-layer model** with the **industrial communication stack**.
> - Justify the choice of **topology** for a given industrial scenario.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The OSI Reference Model in Industrial Context

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) model** (ISO/IEC 7498) defines 7 abstraction layers. Industrial networks do **not** implement all 7 layers — they use a **collapsed / optimized stack**.

| OSI Layer | Industrial Equivalent | Industrial Protocols Using It | KTU Weight |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------------------------|------------|
| 7 — Application | Application Layer | CIP (Ethernet/IP), Profinet CBA, Modbus TCP | High |
| 6 — Presentation | (Often merged with App.) | — | Low |
| 5 — Session | (Often merged with App.) | — | Low |
| 4 — Transport | TCP / UDP (or omitted) | TCP for Ethernet/IP, UDP for Profinet RT | Medium |
| 3 — Network | IP (routing) | Ethernet/IP, Profinet, Modbus TCP | Medium |
| 2 — Data Link | MAC Layer | Ethernet, CAN, Profibus, DeviceNet | **Very High** |
| 1 — Physical | Electrical/Optical layer | RS-485, Ethernet cable, Fiber, Wireless | **Very High** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Reality:** Many fieldbuses like **Profibus DP**, **CAN**, and **DeviceNet** use only **Layers 1, 2, and 7** (the "**3-layer model**"). Modern **Industrial Ethernet** uses **Layers 1, 2, 3, 4, 7**.

---

### 2.2 The Purdue Reference Model (ISA-95)

The **Purdue Reference Model**, formalized by **ISA-95** and **IEC 62264**, is the **gold-standard architecture** for industrial network design and is a frequent 14-mark question in KTU ESE.

It divides the plant into **5 Levels** plus a **demilitarized zone**:

| Level | Name | Typical Devices / Systems | Network Type | Latency |
|-------|------|---------------------------|--------------|---------|
| **L0** | Process / Field Sensors & Actuators | Sensors, Motors, Valves, RTDs | Fieldbus / Sensor-Actuator Bus | < 1 ms |
| **L1** | Basic Control | PLCs, DCS Controllers, RTUs | Control Network (Profibus, DeviceNet) | 1–10 ms |
| **L2** | Supervisory Control | SCADA, HMI, Alarms | Plant Operational Network | 10–100 ms |
| **L3** | Site Manufacturing Operations | MES, Historians, Batch Mgmt | Local Area Network (LAN) | 100 ms–1 s |
| **L3.5** | **Industrial DMZ** (added later) | Firewalls, SIEM, Replication Servers | Buffer Zone for Security | — |
| **L4** | Enterprise / Business | ERP, CRM, BI, Email | Corporate WAN / Internet | Seconds |
| **L5** (optional) | Supply Chain / Cloud | Suppliers, Customers, Cloud | Public Internet | Variable |

---

### 2.3 Industrial Network Topologies

A **topology** is the geometric arrangement of nodes and links. The KTU syllabus expects comparison and application scenarios.

| Topology | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages | Typical Use |
|----------|-------------|------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Bus / Line** | All nodes share a single backbone cable (terminated at both ends) | Simple, cheap, easy to extend | Single cable failure breaks network; collisions | Fieldbus (Profibus, CAN) |
| **Star** | All nodes connect to a central switch/hub | Failure of one node doesn't affect others; easy diagnostics | Hub is single point of failure | Industrial Ethernet |
| **Ring** | Nodes form a closed loop; data travels in one direction (or both) | Predictable latency; fault tolerance with redundancy | One break may isolate a segment unless dual-ring | Token Ring, Fiber Optic, Resilient Ethernet (DLR, MRP) |
| **Mesh** | Every node connects to multiple others | High redundancy, self-healing | Expensive, complex configuration | Wireless (WirelessHART), critical plants |
| **Tree (Hierarchical)** | Combination of star and bus; grouped branches | Scalable for large plants | Root failure breaks sub-trees | Large SCADA + PLC deployments |
| **Hybrid** | Mix of above (e.g., star-of-rings) | Best of multiple worlds | Complex design | Modern smart factories (IIoT) |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Boundary | Application |
|---------|----------------|------------------|-------------|
| Total nodes in a **Bus** | $N_{bus} = N$ (all nodes on one segment) | Limited by **stub length** & **attenuation** | Profibus max ~32 nodes per segment |
| Max nodes in **RS-485 Bus** | $N_{max} = 32$ (standard), up to **256** with repeaters | Unit loads (UL) per driver | Profibus DP, Modbus RTU |
| **Ethernet Collision Domain** (Hub) | $C_{domain} = N$ (all share bandwidth) | CSMA/CD limits segment | Replaced by switches |
| **Switched Ethernet Collision Domain** | $C_{domain} = 1$ per port | Full-duplex, no collisions | Modern Industrial Ethernet |
| Signal Attenuation Budget | $A_{total} = \sum A_{cable} + \sum A_{connector} + A_{splice}$ | dB | Fiber optic design |
| Max segment length (10BASE-T Ethernet) | $L_{max} = 100 \text{ m}$ | meters | Copper twisted-pair |
| Max segment length (Profibus DP) | $L_{max} = 1200 \text{ m}$ (at 9.6 kbps); $\vert 100 \text{ m}$ at 12 Mbps | meters | Fieldbus design |
| Cycle Time (Cyclic Communication) | $T_c = T_{propagation} + T_{processing} + T_{frame\_tx}$ | ms | Real-time guarantee |
| Throughput (Shannon-Hartley context) | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bits/s | Wireless industrial network |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** The Purdue model + topology choice is used by **every plant architect** in **oil & gas, automotive, pharma, and power** industries. Picking the wrong architecture leads to **safety failures** (e.g., mixing enterprise IT with safety-critical control).

---

### 2.5 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Layering

**Why layered architecture?**
1. **Separation of Concerns:** Field-device engineers don't need to know ERP software.
2. **Interoperability:** A Siemens PLC can talk to an ABB HMI if both follow layer standards.
3. **Security:** Defense-in-depth — firewalls placed *between* levels.
4. **Determinism:** Critical layers (L0/L1) operate on isolated, deterministic buses unaffected by enterprise traffic bursts.

**How does data flow?**
- **Vertical flow:** Sensor (L0) → PLC (L1) → SCADA (L2) → MES (L3) → ERP (L4)
- **Horizontal flow:** PLC-to-PLC peer communication on the same control network.
- **Cycle:** L0–L1 is **cyclic (real-time)**. L3–L4 is **acyclic / event-driven**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparing 3-Layer vs 7-Layer Communication

Many KTU questions ask: *"Why do fieldbuses like Profibus use only 3 layers instead of 7?"*

| Aspect | 7-Layer OSI (Full IT Stack) | 3-Layer Industrial (Fieldbus) | Justification |
|--------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|---------------|
| Number of layers | 7 | 3 (Phys, Data Link, App) | Less processing = lower latency |
| Protocol overhead | High (TCP/IP headers) | Very low | Determinism & speed |
| Implementation cost | High memory/CPU needed | Can run on cheap 8-bit MCUs | Field devices are resource-constrained |
| Suitability | IT networks, enterprise | Real-time control | Determinism |
| Standardization | ISO/IEC 7498 | IEC 61158 (Fieldbus), IEC 61784 | Fieldbus-specific |
| Example | HTTP over TCP/IP | Profibus DP, CANopen | — |

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Purdue Level vs Network Type vs Protocol

This is a **high-yield exam table** that maps each Purdue level to its network and protocols:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Purdue Level (L)} \;&\longmapsto\; \text{Network Type} \;\longmapsto\; \text{Representative Protocols} \\
L_0 \;(\text{Process}) \;&\longmapsto\; \text{Sensor/Actuator Bus} \;\longmapsto\; \text{IO-Link, AS-i, HART} \\
L_1 \;(\text{Control}) \;&\longmapsto\; \text{Control / Fieldbus} \;\longmapsto\; \text{Profibus DP, DeviceNet, CANopen, EtherCAT} \\
L_2 \;(\text{Supervisory}) \;&\longmapsto\; \text{Plant LAN} \;\longmapsto\; \text{Ethernet/IP, Profinet, Modbus TCP} \\
L_3 \;(\text{Operations}) \;&\longmapsto\; \text{MES / Historian LAN} \;\longmapsto\; \text{OPC UA, SQL, MQTT} \\
L_4 \;(\text{Enterprise}) \;&\longmapsto\; \text{Corporate WAN} \;\longmapsto\; \text{HTTP/S, SAP, Web Services} 
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — Maximum Cycle Time in a Token-Passing Ring

For a KTU numerical question on ring topology (token bus/ring network), the **maximum token rotation time** is derived as:

**Given:**
- $N$ = number of nodes in the ring
- $T_{THT}$ = Token Holding Time per node (maximum time a node may hold the token)
- $T_{prop,i}$ = propagation delay on link $i$

**Step 1:** Sum the token-holding times for all nodes in the worst case (each node uses the full THT):
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{THT,i} = N \cdot T_{THT}$$

**Step 2:** Sum the propagation delays around the entire ring:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{prop,i} = T_{ring\_prop}$$

**Step 3:** The maximum Token Rotation Time ($TRT_{max}$) is:
$$TRT_{max} = N \cdot T_{THT} + T_{ring\_prop}$$

**Step 4:** For a uniform ring of $N$ nodes and identical links of length $L$ (with propagation delay $\tau$ per meter):
$$TRT_{max} = N \cdot T_{THT} + N \cdot L \cdot \tau$$

**Step 5:** Real-time deadline guarantee — a node waiting for the token has a worst-case latency:
$$W_{max} = N \cdot T_{THT} + T_{ring\_prop}$$

This **$W_{max}$** must be **less than the application's deadline** to guarantee real-time behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Used in **Token Ring (IEEE 802.5)**, **Profibus Token Bus**, and **FDDI** to compute worst-case latency. Critical for **safety-critical** control loops.

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation — Bus Topology Maximum Cable Length

For an **RS-485 / Profibus DP** bus, the maximum cable length is **inversely proportional to the baud rate**. The empirical rule (per IEC 61158) is:

$$L_{max}(bps) = \frac{K}{R}$$

where $K$ is a constant depending on cable type, and $R$ is the baud rate.

**Worked Example (typical Profibus DP values):**

| Baud Rate (kbps) | Max Segment Length (m) |
|------------------|------------------------|
| 9.6 | 1200 |
| 19.2 | 1200 |
| 93.75 | 1200 |
| 187.5 | 1000 |
| 500 | 400 |
| 1500 | 200 |
| 12000 | 100 |

The relationship shows: **higher speed ⇒ shorter cable** because signal rise/fall times become a larger fraction of the bit period.

**Step-by-Step Interpretation:**
1. At 9.6 kbps, one bit lasts $T_b = 1/9600 \approx 104 \ \mu s$.
2. At 12 Mbps, one bit lasts $T_b = 1/12{,}000{,}000 \approx 83 \ ns$.
3. The cable's propagation delay (~5 ns/m) becomes significant at high baud rates.
4. Reflections at cable ends (mismatch) limit useful length.

**Result:** Maximum reliable segment length:
$$L_{max} \approx \frac{T_b}{2 \cdot \tau_{prop}}$$

where $\tau_{prop}$ is the one-way propagation delay per meter.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Purdue Reference Model — Hierarchical Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["Level 4 — Enterprise Network\nERP, CRM, BI, Email, Cloud"]
    L35["Level 3.5 — Industrial DMZ\nFirewalls, SIEM, Replication Servers"]
    L3["Level 3 — Operations Network\nMES, Historians, Batch Mgmt, OPC UA"]
    L2["Level 2 — Supervisory Network\nSCADA, HMI, Alarms, Engineering Stations"]
    L1["Level 1 — Control Network\nPLCs, DCS Controllers, RTUs"]
    L0["Level 0 — Process / Field Network\nSensors, Actuators, Motors, Valves"]

    L4 -- "ERP data, reports" --> L35
    L35 -- "Filtered / Authenticated" --> L3
    L3 -- "Production data, orders" --> L2
    L2 -- "Setpoints, recipes" --> L1
    L1 -- "Cyclic I/O, control signals" --> L0
    L0 -. "Field measurements, status" .-> L1
    L1 -. "Process variables" .-> L2
    L2 -. "Aggregated data" .-> L3
    L3 -. "Production KPIs" .-> L35
    L35 -. "Reports, dashboards" .-> L4
```

---

### 4.2 Industrial Network Topologies Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BUS ["Bus Topology"]
        B1[Node1] --- BUSLINE((Backbone Cable))
        B2[Node2] --- BUSLINE
        B3[Node3] --- BUSLINE
        B4[Node4] --- BUSLINE
        B5[Node5] --- BUSLINE
        T1[Terminator] --- BUSLINE
        T2[Terminator] --- BUSLINE
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STAR ["Star Topology"]
        SW[Central Switch / Hub]
        SW --- S1[Node1]
        SW --- S2[Node2]
        SW --- S3[Node3]
        SW --- S4[Node4]
        SW --- S5[Node5]
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RING ["Ring Topology"]
        R1[Node1] ==> R2[Node2]
        R2 ==> R3[Node3]
        R3 ==> R4[Node4]
        R4 ==> R5[Node5]
        R5 ==> R1
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MESH ["Mesh Topology"]
        M1[Node1] --- M2[Node2]
        M1 --- M3[Node3]
        M1 --- M4[Node4]
        M2 --- M3
        M2 --- M4
        M3 --- M4
    end
```

---

### 4.3 OSI-to-Industrial Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OSI7 ["OSI 7-Layer Model vs Industrial Stack"]
        L7["Layer 7 — Application\n(CIP, Modbus TCP, Profinet CBA)"]
        L6["Layer 6 — Presentation\n(Usually empty)"]
        L5["Layer 5 — Session\n(Usually empty)"]
        L4["Layer 4 — Transport\n(TCP / UDP)"]
        L3["Layer 3 — Network\n(IP)"]
        L2["Layer 2 — Data Link\n(Ethernet MAC / Profibus MAC / CAN)"]
        L1["Layer 1 — Physical\n(RS-485, Fiber, Twisted Pair)"]
        L7 --- L6
        L6 --- L5
        L5 --- L4
        L4 --- L3
        L3 --- L2
        L2 --- L1
    end
```

---

### 4.4 Communication Architecture Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLIENTSERVER ["Client-Server Model"]
        CS_Client[Client HMI] -- "Request" --> CS_Server[Server PLC]
        CS_Server -- "Response" --> CS_Client
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PUBSUB ["Publisher-Subscriber Model"]
        PUB[Publisher PLC] -- "Multicast Tag" --> SUB1[Subscriber HMI]
        PUB -- "Multicast Tag" --> SUB2[Subscriber SCADA]
        PUB -- "Multicast Tag" --> SUB3[Subscriber Historian]
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRODCONS ["Producer-Consumer Model"]
        PROD[Producer] -- "Frame" --> BUS((Network))
        BUS --> CONS1[Consumer A]
        BUS --> CONS2[Consumer B]
        BUS --> CONS3[Consumer C]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Mermaid renders these as logical flow blocks because physical drawings (e.g., cable routing) cannot be represented in flowchart syntax. The above are **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flows** that map the interaction patterns clearly.

---

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Industrial Network Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FIELD["Field Layer\n(Sensors, Actuators,\nDrives, Valves)"]
    CTRL["Control Layer\n(PLC, DCS, RTU,\nMotion Controller)"]
    SUPR["Supervisory Layer\n(SCADA, HMI, Alarm Mgmt,\nAsset Mgmt)"]
    OPER["Operations Layer\n(MES, Historian,\nQuality, Batch)"]
    ENTR["Enterprise Layer\n(ERP, CRM, BI,\nSupply Chain)"]

    FIELD <-->|Fieldbus\n(Profibus, CAN, IO-Link)| CTRL
    CTRL <-->|Industrial Ethernet\n(Ethernet/IP, Profinet, EtherCAT)| SUPR
    SUPR <-->|Plant LAN\n(OPC UA, SQL, MQTT)| OPER
    OPER <-->|Corporate WAN\n(HTTP/S, Web Services)| ENTR
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Industrial Network Architecture." Mention its significance in modern automation.*

**Model Answer:**
Industrial Network Architecture is the structured framework that defines how sensors, controllers, supervisory systems, and enterprise applications are interconnected using defined protocols, topologies, and hierarchical levels. It is significant because it ensures **deterministic real-time communication** at the field level, **interoperability** between multi-vendor devices, **scalability** for plant expansion, and **security** through layer-wise isolation (e.g., via the Purdue Model). *(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 significance points)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*List any three differences between the OSI 7-layer model and the industrial 3-layer fieldbus model.*

**Model Answer:**
1. The OSI model has **7 layers**, while industrial fieldbuses typically use only **3 layers** (Physical, Data Link, Application).
2. OSI is designed for **general IT communication** with high overhead; the 3-layer model is designed for **real-time deterministic control** with minimal overhead.
3. OSI requires significant processing/memory; the 3-layer model can run on **resource-constrained microcontrollers** in field devices. *(3 marks: 1 mark each)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Purdue Reference Model + Topology*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Purdue Reference Model (ISA-95)** in detail. Draw the layered architecture and list typical devices/protocols at each level.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Bus, Star, and Ring topologies** for industrial networks. Give one industrial protocol example for each and justify which is best for a **high-speed automotive assembly line**.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Purdue Reference Model (ISA-95):**
The Purdue Reference Model, formalized by **ISA-95** and adopted in **IEC 62264**, divides industrial automation into **5 functional levels** separated by an **Industrial DMZ (Level 3.5)**:

- **Level 0 — Process / Field Level:** Sensors (temperature, pressure), actuators (valves, motors). Network: sensor-actuator bus. Protocols: **IO-Link, AS-i, HART**.
- **Level 1 — Basic Control Level:** PLCs, DCS controllers, RTUs that execute closed-loop control. Network: control bus. Protocols: **Profibus DP, DeviceNet, EtherCAT**.
- **Level 2 — Supervisory Control Level:** SCADA servers, HMI stations, alarm management. Protocols: **Ethernet/IP, Profinet, Modbus TCP**.
- **Level 3 — Site Manufacturing Operations Level:** MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems), batch management, historians. Protocols: **OPC UA, SQL, MQTT**.
- **Level 3.5 — Industrial DMZ:** Firewalls, SIEM servers, anti-virus, replication servers. Provides buffer between plant and enterprise.
- **Level 4 — Enterprise Level:** ERP, CRM, BI, email. Standard IT protocols.

The model enforces **strict separation** between operational technology (OT, L0–L3) and information technology (IT, L4), allowing **defense-in-depth security** and predictable latency. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram + 3 for explanation + 2 for protocols)*

**(b) Topology Comparison:**

| Aspect | Bus | Star | Ring |
|--------|-----|------|------|
| Cabling | Single backbone | Central switch + drops | Closed loop |
| Fault tolerance | Low (cable break fails all) | High (one node failure ok) | Medium (single break isolates) |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Medium-high |
| Latency | Variable (CSMA) | Predictable per port | Deterministic (token) |
| Example protocol | Profibus DP, CAN | Industrial Ethernet | Token Ring, EtherCAT (logical ring), Resilient Ethernet (DLR, MRP) |

**Best for high-speed automotive assembly line: Star topology with Industrial Ethernet (e.g., Profinet / Ethernet/IP).**
**Justification:**
- Automotive lines require **cycle times < 1 ms** with hundreds of I/O points.
- Star with switched Ethernet gives **deterministic latency per port** (no collisions).
- A central managed switch allows **easy diagnostics, port mirroring, and VLAN segmentation** between work cells.
- Star supports **redundant rings (MRP/DLR)** if combined with hybrid topology for high availability.
- Compared to bus (collision risk) and ring (single break risk), star is most maintainable. *(7 marks: 3 comparison + 2 example protocols + 2 justification)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *OSI + Hybrid Architecture*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain why industrial networks use a **simplified OSI stack** instead of the full 7 layers. Justify with two fieldbus protocol examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a block diagram, explain a **hybrid (star-of-rings) architecture** for a modern smart factory. Mention its advantages and disadvantages.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Why Industrial Networks Use a Simplified OSI Stack:**
Full OSI 7-layer communication is designed for **general-purpose IT** where bandwidth, flexibility, and rich features (sessions, presentation format negotiation) are paramount. Industrial control demands:
1. **Determinism:** A motor-control loop must complete within a strict deadline (e.g., 1 ms). The overhead of TCP handshaking, session establishment, and ASN.1 encoding in layers 5/6 is unacceptable.
2. **Resource constraints:** Field devices are often 8/16-bit microcontrollers with limited RAM. Running full TCP/IP stacks is infeasible.
3. **Real-time priority:** The simplified stack prioritizes cyclic data exchange over best-effort delivery.

**Examples:**
- **Profibus DP** uses only **Layers 1, 2, and 7** — Physical (RS-485), Data Link (Token Passing + Master-Slave), Application (DP-V0/V1/V2 services). It achieves cycle times of < 1 ms with up to 12 Mbps.
- **CAN (Controller Area Network)** uses **Layers 1 and 2** plus a simple application layer (CANopen / DeviceNet). Its arbitration-by-priority mechanism ensures the most urgent message always wins the bus, with deterministic worst-case latency. *(7 marks: 3 reasons + 2 example layers + 2 examples)*

**(b) Hybrid (Star-of-Rings) Architecture for a Smart Factory:**

**Diagram (Block Description):**
```
   [Enterprise (L4): ERP, Cloud]
             |
   [Industrial DMZ (L3.5): Firewall]
             |
   [Operations (L3): MES, Historian]
             |
   [Supervisory (L2): SCADA Server]
             |
       /-------------\
      /               \
   [Ring 1]         [Ring 2]      ← Each ring = a production cell/work area
    |  |  |           |  |  |
   PLC PLC PLC       PLC PLC PLC  ← (L1 Controllers)
    |  |  |           |  |  |
   Field devices     Field devices ← (L0 Sensors/Actuators)
```

**Advantages:**
1. **Scalability:** Each ring is an independent work cell; new cells can be added without disturbing existing ones.
2. **Fault isolation:** A fault in one ring does not propagate to other rings (network segmentation).
3. **Redundancy:** Ring topology offers a backup path; the central star switch offers per-port determinism.
4. **Best of both worlds:** Star simplifies management; ring offers deterministic, redundant communication.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Higher **CAPEX** due to more switches and cables.
2. Complex **network configuration** (VLANs, QoS, MRP/DLR).
3. Requires **skilled OT/IT personnel** for design and maintenance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **omit Level 3.5 (Industrial DMZ)** in the Purdue diagram → **lose 2 marks** (it's worth at least 2 marks in KTU 2024 scheme).
> - For topology questions, students often **forget to justify** the choice — always give a *reason* tied to latency, fault tolerance, or cost.
> - In OSI questions, students list all 7 layers verbatim but **fail to say which layers are skipped** and *why* — losing application/justification marks.
> - Never write "**OSI is bad for industry**" without offering a **replacement reasoning** about determinism and overhead.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Architecture** in industrial networks = **layered framework** combining **OSI principles + Purdue hierarchy + Topology**.
- **OSI 7-layer model** → full IT stack; fieldbuses use a **collapsed 3-layer (or 4-layer) stack** for determinism.
- **Purdue Reference Model (ISA-95):** **5 levels** (L0–L4) + **Industrial DMZ (L3.5)**.
- **Latency decreases** as you go **down** the Purdue levels (L0 fastest, L4 slowest).
- **Topologies:** Bus (cheap, fault-prone), Star (modern, Ethernet), Ring (deterministic), Mesh (redundant).
- **Communication patterns:** Client-Server, Publisher-Subscriber, Producer-Consumer.
- **Modern trend:** Hybrid (star-of-rings), Industrial Ethernet, IIoT, **OPC UA** as the unifying protocol across levels.
- **Cycle time formula:** $T_c = T_{prop} + T_{processing} + T_{frame}$.
- **Max token rotation (ring):** $TRT_{max} = N \cdot T_{THT} + T_{ring\_prop}$.
- **Cable length vs baud rate:** Higher baud rate → shorter cable.
- **Security architecture:** Always enforce **DMZ (Level 3.5)** between OT and IT — this is a frequent KTU 2024 question.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Viva/Exam:**
> **"Purdue's 5 Levels = PPECS"** →
> **P**rocess (L0) → **P**rogrammable Controllers / Basic Control (L1) → **E**ngineering / Supervisory (L2) → **C**omputing / Operations MES (L3) → **S**trategic Enterprise ERP (L4).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bus Access Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bus Access Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bus Access Method (Medium Access Control – MAC Protocol):**
> A *bus access method* is a set of **deterministic or stochastic rules** that govern how multiple stations (nodes, devices, controllers, sensors, actuators) gain the right to **transmit data** on a *shared physical medium* (the bus), ensuring that the integrity, ordering, and real-time delivery of messages are preserved.

In the OSI reference model, this functionality resides in the **lower sub-layer of the Data Link Layer (Layer 2)**, formally called the **MAC sub-layer (IEEE 802.x)**. In KTU’s Industrial Networks (PEICT747) framework, bus access methods form the *backbone* of fieldbus and industrial Ethernet performance, since the choice of MAC directly influences:

- **Determinism** – bounded worst-case latency
- **Throughput efficiency** – useful bits transmitted per unit time
- **Fairness** – equal or priority-weighted access
- **Real-time behaviour** – critical for closed-loop control

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🛣️ "Single-Lane Bridge Analogy"**
> Imagine a *narrow one-lane bridge* connecting two towns. Hundreds of cars (stations) wish to cross. Without rules, head-on collisions (data collisions) and traffic jams (channel underutilisation) occur. A bus access method is the **traffic controller** at each end of the bridge — it decides *who may enter, when, and what to do if two cars meet in the middle.*

- **CSMA/CD** → A car *listens* (CS) for oncoming traffic; if two enter, both *reverse* (CD) and try later.
- **Token Passing** → Only the car holding a *special baton* (token) may enter the bridge.
- **Master–Slave / Polling** → A *traffic warden* (master) explicitly *waves* each slave car forward, one at a time.
- **TDMA** → A *time-slot timetable* is published; each car has a fixed window.

## 1.3 Why Bus Access Methods Matter in Industrial Networks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industrial Metrics (must be memorised):**
> - **Cycle Time** – Time to poll/scan all I/O on the bus.
> - **Jitter** – Variation in delivery delay.
> - **Worst-Case Latency** – Critical for control loops (typically **1–10 ms** for fast motion, **10–100 ms** for process control).
> - **Baud Rate** – Physical signalling speed (e.g., **9.6 kbps to 1 Gbps** depending on bus).

Common **KTU-relevant industrial buses** and their access methods:

| Industrial Network | Bus Access Method |
|---|---|
| **Ethernet/IP / PROFINET** | CSMA/CD (with real-time extensions: RT, IRT) |
| **PROFIBUS-DP** | Hybrid: **Token Passing** between masters + **Master–Slave** polling |
| **Foundation Fieldbus** | **Token Passing** (LAS – Link Active Scheduler) |
| **DeviceNet / CAN** | **CSMA/BA** – bitwise arbitration (priority-based) |
| **Modbus RTU (RS-485)** | **Master–Slave** |
| **AS-Interface** | **Master–Slave** with cycle polling |
| **Token Bus (IEEE 802.4)** | **Token Passing on a logical ring** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Bus Access Methods

Bus access methods can be **broadly classified** into three families. Understanding this taxonomy is essential for any 14-mark KTU question.

### A. Contention-Based (Random Access) Protocols
Each station transmits **without coordination**; collisions are resolved either by **detection (CD)**, **avoidance (CA)**, or **backoff algorithms**.

- **Pure Aloha** – Transmit whenever ready. If a collision occurs, retransmit after a *random* backoff.
- **Slotted Aloha** – Time is divided into *slots*; stations transmit only at the start of a slot.
- **CSMA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access)** – *Listen before talk*. Variants: 1-persistent, non-persistent, p-persistent.
- **CSMA/CD (Collision Detection)** – Used in classic Ethernet (IEEE 802.3). Stations abort transmission upon detecting a collision.
- **CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)** – Used in Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) and industrial wireless. Uses RTS/CTS handshake.

### B. Controlled / Deterministic Access
A **logical token** is passed in a cyclic order; only the token holder may transmit. This is **collision-free** and *deterministic* — a key requirement for industrial networks.

- **Token Ring (IEEE 802.5)** – Logical/physical ring topology.
- **Token Bus (IEEE 802.4)** – Physical bus, *logical ring* of token holders (used in MAP).
- **Token Passing in PROFIBUS** – Hybrid token + master–slave.

### C. Scheduled Access
The medium is *pre-allocated* in time, frequency, or code.

- **TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access)** – Fixed time slots.
- **FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access)** – Frequency bands.
- **CDMA (Code Division Multiple Access)** – Spread-spectrum codes.
- **Master–Slave / Polling** – Master explicitly polls each slave in turn.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Method

| Method | Why was it designed? | How does it solve the access problem? |
|---|---|---|
| Pure Aloha | Simplest possible scheme for satellite/packet-radio | Random retransmission after backoff |
| Slotted Aloha | Doubles capacity of pure Aloha by synchronising slots | Forces transmissions to start at slot boundaries |
| CSMA | Reduces wasted transmissions by sensing the carrier | Station senses idle/busy channel before transmitting |
| CSMA/CD | Detects collisions fast and aborts, saving bandwidth | Collision detected within 2τ; jam signal sent |
| CSMA/CA | Wireless cannot detect collisions (hidden terminal); avoids them | RTS/CTS exchange, ACK, NAV timer |
| Token Passing | Determinism — bounded waiting time | Token circulates in logical ring |
| Master–Slave | Simplicity, low-cost for slow I/O | Central master dictates communication |
| TDMA | Predictable, no contention overhead | Each node has a dedicated time slice |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All numerical values below are **board-exam favourites** (2024 scheme weightage ≥ 2-mark derivation). Memorise the bolded constants.

| # | Protocol / Concept | Key Formula | Symbols & Units | Max Throughput (S<sub>max</sub>) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pure Aloha Throughput** | $$S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$$ | S = throughput (packet/slot), G = offered load | **S<sub>max</sub> = 1/(2e) ≈ 0.184 (18.4 %)** at G = 0.5 |
| 2 | **Slotted Aloha Throughput** | $$S = G \cdot e^{-G}$$ | Same as above | **S<sub>max</sub> = 1/e ≈ 0.368 (36.8 %)** at G = 1.0 |
| 3 | **CSMA Throughput (Non-persistent)** | $$S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1+2a) + e^{-aG}}$$ | a = propagation delay / packet transmission time | Approaches 1 as a → 0 |
| 4 | **CSMA/CD Capacity** | $$S = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.4a}$$ (approx.) | a = t<sub>prop</sub> / t<sub>tx</sub> | Efficiency → 1 when a → 0 |
| 5 | **Token Holding Time Bound** | $$THT \leq T_{cycle}/N$$ | T<sub>cycle</sub> = cycle time, N = number of stations | — |
| 6 | **Utilization (Token Bus)** | $$U = \dfrac{N \cdot t_{msg}}{T_{cycle}}$$ | t<sub>msg</sub> = message transmission time | — |
| 7 | **Maximum Frame Length (CSMA/CD)** | $$L_{max} = R \cdot 2 \cdot t_{prop} \cdot n$$ | R = bit rate, n = number of repeaters | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Never confuse **propagation delay (τ)** with **transmission delay (t<sub>tx</sub>)**.
> - *t<sub>tx</sub>* = frame size / bandwidth → time to push bits onto wire.
> - *τ* = distance / propagation speed → time for a bit to travel end-to-end.
> The dimensionless ratio **a = τ / t<sub>tx</sub>** is the single most important parameter in MAC analysis.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CSMA/CD (Ethernet)** is the de-facto standard for plant-level networks; however, its *non-determinism* led to industrial extensions like **PROFINET IRT** (Isochronous Real-Time) which *reserves time slots* within Ethernet cycles.
- **Token Bus / Token Ring** were the *gold standard* for determinism in **Manufacturing Automation Protocol (MAP)** in the 1980s, used in GM, Boeing factories.
- **Token Passing in PROFIBUS** is the reason it remains one of the most deployed fieldbuses worldwide (over **50 million** nodes shipped).
- **CSMA/BA (Bitwise Arbitration)** of **CAN bus** is uniquely suited to **automotive** networks (cars, trucks) and is the key to **DeviceNet** in industrial automation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section is the **derivation backbone** of Module 3. Every KTU 14-mark question on MAC analysis will have at least one sub-part requiring the following derivations. Use the same logical chain in your answer script.

## 3.1 Pure Aloha — Throughput Derivation

### Assumptions
1. Infinite number of users generating *new* frames according to a **Poisson process** with mean rate λ (frames per frame-time T).
2. The *total* traffic (new + retransmitted) offered per frame-time is **G** (offered load).
3. All frames have a **fixed duration of 1 unit** (T = 1).
4. A frame is *successfully* received if **no other frame overlaps it** by even one bit.

### Step 1 — Define the Vulnerable Period
A collision occurs if **any** other frame is sent during the period **[t₀ – 1, t₀ + 1]**, i.e., a window of length **2 frame-times**.

Therefore, the **vulnerable period = 2T = 2** (in normalised units).

### Step 2 — Probability of k Arrivals in Vulnerable Period
Since arrivals are Poisson with rate G per frame-time, the probability of *k* arrivals in a window of length 2T is:

$$
P(k) = \frac{(2G)^k \cdot e^{-2G}}{k!}
$$

### Step 3 — Probability of Zero Other Arrivals (Successful Transmission)
The frame succeeds only if **k = 0** other frames arrive in the vulnerable window:

$$
P(\text{success}) = P(k=0) = \frac{(2G)^0 \cdot e^{-2G}}{0!} = e^{-2G}
$$

### Step 4 — Throughput Definition
Throughput **S** = (rate of frame generation) × (probability of success per frame):

$$
S = G \cdot P(\text{success})
$$

Substituting:

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-2G}
$$

### Step 5 — Maximise S
Take derivative with respect to G and equate to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dS}{dG} &= \frac{d}{dG}\left[G \cdot e^{-2G}\right] \\
&= e^{-2G} + G \cdot (-2) \cdot e^{-2G} \\
&= e^{-2G} \left(1 - 2G\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting the derivative to zero:

$$
e^{-2G}(1 - 2G) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad G = \frac{1}{2}
$$

### Step 6 — Maximum Throughput
Substitute G = 0.5 back into the throughput equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot e^{-2(1/2)} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot e^{-1} \\
&= \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.1839
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;S_{max}^{\text{Pure Aloha}} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184 \;\;(18.4\%)\;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Slotted Aloha — Throughput Derivation

### Key Difference
Time is divided into **discrete slots of size T**. A station may only transmit at the **beginning of a slot**. This **halves** the vulnerable period from 2T to T.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{success}) &= e^{-G} \quad \text{(since vulnerable period = T, not 2T)} \\
S &= G \cdot e^{-G} \quad \text{(1)} \\
\frac{dS}{dG} &= e^{-G} - G \cdot e^{-G} = e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0 \quad \text{(2)} \\
\Rightarrow G &= 1 \\
S_{max} &= 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;S_{max}^{\text{Slotted Aloha}} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368 \;\;(36.8\%)\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Slotted Aloha **exactly doubles** the capacity of Pure Aloha (0.368 vs 0.184). The trade-off is the requirement for **synchronisation** among all stations, which adds hardware cost.

---

## 3.3 CSMA (Non-Persistent) — Throughput Sketch

For **non-persistent CSMA**, the station *senses the channel*; if **idle**, transmits; if **busy**, waits a *random* time and retries. The analysis uses the dimensionless parameter **a = τ / T** (propagation-to-transmission ratio).

The standard textbook result (Kleinrock, Tobagi):

$$
S = \frac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1+2a) + e^{-aG}}
$$

### Observations
- When **a → 0** (very small propagation delay), $S \rightarrow \dfrac{G}{G+1}$, maxing near **1.0** (perfect channel).
- When **a → 1**, performance degrades.
- For long cables (large a), CSMA loses its advantage.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — All MAC Schemes at a Glance

| Parameter | Pure Aloha | Slotted Aloha | CSMA | CSMA/CD | Token Passing | Master–Slave |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S<sub>max</sub>** | 0.184 | 0.368 | ≈ 1 (a→0) | ≈ 1 (a→0) | ≈ 1 (deterministic) | ≈ 1 (deterministic) |
| **Determinism** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Collision possible?** | Yes | Yes (slot-aligned) | Reduced | Detected & aborted | No (token-based) | No |
| **Synchronisation needed?** | No | Yes (slots) | No | No | Yes (logical ring) | No |
| **Industrial example** | Legacy radio | Early satellite | Wireless sensor | **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)** | **PROFIBUS, MAP** | **Modbus, AS-i** |
| **Worst-case delay** | Unbounded | Unbounded | Bounded* | Bounded* | **Bounded = N·T<sub>token</sub>** | **Bounded = N·T<sub>poll</sub>** |

*\*Bounded only under light/medium load.*

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem:** A Pure Aloha channel carries **G = 0.3** frames per frame-time. Calculate (a) throughput, (b) probability of collision-free transmission.

**Solution:**

(a) $$S = G \cdot e^{-2G} = 0.3 \cdot e^{-0.6} = 0.3 \cdot 0.5488 \approx 0.1646$$

(b) $$P(\text{no collision}) = e^{-2G} = e^{-0.6} \approx 0.5488 \;\;(54.88\%)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Mistake:** Students often write `S = G·G` or `S = e^{-2G}` alone. Always show **all three** steps: (i) define G, (ii) write the formula, (iii) substitute and compute. Partial credit is given per step.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams map the **logical flow** of the three most important bus access methods examined in KTU Module 3. Render-ready and compliant with the Alpha-Rule for node IDs.

## 4.1 Token Passing — Logical Ring over Physical Bus

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Station 1 - Token Holder] -- token --> B[Station 2]
    B -- token --> C[Station 3]
    C -- token --> D[Station 4 - Master]
    D -- token --> E[Station 5]
    E -- token --> A
    A -. has token: transmits data frame .-> BUS[(Shared Physical Bus)]
    B -. monitors bus .-> BUS
    C -. monitors bus .-> BUS
    D -. monitors bus .-> BUS
    E -. monitors bus .-> BUS
```

**Operational Logic:**
1. Only the *current token holder* is permitted to transmit.
2. After transmitting (or after THT expires), the station **passes the token** to its *logical successor*.
3. If the token is lost, an **initialisation procedure** (claim-token) elects a new token holder.
4. Stations not holding the token are in *receive-only* mode.

## 4.2 CSMA/CD — Collision Detection Flow (Ethernet IEEE 802.3)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Station has new frame]) --> Sense1{Sense carrier}
    Sense1 -- Channel idle --> Tx[Start transmission]
    Sense1 -- Channel busy --> Backoff1[Wait random backoff]
    Backoff1 --> Sense1
    Tx --> Monitor{Collision detected?}
    Monitor -- No --> Done([Frame sent successfully])
    Monitor -- Yes --> Jam[Send jam signal]
    Jam --> Backoff2[Binary exponential backoff]
    Backoff2 --> Attempt{Attempts < 16?}
    Attempt -- Yes --> Sense1
    Attempt -- No --> Fail([Frame dropped - error to upper layer])
```

**Operational Logic:**
- Collision must be detected within **2 · τ** (worst-case round-trip propagation).
- The 32-bit **jam signal** ensures all stations recognise the collision.
- **Binary exponential backoff**: kth collision chooses a random slot among 2<sup>k</sup> slots (capped at 2<sup>10</sup>).

## 4.3 Master–Slave / Polling (e.g., Modbus RTU, PROFIBUS-DP slave section)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Master
    participant S1 as Slave 1
    participant S2 as Slave 2
    participant S3 as Slave 3
    M->>S1: Poll request (function code 0x03)
    S1-->>M: Response (registers)
    M->>S2: Poll request
    S2-->>M: Response
    M->>S3: Poll request
    S3-->>M: No response / timeout
    M->>S1: Next cycle begins
```

## 4.4 Bitwise Arbitration (CSMA/BA) — CAN Bus

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Station A - ID 0b1100] -- dominant bit --> BUS[(CAN Bus Wired-AND)]
    B[Station B - ID 0b1010] -- recessive bit --> BUS
    C[Station C - ID 0b1101] -- dominant bit --> BUS
    BUS -- arbitration result --> A
    B -- lost arbitration - backs off --> BX[Station B waits]
    A -- won arbitration - continues transmitting --> ADone[Station A transmits frame]
```

**Engineering Insight:** In CAN, a *dominant* (0) bit **overwrites** a *recessive* (1) bit on the bus. The station transmitting the *lowest* ID wins, ensuring **priority-based, collision-free arbitration** — a key reason CAN is dominant in **automotive** and **factory automation**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define *bus access method*. With a neat diagram, explain the operation of *CSMA/CD* in Ethernet.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A bus access method is the set of rules that determine how multiple stations share a common transmission medium (Kleinrock 1975). In **CSMA/CD** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection), each station:
1. **Senses** the carrier to check whether the bus is idle.
2. If **idle**, begins transmitting.
3. **Monitors** the signal during transmission; if a collision is detected (voltage swing > threshold), the station **aborts**, sends a 32-bit *jam signal*, and waits a **random backoff** (binary exponential: random slot in [0, 2<sup>k</sup>–1]).
4. Retries up to 16 times before declaring failure.

Collision detection must occur within **2 · τ** (worst-case round-trip propagation delay) — this limits the **maximum cable length** for a given frame size and bit rate. *(Full mark answer: 3-step answer with 2τ comment.)*

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Compare *Token Ring* and *Token Bus* access methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Token Ring (IEEE 802.5) | Token Bus (IEEE 802.4) |
|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Physical + logical ring | Physical **bus**, logical **ring** |
| **Token holder** | Next physical neighbour | Next in *logical address order* |
| **Fault tolerance** | Poor (single break disables ring) | Better (physical bus tolerates breaks; logical ring reconfigured) |
| **Industrial use** | IBM Token Ring (legacy LAN) | **MAP (Manufacturing Automation Protocol)** |
| **Speed (typical)** | 4 / 16 Mbps | 1 / 5 / 10 Mbps |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, **ESE Module questions have internal choice**: answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B in full. Each choice has two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### ⭐ Choice A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

#### Q A (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the *Token Passing* bus access method in detail. How does *PROFIBUS* combine token passing with master–slave polling? Illustrate with a diagram.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Token Passing is a *deterministic* MAC protocol in which a special control frame (the *token*) circulates among stations in a *logical ring*. Only the station holding the token has the right to transmit.

**Operation (2 marks):**
- The token is a small frame (3 bytes in IEEE 802.4) that grants transmission rights.
- The token holder may transmit data frames for a bounded period called the **Token Holding Time (THT)**.
- After THT expiry (or completion of data), the station passes the token to its *logical successor* (next MAC address).
- If a station is silent (no data), it forwards the token in ≤ few microseconds.
- **Lost-token recovery:** A designated *bus monitor* or *claim-token* procedure re-generates a token if none is heard within a timeout.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- **Bounded delay** = N × (T<sub>token</sub> + T<sub>msg</sub>).
- **No collisions** (deterministic).
- **Fair** (rotating priority).

**PROFIBUS Hybrid (2 marks):**
**PROFIBUS-DP** uses a *two-tier* access scheme:
- **Tier 1 (Token):** Active stations (masters) exchange a token in a *logical ring*. Only the token-holding master may initiate communication.
- **Tier 2 (Master–Slave):** The token-holding master **polls** its assigned passive stations (slaves — sensors, actuators) using **SD2 (Send/Request Data with Reply)** frames.
- Slaves never initiate communication; they respond only when polled.
- This **hybrid** gives PROFIBUS both **determinism** (token) and **simplicity** (slave devices are cheap).

**Diagram (1 mark):** Include the PROFIBUS layered access — token ring among masters, master–slave star from each master to its slaves.

---

#### Q A (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the throughput of *Pure Aloha*. State the assumptions and find the maximum throughput value.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Infinite population, Poisson arrivals, fixed frame length, infinite buffer.

**Derivation (5 marks):** [Use the step-by-step derivation from Section 3.1 above]
- Vulnerable period = 2T.
- $$P(\text{success}) = e^{-2G}$$
- $$S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$$
- $$\frac{dS}{dG} = 0 \Rightarrow G = 0.5$$
- $$S_{max} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Maximum channel utilisation in Pure Aloha is only **18.4 %** — a poor efficiency that motivated **Slotted Aloha**, **CSMA**, and **CSMA/CD**.

---

### ⭐ Choice B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Q B (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the *Master–Slave* (polling) bus access method. Discuss its advantages and limitations with reference to *Modbus RTU* and *PROFIBUS-DP*.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** In Master–Slave access, a single **master** station controls all communication. Slaves (sensors, actuators, drives) *never transmit spontaneously*; they reply only when polled.

**Operation (2 marks):**
- Master sends a *request frame* (function code + address) to a specific slave.
- Slave *processes* and *replies* with a *response frame* (data + checksum).
- After response (or timeout), master moves to the next slave.
- Cycle repeats; the worst-case latency is **N × t<sub>poll</sub>**, where N is the number of slaves.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- **Simple hardware** for slaves (no collision logic, no token logic).
- **Deterministic** — every slave is queried in a known order.
- **Predictable cycle time** for control loops.
- **Easy diagnostics** — master logs all slave responses / timeouts.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- **Single point of failure** — master crash halts the whole bus.
- **Bandwidth waste** — slaves with no data still get polled.
- **Latency scales with N** — slow for many slaves.
- **Master is a bottleneck** for large systems.

**Modbus RTU (0.5 mark):** Pure master–slave on RS-485, 9.6–115.2 kbps, function codes for read/write registers, used in PLCs, energy meters, VFDs.
**PROFIBUS-DP slave section (0.5 mark):** Slaves (DP-Slaves) are passive; the active DP-Master polls them cyclically with SD2 frames. Used for fast I/O updates (≤ 1 ms cycle).

---

#### Q B (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare and contrast *Pure Aloha*, *Slotted Aloha*, and *CSMA*. Derive the throughput equation for Slotted Aloha and compare its maximum throughput with Pure Aloha.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Pure Aloha | Slotted Aloha | CSMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Synchronisation | None | Required (slots) | None |
| Vulnerable period | 2T | T | < T (carrier sensed) |
| Throughput max | 1/(2e) ≈ 0.184 | 1/e ≈ 0.368 | Approaches 1 (small a) |
| Collision handling | Random backoff | Random backoff | Sense-then-talk |
| Used in | ALOHAnet (1971) | Satellite uplink | Ethernet, Wi-Fi |

**Slotted Aloha Derivation (4 marks):** [Use Section 3.2 derivation]
- Vulnerable period = T (not 2T)
- $$S = G \cdot e^{-G}$$
- $$S_{max} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368 \text{ at } G = 1$$

**Comparison (1 mark):**
$$\frac{S_{max}^{\text{Slotted}}}{S_{max}^{\text{Pure}}} = \frac{1/e}{1/(2e)} = 2$$

Slotted Aloha achieves **exactly double** the maximum throughput of Pure Aloha. CSMA (with small a) goes far beyond both, approaching 100 % efficiency.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on MAC questions (PEICT747):**
> 1. **Confusing τ and t<sub>tx</sub>.** Always explicitly *define* them. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark if you use a formula like $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ without explaining that $2G$ comes from the **2T vulnerable period**.
> 2. **Skipping the "G = 0.5" optimisation step.** Showing only the final $S_{max} = 1/(2e)$ without the differentiation is **incomplete** (loses 2 marks in derivations).
> 3. **Writing CSMA/CD for Token Bus.** Know the *protocol name → method* mapping cold:
>    - Ethernet → CSMA/CD
>    - PROFIBUS → Token + Master–Slave
>    - CAN → CSMA/BA (bitwise arbitration)
>    - Wi-Fi → CSMA/CA
> 4. **Forgetting units.** State *frames per frame-time* for S and G; examiners do check.
> 5. **No diagram for 14-markers.** Always include a labelled block diagram / flow chart in MAC questions — missing diagram costs at least 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 – Bus Access Method):**

- [x] A **bus access method** is a MAC-layer rule set; it sits in the **lower half of Data Link Layer** (OSI Layer 2).
- [x] **Three families:** Contention-based (Aloha, CSMA, CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA), Controlled (Token Ring, Token Bus, Token in PROFIBUS), Scheduled (TDMA, FDMA, Master–Slave).
- [x] **Pure Aloha:** $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$, $S_{max} = 1/(2e) \approx 0.184$ at $G = 0.5$.
- [x] **Slotted Aloha:** $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$, $S_{max} = 1/e \approx 0.368$ at $G = 1$ — **double** the Pure Aloha capacity.
- [x] **CSMA/CD (Ethernet):** Carrier sense + collision detection + binary exponential backoff; collision window = $2\tau$.
- [x] **CSMA/CA (Wi-Fi, WIA):** RTS/CTS handshake avoids hidden terminal problem.
- [x] **CSMA/BA (CAN bus):** Wired-AND dominant bit arbitration; lowest ID wins — priority-based, collision-free.
- [x] **Token Ring (IEEE 802.5):** Physical + logical ring; passes token to next physical neighbour.
- [x] **Token Bus (IEEE 802.4):** Physical bus + logical ring; used in **MAP**.
- [x] **PROFIBUS** is a *hybrid*: **token passing among masters + master–slave polling of slaves**.
- [x] **Master–Slave (Modbus, AS-i):** Simple, deterministic, single-master; slaves never initiate.
- [x] **Worst-case token delay** = $N \times (T_{token} + T_{msg})$ — crucial for real-time guarantees.
- [x] **Parameter a** = $\tau / t_{tx}$ is the key dimensionless ratio for CSMA analysis; performance → 100 % as $a \to 0$.
- [x] **Industrial determinism** is achieved by *controlled/scheduled* methods, not contention-based ones — this is the single most important industrial-networks takeaway.
- [x] **PROFINET IRT** uses *reserved time slots* within Ethernet to add determinism to CSMA/CD.

> **End of Module 3 — Bus Access Method Notes** ✦
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Block Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Block Model in Industrial Networks — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Model (Industry Standard Definition)**
>
> The **Block Model** is a software-based architectural framework used in industrial communication networks (notably **Foundation Fieldbus (FF)**, **PROFIBUS PA**, and **HART**) to organize, describe, and execute the control and communication functions of a field device as a collection of standardized, reusable, and interoperable software objects called **Blocks**. Each block encapsulates a specific function (input, output, control, transducer, resource management, display, etc.) and exposes its parameters and behavior through a uniform interface, allowing devices from different vendors to interoperate seamlessly within a distributed control system (DCS).

In essence, the block model is the **object-oriented functional skeleton** that transforms a dumb sensor or actuator into a *smart*, *self-describing*, and *network-aware* device.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Standard)

| Term | Meaning in Block Model Context |
|---|---|
| **Block** | A software object encapsulating a specific industrial function |
| **Parameter** | A readable/writable data item inside a block |
| **Link Master (LAS)** | The bus arbitrator in FF that schedules block execution |
| **Device Description (DD)** | Vendor-supplied description of block structure and behavior |
| **CFF (Common File Format)** | Engineering representation of block topology offline |
| **VFD (Virtual Field Device)** | Logical grouping of blocks within a single physical device |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Office Building

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — A Smart Office Building**
>
> Imagine a modern office building as a **field device** on an industrial network.
>
> - **Resource Block** = the building's *utility meter room* (tells you the building's identity, vendor, power supply, total uptime).
> - **Transducer Block** = the *sensors and motors physically wired* to windows, doors, AC vents (interface to the physical world).
> - **Function Block (e.g., PID)** = the *thermostat controller* that reads a temperature value and decides how hard the AC should run.
> - **Output Block** = the *command sent* to the AC compressor.
> - **Communication Stack / Block** = the *Wi-Fi router* carrying the data to the cloud.
> - **Display Block** = the *lobby display screen* showing temperature, humidity, and air quality.
>
> Just as a tenant doesn't need to know *how* the thermostat computes the cooling — only its inputs, outputs, and tuning knobs — the engineer configuring a Foundation Fieldbus device interacts with **blocks, not raw hardware registers**. This abstraction is the genius of the block model.

## 1.4 Why the Block Model Matters (KTU Examination Weightage)

- **Interoperability**: A PID block from Vendor A can control a valve from Vendor B, provided both are FF-compliant.
- **Reusability**: The same PID function block can be used in flow, level, and temperature loops.
- **Configurability**: Engineers configure loops *graphically* in tools like **DeltaV**, **PCS 7**, or **FieldCare**.
- **Distributed Control**: Control loops are executed in the field device itself, not a central PLC — enabling **true field-level autonomy**.

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently asks the classification of blocks and the role of each block in 3-mark and 7-mark questions. Master the **six standard block types** below.

## 1.5 The Six Standard Block Types (The Heart of the Topic)

1. **Resource Block (RB)** — Device-level health, identity, and diagnostics.
2. **Transducer Block (TB)** — Couples the function block to the physical I/O hardware.
3. **Function Block (FB)** — Performs the actual control algorithm (AI, AO, PID, etc.).
4. **Communication Block** — Manages messaging between blocks across the network.
5. **Display Block (DB)** — Formats data for local HMI/display.
6. **Diagnostics / Alert Block** — Handles exception reporting and alarms.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Architecture in Foundation Fieldbus (FF)

The block model in FF is defined by the **ISA-88 / IEC 61804** standard. Each physical field device contains **one Resource Block**, **one or more Transducer Blocks**, and **one or more Function Blocks**, all interconnected via a **Virtual Field Device (VFD)** layer.

### 2.1.1 Structural Hierarchy

```
Physical Device
  ├── Virtual Field Device 1 (VFD1)
  │     ├── Resource Block (RB)
  │     ├── Transducer Block 1 (TB1) ── Physical Sensor
  │     ├── Transducer Block 2 (TB2) ── Physical Actuator
  │     ├── Function Block 1 (FB1)  ── AI (Analog Input)
  │     ├── Function Block 2 (FB2)  ── PID Controller
  │     ├── Function Block 3 (FB3)  ── AO (Analog Output)
  │     └── Display Block (DB)
  └── Virtual Field Device 2 (VFD2)  [optional, for secondary function set]
```

## 2.2 Detailed Block Roles

### 2.2.1 Resource Block (RB)
- Contains **device-wide** parameters: manufacturer ID, device serial, DD revision, **mode** (Auto, Manual, Out-of-Service), execution time, diagnostics.
- **Always exactly one per physical device**.
- Manages resource allocation (CPU time, memory).

### 2.2.2 Transducer Block (TB)
- Acts as the **hardware abstraction layer** between a function block and the physical I/O (sensor or actuator).
- Performs **signal conditioning**: linearization, temperature compensation, range scaling (e.g., 4–20 mA → 0–100 °C).
- **Decouples the function block from the actual sensor technology**.

### 2.2.3 Function Block (FB)
- The **computational heart** of the device.
- Standardized types defined by FF: **AI, AO, DI, DO, PID, CSEL (Control Selector), SPLT (Splitter), RAMP, TIMER, ARTH (Arithmetic), CHAR (Signal Characterizer), INTG (Integrator), ISEL (Input Selector), ML (Manual Loader)**.
- Each FB has:
  - **Inputs** (e.g., SP, PV for PID)
  - **Outputs** (e.g., OUT for PID)
  - **Parameters** (e.g., gain `K`, integral time `T_i`, derivative time `T_d`)
  - **Modes** (Auto/Manual/Cas/RCas/ROut/OOS)
  - **Alarm limits** (HI_HI, HI, LO, LO_LO)

### 2.2.4 Communication Block
- Manages the **Virtual Communication Relationship (VCR)** between blocks.
- Handles **Publisher/Subscriber**, **Client/Server**, and **Source/Sink** messaging.

### 2.2.5 Display Block
- Optional; formats data for **on-board LCD** or local HMI.
- Can show engineering units, scaled values, and diagnostic messages.

### 2.2.6 Diagnostic / Alert Block
- Handles **event notification** and **alarm reporting** over the bus.
- Implements the FF alert reporting mechanism (analog alert, discrete alert, update alert).

## 2.3 Function Block Execution Model (The "Macro Cycle")

The **Link Active Scheduler (LAS)** in a Foundation Fieldbus segment controls when each block executes. The execution is divided into a **macro cycle** of fixed duration $T_{macro}$:

$$
T_{macro} = T_{scheduled} + T_{unscheduled} + T_{idle}
$$

Where:
- $T_{scheduled}$: time reserved for cyclic, deterministic block-to-block communication
- $T_{unscheduled}$: time for acyclic, on-demand messaging
- $T_{idle}$: idle time on the bus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**
> The block execution order and communication schedule are stored in a **Schedule** built using **Function Block Application Process (FBAP)** tools. The LAS uses this schedule to trigger block execution deterministically.

## 2.4 The Standard PID Function Block (Most Exam-Relevant)

The PID block implements the **incremental (velocity) PID algorithm** defined by ISA and FF:

$$
\Delta u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) - e(t - \Delta t) + \frac{\Delta t}{T_i} e(t) + \frac{T_d}{\Delta t} \left( e(t) - 2e(t - \Delta t) + e(t - 2\Delta t) \right) \right]
$$

Where:
- $K_c$: Controller gain (unitless)
- $T_i$: Integral time (seconds)
- $T_d$: Derivative time (seconds)
- $\Delta t$: Block execution period (seconds)
- $e(t) = SP - PV$: Error signal

The incremental output is added to the previous actuator value:

$$
u(t) = u(t - \Delta t) + \Delta u(t)
$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> Use this table for last-minute revision. Memorize the **units** of every parameter — examiners love to test them.

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_{macro}$ | Macro cycle period | seconds | Typically **0.25 s – 10 s** |
| $\Delta t$ | Block execution interval | seconds | $\Delta t \geq 0.01$ s |
| $K_c$ | Proportional gain | dimensionless | $0 \leq K_c \leq 100$ (typical) |
| $T_i$ | Integral (reset) time | seconds | $T_i \to \infty$ disables I-term |
| $T_d$ | Derivative time | seconds | $T_d = 0$ disables D-term |
| $SP$ | Setpoint | EU (engineering units) | Bounded by `SP_HI`, `SP_LO` |
| $PV$ | Process variable | EU | Filtered by `PV_FTIME` |
| $OUT$ | Block output | % or EU | Scaled **0–100 %** or engineering range |
| $e(t)$ | Control error | EU | $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$ |
| `GAIN` | FF parameter | $\text{EU}^{-1}$ | Inverse of proportional band |
| `BIAS` | FF parameter | EU | Bias added to OUT |

### 2.5.1 Mode & Status Byte Encoding

| Mode | Bit Pattern | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **OOS** (Out-of-Service) | `0000` | Block bypassed, output frozen |
| **Man** (Manual) | `0001` | Output set manually by user |
| **Auto** | `0010` | Automatic closed-loop control |
| **Cas** (Cascade) | `0011` | SP supplied by upstream PID |
| **RCas** (Remote Cascade) | `0100` | SP supplied by remote DCS host |
| **ROut** (Remote Output) | `0101` | OUT supplied by remote host |
| **IMan** (Initialization Man) | `0110` | Initialization hand-off state |
| **LO** (Local Override) | `0111` | Local backup takes over |
| **ROut + Auto** | mixed | Transition states |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The block model is the **de-facto industry standard** for:
- **Oil & Gas**: Refinery DCS using Foundation Fieldbus H1 segments.
- **Pharmaceuticals**: FDA-validated batch control with PROFIBUS PA devices.
- **Power Plants**: Turbine governor loops using FF-HSE + FF-H1 architecture.
- **Water Treatment**: Long-haul instrumentation with FF wireless backhaul.

It dramatically reduces **engineering hours** — a 50-loop project that would have taken weeks of PLC programming can be commissioned in days by simply linking function blocks on a graph.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Classical PID to FF Incremental PID

The **classical continuous-time PID** is:

$$
u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]
$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate** to obtain a velocity-form (avoids integral windup on each step):

$$
\frac{du(t)}{dt} = K_c \left[ \frac{de(t)}{dt} + \frac{e(t)}{T_i} + T_d \frac{d^2 e(t)}{dt^2} \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Discretize** with execution period $\Delta t$. Apply the **backward-difference approximation**:

$$
\frac{de(t)}{dt} \approx \frac{e(t) - e(t - \Delta t)}{\Delta t}
$$

$$
\frac{d^2 e(t)}{dt^2} \approx \frac{e(t) - 2e(t - \Delta t) + e(t - 2\Delta t)}{\Delta t^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify** the velocity equation into the **incremental (velocity) form**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta u(t)}{\Delta t} &= K_c \left[ \frac{e(t) - e(t-\Delta t)}{\Delta t} + \frac{e(t)}{T_i} + T_d \cdot \frac{e(t) - 2e(t-\Delta t) + e(t-2\Delta t)}{\Delta t^2} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply through by $\Delta t$** to obtain the incremental output change $\Delta u(t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta u(t) = K_c \Bigg[ \big(e(t) - e(t-\Delta t)\big) + \frac{\Delta t}{T_i} e(t) + \frac{T_d}{\Delta t} \big(e(t) - 2e(t-\Delta t) + e(t-2\Delta t)\big) \Bigg]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Explanation**:
> - The first bracketed term $(e(t) - e(t-\Delta t))$ is the **proportional increment** in error.
> - The second term $\dfrac{\Delta t}{T_i} e(t)$ accumulates the **integral action** over one cycle.
> - The third term $\dfrac{T_d}{\Delta t}(\dots)$ approximates the **second derivative** of error (derivative kick is avoided by acting on PV, not SP).

**Step 5 — Compute the new output** by adding $\Delta u(t)$ to the previous output (bump-less transfer):

$$
u(t) = u(t - \Delta t) + \Delta u(t)
$$

**Step 6 — Apply anti-windup clamp** to enforce actuator limits $OUT_{LO} \leq u(t) \leq OUT_{HI}$:

$$
u(t) = \text{clamp}\left(u(t - \Delta t) + \Delta u(t),\ OUT_{LO},\ OUT_{HI}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently give a numerical problem: *"Given $K_c = 2$, $T_i = 10$ s, $T_d = 1$ s, $\Delta t = 1$ s, and three consecutive error values, compute the incremental output."* Memorize the form of $\Delta u(t)$ above.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Incremental PID Calculation

**Given** (KTU typical problem):
- $K_c = 2.0$, $T_i = 10$ s, $T_d = 1$ s, $\Delta t = 1$ s
- $e(t-2\Delta t) = 0.6$ EU, $e(t-\Delta t) = 0.3$ EU, $e(t) = 0.1$ EU
- $u(t - \Delta t) = 35\%$, $OUT_{LO} = 0\%$, $OUT_{HI} = 100\%$

**Compute the three incremental components:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta u_P(t) &= K_c \cdot (e(t) - e(t - \Delta t)) = 2.0 \cdot (0.1 - 0.3) = -0.4 \\
\Delta u_I(t) &= K_c \cdot \frac{\Delta t}{T_i} \cdot e(t) = 2.0 \cdot \frac{1}{10} \cdot 0.1 = 0.02 \\
\Delta u_D(t) &= K_c \cdot \frac{T_d}{\Delta t} \cdot (e(t) - 2e(t-\Delta t) + e(t-2\Delta t)) \\
&= 2.0 \cdot \frac{1}{1} \cdot (0.1 - 0.6 + 0.6) = 2.0 \cdot 0.1 = 0.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total incremental change:**

$$
\Delta u(t) = -0.4 + 0.02 + 0.2 = -0.18 \text{ EU}
$$

**New output (after clamping):**

$$
u(t) = 35\% + (-0.18\% \text{ after EU-to-}\% \text{ scaling, say } K=1) \approx 34.82\%
$$

Since $0\% \leq 34.82\% \leq 100\%$, no clamping is required.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Block Parameter Categories

Every block in FF has its parameters classified into the following standardized categories:

| Category | Purpose | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Setpoint / process values from upstream | `SP`, `PV`, `IN`, `CAS_IN` |
| **Output** | Computed values sent downstream | `OUT`, `BKCAL_OUT`, `RCAS_OUT` |
| **Configuration** | Tuning / static config | `GAIN`, `RESET`, `DERIVATIVE`, `SP_HI`, `SP_LO` |
| **Mode / Status** | Operational state | `MODE_BLK`, `BLOCK_ERR` |
| **Alarm** | Limit parameters | `HI_HI_LIM`, `HI_LIM`, `LO_LIM`, `LO_LO_LIM` |
| **HMI / View** | Display-only values | `OUT_SCALE`, `PV_FTIME` |
| **Diagnostic** | Health monitoring | `BLOCK_ERR`, `XD_ERROR` |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Device Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PD["Physical Device (Smart Valve Positioner)"]
        subgraph VFD["Virtual Field Device VFD1"]
            RB["Resource Block RB - device ID, mode, diagnostics"]
            TB1["Transducer Block TB1 - valve position sensor interface"]
            TB2["Transducer Block TB2 - pneumatic actuator drive"]
            FB1["Function Block FB1 - AI Analog Input valve stem position"]
            FB2["Function Block FB2 - PID setpoint tracking controller"]
            FB3["Function Block FB3 - AO Analog Output to actuator"]
            DB["Display Block DB - LCD on device body"]
            CB["Communication Block CB - VCR publisher subscriber"]
        end
    end

    PV["Physical Sensor stem position"] --> TB1
    TB1 --> FB1
    FB1 --> FB2
    FB2 --> FB3
    FB3 --> TB2
    TB2 --> ACT["Physical Actuator pneumatic diaphragm"]

    RB -. controls .- VFD
    CB -. schedules .-> FB1
    CB -. schedules .-> FB2
    CB -. schedules .-> FB3
    DB -. reads .- FB2
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Control Using Function Blocks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP["Setpoint SP from DCS"] --> PID[PID Function Block]
    AI[AI Function Block in Transmitter] --> PID
    PID --> AO[AO Function Block in Valve Positioner]
    AO --> VALVE["Final Control Element Valve"]
    VALVE --> PROC["Process Vessel"]
    PROC --> SENSOR["Temperature Transmitter Sensor"]
    SENSOR --> AI
    BKCAL["BKCAL_OUT Bump-less back-calculation"] --> SP
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Macro Cycle Execution by LAS

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant LAS as Link Active Scheduler
    participant FB1 as AI Block Transmitter
    participant FB2 as PID Block Controller
    participant FB3 as AO Block Valve
    participant HMI as Operator Workstation

    Note over LAS: Start of Macro Cycle T_macro
    LAS->>FB1: Scheduled trigger CD
    FB1->>FB2: Publisher send PV
    LAS->>FB2: Scheduled trigger CD
    FB2->>FB3: Publisher send OUT
    LAS->>FB3: Scheduled trigger CD
    FB3-->>HMI: Client Server read response unscheduled
    LAS->>HMI: Pass Token for unscheduled communication
    HMI->>FB2: Client read MODE_BLK
    HMI-->>LAS: Return token
    Note over LAS: End of Macro Cycle next cycle repeats
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Block Parameter Categories

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Function Block Parameters] --> CAT1["Input Parameters: SP, PV, IN"]
    P --> CAT2["Output Parameters: OUT, BKCAL_OUT"]
    P --> CAT3["Configuration: GAIN, RESET, DERIVATIVE, OUT_SCALE"]
    P --> CAT4["Mode Status: MODE_BLK, BLOCK_ERR"]
    P --> CAT5["Alarms: HI_HI_LIM, HI_LIM, LO_LIM, LO_LO_LIM"]
    P --> CAT6["Diagnostics: XD_ERROR, UPDATE_EVT"]
```
