| 6 | **Power Limitation (per IEC 60079-11)** | $P_o \le P_i$ AND $V_o \le V_i$ AND $I_o \le I_i$ | V, A, W | Barrier-to-device matching |
| 7 | **MESG (Maximum Experimental Safe Gap)** | $MESG = f(gas\,group)$ | mm | Flameproof (Ex d) |
| 8 | **MIC (Minimum Igniting Current)** | $MIC = f(gas\,group)$ | ratio | Intrinsic Safety |
| 9 | **Cable Parameter Check (Ex i)** | $C_c \le C_a - C_i$ AND $L_c \le L_a - L_i$ | F, H | Cable selection |
| 10 | **Effective Power (Hot-Swap Fieldbus)** | $P_{eff} = \sqrt{P_{max}^2 + P_{transient}^2}$ | W | Segment couplers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** The most frequently asked formula is the **barrier-to-device compatibility check**: $V_o \le V_i$, $I_o \le I_i$, $P_o \le P_i$. Memorize this **as a triplet**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas Refineries (Kochi, BPCL, IOCL):** PROFIBUS-PA segment couplers in Zone 1.
- **Pharmaceutical Plants (Cochin Special Economic Zone):** HART transmitters in Zone 1 for solvent reactors.
- **Paint Manufacturing (Industrial belts):** WirelessHART in Zone 1 with Ex i battery packs.
- **Coal Handling Plants (NTPC, Tata Power):** FOUNDATION Fieldbus for dust environments (Zone 21).
- **Aircraft Refueling Depots:** Ethernet-APL (Advanced Physical Layer) with 10BASE-T1L for intrinsically safe high-speed data in Zone 0.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ethernet-APL (IEEE / IEC 61850-3, IEC 60079-47)** is the **latest KTU-relevant evolution** — enabling **2-wire, intrinsically safe, 10 Mbit/s Ethernet** to field devices, replacing legacy 4–20 mA + HART architectures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Intrinsic Safety Energy-Limitation Derivation

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Maximum Permissible Power ($P_{max}$)

The minimum ignition energy of the most easily ignitable gas mixture (hydrogen-air or carbon disulfide-air) under standard test conditions is approximately:

$$E_{min} \approx 20\,\mu J \quad \text{(for Group IIC gases)}$$

The available ignition energy in a resistive circuit at the end of a cable is given by:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{available} &= \int_{0}^{t_{arc}} v(t)\cdot i(t)\,dt \\[4pt]
&= \int_{0}^{t_{arc}} \frac{V^2}{R}\,dt \quad \text{(purely resistive fault)}
\end{aligned}$$

For a fault that lasts $t_{arc}$, and considering a worst-case DC source with internal resistance $R_i$ and cable resistance $R_c$:

$$E_{available} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{R_i + R_c}\cdot t_{arc}$$

To guarantee $E_{available} \le E_{min}$, the **IEC 60079-11 standard** empirically prescribes:

$$P_{max} = V_{max}\cdot I_{max} \le 1.3\,W \quad \text{(for Group IIC, Level of Protection "ia")}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The empirical 1.3 W limit arises because, at the worst-case spark-test apparatus, the spark ignition energy remains below 20 µJ as long as the steady-state source power does not exceed 1.3 W.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Compliance Check Equations

Let:
- $V_o$ = open-circuit voltage of the barrier
- $I_o$ = short-circuit current of the barrier
- $P_o$ = maximum power of the barrier
- $V_i$, $I_i$, $P_i$ = maximum input ratings of the **field device** (apparatus)

**The compliance triplet must satisfy ALL three simultaneously:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_o &\le V_i \\
I_o &\le I_i \\
P_o &\le P_i
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** A field device is "safe" only if the barrier cannot deliver more voltage, current, or power than the device can safely absorb, even under fault conditions.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Barrier Selection for a Fieldbus PA Segment

### Problem Statement (KTU Typical)
A PROFIBUS-PA segment coupler has output ratings: $V_o = 24\,V$, $I_o = 250\,mA$, $P_o = 1.92\,W$. A pressure transmitter in Zone 1 has input ratings: $V_i = 30\,V$, $I_i = 300\,mA$, $P_i = 2.5\,W$. Verify the safety compliance for **Ex ib** installation.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compare voltages:**

$$V_o = 24\,V \quad \text{vs.} \quad V_i = 30\,V$$

$$24 \le 30 \quad \checkmark \text{(Voltage compliant)}$$

**Step 2 — Compare currents:**

$$I_o = 250\,mA \quad \text{vs.} \quad I_i = 300\,mA$$

$$250 \le 300 \quad \checkmark \text{(Current compliant)}$$

**Step 3 — Compare powers:**

$$P_o = V_o \cdot I_o = 24\,V \times 0.250\,A = 1.92\,W$$

$$P_i = 2.5\,W$$

$$1.92 \le 2.5 \quad \checkmark \text{(Power compliant)}$$

**Step 4 — Final verdict:** All three conditions are satisfied → The combination is **safe for Ex ib Zone 1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Avoidance:** Many students forget the **power check** and pass a barrier that exceeds the device's power rating. Always verify the **triplet**.

## 3.3 Cable Parameter Verification Derivation

For an intrinsically safe circuit, the **distributed capacitance and inductance of the cable** must be considered because at the **end of a long cable**, the energy stored in $C_c$ and $L_c$ can be released as a spark.

The total permissible cable parameters are:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{cable} &\le C_{a} - C_{i} \\
L_{cable} &\le L_{a} - L_{i}
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $C_a$, $L_a$ = maximum allowable external capacitance / inductance of the **associated apparatus** (barrier)
- $C_i$, $L_i$ = internal capacitance / inductance of the **field device**
- $C_{cable}$, $L_{cable}$ = distributed capacitance / inductance of the **cable**

### Worked Example

A barrier specifies $C_a = 250\,nF$, $L_a = 1.5\,mH$. A field device has $C_i = 5\,nF$, $L_i = 0.02\,mH$. The cable to be used has $80\,pF/m$ capacitance and $0.6\,\mu H/m$ inductance. Find the **maximum cable length**.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{cable, max} &= C_a - C_i \\
&= 250\,nF - 5\,nF \\
&= 245\,nF \\
&= 245 \times 10^{-9}\,F
\end{aligned}$$

Maximum length based on capacitance:

$$L_{cap} = \frac{C_{cable,max}}{C_{per\,meter}} = \frac{245 \times 10^{-9}}{80 \times 10^{-12}} = 3062.5\,m$$

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{cable, max} &= L_a - L_i \\
&= 1.5\,mH - 0.02\,mH \\
&= 1.48\,mH \\
&= 1.48 \times 10^{-3}\,H
\end{aligned}$$

Maximum length based on inductance:

$$L_{ind} = \frac{L_{cable,max}}{L_{per\,meter}} = \frac{1.48 \times 10^{-3}}{0.6 \times 10^{-6}} = 2466.67\,m$$

The **smaller** value governs (worst-case limit):

$$L_{max} = \min(3062.5,\,2466.67) = 2466.67\,m \approx 2467\,m$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This result is **critical for fieldbus network design** because it determines the maximum trunk length of a PROFIBUS-PA or FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1 segment in an Ex i environment.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Protection Concept vs. Zone

| Protection Concept | Symbol | Zone 0 | Zone 1 | Zone 2 | Zone 20 | Zone 21 | Zone 22 | Live Maintenance Allowed? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Intrinsic Safety "ia" | Ex ia | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | **Yes** |
| Intrinsic Safety "ib" | Ex ib | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | **Conditional** |
| Intrinsic Safety "ic" | Ex ic | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | **No** |
| Flameproof | Ex d | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | **No** |
| Increased Safety | Ex e | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | **No** |
| Pressurized | Ex p | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | **No** |
| Encapsulation | Ex m | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | **No** |
| Type "n" | Ex nA/nC/nL | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | **Conditional** |
| Dust by Enclosure | Ex tD | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | **No** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hazardous Area Classification — Logical Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Process Area] --> B{Substance Type?}
    B -->|Flammable Gas/Vapor| C[Gas Group Classification]
    B -->|Combustible Dust| D[Dust Group Classification]
    C --> C1{Gas Group?}
    C1 -->|C1 C2 C3| C1A[Sub-group IIA]
    C1 -->|Hydrogen-rich| C1B[Sub-group IIC]
    D --> D1{Dust Type?}
    D1 -->|Flying dust| D1A[Group IIIA]
    D1 -->|Conductive dust| D1B[Group IIIC]
    A --> Z{Release Grade?}
    Z -->|Continuous| Z0[Zone 0 or Zone 20]
    Z -->|Primary| Z1[Zone 1 or Zone 21]
    Z -->|Secondary| Z2[Zone 2 or Zone 22]
    Z0 --> P1[Allowed Protection: Ex ia only]
    Z1 --> P2[Allowed Protection: Ex ib, Ex d, Ex e, Ex p, Ex m]
    Z2 --> P3[Allowed Protection: All Zone 1 + Ex nA, Ex nC, Ex nL, Ex ic]
    P1 --> N1[Industrial Network: PROFIBUS-PA with Zener Barrier]
    P2 --> N2[Industrial Network: FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1 with Isolated Barrier]
    P3 --> N3[Industrial Network: WirelessHART or Ethernet-APL]
```

## 4.2 Intrinsic Safety Barrier — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SafeArea[Safe Area Host Controller] -->|Power + Signal Line| Barrier[Ex i Safety Barrier]
    Barrier --> F1[Fuse / Current Limiter]
    F1 --> Zener[Zener Diode Voltage Clamp]
    Zener --> Resistor[Series Resistor Current Limiter]
    Resistor --> HazAreaOutput[Output to Hazardous Zone]
    HazAreaOutput --> FieldDevice[Field Device Sensor or Actuator]
    FieldDevice --> GroundReturn[Return Path to Barrier Ground]
    GroundReturn --> PE[Plant Protective Earth]
    Barrier -.->|Fault Detection| Fault[Spark Energy Less Than 20 µJ]
```

## 4.3 Industrial Network Architecture in Zone 1 — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ControlRoom[Safe Area Control Room]
        DCS[DCS PLC Controller]
        Card[IS Input Output Card]
    end
    subgraph SafeSide[Safe Area]
        Barrier1[IS Barrier Segment Coupler]
        Barrier2[IS Barrier Segment Coupler]
    end
    subgraph Zone1[Hazardous Area Zone 1]
        Dev1[Pressure Transmitter PROFIBUS PA]
        Dev2[Temperature Transmitter FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1]
        Dev3[Valve Positioner HART]
        Dev4[Gas Analyzer Ethernet APL]
    end
    DCS --> Card
    Card --> Barrier1
    Card --> Barrier2
    Barrier1 -->|Trunk Cable| Dev1
    Barrier1 -->|Spur Cable| Dev2
    Barrier2 -->|Spur Cable| Dev3
    Barrier2 -->|Spur Cable| Dev4
```

## 4.4 Selection Decision Tree — Which Protection Concept to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Network Device Selection in Hazardous Area] --> Q1{Is live maintenance required?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2[Use Ex i - Intrinsic Safety]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is device power less than 1.3 W?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q2
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is the area Zone 1 or Zone 21?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q5[Use Ex d - Flameproof Enclosure]
    Q4 -->|Zone 2 or 22| Q6[Use Ex e or Ex nA]
    Q2 --> R1[Select Zener Barrier or Isolated Barrier]
    Q5 --> R2[Use Explosion Proof Housing with Cable Glands]
    Q6 --> R3[Use Standard Increased Safety Termination]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Interpretation:** In real plants, a single segment coupler serves **multiple field devices** (up to 31 in PROFIBUS-PA or 32 in FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1). The barrier protects the **entire trunk + all spurs**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a hazardous area. How is it classified according to IEC 60079-10-1?**

**Model Answer:**
A hazardous area is an area in which an explosive gas atmosphere or combustible dust is, or may be, present in quantities requiring special precautions for the design, installation, and use of electrical and electronic equipment, including industrial network devices.

According to **IEC 60079-10-1** (explosive gas atmospheres), hazardous areas are classified into three zones:
- **Zone 0:** Place in which an explosive gas atmosphere is present continuously, or for long periods, or frequently.
- **Zone 1:** Place in which an explosive gas atmosphere is likely to occur in normal operation occasionally.
- **Zone 2:** Place in which an explosive gas atmosphere is not likely to occur in normal operation but, if it does occur, will persist for a short period only.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Intrinsic Safety? Why is it preferred for industrial fieldbus networks like PROFIBUS-PA?**

**Model Answer:**
Intrinsic Safety (Ex i) is a type of protection in which the electrical energy in the apparatus and the interconnecting wiring is restricted to a level **below that which could ignite a specified explosive gas atmosphere**, including under fault conditions. The IEC standard governing this is **IEC 60079-11**.

**Why preferred for industrial fieldbus networks:**
1. **Live maintenance (hot-swapping)** of field devices is allowed without gas clearance certificates — critical for continuous process plants.
2. Fieldbus segments (PROFIBUS-PA, FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1) operate at low voltage (±9 V to ±32 V) and low current (4–20 mA), which is naturally suited to Ex i power budgets.
3. Safety barriers provide **galvanic isolation**, protecting the host controller from field-side faults.
4. Lower installation cost compared to flameproof enclosures (Ex d).
5. Compatible with **multi-drop topologies** required by fieldbus protocols.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Module 3 Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** Explain in detail the **temperature classification of gases** according to IEC 60079-0. List the six temperature classes with their maximum surface temperatures and provide **two example gases** for each class. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1 segment coupler in a safe area has ratings $V_o = 17.5\,V$, $I_o = 350\,mA$, $P_o = 2.0\,W$. A control valve positioner in Zone 1 has input ratings $V_i = 24\,V$, $I_i = 250\,mA$, $P_i = 1.0\,W$. Verify whether the combination is **safe for Ex ib installation**. If not, suggest a corrective measure. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Question A

#### Part (a) — Temperature Classification of Gases

The **maximum surface temperature** of any Ex-certified equipment must remain below the **auto-ignition temperature** of the surrounding gas/vapor. IEC 60079-0 divides gases into **six temperature classes (T1 to T6)**:

| Class | Max Surface Temp (°C) | Example Gas 1 | Example Gas 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **T1** | **450** | Methane | Propane |
| **T2** | **300** | Ethylene | Cyclopropane |
| **T3** | **200** | n-Hexane | Kerosene |
| **T4** | **135** | Diethyl ether | Tetrahydrofuran |
| **T5** | **100** | — | — |
| **T6** | **85** | Carbon disulfide | Ethyl nitrate |

> **Notes:** T6 is the **most stringent** classification. Hydrogen and acetylene are not classified by temperature but by gas group (Group IIC).

#### Part (b) — Barrier Compliance Verification

**Step 1 — Voltage Check:**
$$V_o = 17.5\,V \quad \text{vs.} \quad V_i = 24\,V$$
$$17.5 \le 24 \quad \checkmark \text{(Voltage OK)}$$

**Step 2 — Current Check:**
$$I_o = 350\,mA \quad \text{vs.} \quad I_i = 250\,mA$$
$$350 \le 250 \quad \text{FAIL (Current Exceeded)}$$

**Step 3 — Power Check:**
$$P_o = 2.0\,W \quad \text{vs.} \quad P_i = 1.0\,W$$
$$2.0 \le 1.0 \quad \text{FAIL (Power Exceeded)}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
The combination is **NOT safe for Ex ib installation** because the barrier can deliver **more current (350 mA > 250 mA)** and **more power (2.0 W > 1.0 W)** than the device can safely absorb.

**Step 5 — Corrective Measure:**
Replace the segment coupler with a barrier whose output ratings satisfy all three inequalities:

$$V_o \le 24\,V,\quad I_o \le 250\,mA,\quad P_o \le 1.0\,W$$

For example, select a barrier with $V_o = 16\,V$, $I_o = 230\,mA$, $P_o = 0.95\,W$ → all three checks pass.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a)** Explain the **IEC Zone classification system** for explosive gas atmospheres and for combustible dusts. State **two example industries** where each zone is typically encountered. **(7 marks)**

**b)** An Ex ia barrier in the safe area specifies $C_a = 100\,nF$ and $L_a = 2.0\,mH$. The field device in Zone 0 has internal $C_i = 8\,nF$ and $L_i = 0.05\,mH$. The connecting cable has $C = 60\,pF/m$ and $L = 0.5\,\mu H/m$. Determine the **maximum permissible cable length**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Question B

#### Part (a) — IEC Zone Classification System

**For Explosive Gas Atmospheres (per IEC 60079-10-1):**

| Zone | Definition | Example Industry 1 | Example Industry 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zone 0** | Explosive gas atmosphere present **continuously** | Inside an oil storage tank's vapor space | Inside a hydrogenation reactor |
| **Zone 1** | Explosive gas atmosphere **likely** during normal operation | Inside a gasoline pump enclosure | Near an LPG cylinder filling nozzle |
| **Zone 2** | Explosive gas atmosphere **not likely**, but possible abnormally | Battery room periphery | Solvent storage shed |

**For Combustible Dusts (per IEC 60079-10-2):**

| Zone | Definition | Example Industry 1 | Example Industry 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zone 20** | Combustible dust cloud present **continuously** | Inside a flour mill hopper | Inside a grain silo |
| **Zone 21** | Combustible dust cloud **likely** in normal operation | Near a sugar grinding unit | Near a coal conveyor transfer point |
| **Zone 22** | Combustible dust cloud **not normally present** | Bagging station periphery | Open coal stockyard area |

#### Part (b) — Maximum Cable Length

**Step 1 — Maximum cable capacitance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{cable, max} &= C_a - C_i \\
&= 100\,nF - 8\,nF \\
&= 92\,nF \\
&= 92 \times 10^{-9}\,F
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Length limit due to capacitance:**

$$L_{cap} = \frac{92 \times 10^{-9}}{60 \times 10^{-12}} = 1533.33\,m$$

**Step 3 — Maximum cable inductance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{cable, max} &= L_a - L_i \\
&= 2.0\,mH - 0.05\,mH \\
&= 1.95\,mH \\
&= 1.95 \times 10^{-3}\,H
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Length limit due to inductance:**

$$L_{ind} = \frac{1.95 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 3900\,m$$

**Step 5 — Governing limit:**

$$L_{max} = \min(1533.33,\,3900) = 1533.33\,m$$

$$\boxed{L_{max} \approx 1533\,m}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Capacitance is the **governing parameter** in this case because the cable has relatively high distributed capacitance per meter. In practice, Ex ia fieldbus segments rarely exceed 1500 m of cable length.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (as observed in KTU valuation reports):**
> 1. **Forgetting the power check** — many students verify only $V_o \le V_i$ and $I_o \le I_i$ but **omit the power inequality** ($P_o \le P_i$). This loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Ex ia, Ex ib, and Ex ic** — these have progressively weaker protection levels; ia is the most stringent (Zone 0 allowed), ic only Zone 2.
> 3. **Mixing gas and dust terminology** — gas zones are 0/1/2, dust zones are 20/21/22. Mixing them is a **fatal error**.
> 4. **Wrong cable length formula** — using $L_{cap} \times L_{ind}$ multiplication instead of taking the **minimum** of the two.
> 5. **Omitting units** in the final answer of numerical questions — KTU examiners strictly deduct 1 mark for missing units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ Hazardous area = explosive atmosphere (gas or dust) may be present.
- ✅ **Zone 0 / 20** = continuous presence → **Ex ia only**.
- ✅ **Zone 1 / 21** = likely in normal operation → Ex ib, Ex d, Ex e, Ex p, Ex m permitted.
- ✅ **Zone 2 / 22** = not normally present → All Zone 1 concepts + Ex nA, Ex nC, Ex nL, Ex ic.
- ✅ **Temperature classes T1–T6** → Max surface temps **450, 300, 200, 135, 100, 85 °C**.
- ✅ **Intrinsic Safety (Ex i)** is the **preferred concept for industrial fieldbus networks** (PROFIBUS-PA, FOUNDATION Fieldbus, HART).
- ✅ **Compliance triplet** for barriers: $V_o \le V_i$, $I_o \le I_i$, $P_o \le P_i$ — **all three must hold**.
- ✅ **Cable length limit**: $L_{max} = \min\left(\frac{C_a - C_i}{C/m},\ \frac{L_a - L_i}{L/m}\right)$.
- ✅ **Ethernet-APL (IEC 61850-3 / IEC 60079-47)** is the modern intrinsically safe 10 Mbit/s Ethernet extension.
- ✅ **Live maintenance allowed only with Ex i protection** — critical advantage for continuous process plants.
- ✅ **Governing standards for KTU 2024**: IEC 60079-0, IEC 60079-10-1, IEC 60079-10-2, IEC 60079-11, IEC 60079-14.
- ✅ **Power limit for Ex ia Group IIC** ≈ **1.3 W** (derived empirically from spark ignition energy of ~20 µJ).
- ✅ **Dust layer thickness limit** = **5 mm** for temperature classification.
- ✅ **Equipment groups**: Group II (surface industry gases), Group III (dusts), Group I (mining).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MODBUS Networks: Communication Transactions
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MODBUS Networks: Communication Transactions

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **MODBUS Communication Transaction** is a structured, deterministic exchange of Protocol Data Units (PDUs) between a single **Master** (Client) device and one or more **Slave** (Server) devices over a serial or TCP-based industrial network. Each transaction is initiated by the Master and consists of a **Request/Query** frame followed by either a **Normal Response**, an **Exception Response**, or — in the case of a **Broadcast** — no response at all.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):**
> Communication transactions in MODBUS follow a strictly **half-duplex, master-driven, query–response paradigm**. Only the master can initiate a transaction; slaves remain silent until polled. This property is fundamental to deterministic industrial control.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Classroom Roll Call"

Imagine a **strict teacher (Master)** standing in front of a classroom of **40 students (Slaves)**, each with a unique **roll number (Slave Address: 1–247)**.

- The teacher **never** speaks unless asked. Only the teacher can call out a question.
- The teacher calls: *"Roll number 12, what is the temperature reading in your register 0?"*
- Only Roll No. 12 answers: *"It is 45.2 °C."* All others stay silent.
- If Roll No. 12 is sick (error), it raises a red flag: *"Exception – Illegal Register Address."*
- For a fire drill (broadcast), the teacher shouts: *"All students, open the emergency valve!"* — nobody replies; they all execute.

This single-master, multi-slave, polled-acknowledgment model **is** the MODBUS transaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** A transaction in MODBUS is *one* master's request + *one* slave's response (or silence for broadcast). It is **not** a continuous stream — it is a discrete, atomic, polled event.

## 1.3 Physical & Protocol Constants

| Constant | Value | Purpose |
|----------|-------|---------|
| **Max. Slaves per Master** | **247** (1–247; 0 reserved for broadcast) | Addressable node limit on RS-485 |
| **Function Code range** | **1–127** valid; **128–255** exception | Request/Response semantics |
| **PDU size limit** | **253 bytes** (FC + Data) | Defined by MODBUS Application Layer |
| **Inter-frame silence (RTU)** | **3.5 character times** | Frame delimiter |
| **Standard baud rates** | **9600, 19200, 38400, 115200 bps** | Serial link rates |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MODBUS Transaction — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

A complete MODBUS transaction progresses through **five well-defined phases**:

1. **Idle / Silent Phase** — The bus is silent for at least **3.5 character times**. This silence marks the *end* of the previous transaction and the *beginning* of a new one.
2. **Query / Request Phase** — The Master places a fully-formed **ADU (Application Data Unit)** on the bus, containing the slave address, function code, and data payload.
3. **Processing Phase (Slaves)** — All slaves receive the frame. Only the addressed slave (or all slaves in broadcast) decodes the PDU and executes the action (read coils, write register, etc.).
4. **Response Phase** — The addressed slave transmits its response ADU back to the master. **Exception responses** replace normal responses if the slave fails to execute the request.
5. **Silent / Inter-Transaction Phase** — Bus returns to idle; transaction is officially closed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Half-Duplex Constraint:** Because MODBUS RTU/ASCII use RS-485 (a half-duplex physical layer), the bus physically *cannot* carry two signals simultaneously. The master must release the bus and the slave must wait for the master's direction reversal. This is why **transaction turnaround time** is a critical design parameter.

## 2.2 The ADU Frame Anatomy (RTU Mode)

$$
\underbrace{\text{Address}}_{1 \text{ byte}} \ \Big|\ \underbrace{\text{Function Code}}_{1 \text{ byte}} \ \Big|\ \underbrace{\text{Data}}_{N \text{ bytes}} \ \Big|\ \underbrace{\text{CRC-16}}_{2 \text{ bytes}}
$$

The **PDU** (Protocol Data Unit) is the *inner core* — it is **identical** across RTU, ASCII, and TCP. The outer wrapper (Address + Error Check) differs by transport.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Character time (RTU) | $$T_{char} = \frac{11 \text{ bits}}{\text{Baud Rate}}$$ | **seconds** (1 start + 8 data + 1 parity + 1 stop) |
| 2 | Inter-frame silence | $$T_{silent} = 3.5 \cdot T_{char}$$ | Marks frame boundary |
| 3 | Frame transmission time | $$T_{frame} = \frac{N_{bytes} \cdot 11}{Baud}$$ | **N_bytes** = total ADU length |
| 4 | Round-trip transaction time | $$T_{RT} = T_{frame}^{req} + T_{turn} + T_{process} + T_{frame}^{resp}$$ | **T_turn** = bus turnaround (often ≤ 1 char) |
| 5 | Maximum PDU payload | $$N_{data}^{max} = 253 - 1 = 252 \text{ bytes}$$ | FC(1) + Data(N) |
| 6 | CRC-16 polynomial (RTU) | $$G(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^{2} + 1$$ | Modbus standard poly (0xA001 reflected) |
| 7 | LRC-8 (ASCII) | $$LRC = 256 - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} B_i\right) \bmod 256$$ | 1-byte checksum |
| 8 | Exception response FC | $$FC_{exception} = FC_{request} + 0x80$$ | High bit (7) set |
| 9 | Maximum slave count | $$N_{slaves} = 247$$ | Addresses 1–247 |
| 10 | Broadcast address | $$Addr_{BC} = 0x00$$ | No response permitted |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Equations 1–4 are heavily tested in KTU numerical problems. A student who memorizes the character-time formula can solve any "minimum time to poll N slaves" type question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

MODBUS transactions underpin **SCADA systems** in power substations, water-treatment plants, and HVAC controls. The deterministic, polled nature of the transaction model gives engineers **predictable worst-case latency**, which is critical for:

- Protective relaying (IEC 61850 gateways backhaul to MODBUS RTU)
- PLC-to-VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) parameterization
- Remote Terminal Units (RTUs) in oil & gas pipelines
- Building Management Systems (BMS) with thousands of I/O points

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Character Time, Frame Time, and Transaction Round-Trip

The derivation below is presented in the **exhaustive, KTU-valuation-friendly** style. Every algebraic step is justified.

### 3.1.1 Character Time Derivation

A single MODBUS RTU character consists of **11 bits**: 1 start bit + 8 data bits + 1 parity bit (even/odd/none) + 1 stop bit.

$$
T_{char} \;=\; \frac{\text{Number of bits per character}}{\text{Bits transmitted per second}} \;=\; \frac{11}{B}
$$

where **B** is the configured baud rate in bits per second.

**Numerical example:** At $B = 9600$ bps:

$$
T_{char} = \frac{11}{9600} = 1.1458 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \approx 1.146 \text{ ms}
$$

### 3.1.2 Inter-Frame Silence Derivation

The MODBUS specification mandates a bus silence of **3.5 character times** to unambiguously delimit consecutive frames. A start bit cannot occur in the middle of a valid character, so 3.5 char-times guarantees no false framing.

$$
T_{silent} = 3.5 \times T_{char} = 3.5 \times \frac{11}{B} = \frac{38.5}{B}
$$

**Numerical example:** At $B = 19200$ bps:

$$
T_{silent} = \frac{38.5}{19200} = 2.005 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \approx 2.005 \text{ ms}
$$

### 3.1.3 Single Frame Transmission Time

An ADU contains $N_{bytes}$ bytes. Each byte becomes 11 serial bits. Therefore:

$$
T_{frame} = N_{bytes} \times \frac{11}{B}
$$

### 3.1.4 Round-Trip Transaction Time (Master + Slave + Bus Turnaround)

A complete polled transaction comprises four sequential events:

$$
T_{RT} = T_{frame}^{req} + T_{turn} + T_{process} + T_{frame}^{resp}
$$

Let us substitute each term:

$$
T_{RT} = \frac{N_{req} \cdot 11}{B} + T_{turn} + T_{process} + \frac{N_{resp} \cdot 11}{B}
$$

For a typical Read Holding Registers request (8 bytes) and response (7 + 2×N bytes):

$$
N_{req} = 8, \quad N_{resp} = 5 + 2N
$$

So the **polling cycle for N registers** becomes:

$$
T_{RT} = \frac{8 \cdot 11}{B} + T_{turn} + T_{process} + \frac{(5 + 2N) \cdot 11}{B}
$$

Factoring out the baud dependency:

$$
T_{RT} = \frac{11}{B}\left(13 + 2N\right) + T_{turn} + T_{process}
$$

### 3.1.5 Polling N Slaves Sequentially — Worst-Case Cycle Time

If the master must poll $N_{slaves}$ slaves sequentially, each requiring its own transaction, the **worst-case supervisory cycle** is:

$$
T_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{slaves}} T_{RT}^{(i)} + (N_{slaves} - 1) \cdot T_{silent}
$$

If all transactions are identical (homogeneous poll):

$$
T_{cycle} = N_{slaves} \cdot T_{RT} + (N_{slaves} - 1) \cdot T_{silent}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Function Code Categories

| Function Code (Hex) | Name | Direction | PDU Data | Use in Transactions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0x01** | Read Coils | Master→Slave | Start addr + Quantity | Polling discrete outputs |
| **0x02** | Read Discrete Inputs | Master→Slave | Start addr + Quantity | Polling digital inputs |
| **0x03** | Read Holding Registers | Master→Slave | Start addr + Quantity | Most common — read 16-bit regs |
| **0x04** | Read Input Registers | Master→Slave | Start addr + Quantity | Polling analog inputs |
| **0x05** | Write Single Coil | Master→Slave | Output addr + Value | Set one digital output |
| **0x06** | Write Single Register | Master→Slave | Register addr + Value | Set one analog output |
| **0x0F (15)** | Write Multiple Coils | Master→Slave | Addr + Qty + Byte count + Values | Bulk DO write |
| **0x10 (16)** | Write Multiple Registers | Master→Slave | Addr + Qty + Byte count + Values | Bulk AO write |
| **0x80–0xFF** | Exception Response | Slave→Master | Exception Code (1 byte) | Error reporting |

## 3.3 Exception Response — The "Negative Acknowledge" Path

When a slave receives a valid request but cannot execute it (bad address, illegal value, hardware fault), it returns an **exception response** with the high bit of the function code set:

$$
FC_{exception} = FC_{request} \vee 0x80 = FC_{request} + 128
$$

The PDU of an exception is just **two bytes**:

$$
\text{Exception PDU} = \underbrace{FC_{exception}}_{1 \text{ byte}} \ \Big|\ \underbrace{ExcCode}_{1 \text{ byte}}
$$

| Exception Code | Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 0x01 | Illegal Function | Slave does not support FC |
| 0x02 | Illegal Data Address | Register/coil out of range |
| 0x03 | Illegal Data Value | Value out of allowable range |
| 0x04 | Slave Device Failure | Hardware fault during execution |
| 0x05 | Acknowledge | Long-duration command accepted |
| 0x06 | Slave Device Busy | Retry later |
| 0x08 | Memory Parity Error | Internal memory corruption |
| 0x0A | Gateway Path Unavailable | Routing problem |
| 0x0B | Gateway Target No Response | Downstream device dead |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Question:** A MODBUS RTU master polls 10 slaves at 19200 bps, even parity, 1 stop bit. Each transaction is a Read Holding Registers (FC 0x03) for 8 registers. Slave processing time is 2 ms. Bus turnaround is 0.5 ms. Compute the **total worst-case supervisory cycle time**.

**Solution (step-by-step):**

**Step 1:** Compute the character time.

$$
T_{char} = \frac{11}{19200} = 5.729 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.5729 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the inter-frame silence.

$$
T_{silent} = 3.5 \times T_{char} = 3.5 \times 0.5729 = 2.0052 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $N_{req}$ and $N_{resp}$ for FC 0x03 reading 8 registers.

- **Request PDU:** FC(1) + StartAddr(2) + Qty(2) = **5 bytes**; ADU + Addr(1) + CRC(2) = **8 bytes**
- **Response PDU:** FC(1) + ByteCount(1) + 8×2 registers(16) = **18 bytes**; ADU + Addr(1) + CRC(2) = **21 bytes**

**Step 4:** Compute the per-transaction round-trip.

$$
T_{RT} = \frac{8 \cdot 11}{19200} + 0.5 \times 10^{-3} + 2 \times 10^{-3} + \frac{21 \cdot 11}{19200}
$$

Evaluating each term:

$$
\frac{8 \cdot 11}{19200} = \frac{88}{19200} = 4.583 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\frac{21 \cdot 11}{19200} = \frac{231}{19200} = 12.031 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{RT} = 4.583 + 0.5 + 2.0 + 12.031 = 19.114 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the total cycle for 10 slaves.

$$
T_{cycle} = 10 \times 19.114 + 9 \times 2.0052
$$

$$
T_{cycle} = 191.14 + 18.047 = 209.19 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Answer:** $T_{cycle} \approx 209.19$ ms — the master can refresh all 10 slaves roughly **4.78 times per second**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master-Slave Transaction State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> BusIdle

    BusIdle --> BuildingRequest : MasterHasDataToSend
    BuildingRequest --> TransmittingRequest : FrameAssembled
    TransmittingRequest --> WaitingTurnaround : FrameTransmitted
    WaitingTurnaround --> WaitingResponse : BusDirectionReversed
    WaitingTurnaround --> BroadcastConfirmed : AddressEqualsZero

    WaitingResponse --> DecodingResponse : SlaveResponseReceived
    DecodingResponse --> ProcessingNormalResponse : FunctionCodeLessThan128
    DecodingResponse --> ProcessingException : FunctionCodeGreaterOrEqual128

    ProcessingNormalResponse --> UpdatingApplicationData : ValidPDU
    ProcessingException --> LoggingError : ExceptionCodeExtracted
    UpdatingApplicationData --> BusIdle : TransactionComplete
    LoggingError --> BusIdle : ErrorLogged

    BroadcastConfirmed --> BusIdle : AllSlavesExecuted

    WaitingResponse --> TimeoutError : TimerExpired35charTimes
    TimeoutError --> BusIdle : MasterRetriesOrSkips
```

## 4.2 ADU Frame Structure (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Address\n1 byte\n1-247 or 0BC"] --> B["Function Code\n1 byte\n0x01-0x7F"]
    B --> C["Data Payload\n0 to 252 bytes\nPDU-specific"]
    C --> D["Error Check\n2 bytes CRC16 RTU\nor 1 byte LRC ASCII"]
```

## 4.3 Transaction Sequence — Read Holding Registers (FC 0x03)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Master
    participant S1 as Slave Address 0x01
    participant S2 as Slave Address 0x02

    Note over M: T = 0 ms, Bus Idle
    M->>S1: ADU [Addr=0x01][FC=0x03][Start=0x0000][Qty=0x0002][CRC]
    Note over S1: Processing (e.g., 2 ms)
    S1-->>M: ADU [Addr=0x01][FC=0x03][Bytes=4][Data=0x00FA 0x0102][CRC]
    Note over M: 3.5 char silence
    M->>S2: ADU [Addr=0x02][FC=0x03][Start=0x0000][Qty=0x0002][CRC]
    Note over S2: Processing
    S2-->>M: ADU [Addr=0x02][FC=0x03][Bytes=4][Data=0x0064 0x03E8][CRC]
    Note over M: Cycle Complete
```

## 4.4 Exception Response Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Request Received] --> V{Valid Address?}
    V -- No --> Sil[Ignore Silently]
    V -- Yes --> F{Function Supported?}
    F -- No --> E1[Exception FC+0x80\nCode 0x01]
    F -- Yes --> A{Address in Range?}
    A -- No --> E2[Exception FC+0x80\nCode 0x02]
    A -- Yes --> Ex[Execute Action]
    Ex --> Ok{Success?}
    Ok -- Yes --> NR[Normal Response PDU]
    Ok -- No --> E4[Exception FC+0x80\nCode 0x04]
```

## 4.5 Transaction Timing Block Architecture

```mermaid
gantt
    title MODBUS RTU Transaction Timing Timeline
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S

    section Master
    TX_Request (8 bytes)     :a1, 0, 5
    Wait_For_Response        :a2, after a1, 4
    RX_Response (21 bytes)   :a3, after a2, 12

    section Bus Silences
    InterFrame_35char        :crit, b1, after a3, 2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a MODBUS transaction. What is meant by a broadcast transaction, and how does a slave behave in response to it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **MODBUS transaction** is a complete exchange of protocol data units between a master and a slave, consisting of a master's request frame and the slave's response frame (or silence in the case of broadcast). The master initiates every transaction; slaves are passive until polled.

A **broadcast transaction** uses the special slave address **0x00**. The master sends a single request frame to *all* slaves on the bus simultaneously. **Slaves execute the requested write action but do NOT transmit any response** — this prevents bus collisions on a half-duplex RS-485 link. (3 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three function codes used in MODBUS transactions. State the direction (Master→Slave or Slave→Master) and the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Function Code 0x03 (Read Holding Registers)** — Direction: *Master → Slave (Request)*, *Slave → Master (Response)*. Used to read the contents of 16-bit holding registers. (1 mark)
2. **Function Code 0x06 (Write Single Register)** — Direction: *Master → Slave* only. Writes a single 16-bit value to one holding register. (1 mark)
3. **Function Code 0x10 (Write Multiple Registers, 16)** — Direction: *Master → Slave* only. Writes a block of 16-bit registers in a single atomic transaction. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 3, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the MODBUS communication transaction model with reference to the master–slave paradigm. Describe in detail the components of a MODBUS RTU frame, including the ADU structure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how exception responses are generated in MODBUS transactions. List any four standard exception codes with their meanings. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Master–Slave Transaction Model & Frame Structure (7 marks):**

The MODBUS communication model is a **single-master, multi-slave, half-duplex, query–response protocol**. Only the master initiates a transaction; slaves remain silent unless addressed. The transaction flow is: (2 marks)

1. **Bus Idle** — at least 3.5 character times of silence.
2. **Request Phase** — master transmits ADU.
3. **Processing Phase** — addressed slave decodes and executes.
4. **Response Phase** — slave transmits response ADU (or exception).
5. **Inter-transaction silence** — bus returns to idle.

**MODBUS RTU ADU structure** (3 marks):

$$
\text{ADU} = \underbrace{\text{Slave Addr (1 byte)}}_{1-247 \text{ or } 0_{BC}} + \underbrace{\text{PDU}}_{\text{FC(1) + Data(N)}} + \underbrace{\text{CRC-16 (2 bytes)}}_{\text{Error Check}}
$$

- **Slave Address:** Identifies the target. 0x00 = broadcast.
- **PDU (Protocol Data Unit):** Identical across RTU/ASCII/TCP — the application-layer core. **Maximum 253 bytes.**
- **CRC-16:** Polynomial $G(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^2 + 1$ (reflected: 0xA001). Detects all single-bit, double-bit, odd-count-bit, and burst errors ≤ 16 bits. (2 marks)

**(b) Exception Response Mechanism (7 marks):**

When a slave receives a syntactically valid frame (correct CRC, valid address) but cannot execute the request, it returns an **exception response** instead of a normal response. (2 marks)

**Structure of exception response ADU:**

$$
\text{Exception ADU} = \underbrace{\text{Slave Addr (1 byte)}}_{0x01 \text{ to } 0xF7} + \underbrace{(FC_{req} + 0x80)}_{1 \text{ byte}} + \underbrace{\text{Exception Code (1 byte)}}_{0x01 \text{ to } 0x0B} + \underbrace{\text{CRC-16 (2 bytes)}}
$$

**Example (2 marks):**
A master sends `Addr=0x05, FC=0x06, Reg=0x0100, Value=0xFFFF, CRC`.
- If register 0x0100 is read-only → slave responds: `Addr=0x05, FC=0x86, Code=0x02, CRC` (Illegal Data Address).

**Four standard exception codes (3 marks):**

| Code | Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **0x01** | Illegal Function | FC not supported by slave |
| **0x02** | Illegal Data Address | Register/coil out of mapped range |
| **0x03** | Illegal Data Value | Value violates slave's constraints |
| **0x04** | Slave Device Failure | Unrecoverable hardware error during execution |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the total round-trip transaction time in MODBUS RTU. A master polls 5 slaves at 9600 bps to read 4 holding registers each. Given slave processing time = 3 ms, bus turnaround = 1 ms, and 1 start + 8 data + 1 parity + 1 stop bits per character, compute the worst-case supervisory cycle time. **(8 marks)**

**(b)** Compare MODBUS RTU and MODBUS ASCII with respect to frame format, efficiency, error checking, and typical use cases. **(6 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation and Numerical Computation (8 marks):**

**Step 1 — Character time derivation (2 marks):**

$$
T_{char} = \frac{\text{bits per character}}{\text{baud rate}} = \frac{1+8+1+1}{B} = \frac{11}{B} \text{ seconds}
$$

At $B = 9600$ bps:

$$
T_{char} = \frac{11}{9600} = 1.1458 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \approx 1.146 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Frame sizes for FC 0x03 reading 4 registers (1 mark):**

- **Request ADU:** Addr(1) + FC(1) + StartAddr(2) + Qty(2) + CRC(2) = **8 bytes**
- **Response ADU:** Addr(1) + FC(1) + ByteCount(1) + 4×2(8) + CRC(2) = **13 bytes**

**Step 3 — Per-transaction round-trip (2 marks):**

$$
T_{RT} = \frac{N_{req} \cdot 11}{B} + T_{turn} + T_{process} + \frac{N_{resp} \cdot 11}{B}
$$

$$
T_{RT} = \frac{8 \cdot 11}{9600} + 1 + 3 + \frac{13 \cdot 11}{9600}
$$

$$
T_{RT} = 9.167 \text{ ms} + 1 \text{ ms} + 3 \text{ ms} + 14.896 \text{ ms} = 28.063 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Inter-frame silence (1 mark):**

$$
T_{silent} = 3.5 \times 1.146 = 4.010 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Worst-case cycle for 5 slaves (2 marks):**

$$
T_{cycle} = 5 \times T_{RT} + 4 \times T_{silent}
$$

$$
T_{cycle} = 5 \times 28.063 + 4 \times 4.010 = 140.315 + 16.04 = 156.36 \text{ ms}
$$

**Answer:** $T_{cycle} \approx 156.36$ ms (≈ 6.4 supervisory cycles/second).

**(b) MODBUS RTU vs ASCII (6 marks):**

| Parameter | **MODBUS RTU** | **MODBUS ASCII** | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frame format** | Binary, 8-bit bytes | ASCII hex, 7-bit chars, `:` start, `CR LF` end | 1.5 |
| **Efficiency** | Higher (~2×) — 1 byte per register | Lower — 2 ASCII chars per byte | 1.5 |
| **Error check** | **CRC-16** (2 bytes, robust) | **LRC-8** (1 byte, simple checksum) | 1.5 |
| **Delimiter** | **3.5 char silence** | `:` colon + `CR LF` carriage return | 0.5 |
| **Typical use** | Modern industrial, RS-485 high-speed | Legacy systems, debugging, dial-up modems | 1.0 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 3.5 character-time silence** in cycle-time calculations. **Loss: 1–2 marks per problem.**
> 2. **Confusing PDU vs ADU** — students often say "frame" without specifying that the PDU is transport-independent. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Wrong exception FC formula** — writing $FC_{exception} = FC_{request} \times 2$ instead of $+ 0x80$ (i.e., $+128$). **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Forgetting the Address byte + CRC bytes** when computing frame length. The PDU is *not* the full ADU. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 5. **Broadcast transactions DO NOT elicit a response.** Saying "all slaves reply with acknowledgement" is **wrong** and costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- ☐ A **MODBUS transaction** = **1 master request** + **1 slave response** (or silence for broadcast).
- ☐ The transaction model is **half-duplex, master-driven, query–response** — slaves NEVER initiate.
- ☐ **ADU = Address(1) + PDU(≤253) + ErrorCheck(RTU: 2, ASCII: 1)**.
- ☐ **PDU is transport-independent** — same on RTU, ASCII, and TCP.
- ☐ **Character time:** $T_{char} = 11/B$ seconds.
- ☐ **Inter-frame silence:** $T_{silent} = 3.5 \cdot T_{char}$ — always 3.5, never 3, never 4.
- ☐ **Exception response FC = Original FC + 0x80** (set the high bit).
- ☐ **Broadcast address = 0x00**, **no response** permitted, write-only actions allowed.
- ☐ **Max 247 slaves** per master (1–247; 0 reserved).
- ☐ **CRC-16 polynomial:** $G(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^2 + 1$, reflected form **0xA001**.
- ☐ **LRC-8 ASCII formula:** $LRC = -\sum B_i \pmod{256}$ (two's complement of byte sum).
- ☐ **RTU vs ASCII:** RTU is binary + CRC-16 (faster, robust); ASCII is human-readable + LRC-8 (slower, legacy).
- ☐ **Common FCs to memorize:** 0x01, 0x02, 0x03, 0x04, 0x05, 0x06, 0x0F, 0x10.
- ☐ **Round-trip transaction time** = $T_{frame}^{req} + T_{turn} + T_{process} + T_{frame}^{resp}$.
- ☐ **Worst-case cycle** = $N_{slaves} \cdot T_{RT} + (N_{slaves}-1) \cdot T_{silent}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transmission Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Transmission Modes** define the *direction of data flow* and the *temporal relationship* between the transmitter and the receiver over a communication channel. In Industrial Networks (PEICT747), they govern how field devices (sensors, actuators, PLCs, SCADA nodes) exchange deterministic or non-deterministic traffic across the physical medium.

In industrial communication standards such as **Foundation Fieldbus**, **Profibus**, and **Modbus RTU**, the choice of transmission mode directly impacts:
- **Determinism** of cyclic real-time I/O updates,
- **Cycle time** and **jitter** for closed-loop control,
- **Bandwidth utilization** of the physical layer (RS-485, Ethernet, fiber).

The three canonical transmission modes recognized in the KTU syllabus are:

| Mode | Direction Capability | Industrial Example |
|------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Simplex** | One-way only | Broadcast alarm annunciator panel, sensor → PLC uplink on a dedicated line |
| **Half-Duplex** | Two-way, but **one direction at a time** | Walkie-talkie, RS-485 **Profibus DP** token-passing |
| **Full-Duplex** | Two-way **simultaneously** | Industrial Ethernet switch port, RS-422 4-wire link |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parallel vs Serial Transmission** is a *secondary classification* that the KTU module maps onto these modes:
> - **Serial** → bits travel sequentially on a single channel (used in almost all fieldbuses).
> - **Parallel** → multiple bits travel simultaneously on parallel wires (used inside PLC backplanes, e.g., PCI-based racks).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **narrow one-lane bridge** connecting two factories (Factory A and Factory B):

- **Simplex mode** = A toll bridge where traffic is allowed **only from Factory A to Factory B**. Factory B cannot reply. The control room in A can read sensors in B, but cannot send commands back through the same cable.
- **Half-Duplex mode** = The same one-lane bridge, but now traffic is allowed in **either direction, but only one truck at a time**. A signalman (the *token* in Profibus or the *request/response* in Modbus) tells which factory may currently send. This is exactly how **RS-485 two-wire buses** behave.
- **Full-Duplex mode** = Two parallel one-lane bridges, one in each direction. Both factories can ship goods *at the same time*. This is how **100BASE-TX Ethernet** (separate TX/RX pairs) or **RS-422 4-wire** links operate.

This intuition immediately answers the *why* of why industrial protocols choose one mode over another — **cost, determinism, and cabling density** on the plant floor.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick mnemonic — SHuF** (Simplex → Half → Full) corresponds to **increasing channel complexity** and **increasing bidirectional throughput**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Channel capacity (Shannon–Hartley bound)** — defines the theoretical upper bound on bits per second for a given **bandwidth** and **signal-to-noise ratio**.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — measured in **decibels (dB)**.
- **Bit Duration ($T_b$)** — the time occupied by a single bit on the wire, inversely proportional to the **bit rate ($R_b$)**.

These constants will appear in the formula sheet that follows.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Transmission Mode

#### A. Simplex Transmission
- **Direction of data:** Strictly **unidirectional** (A → B only).
- **Channel utilization:** Always at *maximum theoretical capacity* in the forward direction because no reverse traffic consumes bandwidth.
- **Hardware cost:** Lowest. Requires only one transmitter and one receiver per link.
- **Industrial use case:** Sensor data acquisition where the field device is *read-only* (e.g., a temperature transmitter broadcasting on a 4–20 mA current loop).

#### B. Half-Duplex Transmission
- **Direction of data:** **Bidirectional**, but **mutually exclusive in time**.
- **Coordination overhead:** A Medium Access Control (MAC) mechanism decides who transmits. In **Profibus DP**, this is the *token-passing* algorithm; in **Modbus RTU**, it is *master–slave polling*.
- **Effective throughput:** Because the channel is shared in time, the *usable bandwidth per direction* is at most **50 %** of the raw bit rate in symmetrical traffic.
- **Industrial use case:** RS-485 two-wire multidrop buses in factory automation, where 32 nodes share one twisted pair.

#### C. Full-Duplex Transmission
- **Direction of data:** **Bidirectional and simultaneous**.
- **Channel separation:** Achieved either by **frequency-division** (separate bands), **time-division** (separate time-slots on same frequency), or **spatial separation** (separate wire pairs / separate fibers).
- **Effective throughput:** Up to **100 %** of the raw bit rate *per direction*, so **200 % aggregate** if measured bidirectionally.
- **Industrial use case:** Industrial Ethernet (e.g., **PROFINET**, **EtherNet/IP**, **Modbus TCP**) on 100BASE-TX with dedicated TX and RJ45 pair.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and align with the digital-communication fundamentals expected in Module 3 of PEICT747.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | Bit Rate ↔ Bit Duration | $R_b = \dfrac{1}{T_b}$ | $R_b$ = bit rate, $T_b$ = bit duration | bits/s, s |
| 2 | Baud Rate (Modulation) | $R_s = \dfrac{R_b}{\log_2 M}$ | $R_s$ = symbol rate, $M$ = signal levels | bauds |
| 3 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $C$ = capacity, $B$ = bandwidth, $S/N$ = SNR | bits/s, Hz, ratio |
| 4 | Shannon in dB form | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + 10^{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10}\right)$ | SNR expressed in decibels | bits/s |
| 5 | Nyquist (Noiseless) Capacity | $C = 2B \cdot \log_2 M$ | $M$ = discrete signal levels | bits/s |
| 6 | Half-Duplex Per-Direction Throughput | $\eta_{\text{HD}} = \dfrac{R_b \cdot t_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$ | duty cycle of one direction | bits/s |
| 7 | Full-Duplex Aggregate Throughput | $\eta_{\text{FD}} = R_b^{\text{TX}} + R_b^{\text{RX}}$ | sum of forward and reverse bit rates | bits/s |
| 8 | Frame Transmission Time | $t_f = \dfrac{N_{\text{overhead}} + N_{\text{data}}}{R_b}$ | $N$ = bits, $R_b$ = bit rate | seconds |
| 9 | Utilization (Efficiency) | $U = \dfrac{t_{\text{data}}}{t_{\text{data}} + t_{\text{overhead}}}$ | ratio of payload time to total | dimensionless |
| 10 | Propagation Delay | $t_p = \dfrac{d}{v_p}$ | $d$ = distance, $v_p$ = propagation velocity | s, m, m/s |

> **Critical Boundary Conditions**
> - **Nyquist** applies *only* when noise is negligible (industrial shielded cables in EMI-controlled cabinets).
> - **Shannon** gives the *theoretical ceiling* — practical protocols (e.g., Profibus at **12 Mbps**) achieve roughly **50–70 %** of $C$.
> - For **half-duplex**, the maximum *per-direction* throughput $\eta_{\text{HD}} \le \dfrac{R_b}{2}$.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **discrete manufacturing plant** (e.g., an automotive welding line):

- **Simplex analog loops (4–20 mA)** carry sensor readings from each welding robot back to the PLC, freeing the digital bus for control commands.
- **Half-duplex RS-485 (Profibus DP at 1.5 Mbps)** connects 32 I/O blocks in a daisy-chain. The MAC layer guarantees deterministic token rotation.
- **Full-duplex PROFINET IRT (Isochronous Real-Time)** exchanges **isochronous** motion-control telegrams between CNC and servo drive within a **1 ms cycle**, in both directions simultaneously.

Choosing the wrong mode here would either **exceed the cycle time** (production stalls) or **over-spec the cabling cost** (CAPEX overrun). Hence Module 3 is foundational for the design portion of the KTU lab/case-study assessment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Half-Duplex Effective Throughput

We start from first principles. Consider a half-duplex industrial bus shared between **two stations A and B** with a token-rotation cycle time $T_{\text{cycle}}$. Let:

- $T_A$ = total time station A holds the token,
- $T_B$ = total time station B holds the token,
- $T_{\text{overhead}}$ = token-passing latency per cycle (frame gaps, propagation).

The **fraction of channel time** used by A is:

$$
\alpha_A = \dfrac{T_A}{T_{\text{cycle}}}
$$

The **effective throughput in the A→B direction** is:

$$
\eta_A = \alpha_A \cdot R_b
$$

Now apply the constraint that the channel is **shared in time**:

$$
T_A + T_B + T_{\text{overhead}} = T_{\text{cycle}}
$$

For a **symmetric** traffic load where $T_A = T_B$:

$$
2T_A + T_{\text{overhead}} = T_{\text{cycle}}
$$

Solve for $T_A$:

$$
T_A = \dfrac{T_{\text{cycle}} - T_{\text{overhead}}}{2}
$$

Substitute back into $\eta_A$:

$$
\eta_A = \dfrac{T_{\text{cycle}} - T_{\text{overhead}}}{2 \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}} \cdot R_b
$$

$$
\eta_A = \dfrac{R_b}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{overhead}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}\right)
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** As $T_{\text{overhead}} \to 0$, the half-duplex channel delivers *exactly half* the raw bit rate per direction. As overhead grows (e.g., long cables, many nodes), throughput degrades further — this is why Profibus segment lengths are capped at **1200 m at 9.6 kbps** down to **100 m at 12 Mbps**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Frame Transmission Time for a Modbus RTU Frame

Modbus RTU over RS-485 (half-duplex) is the workhorse of industrial SCADA. A standard **Read Holding Registers** request frame contains:

| Field | Size (bits) |
|-------|-------------|
| Address (slave ID) | 8 |
| Function code | 8 |
| Starting address | 16 |
| Quantity of registers | 16 |
| CRC | 16 |
| **Total payload** | **64** |

Let the bit rate be $R_b = 9600$ bps. Compute the frame time:

$$
t_f = \dfrac{N_{\text{bits}}}{R_b}
$$

$$
t_f = \dfrac{64}{9600}
$$

$$
t_f \approx 6.667 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 6.667\,\text{ms}
$$

Including the **3.5 character inter-frame silence** (Modbus RTU spec, t3.5):

$$
t_{3.5} = \dfrac{11 \times 3.5}{R_b} = \dfrac{38.5}{9600}
$$

$$
t_{3.5} \approx 4.01\,\text{ms}
$$

Therefore the **total cycle time per request**:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = t_f + t_{3.5} = 6.667 + 4.01
$$

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} \approx 10.68\,\text{ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> When 32 slaves are polled cyclically by a master, total scan time $= 32 \times 10.68 \approx 342$ ms. This sets the **scan-time lower bound** for SCADA polling loops — a key KTU design parameter.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Shannon Capacity Bound Applied to Industrial Cable

An RS-485 segment on a **Profinet/Profibus** cable has:

- Bandwidth: $B = 20$ MHz (typical for the cable's rated pass-band),
- Measured SNR: $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 40$ dB.

Compute Shannon capacity:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + 10^{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10}\right)
$$

$$
10^{40/10} = 10^{4} = 10000
$$

$$
C = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1 + 10000)
$$

$$
C = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(10001)
$$

Since $\log_2(10001) \approx 13.288$:

$$
C \approx 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot 13.288
$$

$$
C \approx 2.658 \times 10^{8}\,\text{bits/s} \approx 265.8\,\text{Mbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The actual Profibus DP rate of **12 Mbps** uses only **~4.5 %** of the Shannon capacity. The remaining margin is the **engineering safety headroom** against EMI from VFDs, motor contactors, and welding inverters in plant environments.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Mode Comparison — Analytical View} \\
&\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Parameter} & \textbf{Simplex} & \textbf{Half-Duplex} & \textbf{Full-Duplex} \\
\hline
\text{Direction} & A \to B \text{ only} & A \leftrightarrow B \text{ (time-shared)} & A \leftrightarrow B \text{ (simultaneous)} \\
\hline
\eta_{\max}\text{ per dir.} & 1 \cdot R_b & 0.5 \cdot R_b & 1 \cdot R_b \\
\hline
\text{Wire count} & 2 \text{ (or 1 + GND)} & 2 \text{ (twisted pair)} & 4 \text{ (2 pairs) or 2 fibers} \\
\hline
\text{MAC required} & \text{No} & \text{Yes (token/CSMA)} & \text{No (collision-free)} \\
\hline
\text{Typical industrial use} & 4{-}20\,\text{mA, alarm} & \text{RS-485, Profibus DP} & \text{ProfiNet, EtherNet/IP} \\
\hline
\text{Determinism} & \text{Highest (no contention)} & \text{High (token)} & \text{High (IRT)} \\
\hline
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Transmission-Mode Architectural Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional decision tree** an industrial-network designer follows when selecting a transmission mode for a new field segment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start: Plant Segment Specification]) --> nodeB{Is reverse traffic required?}
    nodeB -- No --> nodeC[Choose Simplex Mode]
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeD{Is simultaneous bidirectional needed?}
    nodeD -- No --> nodeE[Choose Half-Duplex Mode]
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeF[Choose Full-Duplex Mode]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Example: 4-20 mA sensor loop]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Example: RS-485 two-wire bus]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Example: PROFINET 100BASE-TX]
    nodeG --> nodeJ([End: Cabling and MAC finalized])
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

### 4.2 Channel-Utilization Block Diagram

This block diagram shows the **time-domain occupancy** of the physical channel by each mode, which is the conceptual basis for the throughput formulas derived in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph nodeCh["Channel Time Axis T"]
        nodeT1["t0"]:::marker
        nodeT2["t1"]:::marker
        nodeT3["t2"]:::marker
        nodeT4["t3"]:::marker
    end
    nodeSx["Simplex: A sends entire T"]:::simplex
    nodeHd["Half Duplex: A then B alternating"]:::half
    nodeFd["Full Duplex: A and B concurrent"]:::full
    nodeT1 --> nodeSx
    nodeT1 --> nodeHd
    nodeT1 --> nodeFd
    classDef marker fill:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef simplex fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    classDef half fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    classDef full fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

### 4.3 Industrial-Protocol-to-Mode Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeRoot([Industrial Network Standards]) --> nodeGrp1["Serial Fieldbus Family"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeGrp2["Industrial Ethernet Family"]
    nodeGrp1 --> nodeRs232["RS-232 (Simplex / Full-Duplex PTP)"]
    nodeGrp1 --> nodeRs485["RS-485 (Half-Duplex Multidrop)"]
    nodeGrp1 --> nodeRs422["RS-422 (Full-Duplex PTP)"]
    nodeGrp1 --> nodeProfibus["Profibus DP (Half-Duplex Token)"]
    nodeGrp2 --> nodeProfinet["PROFINET IRT (Full-Duplex)"]
    nodeGrp2 --> nodeEthernet["EtherNet/IP (Full-Duplex Switched)"]
    nodeGrp2 --> nodeModbusTcp["Modbus TCP (Full-Duplex)"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams:** notice that *every* Industrial Ethernet protocol is **full-duplex by design** because of switched Ethernet's collision-free nature, whereas *every* legacy serial fieldbus is **half-duplex** because of the shared-medium cost economics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Define the three transmission modes used in industrial communication. Give **one industrial protocol example** for each.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Simplex** — Communication occurs in **one direction only**. Example: a 4–20 mA current loop from a temperature transmitter to a PLC analog input card.
2. **Half-Duplex** — Both stations can transmit, but **not simultaneously**; the channel is shared in time using a MAC mechanism. Example: **Profibus DP** over RS-485 (two-wire) with token-passing.
3. **Full-Duplex** — Both stations transmit **simultaneously**, typically on physically separated channels (separate wire pairs or fibers). Example: **PROFINET IRT** on 100BASE-TX Industrial Ethernet.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write "Simplex = unidirectional" without naming the **MAC coordination** required for half-duplex. Examiners specifically deduct 1 mark if no MAC mechanism (e.g., token, polling, CSMA) is mentioned.

---

#### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Differentiate between **serial** and **parallel** transmission. State **two situations** in industrial networks where serial transmission is preferred.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Serial | Parallel |
|--------|--------|----------|
| Wires used | 1 data line (+ ground/clock) | $n$ data lines (e.g., 8, 16, 32) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Crosstalk | Low | High at high frequencies |
| Speed (short distance) | Lower per-bit | Higher per-clock |
| Skew problem | None | Significant above 1 MHz |

**Industrial situations preferring serial transmission:**

1. **Long cable runs** between PLC and remote I/O racks (e.g., RS-485 over 1200 m).
2. **EMI-rich environments** such as motor-control cabinets where parallel cables act as antennas picking up noise.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Pattern — Module-3 Internal Choice: Answer ONE of the two.]**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **operational differences between simplex, half-duplex, and full-duplex transmission modes** with suitable **industrial-protocol examples**. Draw a labelled functional block diagram showing how a half-duplex token-passing bus handles two stations A and B exchanging cyclic process data. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A Profibus DP segment operates at **1.5 Mbps** half-duplex over RS-485 with a token-rotation overhead of **800 µs per cycle**. Two stations A and B each transmit **256-byte** process-data frames cyclically. Calculate:
- (i) the bit duration $T_b$,
- (ii) the frame transmission time for one station,
- (iii) the effective per-direction throughput,
- (iv) the **scan time** for one complete A→B→A cycle, assuming overhead is added once per cycle. **[7 Marks]**

##### Model Solution for (a)

The three modes are described in Section 1.1. The block diagram for the half-duplex token bus:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeMA["Station A PLC"]:::a
    nodeTB["Token Frame"]:::tok
    nodeMB["Station B Remote I/O"]:::b
    nodeMA -- "tA: hold token" --> nodeMB
    nodeMB -- "return token" --> nodeMA
    nodeMA --> nodeTB
    nodeTB --> nodeMB
    classDef a fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    classDef b fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    classDef tok fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
```

**Explanation:** Station A holds the token for time $t_A$, transmits a 256-byte frame to B, then relinquishes the token. Station B repeats the action. Total cycle $T_{\text{cycle}} = t_A + t_B + t_{\text{overhead}}$.

---

##### Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Bit duration:**

$$
T_b = \dfrac{1}{R_b} = \dfrac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_b \approx 6.667 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{s} = 0.6667\,\mu\text{s/bit}
$$

**(ii) Frame transmission time for 256 bytes** (each frame also has a 9-byte Profibus overhead in practice, but the problem statement asks for the *frame size given*):

$$
N_{\text{bits}} = 256 \times 8 = 2048\,\text{bits}
$$

$$
t_f = N_{\text{bits}} \times T_b = 2048 \times 0.6667 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
t_f \approx 1.365\,\text{ms}
$$

**(iii) Effective per-direction throughput (symmetric case $t_A = t_B$):**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{overhead}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}\right)
$$

With $T_{\text{cycle}} = t_A + t_B + t_{\text{overhead}} = 2(1.365) + 0.8 = 3.53$ ms:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{6}}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{0.8}{3.53}\right)
$$

$$
\eta = 750\,000 \times (1 - 0.2266)
$$

$$
\eta = 750\,000 \times 0.7734
$$

$$
\eta \approx 5.80 \times 10^{5}\,\text{bits/s} \approx 580\,\text{kbps per direction}
$$

**(iv) Scan time for A → B → A cycle** (one full token rotation = A→B + B→A + overhead):

$$
T_{\text{scan}} = t_A + t_B + T_{\text{overhead}} = 1.365 + 1.365 + 0.8
$$

$$
T_{\text{scan}} \approx 3.53\,\text{ms}
$$

**Final Answer:** $T_b \approx 0.667\,\mu$s, $t_f \approx 1.365$ ms, $\eta \approx 580$ kbps, $T_{\text{scan}} \approx 3.53$ ms.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a **block-level diagram**, explain how **full-duplex communication** is achieved in a switched Industrial Ethernet network. Compare its determinism with that of a half-duplex shared RS-485 bus. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A shielded twisted-pair industrial cable has a usable bandwidth of **30 MHz** and a measured SNR of **36 dB**. Using the Shannon–Hartley theorem, compute:
- (i) the theoretical channel capacity,
- (ii) the **maximum number of full-duplex voice-grade channels** of **64 kbps** each that the link can carry if it operates in full-duplex mode using frequency-division separation. **[7 Marks]**

##### Model Solution for (a)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodePLC["PLC Ethernet Port"]:::p
    nodeSw["Industrial Switch (Store & Forward)"]:::s
    nodeDrv["Servo Drive Port"]:::d
    nodeHmi["HMI Panel"]:::h
    nodePLC -- "Full-Duplex TX pair" --> nodeSw
    nodeSw -- "Full-Duplex TX pair" --> nodeDrv
    nodeSw -- "Full-Duplex TX pair" --> nodeHmi
    nodeDrv -- "Separate RX pair" --> nodeSw
    nodeHmi -- "Separate RX pair" --> nodeSw
    classDef p fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    classDef s fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    classDef d fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    classDef h fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
```

**Determinism comparison:**

| Aspect | Half-Duplex RS-485 (Profibus) | Full-Duplex Switched Ethernet |
|--------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Contention | Possible (token needed) | None (point-to-point switch port) |
| Worst-case latency | Bounded by token-rotation time | Bounded by switch store-forward delay ($\le 10\,\mu$s) |
| Isochronous support | Limited (DP-V2) | Native (PROFINET IRT) |

---

##### Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Shannon capacity:**

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + 10^{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10}\right)
$$

$$
10^{36/10} = 10^{3.6} \approx 3981.07
$$

$$
C = 30 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1 + 3981.07)
$$

$$
C = 30 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(3982.07)
$$

Since $\log_2(3982.07) \approx 11.96$:

$$
C \approx 30 \times 10^{6} \cdot 11.96
$$

$$
C \approx 3.588 \times 10^{8}\,\text{bits/s} \approx 358.8\,\text{Mbps}
$$

**(ii) Maximum number of full-duplex 64-kbps voice-grade channels:**

For full-duplex FDD, each channel consumes $2 \times 64$ kbps = 128 kbps of bidirectional bandwidth:

$$
N_{\text{channels}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{C}{2 \times 64\,000}\right\rfloor
$$

$$
N_{\text{channels}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{358.8 \times 10^{6}}{128\,000}\right\rfloor
$$

$$
N_{\text{channels}} = \left\lfloor 2803.1\right\rfloor = 2803
$$

**Final Answer:** $C \approx 358.8$ Mbps; **2803 full-duplex 64-kbps channels**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss points in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing duplex with synchronous** — "full-duplex" is *not* the same as "synchronous transmission." Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in half-duplex throughput** — students often write $\eta = R_b$ for half-duplex, losing 2 marks.
> 3. **No unit conversion in Shannon** — writing SNR in dB directly inside $\log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}})$ is incorrect; the dB must be converted back to a ratio *first* (i.e., $10^{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10}$).
> 4. **No MAC mention for half-duplex** — always name the arbitration scheme (token, polling, CSMA-CD).
> 5. **Cable/cost implications omitted** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes *engineering judgment*; a one-line note on cabling cost or EMI tolerance fetches an extra mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Simplex** = one-way; **Half-Duplex** = two-way *time-shared*; **Full-Duplex** = two-way *simultaneous*.
- **Serial transmission** dominates industrial fieldbuses; **parallel** is reserved for short backplane buses.
- **Half-duplex per-direction throughput** $\le \dfrac{R_b}{2}$.
- **Full-duplex aggregate throughput** $\le 2 \cdot R_b$.
- **Shannon capacity** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — never forget the dB-to-linear conversion.
- **Nyquist capacity** $C = 2B \log_2 M$ — applies only in noiseless channels.
- **MAC coordination** is mandatory for half-duplex (token / polling / CSMA).
- **Profibus DP** = canonical half-duplex industrial example.
- **PROFINET / EtherNet/IP / Modbus TCP** = canonical full-duplex industrial examples.
- **4–20 mA current loop** = canonical simplex industrial example.
- **RS-485 (2-wire)** = half-duplex multidrop; **RS-422 (4-wire)** = full-duplex point-to-point.
- **Industrial Ethernet switched topology** is *inherently collision-free*, hence full-duplex.
- Always cite the **MAC mechanism** when describing half-duplex operation in the exam.
- **Scan time** for cyclic polling = (frames per slave) × (frame time + silence).
- **Token rotation overhead** degrades half-duplex throughput as $T_{\text{overhead}} / T_{\text{cycle}}$ grows.
- **Shannon gives the ceiling**; real protocols achieve 40–70 % of $C$.
- **Engineering trade-off:** duplex mode choice ↔ cabling cost ↔ determinism ↔ cycle time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MODBUS Message Framing and Data Highway
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MODBUS Message Framing and Data Highway

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — MODBUS Message Framing and Data Highway

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is MODBUS?

**MODBUS** is an open, royalty-free, serial communication protocol originally published by **Modicon (now Schneider Electric) in 1979** for use with its Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs). It has become a *de facto* standard communication protocol in industrial automation, connecting electronic devices across diverse buses and networks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** MODBUS is the most widely used industrial protocol in the world. According to the official MODBUS Organization, the protocol is deployed in millions of automation devices across process, manufacturing, building automation, and energy sectors.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
> *MODBUS is an application-layer messaging protocol positioned at level 7 of the OSI model, providing client/server (historically master/slave) communication between devices connected on different types of buses or networks.*

### 1.2 What is Message Framing?

**Message Framing** is the structured encapsulation of payload data with addressing, control, and integrity-check fields so that a receiver can:
1. Detect the **start** of a message.
2. Identify **who** is being addressed.
3. Determine **what action** is being requested.
4. Extract the **data payload**.
5. Verify **data integrity** and detect the **end** of the message.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Think of message framing like a *registered postal letter* in the 1990s. Before the letter reaches you, it is placed inside a **standard envelope** that has a fixed format — a stamp in the top-right corner (start marker), a sender's address in the top-left (source address), a recipient's address in the center (destination address), a subject line in the middle (function code), the actual letter inside (data), and a postal seal at the bottom (error check). Even if the post office shuffles thousands of letters, it always knows where each letter starts, who it is for, and whether it was damaged in transit. **MODBUS message framing works exactly the same way.**

### 1.3 What is a Data Highway?

A **Data Highway (DH)** is a *proprietary* industrial local area network (LAN) protocol developed by **Allen-Bradley (now Rockwell Automation)** in the early 1980s. It is designed for communication between PLCs (particularly the PLC-2, PLC-3, and PLC-5 families) and is structurally similar to MODBUS but tailored for Allen-Bradley/Rockwell's automation ecosystem.

**Data Highway Plus (DH+)** is an enhanced, token-passing peer-to-peer version of the original Data Highway, supporting higher speeds and multi-master communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does a KTU student need to know both MODBUS and Data Highway?** While MODBUS is *open* and used by virtually every automation vendor, **Data Highway and DH+ are widely deployed in legacy Allen-Bradley/Rockwell installations** across Kerala's process industries (Cochin Shipyard, FACT, BPCL, and HOCL). Understanding both protocols is essential for plant integration, retrofitting, and inter-vendor gateway design.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Parameter | Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| **MODBUS Origin Year** | **1979 (Modicon)** |
| **OSI Layer** | **Layer 7 (Application Layer)** |
| **Underlying Standards** | **RS-232, RS-422, RS-485, TCP/IP (Modbus TCP)** |
| **MODBUS Standard Document** | **MODBUS Application Protocol Specification V1.1b3** |
| **Data Highway Standard** | **Allen-Bradley proprietary, IEEE 802.4 Token Bus** |
| **Maximum Devices on DH+** | **64 nodes** |
| **Maximum Devices on MODBUS RTU** | **247 slaves (addresses 1–247)** |
| **MODBUS Reserved Address** | **0 (broadcast)** |
| **DH+ Token Rotation** | **Token-passing with logical ring** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 MODBUS Protocol Stack — Where Does Framing Live?

MODBUS operates in two distinct serial modes — **RTU (Remote Terminal Unit)** and **ASCII** — both running over standard serial hardware (RS-232, RS-422, RS-485). The **message framing** is the key difference between the two modes.

$$
\text{MODBUS Serial} = \begin{cases} \text{MODBUS RTU} \longrightarrow \text{Binary framing with CRC-16} \\ \text{MODBUS ASCII} \longrightarrow \text{Hexadecimal ASCII framing with LRC} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"How does MODBUS detect the start and end of a message?"* — the answer differs by mode. **RTU** uses **3.5 character silence**; **ASCII** uses the **colon `:` start character** and **CRLF (`\r\n`) end characters**.

### 2.2 MODBUS RTU Message Frame Structure

The **MODBUS RTU frame** is a **binary, compact, time-silenced** frame. The structure is:

| Field | Size (bytes) | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Start** | **3.5 char times of silence** | Marks the beginning of a new frame |
| **Address Field** | **1 byte (8 bits)** | Slave address: 1–247; 0 = broadcast |
| **Function Code** | **1 byte (8 bits)** | 1–127 valid; 128–255 = exception responses |
| **Data Field** | **N bytes (variable)** | 0–252 bytes of payload (registers, coils, etc.) |
| **CRC-16** | **2 bytes (16 bits)** | Cyclic Redundancy Check, low byte first |
| **End** | **3.5 char times of silence** | Marks the end of the frame |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum RTU Frame Size = 256 bytes** (Address + Function + Data + CRC). The data field itself can hold up to **252 bytes** of payload.

### 2.3 MODBUS ASCII Message Frame Structure

The **MODBUS ASCII frame** is a **human-readable, slower, character-framed** version using printable ASCII characters.

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Start** | **1 character** | Colon `:` (ASCII 0x3A) |
| **Address Field** | **2 hex characters (1 byte)** | Hex-encoded slave address |
| **Function Code** | **2 hex characters (1 byte)** | Hex-encoded function code |
| **Data Field** | **2N hex characters** | Hex-encoded payload |
| **LRC** | **2 hex characters (1 byte)** | Longitudinal Redundancy Check |
| **End** | **2 characters** | `CR` (0x0D) + `LF` (0x0A) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage of ASCII mode:** Human-readable on a serial terminal — engineers can *see* the traffic. **Disadvantage:** Doubles the byte count compared to RTU and runs at lower speeds (typically up to 19,200 bps vs. RTU's 115,200 bps).

### 2.4 The "Why" and "How" Behind Framing Logic — Step-by-Step

**Why address field of only 1 byte?**
The original MODBUS was designed for a single Modicon PLC controlling up to 247 remote I/O drops. The 1-byte address supports 0–255, with 248–255 reserved for future use, yielding 247 usable slave addresses.

**Why 3.5 character silence in RTU?**
- At a given baud rate, a *character time* is the time to transmit one byte (start + data + parity + stop bits).
- A silence of **3.5 character times** is unique because it can *never* occur *within* a valid frame (the longest legal inter-character gap inside a frame is **1.5 character times**).
- This makes framing *self-synchronizing* without needing explicit start/end markers, which is critical for robust industrial noise.

$$
T_{\text{char}} = \frac{11 \text{ bits}}{B \text{ bps}} \quad ; \quad T_{\text{frame silence}} = 3.5 \times T_{\text{char}}
$$

> **Example:** At 9600 bps with 11-bit characters, $T_{\text{char}} = 11/9600 \approx 1.146$ ms. So the inter-frame silence = **3.5 × 1.146 ms ≈ 4.01 ms**.

**Why CRC-16 in RTU but LRC in ASCII?**
- **RTU** uses **CRC-16 (Cyclic Redundancy Check, polynomial 0xA001)** for strong error detection (99.9984% detection of all single-bit and most multi-bit errors).
- **ASCII** uses **LRC (Longitudinal Redundancy Check)** — a simple 8-bit sum complement — because ASCII mode is intended for low-noise, short-distance, low-speed links where CRC overhead is not justified.

**Why function codes?**
Function codes standardize the *kind of operation* requested. A slave sees the function code and routes the request to the appropriate internal register or coil handler. This decoupling is what makes MODBUS *modular* and *vendor-agnostic*.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this entire table** — it is the single most-tested block in MODBUS questions at KTU.

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Unit | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Character time** | $T_{\text{char}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{bits/char}}}{B_{\text{baud}}}$ | seconds (s) | Timing for RTU frame silence |
| **Inter-frame silence (RTU)** | $T_{\text{silence}} = 3.5 \times T_{\text{char}}$ | seconds (s) | Marker between RTU frames |
