| **Inter-character gap (RTU)** | $T_{\text{icg}} = 1.5 \times T_{\text{char}}$ | seconds (s) | Max allowed gap *within* a frame |
| **Max RTU frame size** | $1 + 1 + N_{\text{data}} + 2 = 256$ | bytes | Hard limit on RTU frame |
| **Max data bytes (RTU)** | $N_{\text{data,max}} = 252$ | bytes | Largest legal data field |
| **CRC-16 polynomial** | $0xA001$ (reversed of $0x8005$) | — | MODBUS RTU error check |
| **LRC computation** | $\text{LRC} = [-\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{byte}_i] \pmod{256}$ | — | MODBUS ASCII error check |
| **Broadcast address** | $A = 0$ | — | All slaves listen, no reply |
| **Valid slave addresses** | $1 \leq A \leq 247$ | — | Unique per bus |
| **Function code exception** | $\text{Response} = \text{FC} + 0x80$ | — | Indicates error from slave |
| **MODBUS data memory** | Coils, Discrete Inputs, Input Registers, Holding Registers | — | Four primary register types |
| **Max MODBUS devices** | $247$ slaves (address 0 reserved) | — | Per RS-485 bus |
| **DH+ token hold time** | $\approx 38.4$ ms (typical) | ms | Per node per token rotation |
| **DH+ maximum nodes** | $64$ | — | Logical ring size |
| **DH+ speed** | $57.6$ kbps or $230.4$ kbps | kbps | Physical layer rate |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Process Industries (Kerala — FACT, BPCL, HOCL):** MODBUS RTU over RS-485 connects field instruments (pressure, flow, level transmitters) to SCADA/DCS.
- **Power & Energy:** Substation automation uses **MODBUS TCP** (over Ethernet) for IEDs (Intelligent Electronic Devices) and metering.
- **Building Automation:** HVAC, lighting, and energy meters communicate via MODBUS to Building Management Systems.
- **Water Treatment Plants:** PLC-to-PLC and PLC-to-VFD links commonly use MODBUS RTU.
- **Manufacturing:** DH+ is the *legacy backbone* of Allen-Bradley PLC-5 and SLC-500 networks — many Indian automotive and packaging plants still depend on it.
- **Modern IIoT:** MODBUS remains a critical edge protocol, with gateways translating it to MQTT, OPC-UA, or cloud APIs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Character Time at Various Baud Rates

We are asked to compute the inter-frame silence for a MODBUS RTU bus running at 9600 bps, 8 data bits, no parity, 2 stop bits (the most common configuration: 8N2).

**Step 1 — Determine the total bits per character.**

$$
N_{\text{bits/char}} = 1_{\text{start}} + 8_{\text{data}} + 0_{\text{parity}} + 2_{\text{stop}} = 11 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the character time formula.**

$$
T_{\text{char}} = \frac{N_{\text{bits/char}}}{B_{\text{baud}}} = \frac{11}{9600} \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
T_{\text{char}} = 0.0011458\overline{3} \text{ s} \approx 1.146 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute inter-frame silence (3.5 character times).**

$$
T_{\text{silence}} = 3.5 \times T_{\text{char}} = 3.5 \times 1.146 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{\text{silence}} \approx 4.011 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the maximum allowed inter-character gap inside a frame (1.5 character times).**

$$
T_{\text{icg,max}} = 1.5 \times T_{\text{char}} = 1.5 \times 1.146 \text{ ms} \approx 1.719 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A receiver waits 1.5 character times to detect a *byte boundary* inside a frame, and 3.5 character times to declare the frame *complete*. Any byte arriving after 1.5 character times causes the receiver to discard the partial frame and wait for the next 3.5-character silence.

**Step 5 — Generalize the formula for arbitrary baud rate $B$ with 8N2 framing.**

$$
T_{\text{silence}}(B) = \frac{3.5 \times 11}{B} = \frac{38.5}{B} \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{silence}} = \frac{38.5}{B} \text{ s, with } B \text{ in bps}}
$$

| Baud Rate (bps) | $T_{\text{char}}$ (ms) | $T_{\text{silence}}$ (ms) | $T_{\text{icg,max}}$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1,200 | 9.167 | 32.083 | 13.750 |
| 9,600 | 1.146 | 4.011 | 1.719 |
| 19,200 | 0.573 | 2.005 | 0.859 |
| 38,400 | 0.286 | 1.003 | 0.430 |
| 115,200 | 0.0955 | 0.334 | 0.143 |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: LRC Computation for MODBUS ASCII

**Problem:** Compute the LRC for a MODBUS ASCII message with the following byte payload (hex): `01 03 00 00 00 0A`.

**Step 1 — Sum all bytes of the message (excluding the start colon, end CRLF, and LRC itself).**

$$
S = 0x01 + 0x03 + 0x00 + 0x00 + 0x00 + 0x0A
$$

$$
S = 0x01 + 0x03 + 0x00 + 0x00 + 0x00 + 0x0A
$$

Carrying the arithmetic carefully:

$$
0x01 + 0x03 = 0x04
$$

$$
0x04 + 0x00 = 0x04
$$

$$
0x04 + 0x00 = 0x04
$$

$$
0x04 + 0x00 = 0x04
$$

$$
0x04 + 0x0A = 0x0E
$$

$$
S = 0x0E = 14_{10}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the two's complement of the low-order 8 bits (i.e., negate modulo 256).**

$$
\text{LRC} = (-S) \pmod{256} = (256 - S) \pmod{256}
$$

$$
\text{LRC} = (256 - 14) \pmod{256} = 242 \pmod{256}
$$

$$
\text{LRC} = 242_{10} = 0xF2
$$

**Step 3 — Form the full ASCII frame.**

The full frame (with colon, LRC, CR, LF) becomes:

$$
: \, \texttt{01 03 00 00 00 0A F2} \, \texttt{CR LF}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification by re-summing.**

A valid LRC satisfies: **the sum of all bytes (including LRC) equals zero (mod 256).**

$$
S_{\text{verify}} = 0x01 + 0x03 + 0x00 + 0x00 + 0x00 + 0x0A + 0xF2
$$

$$
0x0E + 0xF2 = 0x100 \equiv 0x00 \pmod{256} \;\;\checkmark
$$

> **Interpretation:** The verification confirms our LRC is correct. This is the standard check implemented in MODBUS ASCII receivers.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: CRC-16 (MODBUS) — Conceptual Walkthrough

The MODBUS RTU CRC-16 uses the polynomial $0xA001$ (bit-reversed form of $0x8005$). The algorithm:

**Step 1 — Initialize the CRC register to `0xFFFF`.**

$$
\text{CRC} = 0xFFFF
$$

**Step 2 — XOR the first message byte with the low byte of CRC.**

$$
\text{CRC}_{\text{lo}} = \text{CRC}_{\text{lo}} \oplus \text{Byte}_1
$$

**Step 3 — Loop 8 times (one per bit of the byte):**
- If the LSB of CRC = 1: shift CRC right by 1 bit, then XOR with `0xA001`.
- If the LSB of CRC = 0: just shift CRC right by 1 bit.

**Step 4 — After processing all bytes, the final 16-bit value is the CRC.**

**Step 5 — The CRC is appended to the frame as **low byte first**, then high byte.**

> [!NOTE]
> For a 14-mark question, examiners usually do **not** require you to perform a full CRC hand-calculation (it is tedious and error-prone). Instead, the KTU 2024 pattern asks you to:
> 1. **State the polynomial** (`0xA001`).
> 2. **Describe the algorithm** (initial value `0xFFFF`, XOR, shift, conditional XOR).
> 3. **State the field order** (low byte first, then high byte).
> 4. **Explain why CRC is used** (vs. parity or checksum).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: MODBUS RTU vs ASCII vs Data Highway vs DH+

| Parameter | MODBUS RTU | MODBUS ASCII | Data Highway (DH) | Data Highway Plus (DH+) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Year / Origin** | 1979 (Modicon) | 1979 (Modicon) | Early 1980s (Allen-Bradley) | 1986 (Allen-Bradley) |
| **Topology** | Master–Slave | Master–Slave | Master–Slave (token-passing) | Peer-to-peer (token bus) |
| **Physical Layer** | RS-232/422/485 | RS-232/422/485 | RS-232 (or RS-422) | RS-422 (differential) |
| **Speed** | Up to 115.2 kbps | Up to 19.2 kbps | 57.6 kbps | 57.6 / 230.4 kbps |
| **Max Devices** | 247 slaves | 247 slaves | 64 nodes | 64 nodes |
| **Frame Start** | 3.5 char silence | `:` (colon) | STX byte (0x02) | STX byte (0x02) |
| **Frame End** | 3.5 char silence | CR+LF | ETX/ETB byte | ETX byte + BCC |
| **Error Check** | CRC-16 | LRC (1 byte) | BCC (Block Check Char) | BCC + TNS check |
| **Address Field Size** | 1 byte | 1 byte (hex-encoded) | 1 byte (source) + 1 byte (dest) | 2 bytes |
| **Token Passing** | No | No | No (polling) | **Yes (IEEE 802.4 token bus)** |
| **Open / Proprietary** | **Open** | **Open** | Proprietary | Proprietary |
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 7 | Layer 7 | Layer 2 + 7 | Layer 2 + 7 |
| **Modern Successor** | MODBUS TCP | (Mostly retired) | EtherNet/IP | EtherNet/IP |

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how MODBUS and DH+ have **converged functionally** in modern plants. Most new Allen-Bradley deployments use **EtherNet/IP** (CIP over Ethernet), but DH+ is still maintained in brownfield plants. MODBUS, by contrast, has *grown* — Modbus TCP, Modbus over UDP, Modbus over TLS — making it the *most durable* industrial protocol of the past 45+ years.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MODBUS RTU Frame Layout — Master-Slave Transaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Master["Master Device (e.g., SCADA / HMI)"]
        M1[1. Silence 3.5 char]
        M2[2. Address Byte - 0x01 to 0xF7]
        M3[3. Function Code - 0x01 to 0x7F]
        M4[4. Data - 0 to 252 bytes]
        M5[5. CRC-16 Low Byte]
        M6[6. CRC-16 High Byte]
        M7[7. Silence 3.5 char]
    end

    BUS[RS-485 Differential Bus - Twisted Pair]

    subgraph Slave["Slave Device (e.g., VFD / RTU)"]
        S1[Receive and Validate Silence]
        S2[Match Address - else ignore]
        S3[Decode Function Code]
        S4[Execute Action on Coils or Registers]
        S5[Compute Response CRC-16]
    end

    M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4 --> M5 --> M6 --> M7 --> BUS
    BUS --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The Master device transmits a complete RTU frame over the RS-485 bus. The **3.5-character silences** at the start and end are *invisible* in the data stream but are detected by the receiver's UART timing logic.

### 4.2 MODBUS RTU Frame — Byte-by-Byte Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[3.5 Char Silence] --> Addr[Address Field - 1 byte - 0x01 to 0xF7]
    Addr --> FC[Function Code - 1 byte - 0x01 to 0x7F]
    FC --> Data[Data Field - 0 to 252 bytes - depends on FC]
    Data --> CRCL[CRC-16 Low Byte]
    CRCL --> CRCH[CRC-16 High Byte]
    CRCH --> End[3.5 Char Silence]

    subgraph Annotations
        A1[Address 0 = Broadcast - no reply]
        A2[FC 0x80 or higher = Exception Response]
        A3[Max Frame = 256 bytes total]
    end
```

### 4.3 MODBUS ASCII Frame — Character-by-Character Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Colon : - 0x3A"] --> B["Address - 2 hex chars - e.g. 0x01"]
    B --> C["Function - 2 hex chars - e.g. 0x03"]
    C --> D["Data - 2N hex chars - payload"]
    D --> E["LRC - 2 hex chars"]
    E --> F["CR - 0x0D"]
    F --> G["LF - 0x0A"]
```

### 4.4 Data Highway Plus (DH+) — Token-Passing Logical Ring

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LogicalRing["DH+ Token-Passing Logical Ring"]
        direction LR
        N1[Node 1 - PLC-5] -->|Token| N2[Node 2 - SLC 5/04]
        N2 -->|Token| N3[Node 3 - PanelView]
        N3 -->|Token| N4[Node 4 - Bridge]
        N4 -->|Token| N5[Node 5 - Drive]
        N5 -->|Token| N1
    end

    HMI[External HMI - Listens Only] -.->|Poll via Token| LogicalRing
    SCADA[SCADA - Bridges to Ethernet] -.-> LogicalRing
```

> **Engineering Insight:** Unlike MODBUS (where only the master initiates), **DH+ allows any node holding the token to initiate communication**. This makes DH+ a **true peer-to-peer** network, while MODBUS RTU remains **strictly master-slave**.

### 4.5 CRC-16 Generation Process (Logical Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Init[Initialize CRC = 0xFFFF] --> LoopStart{For each byte in frame}
    LoopStart -->|Next Byte| XOR1[XOR byte with CRC low]
    XOR1 --> InnerLoop{8 iterations}
    InnerLoop -->|Test LSB of CRC| Cond{LSB = 1?}
    Cond -->|Yes| ShiftXOR[Shift CRC right by 1 bit, XOR with 0xA001]
    Cond -->|No| ShiftOnly[Shift CRC right by 1 bit]
    ShiftXOR --> InnerLoop
    ShiftOnly --> InnerLoop
    InnerLoop -->|All 8 bits done| LoopStart
    LoopStart -->|All bytes done| Final[Final CRC = 16-bit value]
    Final --> Append[Append to frame: low byte first, then high byte]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2, Q2(a)]**

> *With a neat diagram, explain the MODBUS RTU message framing format. List all fields with their sizes.*

**Model Answer:**

The MODBUS RTU (Remote Terminal Unit) message frame is a binary, time-silenced frame used in serial MODBUS communication. It is structured as follows:

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Start | 3.5 character times of silence | Marks the beginning of a new frame |
| Address Field | 1 byte (8 bits) | Slave address; 0 = broadcast, 1–247 = valid, 248–255 = reserved |
| Function Code | 1 byte (8 bits) | 1–127 = valid requests; 128–255 = exception responses |
| Data Field | 0–252 bytes | Carries register/coil addresses, values, or status |
| CRC-16 | 2 bytes (16 bits) | Cyclic Redundancy Check (polynomial 0xA001); low byte first |
| End | 3.5 character times of silence | Marks the end of the frame |

**Key points to remember:**
- The maximum RTU frame size is **256 bytes** total.
- The data field supports up to **252 bytes** of payload.
- The 3.5-character silence rule makes the frame *self-synchronizing* without explicit start/end markers.
- The function code byte is **echoed** in the response (or set to FC + 0x80 for exceptions).

**Frame Diagram (ASCII representation):**

$$
\underbrace{\text{Silence}}_{3.5\,t_c} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Addr}}_{1B} \;\; \underbrace{\text{FC}}_{1B} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Data}}_{0\text{–}252B} \;\; \underbrace{\text{CRC-Lo}}_{1B} \;\; \underbrace{\text{CRC-Hi}}_{1B} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Silence}}_{3.5\,t_c}
$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper, Q1(c)]**

> *Compare MODBUS RTU and MODBUS ASCII message framing in terms of (i) start/end delimiters, (ii) error checking, and (iii) typical applications.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | MODBUS RTU | MODBUS ASCII |
|---|---|---|
| **Start Delimiter** | 3.5 character times of silence (invisible) | `:` (colon, 0x3A) — visible ASCII character |
| **End Delimiter** | 3.5 character times of silence (invisible) | `CR` (0x0D) + `LF` (0x0A) — visible ASCII |
| **Encoding** | Binary (raw 8-bit bytes) | Hexadecimal ASCII (each byte = 2 characters) |
| **Error Check** | CRC-16 (polynomial 0xA001), 16-bit | LRC (Longitudinal Redundancy Check), 8-bit |
| **Frame Size** | Up to 256 bytes | Up to 513 ASCII characters |
| **Max Speed** | Up to 115.2 kbps | Typically up to 19.2 kbps (slow due to 7-bit data + 2× overhead) |
| **Human Readability** | Not human-readable (binary) | Human-readable (printable ASCII) |
| **Error Detection Strength** | Very high (CRC-16 catches all 1-bit and most multi-bit errors) | Moderate (LRC is a simple 1-byte sum) |
| **Typical Applications** | Industrial RS-485 networks, SCADA, VFDs, instrumentation | Low-speed diagnostic links, programming terminals, troubleshooting |
| **Efficiency** | High (compact binary) | Low (doubles byte count) |

**Conclusion:** MODBUS **RTU** is preferred for production industrial networks due to its speed, efficiency, and stronger error detection. MODBUS **ASCII** is used when human readability and ease of debugging are priorities (e.g., during commissioning or with a serial terminal).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q11(a) & (b) Pattern]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a MODBUS network showing the master and multiple slaves. Describe in detail the MODBUS RTU message frame structure with field sizes, function codes, and CRC-16 error checking. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compute the inter-frame silence time for a MODBUS RTU link operating at (i) 9600 bps and (ii) 19200 bps, assuming 8N2 framing (1 start, 8 data, 0 parity, 2 stop bits). If a slave receives a byte gap greater than 1.5 character times, what happens? Justify the choice of 3.5 character times for inter-frame marking. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Architecture and Frame Structure**

A MODBUS network is built on a **master-slave architecture** over a serial bus (typically RS-485). A single master device (e.g., a SCADA system, HMI, or PLC) polls one or more slave devices (e.g., VFDs, RTUs, smart sensors) over a shared differential twisted pair.

**Architectural Diagram:**

```
       ┌──────────────┐
       │  MASTER      │
       │  (SCADA/HMI) │
       └──────┬───────┘
              │ RS-485 differential bus
   ┌──────────┼──────────┬──────────┐
   ▼          ▼          ▼          ▼
 Slave 1  Slave 2  Slave 3  Slave 247
 (VFD)   (RTU)    (Sensor) (Meter)
 0x01     0x02     0x03     0xF7
```

**RTU Message Frame Structure:**

| Field | Size (bytes) | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Address | 1 | Slave address (1–247) |
| Function Code | 1 | Operation code (1–127) |
| Data | 0–252 | Request/response payload |
| CRC-16 | 2 | Error check (low byte first) |
| **Total** | **4–256** | Maximum 256 bytes |

**Function Code Categories (must memorize):**
- `01` — Read Coils
- `02` — Read Discrete Inputs
- `03` — Read Holding Registers
- `04` — Read Input Registers
- `05` — Write Single Coil
- `06` — Write Single Register
- `15 (0x0F)` — Write Multiple Coils
- `16 (0x10)` — Write Multiple Registers

**CRC-16 Error Checking:**
- Polynomial: **`0xA001`** (bit-reversed form of `0x8005`).
- Initial value: **`0xFFFF`**.
- Final CRC is appended to the frame as **low byte first**, then **high byte**.
- CRC-16 detects all 1-bit errors, all 2-bit errors up to a frame size of 32,768 bits, and most longer burst errors.

**Part (b) — Timing Calculations**

Given: 8N2 framing → 1 start + 8 data + 0 parity + 2 stop = **11 bits per character**.

**General formula:**

$$
T_{\text{char}} = \frac{11}{B} \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
T_{\text{silence}} = 3.5 \times T_{\text{char}} = \frac{3.5 \times 11}{B} = \frac{38.5}{B} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Case (i): B = 9600 bps**

$$
T_{\text{char}} = \frac{11}{9600} = 1.1458 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{\text{silence}} = 3.5 \times 1.1458 = 4.0104 \text{ ms} \approx \mathbf{4.01 \text{ ms}}
$$

**Case (ii): B = 19200 bps**

$$
T_{\text{char}} = \frac{11}{19200} = 0.5729 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{\text{silence}} = 3.5 \times 0.5729 = 2.0052 \text{ ms} \approx \mathbf{2.01 \text{ ms}}
$$

**What happens if a slave detects a byte gap > 1.5 character times?**

According to the MODBUS RTU standard, the maximum inter-character gap allowed *within a frame* is **1.5 character times**. If the receiver detects a gap greater than this, it:
1. **Discards** the partial frame currently being assembled.
2. **Resets** its frame buffer and CRC calculator.
3. **Waits** for the next 3.5-character silence to mark the start of a *new* frame.

This protects against noise-induced byte loss, ensuring that corrupted or partial frames are never acted upon.

**Justification for 3.5 character times:**

- It is **longer** than the maximum legal inter-character gap (1.5 t_c) inside a valid frame, ensuring that *no* valid frame can *internally* trigger a false frame end.
- It is **short enough** to keep inter-message latency low, supporting real-time polling.
- It provides a **self-synchronizing** mechanism that does not require additional start/end bytes, maximizing data efficiency.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Q9 Pattern]**

> **(a)** Explain the MODBUS data model including the four primary data tables (Coils, Discrete Inputs, Input Registers, Holding Registers). For each, state the access type, the typical function codes used to read/write, and a real-world example. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the Data Highway Plus (DH+) protocol in detail, covering its physical layer, token-passing mechanism, frame format, and maximum node count. Compare DH+ with MODBUS RTU. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — MODBUS Data Model**

The MODBUS data model defines **four primary data tables** (also called *data registers*) that all MODBUS devices must conceptually implement. These are addressable in two reference spaces:

1. **Bit-addressable space** (coils and discrete inputs)
2. **Word (16-bit)-addressable space** (input and holding registers)

| Table | Type | Access | Read Function Code | Write Function Code | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Coils** | Single bit | Read/Write | `0x01` (Read Coils) | `0x05` (Write Single Coil), `0x0F` (Write Multiple Coils) | Motor start/stop relays, solenoid valves, LED indicators |
| **Discrete Inputs** | Single bit | Read-Only | `0x02` (Read Discrete Inputs) | N/A | Limit switches, push buttons, proximity sensors, safety contacts |
| **Input Registers** | 16-bit word | Read-Only | `0x04` (Read Input Registers) | N/A | Analog sensor readings — temperature, pressure, flow, level |
| **Holding Registers** | 16-bit word | Read/Write | `0x03` (Read Holding Registers) | `0x06` (Write Single Register), `0x10` (Write Multiple Registers) | Setpoints, calibration constants, control loop parameters, VFD speed reference |

> **Key Insight:** The "Input" tables (Discrete Inputs, Input Registers) are **read-only** because they represent *measured* values from the physical process. The "Coil" and "Holding" tables are **read/write** because they represent *control outputs* and *configuration* values written by the master.

**Addressing:**
- All addresses in MODBUS are **0-based** in the protocol but commonly referenced as **1-based** in documentation. For example, "Holding Register 40001" in user documentation = "address 0" in the protocol.
- Each table can hold up to **65,536 (2¹⁶)** elements.

**Part (b) — Data Highway Plus (DH+) and Comparison with MODBUS RTU**

**Data Highway Plus (DH+) — Key Characteristics:**

- **Origin:** Developed by Allen-Bradley (now Rockwell Automation) in **1986** as an enhancement of the original Data Highway.
- **Topology:** **Peer-to-peer** logical ring using **token passing** (based on IEEE 802.4 Token Bus standard).
- **Physical Layer:** **RS-422** differential signaling, allowing longer cable distances and better noise immunity than RS-232.
- **Speed:** Operates at **57.6 kbps** (standard) or **230.4 kbps** (enhanced DH+).
- **Maximum Nodes:** **64 nodes** on a single DH+ link.
- **Maximum Distance:** Up to **3,048 meters (10,000 ft)** at 57.6 kbps, with repeaters allowing further extension.
- **Token Rotation:** Only the node holding the **token** may initiate a message. After transmitting (or after a hold time elapses), it passes the token to the next node in the logical ring.

**DH+ Frame Format:**

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| **STX (0x02)** | Start of Transmission |
| **DST** | Destination node (1 byte) |
| **SRC** | Source node (1 byte) |
| **CMD** | Command byte (priority, message type) |
| **STS** | Status byte (acknowledgment type) |
| **TNS LSB** | Transaction Number (Low byte) — 16-bit sequence |
| **TNS MSB** | Transaction Number (High byte) |
| **Data** | Application-level data (0–244 bytes) |
| **BCC** | Block Check Character (error check) |
| **ETX (0x03)** | End of Transmission |

**Detailed Comparison: DH+ vs MODBUS RTU**

| Feature | DH+ | MODBUS RTU |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Peer-to-peer (token passing) | Master–Slave (polled) |
| **Max Nodes** | 64 | 247 slaves |
| **Speed** | 57.6 / 230.4 kbps | Up to 115.2 kbps |
| **Physical Layer** | RS-422 (differential) | RS-485 (differential) |
| **Frame Start/End** | STX/ETX bytes | 3.5-character silences |
| **Error Check** | BCC (Block Check Character) | CRC-16 |
| **Open Standard?** | Proprietary (Allen-Bradley) | **Open (royalty-free)** |
| **Multi-master Support** | **Yes** (token-passing) | No (single master only) |
| **Modern Successor** | EtherNet/IP | Modbus TCP |
| **Typical Use** | Legacy Allen-Bradley PLC networks | Universal industrial networks |

> **Conclusion:** While MODBUS RTU remains the **most widely deployed** industrial protocol due to its openness and simplicity, **DH+ offers superior real-time performance** for multi-master peer-to-peer applications in Allen-Bradley-centric plants. In modern greenfield installations, both have largely been superseded by Ethernet-based protocols (EtherNet/IP and Modbus TCP), but a deep understanding of both is **essential for maintaining and integrating brownfield industrial systems**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the field order of CRC-16:** Many students append the CRC high byte first. **The MODBUS standard mandates low byte first, then high byte.** Examiners *will* deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Mixing up RTU and ASCII start markers:** RTU uses *invisible* 3.5-character silences; ASCII uses a *visible* colon. Examiners expect you to clearly state which mode uses which.
> 3. **Forgetting the broadcast address convention:** Address `0` = broadcast; addresses `248–255` are reserved. The broadcast message receives no reply.
> 4. **Treating DH+ as MODBUS-compatible:** They are **completely different protocols** from **different vendors**. Many students wrongly claim DH+ is "an Allen-Bradley version of MODBUS." It is not — it predates EtherNet/IP and uses a proprietary token-passing scheme.
> 5. **Function code confusion:** Students often mix up `0x03` (Read Holding Registers) and `0x04` (Read Input Registers). Memorize the four data tables and their associated function codes.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert address basis:** MODBUS user addresses (e.g., 40001) are 1-based; protocol addresses are 0-based. Examiners love to test this off-by-one conversion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Pin This:**

**Core MODBUS RTU Frame:**
- [ ] **Start silence = 3.5 character times** (invisible delimiter)
- [ ] **Address field = 1 byte** (0 = broadcast, 1–247 = valid, 248–255 = reserved)
- [ ] **Function code = 1 byte** (1–127 valid, 128–255 = exception = FC + 0x80)
- [ ] **Data field = 0 to 252 bytes** (variable, function-code-dependent)
- [ ] **CRC-16 = 2 bytes** (polynomial `0xA001`, low byte first, then high byte)
- [ ] **End silence = 3.5 character times** (invisible delimiter)
- [ ] **Maximum total frame size = 256 bytes**

**Core MODBUS ASCII Frame:**
- [ ] **Start = `:`** (colon, 0x3A)
- [ ] **End = `CR` + `LF`** (0x0D 0x0A)
- [ ] **Error check = LRC** (8-bit two's complement of the byte sum)
- [ ] **All values hex-encoded** (each byte = 2 ASCII characters)
- [ ] **Maximum = 513 ASCII characters** total

**Timing Formulas:**
- [ ] $T_{\text{char}} = 11 / B$ seconds (8N2 framing)
- [ ] $T_{\text{silence}} = 38.5 / B$ seconds
- [ ] $T_{\text{icg,max}} = 16.5 / B$ seconds (1.5 char times)
- [ ] **Inter-character gap > 1.5 t_c → frame discarded**

**MODBUS Data Model (4 Tables):**
- [ ] **Coils** (1-bit, R/W, FC 01/05/0F) — discrete outputs
- [ ] **Discrete Inputs** (1-bit, RO, FC 02) — digital inputs
- [ ] **Input Registers** (16-bit, RO, FC 04) — analog measurements
- [ ] **Holding Registers** (16-bit, R/W, FC 03/06/10) — setpoints/config

**Data Highway Plus (DH+) — Key Facts:**
- [ ] **Vendor:** Allen-Bradley / Rockwell Automation (1986)
- [ ] **Architecture:** Token-passing peer-to-peer (IEEE 802.4 Token Bus)
- [ ] **Physical:** RS-422 differential
- [ ] **Speed:** 57.6 / 230.4 kbps
- [ ] **Max nodes:** 64
- [ ] **Frame start:** STX (0x02), end: ETX (0x03)
- [ ] **Error check:** BCC (Block Check Character)
- [ ] **Modern successor:** EtherNet/IP

**Comparison Anchors to Remember:**
- [ ] MODBUS = **open**, DH+ = **proprietary**
- [ ] MODBUS = **master-slave**, DH+ = **peer-to-peer**
- [ ] MODBUS RTU max nodes = **247**, DH+ max nodes = **64**
- [ ] MODBUS RTU error check = **CRC-16**, DH+ = **BCC**
- [ ] MODBUS modern successor = **Modbus TCP**, DH+ = **EtherNet/IP**

**Final Exam Day Strategy:**
1. For 3-mark questions, **draw the frame diagram** — it guarantees full marks.
2. For 14-mark questions, **always solve one numerical** (e.g., timing or LRC) to secure 7 of the 14 marks.
3. **Memorize the 4 data tables and their function codes** — examiners test this every semester.
4. **State the polynomial `0xA001` and the low-byte-first convention** for CRC questions.
5. For DH+ vs MODBUS, use a **comparison table** — it is the examiner's favorite 14-mark format.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Flow Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Information Flow Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Information Flow Requirements (IFR)** in an industrial network define the *quantitative and qualitative specifications* governing how data must be exchanged between the various hierarchical levels of an industrial enterprise — from the field sensors and actuators at Level 0 up to the enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems at Level 4. These requirements specify the **volume of data**, **frequency of transmission**, **latency bounds**, **reliability expectations**, **determinism**, and **security constraints** that the network infrastructure must satisfy to support continuous, real-time, and non-real-time operations of a manufacturing plant.

Mathematically, an Information Flow Requirement tuple is expressed as:

$$IFR = \{V, F, T_{latency}, R, D, S\}$$

where $V$ = data volume per unit time, $F$ = frequency of update, $T_{latency}$ = maximum permissible delay, $R$ = reliability, $D$ = determinism, and $S$ = security level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Information Flow Requirements is the foundational specification step that precedes the selection of any industrial network protocol (e.g., PROFIBUS, EtherCAT, Modbus, OPC UA). Without clearly defining IFR, the engineer cannot justify the choice of network technology for a given application.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the human body as a large industrial enterprise:

- The **nervous system** carries tiny, ultra-fast signals (like reflex actions) — these are like the **real-time control loops** between a PLC and a sensor in a factory. The signal must arrive in **milliseconds** or the system fails.
- The **bloodstream** carries larger volumes of data (oxygen, nutrients) — these are like the **process data** flowing continuously from field devices to the SCADA system.
- The **postal letters and emails** we receive carry high-volume, non-urgent information (financial reports, strategic plans) — these are like the **management information** that travels from production floor to the ERP system for business decisions.

Just as doctors must decide *how fast* a message must travel in the body (nerve vs. blood), an industrial network engineer must decide *how fast, how much, and how reliably* data must flow in the plant. This set of decisions constitutes the **Information Flow Requirements**.

> [!NOTE]
> The *standard industrial data unit* is the **bit** or **byte**, and the standard time unit for real-time flows is **milliseconds (ms)**. Enterprise-level flows are typically measured in **seconds** or **minutes**.

### 1.3 Why Information Flow Requirements Matter in KTU Context

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the PEICT747 course (Industrial Networks) treats IFR as the *bridge* between **business goals** and **network engineering**. Before you can answer the question *"Which fieldbus should I use?"*, you must first answer *"What does the application need from the network?"* — and that is exactly what IFR specifies.

| Industrial Network Level | Typical IFR Concern | Dominant Standard |
|--------------------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Field Level (L0) | Sub-ms latency, high determinism | IO-Link, AS-i |
| Control Level (L1) | 1–10 ms cycle times | PROFIBUS, EtherCAT |
| Cell Level (L2) | 100 ms – 1 s | PROFINET, Modbus TCP |
| Plant Level (L3) | Seconds | OPC UA, Ethernet |
| Enterprise (L4) | Minutes to hours | ERP/Internet protocols |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Information Flow in CIM

The **Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)** model defines five hierarchical levels. At each level, the *nature* of the information flow changes:

1. **Field Level (Level 0):** Raw sensor/actuator signals (e.g., 4–20 mA, digital I/O). Information flow is *bit-level*, highly periodic, and *hard real-time*.
2. **Control Level (Level 1):** PLCs and controllers exchange setpoints, feedback, and interlocks. Information flow is *message-level*, *soft to hard real-time*.
3. **Cell / Supervisory Level (Level 2):** SCADA, HMI, and cell controllers aggregate data. Information flow is *event-driven and cyclic*, with tolerance of ~100 ms.
4. **Plant Level (Level 3):** MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems) — *batch-level* and *recipe-based* data exchange.
5. **Enterprise Level (Level 4):** ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) — *transactional* and *historical* data exchange.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** A common 14-mark question asks students to *"Explain the information flow requirements at each level of the CIM model with examples."* You MUST mention latency, volume, determinism, and typical protocols for each level.

### 2.2 Categories of Information Flow Requirements

The IFR can be decomposed into **six** orthogonal categories, each with measurable metrics:

- **A. Volume Requirement (V):** Amount of data to be transferred per cycle. Measured in *bits/second (bps)* or *bytes/cycle*.
- **B. Timing / Latency Requirement (T):** Maximum time allowed between data generation and consumption. Hard real-time = strict upper bound; soft real-time = statistical bound.
- **C. Frequency / Cycle Time (F):** Periodic update rate. Measured in *Hz* or *ms per cycle*.
- **D. Determinism (D):** Predictability of the latency. A deterministic network guarantees the same latency every cycle.
- **E. Reliability / Availability (R):** Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF), Bit Error Rate (BER), packet loss tolerance.
- **F. Security (S):** Authentication, encryption, integrity protection. Increasingly important with IIoT (Industrial IoT).

### 2.3 Real-Time Classification (Engineering Foundation)

Real-time is *not* synonymous with "fast." It means **predictable within a known bound**. The classification used in industrial networking:

- **Hard Real-Time:** Missing a deadline causes **catastrophic failure** (e.g., emergency stop, anti-collision in robots). Typical bound: $T_{latency} \leq 1$ ms.
- **Firm Real-Time:** Missing a deadline degrades quality but does not cause disaster (e.g., vision inspection). Typical bound: $T_{latency} \leq 10$ ms.
- **Soft Real-Time:** Missing a deadline is tolerable; data becomes stale (e.g., trend display). Typical bound: $T_{latency} \leq 1000$ ms.
- **Non-Real-Time:** No timing constraint (e.g., email, ERP reports).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All vertical bars in the table below use the LaTeX `\vert` macro to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Data Throughput | $R = \dfrac{N_{bits}}{T_{cycle}}$ | bits/s (bps) | 10 Mbps – 1 Gbps |
| 2 | Cycle Time | $T_{cycle} = \dfrac{1}{F}$ | seconds (s) | 0.1 ms – 1 s |
| 3 | Bandwidth Utilization | $\eta = \dfrac{R_{useful}}{R_{link}} \times 100$ | % | $\leq 70\%$ for safety margin |
| 4 | End-to-End Latency | $T_{latency} = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{queue} + T_{proc}$ | seconds (s) | 0.1 ms – 100 ms |
| 5 | Propagation Delay | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v_p}$ where $v_p \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in cable | seconds (s) | $\sim 5$ ns/m |
| 6 | Transmission Delay | $T_{trans} = \dfrac{L_{packet}}{R_{link}}$ | seconds (s) | 0.1 – 10 $\mu$s |
| 7 | Number of nodes pollable | $N_{nodes} = \dfrac{T_{cycle}}{T_{per\_node}} - 1$ (token ring) | dimensionless | 10 – 127 |
| 8 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless | 0.999 – 0.99999 |
| 9 | Bit Error Rate requirement | $BER \leq 10^{-9}$ for control loops | dimensionless | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-12}$ |
| 10 | Response time (sensor $\to$ controller) | $T_{resp} = 2 \cdot T_{latency}$ (round trip) | seconds (s) | 0.5 – 20 ms |
| 11 | Jitter | $J = T_{max} - T_{min}$ over a sequence of transmissions | seconds (s) | $< 1$ $\mu$s for hard RT |
| 12 | Data volume per cycle | $V = N_{nodes} \cdot L_{payload}$ | bits/cycle | 64 – 1500 bytes |
| 13 | Priority inversion time | $T_{inv} = f(\text{scheduler policy})$ | seconds (s) | bounded |
| 14 | Throughput efficiency | $\eta_{eff} = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{packet}} \cdot \eta$ | dimensionless | 0.5 – 0.9 |
| 15 | Maximum nodes for latency | $N_{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{T_{latency,max}}{T_{trans,per\_node}} \right\rfloor$ | dimensionless | depends on link |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World

The IFR framework is the **first step** in any industrial network design project. Industrial engineers use it to:

- **Justify budget** for Ethernet-based vs. legacy fieldbus systems.
- **Select the correct OSI layer** for a given application (e.g., a hard RT loop needs Layer 2, not Layer 7).
- **Validate** that a chosen network can sustain the worst-case data load.
- **Document compliance** with standards such as IEC 61784 (fieldbus profiles), IEC 62443 (industrial security), and ISO/IEC 8802 (real-time Ethernet).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Worst-Case Latency for a Token-Passing Fieldbus

Consider a token-passing bus (e.g., PROFIBUS) with $N$ stations. The token must visit every station. The total cycle time for one complete token rotation is:

$$T_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( T_{trans,i} + T_{prop,i} \right) + T_{IDLE}$$

The transmission time for station $i$ sending a frame of length $L_i$ bits at link rate $R$ is:

$$T_{trans,i} = \frac{L_i}{R}$$

The propagation delay for a cable of length $d$ between stations is:

$$T_{prop,i} = \frac{d_i}{v_p}, \quad v_p = \frac{c}{n_{ref}} \approx 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$$

where $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s and $n_{ref} \approx 1.5$ (refractive index of typical industrial cable).

The **worst-case latency** for a station is the maximum time it must wait for the token:

$$T_{latency,max} = T_{cycle} \le T_{deadline}$$

**Worked Example:** A PROFIBUS segment with $N = 12$ stations, each sending $L = 256$ bits per token visit, link rate $R = 1.5$ Mbps, cable segment 100 m. Find the worst-case latency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{trans,i} &= \frac{256 \text{ bits}}{1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} \\
&= 1.7067 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \\
&= 170.67 \text{ }\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{prop,i} &= \frac{100 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} \\
&= 0.5 \text{ }\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= N \cdot (T_{trans,i} + T_{prop,i}) + T_{IDLE} \\
&= 12 \cdot (170.67 + 0.5) \text{ }\mu\text{s} + 100 \text{ }\mu\text{s (idle)} \\
&= 12 \cdot 171.17 \text{ }\mu\text{s} + 100 \text{ }\mu\text{s} \\
&= 2054.04 \text{ }\mu\text{s} + 100 \text{ }\mu\text{s} \\
&= 2154.04 \text{ }\mu\text{s} \\
&\approx 2.15 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Every station waits at most $\approx 2.15$ ms for its next transmission opportunity. This satisfies the typical **firm real-time** requirement of $T_{latency} \leq 10$ ms for control-level traffic.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Bandwidth Required for a Multi-Node Control Loop

A manufacturing cell has $N = 50$ sensors, each producing $L_{payload} = 16$ bits of data per cycle. The control loop requires an update every $T_{cycle} = 5$ ms. We must size the link bandwidth.

**Step 1 — Compute total useful data per cycle:**

$$V_{cycle} = N \cdot L_{payload} = 50 \cdot 16 = 800 \text{ bits/cycle}$$

**Step 2 — Compute required raw throughput (ignoring protocol overhead):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= \frac{V_{cycle}}{T_{cycle}} \\
&= \frac{800 \text{ bits}}{5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}} \\
&= 1.6 \times 10^5 \text{ bps} \\
&= 160 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply protocol overhead factor.** Industrial Ethernet with TCP/IP + UDP/IP + Ethernet headers has overhead $\approx 64$ bytes per packet for 16 bytes of payload, so the efficiency is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{eff} &= \frac{L_{payload}}{L_{packet}} = \frac{16}{16 + 64} \\
&= \frac{16}{80} = 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the *actual* bandwidth required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{required} &= \frac{R_{min}}{\eta_{eff}} \\
&= \frac{1.6 \times 10^5}{0.20} \\
&= 8 \times 10^5 \text{ bps} \\
&= 800 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Select link capacity with 70% utilization rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{link,min} &= \frac{R_{required}}{0.70} \\
&= \frac{800 \text{ kbps}}{0.70} \\
&\approx 1.14 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A standard 10 Mbps Ethernet link easily satisfies this IFR. However, if the same cell required $T_{cycle} = 0.5$ ms (10× faster), the link would need $\approx 11.4$ Mbps — still within 10 Mbps Ethernet, but with no margin. This is why high-performance systems use **100 Mbps or 1 Gbps** industrial Ethernet.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Availability Calculation for Redundant Network

A redundant ring network has primary path MTBF = 50,000 hours and secondary path MTBF = 50,000 hours. Each switch has MTTR = 4 hours. Compute system availability.

**For a single path:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{single} &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\
&= \frac{50000}{50000 + 4} \\
&= \frac{50000}{50004} \\
&\approx 0.99992
\end{aligned}
$$

**For two parallel paths (system fails only if both fail):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{system} &= 1 - (1 - A_{single})^2 \\
&= 1 - (1 - 0.99992)^2 \\
&= 1 - (0.00008)^2 \\
&= 1 - 6.4 \times 10^{-9} \\
&\approx 0.999999994
\end{aligned}
$$

**Equivalent MTBF of redundant system:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF_{system} &\approx \frac{MTBF_{single}^2}{2 \cdot MTTR} \\
&= \frac{(50000)^2}{2 \cdot 4} \\
&= \frac{2.5 \times 10^9}{8} \\
&= 3.125 \times 10^8 \text{ hours} \\
&\approx 35{,}675 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Redundancy boosts availability from "three nines" (99.992%) to "ten nines" (99.9999994%) — a standard requirement for **process automation** (e.g., oil & gas, pharmaceutical).

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Network Meets the IFR?

Use this matrix to justify a protocol choice in a KTU exam answer.

| IFR Criterion | Sensor-Bus (AS-i) | PROFIBUS DP | PROFINET RT | EtherCAT |
|---------------|-------------------|-------------|-------------|----------|
| Max nodes | 62 | 126 | Unlimited (managed) | 65535 |
| Cycle time | 5 ms | 1 ms | 1 ms | 0.1 ms (100 $\mu$s) |
| Determinism | High | High | Medium | Very high |
| Bandwidth | 167 kbps | 12 Mbps | 100 Mbps | 100 Mbps |
| Hard RT capable | No | Yes | Borderline | Yes |
| Typical use | Binary I/O | Field control | Cell control | Motion control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always justify your answer by mapping **each IFR dimension** (volume, latency, determinism) to a specific column of the table.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: CIM Hierarchy with Information Flow Characteristics

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **five-level CIM model** with the information flow characteristics at each level. This is the single most important diagram for the Module 4 exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["LEVEL 4 - ENTERPRISE / ERP - Volume Very High - Latency Minutes to Hours - Non Real Time"]
    L3["LEVEL 3 - PLANT / MES - Volume High - Latency Seconds - Soft Real Time"]
    L2["LEVEL 2 - CELL / SCADA - Volume Medium - Latency 100 ms Range - Soft Real Time"]
    L1["LEVEL 1 - CONTROL / PLC - Volume Low - Latency 1 to 10 ms - Firm Real Time"]
    L0["LEVEL 0 - FIELD DEVICES - Volume Very Low - Latency Sub ms - Hard Real Time"]

    L4 -->|Bidirectional Reports| L3
    L3 -->|Production Orders| L2
    L2 -->|Setpoints Recipes| L1
    L1 -->|Control Signals| L0
    L0 -->|Raw Sensor Data| L1
    L1 -->|Process Data| L2
    L2 -->|Aggregated KPIs| L3
    L3 -->|Financial Data| L4

    style L0 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style L1 fill:#ffa500,color:#ffffff
    style L2 fill:#ffd93d,color:#000000
    style L3 fill:#6bcf7f,color:#ffffff
    style L4 fill:#4d96ff,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an IFR-Driven Network Design

This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **engineering workflow** for translating Information Flow Requirements into a network design.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Application Requirements] --> B[Identify Data Sources]
    B --> C[Classify Traffic Type]
    C --> D{Real Time Category}
    D -->|Hard RT| E[Sub millisecond Latency]
    D -->|Firm RT| F[1 to 10 ms Latency]
    D -->|Soft RT| G[100 ms plus Latency]
    D -->|Non RT| H[Best Effort Delivery]
    E --> I[Select Layer 2 Protocol e.g. EtherCAT]
    F --> J[Select Fieldbus e.g. PROFIBUS]
    G --> K[Select Industrial Ethernet e.g. PROFINET]
    H --> L[Select IT Protocol e.g. HTTP OPC UA]
    I --> M[Network Validation]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{IFR Satisfied?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Deployment]
    N -->|No| P[Revise Design or Add Bandwidth]
    P --> A

    style A fill:#4d96ff,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#ffa500,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#6bcf7f,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Data Flow Volume vs. Latency Trade-off

This conceptual diagram shows the **inverse relationship** between data volume and acceptable latency at each CIM level. Lower in the hierarchy (towards the field), volumes are small but latencies are tight; higher up, volumes are huge but latencies are relaxed.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Pyr1["Pyramid of IFR Trade off"]
        Top["ENTERPRISE - Massive Volume, Very Loose Latency"]
        Mid1["PLANT - High Volume, Loose Latency"]
        Mid2["CELL - Medium Volume, Moderate Latency"]
        Mid3["CONTROL - Low Volume, Tight Latency"]
        Bot["FIELD - Tiny Volume, Ultra Tight Latency"]
        Top --> Mid1
        Mid1 --> Mid2
        Mid2 --> Mid3
        Mid3 --> Bot
    end

    style Top fill:#4d96ff,color:#ffffff
    style Mid1 fill:#6bcf7f,color:#ffffff
    style Mid2 fill:#ffd93d,color:#000000
    style Mid3 fill:#ffa500,color:#ffffff
    style Bot fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Guide for Students:**
> - **Red (bottom):** Field-level — hard real-time, small packets, microsecond determinism.
> - **Orange / Yellow (middle):** Control and cell — firm to soft real-time, kilobytes per cycle.
> - **Green / Blue (top):** Plant and enterprise — non-real-time, large files and database transactions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define Information Flow Requirements in the context of industrial networks. List any four parameters used to specify IFR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Information Flow Requirements (IFR) are the quantitative and qualitative specifications that define how data must be exchanged between the various levels of an industrial automation hierarchy, including volume, latency, frequency, reliability, determinism, and security.
*(Definition — 2 marks)*

The four key parameters are:
1. **Data volume** per unit time (bps or bytes/cycle)
2. **Latency / timing** bound (maximum permissible delay)
3. **Determinism** (predictability of latency)
4. **Reliability** (MTBF, BER, availability)
*(Parameters listing — 1 mark)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**Differentiate between hard real-time and soft real-time information flow. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Deadline | Strict, must be met always | Tolerant of occasional misses |
| Consequence of miss | Catastrophic / unsafe | Quality degradation |
| Example | Emergency stop signal in a CNC machine | Trend display refresh in SCADA |

*(Differentiation table — 2 marks; one example each — 1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** describe protocols without first defining the IFR. The examiner expects a *requirements-first* answer.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse *latency* with *cycle time*. Latency is the worst-case delay; cycle time is the period of repeated transmission.
> 3. **Do NOT** forget units. Marks are deducted when bandwidth is given without specifying *bps vs. Bps* (1 Byte = 8 bits).
> 4. **Do NOT** skip the **justification** step. Always tie your final network selection back to the *calculated* IFR values.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **five levels of the CIM hierarchy** with the information flow requirements (volume, latency, determinism) at each level. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A plant floor has **40 sensors** generating **2 bytes** of data each every **10 ms**. Compute the **minimum link bandwidth** required assuming a protocol overhead efficiency of **50%**. Recommend a suitable network technology. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — CIM Hierarchy and IFR (7 marks)**

The Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM) hierarchy divides industrial information flow into **five levels**, each with distinct IFR profiles.

**Level 4 — Enterprise (ERP)**
- *Volume:* Very high (gigabytes/day) — financial, supply chain, HR data.
- *Latency:* Minutes to hours.
- *Determinism:* None required.
- *Example protocols:* HTTP, SAP, SQL.
*(1.5 marks)*

**Level 3 — Plant (MES)**
- *Volume:* High — batch records, production logs.
- *Latency:* Seconds.
- *Determinism:* Soft.
- *Example protocols:* OPC UA, SQL replication.
*(1.5 marks)*

**Level 2 — Cell (SCADA / HMI)**
- *Volume:* Medium — alarms, trends, recipes.
- *Latency:* 100 ms range.
- *Determinism:* Soft real-time.
- *Example protocols:* Modbus TCP, PROFINET.
*(1.5 marks)*

**Level 1 — Control (PLC / DCS)**
- *Volume:* Low — setpoints, feedback.
- *Latency:* 1 to 10 ms.
- *Determinism:* Firm real-time.
- *Example protocols:* PROFIBUS, EtherCAT, PROFINET IRT.
*(1.5 marks)*

**Level 0 — Field (Sensors / Actuators)**
- *Volume:* Very low — bit-level, 4–20 mA, digital I/O.
- *Latency:* Sub-millisecond.
- *Determinism:* Hard real-time.
- *Example protocols:* AS-i, IO-Link, bit-bus.
*(1 mark — closure and example protocol)*

---

#### **Part (b) — Bandwidth Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $N = 40$ sensors
- $L_{payload} = 2$ bytes = $16$ bits per sensor per cycle
- $T_{cycle} = 10$ ms $= 10 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- Protocol overhead efficiency $\eta_{eff} = 50\% = 0.50$

**Step 1 — Total useful data per cycle:**

$$V_{cycle} = 40 \times 16 = 640 \text{ bits/cycle}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum raw throughput:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= \frac{V_{cycle}}{T_{cycle}} \\
&= \frac{640}{10 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 64{,}000 \text{ bps} \\
&= 64 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Required bandwidth with overhead:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{required} &= \frac{R_{min}}{\eta_{eff}} \\
&= \frac{64 \text{ kbps}}{0.50} \\
&= 128 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Recommendation (with 70% utilization rule):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{link,min} &= \frac{128}{0.70} \approx 183 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

A standard **PROFIBUS DP link (1.5 Mbps)** or **10 Mbps Industrial Ethernet** easily satisfies this requirement. For this scale, **PROFIBUS DP** is the recommended network technology because it provides deterministic token-passing, robust industrial connectors, and 126-node addressability — leaving plenty of headroom for expansion.
*(3 marks — including final selection + justification)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q. (a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **classification of real-time information flow** in industrial networks. Discuss the consequences of missing a deadline in each category. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Two industrial networks have the following specifications:
- **Network X:** MTBF = 20,000 hours, MTTR = 8 hours
- **Network Y:** MTBF = 30,000 hours, MTTR = 2 hours

Calculate the **availability** of each network and identify which is more suitable for a **process automation** plant requiring **99.99%** availability. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Classification of Real-Time Flow (7 marks)**

Real-time industrial information flow is classified into **four** categories based on the consequence of missing a deadline.

*(Refer to the diagrams in Section 4.1 for visual support.)*

**1. Hard Real-Time** — Missing the deadline causes **catastrophic system failure or safety hazard**. Example: anti-collision sensor on a robotic arm must respond in $\leq 1$ ms. Consequence: physical harm, loss of life, equipment damage.
*(1.5 marks)*

**2. Firm Real-Time** — Missing the deadline causes **unrecoverable error in the current task** but no safety issue. Example: a vision inspection system missing its 10 ms capture window discards the frame. Consequence: defective product passes downstream.
*(1.5 marks)*

**3. Soft Real-Time** — Missing the deadline causes **degraded performance** but the system continues. Example: SCADA trend update delayed by 200 ms. Consequence: operator sees a slightly stale graph.
*(1.5 marks)*

**4. Non-Real-Time** — No deadline. Example: ERP reporting at end of shift. Consequence: none.
*(1 mark)*

**Conclusion:** The engineer MUST classify every data flow in the plant into one of these four categories and assign an appropriate network to each.
*(1.5 marks — synthesis and connection to network selection)*

---

#### **Part (b) — Availability Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:** Network X: $MTBF_X = 20{,}000$ h, $MTTR_X = 8$ h; Network Y: $MTBF_Y = 30{,}000$ h, $MTTR_Y = 2$ h.

**Step 1 — Availability of Network X:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_X &= \frac{MTBF_X}{MTBF_X + MTTR_X} \\
&= \frac{20{,}000}{20{,}000 + 8} \\
&= \frac{20{,}000}{20{,}008} \\
&\approx 0.99960 \\
&= 99.960\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Availability of Network Y:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_Y &= \frac{MTBF_Y}{MTBF_Y + MTTR_Y} \\
&= \frac{30{,}000}{30{,}000 + 2} \\
&= \frac{30{,}000}{30{,}002} \\
&\approx 0.99993 \\
&= 99.993\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Required availability for process automation:**

$$A_{req} = 99.99\% = 0.9999$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Comparison and Decision:**

- $A_X = 0.99960 < 0.9999$ — **Network X FAILS** the requirement.
- $A_Y = 0.99993 < 0.9999$ — **Network Y also fails**, but is much closer.

**Recommendation:** **Neither network alone meets the 99.99% requirement.** The plant must use **Network Y in a redundant configuration** (e.g., dual-ring topology), OR select a higher-MTBF/MTTR alternative. With redundant Network Y:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{Y,redundant} &= 1 - (1 - 0.99993)^2 \\
&\approx 1 - 4.9 \times 10^{-9} \\
&\approx 0.999999995
\end{aligned}
$$

This far exceeds 99.99%. **Therefore, redundant Network Y is the recommended choice.**
*(2 marks — comparison + decision justification)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **IFR Definition:** IFR = {Volume, Frequency, Latency, Reliability, Determinism, Security} — the *specification* of how data flows in an industrial system.
- **CIM has 5 levels:** Field (L0), Control (L1), Cell (L2), Plant (L3), Enterprise (L4). Each has a *distinct* IFR profile.
- **Volume vs. Latency Trade-off:** As you go UP the CIM pyramid, volume increases and latency tolerance loosens.
- **Real-Time has 4 classes:** Hard, Firm, Soft, Non-Real-Time. **Hard ≠ fast; it means predictable within a strict bound.**
- **Throughput formula:** $R = V_{cycle} / T_{cycle}$ — always remember units (bps vs. Bps).
- **Bandwidth with overhead:** $R_{required} = R_{min} / \eta_{eff}$ — use this when protocol headers consume payload space.
- **70% utilization rule:** Never design a network at 100% load — reserve 30% for bursts and growth.
- **Token-passing latency:** $T_{latency,max} \approx N \cdot (L/R + d/v_p)$ for $N$ stations.
- **Availability formula:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$. Redundancy boosts $A$ from "three nines" to "ten nines."
- **Always map IFR → protocol:** Hard RT → EtherCAT, Firm RT → PROFIBUS, Soft RT → PROFINET, Non-RT → OPC UA / HTTP.
- **Common exam trap:** Confusing *latency* (worst-case delay) with *cycle time* (period of transmission).
- **Standard units to remember:** **1 kbps = 10³ bps, 1 Mbps = 10⁶ bps, 1 Gbps = 10⁹ bps, 1 ms = 10⁻³ s, 1 μs = 10⁻⁶ s.**
- **Propagation speed in cable:** $v_p \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s — use this constant for all latency calculations.
- **Industrial BER target:** $BER \leq 10^{-9}$ for control loops; $10^{-12}$ for safety-critical loops.
- **Two words that ALWAYS appear in 14-mark answers:** "**justified by**" — every protocol choice must be justified by an IFR metric.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hierarchical Communication Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hierarchical Communication Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Hierarchical Communication Model (HCM)** in industrial networking is a structured, multi-layered architectural framework that organizes the flow of information across an industrial enterprise by dividing it into distinct functional levels. It is primarily derived from the **Purdue Reference Model for CIM (Computer-Integrated Manufacturing)** and standardized by **ANSI/ISA-95 (IEC 62264)**.

The model defines **five (or six, including Level 0) hierarchical levels** ranging from the physical process (sensors and actuators) up to the enterprise and internet business systems. Each level has its own communication requirements, data rates, latency constraints, and protocol families.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEICT747 Module 4):**
> The Hierarchical Communication Model classifies industrial communication into a layered hierarchy — Field Level, Control Level, Supervisory (SCADA/HMI) Level, Operations/Manufacturing Level, and Enterprise Level — where information granularity decreases as the level increases, while strategic value and data volume increase upward.

### 1.1.1 KTU 2024 Aligned Terminology

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **CIM** | Computer-Integrated Manufacturing |
| **HCM** | Hierarchical Communication Model |
| **ERP** | Enterprise Resource Planning |
| **MES** | Manufacturing Execution System |
| **SCADA** | Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition |
| **PLC** | Programmable Logic Controller |
| **DCS** | Distributed Control System |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Hospital System**
>
> Imagine a large hospital:
> - **Level 0 (Patient's Body)** — The patient's heart, lungs, and organs generating raw signals (like sensors measuring temperature/pressure).
> - **Level 1 (Bedside Monitors & Nurses)** — A nurse at the bedside reads the monitor and calls the doctor if vitals cross a threshold (analogous to PLCs and controllers).
> - **Level 2 (Hospital Floor / Ward)** — A ward supervisor oversees all patients and nurses on one floor (like SCADA/HMI screens).
> - **Level 3 (Hospital Administration)** — The hospital manager looks at reports, statistics, and performance of all wards (like MES).
> - **Level 4 (Hospital Group / Government)** — The overall hospital network allocates budgets, plans expansion, and decides policies (like ERP).
>
> Each level only talks to the level just above and below it, and the **language, speed, and type of information changes** as you move up.

This is exactly how the **Hierarchical Communication Model** works in an industrial plant: lower levels deal with **fast, real-time, deterministic** control signals; higher levels deal with **slow, batch-oriented, business-critical** data.

## 1.3 Why the Hierarchy Exists — The "Why" Behind the Model

- **Determinism vs. Throughput Trade-off:** Field-level communication needs millisecond-level responses; enterprise-level communication needs high throughput but tolerates seconds of latency. A single flat network cannot serve both.
- **Data Aggregation:** Raw process data (e.g., 1000 temperature readings/second) must be **filtered, compressed, and contextualized** as it moves up.
- **Security & Isolation:** Keeping the corporate network isolated from the field network prevents cyber-attacks (e.g., **Stuxnet 2010** exploited flat architectures).
- **Scalability:** Modular levels let plants expand incrementally without redesigning the whole network.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in This Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Quantitative Boundaries (must memorize for KTU):**
> - Field Level latency: **1–10 ms**
> - Control Level latency: **10–100 ms**
> - Supervisory Level latency: **100 ms – 1 s**
> - Enterprise Level latency: **seconds to minutes**
> - Typical field-level data update: **cyclic, 1–10 ms**
> - Typical enterprise data batch: **minutes, hours, or daily**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five (Six) Levels — Exhaustive Breakdown

The Hierarchical Communication Model is conventionally divided as follows (ISA-95 / Purdue Model):

### **Level 0 — Process / Physical Level**
- **Devices:** Sensors, actuators, valves, motors, transmitters, drives.
- **Function:** Direct interaction with the physical process. Converts physical variables (temperature, pressure, flow) to electrical signals.
- **Communication:** Analog 4–20 mA, HART, point-to-point, or simple digital I/O.
- **Data type:** Continuous, raw, high-frequency, time-critical.
- **Latency requirement:** < **10 ms** (hard real-time).

### **Level 1 — Basic Control / Direct Control Level**
- **Devices:** PLCs, DCS controllers, RTUs, intelligent drives, soft-PLCs.
- **Function:** Executes closed-loop control algorithms (PID, cascade, feedforward). Reacts to sensor inputs and commands actuators.
- **Communication:** **PROFIBUS DP, Modbus RTU, FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1, DeviceNet, CANopen, EtherCAT, PROFINET (IRT)**.
- **Data type:** Cyclic I/O, process variables, setpoints.
- **Latency requirement:** < **100 ms**.

### **Level 2 — Supervisory (SCADA / HMI) Level**
- **Devices:** SCADA servers, HMI stations, engineering workstations, alarm/event managers.
- **Function:** Operator visualization, trending, alarm management, recipe handling, supervisory coordination.
- **Communication:** **OPC UA, Modbus TCP, PROFINET (NRT), EtherNet/IP, Industrial Ethernet**.
- **Data type:** Operator screens, historical logs, alarms, batch records.
- **Latency requirement:** **100 ms – 1 s**.

### **Level 3 — Operations / Manufacturing Execution (MES) Level**
- **Devices:** MES servers, historians (e.g., OSIsoft PI), production schedulers, quality management systems.
- **Function:** Production tracking, lot genealogy, OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness), detailed scheduling, KPI reporting.
- **Communication:** **OPC UA, SQL/ODBC, REST APIs, MQTT, web services**.
- **Data type:** Batch records, production orders, quality data.
- **Latency requirement:** **Seconds to minutes**.

### **Level 4 — Enterprise / Business Planning Level**
- **Devices:** ERP systems (SAP, Oracle), CRM, supply chain management, BI dashboards.
- **Function:** Long-term planning, order management, procurement, financial accounting, customer relations.
- **Communication:** **TCP/IP, HTTP/S, SOAP/REST, SQL, SAP RFC, EDI**.
- **Data type:** Orders, invoices, contracts, forecasts.
- **Latency requirement:** **Minutes to hours (non-real-time)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Extension — Level 5 (Cloud / IIoT):** Some KTU-aligned textbooks add a 5th (or 6th) cloud level for IIoT, big-data analytics, and digital twins. KTU 2024 may ask this as a "current trend" question.

## 2.2 Vertical (Inter-Level) vs. Horizontal (Intra-Level) Communication

| Type | Direction | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Vertical** | Up/Down between levels | Field sensor → PLC → SCADA → ERP |
| **Horizontal** | Same level, peer-to-peer | PLC ↔ PLC, HMI ↔ HMI |

Vertical communication uses **gateways** at level boundaries (e.g., a **PROFINET-to-OPC UA gateway** between Level 1 and Level 2).

## 2.3 The "Information Pyramid" — A Core KTU Diagram Concept

As we move from Level 0 → Level 4:
- **Volume of data decreases** (raw → aggregated).
- **Value of data increases** (per-bit strategic importance rises).
- **Latency tolerance increases**.
- **Decision time horizon lengthens** (milliseconds → months).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Parameter Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Cheat Sheet for KTU Board Exams (Module 4):**

| Level | Name | Typical Devices | Typical Protocol | Latency | Data Volume | Real-Time? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Process | Sensors, Actuators | 4-20 mA, HART | < 1 ms | Very High | Hard |
| 1 | Basic Control | PLC, DCS, RTU | PROFIBUS DP, EtherCAT | 1–10 ms | High | Hard |
| 2 | Supervisory | SCADA, HMI | OPC UA, Modbus TCP | 10–100 ms | Medium | Soft |
| 3 | Operations | MES, Historian | OPC UA, SQL, MQTT | 100 ms – 1 s | Low | Soft |
| 4 | Enterprise | ERP, CRM | TCP/IP, HTTP, SOAP | Seconds+ | Very Low | None |

### 2.4.1 Useful Quantitative Relationships

**Throughput vs. Latency Trade-off (Network Calculus simplified):**

For a real-time fieldbus, the worst-case response time $T_{resp}$ is:

$$T_{resp} = T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{queue} + T_{proc}$$

Where:
- $T_{tx}$ = frame transmission time
- $T_{prop}$ = propagation delay
- $T_{queue}$ = queuing delay
- $T_{proc}$ = device processing delay

For a bit-rate $R$ (bits/s) and frame length $L$ (bits):

$$T_{tx} = \frac{L}{R}$$

**Bus cycle time** for a deterministic fieldbus with $N$ stations, each requesting time $t_i$:

$$T_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i + T_{idle}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plant Design:** Engineers use the HCM to select appropriate cabling, switches, and routers per level.
- **Cybersecurity:** Defines **Purdue Levels** that map to **IEC 62443 security zones** (a direct KTU 2024 hot topic).
- **Protocol Selection:** Each level dictates the protocol family (deterministic fieldbuses vs. IT-style TCP/IP).
- **Cost Optimization:** Field-level equipment is more expensive per unit; HCM justifies distributing cost appropriately.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Real Protocols to HCM Levels

> [!NOTE]
> **Detailed Mapping Table (KTU 2024 frequently asked):**

| Level | OSI Layer (Dominant) | ISO/OSI Layers Used | Protocol Examples | Topology | Bandwidth | Determinism |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Layer 1 (Physical) | L1 | 4-20 mA, HART, IO-Link | Point-to-point | Low | N/A (analog) |
| 1 | Layer 1–2 | L1, L2 | PROFIBUS DP, CAN, DeviceNet, EtherCAT, PROFINET IRT | Bus / Line / Ring | 1 Mbps – 100 Mbps | Hard real-time |
| 2 | Layer 3–4 | L2, L3, L4 | PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, Modbus TCP | Star, Tree | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps | Soft real-time |
| 3 | Layer 5–7 | L5, L6, L7 | OPC UA, SQL, MQTT, Web Services | Client-Server | 1 Gbps+ | Best-effort |
| 4 | Layer 7 | L7 | HTTP/S, SOAP, REST, SAP BAPI | WAN, Cloud | 10 Gbps+ | Non-real-time |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Bus Cycle Time Calculation

This is a **classical KTU numerical problem** on the Hierarchical Communication Model.

> **Problem:**
> A PROFIBUS DP segment has $N = 8$ slave devices. Each slave has a fixed message time of $t_i = 1.5$ ms. The bus has a bit-rate $R = 1.5$ Mbps. Each frame is $L = 256$ bits long. There is one master token rotation with idle time $T_{idle} = 0.4$ ms. Calculate:
> 1. Per-device transmission time
> 2. Total bus cycle time
> 3. Worst-case latency for any slave

### **Solution — Step 1: Per-Device Transmission Time**

Each frame is $L = 256$ bits at $R = 1.5 \times 10^6$ bits/s.

$$T_{tx} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{256}{1.5 \times 10^{6}} \text{ seconds}$$

Converting to ms:

$$T_{tx} = \frac{256}{1.5 \times 10^{6}} \times 10^{3} = \frac{256 \times 10^{3}}{1.5 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{256}{1500} \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{tx} = 0.1707 \text{ ms} \approx 0.171 \text{ ms}$$

### **Solution — Step 2: Total Per-Slave Time (Transmission + Processing)**

$$t_i = T_{tx} + T_{proc}$$

For PROFIBUS DP, $T_{proc}$ is the slave's response preparation time. Given directly as $t_i = 1.5$ ms in the problem, we take it as the per-slave service time.

### **Solution — Step 3: Total Bus Cycle Time**

$$T_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i + T_{idle}$$

Substituting:

$$T_{cycle} = 8 \times 1.5 \text{ ms} + 0.4 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{cycle} = 12.0 \text{ ms} + 0.4 \text{ ms} = 12.4 \text{ ms}$$

### **Solution — Step 4: Worst-Case Latency for a Slave**

In a token-passing bus, the worst case is when a slave just missed the token and must wait for the full cycle to return.

$$T_{worst} = T_{cycle} = 12.4 \text{ ms}$$

**Best case:** $T_{best} = 0.4$ ms (if token arrives immediately).

**Average latency:**

$$T_{avg} = \frac{T_{cycle}}{2} = \frac{12.4}{2} = 6.2 \text{ ms}$$

### **Solution — Step 5: Check vs. Real-Time Constraint**

For a typical control loop requiring response within 50 ms:

$$T_{worst} = 12.4 \text{ ms} \ll 50 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

The system satisfies the hard real-time constraint.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step — HCM Mapping for a Real Plant

**Scenario:** A bottling plant with:
- 200 sensors (flow, level, temperature)
- 12 PLCs (Siemens S7-1500)
- 4 SCADA stations
- 1 MES server
- 1 SAP ERP system

**Step 1:** Identify the **functional decomposition**.
- Sensors → Level 0
- PLCs controlling each section → Level 1
- SCADA with line overview → Level 2
- MES for batch tracking → Level 3
- SAP for orders/billing → Level 4

**Step 2:** Choose protocols per level.
- Level 0 → 4-20 mA + HART (analog) or IO-Link (digital)
- Level 1 → **PROFINET IRT** (1 ms cycle, deterministic)
- Level 1↔2 → PROFINET (NRT) via managed switch
- Level 2→3 → **OPC UA** (secure, semantic)
- Level 3↔4 → REST APIs or BAPI to SAP

**Step 3:** Define gateways.
- PROFINET ↔ OPC UA gateway at Level 1/2 boundary.
- OPC UA ↔ REST API adapter at Level 3/4 boundary.

**Step 4:** Apply IEC 62443 zones.
- Zone 1: Levels 0–1 (OT, high security, air-gapped)
- Zone 2: Levels 2–3 (DMZ with firewalls)
- Zone 3: Level 4 (IT, standard IT security)

This four-step mapping is a **standard 7-mark KTU answer** format.

## 3.4 Common Pitfalls in Numerical Derivations

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistakes:**
> 1. Confusing $L$ in **bits** vs **bytes** (always convert: 1 byte = 8 bits).
> 2. Forgetting to convert seconds to ms (multiply by $10^3$).
> 3. Adding $T_{idle}$ only **once per cycle**, not per device.
> 4. Misapplying the formula when devices have **different** $t_i$ (use sum, not $N \times t_i$ with one value).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — The Five-Level HCM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

```mermaid
    B["LEVEL 1 HIGH VOLUME LOW MEDIUM VALUE PROCESS VARIABLES"]:::b
```

## 4.3 Vertical vs. Horizontal Communication Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENTERPRISE["LEVEL 4 ENTERPRISE"]
        E1["ERP SERVER"]

    end

        M2["HISTORIAN"]
    end

    E2 <--> M2

    M2 <--> S2
    S1 <--> P1

    P2 --- F2
```
