# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Hierarchical Communication Model (HCM) used in industrial networks. List its five levels.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Hierarchical Communication Model (HCM)** is a layered architecture for organizing communication in industrial automation systems, derived from the **Purdue Reference Model** and standardized by **ISA-95 / IEC 62264**. It partitions the plant into five hierarchical levels based on function, latency requirements, and data granularity.

The five levels are:
1. **Level 0 — Process Level** (sensors, actuators)
2. **Level 1 — Basic Control Level** (PLCs, DCS, RTUs)
3. **Level 2 — Supervisory Level** (SCADA, HMI)
4. **Level 3 — Operations / Manufacturing Level** (MES, Historians)
5. **Level 4 — Enterprise Level** (ERP, CRM)

(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing all five levels correctly.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between vertical and horizontal communication in the HCM. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Vertical Communication | Horizontal Communication |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Between different hierarchical levels | Between devices at the same level |
| **Purpose** | Aggregation, command dispatch, status reporting | Peer coordination, data sharing |
| **Example** | PLC (L1) sending process data to SCADA (L2) | Two PLCs exchanging interlocking signals (L1↔L1) |

(1.5 marks per column, 1 mark for correct example.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **five levels of the Hierarchical Communication Model** with their functions, typical devices, communication protocols, and latency requirements. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe how a **PROFINET-based plant** maps onto the HCM. Discuss the role of **OPC UA** at the L1/L2 boundary. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Hierarchical Communication Model is a five-layered architecture that classifies industrial communication based on the function, time-criticality, and data semantics of each layer.

**Level 0 — Process Layer (1 mark):**
- **Devices:** Temperature sensors, pressure transmitters, flow meters, control valves, motors.
- **Function:** Direct interaction with the physical process; converts physical variables to electrical signals.
- **Protocols:** 4–20 mA analog, HART, IO-Link.
- **Latency:** < 1 ms (hard real-time).

**Level 1 — Basic Control Layer (1 mark):**
- **Devices:** PLCs (e.g., Siemens S7), DCS controllers, RTUs.
- **Function:** Executes control algorithms (PID), reads sensors, drives actuators.
- **Protocols:** PROFIBUS DP, EtherCAT, PROFINET IRT, CANopen.
- **Latency:** 1–10 ms.

**Level 2 — Supervisory Layer (1.5 marks):**
- **Devices:** SCADA servers, HMI stations, engineering workstations.
- **Function:** Operator visualization, alarm handling, trending, recipe management.
- **Protocols:** PROFINET (NRT), Modbus TCP, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA.
- **Latency:** 10–100 ms.

**Level 3 — Operations Layer (1.5 marks):**
- **Devices:** MES servers, historians (e.g., PI System), quality management.
- **Function:** Production tracking, OEE, batch genealogy, detailed scheduling.
- **Protocols:** OPC UA, SQL, MQTT, REST.
- **Latency:** 100 ms – 1 s.

**Level 4 — Enterprise Layer (1 mark):**
- **Devices:** ERP (SAP, Oracle), CRM, BI dashboards.
- **Function:** Long-term planning, procurement, finance, supply chain.
- **Protocols:** HTTP/S, SOAP, REST, SAP BAPI, EDI.
- **Latency:** Seconds to minutes (non-real-time).

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
```
[ERP L4] ⇄ [MES L3] ⇄ [SCADA L2] ⇄ [PLC L1] ⇄ [Sensors L0]
              ↕ OPC UA       ↕ PROFINET       ↕ PROFINET IRT
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation (3 marks):**
- **L0 → L1:** Field sensors/actuators connect to a Siemens ET200S remote I/O station via **PROFINET IRT** (deterministic, < 1 ms cycle).
- **L1:** The **S7-1500 PLC** processes the I/O and runs control logic.
- **L1 → L2:** A managed Ethernet switch carries **PROFINET NRT** traffic to the SCADA server.
- **L2 → L1/0 Boundary:** A **PROFINET-to-OPC UA gateway** publishes cyclic process data to the supervisory layer in a semantic, secure format.
- **L2 → L3:** The MES queries the OPC UA server for batch progress; historical data goes to a **PI Historian**.
- **L3 → L4:** A REST/SOAP adapter sends aggregated KPIs to SAP ERP.

**Role of OPC UA at L1/L2 Boundary (1 mark):**
- Acts as a **protocol-independent semantic translator**.
- Provides **end-to-end encryption** (X.509 certificates) — bridges OT and IT securely.
- Enables **information modeling** (e.g., companion specs for PLCopen, Weihenstephan).
- Decouples PLC vendors from MES/ERP vendors.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model** as the foundation of the Hierarchical Communication Model. How does it differ from the ISO/OSI model? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A PROFIBUS DP network has **6 slaves** with per-slave service time **$t_i = 2$ ms**, bit rate **$R = 500$ kbps**, frame length **$L = 320$ bits**, and idle time **$T_{idle} = 0.5$ ms**. Compute the **(i)** transmission time per frame, **(ii)** total bus cycle time, and **(iii)** worst-case latency. Is this suitable for a control loop requiring **< 25 ms** response? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**ISA-95 / Purdue Model Background (2 marks):**
- The **Purdue Reference Model for CIM** was developed at Purdue University (1990s) and later standardized as **ANSI/ISA-95** and **IEC 62264**.
- It is **function-oriented** and maps the enterprise into hierarchical levels based on **decision time horizon** and **data granularity**.
- It forms the **conceptual basis** of the Hierarchical Communication Model used in modern Industry 4.0 architectures.

**Hierarchy (2 marks):**
- Levels 0–2: **Process, Control, Supervisory** (OT — Operational Technology).
- Levels 3–4: **Operations, Enterprise** (IT — Information Technology).
- Modern extension: Level 5 (Cloud / IIoT).

**Differences from ISO/OSI (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Purdue / ISA-95 | ISO/OSI |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Functional enterprise modeling | Generic data communication |
| **Scope** | Entire factory/business | Single communication link |
| **Layering Basis** | Decision time & data semantics | Bit-level protocol functions |
| **Levels** | 5–6 enterprise levels | 7 protocol layers |
| **Audience** | Plant managers, control engineers | Network engineers |
| **Time Horizon** | Milliseconds → Years | Microseconds → Seconds |
| **Standardization** | ISA-95, IEC 62264 | ISO/IEC 7498-1 |

**Conclusion (built-in to above):** The Purdue model is **vertical and functional**; OSI is **horizontal and technical**. They are **complementary**, not competing.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- $N = 6$ slaves
- $t_i = 2$ ms (per-slave service time)
- $R = 500 \text{ kbps} = 5 \times 10^5$ bits/s
- $L = 320$ bits
- $T_{idle} = 0.5$ ms

**Part (i) — Transmission time per frame (2 marks):**

$$T_{tx} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{320}{5 \times 10^5} \text{ s}$$

$$T_{tx} = \frac{320}{5 \times 10^5} \times 10^3 \text{ ms} = \frac{320 \times 10^3}{5 \times 10^5} \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{tx} = \frac{320}{500} \text{ ms} = 0.64 \text{ ms}$$

**Part (ii) — Total bus cycle time (3 marks):**

$$T_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i + T_{idle}$$

$$T_{cycle} = 6 \times 2 \text{ ms} + 0.5 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{cycle} = 12.0 \text{ ms} + 0.5 \text{ ms} = 12.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Part (iii) — Worst-case latency & feasibility (2 marks):**

Worst-case latency (token rotation time):

$$T_{worst} = T_{cycle} = 12.5 \text{ ms}$$

Required response: < 25 ms.

$$T_{worst} = 12.5 \text{ ms} < 25 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The PROFIBUS DP network is **suitable** for the control loop, with a comfortable margin of $25 - 12.5 = 12.5$ ms (50% safety margin).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Mixing units:** Students often write $T_{tx} = 0.64$ s instead of ms. Always show the unit conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting $T_{idle}$:** Many omit the idle time, leading to an underestimate of cycle time.
> 3. **Misidentifying worst-case:** In token-bus, the worst case is **one full cycle**, not just the per-device time.
> 4. **Skipping the feasibility check:** Always end with a clear "suitable" or "not suitable" statement with a numerical comparison.
> 5. **Confusing Purdue levels with OSI layers:** They are different models for different purposes; do not equate them.
> 6. **Missing one level in enumeration:** Listing only 4 levels instead of 5 costs a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Hierarchical Communication Model:**

- ✅ HCM = **5 levels** (0–4), based on **Purdue / ISA-95**.
- ✅ **Level 0** = physical process (sensors, actuators).
- ✅ **Level 1** = basic control (PLC, DCS) — **deterministic protocols** (PROFIBUS, EtherCAT).
- ✅ **Level 2** = supervisory (SCADA, HMI) — **soft real-time** Ethernet.
- ✅ **Level 3** = operations (MES, historian) — **OPC UA, SQL, MQTT**.
- ✅ **Level 4** = enterprise (ERP, CRM) — **IT protocols** (HTTP, SOAP, REST).
- ✅ **Vertical communication** = inter-level (uses **gateways**).
- ✅ **Horizontal communication** = intra-level (peer-to-peer).
- ✅ **Information pyramid:** volume ↓ upward, value ↑ upward, latency ↑ upward.
- ✅ **Bus cycle formula:** $T_{cycle} = \sum t_i + T_{idle}$.
- ✅ **Transmission time:** $T_{tx} = L / R$ (always check bit/byte units).
- ✅ **Worst-case latency** in token bus = full cycle time.
- ✅ **HCM ≠ OSI model** — HCM is functional/vertical; OSI is technical/horizontal.
- ✅ **ISA-95 / IEC 62264** is the official standard; **IEC 62443** maps HCM to cybersecurity zones.
- ✅ **Modern extension:** Level 5 = Cloud / IIoT / Digital Twin (Industry 4.0).
- ✅ **Real exam hot topics:** Purdue levels with protocols, OPC UA's role, cycle-time calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Network Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Requirements** in the context of industrial automation are the rigorous set of functional, performance, environmental, safety, and security specifications that a communication network **must** satisfy to reliably interconnect field devices, controllers, sensors, actuators, and supervisory systems across the ISA-95 / IEC 62264 hierarchy (Levels 0–4) of an industrial plant.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framing, **Industrial Network Requirements** are a *constrained optimization problem*: the network must deliver **bounded latency, deterministic behavior, high availability, and guaranteed message delivery** under harsh electromagnetic, thermal, and mechanical stress — while remaining cost-effective and interoperable with heterogeneous devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Office/IT Networks:**
> Industrial networks are **not** designed to maximize bandwidth or throughput. They are designed to **minimize latency variance (jitter)** and **guarantee worst-case delivery deadlines**. This is the cardinal rule of industrial communication.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Operating Theatre vs. The Coffee Shop"

Imagine two communication scenarios:

| Scenario | Office Network (Coffee Shop) | Industrial Network (Operating Theatre) |
|----------|------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Goal** | Convey a large file eventually | Convey a critical "stop" command within **1 millisecond** — every time |
| **Failure consequence** | Email arrives 2 sec late | Robot arm crushes a workpiece / injures a human |
| **Tolerable delay** | Seconds to minutes | **Microseconds to milliseconds** |
| **Worst-case guarantee?** | Best-effort (TCP retry) | **Hard real-time guarantee** (deterministic) |
| **Environment** | Climate-controlled room | **-40 °C to +85 °C**, vibration, EMI from VFDs, dust |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Pump:** Industrial networks are like the *intercom system in an air traffic control tower*. It does not need to stream Netflix, but it absolutely *must* deliver a 200 ms voice packet — every single packet, with no drops, no jitter, no excuses. Network Requirements are the *engineering specification* for that intercom.

---

### 1.3 The Five Pillars of Industrial Network Requirements

The KTU 2024 curriculum organizes network requirements into **five interdependent pillars**:

1. **Real-Time & Determinism** — bounded, predictable delivery times.
2. **Reliability & Availability** — MTBF, MTTR, fault tolerance, redundancy.
3. **Robustness (Environmental)** — temperature, humidity, vibration, EMI/ESD immunity.
4. **Safety & Security** — SIL compliance (IEC 61508), CIP Security, zone/conduit (IEC 62443).
5. **Interoperability & Scalability** — open protocols (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, Modbus TCP), device profiles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (bolded for board exams):**
> - **Availability Target (A): ≥ 99.999 % ("five nines")** for process automation
> - **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF): ≥ 100,000 hours** for switches
> - **Cycle Time (T_cycle): 1 ms – 10 ms** for high-speed motion control
> - **Jitter (ΔL): ≤ 1 µs – 10 µs** for synchronized drives
> - **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC): IEC 61000-4** series compliance
> - **Operating Temperature: -40 °C to +85 °C** (industrial grade)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Five Pillars — Structured Logic

#### **Pillar 1: Real-Time & Determinism**

- **Real-time** ≠ "fast". It means the network delivers a message *before a strict deadline* $D$.
- **Determinism** = the worst-case latency $L_{max}$ is *known, bounded, and repeatable*.
- Three sub-classes (Kopetz classification):
  - **Hard real-time:** Missing the deadline = system failure (e.g., emergency stop). $D \leq 1 \text{ ms}$.
  - **Soft real-time:** Missing the deadline degrades quality (e.g., video monitoring). $D \leq 100 \text{ ms}$.
  - **Non-real-time:** No deadline (e.g., firmware download).

> **"Why" reasoning:** A PLC scanning 1000 I/O points every 1 ms cannot tolerate a *single* 5 ms delay, or the entire closed-loop control becomes unstable (Nyquist violation).

#### **Pillar 2: Reliability & Availability**

- **Reliability (R):** Probability the network performs its function over interval $[0, t]$.
- **Availability (A):** Fraction of time the system is *up and operational*, including repair.
- **Fault tolerance:** Ability to deliver messages even when nodes/links fail (achieved via ring/mesh redundancy, e.g., MRP, PRP, HSR).

#### **Pillar 3: Robustness (Environmental Hardening)**

- Industrial switches are **IP67-rated**, **conformal-coated**, **vibration-tested** (IEC 60068-2-6), and **EMC-certified** (IEC 61000-4-2 to 6).
- Cables use **M12 D-coded / X-coded connectors** instead of RJ45 to resist vibration.

#### **Pillar 4: Safety & Security**

- **Functional Safety:** Network must support **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** 1–4 per IEC 61508 and IEC 61784-3 (e.g., PROFIsafe, CIP Safety, SafetyNET p).
- **IT Security:** Per **IEC 62443**, networks are segmented into **zones & conduits** with firewalls, ACLs, and authentication (RADIUS, TACACS+, 802.1X).

#### **Pillar 5: Interoperability & Scalability**

- **Interoperability:** Devices from different vendors must communicate using **standardized protocols** (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, Modbus TCP, EtherCAT, CC-Link IE).
- **Scalability:** Network must accommodate addition of nodes without redesigning topology or exceeding timing budgets.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains every formula a KTU examiner can ask under "Network Requirements". Memorize the units and definitions — the units are the most-skipped part in the answer scripts.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | **End-to-End Latency** | $L = T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}$ | seconds (s) | Total delay a single packet experiences |
| 2 | **Transmission Delay** | $T_{tx} = \dfrac{N_{bits}}{R_{link}}$ | seconds | Time to push packet bits onto the wire |
| 3 | **Propagation Delay** | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v_{medium}}$ | seconds | Time for signal to traverse physical medium |
| 4 | **Jitter** | $J = L_{max} - L_{min}$ | seconds | Latency variance — *the killer of real-time* |
| 5 | **Effective Throughput** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{payload} \cdot f_{msg}}{N_{total}}$ | bits/s (bps) | Useful data rate excluding overhead |
| 6 | **Cycle Time** | $T_{cycle} = T_{scan} + T_{comm} + T_{reaction}$ | seconds | Time between successive I/O updates |
| 7 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = BW \cdot RTT$ | bits | Data "in flight" — dictates buffer size |
| 8 | **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Fraction of time system is operational |
| 9 | **Unavailability (Downtime/year)** | $U = 1 - A$ | hours/year | Allowed outage window per year |
| 10 | **Signal Propagation Speed (copper)** | $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$ | m/s | ~ 5 ns/m — used in all delay calcs |
| 11 | **Signal Propagation Speed (fiber)** | $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$ | m/s | Same as copper (≈ 0.66c) |
| 12 | **Probability of Packet Loss (Bernoulli)** | $P_{loss} = 1 - (1-p)^{N_{hops}}$ | dimensionless | Used for redundant path selection |
| 13 | **Worst-Case Latency (TDMA)** | $L_{max} = N_{nodes} \cdot T_{slot}$ | seconds | Deterministic bound in scheduled networks |
| 14 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (BER basis)** | $BER \approx \dfrac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{SNR}\right)$ | dimensionless | Link quality metric |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Requirements

> [!TIP]
> - **Automotive paint shop:** 6-axis robots synchronized over **EtherCAT** — sub-100 µs cycle time requirement drives determinism design.
> - **Power substation (IEC 61850):** **GOOSE messages** must be delivered in **< 4 ms** — drives the use of **PRP/HSR** zero-loss redundancy.
> - **Pharmaceutical batch control (ISA-88):** Recipes replicated across 50 PLCs — drives **interoperability** and **redundancy** requirements.
> - **Offshore oil rig:** -20 °C, salt fog, vibration — drives **IP67, M12, conformal coating** environmental specifications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Worst-Case Cycle Time for a Polled Industrial Network

**Problem Setup:** A PROFINET IRT network has **$N = 10$ slaves** connected to a master PLC. The PLC polls each slave in a deterministic round-robin cycle. Each slave requires **$T_{proc} = 50 \,\mu s$** to respond, and the link rate is **$R = 100$ Mbps**. Each response is **$L_{msg} = 256$ bytes** long. Compute the worst-case cycle time.

**Step 1 — Convert message length to bits**

$$
L_{msg} = 256 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} = 2048 \text{ bits}
$$

*Logic:* Network throughput is always expressed in *bits per second*, not bytes. The conversion factor is 8 bits per byte.

**Step 2 — Compute the transmission delay per slave**

$$
T_{tx} = \frac{N_{bits}}{R_{link}} = \frac{2048 \text{ bits}}{100 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}}
$$

$$
T_{tx} = 20.48 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 20.48 \,\mu s
$$

*Logic:* Transmission delay is the time the *last bit* of the packet spends entering the medium. It depends only on packet size and link rate, not distance.

**Step 3 — Compute per-slave service time**

The PLC must wait for both the transmission **and** the slave's processing before issuing the next poll:

$$
T_{service} = T_{tx} + T_{proc} = 20.48 \,\mu s + 50 \,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{service} = 70.48 \,\mu s
$$

*Logic:* The service time is the sum of two sequential, non-overlapping activities — so the delays add linearly.

**Step 4 — Compute the worst-case cycle time (sum over all slaves)**

$$
T_{cycle} = N_{slaves} \times T_{service} = 10 \times 70.48 \,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{cycle} = 704.8 \,\mu s \approx 0.705 \text{ ms}
$$

*Logic:* In a *polled* topology, the master must visit every slave before beginning a new cycle. This is the deterministic worst case — no parallelism, no queuing, no jitter.

**Step 5 — Compare with the real-time deadline**

For a typical motion-control application, the deadline is $D = 1 \text{ ms}$:

$$
T_{cycle} = 0.705 \text{ ms} < D = 1 \text{ ms} \;\; \checkmark \text{ (Real-time compliant)}
$$

*Logic:* The deadline test is the *contract* between the network designer and the control engineer. If $T_{cycle} > D$, the design is rejected.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Jitter Bound for a Scheduled (TDMA) Network

**Problem Setup:** A network with $N = 8$ nodes uses a TDMA scheduler with slot duration $T_{slot} = 25 \,\mu s$. The jitter of the network equals the maximum variation in latency across the 8 time slots.

**Step 1 — Recognize that in TDMA, latency is a multiple of $T_{slot}$**

A node transmitting in slot 1 has latency $1 \cdot T_{slot}$, a node in slot 8 has latency $8 \cdot T_{slot}$. Therefore:

$$
L_{min} = 1 \cdot T_{slot} = 25 \,\mu s
$$

$$
L_{max} = 8 \cdot T_{slot} = 200 \,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the jitter formula**

$$
J = L_{max} - L_{min} = 200 \,\mu s - 25 \,\mu s
$$

$$
J = 175 \,\mu s
$$

*Logic:* Jitter is the *range* of latencies. For synchronized motion control (e.g., 32 axes on a printing press), this jitter must be $< 1 \,\mu s$ — forcing the use of hardware-synchronized protocols like **EtherCAT** with distributed clocks.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Availability → Annual Downtime Conversion

**Problem Setup:** A substation SCADA network claims **$MTBF = 50{,}000$ hours** and **$MTTR = 4$ hours**. Compute its availability and the *allowed* annual downtime.

**Step 1 — Compute Availability**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{50{,}000}{50{,}000 + 4}
$$

$$
A = \frac{50{,}000}{50{,}004} = 0.99992 = 99.992 \%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Unavailability**

$$
U = 1 - A = 1 - 0.99992 = 8 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to annual downtime**

$$
D_{year} = U \times T_{year} = 8 \times 10^{-5} \times 8760 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
D_{year} \approx 0.7008 \text{ hours/year} \approx 42 \text{ minutes/year}
$$

*Logic:* Industrial plant operators quote downtime in *minutes per year*. The "five nines" ($A = 99.999\%$) target allows only **5.26 minutes/year** of outage — and that is the *reason* industrial networks use ring/mesh redundancy (MRP, PRP, HSR).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Requirements to Protocols

| Requirement Category | PROFINET | EtherCAT | Modbus TCP | EtherNet/IP | Industrial Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) |
|----------------------|----------|----------|------------|-------------|---------------------------------|
| **Min Cycle Time** | 31.25 µs (IRT) | 12.5 µs | ~10–50 ms | 1–10 ms | > 5 ms (CSMA/CA) |
| **Determinism** | ★★★★★ (IRT) | ★★★★★ (DW) | ★★ | ★★★ | ★ |
| **Jitter** | < 1 µs | < 1 µs (DC) | High | Medium | Very High |
| **Availability** | High (MRP) | High (cable redundancy) | Low (single master) | Medium (DLR ring) | Low |
| **EMI Robustness** | High (M12, shielded) | High | Medium | Medium | Poor |
| **Safety Support** | PROFIsafe (SIL 3) | FSoE (SIL 3) | None native | CIP Safety | Limited |
| **Best Use Case** | Motion control | High-speed I/O | Legacy SCADA | Discrete ctrl | Mobile AGVs |

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix is your *revision weapon*. KTU examiners love "compare X and Y" questions — this table contains the answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Five Pillars Hierarchy

```mermaid

    P0[INDUSTRIAL NETWORK REQUIREMENTS]

    P0 --> P3[ROBUSTNESS AND ENVIRONMENT]

    P1 --> P1A[Hard Real Time deadline under 1 ms]

    P2 --> P2A[MTBF greater than 100000 hours]

    P3 --> P3A[IP67 Sealing for dust and water]

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> **"List and briefly explain any three key requirements that distinguish an industrial network from a conventional office LAN."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate 1 mark per point):**

1. **Determinism / Bounded Latency:** Industrial networks must guarantee *worst-case* message delivery times (e.g., 1 ms for motion control). Office LANs are best-effort — an email arriving "later" is acceptable; a robot's stop command arriving late is catastrophic. *(1 mark)*

2. **Real-Time Capability / Hard Deadlines:** Industrial networks operate under *hard real-time* constraints, where missing a deadline constitutes a system failure (e.g., emergency-stop, interlocking). Office networks operate *soft real-time* or non-real-time. *(1 mark)*

3. **Environmental Robustness:** Industrial devices are hardened to **-40 °C to +85 °C**, IP67 sealed, EMC-compliant (IEC 61000-4), and use **M12 connectors** to withstand vibration. Office equipment operates only in climate-controlled rooms. *(1 mark)*

*(Acceptable alternative for the third point: Functional Safety / SIL compliance, or High Availability with redundancy.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> **"Define the term 'jitter' in the context of industrial networks. Why is low jitter critical for synchronized motion control?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Jitter $J$ is the variation in end-to-end latency experienced by successive packets, defined mathematically as
$$
J = L_{max} - L_{min}
$$
where $L_{max}$ and $L_{min}$ are the maximum and minimum latencies observed. It is measured in seconds (typically microseconds in industrial systems).

- **Criticality for motion control (1.5 marks):** In multi-axis motion (e.g., a CNC machine with 6 synchronized servos), all axes must receive their position command at *the same instant*. Even a few microseconds of jitter causes **positional error, jerk, and mechanical stress** — degrading product quality and risking machine damage. Protocols like **EtherCAT** use *distributed clocks* to achieve **sub-microsecond synchronization** precisely to eliminate this jitter.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **five major categories of network requirements** for industrial automation systems. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A PROFINET IRT network interconnects **$N = 16$** servo drives with a controller. Each drive exchanges **$L_{msg} = 128$ bytes** per cycle, the link rate is **$R = 100$ Mbps**, and each drive has an internal processing delay of **$T_{proc} = 30 \,\mu s$**. Compute the worst-case cycle time and determine whether the network meets a motion-control deadline of **$D = 500 \,\mu s$**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution A(a) — Five Categories of Network Requirements (7 marks)**

The five major categories are:

**1. Real-Time and Determinism (1.5 marks)**
The network must deliver messages within a *strict, bounded* time window. Determinism requires that the **worst-case latency** $L_{max}$ is known and reproducible. Industrial networks are classified as:
- **Hard real-time:** $L_{max} \leq 1 \text{ ms}$ (motion control, interlocks)
- **Soft real-time:** $L_{max} \leq 100 \text{ ms}$ (HMI refresh, alarming)
- **Non-real-time:** no bound (firmware updates, logging)

**2. Reliability and Availability (1.5 marks)**
Reliability $R(t)$ is the probability of correct operation over time; availability $A$ is the fraction of time the system is operational:

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

Industrial targets demand $A \geq 99.999\%$ ("five nines"). Achieved through **ring/mesh redundancy** (MRP, PRP, HSR), hot-swappable components, and **diagnostics**.

**3. Environmental Robustness (1 mark)**
Field-grade hardware must operate in harsh conditions: **-40 °C to +85 °C**, humidity, dust, vibration, and electromagnetic interference from VFDs and welding equipment. Compliance: **IP67 sealing**, **IEC 61000-4 EMC**, **M12 connectors**.

**4. Safety and Security (1.5 marks)**
- **Functional safety** is mandated by **IEC 61508 / IEC 61784-3** (PROFIsafe, CIP Safety, FSoE) for SIL 1–4.
- **IT security** follows **IEC 62443** — segmentation into zones/conduits, firewalls, **802.1X authentication**, encryption (TLS, MACsec).

**5. Interoperability and Scalability (1.5 marks)**
Devices from multiple vendors must interoperate using **open standards** (PROFINET, EtherCAT, EtherNet/IP, Modbus TCP, OPC UA). The network must scale by adding nodes without violating timing budgets or replacing infrastructure.

*(Block diagram: refer to Section 4.1 — the Five Pillars hierarchy.)*

---

#### **Solution A(b) — Cycle-Time Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Convert message length to bits (1 mark)**
$$
L_{msg} = 128 \text{ bytes} \times 8 = 1024 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2: Compute transmission delay per drive (1 mark)**
$$
T_{tx} = \frac{N_{bits}}{R_{link}} = \frac{1024}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 10.24 \,\mu s
$$

**Step 3: Compute per-drive service time (1.5 marks)**
$$
T_{service} = T_{tx} + T_{proc} = 10.24 + 30 = 40.24 \,\mu s
$$

**Step 4: Compute worst-case cycle time (1.5 marks)**
$$
T_{cycle} = N \times T_{service} = 16 \times 40.24 = 643.84 \,\mu s
$$

**Step 5: Compare with deadline (1.5 marks)**
$$
T_{cycle} = 643.84 \,\mu s > D = 500 \,\mu s \;\; \boldsymbol{\times} \text{ (NOT real-time compliant)}
$$

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):** The design **violates** the motion-control deadline by $\approx 144 \,\mu s$. Remediation: (i) reduce per-drive payload to 64 bytes → $T_{cycle} \approx 322 \,\mu s$, or (ii) use a parallel-processing protocol like **EtherCAT** (which processes "on-the-fly" and is not a polled network) → $T_{cycle} \approx 12.5 \,\mu s$.

---

> **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model — Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **MTBF, MTTR, and Availability** with formulas. A substation communication network has **$MTBF = 80{,}000$ hours** and **$MTTR = 6$ hours**. Compute its availability and the equivalent annual downtime in minutes. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **real-time performance, redundancy mechanisms, and safety support** of **PROFINET, EtherCAT, and Modbus TCP** industrial protocols. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution B(a) — MTBF / MTTR / Availability (7 marks)**

**Definitions (3 marks):**
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The expected time interval between two consecutive failures of a repairable system. Unit: **hours**.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** The average time required to diagnose, repair, and restore a failed system to operation. Unit: **hours**.
- **Availability (A):** The fraction of total time the system is *up* and *functional*, including planned and unplanned downtime. Unit: **dimensionless (0 to 1)** or **percentage**.

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Calculation (2 marks):**
$$
A = \frac{80{,}000}{80{,}000 + 6} = \frac{80{,}000}{80{,}006} = 0.999925
$$
$$
A \approx 99.9925 \%
$$

**Annual Downtime (1 mark):**
$$
D_{year} = (1 - A) \times 8760 \text{ hours} = 7.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 8760 = 0.657 \text{ hours/year}
$$
$$
D_{year} \approx 39.42 \text{ minutes/year}
$$

---

#### **Solution B(b) — Comparison of PROFINET, EtherCAT, Modbus TCP (7 marks)**

| Feature | PROFINET | EtherCAT | Modbus TCP |
|---------|----------|----------|------------|
| **Real-Time Class** | RT (soft, ~1–10 ms); IRT (hard, ~31.25 µs) | Hard real-time (~12.5 µs cycle) | Non-real-time (~10–50 ms) |
| **Cycle Mechanism** | Polled (RT) / Scheduled time-slice (IRT) | "Processing-on-the-fly" (sum of frame) | Master-slave polling, no scheduling |
| **Jitter** | < 1 µs (IRT with sync) | < 1 µs (distributed clocks) | High (variable CSMA/CD) |
| **Redundancy** | MRP (ring), MRPD, PRP, HSR | Cable redundancy (primary/secondary) | None native — single point of failure |
| **Functional Safety** | PROFIsafe (SIL 3) over the same cable | FSoE — Fail Safe over EtherCAT (SIL 3) | None native (separate safety bus required) |
| **Topology** | Line, star, ring | Line, ring, tree (daisy-chain) | Star (Ethernet switch) |
| **Best Suited For** | Process & motion control, factory automation | High-speed motion, machine tools | Legacy SCADA, simple I/O polling |

*Allot ~2.3 marks per row of meaningful comparison. Examiner awards partial credit for identifying distinguishing features even without the full table.*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks-loss traps in "Network Requirements" questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "real-time" with "fast":** Writing "industrial networks are fast" loses 1 mark. Correct phrasing: *"they deliver messages within a guaranteed deadline."*
> 2. **Forgetting units in formulas:** Writing $T_{tx} = 1024/100$ without showing $\times 10^6$ loses 1 mark in the calculation sub-part.
> 3. **Writing $A = MTBF - MTTR$:** This is a very common algebraic mistake. The correct relation is *ratio*, not difference.
> 4. **Omitting the "comparison axis" in compare-type answers:** Always structure as *"with respect to X, PROFINET does A, whereas Modbus does B."*
> 5. **Ignoring environmental requirements:** A surprising number of students answer "reliability" questions by listing only MTBF and forget IP rating, EMC, and temperature range.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Network requirements in industry = bounded latency + determinism + reliability + safety + interoperability.**
- **Real-time ≠ fast; it means deadline-bounded.** Hard real-time: < 1 ms.
- **Jitter is the *killer* of synchronized control** → use protocols with distributed clocks (EtherCAT, PROFINET IRT).
- **Five-nines availability (99.999%)** = at most **5.26 minutes of downtime per year** → requires MRP/PRP/HSR redundancy.
- **Cycle-time formula:** $T_{cycle} = N \times (T_{tx} + T_{proc})$ for a polled network; *cannot* exceed the control-loop deadline.
- **Latency components:** $L = T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}$.
- **Jitter formula:** $J = L_{max} - L_{min}$.
- **Availability formula:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
- **EMC standard:** IEC 61000-4 series (always cite this in environmental questions).
- **Connector standard:** M12 D-coded (100 Mbps) / X-coded (10 Gbps) for industrial Ethernet.
- **Safety standard:** IEC 61508 (SIL) and IEC 61784-3 (safe bus profiles).
- **Security standard:** IEC 62443 (zones and conduits).
- **Key protocols to know cold:** PROFINET (RT/IRT), EtherCAT, Modbus TCP, EtherNet/IP.
- **Signal speed:** $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in both copper and fiber.
- **Industrial temperature range:** **-40 °C to +85 °C** (memorize verbatim).
- **MTBF target for industrial switches:** $\geq 100{,}000$ hours.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OSI Reference Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** OSI Reference Model # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) Reference Model** is a seven-layer, abstract, vendor-neutral conceptual framework standardized by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** under standard **ISO/IEC 7498-1**. It was published in 1984 and revised in 1994. The model defines how heterogeneous computing and communication systems transmit, route, and receive data across a network by partitioning the entire communication process into **seven distinct, hierarchical, and modular abstraction layers**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** > The OSI model is treated as the *universal reference architecture* against which all industrial communication protocols (PROFIBUS, Foundation Fieldbus, Modbus, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA) are mapped. For PEICT747, students must master the **function, PDU, and address type of each layer**, not merely the layer names. ### The Seven Layers (Top-Down View) | # | Layer | Primary Function | Data Unit (PDU) | Addressing | |---|-------|------------------|-----------------|------------| | 7 | **Application** | User interface & network services | Data / Message | — | | 6 | **Presentation** | Data translation, encryption, compression | Data / Message | — | | 5 | **Session** | Dialog control & synchronization | Data / Message | — | | 4 | **Transport** | End-to-end reliability, segmentation | **Segment** (TCP) / **Datagram** (UDP) | Port Number | | 3 | **Network** | Logical addressing & routing | **Packet** | IP Address | | 2 | **Data Link** | Node-to-node framing & error detection | **Frame** | MAC Address | | 1 | **Physical** | Bit transmission over medium | **Bits** / **Symbols** | — | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal Mail System" Imagine you are a Kerala student posting a greeting card to a friend studying in Bangalore. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: Sending a Letter through India Post** 1. **You (Layer 7 - Application):** Write a greeting message on the card. This is the *data* you want to communicate. 2. **Layer 6 - Presentation:** You translate the message from English into Hindi so your friend can read it. This is *encoding/translation*. 3. **Layer 5 - Session:** You call your friend on the phone and say, *"I am about to post a card, please confirm when received."* This establishes a *dialog and synchronization*. 4. **Layer 4 - Transport:** You break a long letter into 4 envelopes (segments) and number them 1, 2, 3, 4 so they can be reassembled correctly. This is *segmentation and reliability*. 5. **Layer 3 - Network:** You write the *destination PIN code* (560001 - Bangalore) and your own PIN code (e.g., 682001 - Kochi) on each envelope. These are *logical addresses* for routing. 6. **Layer 2 - Data Link:** The post office assigns a *local tracking number* and ensures no damage during sorting. The envelope is *framed*. 7. **Layer 1 - Physical:** A postal van, train, or aeroplane physically carries the envelope from Kerala to Karnataka. This is the *physical medium* (copper, fibre, RF, light). When the receiver's friend gets the letter, the process is **reversed (decapsulation)** — envelope opens → translated → segments reassembled → read. --- ## 1.3 Why Does Industrial Networks Need a Reference Model? In a **Plant Floor** or **SCADA** environment, devices like PLCs (Siemens S7-1200), DCS controllers, HMIs, smart sensors (pressure, temperature), and actuators must interoperate. Without a layered model: - A vendor-specific protocol from Honeywell could never talk to a Yokogawa controller. - Diagnosing network faults (cable breaks vs. IP conflicts vs. application crashes) would be impossible. The **OSI model acts as the "common language"** that lets fieldbus protocols like **PROFINET, EtherCAT, Modbus TCP** all be dissected, analyzed, and troubleshot using the same mental framework. > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Mnemonic (Top-Down):** > **A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing > (Application → Presentation → Session → Transport → Network → Data Link → Physical) **Bottom-Up Mnemonic:** > **P**lease **D**o **N**ot **T**hrow **S**ausage **P**izza **A**way > (Physical → Data Link → Network → Transport → Session → Presentation → Application) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Design Principles of the OSI Model The OSI architecture rests on **five foundational design principles** mandated by ISO/IEC 7498-1: 1. **Layering Principle:** Each layer performs a well-defined function and offers services only to the layer directly above it. 2. **Service Abstraction:** A layer is invisible (transparent) to layers above; only *Service Access Points (SAPs)* are exposed. 3. **Encapsulation:** Each layer appends its own header (and sometimes trailer) — known as a **Protocol Control Information (PCI)** — to the Service Data Unit (SDU) received from the layer above. 4. **Peer-to-Peer Communication:** Logically, layer *N* on the source machine communicates with layer *N* on the destination machine using a defined *N-layer protocol*. 5. **Independence of Technology:** The model does not mandate any specific physical medium, making it usable for fibre optics, twisted-pair copper, RF, or even acoustic signalling. --- ## 2.2 The Three-Segment Conceptual Model The OSI standards partition the seven layers into **three functional sub-zones** that KTU examiners frequently test: | Segment | Layers | Function | Example in Industry | |---------|--------|----------|---------------------| | **Network-Access (Lower) Layers** | 1 & 2 | Electrical, mechanical, framing of bits over the local link | RS-485, Ethernet PHY, PROFIBUS physical layer | | **Internet (Middle) Layers** | 3 & 4 | Logical addressing, routing, end-to-end transport | IP routing, TCP/UDP, Modbus TCP transport | | **Application (Upper) Layers** | 5, 6, 7 | Application semantics, syntax, dialog management | OPC UA, EtherNet/IP CIP, Foundation Fieldbus H1 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industrial Insight:** Most **real-time fieldbus protocols** (PROFINET IRT, EtherCAT, Sercos III) collapse the upper three or four layers of OSI into a single optimized application layer because **deterministic latency** is critical in motion control loops. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Layer Reference Matrix > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL:** The following table is the **single most important artifact** for KTU ESE answers. Every 14-mark question on the OSI model expects a structured comparison like this. | Layer | Name | **PDU Name** | Addressing Scheme | Typical Devices/Protocols | Industrial Use-Case | |:-----:|------|:------------:|-------------------|---------------------------|---------------------| | **7** | Application | Message / Data | API Endpoints | HTTP, FTP, **OPC UA, EtherNet/IP CIP** | SCADA-HMI reporting | | **6** | Presentation | Message / Data | — | TLS/SSL, MIME, XDR, ASCII↔EBCDIC | Secure vendor telemetry | | **5** | Session | Message / Data | Session Token | NetBIOS, RPC, **PROFINET RPC** | Establish PLC-PLC context | | **4** | Transport | Segment / Datagram | **Port #** (16-bit) | **TCP (reliable), UDP (best-effort)** | Modbus TCP, DNP3 over TCP | | **3** | Network | Packet | **IP Address** (IPv4 32-bit / IPv6 128-bit) | IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, OSPF | Routing between plant & MES | | **2** | Data Link | Frame | **MAC Address** (48-bit) | Ethernet IEEE 802.3, Wi-Fi 802.11, **PROFIBUS FDL** | Cell-level determinism | | **1** | Physical | Bits / Symbols | — | RS-232, RS-485, 100BASE-TX, Fibre, **HART physical** | Cable & connector specs | ### 2.3.1 Key Boundary Equations The encapsulation process obeys the following recursive identity: $$ PDU^{(N)} = \underbrace{H^{(N)}}_{Header} \;\cup\; \underbrace{SDU^{(N)}}_{Payload\,from\,above} $$ where $\cup$ denotes logical concatenation and the SDU of layer $N$ is the PDU of layer $N+1$: $$ SDU^{(N)} \;=\; PDU^{(N+1)} $$ For Data Link and below, a **Trailer** (often containing CRC) is appended: $$ PDU^{(2)} \;=\; H^{(2)} \;\cup\; SDU^{(2)} \;\cup\; \underbrace{T^{(2)}}_{CRC\,Trailer} $$ --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Troubleshooting (Bottom-Up method):** KTU lab questions often ask, *"A SCADA PC cannot see a PLC. Diagnose the failure."* The OSI model forces an engineer to walk from Layer 1 (link light ON?), Layer 2 (ARP working?), Layer 3 (ping IP?), Layer 4 (port open?), up to Layer 7 (OPC UA service running?). - **Protocol Mapping:** When evaluating whether to migrate from PROFIBUS to PROFINET, engineers map each PROFIBUS layer to its PROFINET counterpart. - **Security Engineering:** Firewalls operate at Layers 3 (packet filter), 4 (stateful, port-aware), and 7 (deep packet inspection — OPC UA filtering). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Encapsulation Sequence Let the **original Application Data** generated by the HMI/SCADA software be the abstract byte-string $\mathcal{D}_7$. **Step 1 — Layer 7 (Application):** The data is treated as an atomic message. $$ PDU^{(7)} = \mathcal{D}_7 $$ No header is added at the application layer for the OSI reference model (although specific protocols like EtherNet/IP do append CIP headers). **Step 2 — Layer 6 (Presentation):** Translation/encryption occurs. For example, the byte-stream might be converted from ASCII to UTF-8. Mathematically: $$ \mathcal{D}_6 = \mathcal{T}_{enc}(\mathcal{D}_7) $$ where $\mathcal{T}_{enc}$ is the encoding transformation. Then: $$ PDU^{(6)} = \mathcal{D}_6 $$ **Step 3 — Layer 5 (Session):** A session identifier is established and a session header $\mathcal{H}_5$ is prepended. $$ PDU^{(5)} = \mathcal{H}_5 \;\cup\; PDU^{(6)} $$ **Step 4 — Layer 4 (Transport):** The session PDU is segmented. Suppose the maximum segment size is $M$ bytes (for Ethernet, $M = 1460$ for TCP). Let the length of $PDU^{(5)}$ be $L$ bytes. $$ \text{Number of segments} \;=\; N \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{L}{M} \right\rceil $$ Each segment $i \in [1, N]$ receives a transport header $\mathcal{H}_4^{(i)}$ containing source port $p_s$, destination port $p_d$, and sequence number $SEQ_i$. $$ PDU^{(4)}_i \;=\; \mathcal{H}_4^{(i)} \;\cup\; \underbrace{PDU^{(5)}[(i-1)M + 1 \;..\; \min(iM, L)]}_{\text{segment slice}} $$ **Step 5 — Layer 3 (Network):** Each segment is encapsulated in an IP packet. The IPv4 header $\mathcal{H}_3$ contains a 32-bit source IP $IP_s$ and destination IP $IP_d$. $$ PDU^{(3)}_i \;=\; \mathcal{H}_3(IP_s, IP_d) \;\cup\; PDU^{(4)}_i $$ **Step 6 — Layer 2 (Data Link):** Each packet is framed. For Ethernet, a 14-byte header $\mathcal{H}_2$ (src MAC, dst MAC, EtherType) is prepended and a 4-byte trailer $T^{(2)}$ (CRC-32) is appended. $$ PDU^{(2)}_i \;=\; \mathcal{H}_2(MAC_s, MAC_d) \;\cup\; PDU^{(3)}_i \;\cup\; T^{(2)}_{CRC} $$ **Step 7 — Layer 1 (Physical):** The frame bits $b_1 b_2 \dots b_k$ are encoded onto the medium using a line code such as Manchester, 8B/10B, or PAM. $$ \text{Physical Signal} \;=\; \mathcal{E}\left(\{b_1, b_2, \dots, b_k\}\right) $$ where $\mathcal{E}$ is the chosen encoding function (e.g., 100BASE-TX uses MLT-3; 1000BASE-T uses 4D-PAM5). --- ## 3.2 Decapsulation (Reverse Process) On the destination side, layers strip their peer-layer's header: $$ \text{Reassembled Data} \;=\; \text{Reassemble}\Big(\big\{PDU^{(4)}_1, PDU^{(4)}_2, \dots, PDU^{(4)}_N\big\}\Big) \;=\; \mathcal{D}_7 $$ This demonstrates the **fundamental symmetry** of the OSI model. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Layer Exists | Layer | Failure If Removed | Direct KTU 14-Mark Question Angle | |-------|--------------------|-----------------------------------| | 1 | No bits on the wire | "Design an RS-485 physical layer for 1.2 km" | | 2 | Collisions, framing errors, no MAC | "Explain CSMA/CD in Ethernet" | | 3 | Cannot route across networks | "Compare IPv4 vs IPv6 addressing" | | 4 | No port multiplexing, no reliability | "TCP vs UDP for SCADA traffic" | | 5 | Cannot manage long-lived PLC sessions | "How does OPC UA session resume after reconnect?" | | 6 | Vendor A bytes unreadable to Vendor B | "Translate IEEE 754 floats vs vendor-specific floats" | | 7 | No common API | "OPC UA vs Modbus at the application layer" | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Marks are awarded for **mapping** any industrial protocol onto the OSI layers. Memorize the mapping for at least **Modbus TCP, PROFIBUS DP, EtherNet/IP, and PROFINET**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 OSI Seven-Layer Reference Architecture ```mermaid graph TD A["LAYER 7 APPLICATION
OPC UA EtherNet IP Modbus TCP"] B["LAYER 6 PRESENTATION
TLS SSL XML JSON Encoding"] C["LAYER 5 SESSION
Dialog Control Synchronization"] D["LAYER 4 TRANSPORT
TCP UDP Port Addressing"] E["LAYER 3 NETWORK
IPv4 IPv6 ICMP Routing"] F["LAYER 2 DATA LINK
Ethernet MAC LLC 802.11"] G["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL
RS485 Fibre RF Copper Signal"] A ==> B B ==> C C ==> D D ==> E E ==> F F ==> G G -.->|Bit Stream Over Medium| F2["RECEIVER SIDE LAYER 1"] F2 -.-> F3["LAYER 2 DATA LINK"] F3 -.-> F4["LAYER 3 NETWORK"] F4 -.-> F5["LAYER 4 TRANSPORT"] F5 -.-> F6["LAYER 5 SESSION"] F6 -.-> F7["LAYER 6 PRESENTATION"] F7 -.-> F8["LAYER 7 APPLICATION DATA"] style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style C fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style D fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#deeaf6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style G fill:#f2f2f2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F2 fill:#f2f2f2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F3 fill:#deeaf6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F4 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F5 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F6 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style F7 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style F8 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Encapsulation Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SRC["SOURCE PLC S7 1200"] L7["L7 Message
OPC UA Read Request"] --> L6["L6 Encoded Bytes"] L6 --> L5["L5 Session Header + L6"] L5 --> L4["L4 Segment
TCP Header + L5"] L4 --> L3["L3 Packet
IP Header + L4"] L3 --> L2["L2 Frame
MAC Header + L3 + CRC"] L2 --> L1["L1 Bits on Copper or Fibre"] end L1 -->|Switched Network| L1R["L1 Bits at Receiver"] L1R --> L2R["L2 Remove CRC and MAC Header"] L2R --> L3R["L3 Remove IP Header"] L3R --> L4R["L4 Reassemble Segments"] L4R --> L5R["L5 Close or Resume Session"] L5R --> L6R["L6 Decode to Native Format"] L6R --> L7R["L7 Deliver to HMI Application"] L7R --> APP["SCADA HMI Display"] style L7 fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff style L1 fill:#f2f2f2,color:#000 style L7R fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff style APP fill:#70ad47,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Protocol Mapping Table — OSI Layers for Common Industrial Protocols ```mermaid graph LR subgraph OSI7["OSI MODEL"] OS7["Layer 7 Application"] OS6["Layer 6 Presentation"] OS5["Layer 5 Session"] OS4["Layer 4 Transport"] OS3["Layer 3 Network"] OS2["Layer 2 Data Link"] OS1["Layer 1 Physical"] end MODBUS["Modbus RTU"] PROFIBUS["PROFIBUS DP"] EIP["EtherNet IP"] MODBUSTCP["Modbus TCP"] PROFINET["PROFINET"] MODBUS --> OS1 MODBUS --> OS2 MODBUS --> OS7 PROFIBUS --> OS1 PROFIBUS --> OS2 PROFIBUS --> OS7 EIP --> OS1 EIP --> OS2 EIP --> OS3 EIP --> OS4 EIP --> OS7 MODBUSTCP --> OS1 MODBUSTCP --> OS2 MODBUSTCP --> OS3 MODBUSTCP --> OS4 MODBUSTCP --> OS7 PROFINET --> OS1 PROFINET --> OS2 PROFINET --> OS3 PROFINET --> OS4 PROFINET --> OS7 style MODBUS fill:#c00000,color:#fff style PROFIBUS fill:#7030a0,color:#fff style EIP fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style MODBUSTCP fill:#ffc000,color:#000 style PROFINET fill:#00b050,color:#fff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > *List the seven layers of the OSI Reference Model in the correct order. For each layer, state the corresponding Protocol Data Unit (PDU) name.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Layer | PDU | |---|-------|-----| | 7 | Application | Message / Data | | 6 | Presentation | Message / Data | | 5 | Session | Message / Data | | 4 | Transport | **Segment** (TCP) / **Datagram** (UDP) | | 3 | Network | **Packet** | | 2 | Data Link | **Frame** | | 1 | Physical | **Bits / Symbols** | *(1 mark for correct sequence, 1 mark for PDUs of L3/L4, 1 mark for PDU of L1/L2.)* --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > *Differentiate between the functions of the Network layer and the Transport layer in the OSI model. Give one example protocol for each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Network Layer (Layer 3):** Responsible for **logical addressing and routing** of packets across multiple networks. It decides the path from source to destination through routers. *Example:* **IPv4 / IPv6, OSPF, BGP**. - **Transport Layer (Layer 4):** Responsible for **end-to-end communication**, **segmentation**, **flow control**, and **reliable delivery** between processes running on the end hosts. It uses **port numbers** for multiplexing. *Example:* **TCP (reliable) or UDP (connectionless)**. *(1 mark each for function distinction, 1 mark for protocol examples.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Alert** > - Students commonly **lose 2 marks** by forgetting to label **which side (sender/receiver)** of the OSI stack they are drawing. Always indicate directionality of data flow. > - In Protocol mapping questions, students map **Modbus TCP to Layers 5 and 6** — this is **incorrect**. Modbus TCP runs only over Layers **1, 2, 3, 4, 7**. There is no formal Layer 5/6 component. > - Do **not** write *"Layer 4 ensures packet delivery"* — packets exist at Layer 3, not Layer 4. Layer 4 carries **segments**. --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Module Internal Choice — Option 1)* > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]** > *(a)* Explain the function of each of the seven layers of the OSI Reference Model with suitable diagrams. *(7 marks)* > *(b)* With neat diagrams, illustrate the **encapsulation** and **decapsulation** processes of a message transmitted from a PLC to a SCADA HMI using Modbus TCP. Mention the PDU at each layer. *(7 marks)* #### **Solution (a) — 7 Marks** A top-down enumeration with one line of justification per layer: 1. **Application (L7):** Provides network services directly to end-user applications (HMI, SCADA software). It defines the *semantics* of the data (e.g., OPC UA read/write, Modbus function codes). 2. **Presentation (L6):** Handles **data representation**, encryption (TLS/SSL), and compression. Converts between formats such as JSON ↔ XML ↔ binary CIP structures. 3. **Session (L5):** Manages **dialog control** between two communicating processes — establishing, maintaining, and terminating sessions. Example: RPC session between HMI and PLC. 4. **Transport (L4):** Ensures **end-to-end** data integrity via **TCP** (reliable, 3-way handshake) or **UDP** (low-latency). Uses **16-bit port numbers** for process multiplexing. 5. **Network (L3):** Provides **logical addressing (IPv4/IPv6)** and **routing** through intermediate routers across plant LANs. 6. **Data Link (L2):** Handles **framing**, **MAC addressing (48-bit)**, error detection (CRC), and media access control (CSMA/CD in classic Ethernet). 7. **Physical (L1):** Converts frames into **electrical/optical/RF signals**; defines connectors, voltages, bit rates (e.g., 100 Mbps on Cat5e, 1 Gbps on fibre). *(1 mark for each layer explained adequately. Add a sketch of the 7-layer stack to secure the diagram marks.)* #### **Solution (b) — 7 Marks** **Encapsulation (Sender — PLC Side):** Starting with the Modbus Application Data Unit (ADU): | Step | Layer | Action | Resulting PDU | |------|-------|--------|---------------| | 1 | 7 | Modbus function code + payload | **Modbus PDU** (e.g., Read Holding Registers) | | 2 | 6 | Encoding (binary little-endian) | **PDU bytes** | | 3 | 5 | (Implicit, no formal Session header in Modbus) | — | | 4 | 4 | Append TCP header (src port, dst port 502, SEQ, ACK) | **TCP Segment** | | 5 | 3 | Append IP header (src IP, dst IP) | **IP Packet** | | 6 | 2 | Append Ethernet MAC header + CRC trailer | **Ethernet Frame** | | 7 | 1 | Encode to Manchester/MLT-3 electrical signal | **Bits on the wire** | **Decapsulation (Receiver — HMI Side):** The HMI reverses each step. The receiving Ethernet NIC strips the CRC and MAC header → IP layer strips IP header → TCP layer reassembles and acknowledges → Application extracts the Modbus function code and responds with register values. *(Diagram — show the PLC block on left with 7 layers, the HMI block on right with 7 layers, and vertical arrows showing downward encapsulation on the PLC side and upward decapsulation on the HMI side. 4 marks for the encapsulation table, 2 marks for decapsulation explanation, 1 mark for diagram clarity.)* --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Module Internal Choice — Option 2)* > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]** > *(a)* Compare the **OSI Reference Model** with the **TCP/IP Reference Model** in terms of layer count, layer names, and protocol examples. *(7 marks)* > *(b)* A chemical plant uses **PROFINET** to connect 50 PLCs to a central SCADA server over a 1 Gbps LAN. Map each PROFINET component to the appropriate OSI layer(s). Explain how the ISO/OSI model helps in troubleshooting a *"PLC not responding"* fault. *(7 marks)* #### **Solution (a) — 7 Marks** **Comparison Table:** | Feature | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model | |---------|-----------|--------------| | Layers | **7** | **4** (sometimes shown as 5) | | Origin | ISO/IEC 7498-1 | DARPA / ARPANET (1970s) | | Layer 7 | Application | **Application** (HTTP, FTP, DNS, OPC UA) | | Layer 6 | Presentation | (merged into Application) | | Layer 5 | Session | (merged into Application) | | Layer 4 | Transport | **Transport** (TCP, UDP) | | Layer 3 | Network | **Internet** (IP, ICMP, ARP) | | Layer 2 | Data Link | **Network Access** (Ethernet, Wi-Fi) | | Layer 1 | Physical | (merged into Network Access) | | Practical Use | Reference / design | Real-world implementation | | Development | ISO-driven | Vendor-driven | **Key Differences:** - OSI is a **theoretical reference model**; TCP/IP is a **practical implementation model**. - OSI distinguishes **Session and Presentation** layers explicitly, while TCP/IP collapses them into the Application layer. - OSI has **strict, distinct boundaries**; TCP/IP layers are **loosely defined**. - TCP/IP protocols (e.g., HTTP, TCP, IP) **predate** the OSI standardization and were the de-facto internet standard. *(3 marks for table, 4 marks for narrative comparison.)* #### **Solution (b) — 7 Marks** **PROFINET → OSI Mapping:** | PROFINET Component | OSI Layer | Justification | |--------------------|-----------|---------------| | 100BASE-TX electrical signalling, MDI connectors, fibre optics | **Layer 1 — Physical** | Defines cables, connectors, voltages | | Ethernet IEEE 802.3 MAC, VLAN tagging, real-time (RT) framing | **Layer 2 — Data Link** | Provides MAC addressing, CSMA/CD, frame delimitation | | IP addressing of PLCs, subnetting | **Layer 3 — Network** | Logical addressing across plant | | UDP (for RT frames) / TCP (for diagnostic) | **Layer 4 — Transport** | Multiplexing of services (e.g., port 34962/34963 for PROFINET RT) | | PROFINET IO device discovery, RPC channel, cyclic data exchange | **Layer 7 — Application** | Application semantics and IO data model | Layers 5 and 6 are **not explicitly used** in PROFINET — they are either implicit or merged into Layer 7. **Troubleshooting "PLC not responding" using OSI:** A maintenance engineer walks **bottom-up**: 1. **Layer 1 check:** Is the PROFINET cable plugged in? Is the link LED green on the switch port? Use a **cable tester** or **time-domain reflectometer (TDR)** if a break is suspected. 2. **Layer 2 check:** Is the MAC address learned by the switch? Any CRC errors in the switch logs? Use **Wireshark** with a port mirror to capture PROFINET frames. 3. **Layer 3 check:** Can you `ping` the PLC's IP address from the SCADA server? If ping fails, check IP/subnet mask/gateway. 4. **Layer 4 check:** Is UDP port 34964 (PROFINET RT) open? Any TCP retransmits on port 34962 (PROFINET Context Manager)? 5. **Layer 7 check:** Is the PLC visible in the PROFINET controller's IO view? Is its device name correctly assigned? Is the GSD file loaded? *(3 marks for mapping table, 3 marks for bottom-up troubleshooting, 1 mark for tools/example.)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read before every KTU exam** - [ ] **7 Layers:** Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical (top → bottom). - [ ] **Mnemonic Top-Down:** **A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing. - [ ] **Mnemonic Bottom-Up:** **P**lease **D**o **N**ot **T**hrow **S**ausage **P**izza **A**way. - [ ] **PDUs:** Bits → Frame → Packet → Segment → Data (L1 → L2 → L3 → L4 → L7). - [ ] **Addresses by Layer:** MAC (L2, 48-bit), IP (L3, 32-bit/128-bit), Port (L4, 16-bit). - [ ] **OSI vs TCP/IP:** OSI = 7 layers, theoretical, ISO/IEC 7498-1. TCP/IP = 4 layers, practical, ARPANET origin. - [ ] **Industrial protocols mapping:** - **Modbus RTU** → only L1, L2, L7. - **Modbus TCP** → L1, L2, L3, L4, L7. - **PROFINET** → L1, L2, L3, L4, L7 (RT/IRT uses L2 cut-through). - **PROFIBUS DP** → L1, L2, L7. - [ ] **Troubleshooting order:** Always **bottom-up** (Physical → Application). - [ ] **Encapsulation identity:** $SDU^{(N)} = PDU^{(N+1)}$ — payload of a layer comes from the layer above. - [ ] **Headers/Trailers:** Layers 2, 3, 4 add **headers**; Layer 2 adds a **CRC trailer**; Layers 5, 6, 7 generally do not add headers in classic OSI. - [ ] **Standard Reference:** ISO/IEC 7498-1 (1984, revised 1994). > **End of Module 4 — Topic: OSI Reference Model**

topics.title: Open Networks: Types of Cable and Optical Fibre Cable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT747 - INDUSTRIAL NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Open Networks: Types of Cable and Optical Fibre Cable ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What are Open Networks? **Open Networks** refer to industrial communication systems that use **publicly accessible, standardized, vendor-neutral transmission media and protocols**. Unlike proprietary "closed" networks (e.g., a single vendor's PLC bus), an open network allows any compliant device from any manufacturer to interoperate on a shared physical medium. The **physical layer** of an open network typically relies on standardized cabling — copper twisted pairs, coaxial cables, or **optical fibre cables (OFC)** — that are specified by international standards such as **ISO/IEC 11801**, **EIA/TIA-568**, and **IEEE 802**. > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus-Aligned):** > *An open network is a multi-vendor, standards-based communication architecture in which the physical transmission medium, data link layer, and application layer are governed by open, non-proprietary specifications, enabling interoperability between heterogeneous industrial devices.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Public Highway" Imagine a **public national highway**: - The **road itself** (asphalt, lanes, guardrails) is the **cable** (twisted pair, coax, or fibre). - The **traffic rules** (speed limits, lane discipline) are the **protocol** (Ethernet, Profinet, Modbus TCP). - The **vehicles** are the **data packets/frames**. Any car (device) that follows the rules can use the highway — that is "open". A private driveway leading only to one factory would be a "closed" or proprietary network. In industrial settings, an **optical fibre cable** is often chosen as the "highway" because it offers huge bandwidth, complete immunity to electrical noise, and long reach — critical in factories with motors, VFDs, and high-voltage equipment. ### 1.3 Classification of Transmission Cables The three principal families of cables used in open industrial networks are: 1. **Twisted-Pair Copper Cable** — balanced-pair copper conductors, used in RS-485, CAN, Profibus DP, Industrial Ethernet. 2. **Coaxial Cable** — single central conductor with a concentric shield, used in legacy fieldbuses and video/CATV. 3. **Optical Fibre Cable (OFC)** — glass or plastic strands guiding light by **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**, used in modern high-speed, noise-free, long-distance links. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** > In KTU Module 4, the focus is on the *physical media* of open networks — particularly the **structure, operating principles, numerical parameters, and selection criteria** of twisted-pair, coaxial, and optical fibre cables. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics at a Glance | Parameter | Typical Value | Cable Type | |---|---|---| | Bandwidth | **100 MHz** | Cat 5e UTP | | Bandwidth | **500 MHz – 2 GHz** | Cat 6A / Coax | | Bandwidth | **THz range** | Single-mode OFC | | Maximum Segment Length | **100 m** | UTP (Ethernet) | | Maximum Segment Length | **up to 40 km** | Single-mode fibre | | Immunity to EMI | **Very High** | Optical Fibre | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Twisted-Pair Cables A twisted pair consists of **two insulated copper conductors (typically 22–24 AWG)** helically twisted together. The twist: - Cancels **common-mode noise** (electromagnetic interference that affects both wires equally). - Reduces **crosstalk** between adjacent pairs. **Variants:** - **UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair)** — no overall shield; cheapest; used for office Ethernet. - **STP (Shielded Twisted Pair)** — each pair has a foil shield; better noise immunity; used in factories. - **S/FTP** — overall braid + per-pair foil; used in Profinet, Industrial Ethernet. > [!NOTE] > For **Profibus DP** and **RS-485**-based industrial fieldbuses, a single shielded twisted pair with a characteristic impedance of **150 Ω** is standard (not the 100 Ω of Ethernet). Always check the standard! ### 2.2 Coaxial Cable A coaxial cable has four concentric layers: 1. **Inner conductor** (solid or stranded copper). 2. **Dielectric insulator** (foamed PE, diameter determines impedance). 3. **Metallic shield** (braid + foil). 4. **Outer jacket** (PVC/LSZH). The **characteristic impedance** $Z_0$ is governed by the geometry: $$Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{d}\right) \;\; \Omega$$ where $D$ is the inner diameter of the shield, $d$ is the outer diameter of the inner conductor, and $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity of the dielectric. **Common industrial types:** - **50 Ω** — used in Ethernet (10BASE-2, 10BASE-5), instrumentation. - **75 Ω** — used in video, CATV (less common in industrial). - **93 Ω** — used in some legacy ARCnet networks. ### 2.3 Optical Fibre Cable — The Heart of Modern Open Networks #### 2.3.1 Operating Principle — Total Internal Reflection (TIR) An optical fibre is a cylindrical dielectric waveguide. The **core** (refractive index $n_1$) is surrounded by the **cladding** (refractive index $n_2$, with $n_2 < n_1$). Light launched into the core at a steep enough angle is trapped because it strikes the core-cladding boundary at an angle greater than the **critical angle** $\theta_c$. **Snell's Law at the core-cladding boundary:** $$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$ The critical angle is reached when $\theta_2 = 90°$: $$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$ For TIR, the ray inside the core must strike the boundary at an angle **greater than** $\theta_c$. #### 2.3.2 Numerical Aperture (NA) The **Numerical Aperture** quantifies the light-gathering ability of a fibre: $$\boxed{\;NA = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}\;}$$ where $\theta_a$ is the maximum acceptance angle (measured from the fibre axis). A larger NA = more light accepted = easier coupling but more modal dispersion. #### 2.3.3 Attenuation in Optical Fibre Signal loss is expressed in **dB/km**: $$\alpha = -\frac{10}{L} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right) \;\; \text{dB/km}$$ where $L$ is the fibre length in km, $P_{in}$ and $P_{out}$ are the input and output optical powers. **Typical attenuation values:** - **Multimode (OM1) @ 850 nm:** ~ **3 dB/km** - **Multimode (OM3) @ 850 nm:** ~ **1.5 dB/km** - **Single-mode (OS1) @ 1310 nm:** ~ **0.4 dB/km** - **Single-mode (OS1) @ 1550 nm:** ~ **0.2 dB/km** (lowest loss window) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **three optical windows** used in practice are **850 nm, 1310 nm, and 1550 nm**, each chosen to balance attenuation, dispersion, and the cost/availability of transceivers. #### 2.3.4 Types of Optical Fibres | Type | Core Diameter | NA | Bandwidth-Distance | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Step-Index Multimode (SI-MM)** | 50/62.5/100 µm | 0.20 – 0.30 | Low (≤ 40 MHz·km) | Legacy, short links | | **Graded-Index Multimode (GI-MM)** | 50/62.5 µm | 0.20 | High (500–2000 MHz·km) | LAN, data centres | | **Single-Mode (SM)** | 8–10 µm | 0.10 – 0.14 | Very High (THz·km) | Telecom, long-haul industrial backbones | - **Step-Index:** The core has a uniform refractive index; light rays travel in straight zig-zag paths → high modal dispersion. - **Graded-Index:** The core index varies parabolically; higher-order rays travel faster in lower-index regions → modal dispersion reduced by orders of magnitude. - **Single-Mode:** The core is so small that only the fundamental mode propagates → eliminates modal dispersion entirely. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{D}{d}\right)$ | Coaxial cable characteristic impedance | Ω | | 2 | $v = \dfrac{c}{n_1}$ | Speed of light inside fibre core | m/s | | 3 | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$ | Critical angle for TIR | radians / degrees | | 4 | $NA = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Numerical Aperture | dimensionless | | 5 | $\alpha = -\dfrac{10}{L}\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | Fibre attenuation coefficient | dB/km | | 6 | $\Delta t_{modal} = \dfrac{L \cdot n_1}{c}\!\left(\dfrac{n_1}{n_2} - 1\right)$ | Modal dispersion (SI fibre) | seconds | | 7 | $B_{max} = \dfrac{0.187}{\Delta t_{modal}}$ | Bandwidth limit (SI fibre) | Hz·m | | 8 | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$ | Output power given attenuation | W / dBm | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These equations let a network engineer *calculate link budgets* — verifying that the received optical power at the photodetector is above the receiver sensitivity and below its saturation threshold, while accounting for connector losses, splice losses, and fibre attenuation. This is a routine step in designing **Profinet over fibre**, **EtherCAT P**, and **FOUNDATION Fieldbus HSE** links in plants. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Numerical Aperture of a Step-Index Fibre **Problem:** A step-index optical fibre has a core refractive index $n_1 = 1.48$ and a cladding refractive index $n_2 = 1.46$. Calculate: (a) The Numerical Aperture (NA). (b) The maximum acceptance angle $\theta_a$ in degrees. (c) The critical angle $\theta_c$ at the core-cladding boundary. **Solution:** **(a) Numerical Aperture** We apply the defining formula for NA: $$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$ Substitute $n_1 = 1.48$ and $n_2 = 1.46$: $$NA = \sqrt{(1.48)^2 - (1.46)^2}$$ $$NA = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316}$$ $$NA = \sqrt{0.0588}$$ $$\boxed{NA \approx 0.2425}$$ **(b) Maximum acceptance angle** The NA equals the sine of the maximum acceptance angle measured from the fibre axis: $$\sin\theta_a = NA = 0.2425$$ Therefore: $$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.2425)$$ $$\boxed{\theta_a \approx 14.04^{\circ}}$$ This is the half-angle of the acceptance cone of light that the fibre can capture. **(c) Critical angle** The critical angle is found from Snell's law at the boundary, with the refracted ray grazing along the interface ($\theta_2 = 90°$): $$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$ $$\sin\theta_c = \frac{1.46}{1.48} = 0.98649$$ $$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.98649)$$ $$\boxed{\theta_c \approx 80.55^{\circ}}$$ Notice that $\theta_c$ is measured **from the normal** to the boundary, while $\theta_a$ is measured **from the fibre axis**. They are complementary: $\theta_c + \theta_a \approx 90°$ (exactly only when $NA \to 0$). --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Fibre Attenuation and Link Budget **Problem:** An LED source launches $P_{in} = 0.5\,\text{mW}$ (i.e. $-3\,\text{dBm}$) of optical power into a single-mode fibre of length $L = 25\,\text{km}$ with attenuation coefficient $\alpha = 0.4\,\text{dB/km}$ at the operating wavelength of 1310 nm. There are two connectors, each introducing a loss of $0.5\,\text{dB}$, and one fusion splice with $0.1\,\text{dB}$ loss. The receiver sensitivity is $-30\,\text{dBm}$. Determine: (a) The total link loss (in dB). (b) The received power (in dBm). (c) The power margin, and comment on link feasibility. **Solution:** **(a) Total link loss** The total loss is the sum of fibre attenuation, connector losses, and splice loss: $$L_{total} = \alpha L + N_{conn} L_{conn} + N_{splice} L_{splice}$$ Substitute: $$L_{total} = (0.4)(25) + (2)(0.5) + (1)(0.1)$$ $$L_{total} = 10.0 + 1.0 + 0.1$$ $$\boxed{L_{total} = 11.1\;\text{dB}}$$ **(b) Received power** Starting from $-3\,\text{dBm}$, subtract the total loss: $$P_{rx}\,[\text{dBm}] = P_{in}\,[\text{dBm}] - L_{total}$$ $$P_{rx} = -3 - 11.1$$ $$\boxed{P_{rx} = -14.1\;\text{dBm}}$$ **(c) Power margin** $$M = P_{rx} - P_{rx,min} = -14.1 - (-30)$$ $$\boxed{M = 15.9\;\text{dB}}$$ Since $M > 0$ and comfortably so, the link is **feasible**. Industry practice demands a **safety margin ≥ 3 dB** to allow for ageing, temperature effects, and repairs. With 15.9 dB available, the link is highly reliable. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Modal Dispersion in Step-Index Fibre **Problem:** A step-index multimode fibre has $n_1 = 1.50$, $n_2 = 1.48$, and length $L = 1\,\text{km}$. Calculate the maximum modal dispersion $\Delta t_{modal}$ and the corresponding bandwidth-length product. **Solution:** The maximum modal dispersion occurs between the highest-order ray (path length $L/\sin\theta_c$) and the axial ray (path length $L$): $$\Delta t_{modal} = \frac{L\,n_1}{c}\!\left(\frac{n_1}{n_2} - 1\right)$$ Substitute the values ($c = 3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$, $L = 1000\,\text{m}$): $$\Delta t_{modal} = \frac{(1000)(1.50)}{3 \times 10^{8}}\!\left(\frac{1.50}{1.48} - 1\right)$$ $$\Delta t_{modal} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times (1.01351 - 1)$$ $$\Delta t_{modal} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.01351$$ $$\boxed{\Delta t_{modal} \approx 67.6\;\text{ns per km}}$$ This severe pulse spreading is why step-index multimode fibre is restricted to short, low-speed links. The approximate bandwidth is: $$B_{max} = \frac{0.187}{\Delta t_{modal}} = \frac{0.187}{67.6 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$\boxed{B_{max} \approx 2.77\;\text{MHz·km}}$$ Compare this to a graded-index fibre, which can deliver **500–2000 MHz·km** — a 1000× improvement. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cable Selection in Industry | Criterion | UTP | STP | Coaxial | Multimode Fibre | Single-Mode Fibre | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Max Segment | 100 m | 100 m | 500 m | 2 km (OM3 @ 10G) | 40–100 km | | Bandwidth | 100 MHz | 100 MHz | 1 GHz | 500–2000 MHz·km | THz·km | | EMI Immunity | Low | Medium | High | **Total** | **Total** | | Cost (per metre) | Very Low | Low | Low–Medium | Medium | High | | Connector | RJ45 | RJ45 | BNC / N | SC / LC / ST | SC / LC / FC | | Fieldbus Use | Ethernet, Profinet | Profibus, RS-485 | Legacy, video | Industrial Ethernet | Plant backbone | | Hazardous Area Use | No | Limited | No | **Yes** (intrinsic safety) | **Yes** | > [!TIP] > **Rule of Thumb for Industrial Networks:** > - Inside cabinet / office → **UTP/STP** > - Plant floor with motors/VFDs → **STP or fibre** > - Long distance / hazardous area → **Single-mode fibre** --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Cross-Sectional Anatomy of an Optical Fibre Cable ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A["INNER STRUCTURE OF OPTICAL FIBRE CABLE"] direction TB node1["Optical Fibres
Glass Strands 8 to 10 um SM or 50 um MM"] --> node2["Buffer Coating
Acrylate 250 um"] node2 --> node3["Strength Member
Aramid Yarn Kevlar"] node3 --> node4["Inner Jacket
PE or LSZH"] node4 --> node5["Armour Layer Optional
Steel Tape or Corrugated Steel"] node5 --> node6["Outer Sheath
Black HDPE UV Resistant"] end subgraph B["LIGHT PROPAGATION PRINCIPLE"] direction TB nodeA["Source LED or LASER"] --> nodeB["Launch Cone NA 0.1 to 0.3"] nodeB --> nodeC["Core n1 higher index"] nodeC --> nodeD["Cladding n2 lower index"] nodeD --> nodeE["Total Internal Reflection at Boundary"] nodeE --> nodeF["Light Confined in Core"] end ``` ### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Comparison of Light Propagation in Fibre Types ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SM["SINGLE MODE FIBRE"] direction TB sm1["Core 8 to 10 um"] --> sm2["Single Ray Path Straight"] sm2 --> sm3["No Modal Dispersion"] sm3 --> sm4["Long Distance 40 to 100 km"] end subgraph GI["GRADED INDEX MULTIMODE"] direction TB gi1["Core 50 to 62.5 um"] --> gi2["Parabolic Index Profile"] gi2 --> gi3["Higher Order Rays Travel Faster in Low Index Region"] gi3 --> gi4["Modal Dispersion Minimised"] end subgraph SI["STEP INDEX MULTIMODE"] direction TB si1["Core 50 to 100 um"] --> si2["Sudden Index Change at Boundary"] si2 --> si3["Zig Zag Ray Paths"] si3 --> si4["Severe Modal Dispersion"] end ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Industrial Open Network Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph field["FIELD LEVEL"] f1["Sensor Transducer 4 to 20 mA"] --> f2["Transmitter with HART"] f2 --> f3["Remote I/O Block"] end subgraph ctrl["CONTROL LEVEL"] f3 --> c1["PLC with Profinet Interface"] c1 --> c2["Industrial Ethernet Switch Managed"] end subgraph enterp["ENTERPRISE LEVEL"] c2 --> e1["SCADA HMI Server"] e1 --> e2["Plant Historian"] e2 --> e3["ERP or MES Cloud"] end subgraph phys["PHYSICAL MEDIA LAYER"] c1 -.->|Cat 6A STP Copper| c2 c2 -.->|Multimode OM3 OFC| e1 e1 -.->|Single Mode OFC| e2 end ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Optical Fibre Communication Link ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TX["TRANSMITTER"] direction TB tx1["Electrical Data Input"] --> tx2["Driver Circuit"] tx2 --> tx3["LED or LASER Diode"] tx3 --> tx4["Optical Coupler Lens"] end subgraph CH["CHANNEL OPTICAL FIBRE"] direction TB tx4 --> ch1["Core"] ch1 --> ch2["Cladding"] ch2 --> ch3["Jacket"] ch3 --> ch4["Attenuation alpha dB per km"] ch4 --> ch5["Dispersion and Splices"] end subgraph RX["RECEIVER"] direction TB ch5 --> rx1["Photodetector PIN or APD"] rx1 --> rx2["Transimpedance Amplifier"] rx2 --> rx3["Decision Circuit"] rx3 --> rx4["Electrical Data Output"] end ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** *Define the term "Numerical Aperture" of an optical fibre. What is its significance in optical fibre communication?* **Model Answer:** Numerical Aperture (NA) is a dimensionless quantity that measures the light-gathering ability of an optical fibre. It is defined as the sine of the maximum acceptance angle $\theta_a$ of light that can be coupled into the fibre and guided by total internal reflection: $$NA = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$ **Significance:** A higher NA means a larger acceptance cone, so the fibre can capture more light from an incoherent source such as an LED, easing coupling tolerance.
